slides.body: You cannot eyeball fairness from an accuracy score. Fairness is a property you compute by comparing outcomes across groups. Let's take loan-approval data and actually measure whether the model treats two groups equally — with code you can run.

slides.title: 1. Approval rate per group
slides.code:
import pandas as pd

# df columns: gender, approved (1 = yes, 0 = no)
rates = df.groupby("gender")["approved"].mean()
print(rates)
# female    0.58
# male      0.71

slides.title: 2. The disparate-impact ratio
slides.code:
di = rates["female"] / rates["male"]
print(round(di, 2))    # -> 0.82

# the "80% rule": a ratio below 0.80 is a
# recognised red flag for adverse impact

slides.title: 3. Proof: proxies leak the attribute
slides.code:
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression

X = df.drop(columns=["gender", "approved"])   # "neutral"
proxy = LogisticRegression(max_iter=500).fit(X, df["gender"])
print(round(proxy.score(X, df["gender"]), 2))  # -> 0.89
# the model recovers gender from "innocent" columns

slides.title: What those numbers mean
slides.body: An 0.82 ratio says women are approved ~18% less often — close to the legal red line. And the 0.89 proxy score proves you can't fix that by hiding gender: the 'neutral' features still encode it. Deleting the column blinds you, it doesn't debias you.

slides.title: 4. A simple mitigation
Measuring fairness in practice
bullets: Pick a fairness metric that fits the problem first
Always slice metrics by group, never just overall
Keep the sensitive attribute — to audit, not to decide
Use a library (Fairlearn, AIF360) for rigour

slides.title: Optimising accuracy alone
slides.body: A model can be 95% accurate overall and still fail one group badly — high accuracy can hide low fairness when a group is a small slice of the data. If you only ever look at the aggregate number, the bias is invisible until someone gets hurt.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 7.
slides.body: Next: the 5 most common mistakes people make about AI fairness.

6-5.caption:
"It's just math, so it's objective." "Delete the race column and it's fair." "More data fixes bias." All wrong — and all harmful. 🚫

These five misconceptions about AI fairness are the ones that get models pulled, companies sued, and people hurt. Here's each, and the fix.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• #1 AI is objective
• #2 removing the sensitive feature fixes it
• #3 more data fixes bias
• #4 fairness has one definition
• #5 ethics is a final checkbox

Get these right and you're ahead of most teams shipping AI today. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Why these myths are costly
slides.body: Fairness mistakes don't just look bad in a slide deck — they ship biased systems that harm people and expose companies to lawsuits and recalls. These five are the most common, and each has a concrete fix you can apply this week.

slides.title: #1 — 'AI is objective'
slides.body: Because it's maths, people assume it's neutral. But a model only reflects its data and its designers' choices — including their blind spots. 'Objective' is exactly the disguise that lets bias pass unchallenged. Treat every model as opinionated until proven otherwise.

slides.title: #2 — 'Just remove the sensitive feature'
slides.body: Dropping 'race' or 'gender' feels like a fix, but proxies (ZIP, name, history) leak the attribute back — and now you can't even measure the bias. Fairness-through-blindness usually fails. Often you must keep the attribute to audit and correct outcomes.

slides.title: #3 — 'More data fixes bias'
slides.body: If the extra data carries the same bias, you've just made the model more confident in being unfair. Volume doesn't equal fairness. What helps is representative data and explicit fairness work — not simply a bigger pile of the same skew.

slides.title: #4 — 'Fairness has one definition'
slides.body: Equal approval rates, equal accuracy, and equal error rates across groups are different goals — and they can be mathematically impossible to satisfy at once. There's no universal 'fair'. You must choose the definition that fits the context, and defend it.

slides.title: #5 — 'Ethics is a final checkbox'
slides.body: Auditing for bias the week before launch is too late — the data, labels, and metric are already locked in. Ethics has to steer the early choices: what data, what to optimise, who's in the loop. Bolted on at the end, it can only document the damage.

slides.title: A fairness checklist as a flow

yes.question: Did you measure outcomes per group?

slides.title: The fixes
bullets: Treat models as opinionated, never neutral
Keep sensitive attributes to audit, not to decide on
Seek representative data, not just more data
Choose a fairness definition that fits the problem
Make ethics shape early decisions, not final ones

slides.title: Day 6 done. Follow for Day 7.
slides.body: Next: How Models Learn — the intuition behind training, no heavy math.

7-1.caption:
A model doesn't memorize answers. It tunes a giant pile of numbers until its guesses stop being embarrassing. 🎛️

"Learning" sounds mystical, but the intuition is simple: start with random settings, make a prediction, measure how wrong you were, nudge the settings to be a little less wrong, and repeat millions of times. That loop is the whole secret.

This post builds the mental model before any math. Once you see learning as guided trial-and-error, every algorithm name you meet later stops being scary.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• what "learning" actually means for a model
• parameters as the dials the model turns
• the guess → check → adjust loop
• why error is the thing being minimized
• how this differs from a programmer writing rules
• the one-sentence version you can repeat

Get the intuition first. The equations are easy after that. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Learning, in one line
slides.body: A model learns by adjusting its internal numbers so its predictions get closer to the right answers. It never sees the rule for the task — it discovers settings that happen to produce good outputs on the examples it's shown.

slides.title: The dials analogy
slides.body: Picture thousands of dials inside the model. Each dial slightly changes the output. Training is the process of slowly turning every dial in the direction that makes the model less wrong, over and over, until the outputs are good.

slides.title: Error is the compass
slides.body: The model has no idea what "correct" means in the abstract. It only knows one number: how far its guess was from the truth. Shrinking that error is the entire objective of training.

slides.title: What the model is NOT doing
bullets: Not memorizing a lookup table
Not understanding meaning
Not following human-written logic
Just minimizing a number called loss

slides.title: From random to right
slides.body: On day one the dials are random, so guesses are nonsense. Each pass through the data shifts them a hair. After enough passes the once-random numbers encode a surprisingly capable function.

slides.title: Learning in 6 lines
slides.code:
params = random_init()
for x, y in data:
    pred = model(x, params)      # guess
    err  = (pred - y) ** 2       # how wrong
    grad = gradient(err, params) # which way to turn
    params -= 0.01 * grad        # nudge the dials

slides.title: The one-sentence version
slides.body: Learning is guided trial-and-error: predict, measure the mistake, and shift the model a little toward being right — repeated until the mistakes are small.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 8.
slides.body: Next we zoom out: why this guess-check-adjust loop matters for everything you'll build.

7-2.caption:
If you don't get how models learn, every AI decision looks like luck or magic. 🔍

The learning loop isn't trivia — it's the reason models need data, the reason they have biases, the reason "more examples" usually beats "cleverer prompt," and the reason a model can be confidently wrong. All of it traces back to one mechanism: minimizing error on whatever data you fed it.

This post is the "so what." Understand the stakes and you'll make better calls about data, evaluation, and trust.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• why this loop is the foundation of all ML
• why data quality decides model quality
• where bias quietly enters
• why models generalize — and when they don't
• what this means for your trust in outputs
• the practical takeaways for builders

Know the mechanism, and the headlines stop fooling you. Save it. 👇

slides.title: It's the foundation of everything
slides.body: Every model you'll ever use — spam filters, recommenders, chatbots, image generators — runs the same guess-check-adjust loop under the hood. Understand it once and the whole field stops being a collection of unrelated buzzwords.

slides.title: Garbage in, garbage learned
slides.body: A model only learns from the examples it sees. If your data is wrong, narrow, or skewed, the model faithfully learns that wrongness. The mechanism has no taste — it minimizes error on whatever you give it.

slides.title: Bias enters through data
slides.body: Because learning copies patterns in the training data, any unfair pattern in that data becomes a learned rule. The model isn't malicious — it's a mirror. That's why "the algorithm is neutral" is a myth.

slides.title: Why you can't fully trust output
bullets: It optimized error, not truth
It can be confidently wrong
It only knows its training world
Always verify high-stakes results

slides.title: More data usually wins
slides.body: Because learning is statistical, the most reliable lever is often more and better examples — not a fancier model. This is why teams obsess over datasets, not just architectures.

slides.title: Same loop, any task
slides.code:
# spam filter, recommender, chatbot:
# the training call looks identical
for batch in dataset:
    loss = error(model(batch.x), batch.y)
    loss.backward()   # find the nudge
    optimizer.step()  # apply the nudge

root.question: Was the training data fair & complete?

slides.title: Takeaways for builders
bullets: Invest in data before models
Audit data for skew and gaps
Measure on data the model never saw
Treat confidence as a guess, not proof

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 8.
slides.body: Next we open the hood: the actual mechanics of how the dials get turned.

7-3.caption:
The model turns its dials with calculus — but the idea is just "walk downhill until you can't go lower." 🏔️

Here's the mechanism most people never see. The model defines an error landscape: high where it's very wrong, low where it's right. Training is rolling a ball downhill on that landscape by repeatedly taking small steps in the steepest-descent direction. That step size is the learning rate, and that direction is the gradient.

This post is the how. No hand-waving — the real moving parts, kept in plain language.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• the loss function as a landscape
• gradients: which way is downhill
• gradient descent step by step
• the learning rate and why it's touchy
• epochs, batches, and iterations
• when descent gets stuck

This is the engine of modern AI. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Loss = a number to shrink
slides.body: A loss function turns "how wrong was this guess" into a single number. Big loss means bad, near-zero means good. The whole job of training is to make this one number as small as it can go.

slides.title: Loss is a landscape
slides.body: Imagine plotting loss against the model's settings. You get a hilly surface — peaks where the model is terrible, valleys where it's great. Training is the search for a low valley on that surface.

slides.title: Gradient = the downhill arrow
slides.body: The gradient is the slope of the loss surface at your current spot. It points in the direction loss increases fastest — so you step the opposite way. That single trick is gradient descent.

slides.title: One training step
bullets: Make predictions on a batch
Compute the loss (how wrong)
Get the gradient (which way is up)
Step the opposite way (downhill)
Repeat with the next batch

slides.title: Gradient descent by hand
slides.code:
lr = 0.1
w = 0.0                      # one dial
for _ in range(50):
    pred = w * x
    loss = ((pred - y) ** 2).mean()
    grad = (2 * x * (pred - y)).mean()  # slope
    w -= lr * grad           # step downhill

slides.title: The learning rate is touchy
slides.body: Steps too big and you leap across the valley and diverge. Steps too small and training crawls for days. The learning rate sets step size, and tuning it is one of the most consequential knobs in ML.

slides.title: Epoch vs batch vs step
slides.body: A step updates the dials once using one batch of examples. An epoch is one full pass over all the data. Real training runs many epochs, taking thousands of small steps along the way.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 8.
slides.body: Next: a full runnable training loop you can paste and watch the loss drop.

7-4.caption:
Talk is cheap — let's watch a model actually learn. 💻

This is the whole loop in real, runnable code: random weights, a guess, a loss, a gradient, a nudge, repeated until the line fits the data. No framework magic first — we do it by hand so you see every moving part, then show the PyTorch version that does the same thing in fewer lines.

Paste it, run it, and watch the loss fall. That "aha" beats any diagram.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• generate a tiny dataset
• a from-scratch gradient-descent loop
• watch the loss shrink each step
• the same thing in PyTorch
• how autograd turns the dials for you
• what to tweak to break (and fix) it

Run it yourself. The intuition becomes permanent. Save it. 👇

slides.title: The plan
slides.body: We fit a straight line y = w·x + b to noisy data using nothing but the guess-check-adjust loop. First by hand with NumPy, then with PyTorch so you see what the framework automates.

slides.title: 1. Make some data
slides.code:
import numpy as np
np.random.seed(0)
x = np.linspace(-1, 1, 100)
# true line: y = 3x + 2, plus noise
y = 3 * x + 2 + np.random.randn(100) * 0.3

slides.title: 2. Learn from scratch
slides.code:
w, b, lr = 0.0, 0.0, 0.1
for step in range(200):
    pred = w * x + b
    err  = pred - y
    loss = (err ** 2).mean()
    dw = (2 * x * err).mean()
    db = (2 * err).mean()
    w -= lr * dw
    b -= lr * db

slides.title: 3. Watch the loss drop
slides.code:
if step % 50 == 0:
        print(f"step {step:3d}  loss {loss:.3f}  w {w:.2f}  b {b:.2f}")
# step   0  loss 13.1  w 0.59  b 0.40
# step  50  loss 0.12  w 2.91  b 1.98
# step 150  loss 0.09  w 3.00  b 2.00

slides.title: 4. Same thing in PyTorch
slides.code:
import torch
X = torch.tensor(x, dtype=torch.float32).reshape(-1,1)
Y = torch.tensor(y, dtype=torch.float32).reshape(-1,1)
model = torch.nn.Linear(1, 1)
opt   = torch.optim.SGD(model.parameters(), lr=0.1)
loss_fn = torch.nn.MSELoss()

slides.title: 5. The framework loop
slides.code:
for step in range(200):
    pred = model(X)
    loss = loss_fn(pred, Y)
    opt.zero_grad()
    loss.backward()   # autograd finds dw, db
    opt.step()        # applies the nudge

slides.title: What autograd did for you
slides.body: By hand we computed the gradients dw and db ourselves. PyTorch's loss.backward() does that automatically for any model — even one with millions of dials. opt.step() then applies the same w -= lr * grad rule you wrote.

slides.title: Break it, then fix it
bullets: Set lr=2.0 → loss explodes (too big)
Set lr=0.0001 → barely moves (too small)
Drop the noise → fits almost perfectly
Add more steps → w,b lock onto 3 and 2

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 8.
slides.body: Next: the traps that make learning fail — and how to spot them early.

7-5.caption:
Most failed models don't crash — they learn the wrong thing and look fine in your notebook. ⚠️

The learning loop is simple, which is exactly why it's easy to fool yourself. You can hit near-zero training loss and still ship something useless, because the model memorized your examples instead of learning the pattern. These are the mistakes that bite beginners again and again.

This post names the traps and gives the quick checks that catch them.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• judging a model on training data
• overfitting: memorizing, not learning
• leaking the answer into the inputs
• a learning rate that's wrong
• confusing low loss with being correct
• the checklist that saves you

Learn the failure modes before they cost you. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Grading on the training set
slides.body: If you measure a model on the same data it trained on, of course it looks great — it has seen the answers. The only honest test is data the model never touched during training.

slides.title: Overfitting
slides.body: When training loss keeps dropping but the model gets worse on new data, it's memorizing noise instead of learning the signal. It aced the practice exam by memorizing it — and fails the real one.

slides.title: Data leakage
slides.body: If a feature secretly encodes the answer — like an ID assigned after the outcome — the model "learns" by cheating. Accuracy looks magical in testing, then collapses in production where the leak isn't there.

slides.title: A classic leak
slides.code:
# BAD: scaling using the whole dataset
mean = X.mean()           # peeks at test rows!
X = (X - mean) / X.std()
X_tr, X_te = split(X)

# GOOD: fit on train, then apply
mean = X_tr.mean()
X_tr = (X_tr - mean) / X_tr.std()
X_te = (X_te - mean) / X_tr.std()

slides.title: Wrong learning rate
slides.body: Too high and the loss explodes or bounces forever; too low and training stalls before it learns anything. If loss isn't steadily dropping, the learning rate is the first dial to suspect.

slides.title: Low loss ≠ correct
slides.body: A model can minimize loss perfectly and still be wrong about the world if the loss or the data was wrong. Optimizing a flawed objective just gets you confidently, precisely wrong answers.

slides.title: The checklist
bullets: Split before you touch the data
Evaluate only on held-out data
Watch the train–test loss gap
Fit scalers on train only
Sanity-check predictions by hand

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 8.
slides.body: Day 8 begins Math for ML — the linear algebra that powers every dial we just turned.

8-1.caption:
Every model you've ever trained is secretly a pile of vectors and matrices. 🧮

Linear algebra is the language ML actually speaks. A data point is a vector. A dataset is a matrix. A neural layer is a matrix multiply. Strip away the framework and what's left is arrays of numbers being added, scaled, and multiplied.

You don't need a full math degree — you need a working mental model of four objects and what they mean.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• what scalars, vectors, matrices and tensors really are
• why your dataset is literally a matrix
• how a feature becomes a coordinate in space
• the shape rules that govern every model
• why "dimensions" means columns, not magic

Get the vocabulary right and half of ML stops looking like wizardry. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Linear Algebra: The Language of ML
What it actually is
slides.body: Linear algebra is the math of vectors, matrices, and the operations between them. In ML it's not abstract theory — every dataset, weight, and prediction is stored as an array of numbers, and training is just doing arithmetic on those arrays at scale.

slides.title: The four core objects
bullets: Scalar: a single number (3.14)
Vector: an ordered list ([2, 0, 5])
Matrix: a grid of numbers (rows × cols)
Tensor: an n-dimensional array (images, batches)

slides.title: Your dataset IS a matrix
slides.body: Each row is one example. Each column is one feature. A spreadsheet of 1000 houses with 5 features is a 1000×5 matrix. The model never sees "houses" — it sees a grid of floats and learns weights that map columns to a target.

slides.title: Features = dimensions
slides.body: Two features means each example is a point on a 2-D plane. Five features means a point in 5-D space — impossible to picture, trivial to compute. "High-dimensional" just means many columns. The math doesn't care how many.

slides.title: See the shapes
slides.code:
import numpy as np

scalar = np.array(3.0)
vector = np.array([2.0, 0.0, 5.0])
matrix = np.array([[1, 2, 3],
                   [4, 5, 6]])

print(scalar.shape)  # ()
print(vector.shape)  # (3,)
print(matrix.shape)  # (2, 3)

slides.title: Shape is everything
slides.body: A shape like (2, 3) means 2 rows, 3 columns. Almost every ML bug you'll hit is a shape mismatch. Reading shapes fluently — and predicting how an operation changes them — is the single most useful linear-algebra skill day to day.

slides.title: Math notation vs code
bullets: x → a vector (1-D array)
X → a matrix, capitalized (2-D array)
xᵢ → the i-th example (a row)
wᵀx → a dot product (weighted sum)

slides.title: What to internalize first
bullets: Row = one example, column = one feature
Vectors are points in space
Matrices transform vectors
Shapes must line up to multiply
Tensors are just stacked matrices

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 9.
slides.body: Next up: WHY this math is non-negotiable for ML — speed, geometry, and intuition.

8-2.caption:
You can learn ML without linear algebra. You just can't understand it. 🔍

Frameworks hide the matrices, so it's tempting to skip the math. But the moment a model misbehaves, vectorizes too slowly, or overfits in a way you can't explain — the abstraction leaks, and you're back to the algebra.

Linear algebra is what makes models fast, what gives "similarity" a precise meaning, and what turns optimization into geometry you can reason about.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• why vectorized math is 100× faster than loops
• how dot products define similarity and search
• why embeddings live in vector space
• how gradients move you across a loss surface
• what you lose when you skip the fundamentals

The people who debug models fastest are the ones who can picture the shapes. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Why Linear Algebra Runs All of ML
Speed: loops vs vectors
slides.body: A Python for-loop over a million numbers is slow because each step pays interpreter overhead. Expressing the same work as one matrix operation lets optimized C/BLAS code and GPUs run it in parallel. Same result, often 100×+ faster.

slides.title: Dot products = similarity
slides.body: The dot product of two vectors measures how aligned they are. That single operation powers cosine similarity, attention scores in Transformers, recommendation matching, and vector-database search. Master it and a dozen ML ideas click at once.

slides.title: Similarity in 3 lines
slides.code:
import numpy as np

a = np.array([1.0, 2.0, 3.0])
b = np.array([2.0, 4.0, 6.0])

cos = a @ b / (np.linalg.norm(a) * np.linalg.norm(b))
print(round(cos, 3))  # 1.0  (same direction)

slides.title: Embeddings live in space
slides.body: Words, images, and users get mapped to vectors so that 'close' means 'similar'. King − Man + Woman ≈ Queen works because meaning is encoded as geometry. Without vector space, there is no embedding, no semantic search, no RAG.

slides.title: Training is geometry
slides.body: Gradient descent treats your loss as a surface over weight-space. The gradient is a vector pointing uphill; you step the opposite way. Every optimizer is linear algebra deciding which direction in high-dimensional space to move.

slides.title: Where it shows up
bullets: Neural layer → matrix multiply + bias
Attention → query·key dot products
PCA / SVD → eigen-decomposition
Recommenders → matrix factorization

slides.title: What you gain
bullets: Debug shape errors instantly
Reason about model speed
Understand embeddings, not just use them
Read research papers without panic
See optimization as movement in space

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 9.
slides.body: Next: HOW the core operations work — dot products, matmul, transpose, broadcasting.

8-3.caption:
Four operations carry almost all of ML. Learn how they actually move the numbers. ⚙️

The dot product, matrix multiplication, the transpose, and broadcasting — that's the working set. A forward pass through a neural network is mostly these, repeated. Once you can do them by hand on tiny examples, the big ones stop being scary.

The trick is to stop treating them as abstract formulas and start treating them as shape transformations: numbers go in with one shape and come out with another, by rules you can predict.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• the dot product as a weighted sum
• matmul as the inner-dimension rule
• why (2,3)·(3,4) gives (2,4)
• what the transpose flips and why
• how broadcasting stretches shapes

Do one example by hand and you'll never fear a shape error again. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Dot product = weighted sum
slides.body: Multiply matching elements, then add them up. [1,2,3]·[4,5,6] = 1·4 + 2·5 + 3·6 = 32. That's it. A single neuron computing wᵀx + b is doing exactly this: a weighted sum of its inputs plus a bias.

slides.title: Matrix multiply
slides.body: To multiply A·B, take each row of A and dot it with each column of B. The result's [i,j] entry is row i of A dotted with column j of B. It's the dot product applied in a grid — and it's the heart of every dense layer.

slides.title: The inner-dimension rule
slides.body: (m, n) · (n, p) = (m, p). The two inner numbers must match (both n) or the multiply is illegal; the outer numbers become the result shape. (2,3)·(3,4) → (2,4). Memorize this and most shape bugs vanish.

slides.title: Transpose flips axes
slides.body: Transposing swaps rows and columns: a (2,3) becomes (3,2). You use it constantly to make shapes line up for a multiply, and the math notation Xᵀ shows up everywhere from normal equations to attention.

slides.title: Broadcasting
slides.body: When shapes don't match, NumPy stretches the smaller one if a dimension is 1 or absent. Adding a bias vector (3,) to a (2,3) matrix copies the vector across both rows. It's how you add a bias to a whole batch in one line.

slides.title: Broadcasting a bias
slides.code:
import numpy as np

X = np.array([[1, 2, 3],
              [4, 5, 6]])   # (2, 3)
b = np.array([10, 20, 30])  # (3,)

print(X + b)  # b is added to EVERY row
# [[11 22 33]
#  [14 25 36]]

slides.title: Rules to keep
bullets: Dot product: multiply, then sum
Matmul: inner dims must match
Result shape = the two outer dims
Transpose to fix alignment
Broadcasting fills size-1 dims

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 9.
slides.body: Next: a full CODE walkthrough — build a forward pass from raw NumPy.

8-4.caption:
Let's build a neural-network forward pass with nothing but NumPy. 💻

No PyTorch, no magic. Just matrices, a multiply, a bias add, and an activation. If you can read these snippets, you understand what every deep-learning framework does under the hood for one layer.

We'll go input → linear layer → activation → output, watching the shapes the whole way. Every framework you'll ever use does exactly this under the hood — it just adds autograd and GPU support on top.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• set up inputs and weights as arrays
• one dense layer as X·W + b
• add a ReLU activation
• stack two layers into a tiny net
• check every shape as you go

Copy it, run it, change the numbers, break it on purpose. That's how the math sticks. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Code a Forward Pass in Pure NumPy
What we're building
slides.body: A 2-layer feed-forward network by hand. Input → hidden layer (ReLU) → output. The only operations are matrix multiply, bias addition, and a max(). This is the literal core of a Dense/Linear layer in any framework.

slides.title: 1. Inputs and weights
slides.code:
import numpy as np
np.random.seed(0)

# 4 examples, 3 features each
X = np.random.randn(4, 3)        # (4, 3)

# layer 1: 3 inputs -> 5 hidden units
W1 = np.random.randn(3, 5)       # (3, 5)
b1 = np.zeros(5)                  # (5,)

slides.title: 2. The linear layer
slides.code:
# dense layer = X @ W + b
z1 = X @ W1 + b1     # (4,3)@(3,5) -> (4,5)
print(z1.shape)      # (4, 5)

# bias (5,) broadcasts across all 4 rows

slides.title: 3. Add an activation
4. Stack a second layer
slides.code:
# layer 2: 5 hidden -> 1 output
W2 = np.random.randn(5, 1)   # (5, 1)
b2 = np.zeros(1)             # (1,)

out = a1 @ W2 + b2   # (4,5)@(5,1) -> (4,1)
print(out.shape)     # (4, 1)
print(out.ravel())   # one prediction per example

slides.title: Why this is the whole trick
slides.body: A real network just stacks more of these layers, swaps activations, and learns the W's via gradient descent. The forward math you just wrote is identical to what PyTorch runs — frameworks add autograd and GPU support, not new arithmetic.

slides.title: Run-it checklist
bullets: Print .shape after every line
Inner dims must match to multiply
Bias length = number of output units
Activation never changes the shape
Output rows = number of examples

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 9.
slides.body: Next: the COMMON MISTAKES that wreck beginners' linear algebra.

8-5.caption:
Most linear-algebra bugs aren't hard math. They're sloppy shapes and bad habits. 🚫

The errors below cost beginners hours: a transposed matrix, a row vector where a column was needed, a (5,) that silently broadcast into nonsense. None require deep theory to fix — just awareness.

The nasty ones don't even throw an error. They run cleanly and quietly learn garbage, so your accuracy tanks while the code looks fine. Knowing the failure modes is how you catch them fast.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• confusing shape (n,) with (n,1)
• forgetting the inner-dimension rule
• mixing up rows (examples) and columns (features)
• silent broadcasting that hides bugs
• looping when you should vectorize
• ignoring numerical scale / overflow

Fix these six and your matrix code gets dramatically more reliable. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 1. (n,) is not (n,1)
slides.body: A 1-D array of shape (5,) is neither a row nor a column vector — and it broadcasts in surprising ways. When you need a true column, reshape to (5,1). Many "why is my result the wrong shape?" bugs trace back to this.

slides.title: The (n,) trap
slides.code:
import numpy as np
v = np.array([1, 2, 3])      # (3,)

print((v * v).shape)         # (3,) elementwise!
print(v.reshape(3, 1).shape) # (3, 1) real column

# v @ v -> 14 (a scalar), NOT a matrix

slides.title: 2. Inner dims don't match
slides.body: Trying (2,3) @ (2,3) throws a ValueError because the inner dims (3 and 2) disagree. You usually meant to transpose one operand. Always check the rule (m,n)·(n,p) before you multiply.

slides.title: 3. Rows vs columns swapped
slides.body: Feed an (features, examples) matrix where the model expects (examples, features) and everything still runs — it just learns garbage. The math won't error; your accuracy will. Always confirm rows = examples.

slides.title: 4. Silent broadcasting
slides.body: Adding a (3,) to a (4,3) works and is usually what you want. But adding a (4,) to a (4,3) fails, and a (1,) to anything succeeds quietly — sometimes masking a real bug. Broadcasting is powerful and easy to misuse.

slides.title: Broadcasting gotcha
slides.code:
import numpy as np
X = np.zeros((4, 3))

X + np.zeros(3)    # OK: (3,) -> each row
# X + np.zeros(4)  # ERROR: (4,) can't align
X + np.zeros((4, 1))  # OK: column broadcasts

# Print shapes before adding when unsure.

slides.title: 5. Looping over vectors
slides.body: Writing a Python for-loop to sum or scale arrays is slow and bug-prone. Reach for vectorized ops: a @ b, X.sum(axis=0), X * 2. They're faster, shorter, and exactly what NumPy/GPUs are built for.

slides.title: 6. Ignoring scale
slides.body: Features on wildly different scales (age 0–100 vs income 0–1e6) distort distances, gradients, and PCA. Standardize before training. Unnormalized data also invites overflow in exp/softmax — subtract the max for stability.

slides.title: The habits that fix all six
bullets: Print .shape obsessively
Reshape (n,) → (n,1) for real columns
Confirm rows = examples
Prefer vectorized ops over loops
Standardize features before training

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 9.
slides.body: Next day: we build on this math to go deeper into ML foundations.

9-1.caption:
Every embedding, every search result, every recommendation comes down to one question: how aligned are two vectors? 🧭

A vector is just an ordered list of numbers — but treat it as an arrow in space and three ideas unlock at once: direction, length, and the angle between two of them. The dot product measures alignment. Cosine similarity turns that into a clean -1 to 1 score.

This post builds the mental model from scratch, no formulas you can't picture.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• what a vector really is (list AND arrow)
• magnitude vs direction
• the dot product as an alignment score
• cosine similarity as the normalized angle
• why direction often beats raw distance

Get these three objects and half of modern AI search stops being a mystery. Save it. 👇

slides.title: A vector is two things at once
slides.body: A vector is an ordered list of numbers — [3, 1] — and an arrow from the origin to that point. The list view lets you compute; the arrow view lets you reason about direction, length, and angle. Hold both pictures at the same time.

slides.title: Magnitude vs direction
bullets: Magnitude: the arrow's length (its size)
Direction: which way it points
‖v‖ = √(v·v) = sqrt of squares summed
Two vectors can share direction, differ in length

slides.title: The dot product
slides.body: Multiply matching components, then add: [3,1]·[2,3] = 3·2 + 1·3 = 9. One number that grows when two vectors point the same way, hits zero when they're perpendicular, and goes negative when they oppose. It's an alignment score.

slides.title: From dot product to angle
slides.body: The dot product also equals ‖a‖‖b‖cos(θ), where θ is the angle between the arrows. Rearrange and you isolate the angle. That link — algebra on one side, geometry on the other — is the whole foundation of similarity.

slides.title: Cosine similarity
slides.body: Divide the dot product by both lengths and you strip out magnitude, leaving just cos(θ): a score from -1 (opposite) through 0 (unrelated) to 1 (identical direction). It answers 'do these point the same way?' regardless of size.

slides.title: All three in NumPy
slides.code:
import numpy as np

a = np.array([3.0, 1.0])
b = np.array([2.0, 3.0])

dot = a @ b                       # 9.0
cos = dot / (np.linalg.norm(a) * np.linalg.norm(b))
print(dot, round(cos, 3))         # 9.0 0.707

slides.title: Why direction beats distance
slides.body: A long document and a short one about the same topic sit far apart by raw distance but point nearly the same way. Cosine ignores length and compares meaning. That's why text and embedding search lean on cosine, not Euclidean distance.

slides.title: The three objects at a glance
bullets: Vector → a point / arrow in space
Magnitude → how long the arrow is
Dot product → raw alignment (size-sensitive)
Cosine → alignment as pure angle (size-free)

slides.title: Lock these in
bullets: Vector = list AND arrow
Dot product = multiply then sum
Dot = ‖a‖‖b‖cos(θ)
Cosine = dot ÷ (lengths)
Cosine in [-1, 1]; direction only

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 10.
slides.body: Next up: WHY these tiny operations quietly power search, RAG, and attention.

9-2.caption:
The dot product is a five-character operation that runs a huge slice of modern AI. ⚡

Semantic search, recommendations, the attention inside every Transformer, clustering, classification — peel back the framework and you find vectors being compared by alignment. Cosine similarity is how machines answer 'are these two things alike?' without anyone hand-coding what 'alike' means.

This post is the motivation: where these operations actually show up and why they're worth mastering deeply.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• how embeddings turn meaning into geometry
• why semantic search is just cosine ranking
• how RAG retrieves the right context
• why attention scores are dot products
• where recommenders and clustering use it

Learn one operation, understand a dozen systems. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Why Dot Products Run Modern AI
Meaning becomes geometry
slides.body: An embedding model maps text, images, or users to vectors so that similar things land close together. Once meaning is position in space, 'how similar?' becomes 'what's the angle between them?' — a cosine away. No rules, just geometry.

slides.title: Semantic search = cosine ranking
slides.body: Embed the query, embed every document, score each with cosine, sort descending. The top results are the closest in meaning, not just keyword matches. 'How do I reset my password?' finds 'account recovery steps' even with zero shared words.

slides.title: RAG retrieves by similarity
slides.body: Retrieval-augmented generation embeds your question, finds the top-k most similar chunks in a vector database by cosine, and feeds them to the LLM as context. The quality of every RAG answer rides on that similarity search picking the right passages.

slides.title: Search in a few lines
slides.code:
import numpy as np

def cosine(a, B):
    a = a / np.linalg.norm(a)
    B = B / np.linalg.norm(B, axis=1, keepdims=True)
    return B @ a              # one score per row of B

scores = cosine(query_vec, doc_matrix)
top3 = scores.argsort()[::-1][:3]   # best matches

slides.title: Attention is dot products
slides.body: Inside a Transformer, each token forms a query vector and compares it to every key vector by dot product. High alignment means 'pay attention here.' The famous QKᵀ in the attention formula is just a grid of dot products turned into weights.

slides.title: Recommenders and clustering
slides.body: Recommenders match a user vector to item vectors by similarity to suggest what fits. Clustering (k-means) and nearest-neighbor classifiers group points by distance or angle. The same compare-two-vectors operation underlies all of them.

slides.title: What you gain
bullets: See search, RAG, attention as one idea
Debug bad retrieval (it's the cosine)
Pick the right metric for the job
Read attention papers without fear
Reason about embeddings, not just call them

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 10.
slides.body: Next: HOW it works — compute dot product, magnitude, and cosine step by step.

9-3.caption:
Let's slow it all the way down and compute these by hand. 📐

The dot product, the magnitude, and cosine similarity are each a few arithmetic steps. Do them once on tiny vectors and the formulas stop being symbols — they become moves you can picture. Then we connect every step back to the angle between two arrows.

This is the mechanics post: every operation, traced line by line.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• dot product as multiply-then-sum
• magnitude as the Pythagorean length
• why dot = ‖a‖‖b‖cos(θ)
• cosine similarity step by step
• reading the score: -1, 0, and 1
• the geometry behind perpendicular = 0

Compute one example by hand and you'll trust the formula forever. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Step 1: dot product
slides.body: Pair up matching components, multiply each pair, sum the results. For a=[1,2,2], b=[2,0,1]: 1·2 + 2·0 + 2·1 = 4. One scalar that captures how much the two vectors reinforce each other component by component.

slides.title: Step 2: magnitude
slides.body: A vector's length is the square root of the sum of its squared components — Pythagoras in n dimensions. ‖a‖ = √(1²+2²+2²) = √9 = 3, and ‖b‖ = √(2²+0²+1²) = √5 ≈ 2.236. These are the scales we'll divide out.

slides.title: Step 3: the bridge
slides.body: The key identity: a·b = ‖a‖‖b‖cos(θ). The dot product equals the two lengths multiplied by the cosine of the angle between them. So if you know the dot product and both lengths, you can solve for the angle directly.

slides.title: Step 4: cosine similarity
slides.body: Divide the dot product by the product of the lengths: cos(θ) = 4 / (3 · 2.236) ≈ 0.596. That's the similarity. Dividing by the lengths cancels magnitude entirely, leaving a pure measure of direction agreement.

slides.title: Reading the score
slides.body: Cosine = 1 means same direction (most similar). 0 means perpendicular (unrelated). -1 means opposite. Most real embedding similarities sit between 0 and 1, since the vectors share a general region of space rather than pointing backward.

slides.title: Verify it in code
slides.code:
import numpy as np

a = np.array([1, 2, 2])
b = np.array([2, 0, 1])

dot = a @ b                         # 4
na, nb = np.linalg.norm(a), np.linalg.norm(b)
print(na, round(nb, 3))             # 3.0 2.236
print(round(dot / (na * nb), 3))    # 0.596

slides.title: Why perpendicular = 0
slides.body: When two vectors meet at 90°, cos(90°) = 0, so their dot product is exactly zero — the positive and negative component products cancel. 'Orthogonal' literally means 'no shared direction,' which is why a zero dot product signals independence.

slides.title: The recipe
bullets: Dot: multiply pairs, then sum
Norm: √(sum of squares)
cos θ = dot ÷ (‖a‖·‖b‖)
Range is always [-1, 1]
0 = perpendicular = unrelated

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 10.
slides.body: Next: a full CODE walkthrough — build a tiny semantic search from scratch.

9-4.caption:
Let's build a tiny semantic search engine with nothing but NumPy. 💻

No vector database, no framework magic. Just vectors, a dot product, a normalization, and an argsort. If you can read these snippets, you understand exactly what Pinecone, FAISS, and every RAG pipeline do at their core: embed, normalize, score by cosine, rank.

We'll go from raw vectors to a ranked list of matches, watching every shape.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• set up document and query vectors
• cosine for a single pair
• vectorize cosine over a whole matrix
• normalize once, then it's just a matmul
• rank and return the top-k results

Copy it, run it, swap in real embeddings. That's how it sticks. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Code a Semantic Search in Pure NumPy
What we're building
slides.body: A minimal semantic search: a query vector, a matrix of document vectors, cosine scores for each, and a ranked top-k. The only operations are matrix multiply, vector norms, and argsort — the literal core of any vector search engine.

slides.title: 1. Vectors and a query
slides.code:
import numpy as np
np.random.seed(0)

# 5 documents, each a 4-dim embedding
docs = np.random.randn(5, 4)     # (5, 4)

# the search query, same dimensionality
query = np.random.randn(4)       # (4,)
print(docs.shape, query.shape)   # (5, 4) (4,)

slides.title: 2. Cosine for one pair
3. Vectorize over all docs
slides.code:
# normalize query and every doc to unit length
q = query / np.linalg.norm(query)
D = docs / np.linalg.norm(docs, axis=1, keepdims=True)

# cosine of unit vectors is just their dot product
scores = D @ q                   # (5,4)@(4,) -> (5,)
print(scores.round(3))           # one score per doc

slides.title: 4. Rank the top-k
slides.code:
k = 3
top = np.argsort(scores)[::-1][:k]   # indices, best first

for rank, i in enumerate(top, 1):
    print(f"{rank}. doc {i}  score={scores[i]:.3f}")
# this ordering IS the search result

slides.title: Why normalize first
slides.body: Once every vector is scaled to length 1, ‖a‖ and ‖b‖ both equal 1, so cosine collapses to a plain dot product. Normalizing the whole matrix once turns the entire search into a single fast matmul — exactly the trick real vector databases use.

slides.title: Swapping in real embeddings
slides.body: Replace the random arrays with outputs from a model like sentence-transformers or an embedding API, and this exact code becomes a working semantic search. FAISS and Pinecone add indexing to scale to millions, but the scoring math is what you just wrote.

slides.title: Run-it checklist
bullets: Query and docs must share dimensionality
Normalize with axis=1, keepdims=True
Unit vectors → cosine = dot product
argsort is ascending; reverse for top
Print .shape after each step

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 10.
slides.body: Next: the COMMON MISTAKES that quietly wreck similarity scores.

9-5.caption:
Most cosine-similarity bugs don't crash. They return a confident, wrong number. 🚫

Forgetting to normalize, mixing up similarity and distance, ignoring zero vectors, comparing embeddings from two different models — none of these throw an error. Your search just quietly returns worse results, and you blame the model instead of the math.

These are the six traps that cost people the most time with vectors and similarity — and the worst part is they hide behind plausible-looking numbers, so you ship them without noticing.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• using raw dot product when you meant cosine
• confusing distance with similarity
• the zero-vector divide-by-zero
• mismatched dimensions or models
• forgetting to normalize before indexing
• reading magnitude as importance

Fix these six and your similarity scores start meaning what you think. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 1. Dot product when you meant cosine
slides.body: A raw dot product rewards long vectors. A frequent, verbose document can outscore a perfect match simply because its vector is bigger. If you care about direction (meaning), not size, normalize — or use cosine — every time.

slides.title: Magnitude skews the dot
slides.code:
import numpy as np
q = np.array([1.0, 1.0])
short = np.array([1.0, 1.0])      # perfect match
long  = np.array([5.0, 0.0])      # off-topic, big

print(q @ short, q @ long)        # 2.0  5.0  (!)
# raw dot ranks the off-topic vector higher

slides.title: 2. Distance vs similarity
slides.body: Cosine similarity is HIGH for good matches; distance is LOW. Sort the wrong direction and your best results land at the bottom. Cosine distance = 1 − cosine similarity. Always know which one your library returns before you rank.

slides.title: 3. The zero vector
slides.body: A zero vector has length 0, so cosine divides by zero and returns NaN or errors. Empty text, an all-zero embedding, or a dropped feature can produce one. Guard against it before normalizing, or those rows silently poison your ranking.

slides.title: Guard the divide
slides.code:
import numpy as np

def safe_cosine(a, b, eps=1e-8):
    na = np.linalg.norm(a)
    nb = np.linalg.norm(b)
    return a @ b / max(na * nb, eps)   # never /0

print(safe_cosine(np.zeros(3), np.ones(3)))  # 0.0

slides.title: 4. Mismatched dims or models
slides.body: Cosine needs equal-length vectors, so a (384,) query against (768,) docs throws. Subtler: embeddings from two different models live in different spaces, so comparing them is meaningless even when the dimensions happen to match. Embed everything with one model.

slides.title: 5. Forgetting to normalize
slides.body: If you index raw vectors and assume dot product equals cosine, every score is silently length-weighted. Normalize to unit length once — at insert time — so later searches are pure cosine. Many vector DBs expect pre-normalized vectors for their cosine mode.

slides.title: 6. Magnitude ≠ importance
slides.body: A longer embedding vector is not a 'stronger' or more relevant one. Magnitude often reflects token count or artifacts, not meaning. Cosine deliberately discards it. Don't read length as confidence — that's exactly what normalizing throws away on purpose.

slides.title: The habits that fix all six
bullets: Normalize vectors before comparing
Confirm: similarity high, distance low
Guard against zero vectors (eps)
One model, one vector space, equal dims
Treat direction as meaning, not length

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 10.
slides.body: Next day: we build on vectors to go deeper into the math behind ML.

10-1.caption:
Machine learning doesn't predict the future. It predicts how likely each future is. 🎲

Underneath almost every model is a single idea: the world is uncertain, and probability is the language for reasoning about that uncertainty honestly. A spam filter doesn't "know" an email is spam — it assigns a 0.97 chance and acts on it.

This post builds the foundation. Before you touch Bayes, distributions, or likelihood, you need a clean mental model of what probability actually represents and why ML leans on it so hard.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• what probability really measures
• random variables and outcomes
• the difference between probability and statistics
• sample space, events, and the axioms
• why ML predictions are probabilities in disguise
• the one-sentence version to remember

Get the intuition first — the formulas land easily after. Save it. 👇

slides.title: What probability measures
slides.body: Probability is a number between 0 and 1 that quantifies how likely an event is. 0 means impossible, 1 means certain. Everything interesting in ML lives in between — degrees of belief about outcomes you can't predict with certainty.

slides.title: Random variables
slides.body: A random variable maps outcomes to numbers. Flip a coin: heads→1, tails→0. Roll a die: outcome→1..6. Models output random variables all the time — a predicted class, a predicted price, a token. The randomness captures what we don't know.

slides.title: Sample space and events
slides.body: The sample space is every possible outcome. An event is a subset of those outcomes you care about. Rolling a die: sample space is {1,2,3,4,5,6}; "even" is the event {2,4,6}. Probability is mass spread over the sample space.

left.title: Probability
items: Given a fair coin, predict flips

right.title: Statistics
items: Given flips, is the coin fair?

slides.title: ML outputs are probabilities
slides.body: A classifier's softmax output isn't a label — it's a probability distribution over labels. "Cat: 0.91, dog: 0.07, fox: 0.02". The argmax is just the most likely class. Keeping the full distribution lets you measure confidence and calibrate.

slides.title: Probabilities sum to 1
slides.code:
import numpy as np

# raw model scores (logits)
logits = np.array([2.0, 1.0, 0.1])

# softmax turns them into a probability distribution
probs = np.exp(logits) / np.exp(logits).sum()
print(probs)          # [0.659 0.242 0.099]
print(probs.sum())    # 1.0

slides.title: Frequentist vs Bayesian
bullets: Frequentist: probability = long-run frequency
Bayesian: probability = degree of belief
Both are valid lenses, used in different methods
ML borrows from both freely

slides.title: The big misread
slides.body: A 70% prediction is not "probably right, basically certain." It means that across many such predictions, about 30% should be wrong. A model that's never wrong on its 70% calls is badly calibrated — overconfident or under.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 11.
slides.body: Next: why probability is the backbone of every loss function you'll ever train against.

10-2.caption:
If you can't measure uncertainty, you can't tell a confident model from a lucky one. 📊

Probability isn't academic decoration in ML — it's load-bearing. The loss functions you minimize, the way you compare models, the decisions a system makes under risk: all of it is probability wearing different clothes.

This post is about why. Why every serious ML practitioner ends up reasoning in distributions, not point estimates, and what breaks when they don't.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• why point predictions hide critical information
• how loss functions are built on probability
• calibration: when 0.8 should mean 0.8
• decision-making under uncertainty
• why probabilities beat raw scores in production
• the cost of ignoring uncertainty

This is the "so what" behind the math. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Point estimates lie by omission
slides.body: "The price will be $200" tells you nothing about risk. "$200 ± $5" vs "$200 ± $150" are wildly different bets. A probability distribution carries the spread, so you know how much to trust the number.

slides.title: Loss functions are probabilistic
slides.body: Cross-entropy, the workhorse classification loss, is literally the negative log-likelihood of the true labels under the model's predicted distribution. Minimizing loss means maximizing the probability the model assigns to the correct answers.

slides.title: Cross-entropy in one line
slides.code:
import numpy as np

# model probabilities for 3 examples, true class index each
probs = np.array([[0.7,0.2,0.1],[0.1,0.8,0.1],[0.2,0.2,0.6]])
true  = np.array([0, 1, 2])

# penalize the prob assigned to the correct class
loss = -np.log(probs[np.arange(3), true]).mean()
print(round(loss, 3))   # 0.339

slides.title: Calibration
slides.body: A model is calibrated when its stated probabilities match reality: of all predictions it labels 0.8, about 80% are correct. Accuracy alone can't catch this. A model can be accurate yet wildly overconfident, which is dangerous in medicine, finance, and safety.

slides.title: Decisions need probabilities
slides.body: The optimal action depends on probability times cost. A 5% chance of a $1M failure outweighs a 90% chance of a $10 inconvenience. Without calibrated probabilities, expected-value decisions are impossible — you're just guessing thresholds.

slides.title: Where uncertainty saves you
bullets: Flag low-confidence cases for human review
Abstain instead of guessing wrong
Combine models by their confidence
Detect drift when confidence drops

slides.title: Treating softmax as truth
slides.body: Raw softmax outputs from a deep net are notoriously overconfident — a 0.99 often isn't 99% likely. You need post-hoc calibration (temperature scaling, Platt scaling) before trusting them as real probabilities for decisions.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 11.
slides.body: Next up: the mechanics — conditional probability, Bayes, and distributions in action.

10-3.caption:
Most of ML reasoning collapses into one question: given what I just saw, what's now more likely? 🔍

That's conditional probability, and Bayes' rule is the machine that flips it around. Once these click, spam filters, medical tests, and Bayesian models stop being separate topics — they're the same gear turning.

This post walks the mechanics: joint, marginal, and conditional probability, independence, and the distributions you'll meet constantly.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• conditional probability, plainly
• joint vs marginal probability
• Bayes' rule and what each term means
• independence and why it simplifies everything
• the distributions ML actually uses
• a worked medical-test example

This is the engine room. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Conditional probability
slides.body: P(A|B) is the probability of A given that B happened. It rescales the world to only the cases where B is true. Formula: P(A|B) = P(A and B) / P(B). Almost all ML inference is computing some P(label | features).

slides.title: Joint vs marginal
slides.body: Joint P(A,B) is the chance both happen together. Marginal P(A) is the chance of A regardless of B — you get it by summing the joint over all values of B. Conditioning, joining, and marginalizing are the three moves you combine endlessly.

slides.title: Bayes' rule
slides.body: Bayes flips a conditional you can't easily measure into one you can. P(A|B) = P(B|A)·P(A) / P(B). It updates a prior belief P(A) into a posterior P(A|B) after seeing evidence B. This single line underpins huge swaths of ML.

slides.title: Bayes: a worked example
slides.code:
# Disease affects 1% of people. Test: 99% sensitive, 95% specific.
p_d   = 0.01            # prior P(disease)
p_pos_given_d  = 0.99   # sensitivity
p_pos_given_nd = 0.05   # 1 - specificity

# total prob of a positive test
p_pos = p_pos_given_d*p_d + p_pos_given_nd*(1-p_d)
# posterior P(disease | positive)
p_d_given_pos = p_pos_given_d*p_d / p_pos
print(round(p_d_given_pos, 3))   # 0.167

slides.title: The base-rate surprise
slides.body: Even a 99% accurate test gives only a ~17% chance of disease after one positive result, because the disease is rare. The prior dominates. Ignoring base rates is one of the most common reasoning errors — and models inherit it from skewed data.

slides.title: Independence
slides.body: A and B are independent if knowing one tells you nothing about the other: P(A|B) = P(A). Independence lets you multiply probabilities instead of tracking a full joint table — the assumption that makes Naive Bayes both naive and fast.

slides.title: Confusing the conditionals
slides.body: P(positive | disease) is NOT P(disease | positive). Swapping the two is the prosecutor's fallacy. Bayes exists precisely to convert between them — and the conversion needs the prior, which people skip.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 11.
slides.body: Next: code it yourself — sampling, distributions, and Bayes in NumPy and SciPy.

10-4.caption:
Probability stops being abstract the moment you sample from it and watch the numbers behave. 💻

This post is hands-on. We'll generate random variables, build distributions, estimate probabilities by simulation, and code Bayes' rule end to end. Run every snippet — intuition you compute sticks far better than intuition you read.

We use NumPy and SciPy, the tools you'll actually reach for in real ML work.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• sampling from distributions with NumPy
• estimating probabilities by Monte Carlo
• plotting a PMF and a Gaussian PDF
• computing likelihood with SciPy
• a clean Naive Bayes-style update
• verifying the law of large numbers

Copy, run, tweak. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 1. Sample from distributions
slides.code:
import numpy as np
rng = np.random.default_rng(42)

coins = rng.binomial(n=1, p=0.5, size=10)   # Bernoulli flips
dice  = rng.integers(1, 7, size=10)         # uniform 1..6
bell  = rng.normal(loc=0, scale=1, size=5)  # Gaussian
print(coins)
print(dice)
print(bell.round(2))

slides.title: 2. Estimate P by simulation
slides.code:
import numpy as np
rng = np.random.default_rng(0)

# P(sum of two dice == 7) by Monte Carlo
rolls = rng.integers(1, 7, size=(1_000_000, 2))
sevens = (rolls.sum(axis=1) == 7).mean()
print(round(sevens, 4))   # ~0.1667  (true = 6/36)

slides.title: 3. PMF and PDF
slides.code:
import numpy as np
from scipy import stats

# Binomial PMF: P(k heads in 10 flips, p=0.5)
k = np.arange(0, 11)
pmf = stats.binom.pmf(k, n=10, p=0.5)
print(pmf.round(3))

# Gaussian PDF density at x=0
print(round(stats.norm.pdf(0, loc=0, scale=1), 3))  # 0.399

slides.title: 4. Likelihood with SciPy
slides.code:
import numpy as np
from scipy import stats

data = np.array([1.9, 2.1, 2.0, 2.3, 1.8])

# how likely is this data under N(mu, 1) for two guesses of mu?
ll_2  = stats.norm.logpdf(data, loc=2.0, scale=1).sum()
ll_5  = stats.norm.logpdf(data, loc=5.0, scale=1).sum()
print(round(ll_2, 2), round(ll_5, 2))  # mu=2 fits far better

slides.title: 5. Bayes update in code
slides.code:
def bayes(prior, likelihood, p_evidence):
    return likelihood * prior / p_evidence

p_d = 0.01
sens, fpr = 0.99, 0.05
p_pos = sens*p_d + fpr*(1-p_d)
posterior = bayes(p_d, sens, p_pos)
print(round(posterior, 3))   # 0.167

slides.title: Why simulate at all?
slides.body: Many real distributions have no clean formula. Monte Carlo sampling estimates any probability you can phrase as "fraction of trials where X happens." It's the brute-force tool behind Bayesian inference, reinforcement learning rollouts, and risk modeling.

slides.title: 6. Law of large numbers
slides.code:
import numpy as np
rng = np.random.default_rng(1)

for n in [10, 1_000, 100_000]:
    est = rng.binomial(1, 0.3, size=n).mean()
    print(n, round(est, 3))
# estimates close in on 0.3 as n grows

slides.title: Practical gotchas
bullets: Always seed your RNG for reproducibility
Work in log-space to avoid underflow
Sum logpdf, don't multiply pdf
More samples = tighter estimate, slower

slides.title: Multiplying tiny probabilities
slides.body: Multiplying many small probabilities underflows to 0.0 in floating point. The fix is universal in ML: sum the log-probabilities instead of multiplying the raw ones, then exponentiate only at the end if needed.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 11.
slides.body: Next: the probability mistakes that quietly wreck real ML systems.

10-5.caption:
Most probability bugs in ML aren't math errors — they're reasoning errors that the math faithfully executes. 🚫

These mistakes are subtle because the code still runs and the model still trains. The damage shows up as overconfident predictions, broken calibration, and decisions that look fine until they fail expensively.

This post is a field guide to the traps. Learn to spot them before they ship.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• confusing P(A|B) with P(B|A)
• ignoring base rates
• assuming independence that isn't there
• trusting raw softmax as probability
• numerical underflow from multiplying probs
• mixing up correlation and causation

Know the failure modes before you trust the output. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Flipping the conditional
slides.body: P(test positive | sick) ≠ P(sick | test positive). They can differ by an order of magnitude. This is the single most common probability error in ML and medicine. Always ask which direction you actually have, and use Bayes to convert.

slides.title: Ignoring base rates
slides.body: When a class is rare, even a strong signal yields a low posterior. Fraud is 0.1% of transactions, so a flag with 90% precision on the signal still produces mostly false alarms. Skip the prior and your thresholds will be wildly off.

slides.title: Assuming independence
slides.body: Naive Bayes multiplies feature probabilities as if independent — usually false. "Free" and "money" co-occur in spam. The model still often works, but the probabilities it reports are distorted, so never read them as well-calibrated.

slides.title: Underflow, and the fix
slides.code:
import numpy as np
probs = np.full(1000, 0.01)

print(np.prod(probs))            # 0.0  -> underflow!
print(np.exp(np.log(probs).sum()))  # same value, but...
print(np.log(probs).sum())       # -4605.17  safe in log-space

slides.title: Raw softmax as truth
slides.body: Deep nets output overconfident softmax scores — a 0.999 may be wrong 5% of the time. Before using these as probabilities for decisions or thresholds, calibrate them (temperature scaling on a held-out set is the standard fix).

slides.title: Fearing density > 1
slides.body: A probability mass (PMF) value must be ≤ 1, but a probability density (PDF) value can exceed 1 — it's density, not probability. Only the integral over a range is a probability. Seeing pdf = 1.6 is not a bug.

slides.title: Correlation as causation
slides.body: A feature that predicts the target probabilistically may not cause it. Models exploit correlation happily, but if you act on the prediction as if it were causal, interventions can backfire. Probability of co-occurrence ≠ mechanism.

slides.title: Quick defense checklist
bullets: Which conditional do I actually have?
Did I include the prior / base rate?
Are my 'independent' features really?
Am I working in log-space?
Are my probabilities calibrated?

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 11.
slides.body: You've got probability down. Next we build on it toward statistics and inference.

11-1.caption:
Before a model can learn from data, you have to know what the data is telling you. 📈

Statistics is the discipline of turning messy observations into honest summaries and defensible conclusions. Every ML pipeline is soaked in it: the mean you center features on, the variance you normalize by, the distribution you assume.

This post lays the foundation. We separate the two halves of statistics, define the words you'll see everywhere, and build a clean mental model of what a "statistic" even is.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• what statistics actually does
• population vs sample, and why it matters
• descriptive vs inferential statistics
• parameters vs statistics (the easy mix-up)
• the types of data you'll measure
• the one-sentence version to keep

Get the vocabulary clean and the rest stops being scary. Save it. 👇

slides.title: What statistics does
slides.body: Statistics is the science of collecting, summarizing, and drawing conclusions from data under uncertainty. It gives you tools to describe what you observed and to reason about what you didn't. In ML, it's the layer between raw numbers and any claim you make.

slides.title: Population vs sample
slides.body: The population is every entity you care about — all users, all emails, all houses. A sample is the subset you actually measured. You almost never see the full population, so you estimate its properties from a sample and accept some error for doing so.

slides.title: Parameters vs statistics
slides.body: A parameter is a fixed number describing the whole population (true mean μ). A statistic is a number computed from your sample (sample mean x̄) used to estimate it. Greek letters for parameters, Latin for statistics — that's the convention.

slides.title: Types of data
slides.body: Numbers come in flavors. Numerical data is continuous (height) or discrete (counts). Categorical data is nominal (colors, no order) or ordinal (small/medium/large, ordered). The type dictates which summary and which test is even valid.

slides.title: Describe a dataset
slides.code:
import numpy as np

sample = np.array([4, 8, 15, 16, 23, 42])

print("n      :", sample.size)        # 6
print("mean   :", sample.mean())      # 18.0
print("median :", np.median(sample))  # 15.5
print("std    :", sample.std(ddof=1)) # 13.45 (sample std)

slides.title: Words you'll see everywhere
bullets: Observation: one row / data point
Variable: one measured property (column)
Distribution: how values are spread out
Estimator: a recipe for guessing a parameter

slides.title: Sample ≠ population
slides.body: The biggest beginner error is treating sample numbers as truth. Your sample mean is an estimate with error baked in. A different sample gives a different number. Statistics is honest precisely because it quantifies that wobble instead of hiding it.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 12.
slides.body: Next: why these dry summaries are load-bearing for every model you'll ever train.

11-2.caption:
You can't fix a model you don't understand, and you can't understand a model whose data you never inspected. 🔬

Statistics isn't a hoop to jump through before the "real" ML. It's what tells you whether your data is trustworthy, whether your result is real or noise, and whether your model is actually better than the last one.

This post is the "so what." Why skipping statistics quietly wrecks projects, and where it earns its keep in a real ML workflow.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• why summary stats catch bugs before models do
• how variance drives feature scaling
• signal vs noise: is the lift real?
• why distributions decide your model choice
• how stats power A/B tests and evaluation
• the cost of ignoring it

This is why every senior ML engineer reaches for stats first. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Stats catch bugs early
slides.body: Before any model, you describe the data: ranges, missing values, means per group. A salary column with a max of 9,999,999 or an age of -3 screams a data bug. Five minutes of summary statistics saves days of debugging a model trained on garbage.

slides.title: Variance drives scaling
slides.body: Features on wildly different scales let the big-variance ones dominate distance and gradient steps. Standardizing — subtract the mean, divide by the standard deviation — is pure statistics, and it's why models like SVMs and k-NN suddenly start working.

slides.title: Standardize features
slides.code:
import numpy as np

X = np.array([[1.0, 2000.0],
              [2.0, 2500.0],
              [3.0, 3000.0]])

# z-score each column: (x - mean) / std
Xz = (X - X.mean(axis=0)) / X.std(axis=0)
print(Xz.round(2))
# every column now has mean 0, std 1

slides.title: Signal vs noise
slides.body: Your new model scored 84% vs the old 83%. Real improvement or luck of the split? Statistics answers this with confidence intervals and significance tests. Without it, teams ship 'improvements' that are pure sampling noise and quietly regress in production.

slides.title: Distributions pick your model
slides.body: Is the target roughly normal, heavily skewed, or count data? That shapes everything — whether you log-transform, use linear vs Poisson regression, or trust a mean at all. Looking at the distribution first is faster than guessing and retraining.

slides.title: Stats earning its keep
bullets: A/B tests: is the new feature better?
Eval: error bars on every metric
Monitoring: detect distribution drift
Sampling: build fair train/test splits

slides.title: Chasing point numbers
slides.body: Reporting '84.2% accuracy' with no spread is the classic trap. With a small test set, that number can swing ±4% on a different split. A metric without a measure of its uncertainty is a confident-sounding guess, not evidence.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 12.
slides.body: Next: how it actually works — center, spread, shape, and the central limit theorem.

11-3.caption:
Almost every statistical summary answers one of three questions: where is the middle, how spread out is it, and what's the shape? 🧭

Nail those three and you can read any dataset. Add one near-magical theorem — the central limit theorem — and you understand why so much of statistics leans on the bell curve even when the raw data isn't normal.

This post is the engine room: the actual mechanics of describing and inferring.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• measures of center: mean, median, mode
• measures of spread: variance and std
• why we divide by n−1, not n
• shape: skew and the normal distribution
• the central limit theorem, plainly
• confidence intervals in one breath

This is the part that makes the formulas click. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Measures of center
slides.body: Mean is the average — sensitive to outliers. Median is the middle value — robust to them. Mode is the most frequent value. For skewed data (incomes, house prices), the median tells a more honest story than the mean.

slides.title: Measures of spread
slides.body: Variance is the average squared distance from the mean; standard deviation is its square root, back in the original units. Small std means values cluster tightly; large std means they're scattered. Spread matters as much as center.

slides.title: Center and spread by hand
slides.code:
import numpy as np

x = np.array([2, 4, 4, 4, 5, 5, 7, 9])

mean = x.mean()                       # 5.0
var  = ((x - mean) ** 2).sum() / (x.size - 1)  # sample variance
std  = var ** 0.5
print(mean, round(var, 2), round(std, 2))
# 5.0 4.57 2.14

slides.title: Why n−1, not n
slides.body: When estimating population variance from a sample, dividing by n underestimates it, because the sample mean is itself fit to the data. Dividing by n−1 (Bessel's correction) corrects this bias. It's why np.std needs ddof=1 for a sample.

slides.title: The normal distribution
slides.body: The bell curve, defined by mean μ and std σ. About 68% of values fall within 1σ of the mean, 95% within 2σ, 99.7% within 3σ. This 68-95-99.7 rule is the fastest gut-check for whether a value is unusual.

slides.title: Central limit theorem
slides.body: Take many samples and average each. Those sample means form a normal distribution — regardless of the original data's shape — as sample size grows. This is why the bell curve is everywhere and why inference about means works even on messy data.

slides.title: CLT in action
slides.code:
import numpy as np

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
# skewed source data (exponential, not normal)
means = [rng.exponential(scale=2, size=30).mean()
         for _ in range(10000)]

print(round(np.mean(means), 2))  # ~2.0
print(round(np.std(means), 2))   # shrinks ~ 1/sqrt(n)
# histogram of `means` looks bell-shaped

slides.title: Confidence intervals
slides.body: A 95% CI is a range that, across repeated sampling, captures the true parameter 95% of the time. It's center ± margin, where the margin grows with spread and shrinks with sample size. It turns a point estimate into an honest range.

slides.title: Reporting mean for skewed data
slides.body: Quoting an average salary of $95k when one founder earns $5M and everyone else earns $50k is technically true and deeply misleading. For skewed distributions, lead with the median and show the spread, not a lone mean.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 12.
slides.body: Next: a hands-on code walkthrough computing all of this on a real dataset.

11-4.caption:
Theory sticks when you compute it yourself. Let's turn the formulas into a few lines of Python you can actually run. 💻

We'll take a small dataset and walk it end to end: describe it, visualize the distribution, test whether two groups truly differ, and build a confidence interval. No black boxes — every number is one function call away, and we build the data ourselves so you know the right answer in advance.

The goal is muscle memory. Read-only stats fades fast; stats you've run and tweaked sticks. By the end you'll have a reusable template for the first pass on almost any tabular dataset.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• load data and get instant summaries
• compute center, spread, and quantiles
• run a two-sample t-test
• build a 95% confidence interval
• check a correlation between variables
• read the output like a pro

Copy, run, tweak. That's how stats stops being abstract. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 0. Setup + sample data
slides.code:
# pip install numpy scipy pandas
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
from scipy import stats

rng = np.random.default_rng(42)
df = pd.DataFrame({
    "group": ["A"] * 50 + ["B"] * 50,
    "score": np.concatenate([rng.normal(70, 8, 50),
                             rng.normal(75, 8, 50)]),
})
print(df.head())

slides.title: 1. Instant summaries
slides.code:
# one call describes every numeric column
print(df["score"].describe())
# count, mean, std, min, 25%, 50%, 75%, max

# group-wise summary
print(df.groupby("group")["score"].agg(["mean", "std"]))

slides.title: 2. Center, spread, quantiles
slides.code:
s = df["score"]

print("mean    :", round(s.mean(), 2))
print("median  :", round(s.median(), 2))
print("std     :", round(s.std(ddof=1), 2))   # sample std
print("IQR     :", round(s.quantile(.75) - s.quantile(.25), 2))
print("95th pct:", round(s.quantile(.95), 2))

slides.title: What we're testing
slides.body: Group A and B have different sample means. But each sample is noisy. The question: is the gap big enough that it's unlikely to be chance? A two-sample t-test gives a p-value — the probability of seeing this gap if the groups were truly identical.

slides.title: 3. Two-sample t-test
slides.code:
a = df.loc[df.group == "A", "score"]
b = df.loc[df.group == "B", "score"]

t, p = stats.ttest_ind(a, b)
print(f"t = {t:.3f}, p = {p:.4f}")

if p < 0.05:
    print("groups differ significantly")
else:
    print("no significant difference")

slides.title: 4. 95% confidence interval
slides.code:
# CI for group B's mean
mean = b.mean()
se   = b.std(ddof=1) / np.sqrt(len(b))   # standard error
lo, hi = stats.t.interval(0.95, df=len(b) - 1,
                          loc=mean, scale=se)
print(f"mean {mean:.2f}, 95% CI [{lo:.2f}, {hi:.2f}]")

slides.title: 5. Correlation check
slides.code:
# add a noisy related variable
df["hours"] = df["score"] * 0.1 + rng.normal(0, 1, 100)

r, p = stats.pearsonr(df["score"], df["hours"])
print(f"Pearson r = {r:.3f}, p = {p:.4f}")
# r near 1 / -1 = strong; near 0 = weak

slides.title: Reading the output
bullets: p < 0.05: result unlikely under 'no difference'
p is NOT the probability you're right
Narrow CI = precise estimate
Always report effect size, not just p

slides.title: p-hacking the result
slides.body: Running ten tests and reporting only the one with p < 0.05 manufactures false discoveries — by chance, 1 in 20 tests 'passes' at the 5% level. Decide your test before you look, and correct for multiple comparisons when you run many.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 12.
slides.body: Next: the traps — the statistical mistakes that quietly sink real projects.

11-5.caption:
Most bad data science isn't bad math — it's good math applied to the wrong question, or read the wrong way. ⚠️

Statistics is full of traps that feel intuitive and are dead wrong. They survive because the formulas still run and the numbers still print. Knowing the failure modes is what separates a result you can defend from one that just looks rigorous.

This post is the field guide to the mistakes that bite hardest.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• correlation is not causation
• why p-values get misread constantly
• the outlier that wrecks your mean
• Simpson's paradox: groups vs aggregate
• sampling bias and survivorship
• small samples and overconfidence

Learn the traps once, dodge them forever. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Correlation ≠ causation
slides.body: Ice cream sales and drownings rise together — both driven by summer heat, neither causing the other. A correlation tells you two things move together, nothing about why. Concluding cause from correlation alone is the most expensive mistake in analytics.

slides.title: Misreading p-values
slides.body: p < 0.05 does NOT mean '95% chance the effect is real.' It means: if there were truly no effect, you'd see data this extreme only 5% of the time. It says nothing directly about your hypothesis being true, and nothing about effect size.

slides.title: One outlier wrecks the mean
slides.code:
import numpy as np

salaries = np.array([48, 52, 50, 55, 49, 51])
print("mean:", salaries.mean())     # 50.83

# a single founder joins
with_ceo = np.append(salaries, 5000)
print("mean:", with_ceo.mean())     # 763.9 (!!)
print("median:", np.median(with_ceo))  # 51.0 (stable)

slides.title: Simpson's paradox
slides.body: A trend that appears in every subgroup can reverse when the groups are combined. A treatment can look worse overall yet be better for both men and women separately, because group sizes differ. Always check whether a hidden variable is mixing things.

slides.title: Sampling bias
slides.body: If your sample isn't representative, no amount of math fixes it. A survey answered only by happy customers, or test data scraped differently from production — both quietly poison every conclusion. Garbage in, confident garbage out.

slides.title: Survivorship bias
slides.body: WWII engineers wanted to armor the bullet holes on returning planes. The statistician Abraham Wald said armor where there are none — those planes didn't come back. You only see the survivors; the missing data is often the whole story.

slides.title: Small samples lie
slides.code:
import numpy as np
rng = np.random.default_rng(1)

# true fair coin, but tiny sample
for n in [10, 100, 10000]:
    flips = rng.integers(0, 2, n)
    print(f"n={n:>5}  heads={flips.mean():.3f}")
# n=10 can read 0.700; big n converges to ~0.500

slides.title: Quick defenses
bullets: Ask 'what else could cause this?'
Report effect size and CI, not just p
Plot the data before trusting a summary
Check subgroups for hidden reversals
Ask who is missing from the sample

yes.leaf: Show a controlled experiment, not a correlation

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 12.
slides.body: Day 11 done. Next theme builds straight on these statistical foundations.

12-1.caption:
Training a model is just calculus running in a loop. 📈

A derivative answers one question: if I nudge this input a tiny bit, how much does the output move? That single idea — the slope at a point — is the engine behind every gradient, every weight update, every "the loss went down."

This post builds the mental model before any formulas. Once you see a derivative as a sensitivity, the rest of machine learning math stops feeling like a wall of symbols.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• what a derivative actually measures
• slope, tangent lines, and "instantaneous" change
• the limit definition, in plain words
• notation: f'(x), dy/dx, and what they share
• why ML cares about slopes at all
• the one-sentence version to keep

Get the intuition first — the rules land easily after. Save it. 👇
