slides.title: A derivative is a rate of change
slides.body: The derivative of a function at a point is how fast its output changes when you nudge the input. Speed is the derivative of position. In ML, the derivative of loss tells you how much error changes when you tweak a weight — the signal that drives learning.

slides.title: Slope of the tangent line
slides.body: Geometrically, the derivative at x is the slope of the line that just touches the curve there. Steep slope means a small input change causes a big output change. Flat slope means the output barely moves. That slope is a single number you can compute everywhere.

slides.title: From average to instantaneous
slides.body: Average rate of change is rise over run between two points: (f(x+h) − f(x)) / h. Shrink h toward zero and that ratio settles on the instantaneous rate — the derivative. Calculus is the trick of taking that shrink seriously, with limits.

slides.title: Slope by shrinking h
slides.code:
def f(x):
    return x**2

x = 3.0
for h in [1, 0.1, 0.01, 0.0001]:
    slope = (f(x + h) - f(x)) / h
    print(h, round(slope, 4))
# 1 -> 7.0, 0.1 -> 6.1, 0.01 -> 6.01, 0.0001 -> 6.0001
# true derivative of x^2 at 3 is 2*3 = 6

slides.title: Notation you'll see
slides.body: f'(x) is Lagrange notation. dy/dx is Leibniz notation, read as the rate y changes per change in x. d/dx[ ] means "take the derivative of." They all mean the same thing: the slope. In ML papers you'll mostly meet ∂ (partial) versions.

slides.title: Why slopes power learning
slides.body: A model improves by following slopes downhill on its loss surface. The derivative says which direction reduces error and by how much. No derivatives means no gradient, and no gradient means no gradient descent — the algorithm behind nearly all training.

slides.title: Quick intuitions
bullets: Positive derivative: function is rising
Negative derivative: function is falling
Zero derivative: flat — peak, valley, or plateau
Bigger magnitude: steeper, faster change

slides.title: The common misread
slides.body: A derivative is not the value of the function — it's the rate the value is changing. f(x) can be huge while f'(x) is zero (a flat plateau), or f(x) can be tiny while f'(x) is enormous (a steep cliff). Confusing height with slope wrecks your intuition.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 13.
slides.body: Next: why this single concept is the load-bearing math under every model you train.

12-2.caption:
Without derivatives, a neural network would have no way to know which way is down. 🧭

Every time you read "the model learned," what actually happened is calculus: the loss was differentiated with respect to millions of weights, and each weight moved a step against its slope. That's it. That's training.

This post is the "so what." Why derivatives aren't optional background math but the literal mechanism that turns data into a working model — and what breaks the moment they misbehave.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• why learning is slope-following, not guessing
• gradients as multi-dimensional derivatives
• how the learning rate scales the step
• vanishing and exploding gradients
• why activation choices are derivative choices
• the cost of getting slopes wrong

This is the engine behind "it works." Save it. 👇

slides.title: Learning is following slopes
slides.body: A model starts with random weights and a high loss. The derivative of loss with respect to each weight points uphill; stepping the opposite way lowers error. Repeat millions of times and the weights settle into a configuration that fits the data. No slope, no learning.

slides.title: The gradient
slides.body: When a function has many inputs, its derivative becomes a vector of partial derivatives — the gradient. Each entry says how loss responds to one weight, holding the rest fixed. The gradient points in the direction of steepest increase, so we step against it.

slides.title: One descent step
slides.code:
# minimize loss = (w - 4)**2 ; true minimum at w = 4
w = 0.0
lr = 0.1
for step in range(5):
    grad = 2 * (w - 4)      # derivative of (w-4)^2
    w = w - lr * grad       # step against the slope
    print(step, round(w, 3))
# 0.8, 1.44, 1.952, 2.362, 2.689  -> climbing toward 4

slides.title: The learning rate scales the step
slides.body: The gradient gives direction and steepness; the learning rate decides how far you move. Too small and training crawls. Too large and you overshoot the valley and diverge. It's the single most important knob, and it only makes sense once you see the derivative behind it.

slides.title: Vanishing and exploding gradients
slides.body: Deep networks multiply many derivatives together via the chain rule. If those slopes are mostly below 1, the product shrinks toward zero and early layers stop learning — vanishing. If above 1, it blows up — exploding. Both are pure calculus problems, not bugs.

slides.title: Why this matters in practice
bullets: ReLU beat sigmoid largely on derivative behavior
Gradient clipping tames exploding slopes
Normalization keeps gradients well-scaled
Debugging training often means inspecting gradients

slides.title: Ignoring the slope's scale
slides.body: People tune learning rate by trial and error without realizing it's fighting the gradient's magnitude. If gradients are huge, even a 'small' learning rate explodes; if tiny, a 'normal' rate does nothing. Watch gradient norms, not just the loss curve.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 13.
slides.body: Next: the mechanics — the rules that let you differentiate anything, including the chain rule.

12-3.caption:
You don't compute derivatives from the limit every time. You learn five rules and compose them. 🔧

The magic that makes backprop possible is that derivatives follow simple, mechanical rules — and the chain rule lets you stack them through arbitrarily deep functions. Master these and you can differentiate any network by hand on paper.

This post is the engine room: the power rule, product and quotient rules, the chain rule, and the partial derivatives that turn into gradients.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• the core differentiation rules
• the chain rule, the heart of backprop
• partial derivatives and the gradient vector
• a worked chain-rule example
• derivatives of common ML functions
• how the rules compose into backprop

This is the machinery. Save it. 👇

slides.title: The power rule
slides.body: The workhorse: d/dx[x^n] = n·x^(n-1). So x^2 differentiates to 2x, x^3 to 3x^2, and a constant to 0. Combined with the fact that derivatives are linear — you can differentiate term by term — this alone handles every polynomial.

slides.title: Product and quotient rules
slides.body: For a product: (f·g)' = f'·g + f·g'. For a quotient: (f/g)' = (f'·g − f·g') / g². You can't just multiply or divide the separate derivatives — these rules keep the cross-terms that make the answer correct.

slides.title: The chain rule
slides.body: For nested functions, differentiate outside-in and multiply: if y = f(g(x)), then dy/dx = f'(g(x)) · g'(x). This is THE rule for neural nets — a network is just deeply nested functions, and backprop is the chain rule applied layer by layer.

slides.title: Chain rule, worked
slides.code:
# y = (3x + 1)^2 . Let g = 3x+1, f = g^2.
# dy/dx = f'(g)*g'(x) = 2*(3x+1) * 3
def y(x):  return (3*x + 1)**2
def dy(x): return 2*(3*x + 1) * 3

x = 2.0
h = 1e-6
numeric = (y(x+h) - y(x)) / h
print(round(dy(x), 4), round(numeric, 4))  # 42.0  ~42.0

slides.title: Partial derivatives
slides.body: When a function has several inputs, a partial derivative ∂f/∂x measures change in one variable while freezing the others. Stack all the partials into a vector and you get the gradient ∇f — the object gradient descent actually steps along.

slides.title: Gradient of a 2-variable function
Forgetting the inner derivative
slides.body: The most common error is differentiating the outer function and stopping. d/dx[(3x+1)^2] is NOT 2(3x+1) — you must multiply by the inner derivative 3, giving 6(3x+1). Drop the inner factor and your gradients are silently wrong.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 13.
slides.body: Next: code it yourself — symbolic, numeric, and automatic differentiation in Python.

12-4.caption:
Three ways to get a derivative in code — and only one of them scales to a neural network. 💻

This post is hands-on. We compute derivatives three ways: numerically (finite differences), symbolically (SymPy), and automatically (PyTorch autograd, the method that actually powers deep learning). Run every snippet and watch them agree.

By the end you'll see why autograd won: it's exact like symbolic differentiation, but cheap enough to run on millions of parameters at once. The same backward() call that handles a toy function scales straight to a real network — that scaling property is the whole reason deep learning is practical.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• numeric derivatives with finite differences
• symbolic derivatives with SymPy
• automatic differentiation with PyTorch
• gradients of a multi-variable function
• one full gradient-descent loop
• checking autograd against numeric

Copy, run, tweak. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 1. Numeric (finite difference)
2. Symbolic (SymPy)
slides.code:
# pip install sympy
import sympy as sp

x = sp.symbols('x')
f = x**3 - 2*x
fprime = sp.diff(f, x)
print(fprime)              # 3*x**2 - 2
print(fprime.subs(x, 2))   # 10

slides.title: 3. Automatic (PyTorch autograd)
slides.code:
import torch

x = torch.tensor(2.0, requires_grad=True)
f = x**3 - 2*x
f.backward()          # backprop the derivative
print(x.grad.item())  # 10.0  exact, no formula needed

slides.title: 4. Gradient of many variables
slides.code:
import torch

w = torch.tensor([1.0, 2.0], requires_grad=True)
# f = w0^2 + 3*w0*w1
f = w[0]**2 + 3*w[0]*w[1]
f.backward()
print(w.grad)   # tensor([8., 3.])  matches 2w0+3w1, 3w0

slides.title: 5. A real descent loop
slides.code:
import torch

w = torch.tensor(0.0, requires_grad=True)
lr = 0.1
for step in range(50):
    loss = (w - 4)**2          # minimum at w=4
    loss.backward()            # compute dloss/dw
    with torch.no_grad():
        w -= lr * w.grad       # step downhill
        w.grad.zero_()         # reset for next step
print(round(w.item(), 3))      # ~4.0

slides.title: Practical notes
bullets: Use central difference, not forward, for numeric
Zero gradients each step or they accumulate
Wrap weight updates in torch.no_grad()
Symbolic is great for understanding, not for big nets

slides.title: 6. Gradient check
slides.code:
import torch

def f(x): return x**3 - 2*x
xt = torch.tensor(2.0, requires_grad=True)
f(xt).backward()
auto = xt.grad.item()
num  = (f(torch.tensor(2.0+1e-4)) - f(torch.tensor(2.0-1e-4))) / 2e-4
print(round(auto,4), round(num.item(),4))  # 10.0  10.0

slides.title: Forgetting to zero gradients
slides.body: PyTorch accumulates gradients by default — each .backward() adds to .grad instead of replacing it. Skip w.grad.zero_() (or optimizer.zero_grad()) and your steps use stale, summed gradients, and training quietly goes haywire.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 13.
slides.body: Next: the calculus mistakes that silently break models — and how to avoid them.

12-5.caption:
Most "my model won't train" bugs are actually calculus mistakes in disguise. 🐛

Derivatives are mechanical, which means the errors are too — and they're predictable. A dropped chain-rule factor, a flat gradient region, a slope you assumed existed where it doesn't. Knowing the failure modes saves you days of staring at a stuck loss curve.

This post is the field guide to the derivative mistakes that bite real practitioners, and the fixes.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• dropping the inner chain-rule factor
• confusing the function with its slope
• non-differentiable points (ReLU at 0)
• vanishing gradients from saturating activations
• numeric differentiation step-size traps
• forgetting gradients accumulate

Learn the traps before they cost you a week. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Dropping the chain-rule factor
slides.body: The #1 hand-derivation bug: differentiating the outer function and forgetting to multiply by the inner derivative. d/dx[sin(2x)] is 2·cos(2x), not cos(2x). In backprop this means a whole layer's gradient is off by a factor — and the model trains wrong, not crashes.

slides.title: Confusing height with slope
slides.body: A large loss does not mean a large gradient, and a small loss does not mean you're done. You can sit on a high but flat plateau (big f, tiny f') and barely move. Always reason about the slope separately from the value.

slides.title: Assuming differentiability everywhere
slides.body: ReLU has a kink at 0 — no unique slope there. Absolute value, max, and step functions have non-smooth or undefined points too. Frameworks pick a subgradient (ReLU'(0)=0 by convention), but if you hand-derive you can land on a slope that doesn't exist.

slides.title: The ReLU kink
slides.code:
import torch

x = torch.tensor(0.0, requires_grad=True)
y = torch.relu(x)
y.backward()
print(x.grad.item())   # 0.0  (chosen subgradient at the kink)
# slope is 0 for x<0, 1 for x>0, undefined exactly at 0

slides.title: Saturating activations kill gradients
slides.body: Sigmoid and tanh flatten out at their extremes, so their derivative goes to ~0. Stack several and the chain rule multiplies these tiny slopes, vanishing the gradient before it reaches early layers. They stop learning entirely. This is why ReLU largely replaced them.

slides.title: Vanishing, demonstrated
slides.code:
import numpy as np

# sigmoid derivative maxes at 0.25; multiply across 10 layers
slope_per_layer = 0.25
print(slope_per_layer ** 10)   # ~9.5e-07 -> effectively zero
# the early-layer gradient is a millionth of the signal

slides.title: Numeric step-size traps
slides.body: Finite differences need a Goldilocks h. Too large and you measure a secant, not the tangent — truncation error. Too small and floating-point subtraction loses precision — rounding error blows up. Around 1e-6 to 1e-8 with central differences is usually safe.

slides.title: Fixes that work
bullets: Use ReLU/GELU to keep slopes healthy
Batch/layer norm to stabilize gradient scale
Gradient clipping for exploding slopes
Always zero gradients before .backward()
Gradient-check hand-coded derivatives numerically

slides.title: Trusting a stuck loss as 'converged'
slides.body: A flat loss can mean a true minimum — or a saddle point, a plateau, or vanished gradients. Don't assume you've converged because loss stopped moving. Inspect the gradient norm: near-zero everywhere is suspicious in a deep net, not a victory.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 13.
slides.body: Next up in the Math for ML track: integrals and the area-under-the-curve intuition.

13-1.caption:
Training a model is just rolling a ball downhill — and gradient descent is the rule for which way is down. ⛰️

Every loss surface is a landscape: high where the model is wrong, low where it's right. Gradient descent is the algorithm that, knowing only the slope under its feet, takes step after step toward the lowest point it can find.

This post builds the picture before any formulas. We define the loss surface, the gradient, the step, and the one update rule that powers almost all of modern ML.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• the loss surface as a landscape
• what a gradient actually points at
• why we move in the NEGATIVE gradient direction
• the single update rule, in plain words
• the role of the learning rate
• the one-sentence version to keep

See the hill and the rest is obvious. Save it. 👇

slides.title: The loss surface
slides.body: Picture your model's error as height over a landscape. Each point on the ground is one setting of the parameters; the height there is how wrong the model is. Training means finding the lowest valley. The whole job of gradient descent is to walk you downhill.

slides.title: What a gradient is
slides.body: The gradient is the vector of slopes — one partial derivative per parameter. It points in the direction of STEEPEST ASCENT, the way the loss rises fastest. Its length tells you how steep that climb is. It's the local 'which way is up' arrow at your feet.

slides.title: Move the OTHER way
slides.body: The gradient points uphill, but we want to go down. So we step in the opposite direction: minus the gradient. That single sign flip is the heart of the method. Move against the slope and loss goes down; the steeper it is, the bigger the natural pull.

slides.title: The update rule
slides.body: One line runs the show: θ ← θ − η·∇L(θ). Take your parameters θ, compute the gradient ∇L, scale it by a small learning rate η, and subtract. Repeat. Everything else in optimization is a variation on this single nudge.

slides.title: One step, by hand
slides.code:
# minimize f(x) = x**2, slope f'(x) = 2x
x = 5.0          # start here
eta = 0.1        # learning rate

grad = 2 * x     # = 10.0, points uphill
x = x - eta * grad   # step downhill
print(x)         # 4.0  -> closer to the minimum at 0

slides.title: The learning rate
slides.body: η controls step size. Too small and you crawl; too large and you overshoot the valley and bounce. It's the single most important knob you set. Visually, it's how far you slide each time you check the slope under your feet.

slides.title: The mental model
bullets: Surface = loss over parameters
Gradient = steepest-uphill arrow
We step downhill = minus gradient
Learning rate = step length
Flat ground = minimum reached

slides.title: Gradient ≠ direction to the goal
slides.body: A common misread: the gradient points 'toward the answer.' It doesn't. It only knows the LOCAL slope right where you stand. It's a flashlight, not a map. Follow it step by step and you descend, but it has no idea where the valley floor actually is.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 14.
slides.body: Next: why this one tiny rule is the engine behind nearly every model you'll train.

13-2.caption:
There are millions of parameters in a modern model and exactly zero formulas that solve for them directly. Gradient descent is how we cope. 🚀

For most real losses, you can't just set the derivative to zero and solve — the math has no closed form, or the surface is too big to invert. So instead of solving, we search: take a step downhill, look again, repeat. That's the whole reason this humble rule runs all of deep learning.

This post is the 'so what.' Where gradient descent earns its place and what breaks without it.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• why closed-form solutions usually don't exist
• how it scales to billions of parameters
• why it's the universal optimizer for ML
• the speed-vs-stability trade it manages
• what the loss curve tells you about training
• the cost of getting the slope wrong

This is why every framework ships an optimizer. Save it. 👇

slides.title: No formula to solve
slides.body: For a plain line you can solve for the best weights directly. For a neural net you can't — the loss is a tangled, non-linear function of millions of weights with no closed form. Gradient descent sidesteps this: it never solves, it just keeps stepping downhill.

slides.title: It scales
slides.body: The update touches each parameter independently using only its own slope, so it costs about the same per step whether you have 3 weights or 3 billion. That linear scaling is exactly why the same rule trains a toy regression and a frontier LLM.

slides.title: One optimizer, every model
slides.body: Linear regression, logistic regression, SVMs, every neural network — they all minimize some loss, and gradient descent (or a variant) is the engine for all of them. Learn it once and you understand how essentially every model is fit.

slides.title: Fit a line by descending
slides.code:
import numpy as np
x = np.array([1, 2, 3, 4.0]); y = 2 * x + 1   # true: w=2, b=1
w = b = 0.0; eta = 0.01
for _ in range(2000):
    yhat = w * x + b
    err  = yhat - y
    w -= eta * (2 * (err * x).mean())   # dL/dw
    b -= eta * (2 * err.mean())         # dL/db
print(round(w, 2), round(b, 2))   # ~2.0 1.0

slides.title: Read the loss curve
slides.body: Plot loss versus step and the training tells its own story: a smooth drop means healthy learning, a plateau means you're stuck or done, a jagged spike means the learning rate is too high. The curve is your dashboard for every run.

slides.title: Where you meet it
bullets: Every deep-learning training loop
Logistic & linear regression fitting
Recommender and embedding models
Fine-tuning and LoRA on LLMs

slides.title: Thinking it's optional math
slides.body: Beginners treat the optimizer as a black box they can ignore. But a misjudged learning rate, a bad init, or an unscaled feature shows up directly as a loss curve that won't drop. Understanding descent is what turns 'it won't train' into a fixable problem.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 14.
slides.body: Next: the actual mechanics — gradients, steps, and the three flavors of descent.

13-3.caption:
Under the hood, gradient descent is a tight loop of four moves repeated thousands of times. 🔁

Forward: predict and measure the loss. Backward: get the gradient. Step: nudge the parameters downhill. Repeat. Once you see the loop, the famous variants — batch, stochastic, mini-batch — are just choices about how much data you look at before each step.

This post is the engine room: the real mechanics, the math made concrete, and the three flavors you'll actually use.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• the four-step loop, precisely
• how the gradient is computed from the loss
• batch vs stochastic vs mini-batch
• why mini-batch wins in practice
• momentum and adaptive steps in one line
• convergence: how you know it's done

This is the part that makes the loop click. Save it. 👇

slides.title: The four-step loop
slides.body: Every iteration does the same four things: run a forward pass to get predictions and loss, run backward to get the gradient of loss w.r.t. each parameter, update parameters by stepping against the gradient, then repeat on the next batch. That's the entire training loop.

slides.title: Where the gradient comes from
slides.body: The gradient is just the loss's partial derivative w.r.t. every parameter, found by the chain rule (backpropagation). For squared error on a line, dL/dw = mean(2·(ŷ−y)·x). You rarely compute it by hand — autograd does it — but it's plain calculus underneath.

slides.title: The core loop
Mini-batch: the default
slides.body: Mini-batch uses a small chunk (say 32–256 samples) per step. You get a gradient estimate that's stable enough to trust but cheap enough to compute, plus GPU-friendly parallelism. It's the practical sweet spot, which is why nearly all training uses it.

slides.title: Momentum & adaptive steps
slides.body: Plain steps can zig-zag in narrow valleys. Momentum adds a running average of past gradients so you build speed in consistent directions. Adam goes further, giving each parameter its own adaptive learning rate. Both are still 'step downhill,' just smarter.

slides.title: When it's converged
bullets: Loss stops decreasing meaningfully
Gradient magnitude near zero
Validation loss flattens
Updates smaller than your tolerance

slides.title: Confusing epoch and step
slides.body: A step is one parameter update on one batch; an epoch is one full pass over the data, which is many steps. People expect loss to drop per epoch and panic at within-epoch noise. Track the trend across steps, not every jittery point.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 14.
slides.body: Next: a from-scratch implementation you can run and watch descend, line by line.

13-4.caption:
You don't really understand gradient descent until you've written one in ten lines and watched the loss fall. 💻

No framework, no magic — just NumPy, a loss, a gradient, and a loop. We'll minimize a simple function, then fit a real regression, then visualize the descent so you can SEE the ball roll into the valley.

This post is code-heavy on purpose. Type it, run it, change the learning rate, break it. That's how the intuition sticks.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• minimize x² from scratch
• fit linear regression by descent
• track and plot the loss curve
• visualize the path on the surface
• compare three learning rates
• the runnable takeaways

Run it once and it's yours forever. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 1. Minimize x² from scratch
slides.code:
def grad(x):           # f(x)=x**2 -> f'(x)=2x
    return 2 * x

x, eta, history = 5.0, 0.1, []
for step in range(30):
    x -= eta * grad(x)      # step downhill
    history.append(x)

print(round(x, 4))          # ~0.0008, near the minimum 0

slides.title: 2. Linear regression by descent
slides.code:
import numpy as np
X = np.array([1, 2, 3, 4, 5.0])
y = 3 * X + 2 + np.random.randn(5) * 0.1
w = b = 0.0; eta = 0.01; losses = []
for _ in range(1000):
    yhat = w * X + b
    losses.append(((yhat - y) ** 2).mean())
    err = yhat - y
    w -= eta * 2 * (err * X).mean()
    b -= eta * 2 * err.mean()
print(round(w, 2), round(b, 2))   # ~3.0 2.0

slides.title: 3. Plot the loss curve
slides.code:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
plt.plot(losses)
plt.xlabel('step'); plt.ylabel('MSE loss')
plt.title('Loss falling as we descend')
plt.yscale('log')          # log scale shows the late slowdown
plt.show()

slides.title: 4. Watch the path on x²
slides.code:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt, numpy as np
grid = np.linspace(-5, 5, 100)
plt.plot(grid, grid ** 2)                 # the bowl
plt.scatter(history, [h ** 2 for h in history],
            c=range(len(history)), cmap='autumn')  # the steps
plt.title('The ball rolling into the valley')
plt.show()

slides.title: 5. Compare learning rates
Reading the output
slides.body: With η=0.1 the value glides to zero. With η=0.01 it barely moves in 20 steps — correct but slow. With η=1.01 each step overshoots further than the last and the value explodes. Same algorithm, three outcomes, one knob.

slides.title: Try these tweaks
bullets: Change the start point — same minimum?
Add momentum: v = 0.9·v − η·g
Swap MSE for absolute error
Print the gradient each step to watch it shrink

slides.title: Forgetting to scale features
slides.body: Run regression with X in the thousands and y near zero and the gradients blow up unless you shrink η drastically. The fix isn't a tiny learning rate — it's standardizing your features first so every parameter sees a comparable slope. Most 'it won't converge' bugs are this.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 14.
slides.body: Next: the traps — bad learning rates, local minima, vanishing gradients, and how to dodge them.

13-5.caption:
Gradient descent fails in a handful of classic ways, and every one of them has a visual tell. 🛑

Loss exploding to NaN, a curve that flatlines instantly, training that crawls, or a model stuck in a so-so valley — these aren't mysteries. Each is a named failure mode with a known cause and a standard fix. Learn the symptoms and debugging training stops feeling like guesswork.

This post is the field guide to what goes wrong and how to spot it on the loss curve.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• learning rate too high → divergence
• learning rate too low → crawling
• local minima and saddle points
• unscaled features wrecking the gradient
• vanishing & exploding gradients
• the debugging checklist

Know the failure modes before you trust the run. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Learning rate too high
slides.body: Set η too large and each step overshoots the valley, landing further up the far wall. Loss oscillates, then blows up to inf or NaN. The tell is a curve that spikes upward or goes haywire in the first few steps. Fix: cut η by 10× and retry.

slides.title: Learning rate too low
slides.body: Too small and you're correct but glacial — loss inches down and you waste hours barely moving. The tell is a nearly flat, slowly sloping curve. Fix: raise η, use a warmup-then-decay schedule, or switch to an adaptive optimizer like Adam.

slides.title: Local minima & saddles
slides.body: Non-convex surfaces have valleys that aren't the deepest, and saddle points that are flat in some directions. Plain descent can stall there. In high dimensions saddles are the bigger issue — and noise from mini-batches plus momentum is usually enough to escape.

slides.title: Unscaled features
slides.body: If one feature ranges 0–1 and another 0–100000, the loss surface becomes a long thin ravine. Descent zig-zags across it and barely moves forward. The fix is pure preprocessing: standardize features so the surface is round and the gradient points usefully.

slides.title: The standardize fix
slides.code:
import numpy as np
# before: gradients dominated by the big-scale column
X_std = (X - X.mean(axis=0)) / X.std(axis=0)
# now every feature has mean 0, std 1
# the loss surface is round -> descent goes straight in
# and a single learning rate works for all weights

slides.title: Vanishing & exploding gradients
slides.body: In deep nets, gradients multiply layer by layer. Repeated small factors shrink them toward zero (early layers stop learning); large factors blow them up (loss goes NaN). Fixes: good init, ReLU-style activations, normalization layers, and gradient clipping.

yes.leaf: rate too high or exploding grads -> lower eta, clip
rate too low or unscaled features -> raise eta, standardize

slides.title: Debugging checklist
bullets: Plot the loss curve first — always
Standardize features before training
Sweep learning rates by 10× (1e-1…1e-4)
Clip gradients if they spike
Check for NaNs in data and loss

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 14.
slides.body: Next week we build on this — the math foundations that make every model trainable.

15-1.caption:
Before frameworks, before AI, before anything fancy — there's the language. And Python's whole appeal is that it reads almost like English. 🐍

Python is a high-level, general-purpose language built around one idea: code should be obvious. No curly braces, no semicolons, no boilerplate — just indentation and words. That's why it became the default tongue of data science and machine learning.

This post defines the core vocabulary you'll use every single day: what Python is, what a variable, type, and expression actually are, and how the interpreter reads your file top to bottom.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• what Python is and why it's everywhere
• variables as labels, not boxes
• the handful of built-in types you'll use
• statements vs expressions
• how the interpreter runs your file
• the mental model to keep

Learn the words first. Save it. 👇

slides.title: What Python is
slides.body: Python is a high-level, interpreted, general-purpose language. High-level means it hides memory and machine details. Interpreted means it runs line by line, no compile step you manage. It reads close to plain English, which is exactly why it became the default language of data science and AI.

slides.title: Why it's everywhere
slides.body: Readability plus a giant ecosystem. NumPy, pandas, PyTorch, scikit-learn — the entire ML stack lives in Python. You write less code to do more, and the libraries you'll need are already built. Learn Python and the AI world opens up.

slides.title: Variables are labels
slides.body: A variable is just a name pointing at a value. `x = 5` doesn't put 5 in a box called x; it sticks a label 'x' on the value 5. Reassign and the label moves to a new value. No type declarations — Python figures out the type from what you assign.

slides.title: The built-in types
slides.body: You'll spend 90% of your time in a few: int and float for numbers, str for text, bool for True/False, and the containers list, tuple, dict, set. Everything in Python is an object with a type — check it any time with type(x).

slides.title: Types in action
slides.code:
name = "Saurav"      # str
age = 28              # int
height = 1.75         # float
is_learning = True    # bool
skills = ["python", "ai"]  # list

print(type(age))      # <class 'int'>
print(type(skills))   # <class 'list'>

slides.title: Statements vs expressions
slides.body: An expression produces a value: 2 + 3, len(name), x > 0. A statement does something: an assignment, an if, a loop, a def. Roughly: expressions are evaluated, statements are executed. Most lines you write are one or the other.

slides.title: How your file runs
slides.body: Python reads the file top to bottom, one statement at a time. There's no separate compile-and-link dance you manage — you hand the .py file to the interpreter and it executes immediately. Define things before you use them, because order matters.

slides.title: The mental model
bullets: Python = readable, runs line by line
Variables = names pointing at values
Everything is a typed object
Expressions make values; statements act
Interpreter runs the file top to bottom

slides.title: Python isn't 'typeless'
slides.body: Beginners hear 'no type declarations' and assume types don't exist. They do — Python is strongly typed, it just infers the type at runtime. `"3" + 5` won't silently coerce; it raises a TypeError. Dynamic, not loose.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 16.
slides.body: Next: why these basics matter — the foundation every line of AI code stands on.

15-2.caption:
You can't skip the basics. Every PyTorch model, every pandas pipeline, every API call you'll write is just Python fundamentals stacked on top of each other. 🧱

People rush to import torch and wonder why the errors make no sense. The truth: 90% of beginner ML bugs aren't ML bugs — they're a misunderstood list, a mutable default, a type mismatch. The basics are the debugging language.

This post is the 'why bother.' Where these fundamentals actually pay off and what breaks when you skip them.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• why fundamentals beat framework-hopping
• how readability becomes maintainability
• the basics hiding inside every ML line
• why types and structures decide your bugs
• the compounding return on clean basics
• what 'pythonic' really buys you

Master the floor before you build the tower. Save it. 👇

slides.title: The basics are the bugs
slides.body: Most beginner ML errors aren't about models — they're a list where you wanted an array, a string where you needed an int, a mutated dict. If you can't read the fundamentals fluently, every traceback looks like noise. Fluency in basics is fluency in debugging.

slides.title: Readability is leverage
slides.body: Code is read far more often than it's written. Python's clean syntax means you and your teammates spend less time decoding and more time thinking. In ML, where experiments pile up fast, readable code is the difference between iterating and drowning.

slides.title: Hiding inside every ML line
slides.body: `df[df.age > 18]` is boolean indexing on a structure. `[x**2 for x in data]` is a comprehension. `model(**config)` is dict unpacking. The 'advanced' AI code you admire is plain Python basics composed together. Learn them once, recognize them everywhere.

slides.title: ML code is just basics
slides.code:
# every piece here is a Python fundamental
scores = {"acc": 0.91, "f1": 0.88}
for metric, value in scores.items():   # dict iteration
    print(f"{metric}: {value:.2f}")    # f-string + format

best = [m for m, v in scores.items() if v > 0.9]  # comprehension
print(best)   # ['acc']

slides.title: Types decide your fate
slides.body: Pick the wrong structure and everything downstream suffers. A list when you needed a set means slow lookups. A float where you wanted an int breaks indexing. Knowing what each type costs and guarantees is what separates code that scales from code that crawls.

slides.title: The compounding return
bullets: One week of basics saves months of confusion
Fundamentals transfer across every library
Clean basics = faster experiments
You debug instead of guessing

slides.title: Framework-hopping too early
slides.body: Jumping straight to TensorFlow or LangChain before you understand a dict feels productive but isn't. You end up pattern-matching tutorials without understanding them. When the tutorial diverges from your problem, you're lost. Solidify Python first; frameworks become easy.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 16.
slides.body: Next: how Python actually works under the hood — the interpreter, objects, and execution model.

15-3.caption:
Python looks simple on the surface, but knowing what happens underneath makes you dangerous. ⚙️

When you run a script, the interpreter parses it, compiles it to bytecode, and a virtual machine executes it. Variables are names bound to objects on the heap. Indentation isn't style — it's syntax that defines structure. Understanding this turns 'why did it do that?' into 'of course it did that.'

This post is the engine room: how Python executes, how it stores values, and how control flow and functions actually work.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• the interpreter pipeline, step by step
• objects, references, and the heap
• why indentation defines blocks
• how control flow chooses a path
• how functions take and return values
• mutable vs immutable, demystified

See the machine and the language clicks. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Source to execution
slides.body: Your .py file goes through a pipeline: the interpreter parses the text into a tree, compiles that to bytecode (.pyc), and the Python Virtual Machine executes the bytecode instruction by instruction. You never see most of this — you just run the file — but it's why Python feels instant yet still 'compiled.'

slides.title: Objects and references
slides.body: Every value is an object living on the heap with an identity, a type, and a value. Variables are just names bound to those objects. Assigning `b = a` doesn't copy — both names point at the same object. That single fact explains most surprising Python behavior.

slides.title: Indentation is structure
slides.body: Most languages mark blocks with braces. Python uses indentation — the whitespace IS the syntax. A consistent indent opens a block; dedenting closes it. This forces readable layout, but it also means a stray space is a real error, not a cosmetic one.

slides.title: Control flow + functions
How functions work
slides.body: `def` creates a function object and binds a name to it. Calling it runs the body in a fresh local scope; arguments are passed by assigning them to parameter names. `return` hands a value back and exits. No return? You get None automatically.

slides.title: The execution model
bullets: Parse → compile → run bytecode
Values are objects on the heap
Names are bindings, not boxes
Indentation defines every block
return ends a function; default is None

slides.title: Mixing tabs and spaces
slides.body: Because indentation is syntax, mixing tabs and spaces produces IndentationError or, worse, code that looks aligned but isn't. Pick spaces (PEP 8 says 4) and let your editor enforce it. This bites nearly every beginner exactly once.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 16.
slides.body: Next: real, runnable code — variables, structures, loops, and functions you can type today.

15-4.caption:
Enough theory. Here's Python you can paste into a file and run right now. 💻

We'll walk the absolute core: variables and types, the four big data structures, loops and conditionals, then functions — the building blocks of every program you'll ever write. Each snippet is short, real, and correct. Type them, run them, change a value, watch what breaks.

This post is code-heavy on purpose. Reading Python teaches you nothing; running it teaches you everything.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• variables, types, and f-strings
• lists, dicts, tuples, sets
• loops and conditionals
• functions with arguments and returns
• comprehensions, the pythonic shortcut
• the runnable takeaways

Run it once and it's yours. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 1. Variables, types, f-strings
slides.code:
name = "Saurav"          # str
age = 28                  # int
pi = 3.14159              # float
learning = True           # bool

# f-string: embed values right in the text
print(f"{name} is {age}, pi rounds to {pi:.2f}")
# Saurav is 28, pi rounds to 3.14

slides.title: 2. The four structures
slides.code:
fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]   # list: ordered, mutable
point = (10, 20)                          # tuple: fixed, immutable
scores = {"math": 90, "cs": 95}           # dict: key -> value
unique = {1, 2, 2, 3}                      # set: no duplicates

print(fruits[0])        # apple
print(scores["cs"])     # 95
print(unique)           # {1, 2, 3}

slides.title: 3. Loops and conditionals
4. Functions
slides.code:
def average(numbers, round_to=2):
    """Return the mean of a list of numbers."""
    total = sum(numbers)
    mean = total / len(numbers)
    return round(mean, round_to)

print(average([90, 95, 72]))        # 85.67
print(average([90, 95, 72], 0))     # 86

slides.title: 5. Comprehensions
slides.code:
nums = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]

squares = [n**2 for n in nums]            # transform
evens = [n for n in nums if n % 2 == 0]   # filter
labels = {n: "even" if n % 2 == 0 else "odd" for n in nums}  # dict

print(squares)   # [1, 4, 9, 16, 25, 36]
print(evens)     # [2, 4, 6]

slides.title: Runnable takeaways
bullets: f-strings beat string concatenation
Pick the structure that fits the job
for...in iterates anything iterable
Functions return None unless you say return
Comprehensions replace simple loops

slides.title: Off-by-one with range
slides.body: `range(5)` yields 0,1,2,3,4 — it stops BEFORE 5. Indexing starts at 0 too, so `fruits[3]` on a 3-item list raises IndexError. Half-open ranges are a feature once you internalize them, but they trip up everyone at first.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 16.
slides.body: Next: the beginner traps that waste hours — and how to avoid every one.

15-5.caption:
The bugs that cost beginners the most hours aren't exotic — they're the same five traps, over and over. 🪤

Mutable default arguments. Confusing copy with reference. Integer vs float surprises. Indentation slips. Misreading scope. None of these are hard once you've been burned, but the first burn stings. Let's get you the lesson without the pain.

This post is a field guide to the classic Python mistakes, each with the fix and the reasoning behind it.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• the mutable default argument trap
• why `b = a` doesn't copy a list
• == vs is, value vs identity
• off-by-one and range pitfalls
• late binding in closures
• the habits that prevent all of these

Learn from these so you don't have to. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 1. Mutable default args
slides.body: A default argument is created ONCE, when the function is defined — not per call. Use a mutable default like [] and every call shares the same list, so it 'remembers' across calls. The fix is the None sentinel pattern.

slides.title: The fix: None sentinel
slides.code:
# BUG: shared list across calls
def add(item, bucket=[]):
    bucket.append(item)
    return bucket
print(add(1)); print(add(2))   # [1]  then [1, 2]  😱

# FIX:
def add(item, bucket=None):
    if bucket is None:
        bucket = []
    bucket.append(item)
    return bucket

slides.title: 2. Reference, not copy
slides.body: `b = a` on a list makes b point at the SAME list as a — change one and both change. To get an independent copy use a.copy() or list(a); for nested data use copy.deepcopy. This is the single most common surprise for newcomers.

slides.title: Copy vs alias
slides.code:
a = [1, 2, 3]
b = a            # alias: same object
b.append(4)
print(a)         # [1, 2, 3, 4]  -- a changed too!

c = a.copy()     # real copy
c.append(5)
print(a)         # [1, 2, 3, 4]  -- a untouched

slides.title: 4. Off-by-one & ranges
slides.body: `range(n)` stops before n, and indexing starts at 0. So a list of length 3 has valid indices 0,1,2 — `lst[3]` raises IndexError. Loop bounds and slice ends are exclusive on the right. Count from zero and the off-by-ones disappear.

slides.title: 5. Late binding in loops
slides.code:
# BUG: all functions print 2
funcs = [lambda: i for i in range(3)]
print([f() for f in funcs])   # [2, 2, 2]  😱

# FIX: capture i now with a default arg
funcs = [lambda i=i: i for i in range(3)]
print([f() for f in funcs])   # [0, 1, 2]

slides.title: Habits that prevent all of it
bullets: Default args: None, never [] or {}
Copy explicitly when you need independence
Use 'is' only for None
Count from zero, ranges stop early
Bind loop vars with default args

slides.title: Silencing instead of reading
slides.body: The biggest meta-mistake: wrapping code in try/except and ignoring the error to make it 'work.' The traceback is telling you exactly what's wrong, on which line. Read it bottom-up. The error message is a feature, not an obstacle.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 16.
slides.body: Next: Day 16 builds on these basics — get ready to level up your Python.

16-1.caption:
Choosing the right data structure isn't a style choice — it's the difference between code that flies and code that crawls. 🧱

A data structure is just a way to organize values in memory so you can store, find, and change them efficiently. Python ships four workhorses built in: list, tuple, dict, and set. Each makes a different promise about order, mutability, and lookup speed.

This post is the map. Learn what each container IS before you learn when to reach for it.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• what a data structure actually means
• the four built-in containers, defined
• mutable vs immutable, plainly
• ordered vs unordered
• how each one stores its values
• the one-line cheat sheet to remember

Know the containers and half of Python clicks. Save it. 👇

slides.title: What a data structure is
slides.body: A data structure is a way of organizing values in memory so you can store and retrieve them efficiently. The structure you pick decides what's cheap and what's expensive: looking things up, adding, removing, keeping order. Python gives you four built-in ones to cover almost every everyday need.

slides.title: list — ordered, changeable
slides.body: A list holds an ordered sequence of items you can change in place: append, insert, sort, remove. Written with square brackets. It's the default 'pile of things' container — use it whenever order matters and the collection will grow or shrink.

slides.title: tuple — ordered, fixed
slides.body: A tuple is an ordered sequence too, but frozen: once created you can't add, remove, or reassign items. Written with parentheses. Use it for fixed records — a coordinate (x, y), an RGB color — where the shape should never change.

slides.title: dict — key → value map
slides.body: A dict stores pairs: each unique key maps to a value, looked up by key instead of position. Written with curly braces and colons. It's how you model 'named' data — a user record, a config, a count per word — with near-instant lookup by key.

slides.title: set — unique, unordered
slides.body: A set is an unordered collection of unique values — duplicates are dropped automatically. Written with curly braces. Reach for it when membership and uniqueness are the point: deduping a list, or asking 'is this item already here?' fast.

slides.title: All four in one breath
slides.code:
nums   = [1, 2, 2, 3]        # list  - ordered, mutable
point  = (10, 20)            # tuple - ordered, frozen
user   = {"name": "Ada"}    # dict  - key -> value
unique = {1, 2, 3}          # set   - unique, unordered

print(type(nums), type(point), type(user), type(unique))

slides.title: The one-line cheat sheet
bullets: list = ordered, editable pile
tuple = frozen ordered record
dict = key → value lookup
set = unique, unordered bag
Strings act like immutable lists of chars

slides.title: Treating them as interchangeable
slides.body: Beginners default to lists for everything, then write slow membership checks and clumsy lookups. The container is a decision, not a default. Asking 'do I look up by key, by position, or just need uniqueness?' picks the right one in one step.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 17.
slides.body: Next: why this choice quietly decides whether your code is fast or painfully slow.

16-2.caption:
The same loop can run in a blink or grind for minutes — and often the only difference is which container you reached for. ⚡

Data structures aren't academic trivia. The right one turns an O(n) scan into an O(1) lookup, kills duplicate bugs before they happen, and makes your intent obvious to the next reader. The wrong one ships code that works on 100 rows and dies on 100,000.

This post is the 'so what' — where the choice actually pays off.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• why list lookups get slow and dicts don't
• how sets make dedup and membership instant
• how the right container documents intent
• immutability as a correctness tool
• the real cost of the wrong choice at scale
• where you'll feel this every day

This is the difference between code that scales and code that stalls. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Lookup speed is the headline
slides.body: Checking 'is x in here?' costs very different amounts. In a list, Python scans item by item — O(n). In a dict or set, it jumps straight to the answer by hashing the key — O(1) on average. On big data that gap is the difference between instant and unusable.

slides.title: Same task, 1000x apart
slides.code:
import time
big = list(range(1_000_000))
big_set = set(big)

t = time.time(); 999_999 in big;     print("list:", time.time() - t)
t = time.time(); 999_999 in big_set; print("set: ", time.time() - t)
# list scans a million items; set answers instantly

slides.title: Sets kill duplicate bugs
slides.body: Deduping a list with sets is one call: set(items). Membership is instant. A whole class of bugs — double-counting, sending the same email twice, re-processing an ID — disappears when uniqueness is enforced by the container instead of by hand-written checks.

slides.title: The right container documents intent
slides.body: A tuple says 'this won't change.' A dict says 'look me up by name.' A set says 'these are unique.' The reader learns your design from the type alone — no comment needed. Choosing well makes code self-explaining and harder to misuse.

slides.title: Immutability prevents whole bug classes
slides.body: Because tuples and frozensets can't change, they're safe to share, hash, and use as dict keys. You can't accidentally mutate a value another part of the code relies on. Immutability isn't a limitation — it's a guarantee you trade flexibility for.

slides.title: The O(n²) trap, fixed
slides.code:
# SLOW: list membership inside a loop -> O(n*m)
common = [x for x in a if x in b]          # b is a list

# FAST: hash the lookup side once -> O(n)
b_set = set(b)
common = [x for x in a if x in b_set]

slides.title: Where you feel this daily
bullets: Counting / grouping with dicts
Dedup and 'seen?' checks with sets
Returning fixed records as tuples
Joining two collections fast
Caching results keyed by input

slides.title: Optimizing the loop, not the structure
slides.body: People rewrite loops, add caches, and reach for libraries while the real cost is a list where a set belonged. The structure dominates the algorithm's complexity. Fix the container first; the speedup is usually orders of magnitude, not percent.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 17.
slides.body: Next: under the hood — how lists, dicts, and sets actually store your data.

16-3.caption:
Lists, dicts, and sets feel like magic until you see the machinery — then their speed (and their quirks) make total sense. 🔧

A list is a resizable array of references. A dict and a set are hash tables. Those two facts explain almost everything: why dict lookup is instant, why sets have no order, why appending is cheap but inserting at the front isn't, and why only hashable things can be keys.

This post is the engine room. Real mechanics, not metaphors.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• a list is an array of pointers
• why append is O(1) but insert(0) is O(n)
• how hashing turns a key into a slot
• why dict/set need hashable keys
• how collisions are handled
• why sets and dicts look 'unordered'

See the machine and the behavior stops surprising you. Save it. 👇

slides.title: A list is an array of references
slides.body: A Python list is a contiguous block of pointers to objects, with spare slots at the end. Indexing is instant because it's just arithmetic on a base address. The values live elsewhere on the heap; the list only holds the references to them.

slides.title: Append is cheap, insert(0) isn't
slides.body: Appending usually drops into a spare slot — amortized O(1). Inserting at the front means shifting every existing pointer up one position — O(n). Same for pop(0). When you need fast ends-and-fronts, collections.deque gives O(1) on both sides.

slides.title: Feel the difference
slides.code:
from collections import deque

lst = [1, 2, 3]
lst.append(4)        # O(1) amortized - lands in a spare slot
lst.insert(0, 0)     # O(n) - shifts everything right

dq = deque([1, 2, 3])
dq.appendleft(0)     # O(1) - deque is fast at BOTH ends

slides.title: Dicts and sets are hash tables
slides.body: A dict stores entries in slots chosen by hash(key) % table_size. To find a key, Python hashes it and jumps straight to its slot — no scanning. A set is the same machine without the values. That's why both give average O(1) lookup.

slides.title: Hashable = usable as a key
Collisions and order
slides.body: Two keys can hash to the same slot — a collision — so Python probes nearby slots until it finds room. Sets don't track insertion order at all. Dicts DO preserve insertion order (since 3.7) as an implementation guarantee, but you still access by key, not position.

slides.title: The mechanics that explain behavior
bullets: list = array of pointers → O(1) index
append O(1), insert(0) O(n)
dict/set = hash table → O(1) lookup
Keys must be hashable (immutable)
Sets unordered; dicts keep insert order

slides.title: Assuming O(1) is guaranteed
slides.body: Hash lookups are O(1) on AVERAGE. With pathological keys or many collisions, performance degrades. And mutating an object after using it as a key corrupts the table. Keep keys immutable and you keep the guarantee.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 17.
slides.body: Next: a hands-on tour — the operations you'll actually type every day, in code.

16-4.caption:
Reading about containers is fine. Typing them until the operations are muscle memory is what makes you fast. 💻

This post is deliberately code-heavy. We'll create, slice, and mutate each of the four containers, then do the moves you'll reach for constantly: comprehensions, counting with dicts, set algebra, and unpacking. Type it, run it, tweak it.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• list creation, slicing, and comprehensions
• dict building, counting, and iteration
• set algebra: union, intersection, difference
• tuple unpacking and packing
• the patterns you'll reuse forever
• the gotchas hiding in plain code

Run every snippet once and these stop being syntax you look up. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 1. Lists: slice & comprehend
slides.code:
nums = [5, 1, 4, 2, 3]
nums.sort()                 # in place -> [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
print(nums[1:4])            # slice -> [2, 3, 4]
print(nums[::-1])           # reverse -> [5, 4, 3, 2, 1]

squares = [n * n for n in nums if n % 2]   # comprehension
print(squares)              # [1, 9, 25]

slides.title: 2. Dicts: build, count, iterate
3. Cleaner counting
slides.code:
from collections import Counter
words = "a b a c b a".split()

counts = Counter(words)
print(counts)               # Counter({'a': 3, 'b': 2, 'c': 1})
print(counts.most_common(2))  # [('a', 3), ('b', 2)]

# dict comprehension: square each value
print({k: v * v for k, v in counts.items()})

slides.title: 4. Set algebra
5. Tuple packing & unpacking
slides.code:
point = (10, 20)
x, y = point                 # unpack
print(x, y)                  # 10 20

a, b = b, a = 1, 2           # swap with no temp var

first, *rest = [1, 2, 3, 4]   # star captures the tail
print(first, rest)            # 1 [2, 3, 4]

slides.title: Patterns you'll reuse forever
bullets: .get(k, default) avoids KeyError
Counter for any frequency task
set() to dedup in one call
Comprehensions over manual loops
Unpacking for clean multi-return

slides.title: 6. Nesting them together
slides.code:
users = [
    {"name": "Ada", "tags": {"py", "ml"}},
    {"name": "Lin", "tags": {"py", "go"}},
]
all_tags = set()
for u in users:
    all_tags |= u["tags"]    # accumulate union
print(sorted(all_tags))      # ['go', 'ml', 'py']

slides.title: The mutable-default surprise
slides.body: Comprehensions and Counter feel clean, but watch shared references: a list of lists built with [[0]*3]*3 shares one inner list, so editing one edits all. Build nested structures with a comprehension instead: [[0]*3 for _ in range(3)].

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 17.
slides.body: Next: the traps — the container mistakes that bite even experienced Python devs.

16-5.caption:
Most container bugs in Python aren't exotic — they're the same five traps, hit over and over. 🪤

These are the ones that pass code review, work in the demo, and then corrupt data or mysteriously share state in production. Once you've seen them named, you'll spot them instantly in your own code and everyone else's.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• the mutable default argument trap
• copy vs reference (shallow surprises)
• mutating a list while looping over it
• unhashable keys and set quirks
• relying on order where there is none

Learn the five and you'll dodge most container pain forever. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 1. Mutable default arguments
slides.body: A default like def f(items=[]) is created ONCE and reused across every call, so it secretly accumulates state between calls. The fix is the None sentinel: default to None, then build a fresh list inside the function body.

slides.title: The default-arg trap & fix
slides.code:
def bad(item, bucket=[]):       # created once!
    bucket.append(item)
    return bucket
print(bad(1)); print(bad(2))    # [1] then [1, 2]  - leak!

def good(item, bucket=None):
    if bucket is None:
        bucket = []             # fresh each call
    bucket.append(item)
    return bucket

slides.title: 2. Assignment is not a copy
slides.body: b = a doesn't copy a list — it makes b another name for the same object. Mutate through one and the other 'changes' too. Use a.copy() or list(a) for a shallow copy, and copy.deepcopy for nested structures.

slides.title: Reference vs copy
slides.code:
a = [1, 2, 3]
b = a               # SAME list, two names
b.append(4)
print(a)            # [1, 2, 3, 4]  - surprise

c = a.copy()        # independent shallow copy
c.append(5)
print(a)            # [1, 2, 3, 4]  - unchanged

slides.title: 3. Mutating while iterating
slides.body: Removing items from a list while looping over it skips elements or throws — the index moves under you. Iterate over a copy (for x in items[:]) or, better, build a new filtered list with a comprehension and reassign.

slides.title: Don't delete while looping
slides.code:
nums = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]

# WRONG: skips elements as the list shrinks
# for n in nums:
#     if n % 2 == 0: nums.remove(n)

# RIGHT: build a new list, then reassign
nums = [n for n in nums if n % 2]
print(nums)         # [1, 3, 5]

slides.title: 4. Unhashable keys
slides.body: Only immutable, hashable objects can be dict keys or set members — so lists and dicts can't. Convert a list key to a tuple. And remember a set itself is unhashable; use frozenset when you need a set inside a set.

slides.title: 5. Trusting order that isn't there
slides.body: Dicts keep insertion order, but sets do NOT — never rely on the order a set prints. And don't index a dict by position; access by key. Treating unordered containers as ordered produces bugs that only appear on other machines or versions.

slides.title: The five, in one glance
bullets: Default arg = None, build inside
b = a shares; use .copy()
Never mutate the list you're looping
Keys must be immutable / hashable
Sets are unordered — don't trust order

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 17.
slides.body: Next day, next topic — we keep building your Python foundation, one container at a time.

17-1.caption:
A comprehension isn't a clever trick — it's a sentence that says 'build me this collection from that one.' 🧠

A list or dict comprehension is a single expression that constructs a new list, dict, or set by describing what each element should be, where it comes from, and which ones to keep. It replaces the build-an-empty-container-then-loop-and-append ritual with one readable line.

This post is the map: what a comprehension actually IS before you learn why it matters or how to wield it.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• what a comprehension really means
• the three parts every comprehension has
• list vs dict vs set comprehension
• how it maps onto a plain for-loop
• the optional filter clause
• the one mental model to keep

Get the shape in your head and the rest is just syntax. Save it. 👇

slides.title: What a comprehension is
slides.body: A comprehension is a single expression that builds a whole collection by describing its elements. Instead of creating an empty list and appending in a loop, you write the rule once: 'this value, for each item, where this is true.' Python does the looping for you and hands back the finished container.

slides.title: The three moving parts
slides.body: Every comprehension has an OUTPUT expression (what each element becomes), a SOURCE clause (for x in iterable), and an optional FILTER (if condition). Read it left to right: the output is what you keep, the for says where values come from, the if decides which survive.

slides.title: List comprehension, minimal
slides.code:
# output     source
squares = [n * n for n in range(5)]
print(squares)        # [0, 1, 4, 9, 16]

# output  source         filter
evens = [n for n in range(10) if n % 2 == 0]
print(evens)          # [0, 2, 4, 6, 8]

slides.title: Dict and set versions
slides.body: Swap the brackets and you change the result type. Curly braces with a key:value pair build a dict. Curly braces with a single expression build a set. Same three parts, different container — the syntax is one idea reused three ways.

slides.title: All three containers
slides.code:
nums = [1, 2, 3, 2, 1]

lst  = [n * n for n in nums]            # list (keeps dups)
st   = {n * n for n in nums}            # set  (drops dups)
dct  = {n: n * n for n in nums}         # dict (key -> value)

print(lst)   # [1, 4, 9, 4, 1]
print(st)    # {1, 4, 9}
print(dct)   # {1: 1, 2: 4, 3: 9}

slides.title: It's a loop, rewritten
slides.body: A comprehension is not a new feature — it's syntax sugar over a for-loop that builds a collection. Anything a comprehension does, an explicit loop can do. The comprehension just collapses the empty-container-plus-append pattern into one expression that reads like its intent.

slides.title: The mental model
bullets: Read it as: output FOR each item IF keep
Brackets pick the container type
[] list, {} set, {k:v} dict
The filter is optional
It always returns a NEW collection

slides.title: Thinking it's just shorthand for speed
slides.body: Newcomers treat comprehensions as a typing shortcut and stop there. The real win is that the line names a transformation: 'these values, from that source, filtered like so.' That clarity — not character count — is why comprehensions are everywhere in idiomatic Python.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 18.
slides.body: Next: why comprehensions are the difference between Python that reads clearly and Python that reads like noise.

17-2.caption:
The same transform written as a four-line loop or a one-line comprehension does the identical work — but one of them tells the reader your intent in a glance. ✨

Comprehensions matter because they compress the most common operation in programming — 'turn this collection into that one' — into a single declarative line. Less boilerplate, fewer off-by-one bugs, less mutable state to track, and usually faster execution too.

This post is the 'so what': where comprehensions actually pay off in real code.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• why declarative beats imperative here
• the boilerplate they delete
• why they're often faster than loops
• how they reduce a class of bugs
• when a loop is still the right call
• where you'll reach for them daily

This is what separates readable Python from a wall of loops. Save it. 👇

slides.title: They declare intent, not steps
slides.body: A loop tells the machine HOW: make an empty list, iterate, append. A comprehension tells the reader WHAT: 'the squares of the evens.' Declarative code is easier to scan and harder to break, because there's no intermediate state for a bug to hide in.

slides.title: Four lines become one
slides.code:
# imperative: state to manage, easy to fumble
result = []
for row in rows:
    if row.active:
        result.append(row.name)

# declarative: the same intent, one line
result = [row.name for row in rows if row.active]

slides.title: They delete boilerplate
slides.body: The empty-list-then-append pattern appears constantly. Each occurrence is three throwaway lines and a mutable temp variable. Comprehensions remove the ceremony so the transformation stands alone — and code with less ceremony has fewer places to introduce mistakes.

slides.title: They're usually faster
slides.body: A comprehension runs its loop in optimized C internally and skips the repeated attribute lookup of list.append. For pure build-a-collection work it's typically 20-40% faster than the equivalent explicit loop — free speed for code that's also shorter.

slides.title: They cut a class of bugs
slides.body: No temp variable means no risk of appending to the wrong list, forgetting to reset it between runs, or mutating it elsewhere. The result is computed and bound in one atomic step. Fewer moving parts, fewer ways to be wrong.

right.title: Use a plain loop
items: You'd need a comment to explain

slides.title: The readability cliff
Where you'll reach for them
bullets: Mapping a function over a list
Filtering rows by a condition
Building a lookup dict from records
Deduping into a set
Flattening or reshaping data

slides.title: Cramming logic in for the one-liner flex
slides.body: Comprehensions reward clarity, not density. A triple-nested comprehension with two filters and a ternary is harder to read than the loop it replaced. The point was never 'one line at any cost' — it was 'intent at a glance.' If it stops being glanceable, unfold it.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 18.
slides.body: Next: under the hood — exactly how Python evaluates a comprehension, left to right.

17-3.caption:
Comprehensions feel like syntax magic until you watch Python evaluate one piece by piece — then the nesting rules and scoping quirks stop being mysterious. 🔍

Under the hood a comprehension is a tightly optimized loop with its own little scope. Reading order matters, nesting reads outer-to-inner, and the whole thing runs in a hidden function so its loop variable never leaks. Get the evaluation order right and you'll never misorder a nested comprehension again.

This post is the engine room.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• the exact left-to-right reading order
• how nested for-clauses unfold
• why the loop variable doesn't leak
• filter placement: if vs ternary
• how generator expressions differ
• the scoping rule that trips people up

See the machine and the syntax becomes obvious. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Reading order
slides.body: Read a comprehension by moving the OUTPUT expression to the end mentally. '[expr for x in src if cond]' evaluates as: for each x in src, if cond is true, compute expr and collect it. The output is written first but evaluated last — that's the one inversion to internalize.

slides.title: Nested for-clauses unfold outward
slides.body: Multiple for-clauses read in the SAME order as nested loops, left to right: the leftmost for is the outer loop. '[x for row in grid for x in row]' means 'for each row, for each x in that row.' The order of the fors matches how you'd indent them.

slides.title: Nesting maps to indentation
slides.code:
grid = [[1, 2], [3, 4]]

flat = [x for row in grid for x in row]
print(flat)              # [1, 2, 3, 4]

# identical loop ordering:
flat = []
for row in grid:         # leftmost for = outer
    for x in row:        # rightmost for = inner
        flat.append(x)

slides.title: The loop variable is scoped
slides.body: In Python 3, a comprehension runs inside its own implicit function, so its loop variable lives in that scope and never leaks out. After '[n for n in range(5)]', the name n does not exist outside. This is a deliberate fix for a Python 2 wart where it DID leak.

slides.title: No leakage
Filter vs transform-then-keep
slides.body: An 'if' AFTER the for-clause is a filter: it decides whether to include an item. An 'if/else' BEFORE the for is a ternary: it changes the value but keeps every item. Same keyword, completely different jobs — position is everything.

slides.title: if's two positions
slides.code:
nums = range(6)

# filter: drops items (if at the end, no else)
kept = [n for n in nums if n % 2 == 0]
print(kept)              # [0, 2, 4]

# ternary: keeps all, transforms (if/else up front)
labeled = ["even" if n % 2 == 0 else "odd" for n in nums]
print(labeled)           # ['even','odd','even','odd','even','odd']

slides.title: Generator expressions: same syntax, lazy
slides.body: Swap the brackets for parentheses and you get a generator expression: same evaluation rules, but it yields items one at a time instead of building the whole collection in memory. Use it when you only iterate once or the source is huge — it's the memory-friendly cousin.

slides.title: The rules that explain it all
bullets: Output written first, evaluated last
Leftmost for = outermost loop
Loop var is scoped, never leaks
'if' at end filters; 'if/else' up front maps
( ) makes it a lazy generator

slides.title: Misordering nested for-clauses
slides.body: People flip the fors and reference a variable before its loop defines it, getting a NameError or wrong results. The cure is mechanical: write the fors in the exact order you'd nest the loops, outer first. If you can indent it as a loop, you can order the comprehension.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 18.
slides.body: Next: hands-on — the comprehension patterns you'll actually type every week, in code.

17-4.caption:
Reading about comprehensions is fine. Typing the dozen patterns you'll actually reuse is what makes them automatic. 💻

This post is deliberately code-heavy. We'll map, filter, build dicts from pairs, invert a dict, dedupe with sets, flatten nested data, and reach for a generator expression — the moves that cover most real-world use. Type each one, run it, tweak it.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• list map and filter patterns
• dict comprehension: build and invert
• set comprehension for dedup
• flattening nested lists
• conditional (ternary) elements
• the generator-expression escape hatch

Run every snippet once and these stop being syntax you look up. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 1. Map and filter a list
slides.code:
prices = [10, 25, 4, 60, 18]

# map: add 8% tax to every price
with_tax = [round(p * 1.08, 2) for p in prices]
print(with_tax)          # [10.8, 27.0, 4.32, 64.8, 19.44]

# filter: keep only the big ones
big = [p for p in prices if p > 20]
print(big)               # [25, 60]

slides.title: 2. Build and invert a dict
3. Dedup with a set, flatten a grid
slides.code:
tags = ["py", "ai", "py", "ml", "ai"]
unique = {t for t in tags}            # set comprehension
print(unique)            # {'py', 'ai', 'ml'}

grid = [[1, 2], [3, 4], [5, 6]]
flat = [x for row in grid for x in row]   # nested
print(flat)              # [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]

slides.title: 4. Conditional elements + generator
slides.code:
nums = range(6)

# ternary: transform every item
parity = ["even" if n % 2 == 0 else "odd" for n in nums]
print(parity)            # ['even','odd','even','odd','even','odd']

# generator expression: lazy, memory-light sum
total = sum(n * n for n in range(1_000_000))
print(total)             # 333332833333500000

slides.title: 5. Real task: parse a CSV line
slides.code:
line = "ada,30,paris"
fields = ["name", "age", "city"]

record = {k: v for k, v in zip(fields, line.split(","))}
print(record)            # {'name': 'ada', 'age': '30', 'city': 'paris'}

# cast just the numeric field
record["age"] = int(record["age"])
print(record["age"] + 1) # 31

slides.title: Patterns to memorize
bullets: [f(x) for x in xs] — map
[x for x in xs if cond] — filter
{k: v for ...} — build a lookup
{x for x in xs} — dedup
(f(x) for x in xs) — lazy stream

slides.title: zip + comprehension = records
slides.body: Pairing zip with a dict comprehension is the cleanest way to turn two parallel lists — headers and values — into one keyed record. It's the backbone of parsing rows, building config maps, and reshaping data without a single explicit loop.

slides.title: 6. Group with setdefault vs comp limits
Reaching for a comprehension when you need accumulation
slides.body: A comprehension builds each element independently — it can't easily carry state between iterations. Grouping, running totals, or 'last-seen' logic belong in a plain loop or a tool like defaultdict. Force them into a comprehension and you'll either lose data or write something unreadable.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 18.
slides.body: Next: the comprehension mistakes that bite everyone — and how to dodge them.

17-5.caption:
Comprehensions are sharp tools. The same edge that makes them elegant will cut you if you nest too deep, hide side effects, or forget they build everything at once. 🩹

Most comprehension bugs aren't syntax errors — they're readability disasters, silent memory blowups, and misplaced filters that quietly drop or duplicate data. Knowing the failure modes is what turns a beginner one-liner into production code.

This post is the field guide to what goes wrong.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• the unreadable nested one-liner trap
• building a giant list when you needed a stream
• if-filter vs if/else confusion
• side effects that don't belong
• the late-binding closure gotcha
• how to know when to just write a loop

Learn the failure modes before they ship. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 1. The unreadable mega-comprehension
slides.body: Stacking two or three for-clauses with filters and a ternary into one line wins nothing. It's slower to read than the loop it replaced and impossible to debug line by line. If you can't grasp it in one glance, that's the signal to unfold it.

slides.title: Too dense -> unfold it
2. Building a list you'll only stream
slides.body: A list comprehension materializes every element in memory at once. If you only iterate the result once — say, feeding it to sum() or any() — you've built a giant throwaway list for nothing. A generator expression streams items lazily and uses near-constant memory.

slides.title: List vs generator memory
slides.code:
# wasteful: builds a 10M-element list just to sum it
total = sum([n * n for n in range(10_000_000)])

# lean: generator streams one value at a time
total = sum(n * n for n in range(10_000_000))
# same answer, a fraction of the memory

slides.title: 3. Filter vs ternary mix-up
slides.body: Put 'if' at the end and it FILTERS (drops items). Put 'if/else' before the for and it TRANSFORMS (keeps all). Confusing the two silently changes your output length — you either lose rows you meant to keep or keep rows you meant to drop. No error, just wrong data.

slides.title: Same words, different result
slides.code:
nums = range(5)

filtered = [n for n in nums if n % 2]          # drops evens
print(filtered)            # [1, 3]

mapped = [n if n % 2 else 0 for n in nums]     # keeps all
print(mapped)              # [0, 1, 0, 3, 0]

slides.title: 4. Side effects don't belong
slides.code:
# WRONG: a list of None, just to print
[print(x) for x in items]      # builds [None, None, ...]

# RIGHT: a plain loop expresses 'do, don't collect'
for x in items:
    print(x)

slides.title: 5. The late-binding closure trap
slides.body: Build a list of lambdas in a comprehension and they all capture the SAME loop variable by reference — so every one sees its final value. The fix is a default-argument binding that snapshots the value at creation time. This bites people building callbacks or handlers in a loop.

slides.title: Capture the value, not the variable
slides.code:
# BUG: all funcs return 2 (the last i)
funcs = [lambda: i for i in range(3)]
print([f() for f in funcs])    # [2, 2, 2]

# FIX: bind i now via a default arg
funcs = [lambda i=i: i for i in range(3)]
print([f() for f in funcs])    # [0, 1, 2]

slides.title: Stay out of trouble
bullets: One glance to read, or unfold it
Streaming once? use a generator ()
'if' at end filters; 'if/else' up front maps
No side effects inside — collect only
Bind loop vars with i=i in lambdas

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 18.
slides.body: Next up in the series: building on Python's data toolkit with the next core skill.

19-1.caption:
A decorator isn't magic syntax — it's a function that takes a function and hands you back a new one. 🎁

A decorator is a callable that wraps another callable to add behavior — logging, timing, caching, access checks — without touching the original function's body. The @ symbol is just sugar for 'pass this function through that wrapper and rebind the name.'

This post is the map: what a decorator actually IS, before you learn why it matters or how the closure plumbing works.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• what a decorator really means
• why functions being objects makes it possible
• what the @ symbol desugars to
• wrapping vs replacing behavior
• the one mental model to keep
• where you've already seen them

Get the shape in your head and the @ stops looking like magic. Save it. 👇

slides.title: What a decorator is
slides.body: A decorator is a function that takes another function as input and returns a new function that usually adds behavior around the original. You don't edit the original code — you wrap it. The @name line above a function says 'feed this function to that wrapper and keep the result under the same name.'

slides.title: Functions are objects
slides.body: Decorators are only possible because Python treats functions as first-class objects. You can assign a function to a variable, pass it as an argument, return it from another function, and store it in a list. A decorator simply exploits all four of those abilities at once.

slides.title: The @ is just sugar
Wrap, don't rewrite
slides.body: The original function still exists unchanged inside the wrapper. The decorator's job is to run some code before or after calling it, or to decide whether to call it at all. The caller sees the same name and usually the same signature — they just get extra behavior for free.

slides.title: A real, minimal decorator
The mental model
bullets: A decorator is function in, function out
@ rebinds the name to the wrapper
The original is wrapped, not edited
The wrapper decides if/when to call it
Behavior is added, signature is kept

slides.title: You've already used them
slides.body: If you've written @staticmethod, @property, @app.route, or @pytest.fixture, you've used decorators. Frameworks lean on them heavily because they let you attach behavior — routing, caching, validation — declaratively, right above the function it applies to.

slides.title: Thinking @ is special syntax
slides.body: Beginners treat @ as a mysterious keyword with its own rules. It isn't. It's pure shorthand for 'func = decorator(func)'. Once you see that the @ line is just an assignment in disguise, every decorator — even the parameterized ones — reduces to ordinary function calls you can trace by hand.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 20.
