slides.body: Next: why decorators are how Python keeps cross-cutting concerns out of your business logic.

19-2.caption:
Logging, timing, retries, auth checks — none of that is your function's actual job, yet it ends up tangled inside it. Decorators pull it back out. ✨

Decorators matter because they isolate cross-cutting concerns: the behavior that many functions need but none of them are really about. Instead of copy-pasting a timer or a permission check into twenty functions, you write it once and apply it with one line — keeping each function focused on the one thing it exists to do.

This post is the 'so what': where decorators earn their keep in real code.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• what a cross-cutting concern is
• the copy-paste problem they solve
• how they keep business logic clean
• why frameworks are built on them
• the DRY and single-responsibility win
• when a plain function is better

This is the difference between layered code and a tangle. Save it. 👇

slides.title: They isolate cross-cutting concerns
slides.body: Some behavior cuts across many functions but belongs to none of them: logging, timing, caching, retries, auth. Bake it into each function and you've smeared one concern across the codebase. A decorator captures it once and layers it on top, leaving each function about its own job.

slides.title: The copy-paste problem
slides.code:
def load_user(id):
    start = time.time()          # timing noise
    result = db.get(id)
    log(f"took {time.time()-start}s")
    return result

def load_order(id):
    start = time.time()          # SAME noise again
    result = db.get(id)
    log(f"took {time.time()-start}s")
    return result

slides.title: The same code, decorated
Business logic stays readable
slides.body: The decorated version says exactly what each function does and nothing else. The timing, the logging, the retry policy — all the supporting machinery — moves to the decorator. A reader scanning load_user sees a database call, not a stopwatch. That clarity scales as the cross-cutting needs pile up.

slides.title: Frameworks are built on them
slides.body: Flask routes a URL with @app.route, pytest registers fixtures with @fixture, Django caches views with @cache_page. These libraries expose their power as decorators because it lets you opt into behavior declaratively — right above the function — without inheriting a base class or editing config.

slides.title: What they buy you
bullets: DRY: write the concern once
Single responsibility per function
Add or remove behavior in one line
Composable: stack several at once
Reusable across the whole codebase

left.title: Reach for a decorator
items: Want it declarative at def site

slides.title: Hiding important logic in a decorator
slides.body: The power to make behavior invisible is also the trap. If a decorator silently swallows exceptions, mutates arguments, or changes return types, readers can't see it at the call site. Use decorators for concerns that are genuinely orthogonal — not to smuggle core logic out of sight where it surprises people.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 20.
slides.body: Next: under the hood — closures, *args/**kwargs, and exactly how the wrapper gets built.

19-3.caption:
Decorators feel like magic until you watch one get built — then closures, *args, and the @ rewrite stop being mysterious. 🔍

Under the hood a decorator is a higher-order function that returns a closure. The inner wrapper captures the original function, accepts arbitrary arguments with *args/**kwargs, does its extra work, and forwards the call. functools.wraps then copies the metadata over so the wrapper doesn't lie about its identity.

This post is the engine room.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• the higher-order function at the core
• why the wrapper needs *args/**kwargs
• how the closure captures the original
• what functools.wraps fixes
• how stacked decorators apply
• the exact order they run in

See the machine and the @ becomes obvious. Save it. 👇

slides.title: A function that returns a function
slides.body: A decorator is a higher-order function: it takes a function and returns a function. Inside it you define a nested wrapper, and you return the wrapper — not the result of calling it. The returned wrapper is what the name gets rebound to, so calling the decorated name actually runs the wrapper.

slides.title: The standard skeleton
Why *args and **kwargs
slides.body: A general decorator can't know how many arguments the wrapped function takes. By declaring wrapper(*args, **kwargs) it accepts any call shape, then forwards everything unchanged with func(*args, **kwargs). Without this, your decorator would only work on functions with one fixed signature.

slides.title: The closure captures func
slides.body: When decorator(func) returns, the wrapper still needs func to do its job. It survives because wrapper is a closure: it closes over the func variable from the enclosing scope. Each decorated function gets its own wrapper holding its own captured func — that's how one decorator serves many functions.

slides.title: Tracing one call
What functools.wraps fixes
slides.body: A naked wrapper replaces the original, so func.__name__ becomes 'wrapper' and the docstring vanishes. functools.wraps copies __name__, __doc__, and other metadata from the original onto the wrapper. Skip it and debuggers, help(), and introspection all see the wrapper instead of your real function.

slides.title: Stacking applies bottom-up
slides.code:
@a
@b
def f(): ...

# desugars to:
f = a(b(f))

# b wraps f first, then a wraps that.
# At call time a runs OUTERMOST, b inner.

slides.title: Confusing apply order with run order
slides.body: Stacked decorators are APPLIED bottom-up (the one nearest the def wraps first) but RUN top-down (the topmost executes its before-code first). People reverse these and get confused about why the outer decorator sees the result of the inner one. Read @a @b def f as f = a(b(f)) and the order is unambiguous.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 20.
slides.body: Next: real decorators you'll actually write — timing, caching, retry, and one that takes arguments.

19-4.caption:
Theory's done. Here are decorators you'll actually paste into real projects — a timer, a memo cache, a retry, and one that takes its own arguments. 🛠️

Every snippet here is runnable and follows the functools.wraps skeleton from the last post. We build up from the simplest wrapper to the trickiest case: a decorator factory — a function that returns a decorator so you can write @retry(times=3).

This post is code-heavy. Read it with a REPL open.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• a clean timing decorator
• a memoization cache from scratch
• a retry-on-failure decorator
• a decorator that takes arguments
• how the extra layer of nesting works
• the stdlib version you'd really use

Copy, run, and tweak each one. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 1. Timing decorator
2. Memoization cache
3. Retry on failure
4. A decorator with arguments
Three layers, not two
slides.body: A parameterized decorator needs an extra layer. The outer function takes the arguments and returns a decorator; that decorator takes the function and returns the wrapper. @retry(times=5) calls retry first, which returns the actual decorator that then wraps flaky. Three nested functions, three jobs.

slides.title: 5. The stdlib version
Patterns to remember
bullets: Always wrap with @functools.wraps
Use *args, **kwargs to stay general
State (like a cache) lives in the closure
Args needed? add a third layer
Check stdlib first: lru_cache, cached_property

slides.title: Forgetting the extra layer
slides.body: The classic bug: writing @retry(times=3) but defining retry as a normal two-layer decorator. Python calls retry(3) expecting it to RETURN a decorator, but it tries to treat 3 as the function to wrap and crashes. If the @ line has parentheses, your decorator needs three layers, not two.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 20.
slides.body: Next: the decorator traps that bite everyone — lost metadata, shared mutable state, and decorating methods.

19-5.caption:
Decorators are easy to write and easy to get subtly wrong. Here are the traps that bite everyone — and the one-line fixes. ⚠️

Most decorator bugs aren't about syntax; they're about identity and timing. You lose the function's name, you accidentally share state across calls, you evaluate arguments once instead of every time, or you forget that methods get a self the wrapper has to pass along.

This post is the field guide to those failure modes.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• forgetting functools.wraps
• the parentheses-vs-no-parentheses mix-up
• shared mutable default state
• evaluating args at decoration time
• decorating methods and self
• swallowing exceptions silently

Know these and your decorators stop lying to you. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 1. Forgetting functools.wraps
slides.body: Without @functools.wraps, the wrapper overwrites the original's identity. func.__name__ becomes 'wrapper', the docstring disappears, and help() and debuggers all report the wrong thing. It's invisible until something introspects the function — then it breaks confusingly.

slides.title: wraps: before and after
2. The parentheses mix-up
slides.body: @retry and @retry() are not the same. A plain decorator is used as @retry. A decorator factory that takes arguments is used as @retry() or @retry(3). Use one as the other and you get a cryptic error — either a function where a decorator was expected, or a decorator applied to None.

slides.title: Same trap, in code
3. Accidentally shared state
slides.body: A cache or counter defined in the decorator's scope is shared by every call to that one wrapped function — usually what you want for memoization, but a surprise if you expected per-call state. And a dict cache never evicts, so it can leak memory unbounded on long-running processes.

slides.title: 4. Decorating methods
slides.body: Methods receive self as their first argument. As long as your wrapper uses (*args, **kwargs) and forwards them, self rides along automatically and the decorator just works. The trap is hardcoding wrapper(arg) with a fixed signature — then self lands in the wrong slot and everything breaks.

slides.title: Method-safe wrapper
5. Silently swallowing errors
slides.body: A retry or guard decorator that catches Exception and returns None on failure hides real bugs. The call site looks like it succeeded but got nothing. Either re-raise after exhausting retries, or narrow the except to the specific error you mean to handle — never blanket-catch and stay quiet.

slides.title: The pre-flight checklist
bullets: @functools.wraps on every wrapper
Match @deco vs @deco() to the layers
Know if your state is shared or per-call
Use *args/**kwargs so methods work
Re-raise or narrow — don't swallow
Prefer stdlib decorators when they fit

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 20.
slides.body: Day 19 wrapped. Next up: a fresh Python concept — generators and lazy evaluation.

20-1.caption:
A class isn't a magic keyword — it's a blueprint that says 'here's what this kind of thing knows and what it can do.' 🧱

A class bundles data (attributes) and behavior (methods) into one named type. An object is a concrete thing built from that blueprint, with its own copy of the data. That's the whole idea of object-oriented programming: model your problem as interacting things, not loose variables and functions.

This post is the map: what a class and an object actually ARE before you learn why they matter or how to wield them.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• what a class vs an object really is
• attributes vs methods
• why self is not magic
• the four pillars of OOP, named
• the blueprint-vs-house mental model
• when OOP is the right tool

Get the shape in your head and the syntax is just filling in the blueprint. Save it. 👇

slides.title: What a class is
slides.body: A class is a blueprint for a kind of thing. It declares what data each instance will hold (attributes) and what it can do (methods), all under one name. The class itself isn't a thing — it's the template. You don't live in the blueprint; you live in the house built from it.

slides.title: Class vs object
slides.body: An object (or instance) is a concrete thing made from a class. One class, many objects — each with its own copy of the data. 'Dog' is the class; your dog Rex and my dog Fido are two objects of that class, each with a different name and age but the same behaviors.

slides.title: A class, minimal
slides.code:
class Dog:
    def __init__(self, name, age):
        self.name = name      # attribute
        self.age = age        # attribute

    def bark(self):           # method
        return f"{self.name} says woof"

rex = Dog("Rex", 3)           # an object
print(rex.bark())             # Rex says woof

slides.title: Attributes vs methods
slides.body: Attributes are the data an object holds — its state, like name and age. Methods are functions defined inside the class — the behavior, like bark(). State is what an object IS at a moment; behavior is what it can DO. A class glues the two together so they travel as one unit.

slides.title: self is just 'this object'
slides.body: Every method's first parameter is self — a reference to the specific object the method was called on. When you write rex.bark(), Python passes rex in as self automatically. self isn't a keyword; it's a convention for saying 'the instance I'm working on right now.'

slides.title: It's modeling, not ceremony
slides.body: OOP is a way to model your problem as interacting things with state and behavior. A bank account that knows its balance and can deposit; a user that has an email and can log in. The class draws the boundary around what belongs together so the rest of the program talks to one tidy object.

slides.title: The mental model
bullets: Class = blueprint, object = the thing
Attributes = data (state)
Methods = behavior (actions)
self = 'this particular object'
One class makes many objects

slides.title: Thinking a class is just a fancy dict
slides.body: Newcomers see a class as a container for variables and miss the point: a class binds behavior to that data. A dict holds values; a class holds values AND the operations that keep them valid. That coupling — guarding state with the methods allowed to change it — is what a class buys you over a bag of variables.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 21.
slides.body: Next: why OOP matters — what bundling state and behavior actually buys you in real codebases.

20-2.caption:
Functions on loose dictionaries scale until they don't. The moment your data and the rules that govern it drift apart, bugs move in. 🔒

OOP matters because it keeps related data and the code that operates on it in one place, behind a controlled interface. That means fewer invalid states, easier reuse through inheritance, and the ability to swap implementations without touching callers. It's how you tame complexity as a program grows.

This post is the 'so what': where classes actually pay off in real code.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• why bundling state + behavior reduces bugs
• how encapsulation protects invariants
• why inheritance beats copy-paste
• polymorphism: code that doesn't care about type
• when a plain function is still better
• where you'll reach for OOP daily

This is what separates a maintainable codebase from a pile of helpers. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Data and rules stay together
slides.body: When the balance lives in one place and the only way to change it is deposit() and withdraw(), the rules can't be bypassed. Scatter the same logic across free functions and a stray line somewhere sets balance to a negative number. Bundling state with its guardian methods is how you keep data valid.

slides.title: Loose data vs an object
Encapsulation protects invariants
slides.body: An invariant is a truth that must always hold — balance never negative, a list never has duplicates. Encapsulation hides the raw data and exposes only methods that preserve those truths. Callers can't put the object into an illegal state because they never touch the internals directly.

slides.title: Inheritance beats copy-paste
slides.body: When ten classes share most of their behavior, inheritance lets you write the shared part once in a base class and specialize only what differs. Fix a bug in the base and every subclass benefits. The alternative — copying the logic into each class — means fixing the same bug ten times and missing one.

slides.title: Polymorphism: ignore the type
slides.body: If every shape has an .area() method, a function can total the area of a list of shapes without knowing whether each is a Circle or a Square. Add a Triangle later and the function needs no change. Code that talks to an interface, not a concrete type, bends instead of breaking as requirements grow.

slides.title: One loop, many types
slides.code:
class Circle:
    def __init__(self, r): self.r = r
    def area(self): return 3.14159 * self.r ** 2

class Square:
    def __init__(self, s): self.s = s
    def area(self): return self.s ** 2

shapes = [Circle(2), Square(3)]
print(sum(s.area() for s in shapes))  # doesn't care which

slides.title: When NOT to use a class
Where you'll reach for OOP
bullets: Models: User, Order, Account
GUI widgets and game entities
Plugins sharing a base interface
ML estimators (fit / predict)
Anything with state + lifecycle

slides.title: Wrapping everything in a class out of habit
slides.body: OOP is a tool, not a religion. A module of pure functions is often clearer than a class with no real state. If your 'class' has one method and no attributes worth protecting, it wanted to be a function. Reach for a class when data and behavior genuinely belong together — not because Java taught you to.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 21.
slides.body: Next: under the hood — how Python actually creates objects, resolves attributes, and finds methods.

20-3.caption:
Classes feel like magic until you watch Python build an object step by step — then __init__, self, and the way method lookups travel up the inheritance chain all click. 🔍

Under the hood, an object is a namespace plus a pointer to its class. Attribute access follows a defined search order: instance first, then the class, then its parents. Knowing that order explains where your data actually lives and why a method you didn't define still runs.

This post is the engine room.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• what __init__ really does (and doesn't)
• how self gets passed automatically
• instance vs class attributes
• the attribute lookup order (the MRO)
• how super() calls the parent
• why methods are just functions in the class

See the machine and the syntax becomes obvious. Save it. 👇

slides.title: __init__ initializes, it doesn't create
slides.body: Calling Dog('Rex') does two things: Python creates a blank object, then runs __init__ on it to set up its attributes. __init__ is the initializer, not the 'constructor' that allocates the object — that's __new__, which you rarely touch. By the time __init__ runs, self already exists; your job is to fill it in.

slides.title: self is passed for you
slides.body: rex.bark() is shorthand. Python rewrites it as Dog.bark(rex) — it finds bark on the class and passes the instance in as the first argument, self. That's why every method declares self: it's the slot that receives the object the method was called on. Forget it and Python complains it got an extra positional argument.

slides.title: rex.bark() == Dog.bark(rex)
slides.code:
class Dog:
    def __init__(self, name): self.name = name
    def bark(self): return f"{self.name} woofs"

rex = Dog("Rex")
print(rex.bark())          # Rex woofs
print(Dog.bark(rex))       # Rex woofs  (the same call)

slides.title: Instance vs class attributes
slides.body: An attribute set on self belongs to that one object. An attribute set in the class body is shared by every instance. Lookup checks the instance first, then falls back to the class. This is why a class-level mutable default (like a shared list) is a classic trap — every object sees the same one.

slides.title: Shared vs per-object state
slides.code:
class Dog:
    species = "Canis familiaris"   # class attr: shared
    def __init__(self, name):
        self.name = name             # instance attr: per-object

a, b = Dog("Rex"), Dog("Fido")
print(a.species, b.species)        # both: Canis familiaris
print(a.name, b.name)              # Rex Fido (different)

slides.title: Attribute lookup climbs the chain
slides.body: Ask for rex.x and Python searches in order: the instance's own namespace, then its class, then each parent class up the inheritance chain. The first match wins. This ordered search — the Method Resolution Order, or MRO — is exactly why a subclass can use a method it never defined: it's found on a parent.

slides.title: super() calls the parent
The rules that explain it all
bullets: __init__ fills in an already-made object
rex.m() becomes Class.m(rex)
Lookup: instance -> class -> parents
First match in the MRO wins
super() delegates to the parent

slides.title: Mutable class attributes shared by all
slides.body: Define `tags = []` in the class body and every instance shares ONE list — append to one object's tags and they all change. The fix is to create per-instance state inside __init__ with self.tags = []. This bug is silent until two objects mysteriously share data, and it traces straight back to the instance-then-class lookup order.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 21.
slides.body: Next: hands-on — build a real class with inheritance, properties, and dunder methods, in code.

20-4.caption:
Reading about classes is fine. Building one — with a constructor, inheritance, a property, and dunder methods — is what makes OOP automatic. 💻

This post is deliberately code-heavy. We'll define a base class, subclass it with super(), add a computed property that looks like an attribute, make objects print nicely with __repr__, and support value equality. Then we'll add an alternate constructor with @classmethod and let @dataclass generate the boilerplate for us. Each snippet is short, real, and runnable — type it, run it, then change one thing and watch what happens.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• a class with __init__ and methods
• inheritance and super()
• @property for computed attributes
• __repr__ and __eq__ dunder methods
• @classmethod as an alternate constructor
• @dataclass: the boilerplate killer

Run every snippet once and classes stop being syntax you look up. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 1. A class with state + behavior
2. Inheritance with super()
3. @property: a computed attribute
slides.code:
class Circle:
    def __init__(self, r): self.r = r

    @property
    def area(self):                 # called like an attribute
        return 3.14159 * self.r ** 2

c = Circle(2)
print(c.area)                       # 12.56636  (no parens!)

slides.title: 4. __repr__ and __eq__ dunders
5. @classmethod: alternate constructor
Patterns to memorize
bullets: __init__(self, ...) — set up state
super().__init__() — reuse parent
@property — method that reads like data
__repr__ — a useful print()
@classmethod — alternate constructors

slides.title: Dunders hook into Python's syntax
slides.body: Methods named with double underscores let your objects plug into built-in operations. __repr__ powers print() and the REPL, __eq__ powers ==, __len__ powers len(), __add__ powers +. Implement the dunder and your object behaves like a native type — that's how Python makes custom classes feel first-class.

slides.title: 6. @dataclass kills boilerplate
slides.code:
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class Point:
    x: int
    y: int                          # __init__, __repr__, __eq__
                                    # all generated for you
p = Point(1, 2)
print(p)                            # Point(x=1, y=2)
print(p == Point(1, 2))             # True

slides.title: Hand-writing what @dataclass generates
slides.body: Typing __init__, __repr__, and __eq__ by hand for a class that's mostly data is wasted effort and a bug surface — forget a field in __eq__ and equality lies. For data-holding classes, @dataclass generates all of it correctly from the field annotations. Reach for it before you write your fourth boilerplate __init__.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 21.
slides.body: Next: the OOP mistakes that bite everyone — and how to dodge them.

20-5.caption:
Classes are powerful tools. The same flexibility that lets you model anything will cut you if you share mutable state, inherit too deeply, or forget self. 🩹

Most OOP bugs aren't syntax errors — they're shared mutable defaults, broken equality, inheritance tangles, and god-classes that know too much. Knowing the failure modes is what turns a beginner's class into code that survives a growing codebase.

This post is the field guide to what goes wrong.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• the shared mutable class-attribute trap
• forgetting self (and the errors it throws)
• the mutable default argument bug
• inheritance overused: prefer composition
• the god-class that does everything
• why == breaks without __eq__

Learn the failure modes before they ship. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 1. Mutable class attribute, shared by all
slides.body: Putting a list or dict in the class body makes ONE object shared across every instance. Append to one object's list and every other object sees the change. It looks like per-object state but isn't. Per-instance data must be created inside __init__ on self, not declared in the class body.

slides.title: The shared-state bug and fix
2. Forgetting self
slides.body: Leave self out of a method signature and Python passes the instance into the wrong slot, raising a TypeError about arguments. Access an attribute as just `balance` instead of `self.balance` and you get a NameError or read the wrong variable. self is mandatory — it's how a method reaches the object it belongs to.

slides.title: self is not optional
slides.code:
class Counter:
    def __init__(self): self.n = 0
    def bump(self):            # forgetting self here...
        self.n += 1            # ...breaks this line
        return self.n

c = Counter()
print(c.bump())                # 1
# def bump(): -> TypeError: takes 0 args but 1 given

slides.title: 3. Mutable default argument
slides.body: A default like def __init__(self, items=[]) is evaluated ONCE when the function is defined, so every call that omits items shares the same list. Two 'empty' objects end up sharing state. The fix is the None sentinel: default to None, then create a fresh list inside the body.

slides.title: The None-sentinel fix
4. Inheritance where composition fits
slides.body: Deep inheritance trees are brittle: a change in a base ripples through every subclass, and 'is-a' relationships get forced where 'has-a' is the truth. A Car isn't a kind of Engine — it HAS one. Prefer composition: hold an object as an attribute and delegate to it, rather than inheriting just to reuse code.

slides.title: 5. The god-class
slides.body: A class that holds half the program's state and has thirty methods isn't object-oriented — it's a procedural blob wearing a class. It's hard to test, impossible to reuse, and every change risks breaking something unrelated. Split responsibilities: each class should do one thing and know only what it needs.

slides.title: 6. == breaks without __eq__
Stay out of trouble
bullets: Per-object state goes in __init__ on self
Never default an arg to [] or {}
Always declare self first
Favor composition over deep inheritance
Define __eq__ if value-equality matters

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 21.
slides.body: Next up in the series: building on Python's OOP foundation with the next core skill.

21-1.caption:
A list hands you everything up front. A generator hands you one value at a time, only when you ask. 🌀

An iterator is any object that knows how to produce a next value on demand and signal when it's done. A generator is the easiest way to build one: write a normal-looking function, use yield instead of return, and Python turns it into a lazy stream you can loop over.

This post is the map — what these words actually mean — before you learn why they matter or how the protocol works under the hood.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• what an iterable, iterator, and generator each are
• how yield differs from return
• why "lazy" means values on demand
• where you've already used iterators
• the one mental model to keep

Get the vocabulary straight and the rest clicks. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Iterable vs iterator
slides.body: An iterable is anything you can loop over — a list, string, dict, or file. An iterator is the thing doing the work: it remembers where it is and produces the next value when asked. Calling iter() on an iterable gives you its iterator; calling next() on that iterator pulls one value.

slides.title: What a generator is
slides.body: A generator is an iterator you create by writing a function with yield instead of return. Calling that function doesn't run the body — it hands back a generator object. Each time you ask for the next value, the body runs up to the next yield, pauses there, and remembers everything for next time.

slides.title: yield vs return
slides.code:
def with_return():
    return [1, 2, 3]       # builds the whole list now

def with_yield():
    yield 1                 # pauses here
    yield 2                 # resumes here next call
    yield 3

print(with_return())        # [1, 2, 3]
print(with_yield())         # <generator object ...>

slides.title: Lazy means on demand
slides.body: A list computes and stores every element immediately. A generator computes nothing until you pull a value, then computes just that one and forgets it. This is why a generator can represent an infinite or huge sequence — the numbers don't exist until the moment you ask for each.

slides.title: Looping is just next() in disguise
slides.code:
g = (n * n for n in range(3))

# a for loop does this for you:
print(next(g))   # 0
print(next(g))   # 1
print(next(g))   # 4
print(next(g))   # raises StopIteration

slides.title: You've already used them
slides.body: range, enumerate, zip, map, filter, and open() file objects are all iterators or produce them. Every for loop you've written quietly calls iter() then next() over and over until StopIteration. You've been consuming this protocol since your first loop — now you'll build it on purpose.

slides.title: The mental model
bullets: Iterable = can be looped over
Iterator = remembers its position
Generator = easy way to make an iterator
yield pauses; return ends
Values appear one at a time, on demand

right.title: Generator
items: One in memory at a time

slides.title: Thinking a generator is a list
slides.body: A generator object isn't a sequence of values — it's a recipe for producing them. You can't index it, take its len(), or loop over it twice. Once consumed, it's exhausted and silently yields nothing. Confusing the recipe for the dish is the single most common beginner trap here.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 22.
slides.body: Next: why generators matter — memory, streaming, and pipelines that never load everything at once.

21-2.caption:
Loading a 10GB log file into a list will crash your process. Streaming it through a generator uses a few kilobytes. 💾

Generators matter because they decouple how much data you process from how much memory you hold. By producing one value at a time, they let you handle infinite streams, huge files, and chained transformations without ever materializing the whole thing.

This post is the "so what" — where laziness turns into real wins in production code.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• constant memory vs loading everything
• processing data bigger than RAM
• representing infinite sequences
• building composable pipelines
• short-circuiting work you never needed
• when a plain list is still the right call

This is the difference between code that scales and code that OOMs. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Constant memory footprint
slides.body: A list of a million rows holds a million objects in RAM at once. A generator holds one. When you only need to look at each item briefly — sum it, filter it, write it out — there's no reason to keep them all. Generators turn memory from O(n) into O(1).

slides.title: The memory difference
slides.code:
# list: every line in RAM at once
lines = [line for line in open("huge.log")]
total = sum(len(l) for l in lines)

# generator: one line at a time
lines = (line for line in open("huge.log"))
total = sum(len(l) for l in lines)   # same result, tiny memory

slides.title: Data bigger than RAM
slides.body: You can iterate a file or database cursor that's larger than your machine's memory because you never hold more than the current chunk. The classic example is processing a multi-gigabyte CSV row by row — impossible to load fully, trivial to stream.

slides.title: Infinite sequences are fine
slides.code:
def naturals():
    n = 0
    while True:        # never ends
        yield n
        n += 1

g = naturals()
print(next(g), next(g), next(g))   # 0 1 2
# the loop only runs as far as you pull

slides.title: Pipelines compose cleanly
slides.body: Because each stage is itself a generator, you can chain them: read, filter, transform, aggregate. Nothing runs until the final consumer pulls a value, and then exactly one item flows through the whole chain at a time. You get the readability of separate steps with the efficiency of a single pass.

slides.title: Short-circuit for free
slides.body: Pair a generator with next(), any(), or a break and you stop the moment you've found what you need. Searching for the first error line in a giant file reads only up to that line — the rest is never computed. A pre-built list would have done all the work regardless.

slides.title: What they buy you
bullets: O(1) memory for O(n) data
Handle streams larger than RAM
Model infinite sequences safely
Compose multi-stage pipelines
Stop early without wasted work

slides.title: Premature laziness
slides.body: Generators aren't free wins everywhere. If you need random access, multiple passes, or the length, a list is simpler and faster. Reaching for a generator on a 10-item list adds complexity with zero benefit. The payoff appears with large, streamed, or infinite data — match the tool to the scale.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 22.
slides.body: Next: under the hood — the iterator protocol, what next() really does, and how yield pauses a function.

21-3.caption:
A for loop looks simple, but underneath it's calling iter() once and next() forever — until something raises StopIteration. 🔍

Under the hood, iteration is a protocol: __iter__ returns an iterator, __next__ produces the next value or raises StopIteration to signal the end. Generators implement this protocol for free — yield suspends the function, freezing its local state, and resumes it exactly where it left off when you ask again.

This post is the engine room.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• the __iter__ / __next__ protocol
• what a for loop desugars into
• how StopIteration ends the loop
• how yield freezes and resumes state
• building an iterator the manual way
• generator expressions vs functions

See the machine and the loop stops being magic. Save it. 👇

slides.title: The iterator protocol
slides.body: Two dunder methods define iteration. __iter__ returns an iterator object — often self. __next__ returns the next value, or raises StopIteration when there's nothing left. Anything implementing both is a valid iterator and works in any for loop, comprehension, or call to next().

slides.title: What a for loop really is
StopIteration ends it
slides.body: An iterator signals exhaustion by raising StopIteration, not by returning a sentinel. The for loop catches that exception and exits quietly. In a generator, simply reaching the end of the function body (or a bare return) raises StopIteration for you — you never raise it by hand.

slides.title: yield freezes the frame
slides.body: When execution hits yield, Python suspends the function: the instruction pointer, local variables, and the call stack frame are all kept alive. The value after yield is handed to the caller. On the next next() call, the function resumes on the line after yield with all that state intact.

slides.title: Building an iterator by hand
The same thing as a generator
slides.code:
def countdown(n):
    while n > 0:
        yield n
        n -= 1

print(list(countdown(3)))   # [3, 2, 1]
# yield handles __iter__, __next__, and
# StopIteration automatically

slides.title: Two ways to write generators
bullets: Function with yield: full logic, loops, conditions
Expression (x for x in it): one-line, comprehension-style
Both return generator objects
Both are single-use iterators
Use parens for genexp, brackets for list comp

slides.title: Forgetting __iter__ returns the iterator
slides.body: A common bug: writing __next__ but no __iter__, or having __iter__ return something without __next__. The for loop calls iter() first, so both must line up. With a generator function none of this applies — yield wires up the entire protocol, which is exactly why it's the preferred approach.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 22.
slides.body: Next: a hands-on code example — building a real lazy data pipeline end to end.

21-4.caption:
Let's build something real: a lazy log-processing pipeline that reads a huge file, filters it, parses it, and aggregates — all in constant memory. 🛠️

This post is code-first. We'll write each stage as a generator, chain them together, and watch a single item flow through the whole pipeline at a time. No intermediate lists, no loading the file, no wasted work.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• a generator that streams file lines
• a filter stage that keeps only errors
• a parse stage that extracts fields
• chaining stages into one lazy pipeline
• consuming it with sum and yield from
• the full runnable script

Copy it, run it on your own logs, and feel the difference. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 1. Stream the lines
slides.code:
def read_lines(path):
    with open(path) as f:
        for line in f:          # file objects are iterators
            yield line.rstrip("\n")

# nothing is read until you iterate the result

slides.title: 2. Filter to error lines
3. Parse each line
4. Chain and consume
slides.code:
lines   = read_lines("app.log")
errors  = only_errors(lines)
records = parse(errors)

# only here does data actually flow:
count = sum(1 for _ in records)
print(f"{count} errors")

slides.title: Pull, not push
slides.body: The consumer drives everything. When sum asks for one record, parse asks only_errors for one line, which asks read_lines for one line off disk. One item threads through all four stages, then the next. No stage runs ahead or buffers — that's the laziness paying off.

slides.title: 5. Delegate with yield from
Pipeline rules of thumb
bullets: Each stage takes an iterable, yields an iterable
Keep stages small and single-purpose
Nothing runs until a consumer pulls
Use yield from to flatten nested generators
The final consumer (sum, list, loop) drives it

slides.title: The whole script
Consuming the generator twice
slides.body: After you loop over records once, it's empty — generators don't rewind. If you need the count and the data, either re-create the pipeline or materialize once with list(). The bug shows up as a second loop that silently does nothing because the stream is already exhausted.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 22.
slides.body: Next: the common mistakes — exhaustion, lazy-closure bugs, and silent infinite loops.

21-5.caption:
Generators are elegant until they bite you: empty on the second loop, leaking late-binding bugs, or looping forever with no exit. ⚠️

This post is the failure-mode catalog. Each of these traps comes straight from real code, and each has a clean fix once you understand why the generator behaves the way it does.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• why a generator is empty the second time
• the late-binding closure trap
• forgetting an exit on an infinite generator
• calling len() or indexing a generator
• mixing up genexp parens and list brackets
• exceptions that vanish inside generators

Learn the traps now so you don't debug them at 2am later. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Exhausted after one pass
slides.body: A generator yields each value exactly once. After a loop, sum, or list() drains it, every later iteration sees nothing — no error, just empty. This silently breaks code that assumes it can reuse the sequence. If you need it twice, store it in a list or rebuild the generator.

slides.title: The double-consume bug
slides.code:
nums = (n for n in range(3))

print(list(nums))   # [0, 1, 2]
print(list(nums))   # []  <- already exhausted!

# fix: materialize if you need it twice
nums = list(range(3))

slides.title: Late-binding closure trap
slides.body: Generators capture variables by reference, not value. Build a list of generators in a loop and they may all see the loop variable's final value. The body doesn't run until you iterate — by then the loop is over. Bind the value as a default argument or with an inner function to fix it.

slides.title: Late binding in action
slides.code:
gens = [(i * x for x in range(2)) for i in range(3)]
print([list(g) for g in gens])
# [[0, 0], [0, 2], [0, 4]]  <- i is captured fresh per comp

# but with shared closures over a loop var, all see the last i
funcs = []
for i in range(3):
    funcs.append(lambda: i)
print([f() for f in funcs])   # [2, 2, 2]  <- gotcha

slides.title: Infinite generator, no exit
slides.body: A while True generator never stops on its own. Feed it to list(), sorted(), sum(), or any() over a condition that's never met and your program hangs and eats memory forever. Always pair an infinite generator with a bounded consumer: itertools.islice, a break, or next() a fixed number of times.

slides.title: Bounding an infinite stream
slides.code:
from itertools import islice

def naturals():
    n = 0
    while True:
        yield n
        n += 1

# DON'T: list(naturals())  -> hangs forever
print(list(islice(naturals(), 5)))   # [0, 1, 2, 3, 4]

slides.title: Treating it like a sequence
slides.body: Generators have no len() and no indexing. len(g) raises TypeError; g[0] raises TypeError. They also don't support 'in' without consuming part of the stream. If you reach for these, you actually want a list — or use next() and itertools instead of index math.

root.question: Need the data more than once?

yes.leaf: Use a list, not a generator

no.leaf: A generator is a good fit

slides.title: Exceptions don't fire until consumed
slides.code:
def risky():
    yield 1
    raise ValueError("boom")

g = risky()        # no error yet
print(next(g))     # 1
print(next(g))     # ValueError raised HERE, on consume

slides.title: Stay out of trouble
bullets: Assume single-use; list() if you need more
Bind loop vars explicitly in closures
Always bound infinite generators
No len/index — that means use a list
Errors surface when you pull, not when you call

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 22.
slides.body: Next day, next topic — keep building your Python foundations one day at a time.

22-1.caption:
Your code spends most of its life waiting — for a network reply, a disk read, a database row. Async/await is how you stop wasting that wait. ⏳

async/await is Python's syntax for cooperative concurrency: a single thread that juggles many waiting tasks by switching whenever one of them pauses for I/O. It's not threads, it's not multiple cores. It's one worker who, instead of standing idle while the kettle boils, goes and chops the vegetables.

This post is the map: what a coroutine, the event loop, and await actually ARE before you learn why they matter or how to use them.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• what a coroutine really is
• what async and await actually do
• the event loop in one sentence
• concurrency vs parallelism
• why it's single-threaded and still fast
• when async is the right tool

Get the shape in your head and the syntax stops looking like magic. Save it. 👇

slides.title: What a coroutine is
slides.body: A coroutine is a function that can pause itself partway through and hand control back, then resume later from exactly where it stopped. You define one with 'async def'. Calling it doesn't run it — it returns a coroutine object, a paused plan of work waiting to be driven by an event loop.

slides.title: What await does
slides.body: await means 'this might take a while — pause me here and let other work run until the result is ready.' It only works inside an async function. When you await something, your coroutine yields control to the event loop instead of blocking the whole program while it waits.

slides.title: The shape of async code
slides.code:
import asyncio

async def greet(name):
    print(f"hi {name}")
    await asyncio.sleep(1)   # pause, let others run
    print(f"bye {name}")

asyncio.run(greet("Sam"))    # event loop drives it

slides.title: The event loop
slides.body: The event loop is the scheduler that runs your coroutines. It picks a task, runs it until it hits an await, parks it while it waits, and switches to another ready task. One loop, one thread, many tasks taking turns. asyncio.run() starts a loop, runs your coroutine to completion, then shuts it down.

slides.title: Single-threaded, still fast
slides.body: Async doesn't make any one task faster. It removes idle time. While task A waits 200ms for an API, the loop runs tasks B, C, and D. Stack a thousand waiting tasks and they overlap instead of queueing. That's why one async thread can handle thousands of slow connections at once.

slides.title: Coroutine vs task
slides.body: A coroutine object is inert until something drives it. A Task wraps a coroutine and schedules it on the loop to run concurrently with others. await on a coroutine runs it inline; asyncio.create_task() launches it to run alongside the current code. That distinction is the whole game of doing things at the same time.

slides.title: The mental model
bullets: async def = a pausable function
await = pause here, let others run
Event loop = the turn-taking scheduler
Concurrency = overlap, not more cores
Best when you're waiting on I/O

slides.title: Thinking async = faster everything
slides.body: Async only helps when your program waits. If your code is crunching numbers with no I/O, async adds overhead and zero speedup — the loop never gets a chance to switch because nothing pauses. For CPU-bound work you want multiprocessing or a thread pool, not coroutines. Know which bottleneck you have first.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 23.
slides.body: Next: why async matters — the throughput wins that make it worth the mental rewiring.

22-2.caption:
A blocking server handles one slow request, then the next, then the next — and your users feel every millisecond. Async lets one process serve thousands without breaking a sweat. 🚀

Async/await matters because most real software is I/O-bound: it waits on networks, disks, and databases far more than it computes. Async turns that dead waiting time into useful overlap, so you get massive throughput from a single thread without the memory cost of a thread per connection.

This post is the 'so what': where async actually pays off in production.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• why I/O-bound work dominates real apps
• how one thread beats a thread-per-request
• the latency you hide by overlapping waits
• why async powers modern web frameworks
• the cost: it's contagious and trickier
• when threads or processes win instead

This is the difference between a server that scales and one that falls over. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Real apps mostly wait
slides.body: Fetch a user, call an API, query a database, read a file — each is a wait measured in milliseconds, an eternity for a CPU. Most backends spend the bulk of their time idle, blocked on something external. Async exists to reclaim that time by running other work during every wait.

left.title: Blocking (sequential)
items: Wait A, then wait B, then C

slides.title: The throughput win, measured
slides.code:
import asyncio, time

async def task(n):
    await asyncio.sleep(1)   # pretend: slow I/O
    return n

async def main():
    start = time.perf_counter()
    # all three wait at the same time
    await asyncio.gather(task(1), task(2), task(3))
    print(f"{time.perf_counter() - start:.1f}s")  # ~1.0s

asyncio.run(main())

slides.title: One thread, thousands of clients
slides.body: The old model gives each connection its own thread, and threads cost memory and context-switching. An async server keeps thousands of connections on one thread, parking each while it waits. That's how a single process handles tens of thousands of concurrent sockets that a thread-per-request model could never afford.

slides.title: Where async lives in your stack

stages.label: Async server
stages.detail: one loop parks each on I/O

slides.title: It powers the modern web
slides.body: FastAPI, Starlette, aiohttp, and ASGI servers like Uvicorn are async-first for exactly this reason. WebSockets, server-sent events, and long-poll endpoints hold connections open for ages — feasible only when waiting is nearly free. Async is the foundation under most high-concurrency Python services today.

slides.title: The cost: async is contagious
slides.body: Once one function is async, its callers usually must be too — await only works inside async. A single blocking call (a sync DB driver, time.sleep, heavy CPU) freezes the entire loop and every other task with it. Async buys throughput but charges you in discipline: the whole call chain has to play along.

slides.title: When async earns its keep
bullets: High-concurrency web servers
Scraping or calling many APIs
Chat, websockets, streaming
Proxies and gateways
Anything dominated by waiting

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 23.
slides.body: Next: how it actually works — coroutines, the loop, and what await does under the hood.

22-3.caption:
await looks like it 'waits,' but under the hood it does the opposite — it suspends your function and hands control away so something else can run. 🔍

Under the hood, a coroutine is a resumable state machine. The event loop drives it: it calls into the coroutine, the coroutine runs until an await yields control, the loop parks it on whatever it's waiting for, and resumes it later. No threads, no preemption — every switch happens at an explicit await.

This post is the engine room.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• what 'async def' really builds
• how await yields control to the loop
• how the loop schedules and resumes tasks
• create_task vs gather vs await
• why one blocking call jams everything
• how the loop knows when I/O is ready

See the machine and the rules stop feeling arbitrary. Save it. 👇

slides.title: A coroutine is a state machine
slides.body: 'async def' doesn't define a normal function — it builds a coroutine factory. Calling it returns a coroutine object that remembers where it is. Each time the loop resumes it, it runs to the next await, saves its position and locals, and yields. It's a function you can freeze and thaw at marked points.

slides.title: await yields, the loop catches
slides.body: When you await an awaitable, your coroutine suspends and passes a 'wake me when this is done' signal up to the event loop. The loop records what you're waiting on, frees the thread, and runs other ready tasks. When your awaited thing completes, the loop resumes your coroutine right after the await.

slides.title: Inline await vs concurrent tasks
slides.code:
import asyncio

async def work(n):
    await asyncio.sleep(n)
    return n

async def main():
    # sequential: total ~3s
    a = await work(1)
    b = await work(2)

    # concurrent: total ~2s
    a, b = await asyncio.gather(work(1), work(2))

asyncio.run(main())

slides.title: Task = scheduled coroutine
slides.body: asyncio.create_task() wraps a coroutine in a Task and hands it to the loop to run concurrently, starting immediately at the next loop tick. Plain await runs the coroutine inline and waits for it. gather() schedules many as tasks and waits for all. Tasks are how you get true overlap rather than one-after-another.

slides.title: Watch the interleave
slides.code:
import asyncio

async def ticker(name, n):
    for i in range(n):
        print(name, i)
        await asyncio.sleep(0)   # yield control

async def main():
    await asyncio.gather(ticker("A", 3), ticker("B", 3))

asyncio.run(main())
# A 0 / B 0 / A 1 / B 1 / A 2 / B 2  -> they interleave

slides.title: How it watches I/O
slides.body: The loop doesn't poll in a busy spin. It hands all pending sockets to the OS via a selector (epoll on Linux, kqueue on macOS) and sleeps until the OS reports which are readable or writable. Then it wakes exactly the tasks whose I/O is ready. That OS handoff is what makes thousands of idle connections nearly free.

slides.title: One blocking call jams the loop
slides.body: The loop only switches at an await. If a coroutine calls a blocking function — time.sleep, a sync requests call, a tight CPU loop — it never yields, so the single thread is stuck and every other task starves. The fix: await async equivalents, or push blocking work to a thread with loop.run_in_executor.

slides.title: The model in one breath
bullets: Coroutine = freezable state machine
await = explicit yield point
Loop = picks, parks, resumes
Task = coroutine scheduled to overlap
Switches only at await — never preempted

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 23.
slides.body: Next: a hands-on code example — fetching many URLs concurrently with asyncio.

22-4.caption:
Let's build the canonical async win: fetch a pile of URLs all at once instead of one painful request at a time. 🛠️

This is the example that makes async click. We'll write a coroutine that fetches one URL, launch dozens concurrently with gather, and watch total time collapse from the sum of all requests to roughly the slowest single one. Real, runnable, the pattern you'll reach for constantly.

This post is code-heavy — type it out.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• a single async fetch with aiohttp
• running many fetches concurrently
• gather vs as_completed
• bounding concurrency with a semaphore
• timeouts and error handling done right
• the timing proof that it works

Copy it, run it, then make it yours. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 1. One async fetch
slides.code:
# pip install aiohttp
import aiohttp

async def fetch(session, url):
    async with session.get(url) as resp:
        resp.raise_for_status()
        return await resp.text()

slides.title: 2. Fetch many concurrently
slides.code:
import asyncio, aiohttp

async def fetch_all(urls):
    async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:
        tasks = [fetch(session, u) for u in urls]
        return await asyncio.gather(*tasks)

urls = ["https://example.com"] * 20
pages = asyncio.run(fetch_all(urls))
print(len(pages), "pages fetched")

slides.title: What gather buys you
slides.body: asyncio.gather(*tasks) schedules every fetch as a concurrent task and waits for all of them, returning results in input order. Twenty requests that each take a second don't take twenty seconds — they overlap and finish in about one. The session is shared so connections are pooled and reused.

slides.title: 3. Bound concurrency with a semaphore
slides.code:
import asyncio, aiohttp

async def fetch_capped(sem, session, url):
    async with sem:                      # at most N at once
        async with session.get(url) as r:
            return await r.text()

async def fetch_all(urls, limit=10):
    sem = asyncio.Semaphore(limit)
    async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:
        tasks = [fetch_capped(sem, session, u) for u in urls]
        return await asyncio.gather(*tasks)

slides.title: Why cap it
slides.body: Launching 10,000 requests at once will exhaust file descriptors, hammer the target server, and likely get you rate-limited or blocked. A Semaphore lets only N coroutines past the gate at a time; the rest wait their turn. You keep the concurrency win while staying a polite, stable client.

slides.title: 4. Timeouts + per-task errors
slides.code:
import asyncio, aiohttp

async def safe_fetch(session, url):
    try:
        timeout = aiohttp.ClientTimeout(total=5)
        async with session.get(url, timeout=timeout) as r:
            r.raise_for_status()
            return await r.text()
    except Exception as e:
        return f"ERR {url}: {e}"

async def main(urls):
    async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as s:
        # return_exceptions keeps one failure from killing all
        return await asyncio.gather(*(safe_fetch(s, u) for u in urls),
                                    return_exceptions=False)

slides.title: 5. Prove it's faster
slides.code:
import asyncio, time

async def slow(n):
    await asyncio.sleep(1)
    return n

async def main():
    t = time.perf_counter()
    await asyncio.gather(*(slow(i) for i in range(50)))
    print(f"50 tasks in {time.perf_counter()-t:.2f}s")

asyncio.run(main())   # ~1.0s, not 50s

slides.title: Production checklist
bullets: Reuse one ClientSession, don't make per-request
Always set a ClientTimeout
Cap concurrency with a Semaphore
Handle per-task errors explicitly
Never call blocking I/O inside a coroutine

slides.title: Forgetting to await
slides.body: Write fetch_all(urls) without await and you get a coroutine object that never runs — often with a 'coroutine was never awaited' warning and no work done. async functions are lazy: calling them only builds the plan. You must await them or schedule them as a task for anything to actually happen.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 23.
slides.body: Next: the common async mistakes that silently kill your concurrency.

22-5.caption:
Most async bugs aren't crashes — they're code that looks concurrent, runs perfectly, and is secretly as slow as the blocking version it replaced. 🐛

The async traps are sneaky: a forgotten await, a blocking call hiding in the loop, awaiting tasks one at a time when you meant to overlap them. Your program works, your tests pass, and your throughput quietly tanks. Knowing the failure modes is half of writing fast async.

This post is the field guide to what goes wrong.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• the forgotten await that does nothing
• blocking calls that freeze the whole loop
• awaiting in a loop instead of gathering
• mixing sync libraries into async code
• fire-and-forget tasks that vanish
• swallowed exceptions in gather

Dodge these six and your async actually goes fast. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 1. The forgotten await
slides.body: Calling an async function without await builds a coroutine object and discards it — no work runs, and you get a RuntimeWarning if you're lucky. The code 'succeeds' doing nothing. Any time you call something defined with async def, it must be awaited or scheduled as a task.

slides.title: Forgotten await, fixed
slides.code:
async def save(x):
    await db.write(x)

async def main():
    save(1)          # BUG: builds coroutine, runs nothing
    await save(1)    # correct: actually runs
    asyncio.create_task(save(2))  # or schedule it

slides.title: 2. Blocking the event loop
slides.body: time.sleep(), a sync requests.get(), pandas crunching, or any CPU-heavy loop never yields, so the single thread freezes and every other task stalls until it returns. The whole point of async dies. Use asyncio.sleep, an async client, or push the work to a thread.

slides.title: Blocking, fixed
slides.code:
import asyncio

# BAD: freezes the loop for 2s
# time.sleep(2)
# requests.get(url)

await asyncio.sleep(2)                # good: yields

# offload unavoidable blocking work:
loop = asyncio.get_running_loop()
result = await loop.run_in_executor(None, heavy_cpu_fn, data)

slides.title: 3. Awaiting in a loop
slides.body: A for-loop that awaits each call before starting the next is just sequential code with extra steps — you lose all overlap. This is the most common reason 'async' code is no faster. Build the tasks first, then await them together with gather.

slides.title: Sequential vs concurrent
slides.code:
# SLOW: one at a time, total = sum of all
results = []
for u in urls:
    results.append(await fetch(u))

# FAST: all at once, total = the slowest one
results = await asyncio.gather(*(fetch(u) for u in urls))

slides.title: 4. Mixing sync libraries in
slides.body: Dropping a synchronous database driver, the plain requests library, or a blocking SDK into a coroutine reintroduces the exact stall async was meant to remove. Match async with async: aiohttp/httpx, asyncpg, aiofiles. If no async version exists, wrap the sync call in run_in_executor.

slides.title: 5. Fire-and-forget tasks vanish
slides.body: create_task returns a Task, but if you don't keep a reference, the loop may garbage-collect it mid-flight and the work silently disappears. Worse, exceptions inside it can go unreported. Store your tasks in a list (or use a TaskGroup in 3.11+) and await them before exiting.

slides.title: TaskGroup keeps tasks safe
slides.code:
import asyncio

async def main():
    # Python 3.11+: awaits all, propagates errors, cancels on failure
    async with asyncio.TaskGroup() as tg:
        tg.create_task(fetch("a"))
        tg.create_task(fetch("b"))
    # both done here; any exception re-raised

slides.title: The async safety checklist
bullets: Await (or schedule) every coroutine
No blocking calls inside the loop
gather to overlap, don't await in a loop
Use async-native libraries
Keep references to your tasks
Handle exceptions from gather/TaskGroup

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 23.
slides.body: Next day, new topic. You now know async/await end to end — concept to pitfalls.

23-1.caption:
Two projects on your laptop want two different versions of the same library. Install them globally and one of them breaks. A virtual environment is how you stop that fight before it starts. 📦

A virtual environment is an isolated, per-project copy of Python's package world. Each project gets its own folder of installed dependencies, so what you install for project A never touches project B — or the system Python your OS depends on. uv is the new, blazing-fast tool that creates and manages these environments in milliseconds instead of seconds.

This post is the map: what an environment actually is, and where uv fits.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• what a virtual environment really is
• why global installs cause chaos
• what lives inside the .venv folder
• what uv is and why it's fast
• how it relates to pip and venv
• the one-line mental model

Get the concept first and the commands stop feeling magic. Save it. 👇

slides.title: What a virtual environment is
slides.body: A virtual environment is a self-contained directory holding its own Python interpreter link and its own set of installed packages. When it's active, 'python' and 'pip' point inside it instead of at the system. Every project gets its own sealed box of dependencies that can't leak into other projects.

slides.title: Why globals cause chaos
slides.body: Install everything globally and every project shares one pile of packages. Project A needs Django 4, project B needs Django 5 — they can't both have it. Worse, your OS uses the system Python too, so a bad install can break system tools. Isolation removes the whole class of conflict.

slides.title: One global pile vs isolated boxes
What lives in .venv
slides.body: Creating an environment makes a folder, usually .venv, containing a link to a Python interpreter, a Scripts/ (or bin/) directory with python and pip, and a site-packages/ where your dependencies get installed. Activating it just puts that Scripts/ folder first on your PATH so its tools win.

slides.title: Create one with uv
slides.code:
# install uv once (pip, brew, or the installer)
# then, inside your project folder:
uv venv
# creates .venv with a Python interpreter

# add a dependency
uv pip install requests

slides.title: What uv is
slides.body: uv is a Python package and environment manager written in Rust by Astral. It replaces pip, virtualenv, and pip-tools with one fast tool. It creates environments, installs packages, resolves and locks dependencies, and even installs Python versions — all far quicker than the older tooling.

slides.title: The mental model
bullets: .venv = a sandboxed package set
Activate = point python/pip inside it
uv = the fast tool that builds it
One environment per project
Never install globally again

slides.title: Thinking it copies all of Python
slides.body: A virtual environment doesn't duplicate the whole interpreter. It links back to a base Python and only isolates the package layer. That's why it's cheap to create and delete. It's a thin overlay, not a full copy — which is exactly why you should make one per project without worrying about disk space.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 24.
slides.body: Next: why this matters — reproducibility, teammates, and deploys that don't surprise you.

23-2.caption:
"It works on my machine" is the most expensive sentence in software. Virtual environments and a lockfile are how you delete it for good. 🔒

Virtual environments matter because software isn't just your code — it's your code plus the exact versions of everything it depends on. Without isolation and pinning, the same project behaves differently on your laptop, your teammate's, and the server. uv turns dependency management into something fast and reproducible enough that you'll actually do it.

This post is the 'so what': where this discipline pays off.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• why "works on my machine" happens
• how a lockfile makes installs reproducible
• why teammates onboard in seconds
• how clean envs prevent dependency rot
• why CI and deploys get predictable
• the real cost of skipping it

This is the line between a project that scales to a team and one that doesn't. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Code is code + its versions
slides.body: Your program depends on libraries, and those libraries have versions with different behavior and bugs. Run it with requests 2.25 today and 2.32 next year and the results can change. The environment captures those exact versions, so 'your code' actually means the same thing everywhere it runs.

slides.title: Killing 'works on my machine'
slides.body: That phrase means your environment differs from someone else's in a way nobody wrote down. A virtual environment plus a lockfile writes it all down: every package, every exact version, pinned. Anyone who restores from the lock gets a byte-for-byte match of your dependency tree.

slides.title: Reproducible onboarding
slides.code:
# a new teammate clones the repo
git clone https://github.com/team/app.git
cd app

# one command builds the exact env
uv sync
# reads pyproject.toml + uv.lock,
# creates .venv, installs pinned versions

uv run python app.py   # just works

slides.title: Teammates onboard in seconds
slides.body: Without isolation, onboarding is a README full of 'pip install this, then that, oh and downgrade this.' With a locked environment it's one command. New hires, CI runners, and your future self all rebuild the identical setup instead of debugging a hand-assembled one.

slides.title: Deploys stop surprising you
slides.body: When CI, staging, and production all run 'uv sync' against the same lockfile, the env that passed tests is the env that ships. No more 'it built last week.' The lockfile is the contract: the version set you tested is the version set deployed, every single time.

slides.title: Where this pays off
bullets: Onboarding new teammates fast
CI that mirrors local exactly
Deploys you can trust
Long-lived projects that don't rot
Reproducing an old bug report

slides.title: The cost of skipping it
slides.body: Skip environments and isolation feels fine for a week. Then a global upgrade for project A silently breaks project B, a deploy pulls a new minor version with a bug, and you spend a day diffing 'pip freeze' between two machines. The discipline is cheap up front; the chaos compounds.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 24.
slides.body: Next: how it works — PATH, activation, resolution, and what uv does under the hood.

23-3.caption:
Activating an environment feels like magic, but it's just one trick: it rearranges your PATH so a different 'python' wins. 🔍

Under the hood, a virtual environment is mostly a folder and a path trick. Activation prepends its Scripts/ directory to your PATH, so typing 'python' finds the env's interpreter first. That interpreter is configured to look in the env's own site-packages. uv adds a fast Rust resolver on top that figures out a compatible set of versions and installs them with a global cache.

This post is the engine room.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• what activation actually changes
• how Python finds the right packages
• how the resolver picks versions
• what the lockfile records
• how uv's cache makes installs instant
• why uv run skips manual activation

See the machine and the commands make sense. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Activation is a PATH trick
slides.body: Your shell finds 'python' by scanning the PATH list left to right and taking the first match. Activating an environment prepends its Scripts/ (Windows) or bin/ (Unix) folder to PATH, so the env's python and pip are found before the system ones. Deactivating just removes that entry.

slides.title: How Python finds packages
slides.body: The env's interpreter has a pyvenv.cfg pointing at its base Python, and its sys.path is set so the env's own site-packages comes first. So 'import requests' loads the copy installed in this env, ignoring whatever the system has. The interpreter itself is shared; only the package search path is isolated.

slides.title: The resolver's job
slides.body: When you ask for 'flask' and 'pandas', each pulls in its own dependencies, and those can demand conflicting versions of a shared library. A resolver searches for one set of versions that satisfies every constraint at once. uv's resolver is written in Rust and does this in a fraction of pip's time.

slides.title: Inspect the env from inside
slides.code:
import sys

# the active interpreter's path
print(sys.executable)
# .../myproject/.venv/Scripts/python.exe

# where it searches for packages
print(sys.path[-1])
# .../myproject/.venv/Lib/site-packages

slides.title: Why uv installs feel instant
slides.body: uv keeps a single global cache of downloaded and built packages. The first time you install a wheel it's fetched and cached; after that, installing it into any env is a hard link or copy from the cache, not a download. Combine that with parallel installs and a Rust resolver and full envs build in milliseconds.

slides.title: uv run: no manual activate
slides.code:
# uv run finds .venv, ensures it's synced,
# then runs the command inside it
uv run python app.py
uv run pytest

# no 'source activate' needed at all

slides.title: Forgetting which python is active
slides.body: If a command behaves oddly, your shell is probably calling the wrong python. A stale activation, a new terminal that never activated, or an editor using its own interpreter are the usual culprits. Check sys.executable or 'which python' first — most 'missing module' errors are really 'wrong environment' errors.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 24.
slides.body: Next: a full worked example — init a project, add deps, lock, and run with uv.

23-4.caption:
Enough theory — here's a real project, start to finish, managed entirely with uv. From empty folder to a locked, reproducible app. 💻

This is the complete workflow: initialize a project, pin a Python version, add and remove dependencies, separate dev tools from runtime deps, lock everything, and run your code without ever typing 'activate.' Every command here is real and runnable.

Follow it once and uv becomes muscle memory.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• uv init to scaffold a project
• uv add to install and record deps
• dev dependencies vs runtime
• uv lock and uv sync
• uv run to execute in the env
• reading the files uv generates

Copy these commands into your next project. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 1. Start a project
slides.code:
# scaffold a new project folder
uv init weather-app
cd weather-app

# uv created:
#   pyproject.toml  (project metadata + deps)
#   .python-version (pinned interpreter)
#   main.py         (a starter file)
#   README.md

slides.title: 2. Add dependencies
slides.code:
# add runtime deps (creates .venv on first add)
uv add requests rich

# add a dev-only dependency
uv add --dev pytest ruff

# uv resolves, installs, updates
# pyproject.toml AND writes uv.lock

slides.title: 3. What pyproject.toml holds
slides.code:
[project]
name = "weather-app"
version = "0.1.0"
requires-python = ">=3.11"
dependencies = [
    "requests>=2.32",
    "rich>=13.0",
]

[dependency-groups]
dev = ["pytest>=8.0", "ruff>=0.6"]

slides.title: 4. Write and run the app
slides.code:
# main.py
import requests
from rich import print

r = requests.get("https://api.github.com")
print(f"[green]status:[/green] {r.status_code}")

# run it inside the env, no activate:
# $ uv run python main.py

slides.title: Runtime vs dev deps
slides.body: Runtime dependencies (requests, rich) are what your app needs to run in production. Dev dependencies (pytest, ruff) are only needed while building and testing. Keeping them separate means a deploy installs a lean set, while developers get the full toolkit. 'uv add --dev' records the difference.

slides.title: 5. Lock, sync, and clean up
slides.code:
# re-resolve and refresh the lockfile
uv lock

# build .venv to match the lock exactly
uv sync

# remove a dependency you no longer need
uv remove rich

# run your test suite in the env
uv run pytest

slides.title: What to commit to git
slides.body: Commit pyproject.toml, uv.lock, and .python-version — together they fully describe the environment. Do NOT commit the .venv folder; it's a rebuildable artifact. Anyone who clones the repo runs 'uv sync' and gets the identical environment from the files you committed.

slides.title: The commands you'll use daily
bullets: uv init — start a project
uv add / uv remove — manage deps
uv sync — rebuild env from lock
uv run — execute in the env
uv lock — refresh exact pins

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 24.
slides.body: Next: common mistakes — the env traps that waste hours, and how to dodge them.

23-5.caption:
Most virtual-environment pain isn't hard — it's the same five mistakes, made over and over, that quietly eat an afternoon. Here's how to dodge all of them. 🚧

Virtual environments are simple in theory and confusing in practice, because the failures are invisible: nothing crashes, you're just secretly using the wrong python or installing into the wrong place. These are the traps that catch beginners and seniors alike, and the habits that make them go away.

This post is the field guide to not shooting yourself in the foot.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• installing into the global Python by accident
• committing the .venv folder to git
• forgetting to activate (or which one is)
• not committing the lockfile
• mixing pip and uv in one env
• pinning nothing and hoping

Learn these once and stop paying for them. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Installing into global Python
slides.body: You run 'pip install' before creating or activating an env, and it lands in your system Python. Now project deps are global, versions collide, and you can't reproduce the setup. The fix: always create the env first, and prefer 'uv add' / 'uv run', which target the project env automatically.

slides.title: Committing the .venv folder
slides.body: The .venv directory is large, machine-specific, and fully rebuildable — it has no business in git. Committing it bloats the repo and breaks on other OSes because the interpreter paths are absolute. Add .venv to .gitignore and commit the lockfile instead; that's the portable, tiny source of truth.

slides.title: The .gitignore line
slides.code:
# .gitignore
.venv/
__pycache__/
*.pyc

# DO commit these:
#   pyproject.toml
#   uv.lock
#   .python-version

slides.title: Forgetting which env is active
slides.body: A new terminal doesn't inherit activation, and editors often pick their own interpreter. You install a package, then 'import' fails because that terminal is on a different python. The error says 'No module named X' but the real problem is the wrong environment. Verify before you debug deeper.

slides.title: Check which python you're on
slides.code:
import sys
print(sys.executable)
# should end in .../<project>/.venv/...

# from the shell, equivalently:
#   Windows: where python
#   Unix:    which python

slides.title: Not committing the lockfile
slides.body: pyproject.toml says '>=2.32' — a range, not a version. Without uv.lock, two people running 'uv sync' months apart can get different resolved versions, and your reproducibility evaporates. The lockfile is the exact, frozen answer. Commit it, and treat changes to it as deliberate, reviewable events.

slides.title: Mixing pip and uv blindly
slides.body: Running raw 'pip install' inside a uv project can put packages in the env without updating pyproject.toml or uv.lock, so your declared state and actual state drift apart. If you must use pip, use 'uv pip install' so uv stays aware. Better: standardize on 'uv add' so the manifest is always the truth.

slides.title: The habits that prevent all of this
bullets: One env per project, always
Use uv add / uv run, not raw pip
Gitignore .venv, commit the lock
Check sys.executable when confused
Treat the lockfile as source of truth

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 24.
slides.body: Day 24 starts a new topic — follow so you don't miss it.

24-1.caption:
A Python list of a million numbers is a million little boxes scattered across memory, each wrapped in object overhead. NumPy throws all of that away. 🧮

NumPy is the foundation of scientific Python. At its heart is one idea: the ndarray, a grid of values of a single type packed tightly into one block of memory. That single design choice is what makes NumPy fast, compact, and the layer that pandas, scikit-learn, and PyTorch are all built on top of.

This post is the map: what an array actually IS before we get into why it matters or how to wield it.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• what an ndarray really is
• shape, dtype, and dimensions explained
• how it differs from a Python list
• what vectorization means
• why everything in data science sits on top of it

Get the core object straight and the rest of NumPy clicks. Save it. 👇

slides.title: What NumPy is
slides.body: NumPy is the core library for numerical computing in Python. It gives you one central object — the n-dimensional array, or ndarray — plus a huge toolbox of fast operations over it. Almost every data, ML, and scientific library in Python stores its numbers as NumPy arrays under the hood.

slides.title: The ndarray
slides.body: An ndarray is a grid of values, all of the same type, laid out in one contiguous block of memory. It has a fixed size once created and a known shape. Think of it as a typed, multi-dimensional tensor of numbers — not a flexible container, but a tight numerical buffer.

slides.title: Your first array
slides.code:
import numpy as np

a = np.array([1, 2, 3, 4])
print(a)          # [1 2 3 4]
print(a.shape)    # (4,)
print(a.dtype)    # int64
print(a.ndim)     # 1

slides.title: Shape, dtype, ndim
slides.body: Three attributes describe any array. shape is a tuple of its size along each axis, like (3, 4) for 3 rows and 4 columns. dtype is the single data type of every element, like int64 or float32. ndim is the number of axes — 1 for a vector, 2 for a matrix, more for tensors.

slides.title: Vectorization
