slides.body: Next: the traps — dead ReLUs, double softmax, and the activation mistakes that fail silently.

42-5.caption:
Most activation bugs never throw an error. Your loss just sits flat, half your neurons output zero forever, or your 'probabilities' quietly stop being probabilities. ⚠️

The usual suspects: sigmoid/tanh buried in deep hidden layers, dead ReLUs from a bad learning rate, double-applied softmax, the wrong output activation for the task, and unscaled inputs that saturate everything. Each one silently degrades the model.

This post is the failure manual: the specific activation mistakes and the fix for each.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• sigmoid/tanh in deep hidden layers
• dead ReLUs and how to revive them
• applying softmax twice
• wrong output activation for the task
• unscaled inputs that saturate
• relu vs softmax confusion

Learn the failure modes before you trust the output. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Sigmoid/tanh in deep hidden layers
slides.body: Using sigmoid or tanh throughout a deep hidden stack is the classic vanishing-gradient trap. Their derivatives are small and compound toward zero across layers, so early layers barely learn and training stalls with no error. The fix: default to ReLU or a variant in hidden layers, and reserve sigmoid/tanh for outputs or shallow nets.

slides.title: Dead ReLUs
slides.body: A ReLU neuron whose input is always negative outputs zero forever — and its gradient is zero too, so it never recovers. Often caused by too-high a learning rate pushing weights into a bad region, or large negative bias. You can lose a big fraction of a layer this way, silently shrinking capacity.

slides.title: Fix: LeakyReLU keeps a gradient
slides.code:
import torch.nn as nn

# dying:   nn.ReLU()           -> 0 gradient for z < 0
# revived: nn.LeakyReLU(0.01)  -> small slope keeps it alive
model = nn.Sequential(
    nn.Linear(20, 64), nn.LeakyReLU(0.01),
    nn.Linear(64, 3))
# also: lower the learning rate, check weight init

slides.title: Applying softmax twice
slides.body: PyTorch's CrossEntropyLoss already applies log-softmax internally. Add a softmax layer to your model and feed it to CrossEntropyLoss and you softmax twice — the gradients flatten and the model learns slowly or not at all. No exception is raised; the loss just behaves oddly. The fix: output raw logits, let the loss handle softmax.

slides.title: Wrong output activation
slides.body: Softmax on a regression head, no activation on a probability head, or sigmoid where you needed softmax — each produces nonsense that still 'runs'. Match the output to the task: linear for unbounded regression, sigmoid for one binary probability, softmax for mutually exclusive classes, multiple sigmoids for multi-label.

slides.title: Unscaled inputs saturate everything
slides.body: Feed huge raw values into sigmoid or tanh and every neuron saturates at 0 or 1 immediately, where the derivative is ~0 — the network can't learn from step one. Even ReLU behaves badly with wildly scaled inputs. The fix: standardize inputs to roughly zero mean and unit variance before the first layer.

slides.title: Fix: standardize before the net
slides.code:
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

scaler  = StandardScaler().fit(X_train)   # train stats only
X_train = scaler.transform(X_train)
X_test  = scaler.transform(X_test)
# now sums land in the activation's responsive range

slides.title: ReLU vs softmax confusion
slides.body: These solve different problems. ReLU is a per-neuron hidden-layer nonlinearity; softmax is a layer-wide output normalizer. Using ReLU as your classifier output gives you raw unnormalized numbers, not probabilities; using softmax in hidden layers cripples them by forcing activations to compete and sum to 1. Keep their jobs separate.

slides.title: The fixes, in one place
bullets: ReLU/variants in hidden layers
Dead ReLU? LeakyReLU + lower lr
Raw logits + CrossEntropyLoss (no double softmax)
Match output activation to the task
Standardize inputs to avoid saturation
ReLU = hidden, softmax = output

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 43.
slides.body: Next topic: Loss Functions — how a model measures its own wrongness, in depth.

43-1.caption:
Backpropagation sounds like dark magic. It's actually one rule from first-year calculus, applied with bookkeeping. 🧮

Strip away the mystique and backprop is just the chain rule run in reverse. The network makes a prediction, you measure the error, and then you ask one question for every weight: if I nudge you a little, how much does the error change? Backprop answers that question for millions of weights at roughly the cost of a single forward pass.

This post is the map. Before we argue why it matters or trace the math by hand, get crystal clear on what backpropagation IS and the vocabulary that surrounds it.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• the one-sentence definition that sticks
• gradient, loss, and partial derivative
• why it's called 'back' propagation
• the forward pass it depends on
• how it differs from gradient descent

Get the mental model straight and the math stops being scary. Save it. 👇

slides.title: The chain rule, run backward
slides.body: Backpropagation is an algorithm for computing the gradient of the loss with respect to every weight in a network. It does this by applying the calculus chain rule layer by layer, starting at the output and working back toward the input. The result tells you exactly how to change each weight to make the prediction less wrong.

slides.title: It computes gradients, nothing more
slides.body: A crucial distinction: backprop does NOT update the weights. It only computes the gradient — the slope of the loss with respect to each parameter. A separate step, the optimizer, uses that gradient to actually move the weights. Backprop is the measurement; gradient descent is the action.

slides.title: The vocabulary, precisely
bullets: Loss: one number for how wrong the prediction is
Gradient: the slope of loss vs. each weight
Partial derivative: change in loss from one weight alone
Chain rule: derivative of nested functions
Cache: forward-pass values reused going back

slides.title: Why 'back' propagation
slides.body: The error is only known at the very end — at the output. To assign blame to a weight buried in an early layer, you have to propagate that error signal backward through every layer it passed through. Each layer hands the next-earlier layer its share of responsibility. The information literally flows from output to input.

slides.title: It depends on the forward pass
slides.body: Backprop cannot run alone. The forward pass first computes and stores intermediate values at every layer. The backward pass then reuses exactly those cached values to evaluate the chain rule. No forward pass, no saved activations, no gradients. The two are a matched pair, run one after the other.

slides.title: The chain rule in one line
slides.code:
# loss L depends on output a, which depends on weight w
# chain rule: dL/dw = dL/da * da/dw

dL_da = 2 * (a - y)     # derivative of (a - y)^2
da_dw = x               # derivative of a = w*x
dL_dw = dL_da * da_dw   # multiply the links together

slides.title: Why it was the breakthrough
slides.body: The idea of gradients is old. The breakthrough was computing them efficiently. A naive approach would perturb each weight separately — impossibly slow for millions of parameters. Backprop gets every gradient in roughly one extra pass by reusing shared computation. That efficiency is what made training deep networks practical.

slides.title: The mental model, locked
bullets: Backprop = chain rule, output to input
It computes gradients, not updates
Needs cached forward-pass values
Gradient descent does the stepping
Efficient: ~one extra pass for all weights

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 44.
slides.body: Next: why this one algorithm is the reason deep learning exists at all.

43-2.caption:
Without backpropagation, there is no deep learning. Not slower deep learning — none at all. ⚙️

It matters because of a single property: it makes gradients cheap. Computing how every weight affects the loss used to be prohibitively expensive, scaling with the number of weights. Backprop collapses that to roughly one extra pass over the network, no matter how many millions of parameters you have. That one efficiency unlocked everything that followed.

This post is the 'so what': why a 1980s algorithm is still the beating heart of every model you use today.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• why naive gradients don't scale
• how backprop makes them cheap
• why every framework is built on it
• what 'autograd' really automates
• the limits and failure modes
• where it quietly runs today

Understand why it matters and the whole field's history clicks. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Naive gradients don't scale
slides.body: You could estimate each weight's gradient numerically: nudge it, re-run the whole network, see how the loss changed. But that costs one full forward pass per weight. For a model with ten million weights, that's ten million forward passes for a single training step. Completely infeasible.

slides.title: Backprop makes them cheap
slides.body: Backprop computes the gradient for every weight in roughly the cost of a single forward pass — regardless of how many weights there are. It does this by reusing intermediate results: the error signal computed for a later layer is recycled to compute the gradient for an earlier one. Shared work, computed once.

diagram.axisLabel: relative cost to get all gradients

slides.title: Every framework runs on it
slides.body: PyTorch, TensorFlow, JAX — every deep learning framework is, at its core, an automatic implementation of backpropagation. When you call loss.backward(), the framework walks the computation graph in reverse and applies the chain rule for you. The thing you take for granted IS backprop, automated.

slides.title: Autograd automates the bookkeeping
slides.body: Automatic differentiation (autograd) records every operation in a graph as the forward pass runs. To get gradients, it traverses that graph backward, applying a known derivative rule at each node. You write only the forward math; the backward pass is derived for free. That automation is what makes experimentation fast.

slides.title: It has real limits
slides.body: Backprop needs the whole forward pass stored in memory to run backward — a real cost for huge models, addressed with tricks like gradient checkpointing. It also only works on differentiable operations, and the chain of multiplications can make gradients vanish or explode in deep networks. It is powerful, not magic.

slides.title: Autograd in three lines
slides.code:
import torch

w = torch.tensor(2.0, requires_grad=True)
loss = (w * 3 - 1) ** 2     # build the graph forward
loss.backward()             # autograd walks it backward
print(w.grad)               # dL/dw, computed for you

slides.title: Why it matters, in one place
bullets: Makes gradients cheap at any scale
Powers every major framework
Autograd derives the backward pass
Has memory and stability limits
Trained nearly every model you use

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 44.
slides.body: Next: open the hood — trace the gradient backward through a network, one step at a time.

43-3.caption:
Let's actually trace it. One tiny network, real numbers, and the gradient flowing backward step by step. 🔧

The best way to kill the mystery is to do the bookkeeping by hand on a network small enough to fit on one screen. We'll run a forward pass, compute the loss, then walk backward — applying the chain rule at each layer and reusing the values we cached on the way in. By the end you'll see exactly where each gradient comes from.

This post is the engine room: the four backward steps and the chain rule that links them.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• the forward pass that sets up the cache
• the loss gradient that starts the flow
• backprop through the activation
• backprop through the linear layer
• how the signal reaches earlier layers
• the full forward-and-backward loop

Do the trace once and backprop stops being magic. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Step 0: the forward pass
slides.body: Push the input forward and SAVE every intermediate value. For one neuron: z = w·x + b, then a = f(z), then loss L = (a - y)². You'll need z, a, and x again on the way back, so the forward pass caches them. Nothing is thrown away — backprop reuses all of it.

slides.title: Step 1: gradient of the loss
slides.body: Backprop starts at the very end, where the error is known. Differentiate the loss with respect to the output: for L = (a - y)², the gradient dL/da = 2(a - y). This single number is the error signal. Everything that follows just routes this signal backward through the network.

slides.title: Step 2: through the activation
slides.body: The output a came from an activation a = f(z). Multiply the incoming signal by the activation's local derivative: dL/dz = dL/da · f'(z). For sigmoid, f'(z) = a(1-a); for ReLU, f'(z) is 1 if z>0 else 0. Now you have the gradient with respect to the pre-activation z.

slides.title: Step 3: through the linear layer
slides.body: The pre-activation was z = w·x + b. Apply the chain rule once more: dL/dw = dL/dz · x, and dL/db = dL/dz · 1. These are the gradients you actually wanted — how the loss changes with each weight and bias. The input x, cached in step 0, is what makes this multiplication possible.

slides.title: Step 4: keep going backward
slides.body: To reach an earlier layer, pass the signal through the weights: dL/dx = dL/dz · w. That dL/dx becomes the incoming gradient for the previous layer, which repeats steps 2 and 3 with its own cached values. The same four moves, layer after layer, all the way to the input.

slides.title: Forward pass with cache
slides.code:
import numpy as np

x, y = 1.5, 0.0
w, b = 0.8, 0.1

z = w * x + b              # cache z
a = 1 / (1 + np.exp(-z))   # sigmoid, cache a
L = (a - y) ** 2           # the loss
print(z, a, L)

slides.title: Backward pass by hand
slides.code:
dL_da = 2 * (a - y)        # step 1: loss gradient
da_dz = a * (1 - a)        # sigmoid derivative
dL_dz = dL_da * da_dz      # step 2: through activation

dL_dw = dL_dz * x          # step 3: gradient for w
dL_db = dL_dz * 1          # gradient for b
print(dL_dw, dL_db)

slides.title: The four backward steps
bullets: Cache values in the forward pass
Start with dL/da at the output
Multiply by f'(z) thru activation
Multiply by x to get dL/dw
Multiply by w to flow to earlier layers

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 44.
slides.body: Next: a full runnable backprop — implement it from scratch, then check it against PyTorch.

43-4.caption:
Enough tracing on paper — let's implement backpropagation from scratch in pure numpy, then verify it against PyTorch's autograd. 💻

This post is a complete, runnable build: a tiny two-layer network with a hand-written forward pass, a hand-written backward pass, one gradient-descent step, and a side-by-side check that our gradients match the framework's. By the end you'll have backprop you wrote yourself, proven correct.

This is the angle where backprop stops being a diagram and becomes code you can step through.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• set up the network and data
• the forward pass, caching activations
• the backward pass, layer by layer
• one gradient-descent update
• verify against PyTorch autograd

Copy it, run it, change a number, watch the gradient move. That's how it sticks. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 0. Setup: data + weights
slides.code:
import numpy as np
np.random.seed(0)

x = np.array([[1.0], [2.0]])        # 2 features
y = np.array([[1.0]])               # target

W1 = np.random.randn(3, 2) * 0.1    # hidden: 3 units
b1 = np.zeros((3, 1))
W2 = np.random.randn(1, 3) * 0.1    # output: 1 unit
b2 = np.zeros((1, 1))

slides.title: 1. Forward pass (cache everything)
slides.code:
def relu(z): return np.maximum(0, z)

z1 = W1 @ x + b1        # hidden pre-activation
a1 = relu(z1)           # hidden activation
z2 = W2 @ a1 + b2       # output pre-activation
a2 = z2                 # linear output
L = np.mean((a2 - y) ** 2)   # MSE loss
print('loss:', L)

slides.title: 2. Backward pass (chain rule)
slides.code:
# output layer
dL_da2 = 2 * (a2 - y) / y.size   # dL/d output
dL_dW2 = dL_da2 @ a1.T           # gradient for W2
dL_db2 = dL_da2

# hidden layer (flow signal back through W2)
dL_da1 = W2.T @ dL_da2
dL_dz1 = dL_da1 * (z1 > 0)       # ReLU derivative
dL_dW1 = dL_dz1 @ x.T            # gradient for W1
dL_db1 = dL_dz1

slides.title: 3. One gradient-descent step
slides.code:
lr = 0.1
W2 -= lr * dL_dW2       # step against the gradient
b2 -= lr * dL_db2
W1 -= lr * dL_dW1
b1 -= lr * dL_db1
# re-run forward: loss should now be lower

slides.title: Why the gradients transpose
slides.body: Notice dL_dW2 = dL_da2 @ a1.T — the cached activation gets transposed. That's the matrix form of dL/dw = dL/dz · x from the last post: each weight's gradient is its incoming signal times the input that flowed through it. The shapes have to line up with the weight matrix, which is why the transposes appear.

slides.title: 4. Verify against PyTorch
slides.code:
import torch

xt = torch.tensor(x); yt = torch.tensor(y)
W1t = torch.tensor(W1, requires_grad=True)
# ... wrap b1, W2, b2 the same way ...
a1t = torch.relu(W1t @ xt + b1t)
a2t = W2t @ a1t + b2t
loss = ((a2t - yt) ** 2).mean()
loss.backward()
print(torch.allclose(W1t.grad, torch.tensor(dL_dW1)))

slides.title: Wrap it in a loop
slides.body: A real training run repeats these four steps over many batches and epochs. Each iteration: forward to predict and cache, compute loss, backward to fill gradients, step the weights. The hand-written version makes visible exactly what model(x), loss.backward(), and optimizer.step() do for you in a framework.

slides.title: The build, in order
bullets: Init weights small + random
Forward pass, cache activations
Backward: output layer first, then hidden
Transpose to match weight shapes
Step each weight against its gradient
Verify against autograd with allclose

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 44.
slides.body: Next: the traps — vanishing gradients, forgotten zero_grad, and the bugs backprop won't warn you about.

43-5.caption:
Backprop will compute a perfectly correct gradient of a completely broken setup — and never tell you something's wrong. ⚠️

Most backprop failures aren't bugs in the math; they're traps around it. Gradients that vanish or explode through deep chains, accumulated gradients you forgot to clear, a detached tensor that silently blocks the signal, in-place ops that corrupt the graph. None of these throw an error. The loss just sits there, or NaNs appear from nowhere.

This post is the failure manual: the specific ways backprop goes wrong and the fix for each.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• vanishing and exploding gradients
• forgetting to zero the gradients
• accidentally breaking the graph
• in-place ops that corrupt backward
• non-differentiable operations
• not checking your hand-written gradients

Learn the failure modes before you trust the loss curve. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Vanishing / exploding gradients
slides.body: Backprop multiplies many local derivatives together as the signal flows back. If they're consistently below 1, the gradient shrinks toward zero and early layers stop learning. Above 1, it blows up to NaN. Saturating activations like sigmoid are a classic cause of vanishing gradients in deep stacks.

slides.title: Fix: ReLU, init, clipping
slides.code:
import torch.nn as nn

model = nn.Sequential(
    nn.Linear(64, 64), nn.ReLU(),   # non-saturating
    nn.Linear(64, 64), nn.ReLU())
# explosion guard during training:
nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(model.parameters(), max_norm=1.0)

slides.title: Forgetting zero_grad()
slides.body: PyTorch accumulates gradients into .grad by default — every backward() adds to what's already there. Skip optimizer.zero_grad() and you sum gradients across batches, so updates are wrong and training degrades. The fix is one line before each backward call, every single iteration.

slides.title: Fix: clear grads each step
slides.code:
for xb, yb in loader:
    optimizer.zero_grad()        # <-- easy to forget
    loss = loss_fn(model(xb), yb)
    loss.backward()              # fills fresh gradients
    optimizer.step()

slides.title: Accidentally breaking the graph
slides.body: Calling .detach(), .item(), .numpy(), or wrapping code in torch.no_grad() cuts the tensor out of the computation graph. Do it mid-network and the gradient can't flow past that point — those weights silently never update. The fix: keep tensors attached until after backward; detach only for logging or true constants.

slides.title: In-place ops corrupt backward
slides.body: In-place operations (x += 1, relu_(), x[mask] = 0) overwrite values that backprop needs for the chain rule. PyTorch often raises 'a variable needed for gradient has been modified', but subtle cases pass silently with wrong gradients. The fix: prefer out-of-place ops inside the forward pass.

slides.title: Non-differentiable operations
slides.body: argmax, rounding, hard thresholds, and most sampling have zero or undefined derivatives — backprop can't pass a useful signal through them. Put one in your forward path and upstream weights get no gradient. The fix: use differentiable relaxations like softmax, or straight-through estimators when you truly need a hard choice.

slides.title: Not checking hand-written grads
slides.body: If you implement backward yourself, an off-by-a-transpose or missing factor produces gradients that look plausible but are wrong — training just underperforms. The fix: gradient-check. Compare your analytic gradient to a numerical estimate (loss(w+ε) - loss(w-ε)) / 2ε; they should match to several decimals.

slides.title: Fix: numerical gradient check
The fixes, in one place
bullets: ReLU + good init + grad clipping
zero_grad() before every backward
Keep tensors attached to the graph
Avoid in-place ops in forward
Use differentiable ops (or relaxations)
Gradient-check hand-written backward

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 44.
slides.body: Next topic: Gradient Descent variants — SGD, momentum, and Adam, and how each uses these gradients.

44-1.caption:
A regular neural network looks at a 200x200 image as 40,000 unrelated numbers. A CNN looks at it as a picture. 🖼️

A convolutional neural network is a neural network built specifically for data with spatial structure — images, mostly. Instead of connecting every pixel to every neuron, it slides small learnable filters across the image to detect local patterns: edges, then textures, then shapes, then objects. The same filter is reused everywhere, so the network learns far fewer parameters and respects the fact that a cat is a cat wherever it appears.

This post is the map. Before we argue why CNNs matter or trace convolution by hand, get crystal clear on what a CNN IS and the vocabulary around it.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• the one-sentence definition that sticks
• filter, feature map, stride, and padding
• why fully-connected nets fail on images
• the three ideas that define a CNN
• how a CNN differs from an MLP

Get the mental model straight and the rest is detail. Save it. 👇

slides.title: A network that slides filters
slides.body: A convolutional neural network is a neural network that processes grid-like data by sliding small learnable filters across it. Each filter detects one local pattern — an edge, a corner, a texture — anywhere it appears. Stack these layers and the network learns simple patterns first, then combines them into complex ones.

slides.title: Built for spatial structure
slides.body: The defining assumption is that nearby pixels are related and distant ones often are not. A CNN bakes that assumption into its architecture instead of forcing the network to learn it from scratch. This is why CNNs dominate vision: the structure of the data and the structure of the model match.

slides.title: The vocabulary, precisely
bullets: Filter/kernel: small grid of learnable weights
Feature map: filter's output across the image
Stride: how far the filter hops each step
Padding: pixels added at the border
Pooling: downsampling that shrinks the map

slides.title: Why fully-connected nets fail here
slides.body: Flatten a 200x200 RGB image and a single fully-connected layer of 1000 units needs 120 million weights — for one layer. It also throws away geometry: pixel (5,5) and (5,6) become unrelated inputs. A CNN avoids both problems by sharing small filters and preserving the 2D layout.

slides.title: The three defining ideas
bullets: Local connectivity: each neuron sees a small patch
Parameter sharing: one filter reused everywhere
Translation invariance: a pattern is found anywhere

slides.title: A CNN in five lines
slides.code:
import torch.nn as nn

model = nn.Sequential(
    nn.Conv2d(3, 16, kernel_size=3),  # 3 in-channels -> 16 filters
    nn.ReLU(),
    nn.MaxPool2d(2),                  # halve the spatial size
    nn.Flatten(),
    nn.Linear(16 * 15 * 15, 10))      # classify into 10 classes

left.title: MLP (dense)
items: Must relearn a pattern per location

right.title: CNN (conv)
items: Each neuron sees a small patch

slides.title: The feature hierarchy
slides.body: Stacking conv layers builds a hierarchy. Early layers detect edges and simple textures. Middle layers combine those into parts — an eye, a wheel, a corner. Deep layers assemble parts into whole objects. The network discovers this hierarchy itself; you never hand-code what an 'edge filter' should look like.

slides.title: The mental model, locked
bullets: CNN = slide learnable filters over a grid
Local connectivity + parameter sharing
Feature maps stack into a hierarchy
Pooling shrinks, conv detects
Built for images, not flat vectors

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 45.
slides.body: Next: why this one architecture set off the entire deep learning revolution.

44-2.caption:
In 2012, one CNN cut the top image-recognition error rate nearly in half overnight. The deep learning boom started there. 🚀

CNNs matter because they solved the problem everyone was stuck on: making sense of raw pixels without hand-engineered features. For decades, computer vision meant experts designing edge and corner detectors by hand. A CNN learns those detectors itself, directly from data, and learns better ones than humans ever wrote.

This post is the 'so what': why an architecture from the 1980s, scaled up in 2012, still underpins how machines see.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• the hand-engineered features CNNs replaced
• why parameter sharing is the superpower
• the AlexNet moment that changed everything
• where CNNs run today, from phones to hospitals
• what they made possible beyond images
• the honest limits worth knowing

Understand why it matters and the field's history clicks. Save it. 👇

slides.title: They killed hand-engineered features
slides.body: Before CNNs, vision meant humans designing feature detectors — SIFT, HOG, hand-tuned edge filters — then feeding them to a classifier. It was slow, brittle, and capped by human imagination. CNNs learn the features end to end from raw pixels, and the learned features consistently beat the hand-made ones.

slides.title: Parameter sharing is the superpower
slides.body: One 3x3 filter is just 9 weights, yet it scans an entire image. A vertical-edge detector is useful in every region, so the network learns it once and reuses it everywhere. That sharing slashes parameters, prevents overfitting, and is exactly why CNNs train on images that would drown a dense network.

diagram.axisLabel: params for one layer on a 200x200 image

slides.title: The AlexNet moment
slides.body: In 2012, AlexNet — a deep CNN trained on two GPUs — won the ImageNet competition by a margin so large it ended the debate. Error dropped from ~26% to ~16% in one year. Within months, nearly every serious vision lab switched to deep CNNs. That single result kicked off the modern deep learning era.

slides.title: Transfer learning in seconds
slides.code:
import torchvision.models as models
import torch.nn as nn

net = models.resnet18(weights='DEFAULT')   # pretrained CNN
net.fc = nn.Linear(512, 5)                 # swap the head: 5 classes
# now fine-tune on your tiny dataset and reuse learned features

slides.title: Beyond images
slides.body: The convolution idea generalizes. 1D convolutions read audio waveforms and text; 3D convolutions read video and medical volumes. CNNs also power the perception front-end of many robotics and self-driving systems. Anywhere data has local structure on a grid, convolution is a natural fit.

slides.title: The honest limits
slides.body: CNNs have a fixed-size receptive field, so capturing long-range, whole-image relationships takes many layers. They are not naturally rotation-invariant, and they are data-hungry. For tasks needing global context, Vision Transformers now compete with or beat them — though CNNs remain efficient and dominant on-device.

slides.title: Why it matters, in one place
bullets: Replaced hand-engineered features
Parameter sharing makes images tractable
AlexNet 2012 launched the boom
Run on phones, scanners, cars, satellites
Generalize to audio, video, volumes

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 45.
slides.body: Next: open the hood — see exactly how convolution, stride, padding, and pooling work.

44-3.caption:
Convolution sounds intimidating. It's really just sliding a tiny grid over an image and multiplying. 🔧

Let's actually trace it. We'll take one small filter, slide it across an image patch by patch, and watch a feature map appear. Then we'll add the knobs that control it — stride, padding, channels — and see how pooling shrinks the result. By the end you'll know exactly what every number in nn.Conv2d means.

This post is the engine room: the mechanics of convolution, step by step, no hand-waving.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• the convolution operation, one window at a time
• how a feature map is built
• stride and padding, and the output-size formula
• channels: stacking filters into depth
• pooling and the ReLU between layers
• the full conv-pool-conv flow

Do the trace once and convolution stops being scary. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Step 1: one window
slides.body: Place the filter over the top-left patch of the image. Multiply each filter weight by the pixel underneath it, sum all the products, and write that single number to the output. That dot product is the whole operation. A large output means the patch strongly matches the pattern the filter encodes.

slides.title: Step 2: slide and repeat
slides.body: Shift the filter one position to the right and repeat the multiply-and-sum. Slide across the whole row, then down to the next, covering the entire image. Each position produces one output value. Collected together, those values form a 2D grid called a feature map — a heat map of where the pattern appears.

slides.title: Step 3: stride and padding
slides.body: Stride is how far the filter jumps each step. Stride 1 overlaps heavily; stride 2 skips every other position and halves the output size. Padding adds a border of zeros so the filter can sit on edge pixels, which keeps the output the same size as the input instead of shrinking it.

slides.title: The output-size formula
slides.code:
# output = floor((W - K + 2P) / S) + 1
# W=input size, K=kernel, P=padding, S=stride

# 32x32 image, 3x3 kernel, padding 1, stride 1:
out = (32 - 3 + 2*1) // 1 + 1   # = 32, size preserved

# same but stride 2:
out = (32 - 3 + 2*1) // 2 + 1   # = 16, halved

slides.title: Step 4: channels add depth
slides.body: Color images have 3 channels (R,G,B), so a filter is actually 3x3x3 and sums across all three. Each filter outputs one feature map; use 16 filters and you get 16 stacked maps — a tensor 16 deep. The next layer's filters then convolve across all 16, building richer patterns from simpler ones.

slides.title: Step 5: ReLU, then pool
slides.body: After convolution, a ReLU zeros out negative responses, keeping only where the pattern fired. Then pooling — usually 2x2 max — takes the strongest value in each small window, halving width and height. This shrinks the map, drops detail, and makes the features robust to tiny shifts.

slides.title: Convolution by hand
slides.code:
import numpy as np

img = np.array([[1,2,0],[0,1,3],[2,1,0]])
filt = np.array([[1,0],[0,-1]])      # 2x2 filter

out = np.zeros((2,2))
for i in range(2):
    for j in range(2):
        patch = img[i:i+2, j:j+2]
        out[i,j] = np.sum(patch * filt)   # multiply + sum
print(out)

slides.title: One conv block, end to end
The mechanics, in order
bullets: Multiply + sum over each window
Slide everywhere to build a feature map
Stride skips; padding preserves size
Each filter adds one output channel
ReLU keeps positives, pooling shrinks

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 45.
slides.body: Next: build and train a full CNN in PyTorch, layer by layer.

44-4.caption:
Enough theory — let's build a CNN that actually classifies images, train it, and watch the accuracy climb. 💻

This post is a complete, runnable build: load a real dataset, define a small CNN with two conv blocks and a classifier head, write the training loop, and evaluate it. By the end you'll have a working image classifier you wrote yourself, plus the exact pattern you'll reuse for every CNN you build after.

This is the angle where a CNN stops being a diagram and becomes code you can run tonight.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• load and normalize the data
• define the conv-pool-conv architecture
• why the layer shapes line up
• the training loop in full
• evaluate accuracy on held-out data

Copy it, run it, change a layer, watch the accuracy move. That's how it sticks. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 0. Load the data
slides.code:
import torch
from torchvision import datasets, transforms
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader

tf = transforms.Compose([
    transforms.ToTensor(),
    transforms.Normalize((0.5,), (0.5,))])   # scale to [-1,1]

train = datasets.MNIST('.', train=True, download=True, transform=tf)
loader = DataLoader(train, batch_size=64, shuffle=True)

slides.title: 1. Define the CNN
slides.code:
import torch.nn as nn

class CNN(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.conv1 = nn.Conv2d(1, 16, 3, padding=1)   # 28x28 -> 28x28
        self.conv2 = nn.Conv2d(16, 32, 3, padding=1)  # 14x14 -> 14x14
        self.pool = nn.MaxPool2d(2)                   # halves H,W
        self.fc = nn.Linear(32 * 7 * 7, 10)           # classify

slides.title: 2. The forward pass
slides.code:
def forward(self, x):
        x = self.pool(torch.relu(self.conv1(x)))  # 28 -> 14
        x = self.pool(torch.relu(self.conv2(x)))  # 14 -> 7
        x = x.flatten(1)                          # to (batch, 1568)
        return self.fc(x)                         # logits for 10 classes

slides.title: Why 32 * 7 * 7
slides.body: MNIST images start at 28x28. Two 2x2 poolings halve the size twice: 28 -> 14 -> 7. The last conv layer produced 32 channels, so the feature tensor is 32 x 7 x 7 = 1568 values per image. That's exactly the number the flattened vector feeds into the final Linear layer. Get this arithmetic wrong and the shapes won't match.

slides.title: 3. Train the model
slides.code:
model = CNN()
opt = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-3)
loss_fn = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()

for epoch in range(3):
    for xb, yb in loader:
        opt.zero_grad()
        loss = loss_fn(model(xb), yb)
        loss.backward()
        opt.step()
    print(f'epoch {epoch} loss {loss.item():.3f}')

slides.title: 4. Evaluate accuracy
slides.code:
test = datasets.MNIST('.', train=False, transform=tf)
test_loader = DataLoader(test, batch_size=256)

model.eval()
correct = 0
with torch.no_grad():
    for xb, yb in test_loader:
        pred = model(xb).argmax(1)
        correct += (pred == yb).sum().item()
print('accuracy:', correct / len(test))   # ~0.98

slides.title: What each block does
slides.body: conv1 + ReLU + pool finds simple strokes and halves the image. conv2 + ReLU + pool combines those into digit parts and halves it again. Flatten turns the 32x7x7 feature volume into a vector, and the Linear head maps it to 10 class scores. CrossEntropyLoss turns those scores into a training signal.

slides.title: The build, in order
bullets: Normalize inputs before training
conv -> ReLU -> pool, repeated
Track spatial size through each pool
Flatten, then a Linear classifier head
Adam + CrossEntropy, then eval with no_grad

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 45.
slides.body: Next: the traps — shape mismatches, no normalization, and the CNN bugs that won't error out.

44-5.caption:
Your CNN will happily train to 60% accuracy and never tell you the real ceiling was 99%. ⚠️

Most CNN failures aren't crashes — they're quiet underperformance. A flatten size you computed wrong throws a loud error, but forgetting to normalize, leaking test data through augmentation, or letting the receptive field stay too small just gives you a mediocre number you can't explain. Knowing the traps is what separates a working model from a wasted week.

This post is the failure manual: the specific ways CNNs go wrong and the fix for each.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• the flatten shape mismatch
• forgetting to normalize inputs
• too few filters or too small a network
• data augmentation done wrong
• forgetting model.eval() at test time
• ignoring overfitting on small datasets

Learn the failure modes before you trust the accuracy. Save it. 👇

slides.title: The flatten shape mismatch
slides.body: The most common first error: the Linear layer's input size doesn't match the flattened feature volume. You wrote 32*7*7 but your pooling left it at 32*8*8, and PyTorch throws a 'mat1 and mat2 shapes cannot be multiplied' error. It's loud, but it stops beginners cold.

slides.title: Fix: let the shape compute itself
slides.code:
import torch

dummy = torch.zeros(1, 1, 28, 28)        # one fake image
feat = model.features(dummy)             # run conv stack only
flat = feat.flatten(1).shape[1]          # exact flatten size
self.fc = nn.Linear(flat, 10)            # no hand arithmetic

slides.title: Forgetting to normalize
slides.body: Feed raw 0-255 pixel values into a CNN and the activations explode, gradients destabilize, and training crawls or diverges. The fix is to scale inputs to a small, centered range — typically zero mean and unit variance per channel. This single transform is often the difference between 70% and 98% accuracy.

slides.title: Fix: normalize with dataset stats
slides.code:
from torchvision import transforms

tf = transforms.Compose([
    transforms.ToTensor(),               # scales 0-255 -> 0-1
    transforms.Normalize(                # then center + scale
        mean=[0.485, 0.456, 0.406],      # ImageNet RGB stats
        std=[0.229, 0.224, 0.225])])

slides.title: Too small a network
slides.body: A handful of filters can't represent the variety of patterns in real images, so the model underfits — train and test accuracy both stall low. The fix is more capacity: more filters per layer and more conv blocks, increasing channel count with depth (16 -> 32 -> 64). Add capacity before you blame the data.

slides.title: Augmentation done wrong
slides.body: Augmentation (flips, crops, rotations) fights overfitting — but two traps bite. First, never augment the test set; evaluate on clean data. Second, only apply label-preserving transforms: flipping a '6' makes it a '9', and horizontal-flipping text or road signs corrupts the label. Match the augmentation to the task.

slides.title: Forgetting model.eval()
slides.body: Layers like dropout and batch-norm behave differently in training and inference. Skip model.eval() at test time and dropout keeps randomly zeroing activations while batch-norm uses batch statistics instead of running averages — so your accuracy reads lower and noisier than the model actually achieves. One line fixes it.

slides.title: Fix: the eval ritual
slides.code:
model.eval()                 # dropout off, batchnorm uses running stats
with torch.no_grad():        # no gradient tracking -> faster, less memory
    preds = model(test_batch)
model.train()                # switch back before more training

slides.title: Ignoring overfitting
slides.body: On a small dataset, a CNN can memorize the training set — train accuracy near 100% while test accuracy stalls. The fixes: data augmentation, dropout, weight decay, early stopping, and above all transfer learning from a pretrained model. Watch the gap between train and validation curves, not just the train loss.

slides.title: The fixes, in one place
bullets: Compute the flatten size, don't guess it
Normalize inputs every time
Add filters/depth before blaming data
Augment train only, label-preserving
model.eval() + no_grad() at test
Watch the train-vs-val gap for overfitting

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 45.
slides.body: Next topic: Recurrent Neural Networks — how networks handle sequences and memory.

45-1.caption:
Most neural networks forget everything the instant they finish a prediction. A recurrent network is the one that remembers. 🔁

A Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) is built for sequences — text, audio, sensor readings, anything where order carries meaning. Instead of treating each input as isolated, it carries a hidden state from one step to the next, so what it saw a moment ago shapes what it does now. The same small set of weights is reused at every position, sliding along the sequence like a loop.

This post is the map. Before we argue why it matters or trace the math, get crystal clear on what an RNN IS and the vocabulary around it.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• the one-sentence definition that sticks
• hidden state, time step, and the recurrence
• why weight sharing is the whole trick
• how it differs from a feedforward net
• the sequence shapes RNNs handle

Get the mental model straight and the math stops being scary. Save it. 👇

slides.title: What a Recurrent Neural Network Really Is
A network with a memory
slides.body: A recurrent neural network processes a sequence one element at a time, and at each step it keeps a hidden state that summarizes everything it has seen so far. That state is fed back into the network as input for the next step. The loop is what gives the model memory: the past flows forward to influence the present.

slides.title: The recurrence, in one line
slides.body: Every RNN boils down to one update rule applied over and over: the new hidden state is a function of the previous hidden state and the current input. That single equation, repeated at every time step with the same weights, is the entire idea. Everything else is detail.

slides.title: The vocabulary, precisely
bullets: Sequence: ordered inputs x1, x2, ... xT
Time step: one position in the sequence
Hidden state h: the running memory
Recurrence: h_t depends on h_{t-1}
Weight sharing: same weights at every step

slides.title: The loop that gives it memory
Weight sharing is the trick
slides.body: An RNN does not learn a separate set of weights for position 1, position 2, and so on. It learns ONE set and applies it at every step. This keeps the parameter count fixed no matter how long the sequence, and it lets the model generalize a pattern learned at one position to any other position.

slides.title: The recurrence as code
slides.code:
import numpy as np

def rnn_step(x_t, h_prev, Wx, Wh, b):
    # one time step of a vanilla RNN
    return np.tanh(x_t @ Wx + h_prev @ Wh + b)

h = np.zeros(hidden_size)      # start with empty memory
for x_t in sequence:           # walk the sequence
    h = rnn_step(x_t, h, Wx, Wh, b)

slides.title: Many shapes, one mechanism
slides.body: The same recurrent core handles different sequence tasks: one-to-many (image to caption), many-to-one (review to sentiment), and many-to-many (translation, tagging). What changes is where you read inputs and outputs, not the loop itself. That flexibility is why RNNs dominated sequence modeling for years.

slides.title: The mental model, locked
bullets: RNN = a loop with a hidden state
h_t depends on h_{t-1} and x_t
Same weights reused at every step
Built for ordered, variable-length data
Output can depend on the whole history

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 46.
slides.body: Next: why this looped design unlocked language, speech, and time series — and what it made possible.

45-2.caption:
A feedforward net sees a sentence as a bag of disconnected words. An RNN reads it like a sentence — in order, with memory. That difference is everything. 📜

RNNs mattered because they were the first practical way to let a neural network handle data where order is the meaning. Speech, text, stock prices, sensor streams — none of it makes sense if you scramble it. By carrying state across time, RNNs could model context, dependency, and history with a fixed, modest parameter budget.

This post is the 'so what': why a looped network reshaped how we model sequences, and where it still earns its keep.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• why order-blind models fail on sequences
• what hidden state buys you
• why weight sharing beats brute force
• the tasks RNNs unlocked
• their honest limits vs Transformers
• where they still run today

Understand why it matters and the field's last decade clicks. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Order-blind models fail on sequences
slides.body: Feed a sentence to a plain feedforward net and you must flatten it into a fixed vector, throwing away order. 'Dog bites man' and 'man bites dog' look nearly identical to it. For anything where sequence carries meaning — language, audio, time series — that is fatal. You need a model that respects order.

slides.title: Hidden state buys you context
slides.body: The hidden state is a running summary of the past. When the RNN reaches the word 'it', the state already encodes what 'it' might refer to. That memory lets the model resolve dependencies that span many steps, which is exactly what understanding a sequence requires. Context is no longer thrown away.

slides.title: Weight sharing beats brute force
slides.body: Without recurrence, handling a 100-word sentence would mean learning 100 separate weight sets — and a 101-word sentence would break. RNNs reuse one weight set across all positions, so parameter count stays fixed and any length is allowed. A pattern learned at step 3 instantly transfers to step 80.

slides.title: They made variable length normal
slides.body: Before RNNs, variable-length input was a constant headache — pad, truncate, hand-engineer windows. The recurrent loop ingests a sequence of any length naturally, one step at a time, and emits a summary or a per-step output. That single capability opened up an entire class of problems.

slides.title: The honest limits
slides.body: RNNs process steps strictly in order, so they cannot parallelize across time — slow to train on long sequences. Vanilla RNNs also struggle to remember far-back information because gradients vanish. Transformers later beat them on both fronts with attention. RNNs were a leap, not the final word.

slides.title: Sentiment from a sequence
slides.code:
import torch.nn as nn

# many-to-one: a whole review -> one sentiment label
rnn = nn.RNN(input_size=300, hidden_size=128, batch_first=True)
classifier = nn.Linear(128, 2)

outputs, h_n = rnn(review_embeddings)   # h_n = final state
logits = classifier(h_n[-1])            # classify the summary

slides.title: Where they still earn their keep
slides.body: RNNs and their gated cousins (LSTM, GRU) are still strong where sequences are long but each step is cheap, where data streams in real time, or where memory and compute are tight — on-device speech, embedded sensors, low-latency forecasting. Not every problem needs a giant Transformer.

slides.title: Why it matters, in one place
bullets: Respects order, the meaning of sequences
Hidden state captures context
Weight sharing handles any length
Unlocked language, speech, time series
Still useful for streaming, on-device

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 46.
slides.body: Next: open the hood — trace the hidden state forward and watch the gradient flow back through time.

45-3.caption:
Let's actually trace it. One tiny RNN, real numbers, and the hidden state flowing forward step by step. 🔧

The best way to kill the mystery is to do the bookkeeping by hand. We'll initialize an empty memory, run the recurrence over a short sequence, and watch the hidden state evolve. Then we'll unroll the loop and see why training an RNN means backpropagating through time — and why that's where vanishing gradients sneak in.

This post is the engine room: the forward recurrence, the unroll, and the backward pass through time.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• the recurrence equation, decoded
• how the hidden state updates each step
• unrolling the loop into a deep net
• backpropagation through time (BPTT)
• why gradients vanish or explode
• the full forward-and-backward loop

Do the trace once and the loop stops being magic. Save it. 👇

slides.title: An RNN, Traced Step by Step
The recurrence equation
slides.body: The core update is h_t = tanh(W_x · x_t + W_h · h_{t-1} + b). W_x weights the new input, W_h weights the previous memory, and tanh squashes the result into [-1, 1]. An optional output is y_t = W_y · h_t. These three weight matrices are all the parameters an RNN has.

slides.title: The forward pass, in order
bullets: Start with h_0 = 0 (empty memory)
Step t: read x_t and previous h_{t-1}
Compute h_t = tanh(W_x x_t + W_h h_{t-1} + b)
Optionally emit y_t from h_t
Repeat until the sequence ends

slides.title: Forward pass, with cache
slides.code:
import numpy as np

h = np.zeros((H,))
cache = []                       # save states for backprop
for x_t in sequence:
    z = Wx @ x_t + Wh @ h + b
    h = np.tanh(z)               # update memory
    cache.append((x_t, h, z))    # stash for the backward pass

slides.title: Unrolling turns it into a deep net
Backprop through time (BPTT)
slides.body: To train, you unroll the loop and treat each time step as a layer of one very deep network — that share the same weights. Then you run ordinary backpropagation across the unrolled graph. Because the weights are shared, each weight's gradient is the SUM of its contributions at every time step.

slides.title: BPTT, the gradient flow
slides.code:
dWx = dWh = db = 0
dh_next = 0
for x_t, h, z in reversed(cache):     # walk time backward
    dh = dh_from_output + dh_next      # grad into this step
    dz = (1 - h**2) * dh               # through tanh
    dWx += np.outer(dz, x_t)
    dWh += np.outer(dz, h_prev)
    dh_next = Wh.T @ dz                # pass grad to t-1

diagram.axisLabel: gradient magnitude vs distance back in time

slides.title: The vanishing / exploding problem
slides.body: BPTT multiplies the same W_h factor once per step. If its values are below 1, the gradient shrinks toward zero over many steps — the net forgets distant context. If above 1, it blows up. This is exactly why vanilla RNNs struggle with long-range dependencies, and why LSTMs and GRUs were invented.

slides.title: The full loop, framework version
slides.code:
import torch, torch.nn as nn

rnn = nn.RNN(input_size=8, hidden_size=16, batch_first=True)
out, h_n = rnn(x)              # forward: out is per-step, h_n final
loss = criterion(out, target)
loss.backward()               # autograd does BPTT for you
nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(rnn.parameters(), 5.0)  # tame explosions

slides.title: The trace, locked in
bullets: h_t = tanh(W_x x_t + W_h h_{t-1} + b)
Cache states on the forward pass
Unroll = one deep net, shared weights
BPTT sums gradients across all steps
Repeated W_h causes vanish/explode

slides.body: Next: build and train a real RNN end to end in PyTorch, from data to predictions.

45-4.caption:
Enough theory. Here's a complete, runnable RNN in PyTorch — from raw sequence to trained predictions. 💻

We'll build a character-level model that learns to predict the next item in a sequence: define the data, wire up an nn.RNN, write the training loop with backprop-through-time, and generate from the trained model. Every snippet is real and runs. By the end you'll have the full skeleton you can adapt to text, sensor data, or any sequence task.

This post is code-heavy on purpose. Copy it, run it, then break it to learn.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• prep a sequence dataset
• define the RNN model class
• the training loop with BPTT
• gradient clipping for stability
• generating from the trained model
• the LSTM one-line upgrade

Run it once and the loop becomes muscle memory. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 0. Setup + tiny dataset
slides.code:
import torch, torch.nn as nn

text = "hello recurrent world"
chars = sorted(set(text))
stoi = {c: i for i, c in enumerate(chars)}
itos = {i: c for c, i in stoi.items()}
data = torch.tensor([stoi[c] for c in text])
V = len(chars)                 # vocabulary size

slides.title: 1. Define the model
2. The training loop (BPTT)
slides.code:
model = CharRNN(V)
opt = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-2)
loss_fn = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()

for epoch in range(300):
    x = data[:-1].unsqueeze(0)     # inputs
    y = data[1:]                   # next-char targets
    logits, _ = model(x)           # forward
    loss = loss_fn(logits.squeeze(0), y)
    opt.zero_grad()
    loss.backward()                # autograd runs BPTT
    nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(model.parameters(), 5.0)
    opt.step()

slides.title: 3. Generate from the model
slides.code:
model.eval()
h = None
idx = torch.tensor([[stoi['h']]])     # seed character
out = ['h']
for _ in range(20):
    logits, h = model(idx, h)
    idx = logits[:, -1].argmax(-1, keepdim=True)
    out.append(itos[idx.item()])
print(''.join(out))                    # sampled sequence

slides.title: Shapes that trip people up
bullets: nn.RNN expects (batch, time, features) with batch_first=True
out is per-step; h_n is just the final state
CrossEntropyLoss takes raw logits, not softmax
Targets are inputs shifted by one position
Detach hidden state between batches to truncate BPTT

slides.title: 4. One-line upgrade to LSTM
slides.code:
# swap the cell — same training loop works unchanged
self.rnn = nn.LSTM(hidden, hidden, batch_first=True)

# note: LSTM returns h as a (h_n, c_n) tuple
out, (h_n, c_n) = self.rnn(x)   # c_n is the cell state

slides.title: Common runtime errors
slides.body: Forgetting batch_first gives a confusing shape mismatch. Passing softmax probabilities into CrossEntropyLoss double-counts the softmax. Carrying the hidden state across batches without .detach() makes the graph grow until you run out of memory. Skipping gradient clipping lets a single exploding step wreck training.

slides.title: The build, locked in
bullets: Embed -> RNN -> Linear head is the standard stack
loss.backward() does BPTT automatically
Always clip gradients for RNNs
Targets = inputs shifted by one
Swap nn.RNN -> nn.LSTM with almost no changes

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 46.
slides.body: Next: the traps — the mistakes that quietly break RNN training and how to avoid them.

45-5.caption:
Most broken RNNs aren't broken by hard math. They're broken by five quiet, avoidable mistakes. ⚠️

RNNs have sharp edges that don't throw errors — they just give you a model that won't learn, forgets everything, or trains at a crawl. Knowing the traps in advance saves you days of staring at a flat loss curve. Each of these has a clean fix once you can name it.

This post is the field guide: the mistakes, why they bite, and the one-line cure for each.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• ignoring exploding gradients
• expecting long-term memory from a vanilla RNN
• mishandling the hidden state across batches
• wrong tensor shapes and batch ordering
• double-applying softmax in the loss
• padding without masking

Learn the failure modes before you trust the output. Save it. 👇

slides.title: RNN Mistakes That Quietly Kill Training
Ignoring exploding gradients
slides.body: BPTT multiplies gradients across many time steps, so they can blow up into NaNs and destroy your weights in a single step. The fix is one line: clip the gradient norm before the optimizer step. Almost every working RNN training loop has gradient clipping in it for exactly this reason.

slides.title: Fix: clip the gradient norm
slides.code:
loss.backward()
# cap the global gradient norm at 5.0 before stepping
nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(model.parameters(), max_norm=5.0)
optimizer.step()

slides.title: Expecting long memory from a vanilla RNN
slides.body: Vanilla RNNs forget context from many steps back because gradients vanish through repeated multiplication. If your task needs long-range memory — matching brackets, long-document context — a plain RNN will silently underperform. Reach for an LSTM or GRU, whose gating preserves signal over long spans.

slides.title: Mishandling the hidden state
slides.body: Two opposite errors: carrying the hidden state across batches WITHOUT detaching it grows the autograd graph until memory explodes; and re-zeroing it mid-sequence erases context you needed. Detach between truncated chunks, and only reset at true sequence boundaries.

slides.title: Fix: detach between chunks
slides.code:
h = None
for chunk in chunks:                 # truncated BPTT
    logits, h = model(chunk, h)
    loss = loss_fn(logits, targets)
    loss.backward()
    h = h.detach()                   # cut the graph, keep values
    optimizer.step(); optimizer.zero_grad()

slides.title: Wrong shapes and batch order
slides.body: nn.RNN defaults to (time, batch, features). Pass (batch, time, features) without batch_first=True and the model trains on garbage without erroring. Always set batch_first=True if your data is batch-first, and double-check the dimension order before you trust a loss curve.

right.title: Silent (worse)
items: Wrong batch dim, trains on noise

slides.title: Softmax and padding traps
slides.body: CrossEntropyLoss applies log-softmax internally, so feeding it softmax outputs double-counts and flattens gradients — pass raw logits. And when you pad variable-length sequences, mask the padding (use pack_padded_sequence) or the model wastes capacity learning from meaningless pad tokens.

slides.title: Fix: mask the padding
slides.code:
from torch.nn.utils.rnn import pack_padded_sequence

# lengths = real length of each sequence in the batch
packed = pack_padded_sequence(embeds, lengths,
                              batch_first=True, enforce_sorted=False)
out, h_n = rnn(packed)        # RNN now ignores pad steps

slides.title: The checklist, locked in
bullets: Clip gradient norm every step
Use LSTM/GRU for long-range memory
Detach hidden state between chunks
Set batch_first and verify shapes
Pass raw logits; pack padded sequences

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 46.
slides.body: Next day, new topic: we move from recurrence to the architecture that replaced it — attention.

46-1.caption:
A vanilla RNN forgets the start of a sentence by the time it reaches the end. LSTMs and GRUs are the fix — recurrent cells that actually hold on to what matters. 🚪

LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory) and GRU (Gated Recurrent Unit) are the same recurrence you already know, with one upgrade: gates. A gate is a small learned valve, between 0 and 1, that decides how much information to keep, throw away, or let through at each step. With gates, the cell can carry a signal across hundreds of steps without it vanishing.

This post is the map. Before the why, the math, or the code, lock in what these gated cells ARE and the vocabulary around them.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• what a gate actually is
• the LSTM cell state vs hidden state
• the three LSTM gates, named
• how a GRU simplifies the idea
• why gating beats a plain RNN

Get the mental model straight and the equations stop being scary. Save it. 👇

slides.title: What LSTMs and GRUs Really Are
Gated cells, in one line
slides.body: An LSTM or GRU is a recurrent cell with learned gates: small valves that output a number between 0 and 1 for each piece of state. They decide what to forget, what to store, and what to expose. The recurrence is the same as an RNN — the gates are what let memory survive across many steps.

slides.title: What a gate is
slides.body: A gate is just a sigmoid layer producing values in [0, 1], multiplied elementwise against a vector. A 1 means 'let this through fully', a 0 means 'block it'. Because the network learns the gate weights, it learns WHEN to remember and WHEN to forget — instead of being forced to overwrite its memory every step like a vanilla RNN.

slides.title: The LSTM's two memories
slides.body: An LSTM carries two vectors, not one. The cell state c_t is the long-term memory highway — information can ride it across many steps almost untouched. The hidden state h_t is the short-term, exposed output used for predictions. Separating long-term storage from the working output is the LSTM's core trick.

slides.title: The three LSTM gates, named
bullets: Forget gate: how much of c_{t-1} to keep
Input gate: how much new info to write
Candidate: the new content to maybe add
Output gate: how much of c_t to expose as h_t

slides.title: The GRU: a leaner cousin
slides.body: A GRU merges the LSTM's ideas into fewer parts. It has just two gates — an update gate that blends old and new state, and a reset gate that decides how much past to ignore when forming new content. There is no separate cell state; h_t does both jobs. Fewer parameters, often comparable accuracy.

slides.title: Same loop, swap the cell
slides.code:
import torch.nn as nn

# vanilla RNN vs the two gated cells — same call signature
rnn  = nn.RNN(input_size=64, hidden_size=128, batch_first=True)
lstm = nn.LSTM(input_size=64, hidden_size=128, batch_first=True)
gru  = nn.GRU(input_size=64, hidden_size=128, batch_first=True)

out, state = lstm(x)   # state = (h_n, c_n) for LSTM
out, h_n   = gru(x)    # GRU returns a single state

left.title: Vanilla RNN
items: One state, fully overwritten each step

right.title: LSTM / GRU
items: Learns what to keep vs forget

slides.title: Why gating works at all
slides.body: The cell state is updated mostly by ADDITION, not by being squashed through a matrix every step. That additive path is an uninterrupted route for both information and gradients to travel across time. It is the single structural change that defeats the vanishing-gradient problem that crippled vanilla RNNs.

slides.title: The mental model, locked
bullets: Gate = learned valve in [0,1]
LSTM: cell state (long) + hidden (short)
Three LSTM gates: forget, input, output
GRU: update + reset, no cell state
Additive memory path beats vanishing gradients

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 47.
slides.body: Next: why these gated cells mattered so much — what they unlocked that vanilla RNNs never could.

46-2.caption:
For most of the 2010s, if a product understood your speech or finished your sentence, an LSTM was almost certainly underneath it. That is not an accident. 🏆

Vanilla RNNs were elegant but couldn't remember. Gradients vanished over long spans, so the model lost the thread on anything beyond a handful of steps. LSTMs and GRUs solved that with gating — and in doing so they made sequence modeling actually WORK at scale. Machine translation, speech recognition, and early language models all rode on them before Transformers arrived.

This post is the 'so what': why gated cells were a turning point, and where they still beat the giants today.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• the exact RNN failure they fixed
• why long-range memory unlocks real tasks
• LSTM vs GRU — when each wins
• the products they powered
• their honest limits vs Transformers
• where they still run in 2026

Understand why they mattered and a decade of NLP clicks. Save it. 👇

slides.title: They fixed the fatal RNN flaw
slides.body: Vanilla RNNs lose distant context because gradients shrink toward zero through repeated multiplication during training. An LSTM's cell state updates by addition along an almost-uninterrupted path, so the signal — and its gradient — survives across hundreds of steps. This single fix turned recurrence from a toy into a workhorse.

slides.title: Long memory unlocks real tasks
slides.body: Many tasks need information from far back: a translation must remember the subject of a long clause, a speech model must track context across a phrase, a code model must match an opening brace. Vanilla RNNs simply couldn't. Gated cells could, which is why they suddenly made these problems tractable at production quality.

slides.title: LSTM vs GRU — when each wins
They powered real products
slides.body: Before Transformers, gated RNNs were the backbone of Google's neural machine translation, Apple and Amazon speech recognition, Gmail Smart Reply, and countless time-series forecasters. For years, 'state of the art on sequences' essentially meant 'a well-tuned LSTM'. They were the bridge between toy RNNs and modern NLP.

slides.title: The honest limits
slides.body: Gated cells still process steps strictly in order, so they can't parallelize across time — slow to train on long sequences. They ease the vanishing-gradient problem but don't fully eliminate it. Transformers later beat them by connecting any two positions directly with attention and training fully in parallel. Gated RNNs were a leap, not the summit.

slides.title: The seq2seq pattern they enabled
slides.code:
import torch.nn as nn

# encoder compresses the source into a context vector
encoder = nn.LSTM(emb, hid, batch_first=True)
decoder = nn.LSTM(emb, hid, batch_first=True)

_, (h, c) = encoder(src_embeds)      # final state = context
out, _    = decoder(tgt_embeds, (h, c))   # condition on it

slides.title: Where they still earn their keep in 2026
slides.body: Gated RNNs remain strong where sequences are long but each step is cheap, where data streams in real time, or where compute and memory are tight: on-device keyword spotting, embedded sensors, low-latency forecasting, and small-data settings where a Transformer would overfit. Not every problem needs a billion parameters.

slides.title: Why they matter, in one place
bullets: Fixed the vanishing-gradient flaw via gating
Made long-range memory practical
Powered translation, speech, smart reply
GRU = lighter, LSTM = more robust
Still win for streaming, on-device, small data

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 47.
slides.body: Next: open the cell — trace every gate, the cell-state update, and watch why the gradient survives.

46-3.caption:
Let's actually open the cell. Every gate, every equation, and the one update that lets memory survive a thousand steps. 🔬

The mystery of LSTMs evaporates once you trace the four equations in order: forget, input, candidate, output. We'll see exactly how the cell state c_t is updated by addition rather than overwrite, why that additive path keeps gradients alive, and then how a GRU collapses the same idea into two gates and one state.

This post is the engine room: the gate equations, the cell-state update, and why gating defeats the vanishing gradient.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• the four LSTM equations, decoded
• the additive cell-state update
• why gradients stop vanishing
• the GRU's two-gate simplification
• gate values traced on a real step
• LSTM vs GRU math, side by side

Do the trace once and gates stop being magic. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Inside an LSTM, Gate by Gate
The four LSTM equations
slides.body: Each step computes four things from x_t and h_{t-1}: f_t = sigmoid(...) forget gate, i_t = sigmoid(...) input gate, g_t = tanh(...) candidate content, o_t = sigmoid(...) output gate. Sigmoid gates live in [0,1] and act as valves; tanh produces the new content in [-1,1]. Four small linear-plus-nonlinearity layers, that's it.

slides.title: How one LSTM step runs
bullets: Compute gates f, i, o and candidate g
c_t = f_t * c_{t-1} + i_t * g_t
Forget scales old memory, input scales new
h_t = o_t * tanh(c_t)
Pass c_t and h_t to the next step

slides.title: An LSTM cell, from scratch
slides.code:
import numpy as np

def sigmoid(z): return 1 / (1 + np.exp(-z))

def lstm_step(x, h, c, W, b):
    z = W @ np.concatenate([x, h]) + b   # one matmul for all gates
    f, i, g, o = np.split(z, 4)
    f, i, o = sigmoid(f), sigmoid(i), sigmoid(o)
    g = np.tanh(g)
    c = f * c + i * g                    # additive update
    h = o * np.tanh(c)
    return h, c

slides.title: Why gradients stop vanishing
slides.body: In a vanilla RNN the gradient is multiplied by a weight matrix at every step, so it shrinks or explodes. In an LSTM the cell state flows through c_t = f_t * c_{t-1} + ..., a near-identity, additive path. When the forget gate is open (near 1), the gradient passes backward almost unchanged — a 'constant error carousel' that carries signal far back in time.

slides.title: The GRU, in two gates
slides.body: A GRU drops the separate cell state. A reset gate r_t decides how much past to use when forming candidate content; an update gate z_t interpolates: h_t = (1 - z_t) * h_{t-1} + z_t * h_tilde. When z_t is near 0 the old state is kept verbatim — the same additive, gradient-preserving trick with fewer moving parts.

slides.title: A GRU cell, from scratch
slides.code:
def gru_step(x, h, W, b):
    xh = np.concatenate([x, h])
    z = sigmoid(Wz @ xh + bz)            # update gate
    r = sigmoid(Wr @ xh + br)            # reset gate
    xrh = np.concatenate([x, r * h])     # gate the past
    h_tilde = np.tanh(Wh @ xrh + bh)     # candidate
    h = (1 - z) * h + z * h_tilde        # interpolate
    return h

slides.title: LSTM vs GRU, side by side
Trace the gates on one step
slides.code:
# forget gate near 1 -> keep old memory
f = np.array([0.95, 0.10])   # keep dim0, drop dim1
i = np.array([0.20, 0.90])   # write little, then a lot
g = np.array([0.5, -0.8])    # candidate content
c_prev = np.array([2.0, 2.0])
c = f * c_prev + i * g       # -> [1.0, 0.49 ...]
print(c)                     # dim0 mostly kept, dim1 rewritten

slides.title: The trace, locked in
bullets: LSTM: f, i, g, o then c_t = f*c + i*g
h_t = o * tanh(c_t)
Additive c_t path preserves gradients
GRU: z and r, h interpolates old vs new
GRU = fewer params, similar power
