Absolute = index, relative = distance
Without it, sequence order is lost

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 52.
slides.body: Next: why this tiny add-on decides whether your model understands language at all.

51-2.caption:
Drop positional encoding and your Transformer doesn't break — it just quietly becomes a worse model that confuses subject and object, scrambles syntax, and can't count. 🧭

Order is not decoration in language; it carries meaning. "The cat the dog chased" and "the dog the cat chased" are built from the same words but mean opposite things. Code, time series, and DNA are the same: position is signal. Positional encoding is what lets attention exploit that signal instead of ignoring it, and the design you pick decides how far your model can generalize beyond the lengths it trained on.

This post is the 'so what': why order matters, what you lose without it, and why length generalization is the hard open problem.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• why word order changes meaning
• what a model loses with no positions
• the length-extrapolation problem
• why this drove RoPE, ALiBi and friends
• where position matters beyond text

Understand the stakes before you pick a scheme. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Why Position Is the Difference Between Sense and Nonsense
Order carries meaning
slides.body: Natural language uses word order to assign roles: who did what to whom. 'Dog bites man' and 'man bites dog' share every word but mean opposite things. A model that can't see order can't reliably tell subject from object, parse nested clauses, or follow instructions step by step. Position is not optional context — it's core grammar.

slides.title: What you lose without it
slides.body: Strip positional encoding and the Transformer collapses toward a bag-of-words model. It still picks up which tokens co-occur, but loses syntax, counting, and any task that depends on sequence. Benchmarks drop sharply on anything order-sensitive, and the failures are subtle: fluent text that says the wrong thing.

diagram.axisLabel: accuracy without positional encoding (lower = hurt more)

slides.title: The length-extrapolation problem
slides.body: Train a model on sequences up to 2,048 tokens, then feed it 8,000. Many positional schemes have never seen those positions and degrade badly. This is length extrapolation, and it's a central reason long-context models are hard. The encoding you choose largely determines how gracefully the model stretches past its training length.

slides.title: Trained range vs the real world
Why this birthed a whole field
slides.body: The original sinusoidal scheme worked but extrapolated poorly, so researchers built better answers: learned embeddings, relative position, rotary embeddings (RoPE), and ALiBi's distance penalty. Each is an attempt to encode order in a way that scales to longer contexts. The race for long context is, in large part, a race over positional encoding.

slides.title: Proof: shuffling wrecks an order task
slides.code:
import random

def is_sorted(seq):
    return seq == sorted(seq)

x = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
print(is_sorted(x))            # True
random.shuffle(x)
print(is_sorted(x))            # almost always False
# a model blind to order cannot learn 'is_sorted'

slides.title: Why it matters, in one place
bullets: Word order encodes who-did-what
No position -> bag-of-words behavior
Order-sensitive tasks crater without it
Length extrapolation is the hard part
Encoding choice decides long-context reach

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 52.
slides.body: Next: open the hood on the original sinusoidal formula — sines, cosines, and why they were chosen.

51-3.caption:
The original Transformer encoded position with a wall of sines and cosines — and the choice was far more deliberate than it looks. 🌊

Each position gets a vector where different dimensions oscillate at different frequencies, like the hands of a clock running at many speeds. Low dimensions wave slowly, high dimensions fast, so every position gets a unique fingerprint. The magic is that the offset between two positions can be written as a fixed linear transform — which means the model can reason about relative distance using nothing but these absolute codes.

This post is the engine room: the exact formula, why sinusoids, learned vs fixed, and a peek at rotary embeddings.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• the sin/cos formula, term by term
• why many frequencies, not one
• the linear-offset property that matters
• fixed sinusoidal vs learned embeddings
• how RoPE rotates instead of adds

Trace the math once and the design stops feeling arbitrary. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Sinusoidal Encoding, Traced Term by Term
The formula
slides.body: For position pos and dimension i: even dimensions use PE = sin(pos / 10000^(2i/d)), odd dimensions use cos of the same argument. d is the model dimension. Each dimension is a sinusoid whose wavelength grows geometrically from short to very long across the vector.

slides.title: Reading the parts
bullets: pos: which position in the sequence
i: which dimension of the vector
d: total embedding dimension
10000^(2i/d): the per-dimension wavelength
sin on even dims, cos on odd dims

slides.title: Why many frequencies
slides.body: A single sine repeats, so far-apart positions could collide. Stacking many frequencies — fast and slow — gives every position a unique combined pattern, the way hour, minute and second hands together pin an exact time. Low dimensions capture coarse position, high dimensions fine detail.

diagram.axisLabel: oscillation frequency by dimension (high = short wavelength)

slides.title: The property that matters
slides.body: Because of trig identities, the encoding at position pos+k is a fixed linear function of the encoding at pos — the same rotation for any pos. So the model can compute relative offsets with a learned linear map, getting relative-position reasoning out of purely absolute codes. That's the clever part.

slides.title: Sinusoidal encoding from scratch
slides.code:
import numpy as np

def positional_encoding(seq_len, d):
    pos = np.arange(seq_len)[:, None]
    i = np.arange(d)[None, :]
    angle = pos / np.power(10000, (2 * (i // 2)) / d)
    pe = np.where(i % 2 == 0, np.sin(angle), np.cos(angle))
    return pe                       # shape (seq_len, d)

print(positional_encoding(5, 8).shape)   # (5, 8)

slides.title: RoPE: rotate, don't add
slides.body: Rotary Position Embedding skips the addition entirely. It rotates the query and key vectors by an angle proportional to their position, so the dot product between them depends only on their RELATIVE distance. This bakes relative position directly into attention and extrapolates better — which is why most modern LLMs use it.

slides.title: The mechanics, locked
bullets: sin on even dims, cos on odd dims
Geometric range of wavelengths
Many frequencies = unique fingerprints
Offset = fixed linear transform
RoPE rotates Q,K for relative position

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 52.
slides.body: Next: build and visualize positional encodings in PyTorch, then wire them into a model.

51-4.caption:
Enough theory. Here's a complete, runnable positional-encoding toolkit — sinusoidal from scratch, a learned variant, RoPE, and the wiring into a model. 💻

We'll build the classic sinusoidal table, plug it into an embedding layer, implement a learned alternative, write a minimal rotary embedding, and verify the relative-offset property actually holds. Every snippet runs as-is. You'll finish with code you can paste into a real Transformer and a way to sanity-check that your positions are doing what you think.

This post is code-heavy on purpose. Run it, print the shapes, and plot a row to see the wave.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• sinusoidal encoding as a clean function
• adding it to token embeddings correctly
• a learned positional embedding layer
• a minimal RoPE implementation
• verifying the relative-distance property
• the shapes at every step

Run it once and positions stop being abstract. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 0. Sinusoidal encoding
slides.code:
import torch, math

def sinusoidal(seq_len, d):
    pe = torch.zeros(seq_len, d)
    pos = torch.arange(seq_len).unsqueeze(1)
    div = torch.exp(torch.arange(0, d, 2) * (-math.log(10000.0) / d))
    pe[:, 0::2] = torch.sin(pos * div)
    pe[:, 1::2] = torch.cos(pos * div)
    return pe                       # (seq_len, d)

slides.title: 1. Add it to embeddings
2. A learned alternative
3. Minimal RoPE
slides.code:
def rope(x, base=10000):
    # x: (..., T, d) with d even
    T, d = x.shape[-2], x.shape[-1]
    pos = torch.arange(T).unsqueeze(1)
    i = torch.arange(0, d, 2)
    theta = pos / base ** (i / d)     # (T, d/2)
    cos, sin = theta.cos(), theta.sin()
    x1, x2 = x[..., 0::2], x[..., 1::2]
    out = torch.stack([x1*cos - x2*sin, x1*sin + x2*cos], -1)
    return out.flatten(-2)            # rotated x

slides.title: 4. Run + check shapes
slides.code:
emb = Embed(vocab=100, d=32)
ids = torch.randint(0, 100, (2, 10))   # (B=2, T=10)
out = emb(ids)
print(out.shape)                        # torch.Size([2, 10, 32])
print(sinusoidal(10, 32).shape)         # torch.Size([10, 32])

slides.title: 5. Verify the relative property
slides.code:
pe = sinusoidal(50, 64)
# distance between encodings depends mainly on the GAP
d_5_10  = (pe[5]  - pe[10]).norm()
d_25_30 = (pe[25] - pe[30]).norm()
print(round(float(d_5_10), 3),
      round(float(d_25_30), 3))   # close: same gap of 5

slides.title: Shapes & gotchas
bullets: PE shape is (seq_len, d) — broadcast over batch
Register PE as a buffer, not a parameter
Slice pe[:T] to match the actual length
RoPE needs an even dimension d
Learned pos caps out at max_len

slides.title: The build, locked
bullets: sinusoidal() returns a (T,d) table
Add it to token embeddings, sliced to T
Learned = nn.Embedding over positions
RoPE rotates pairs of dims by angle
Check the relative-distance property

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 52.
slides.body: Next: the silent positional-encoding mistakes that corrupt results without a single error.

51-5.caption:
Positional bugs almost never crash. They just hand you a model that trains fine, scores fine in the lab, then falls apart on longer inputs or subtly mangles meaning. ⚠️

Forget to add the encoding and your Transformer silently becomes a bag of words. Slice the table to the wrong length and positions misalign by one. Use learned embeddings then feed a longer sequence and you index out of range — or worse, clamp and lie. Each trap is quiet, and each has a clean fix once you can name it.

This post is the field guide: the five mistakes, why they bite, and the cure for each.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• forgetting to add positions at all
• length mismatch and off-by-one slicing
• exceeding a learned encoding's max length
• double-counting position across layers
• expecting extrapolation that won't happen

Learn the failure modes before you trust the loss curve. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Positional Encoding Mistakes That Bite Silently
Forgetting to add positions
slides.body: Define a beautiful positional table, then forget the one line that adds it to the embeddings. No error fires — the shapes still match. The model just trains as a bag of words, plateaus on anything order-sensitive, and you blame the architecture. The fix is the single addition you skipped.

slides.title: Fix: actually add it
slides.code:
# WRONG: PE computed but never used
x = self.tok(ids)
# pe = self.pe[:x.size(1)]   # forgotten!

# RIGHT: add positions to token embeddings
x = self.tok(ids) + self.pe[:x.size(1)]

slides.title: Off-by-one / length mismatch
slides.body: Your PE table is (max_len, d) but the batch is shorter. Add the full table and shapes clash — or you slice with the wrong index and every position shifts by one. A one-slot shift is subtle: the model still trains, just with smeared positional signal and a quiet accuracy tax.

slides.title: Fix: slice to the real length
slides.code:
T = x.size(1)
# slice exactly to the sequence length, from 0
x = x + self.pe[:T]            # not self.pe, not pe[1:T+1]
assert self.pe[:T].shape == x.shape[1:]

slides.title: Exceeding learned max length
slides.body: Learned positional embeddings define exactly max_len vectors. Feed a longer sequence and you index out of range — a crash if you're lucky, a silent clamp or wraparound if you're not. Either way, positions beyond training are undefined. Sinusoidal and RoPE avoid this by being defined for any position.

slides.title: Double-counting position
slides.body: Add positions once at the input — not again inside every layer. Some people re-add the encoding at each block, or stack a learned scheme on top of RoPE, doubling the signal and confusing the model. Inject position at the right place, once (RoPE is the exception: it acts inside attention, by design).

yes.leaf: Apply inside attention to Q,K — no input add

slides.title: Expecting free extrapolation
slides.body: Training to 2k then running 16k and expecting coherence is wishful thinking. Most schemes degrade past their training length; even RoPE needs tricks like position interpolation or NTK scaling. Don't assume long-context works — measure it at the length you'll actually deploy, and extend deliberately.

slides.title: The checklist, locked
bullets: Actually ADD the encoding to embeddings
Slice the table to the real length, from 0
Watch learned max_len on long inputs
Inject position once, in the right place
Never assume free length extrapolation
Measure at your real deployment length

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 52.
slides.body: Next day, new topic: snap attention, positions and feed-forward together into the full Transformer block.

52-1.caption:
An embedding is just a list of numbers — but if you let yourself SEE it as a point in space, half the mystery of modern AI evaporates. 🗺️

A model turns a word, an image, or a sentence into a vector: an arrow pointing somewhere in a high-dimensional room. The trick is that the geometry means something. Things that are similar land close together; things that differ drift apart. "Distance" becomes "how related," and the whole abstract idea of meaning gets a shape you can reason about.

This post is the mental model: what an embedding is, why we draw it as a point, and the vocabulary you'll reuse every time you touch a vector.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• what an embedding actually is
• why a vector is a point in space
• what "close together" means for meaning
• dense vs sparse, and why dense won
• the words you'll see everywhere

Get the picture in your head and the rest of the week clicks. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Embeddings: Turning Meaning Into a Map
The one-line idea
slides.body: An embedding is a fixed-length list of numbers that represents a thing — a word, image, user, or sentence — as a point in space. The key property is that geometry encodes meaning: similar items sit near each other, unrelated items sit far apart. A model learns these positions so distance becomes a usable signal.

slides.title: Why a vector is a point
slides.body: A vector of d numbers is just coordinates in a d-dimensional space, exactly like (x, y) is a point on a page. We can't draw 768 dimensions, but the intuition holds: each number nudges the point along one axis. Once you see vectors as locations, similarity, clusters, and directions all become things you can picture.

slides.title: Close = related
slides.body: Distance in embedding space is a proxy for relatedness. 'cat' and 'kitten' land near each other; 'cat' and 'spreadsheet' do not. The model never sees the word 'similar' — it just arranges points so that the things it should treat alike are neighbors. That layout is the whole point.

slides.title: Why dense won
slides.body: One-hot vectors give every word its own slot, so 'cat' and 'dog' are exactly as far apart as 'cat' and 'galaxy' — meaningless geometry. Dense embeddings pack meaning into a few hundred shared dimensions, so related items overlap. Less memory, more signal, and a space where math actually reflects semantics.

slides.title: An embedding is just an array
slides.code:
import numpy as np

# a tiny 4-dimensional embedding for 'cat'
cat = np.array([0.91, -0.20, 0.33, 0.05])

print(cat.shape)   # (4,)  -> a point in 4D space
print(cat[0])      # 0.91  -> position along axis 0

slides.title: From thing to vector to use
Vocabulary lock-in
bullets: Dimension (d): length of the vector
Dense: all numbers carry meaning
Embedding space: the high-D room
Neighbor: a nearby, similar point
Similarity: closeness in that space

slides.title: The mental model, locked
bullets: Embedding = a point in space
Geometry encodes meaning
Close together = related
Dense beats one-hot for semantics
Distance is the signal you exploit

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 53.
slides.body: Next: why this one trick quietly powers search, recommendations, and RAG.

52-2.caption:
If you can turn anything into a point in space, you can do something almost magical: find the nearest neighbors and call it intelligence. 🔍

That single move — embed, then measure distance — is the engine under semantic search, recommendations, deduplication, clustering, and retrieval-augmented generation. You stop matching exact keywords and start matching MEANING. "How do I reset my password" finds the doc titled "Account recovery steps," even with zero words in common, because the two land close in the space.

This post is the 'so what': why embeddings matter, what they unlock, and where the geometry quietly pays the bills.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• why keyword search breaks
• how embeddings power semantic search
• why RAG depends on them
• recommendations as nearest neighbors
• the cross-domain superpower (text, image, audio)

Understand the payoff before you tune a single parameter. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Why Embeddings Quietly Run the Internet
Keyword search is brittle
slides.body: Classic search matches strings: type 'car' and you miss 'automobile', 'vehicle', and 'sedan'. It has no idea those words mean the same thing. The moment a user phrases things differently than your documents, exact-match search fails — and users almost always phrase things differently.

slides.title: Semantic search, the fix
slides.body: Embed the query and every document into the same space, then return the documents whose vectors sit closest to the query's. Now 'how do I reset my password' matches 'account recovery steps' even with no shared words, because both land in the same neighborhood. You search by meaning, not by spelling.

slides.title: RAG runs on this
slides.body: Retrieval-augmented generation embeds your knowledge base, embeds the user's question, and pulls the closest chunks to feed the model as context. Every RAG pipeline is an embedding-and-nearest-neighbor step bolted to an LLM. If retrieval grabs the wrong neighbors, the model answers from the wrong source — so the geometry is the quality ceiling.

slides.title: Recommendations = neighbors
slides.body: Embed users and items into a shared space and 'what should we show next' becomes 'what's nearest'. Songs you'd like sit close to songs you've played; products you'd buy sit close to your past purchases. Netflix, Spotify, and shopping feeds are, at heart, nearest-neighbor lookups over learned vectors.

slides.title: Search by meaning in 8 lines
The cross-domain trick
slides.body: Embeddings aren't just for text. Images, audio, code, and even users can be mapped into vector spaces — and models like CLIP put text and images in the SAME space, so 'a photo of a dog' lands near actual dog photos. One geometry, many modalities. That's why embeddings are the universal interface of modern AI.

slides.title: Why it matters, in one place
bullets: Keyword search misses meaning
Embeddings search by meaning
RAG is embed + nearest neighbor
Recommendations are neighbor lookups
Same idea spans text, image, audio

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 53.
slides.body: Next: open the hood — how vectors get trained and how we measure 'close'.

52-3.caption:
Embeddings feel like magic until you see the two gears underneath: how the numbers get learned, and how we measure 'close'. 🔧

Nobody hand-writes these vectors. A model starts with random numbers and nudges them so that things appearing in similar contexts end up nearby — 'you shall know a word by the company it keeps'. Then, to actually USE the space, you pick a distance: cosine similarity for direction, Euclidean for straight-line gap. The choice quietly shapes every result you get back.

This post is the engine room: how training shapes the space, what the famous king−man+queen analogy really shows, and how cosine and Euclidean differ.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• the distributional hypothesis
• how contrastive training pulls and pushes
• cosine vs Euclidean distance
• why direction often beats magnitude
• vector arithmetic and analogies

Trace it once and the geometry stops feeling like a black box. Save it. 👇

slides.title: How the Space Gets Built — and Measured
The distributional idea
slides.body: The training principle is old: 'you shall know a word by the company it keeps.' Words appearing in similar contexts should get similar vectors. So we train a model to predict a word from its neighbors (or vice versa), and the side effect is an embedding space where context-similar items cluster together.

slides.title: Pull together, push apart
slides.body: Modern embedding training is contrastive: take pairs that should be similar (a sentence and its paraphrase) and pull their vectors closer, while pushing unrelated pairs apart. Repeat over millions of examples and the space self-organizes — neighbors become meaningful, distance becomes trustworthy. No one places the points by hand.

slides.title: Cosine vs Euclidean
bullets: Euclidean: straight-line distance between points
Cosine: angle between the two arrows
Cosine ignores length, cares about direction
Most text search uses cosine
Normalize first and the two agree

slides.title: Why direction often wins
slides.body: In text, a vector's length often tracks how common or long a piece is, not what it means. Two documents about the same topic can have very different magnitudes but point the same way. Cosine throws away length and keeps direction, so it usually matches semantic similarity better than raw Euclidean distance.

slides.title: Cosine vs Euclidean, side by side
slides.code:
import numpy as np

a = np.array([1.0, 1.0])
b = np.array([2.0, 2.0])   # same direction, longer

euclid = np.linalg.norm(a - b)
cosine = a @ b / (np.linalg.norm(a) * np.linalg.norm(b))

print(round(euclid, 3))   # 1.414 -> 'far' by distance
print(round(cosine, 3))   # 1.0   -> identical by angle

slides.title: The famous analogy
slides.code:
import numpy as np

king  = np.array([0.8, 0.6, 0.1])
man   = np.array([0.7, 0.1, 0.2])
woman = np.array([0.2, 0.1, 0.2])

queen_guess = king - man + woman
print(queen_guess)   # should land near the real 'queen' vector

slides.title: Engine room, summarized
bullets: Similar context -> similar vector
Contrastive: pull + pairs, push - pairs
Cosine = angle, Euclidean = gap
Direction usually beats magnitude
Analogies live as vector arithmetic

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 53.
slides.body: Next: get your hands dirty — embed real text and rank it with code.

52-4.caption:
Enough theory — let's embed real sentences, see the raw numbers, and rank them by meaning. 💻

In this post you build a tiny semantic search end to end: load a sentence-embedding model, turn a handful of sentences into vectors, then score a query against them with cosine similarity. You'll watch unrelated sentences score low and paraphrases score high — proof that the geometry actually works. Then we reduce the vectors to 2D so you can literally plot the meaning.

This post is code-heavy and runnable. Copy it, run it, change the sentences, and feel the space respond.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• load a real embedding model
• embed sentences into vectors
• rank by cosine similarity
• reduce to 2D and plot it
• read the shape of the result

Run it once and it stops being abstract. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Embeddings, Hands-On: Search You Can Run
0. Install + load a model
slides.code:
# pip install sentence-transformers scikit-learn matplotlib
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer

model = SentenceTransformer("all-MiniLM-L6-v2")  # 384-dim vectors
print(model.get_sentence_embedding_dimension())   # 384

slides.title: 1. Embed some sentences
slides.code:
sentences = [
    "How do I reset my password?",
    "Steps to recover your account",
    "The weather is nice today",
]

vecs = model.encode(sentences)
print(vecs.shape)   # (3, 384) -> 3 points in 384D space

slides.title: 2. Rank a query by meaning
slides.code:
from sentence_transformers import util

query = model.encode("forgot my login")
scores = util.cos_sim(query, vecs)[0]

for s, sent in sorted(zip(scores, sentences), reverse=True):
    print(round(float(s), 3), sent)
# ~0.55 recover account / ~0.50 reset password / ~0.05 weather

lines.text: 0.50  How do I reset my password?

slides.title: 3. Reduce to 2D to plot it
slides.code:
from sklearn.decomposition import PCA
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

xy = PCA(n_components=2).fit_transform(vecs)
plt.scatter(xy[:, 0], xy[:, 1])
for (x, y), s in zip(xy, sentences):
    plt.annotate(s, (x, y))
plt.show()   # the two account sentences sit close together

slides.title: Reading the result
bullets: Two account sentences cluster tightly
The weather sentence sits far off
Distance matched meaning, not words
PCA loses detail but keeps gist
This is semantic search in miniature

slides.title: 4. Turn it into a search function
slides.code:
import numpy as np

def search(query, corpus, model, k=2):
    qv = model.encode(query)
    cv = model.encode(corpus)
    scores = util.cos_sim(qv, cv)[0].numpy()
    top = np.argsort(scores)[::-1][:k]
    return [(corpus[i], round(float(scores[i]), 3)) for i in top]

print(search("can't sign in", sentences, model))

slides.title: Hands-on recap
bullets: A model turns text into 384-D vectors
cos_sim ranks by meaning
PCA lets you plot the space
k controls how many you return
Swap the sentences and re-run

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 53.
slides.body: Next: the traps — where embeddings quietly lie to you.

52-5.caption:
Embeddings fail quietly. The code runs, the numbers look fine, and the results are subtly wrong — which is the most dangerous kind of bug. ⚠️

The traps are almost never in the math; they're in the assumptions. Mixing vectors from two different models. Trusting a 2D plot as if it were the real space. Forgetting to normalize before cosine. Treating 'nearby' as 'true' when the model just learned your data's biases. Each one produces plausible output that sends you down the wrong path.

This post is the field guide: the mistakes that bite people in production and exactly how to avoid each one.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• comparing vectors across models
• misreading 2D projections
• forgetting to normalize
• the curse of dimensionality
• mistaking 'close' for 'correct'

Learn the failure modes before they cost you a launch. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Mixing models
slides.body: Vectors from model A and model B live in different spaces, even if both are 768-dim. Comparing them with cosine gives numbers, but the numbers are meaningless. Always embed your query and your corpus with the exact same model and version. Re-embed everything when you upgrade.

slides.title: Trusting the 2D plot
slides.body: PCA and t-SNE squash hundreds of dimensions into two so you can look at them — but a lot is lost. Points that look close in 2D may be far in the real space, and t-SNE distances between clusters are not meaningful. Use projections for intuition, never for decisions.

slides.title: Forgetting to normalize
slides.body: Cosine similarity assumes you care about direction, not length. If your index uses dot product on un-normalized vectors, longer vectors score artificially high and dominate results. Normalize vectors to unit length up front, or use a cosine index — then dot product and cosine agree.

slides.title: Normalize before you compare
slides.code:
import numpy as np

def normalize(v):
    return v / np.linalg.norm(v)

a = np.array([10.0, 0.0])   # long
b = np.array([1.0, 0.0])    # short, same direction

print(a @ b)                       # 10.0  -> length inflates it
print(normalize(a) @ normalize(b)) # 1.0   -> direction only

slides.title: The curse of dimensionality
slides.body: In very high dimensions, distances between random points bunch up — everything starts to look roughly equidistant. Good embeddings fight this by being learned, but it's why naive distance on raw high-D data fails, and why approximate-nearest-neighbor indexes and dimensionality matter so much at scale.

diagram.axisLabel: contrast between nearest and farthest (higher = clearer)

slides.title: 'Close' is not 'correct'
slides.body: Embeddings encode whatever pattern the training data contained — including bias and spurious correlations. Two items can be neighbors for the wrong reason: shared boilerplate, similar length, or a stereotype the model absorbed. Nearest-neighbor results are a hypothesis, not ground truth. Always validate retrieval quality.

slides.title: Guardrail: confirm same model + dims
Avoid all five
bullets: Same model + version for everything
Plots for intuition, not decisions
Normalize before cosine / dot
Mind dimensionality at scale
Validate that neighbors are right

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 53.
slides.body: Next day: a new topic — keep the streak going.

53-1.caption:
PyTorch is not a neural network library you call. It's a thing that does calculus for you, on tensors, on a GPU, while you write ordinary Python. 🔥

Strip away the hype and PyTorch is three ideas stacked together: a NumPy-like array (the tensor) that runs on GPUs, an engine that records every operation and hands you the gradient (autograd), and a thin set of building blocks (nn.Module) for assembling models. Everything else is convenience on top.

This post is the map. Before we argue why researchers reach for it or open up how the gradients actually flow, get clear on what PyTorch IS — and what each of its core pieces is for.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• what a tensor is and why it isn't just a NumPy array
• the three layers: tensor, autograd, nn
• define-by-run vs static graphs
• where the GPU fits in
• what PyTorch is NOT

Get the mental model first; the API stops feeling random. Save it. 👇

slides.title: What PyTorch actually is
slides.body: PyTorch is a Python library for tensor math with automatic differentiation. You write normal code that multiplies and adds arrays; PyTorch quietly records those operations and can compute the derivative of any output with respect to any input. That single capability is what turns Python into a deep-learning engine.

slides.title: The tensor
slides.body: A tensor is an n-dimensional array, like a NumPy array, but with two superpowers: it can live on a GPU, and it can track the operations done to it so gradients flow back. A scalar is a 0-D tensor, a vector 1-D, a matrix 2-D, an image batch 4-D.

slides.title: Your first tensor
slides.code:
import torch
x = torch.tensor([[1., 2.], [3., 4.]])
print(x.shape)      # torch.Size([2, 2])
print(x.dtype)      # torch.float32
print(x.device)     # cpu
print(x @ x)        # matrix multiply

levels.label: Tensor
levels.sub: n-D array on CPU or GPU

slides.title: Define-by-run
slides.body: PyTorch builds its computation graph as your code executes, not ahead of time. Each forward pass can take a different path — a Python if or loop just works. This 'define-by-run' style is why PyTorch feels like plain Python and is easy to debug with print and a debugger.

slides.title: What PyTorch is NOT
slides.body: It is not an autoML tool, not a training loop you call once, and not a model zoo by itself. PyTorch gives you the primitives; you write the loop. Higher-level wrappers like Lightning or fastai sit on top. Knowing the boundary keeps you from looking for buttons that don't exist.

slides.title: The 30-second mental model
bullets: Tensor = GPU-capable NumPy array
autograd = free derivatives
nn.Module = Lego blocks for models
You still write the training loop
Everything is just Python objects

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 54.
slides.body: Next: why PyTorch took over research and is now eating production too.

53-2.caption:
In 2017, TensorFlow owned deep learning. By 2020, nearly every new research paper shipped its code in PyTorch. That flip wasn't marketing — it was ergonomics. 📈

PyTorch won because it let researchers think in Python instead of fighting a graph compiler. When your experiment is a loop with a few if-statements and you want to print a tensor mid-forward, a define-by-run framework saves hours every day. Production caught up later, once TorchScript and compilation closed the speed gap.

This post is about the WHY: the specific pains PyTorch removed, the ecosystem that grew around it, and where it still costs you.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• why research abandoned static graphs
• the debugging story that sold people
• the ecosystem flywheel (HF, torchvision, Lightning)
• research-to-production, one language
• where PyTorch still bites you

Understand the why and you'll know when to reach for it. Save it. 👇

slides.title: The core reason: it feels like Python
slides.body: Deep learning research is iteration: change a line, run, inspect, repeat. Static-graph frameworks made you define the whole computation, compile it, then run it in a session — so a one-line tweak meant rebuilding the graph. PyTorch executes line by line, so the edit-run-inspect loop collapses to seconds.

slides.title: Debugging that actually works
slides.body: Because the graph is your live Python stack, a bug surfaces where it happens. You drop a breakpoint inside forward(), step through, and print intermediate tensors as real values. No special session, no symbolic placeholders. For people who spend most of their time finding why a model won't train, this alone is decisive.

slides.title: Debug mid-forward, no ceremony
The ecosystem flywheel
slides.body: Once researchers wrote in PyTorch, libraries followed. Hugging Face Transformers, torchvision, timm, and PyTorch Lightning all assume PyTorch tensors and modules. More tools pulled in more users, which pulled in more tools. Network effects locked it in.

slides.title: One language, lab to prod
slides.body: The same model you prototype can be exported with TorchScript or compiled with torch.compile and served behind TorchServe — no rewrite into a separate inference framework. That research-to-production continuity is a big reason companies, not just labs, now standardize on it.

slides.title: Where it still bites
bullets: Eager mode can be slower than compiled graphs
Mobile / edge story is thinner than TFLite
You own the training loop — more to get wrong
Version churn between major releases

slides.title: When to reach for PyTorch
bullets: Research or custom architectures
You need fast iteration + debugging
You live in the HF ecosystem
You want lab-to-prod in one stack

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 54.
slides.body: Next: the mechanics — how autograd actually computes your gradients.

53-3.caption:
When you call loss.backward(), no magic happens. PyTorch just walks backward through a graph it secretly recorded on the way forward, applying the chain rule one node at a time. ⚙️

The trick is the computation graph. Every operation on a tensor with requires_grad=True adds a node that remembers how to compute its own local derivative. Run the forward pass and you've also built the recipe for the backward pass. Calling backward() replays that recipe in reverse.

This post opens the hood: how the graph is built, what reverse-mode autodiff is doing, where gradients land, and why you must zero them.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• how the graph is recorded during forward
• reverse-mode autodiff and the chain rule
• grad_fn: the breadcrumbs
• where gradients accumulate (.grad)
• why optimizer.zero_grad() exists

See the graph and backprop stops being a black box. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Forward pass builds a graph
slides.body: Mark a tensor with requires_grad=True and PyTorch starts recording. Each operation creates a new tensor that stores a grad_fn — a reference to the function that knows its local derivative and its parents. By the end of the forward pass you have a directed graph from inputs to the final loss.

slides.title: Watch the graph form
slides.code:
import torch
x = torch.tensor(2.0, requires_grad=True)
y = x ** 2 + 3 * x
print(y)            # tensor(10., grad_fn=<AddBackward0>)
print(y.grad_fn)    # the recorded op

slides.title: backward() runs the chain rule
slides.body: Calling y.backward() starts at the output and propagates a gradient backward through each grad_fn. At every node PyTorch multiplies the incoming gradient by that node's local derivative — the chain rule, applied mechanically. This is reverse-mode autodiff: one backward sweep gives every input's gradient.

slides.title: Get the gradient
slides.code:
y.backward()        # dy/dx where y = x^2 + 3x
print(x.grad)       # tensor(7.)  -> 2*x + 3 at x=2

slides.title: Gradients land in .grad
slides.body: After backward(), each leaf tensor with requires_grad holds its gradient in the .grad attribute. Model parameters are exactly such leaves, so param.grad now tells the optimizer which direction reduces the loss. The graph itself is freed after backward unless you ask to retain it.

slides.title: Why backward is reverse, not forward
slides.body: Deep nets have one scalar loss but millions of parameters. Reverse-mode computes the gradient of one output with respect to all inputs in a single pass, which is cheap. Forward-mode would need one pass per parameter. That asymmetry is why every framework backpropagates.

slides.title: Gradients accumulate by default
slides.body: PyTorch ADDS new gradients onto whatever is already in .grad. That's deliberate (it enables gradient accumulation across mini-batches), but it means if you forget to clear them, gradients from old batches pile up and your updates go haywire. Hence optimizer.zero_grad() every step.

slides.title: The canonical step order
slides.code:
optimizer.zero_grad()   # clear old grads
loss = loss_fn(model(x), y)
loss.backward()          # fill .grad
optimizer.step()         # update params

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 54.
slides.body: Next: a full, runnable training loop from data to trained model.

53-4.caption:
Theory closes when you see the whole loop run. Here is a complete PyTorch training script — model, data, loss, optimizer, and the loop — small enough to read, real enough to run. 💻

Every PyTorch program has the same skeleton: define a model as an nn.Module, wrap your data in a DataLoader, pick a loss and an optimizer, then loop: forward, loss, zero_grad, backward, step. Memorize that rhythm once and every example you ever read becomes familiar.

This is the code-heavy post. We build a tiny classifier end to end and call out the line that beginners always get wrong.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• defining a model with nn.Module
• DataLoader for batching
• the five-line training loop
• switching train vs eval mode
• moving everything to the GPU

Copy it, run it, then change one thing. Save it. 👇

slides.title: A Full PyTorch Loop in 30 Lines
1. Define the model
2. Data + DataLoader
slides.code:
from torch.utils.data import TensorDataset, DataLoader

X = torch.randn(150, 4)
y = torch.randint(0, 3, (150,))
ds = TensorDataset(X, y)
loader = DataLoader(ds, batch_size=16, shuffle=True)

slides.title: 3. Loss + optimizer
slides.code:
model = Net()
loss_fn = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()
optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-3)

slides.title: 4. The training loop
slides.code:
model.train()
for epoch in range(10):
    for xb, yb in loader:
        optimizer.zero_grad()
        preds = model(xb)
        loss = loss_fn(preds, yb)
        loss.backward()
        optimizer.step()
    print(epoch, loss.item())

slides.title: train() vs eval()
slides.body: model.train() and model.eval() flip a switch on layers like Dropout and BatchNorm, which behave differently during training and inference. Forgetting model.eval() at test time is a classic bug: dropout stays on and your accuracy looks worse than it is.

slides.title: 5. Evaluate correctly
slides.code:
model.eval()
with torch.no_grad():          # skip graph building
    logits = model(X)
    acc = (logits.argmax(1) == y).float().mean()
print('acc', acc.item())

slides.title: Move it to the GPU
slides.body: Pick a device once, then send the model and every batch to it. Tensors must share a device for an op to run, so move both model and data. The .to(device) pattern is the standard idiom and scales from your laptop to a cluster.

slides.title: 6. GPU in three lines
slides.code:
device = 'cuda' if torch.cuda.is_available() else 'cpu'
model.to(device)
# inside loop:
xb, yb = xb.to(device), yb.to(device)

slides.title: The rhythm to memorize
bullets: zero_grad -> forward -> loss
loss.backward() -> optimizer.step()
model.train() to train, eval() to test
no_grad() around evaluation
everything on the same device

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 54.
slides.body: Next: the silent bugs that make PyTorch code 'run' but train wrong.

53-5.caption:
The cruel thing about PyTorch bugs is that the code runs. No crash, no error — your loss just refuses to go down, or quietly leaks memory until it dies an hour in. 🩹

Most PyTorch mistakes aren't syntax. They're conceptual: forgetting to clear gradients, leaving dropout on at test time, mismatching devices, or holding onto the graph when you only wanted a number. The fixes are one line each — once you know the symptom.

This post is the field guide to the five errors that burn almost everyone, what they look like, and the exact line that fixes them.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• forgetting optimizer.zero_grad()
• .item() vs keeping tensors (memory leak)
• train() / eval() mode mistakes
• CPU/GPU device mismatches
• wrong loss/activation pairing

Learn the symptoms now, not at 2am. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 1. Forgetting zero_grad()
slides.body: Skip optimizer.zero_grad() and gradients from every previous batch keep adding up. Your effective learning rate explodes, loss diverges or oscillates, and nothing crashes. The fix is one line at the top of each step.

slides.title: Fix: clear before backward
slides.code:
for xb, yb in loader:
    optimizer.zero_grad()   # <- the missing line
    loss = loss_fn(model(xb), yb)
    loss.backward()
    optimizer.step()

slides.title: 2. Accumulating tensors, not floats
slides.body: Writing total_loss += loss keeps the whole computation graph alive because loss is a graph-attached tensor. Memory climbs every iteration until you OOM. Add .item() (or .detach()) to store the plain number instead.

slides.title: Fix: detach for logging
slides.code:
running = 0.0
for xb, yb in loader:
    loss = loss_fn(model(xb), yb)
    running += loss.item()   # float, not tensor

slides.title: 3. Wrong mode at test time
slides.body: Evaluate without model.eval() and Dropout stays active and BatchNorm keeps updating its running stats from your test batch. Results look noisy and worse than reality. Always eval() before inference, and wrap it in torch.no_grad().

slides.title: Fix: eval + no_grad
slides.code:
model.eval()
with torch.no_grad():
    preds = model(X_test)
model.train()   # switch back to keep training

slides.title: 4. Device mismatch
slides.body: Model on the GPU, a batch still on the CPU, and you get 'Expected all tensors to be on the same device'. It happens the moment you forget to move one input. Send model and every batch to the same device, every step.

slides.title: 5. Loss / activation mismatch
slides.body: CrossEntropyLoss already applies log-softmax internally, so feed it raw logits, NOT a softmax output. Adding your own softmax double-counts and trains poorly. Likewise pair BCEWithLogitsLoss with raw logits, not a sigmoid.

slides.title: Fix: feed logits, not probabilities
slides.code:
# WRONG: nn.CrossEntropyLoss()(softmax(logits), y)
# RIGHT:
loss = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()(logits, y)  # logits!

slides.title: The 2am checklist
bullets: zero_grad every step
.item() for logging
eval() + no_grad() to test
same device for model + data
logits into CrossEntropyLoss

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 54.
slides.body: Next: we leave PyTorch and tackle the next deep-learning building block.

54-1.caption:
TensorFlow is not one thing. It's a numerical engine, a graph compiler, a high-level model API, and a whole deployment family — bundled under one name. 🧩

Strip the brand away and the core idea is simple: you describe computations on tensors (n-dimensional arrays), and TensorFlow runs them fast on CPU, GPU, or TPU, while computing gradients for you. On top of that sits Keras, the friendly API most people actually write.

This post is the map. Before we argue why it still matters or open up how the execution model works, get clear on what TensorFlow IS and what each layer is for.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• what a tensor is and why TF is built around it
• the layers: ops, autodiff, Keras, deployment
• eager vs graph execution in one breath
• where the TPU and the rest of the family fit
• what TensorFlow is NOT

Get the mental model first; the API stops feeling huge. Save it. 👇

slides.title: What TensorFlow actually is
slides.body: TensorFlow is an open-source library for numerical computation built around tensors. You describe math on arrays; TF runs it efficiently on CPU, GPU, or TPU and can compute the gradient of any output with respect to any input. That autodiff plus hardware acceleration is what turns it into a deep-learning engine.

slides.title: The tensor
slides.body: A tensor is an n-dimensional array with a fixed dtype, like a NumPy array but designed to run on accelerators and flow through a computation graph. A scalar is rank-0, a vector rank-1, a matrix rank-2, a batch of RGB images rank-4. The whole library is named after this object.

slides.title: Your first tensor
slides.code:
import tensorflow as tf
x = tf.constant([[1., 2.], [3., 4.]])
print(x.shape)    # (2, 2)
print(x.dtype)    # <dtype: 'float32'>
print(tf.matmul(x, x))  # matrix multiply
print(x.numpy())  # back to NumPy

slides.title: Eager by default, graphs on demand
slides.body: Modern TensorFlow runs eagerly: operations execute immediately and return values, just like NumPy. When you want speed, you wrap a function in @tf.function and TF traces it into a static graph it can optimize and run without Python overhead. You get interactivity and compiled performance.

slides.title: What TensorFlow is NOT
slides.body: It is not only Keras, not a magic AutoML button, and not a single executable. It is a stack: low-level ops, autodiff, the Keras model API, and a deployment family. Knowing the boundary keeps you from hunting for features in the wrong layer or confusing TF 1.x sessions with today's eager API.

slides.title: The 30-second mental model
bullets: Tensor = accelerator-ready NumPy array
GradientTape = free derivatives
Keras = the API you actually write
@tf.function = compile a graph for speed
Deployment family = run it anywhere

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 55.
slides.body: Next: why TensorFlow still matters when PyTorch owns research.

54-2.caption:
PyTorch won the research papers. So why does TensorFlow still run inside your phone, your browser, and half the production ML at large companies? 🏭

Because winning research and winning deployment are different games. TensorFlow's real moat was never the training API — it was the path from a trained model to a running product: serving at scale, on-device inference, and the browser. That ecosystem was years ahead and is still deeply entrenched.

This post is about the WHY: the specific problems TF solved first, where it remains the pragmatic choice, and where it now costs you.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• why deployment, not research, is TF's moat
• TFLite, TF.js, and TF Serving in one breath
• the TPU advantage at Google scale
• the production stability story
• where TensorFlow now bites you

Understand the why and you'll know when to reach for it. Save it. 👇

slides.title: The moat is deployment, not training
slides.body: Researchers prototype; companies ship. TensorFlow's lasting advantage was a mature path from a trained model to a running product — on servers, phones, and browsers — years before the alternatives. Code already in production rarely gets rewritten, so that early lead still shapes the landscape today.

slides.title: Run anywhere: the deployment family
slides.body: TF Serving hosts models behind a high-throughput API. TFLite shrinks and quantizes models to run on phones and microcontrollers. TensorFlow.js runs models directly in the browser with no server. One trained model, three very different targets, all first-class — that breadth is hard to match.

slides.title: TPUs are first-class
slides.body: TensorFlow was co-designed with Google's TPUs, custom chips built for tensor math at massive scale. If you train on Google Cloud TPUs, TF gives you the smoothest path to that hardware. For very large training jobs at Google-scale economics, that integration is a real edge.

slides.title: Save once, serve anywhere
slides.code:
model.save('my_model.keras')

# Convert to a phone-ready TFLite model
converter = tf.lite.TFLiteConverter.from_keras_model(model)
converter.optimizations = [tf.lite.Optimize.DEFAULT]
tflite_model = converter.convert()
open('model.tflite', 'wb').write(tflite_model)

slides.title: Stability and tooling
slides.body: Production teams value predictability. TF offers SavedModel as a stable, language-agnostic format, TensorBoard for visualization, and TFX for end-to-end ML pipelines. For an organization standardizing infrastructure, this mature, batteries-included tooling often outweighs raw research ergonomics.

slides.title: Where it now bites
bullets: Research mindshare has shifted to PyTorch
Two eras (1.x graphs vs 2.x eager) confuse docs
Heavier API surface to learn
Fewer bleeding-edge model implementations first

slides.title: When to reach for TensorFlow
bullets: On-device or in-browser inference
You train on Google Cloud TPUs
You need a mature serving + pipeline stack
An existing TF codebase to extend

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 55.
slides.body: Next: the mechanics — how GradientTape and graphs actually work.

54-3.caption:
When TensorFlow computes a gradient, there's no magic. It records your forward computation onto a tape, then replays it backward applying the chain rule, one operation at a time. 🎞️

The trick is tf.GradientTape. Inside its context, every operation on a watched tensor is logged. When you ask for a gradient, TF walks that recording in reverse — reverse-mode autodiff — to get the derivative of your loss with respect to every parameter. @tf.function then compiles the hot path into a fast graph.

This post opens the hood: how the tape records, how reverse-mode works, what @tf.function actually does, and how a training step fits together.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• how GradientTape records the forward pass
• reverse-mode autodiff and the chain rule
• what watched tensors are
• what @tf.function traces into a graph
• how one training step ties it together

See the tape and backprop stops being a black box. Save it. 👇

slides.title: The tape records the forward pass
slides.body: Open a tf.GradientTape context and TF starts logging operations onto a tape. Variables are watched automatically; other tensors you watch explicitly. Each recorded op remembers how to compute its own local derivative. By the end of the forward pass you have a full recipe for the backward pass.

slides.title: Watch the tape record
slides.code:
import tensorflow as tf
x = tf.Variable(3.0)
with tf.GradientTape() as tape:
    y = x ** 2 + 2 * x      # recorded
dy_dx = tape.gradient(y, x)  # replays backward
print(dy_dx.numpy())         # 2*3 + 2 = 8.0

slides.title: Reverse-mode autodiff
slides.body: TF walks the tape from the loss back to the inputs, multiplying local derivatives via the chain rule. Reverse mode computes the gradient of one scalar output with respect to many inputs in a single backward pass — exactly the shape of deep learning, where one loss depends on millions of parameters.

slides.title: Watched vs unwatched
slides.body: tf.Variable objects are watched by default, so model weights get gradients automatically. A plain tf.constant is not watched — call tape.watch(t) to track it. If you read a value outside the tape's context, it won't be recorded and its gradient comes back as None.

slides.title: @tf.function compiles a graph
slides.body: Decorate a function with @tf.function and TF traces it once into a static graph, then reuses that graph on later calls. The graph fuses operations and drops Python overhead, often a big speedup for training steps. Tracing keys on input shape and dtype, so changing shapes can trigger costly re-tracing.

slides.title: Compile the hot path
slides.code:
@tf.function
def train_step(x, y):
    with tf.GradientTape() as tape:
        preds = model(x, training=True)
        loss = loss_fn(y, preds)
    grads = tape.gradient(loss, model.trainable_variables)
    optimizer.apply_gradients(zip(grads, model.trainable_variables))
    return loss

slides.title: One training step, in order
bullets: Forward pass inside the tape
Compute the scalar loss
tape.gradient(loss, weights)
optimizer.apply_gradients(...)
Repeat over batches

slides.title: Tape gotchas
bullets: Default tape is used once; pass persistent=True to reuse
Ops outside the context aren't recorded
tf.constant needs explicit tape.watch
Re-tracing on every new shape kills speed

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 55.
slides.body: Next: a full runnable example — build, train, and evaluate a model.

54-4.caption:
Enough theory. Here is a complete TensorFlow program: load data, build a model, train it, evaluate it — the whole loop, end to end. 💻

We'll classify handwritten digits with Keras, the high-level API. You'll see the four moves that repeat in almost every TF project: prepare the data, define the model, compile with a loss and optimizer, then fit and evaluate. The same skeleton scales from this toy to real systems.

This post is code-heavy on purpose. Read it once, then keep it as a template you can adapt.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• load and normalize the MNIST dataset
• build a Sequential model in a few lines
• compile with loss, optimizer, and metrics
• fit, evaluate, and predict
• save the trained model for deployment

Copy it, run it, change one thing, run again. Save it. 👇

slides.title: The four moves of every TF project
bullets: Prepare the data
Define the model
Compile: loss + optimizer + metrics
Fit, then evaluate

slides.title: 1. Load + normalize data
slides.code:
import tensorflow as tf

(x_train, y_train), (x_test, y_test) = tf.keras.datasets.mnist.load_data()
# pixels 0-255 -> 0-1 for stable training
x_train, x_test = x_train / 255.0, x_test / 255.0
print(x_train.shape)  # (60000, 28, 28)

slides.title: 2. Build the model
slides.code:
model = tf.keras.Sequential([
    tf.keras.layers.Flatten(input_shape=(28, 28)),
    tf.keras.layers.Dense(128, activation='relu'),
    tf.keras.layers.Dropout(0.2),
    tf.keras.layers.Dense(10, activation='softmax'),
])
model.summary()

slides.title: 3. Compile the model
slides.code:
model.compile(
    optimizer='adam',
    loss='sparse_categorical_crossentropy',
    metrics=['accuracy'],
)

slides.title: 4. Fit, evaluate, predict
slides.code:
model.fit(x_train, y_train, epochs=5, validation_split=0.1)

test_loss, test_acc = model.evaluate(x_test, y_test)
print(f'test accuracy: {test_acc:.3f}')  # ~0.978

probs = model.predict(x_test[:1])
print(probs.argmax())  # predicted digit

slides.title: What compile actually wires up
slides.body: compile() attaches three things to the model: the optimizer (how weights update, e.g. Adam), the loss (the number to minimize — here cross-entropy over 10 classes), and metrics (what to report, like accuracy). Nothing trains until you call fit; compile just records the recipe.

slides.title: Save it for deployment
slides.code:
model.save('mnist_model.keras')

# Later, in any process:
restored = tf.keras.models.load_model('mnist_model.keras')
restored.evaluate(x_test, y_test)

slides.title: Adapt this template
bullets: Swap the dataset loader for your data
Change layer sizes / add Conv2D for images
Try a different optimizer or learning rate
Raise epochs and watch validation loss
Save the model when accuracy is good

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 55.
slides.body: Next: the mistakes that quietly wreck your TensorFlow models.

54-5.caption:
Your TensorFlow model runs without errors and still learns nothing useful. Most of the time it's not a bug in TF — it's one of a handful of classic mistakes. ⚠️

These traps rarely throw exceptions. They show up as a loss that won't drop, training that's mysteriously slow, or great training accuracy that collapses in production. Each one is easy to fix once you know to look for it.

This post is the checklist I run when a model misbehaves. Learn the failure modes before you trust the output.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• forgetting to normalize your inputs
• wrong loss for your label format
• re-tracing @tf.function on every shape
• data leakage between train and test
• judging a model by training accuracy alone

Know the traps and debugging gets fast. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 1. Not normalizing inputs
slides.body: Feeding raw 0-255 pixels or wildly different feature scales makes gradients explode or crawl. The model may never converge. Scale inputs to roughly 0-1 or zero-mean unit-variance before training. This single line fixes more 'my loss won't drop' problems than anything else.

slides.title: Fix: scale your inputs
slides.code:
# wrong: raw pixels 0-255 straight in
# right: bring them into a small range
x_train = x_train / 255.0
x_test = x_test / 255.0
# tabular: use a Normalization layer
norm = tf.keras.layers.Normalization()
norm.adapt(x_train)

slides.title: 2. Wrong loss for your labels
slides.body: If labels are integers (0,1,2,...) use sparse_categorical_crossentropy. If they're one-hot vectors use categorical_crossentropy. Mismatch it and you get a shape error or, worse, silently bad training. Same care: use a softmax + crossentropy pair, not a sigmoid for a multiclass head.

slides.title: 3. Re-tracing @tf.function
slides.body: tf.function traces a new graph for every distinct input shape or dtype, and Python ints/floats passed as arguments trigger even more traces. Retracing on every step erases the speedup and floods you with warnings. Pass tensors, fix shapes, and watch for the retrace warning.

slides.title: Fix: stop the retrace
4. Data leakage
slides.body: If you fit a scaler, impute, or augment using the full dataset before splitting, test information leaks into training. Your reported accuracy is inflated and won't hold in production. Split first, then fit any preprocessing on the training set only and apply it to test.

slides.title: 5. Trusting training accuracy
slides.body: A model can memorize the training set and look perfect while failing on new data. Always watch a validation/test metric, not just training loss. A growing gap between train and validation is overfitting — add dropout, regularization, or more data.

slides.title: The pre-flight checklist
bullets: Inputs scaled to a small range
Loss matches label format
@tf.function not retracing each step
Preprocessing fit on train only
Judge by validation, not training

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 55.
slides.body: Next day: a new topic in the 100 Days of AI series.

55-1.caption:
Computers are built on numbers. Human language is built on ambiguity, context, and 5,000 years of mess. NLP is the bridge between the two. 🗣️

Natural Language Processing is the field that gets machines to read, understand, and generate human language — text or speech. It's not one algorithm; it's a stack of tasks: splitting text into pieces, mapping words to numbers, modeling meaning, and producing language back. Every chatbot, search bar, translator, and autocomplete you touch sits on top of it.

This post is the map. Before we argue why NLP matters or open up how a pipeline actually runs, get clear on what NLP IS — and the core problem it's fighting.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• what NLP actually means
• why language is hard for machines
• the spectrum of NLP tasks
• understanding vs generation (NLU vs NLG)
• what NLP is NOT

Get the mental model first and the jargon stops feeling random. Save it. 👇

slides.title: The one-line definition
slides.body: Natural Language Processing is the branch of AI that lets computers work with human language — reading, interpreting, and producing text or speech. The goal is to turn unstructured words into something a machine can act on, and to turn machine output back into fluent language.

slides.title: Why language breaks computers
slides.body: Code is exact; language is not. 'I saw her duck' has two meanings. 'It' can point anywhere. Sarcasm flips a sentence's intent. Spelling, slang, and context all shift meaning. NLP exists because none of this maps cleanly onto if-statements — it has to be learned from data.

slides.title: NLP is a stack of tasks
slides.body: There is no single 'NLP'. It's a family: tokenization, part-of-speech tagging, named-entity recognition, sentiment analysis, translation, summarization, question answering, and text generation. Real applications chain several of these together into a pipeline.

slides.title: NLP in three lines
slides.code:
from transformers import pipeline
clf = pipeline("sentiment-analysis")
print(clf("I love this, but shipping was slow"))
# [{'label': 'POSITIVE', 'score': 0.97}]

slides.title: What NLP is NOT
slides.body: NLP is not a single model, and modern NLP is not hand-written grammar rules. It's also not true 'understanding' in the human sense — a model maps statistical patterns, it doesn't know meaning the way you do. Keeping that boundary clear stops you from over-trusting the output.

slides.title: The 30-second model
bullets: NLP = machines + human language
Language is hard because it's ambiguous
It's a stack of tasks, not one thing
NLU reads, NLG writes
Patterns, not real understanding

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 56.
slides.body: Next: why NLP quietly runs half the software you use every day.

55-2.caption:
You used NLP a dozen times today without noticing — the spam filter, the search suggestion, the translated review, the reply your inbox drafted for you. 📈

NLP matters because language is the interface for almost everything humans do with computers. Unlock it and you can search documents by meaning, route support tickets automatically, read a million reviews in seconds, and let people talk to software instead of clicking through it. The arrival of large language models turned a niche research field into infrastructure.

This post is about the WHY: the concrete value NLP creates, where it already lives, and where it still fails.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• why language is the ultimate interface
• NLP you already use daily
• the business value: scale + speed
• the LLM inflection point
• where NLP still falls down

Understand the why and you'll know where to deploy it. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Language is the interface
slides.body: Most human knowledge is stored as text and most human intent is expressed as language. A machine that can process language can therefore touch almost any domain — law, medicine, support, search, code. That generality is why NLP is the highest-leverage area of AI right now.

slides.title: NLP you already use
bullets: Spam filters sorting your inbox
Search autocomplete + 'did you mean'
Google Translate
Siri / Alexa voice commands
Autocorrect and smart reply

slides.title: The business case: scale
slides.body: A human reads maybe a few hundred reviews a day. An NLP model reads a million in minutes and tags each by sentiment, topic, and urgency. The value isn't replacing one analyst — it's doing work at a volume no team of humans could ever reach, consistently and instantly.

slides.title: The LLM inflection point
slides.body: Before 2018, each NLP task needed its own model and labeled dataset. Large language models changed that: one pretrained model now handles translation, summarization, Q&A, and classification with a prompt. The barrier to building NLP features collapsed from months to an afternoon.

slides.title: Months of work, now a prompt
slides.code:
from transformers import pipeline
summarizer = pipeline("summarization")
text = open("long_report.txt").read()
print(summarizer(text, max_length=60)[0]["summary_text"])

slides.title: Where NLP still falls down
bullets: Hallucinated, confident-but-wrong answers
Bias inherited from training text
Struggles with low-resource languages
Sarcasm, irony, deep context
No real grounding in facts

slides.title: When NLP earns its keep
bullets: You have lots of unstructured text
The task is repetitive at scale
Approximate answers are acceptable
A human reviews high-stakes output

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 56.
slides.body: Next: open the hood — how a sentence travels through an NLP pipeline.

55-3.caption:
A model can't read the word 'cat'. It can only do math on numbers. So every NLP system runs the same hidden assembly line: turn text into pieces, pieces into numbers, numbers into meaning. ⚙️

The classic pipeline is text in, prediction out, with four moves in between: clean and tokenize the raw string, map tokens to vectors (embeddings), feed those vectors through a model that captures context, then decode the result into a label or new text. Modern Transformers fuse some steps, but the shape is the same.

This post opens the hood: tokenization, embeddings, the role of attention, and how output gets decoded.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• the four stages of an NLP pipeline
• tokenization: splitting text into units
• embeddings: words as vectors
• attention and context
• decoding back into language

See the pipeline and the black box turns into plumbing. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Stage 1: tokenization
slides.body: Raw text is split into tokens — words, sub-words, or characters. Modern models use sub-word tokenization, so 'unhappiness' becomes 'un', 'happi', 'ness'. This keeps the vocabulary small while still handling rare and made-up words. Each token then maps to an integer ID.

slides.title: Tokenize a sentence
slides.code:
from transformers import AutoTokenizer
tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("bert-base-uncased")
print(tok.tokenize("NLP is unbelievable"))
# ['nlp', 'is', 'un', '##bel', '##iev', '##able']

slides.title: Stage 2: embeddings
slides.body: Each token ID is mapped to a dense vector — a list of numbers that places the word in a 'meaning space'. Words used in similar contexts land near each other, so 'king' and 'queen' sit close. This is how raw symbols get turned into something math can measure.

slides.title: Stage 3: context with attention
slides.body: A word's meaning depends on its neighbors — 'bank' near 'river' vs near 'money'. The Transformer's attention mechanism lets each token look at every other token and reweight its representation accordingly. This is what gives modern NLP its sense of context.

slides.title: Stage 4: decoding
slides.body: The model's final vectors are turned into something useful: a softmax over labels for classification, or a token-by-token sampling loop for generation. In generation, the model predicts the next token, appends it, and repeats — which is exactly how ChatGPT writes a sentence.

slides.title: The full pipeline in code
slides.code:
from transformers import pipeline
ner = pipeline("ner", grouped_entities=True)
out = ner("Saurav works at CoreITX in Texas")
for e in out:
    print(e["word"], e["entity_group"])
# Saurav PER | CoreITX ORG | Texas LOC

slides.title: The pipeline in one breath
bullets: Text → tokens (split)
Tokens → vectors (embed)
Vectors → context (attention)
Context → answer (decode)
Transformers fuse the middle

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 56.
slides.body: Next: a full runnable NLP example you can paste and execute.

55-4.caption:
Enough theory. Here's NLP you can run right now — load a model, classify text, extract entities, and build a tiny meaning-search, all in a few lines of Python. 💻

Modern NLP is shockingly accessible. The Hugging Face transformers library wraps state-of-the-art models behind a one-line pipeline() call, and sentence-transformers turns any sentence into a vector you can compare. You don't need a GPU or a PhD to get useful results — you need about 20 lines.

This is the code-heavy post. We'll do sentiment, NER, zero-shot classification, and semantic similarity, then call out the line beginners always botch.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• one-line pipelines for common tasks
• zero-shot classification with no training
• turning sentences into vectors
• semantic similarity / search
• the tokenization gotcha

Copy it, run it, then swap in your own text. Save it. 👇

slides.title: NLP You Can Run in 20 Lines
0. Install + import
slides.code:
# pip install transformers sentence-transformers torch
from transformers import pipeline
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer, util

slides.title: 1. Sentiment + NER
slides.code:
sentiment = pipeline("sentiment-analysis")
print(sentiment("The setup was painful but worth it"))
# [{'label': 'POSITIVE', 'score': 0.92}]

ner = pipeline("ner", grouped_entities=True)
print(ner("Apple released the iPhone in 2007"))

slides.title: 2. Zero-shot classification
slides.code:
clf = pipeline("zero-shot-classification")
result = clf(
    "My internet has been down for two days",
    candidate_labels=["billing", "technical", "sales"])
print(result["labels"][0])   # 'technical' — no training!

slides.title: 3. Sentences → vectors
slides.code:
model = SentenceTransformer("all-MiniLM-L6-v2")
sentences = ["How do I reset my password?",
             "I forgot my login",
             "What are your store hours?"]
emb = model.encode(sentences)
print(emb.shape)   # (3, 384)

slides.title: 4. Semantic search
slides.code:
query = model.encode("can't sign in")
scores = util.cos_sim(query, emb)[0]
best = scores.argmax().item()
print(sentences[best])
# 'I forgot my login'  — meaning, not keywords

slides.title: Why this works with no training
slides.body: Each pipeline downloads a model already pretrained on huge text corpora. Sentiment and NER fine-tuned versions ship ready to use; zero-shot uses a model trained on entailment so it can score arbitrary labels. You inherit millions of dollars of training for the cost of a download.

slides.title: The gotcha: input length
slides.body: Models have a max token limit (often 512). Paste a long document into one pipeline call and it silently truncates — you analyze only the first chunk and never see a warning. Always split long text into windows, or check tokenizer length before sending it in.

slides.title: Check length before you send
slides.code:
tok = sentiment.tokenizer
n = len(tok(text)["input_ids"])
if n > 512:
    print(f"Too long: {n} tokens — chunk it first")

slides.title: Ship-it checklist
bullets: Use pipeline() to prototype fast
Pin model names for reproducibility
Batch inputs for speed
Watch the token limit
Cache models, don't redownload

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 56.
slides.body: Next: the mistakes that quietly wreck NLP projects.

55-5.caption:
Most NLP projects don't fail on the model. They fail on the data, the evaluation, and the quiet assumptions nobody checked. 🧹

The library makes the easy 80% trivial — but the failures cluster in the other 20%: skipping text cleaning, judging a model on accuracy when classes are imbalanced, leaking test data into training, and trusting a fluent LLM answer that's confidently wrong. None of these throw an error. They just give you a system that looks fine in the demo and breaks in production.

This post is the pre-mortem: the five mistakes that bite almost everyone, and the fix for each.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• why 'just throw text at the model' fails
• accuracy as a misleading metric
• data leakage in text splits
• trusting fluent-but-wrong output
• ignoring language + domain mismatch

Learn the failure modes before they cost you. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 1. Skipping text preprocessing
slides.body: Raw text is filthy: HTML tags, emoji, inconsistent casing, duplicate whitespace, and encoding junk. Feed it in unfiltered and your model learns noise. For classic ML you must clean; even for LLMs, garbage context produces garbage answers. Cleaning is not optional.

slides.title: Minimal cleaning
2. Trusting accuracy alone
slides.body: If 95% of your emails are not-spam, a model that says 'not spam' every time scores 95% accuracy and catches zero spam. On imbalanced text data — which is most of it — accuracy lies. Look at precision, recall, and F1 per class instead.

slides.title: 3. Data leakage
slides.body: Split your data AFTER deduplicating and grouping. If the same review (or near-duplicate) lands in both train and test, your model memorizes it and reports inflated scores. With text, leakage hides in duplicates, overlapping time windows, and shared users across the split.

slides.title: Dedup before you split
slides.code:
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
df = df.drop_duplicates(subset="text")   # kill leaks
train, test = train_test_split(
    df, test_size=0.2, stratify=df["label"])

slides.title: 4. Trusting fluent output
slides.body: An LLM's confidence has nothing to do with its correctness. It will invent citations, APIs, and facts in flawless prose. Fluency is not truth. For anything that matters, ground answers in real sources and verify — never ship raw generation into a high-stakes decision.

slides.title: 5. Language + domain mismatch
slides.body: A model trained on English news will stumble on Spanish tweets, legal contracts, or medical notes. Vocabulary, tone, and structure all shift. Always check that your model's training domain matches your data — or fine-tune on in-domain text before you rely on it.

slides.title: The pre-flight checklist
bullets: Clean text before modeling
Use F1, not just accuracy
Dedup, then split
Ground + verify LLM output
Match model domain to your data

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 56.
slides.body: Next day, next topic — we go deeper into the NLP and LLM stack.

56-1.caption:
Before a model reads a single word, it cuts your text into pieces — and those pieces aren't words. They're tokens. 🔪

Tokenization is the very first thing that happens to any text you send a language model: the raw string gets sliced into discrete units, each mapped to an integer ID the model can look up. Get this wrong and everything downstream — embeddings, attention, generation — operates on garbage. Get it right and the model handles typos, emoji, code, and made-up words gracefully.

This post is the concept. Before we argue why tokenization matters or open up how BPE actually works, get crystal clear on what a token IS.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• what a token actually is
• word vs sub-word vs character tokens
• why models don't use whole words
• how text becomes integer IDs
• vocabulary, the model's fixed dictionary

Nail the mental model and the rest of the series clicks. Save it. 👇

slides.title: The one-line definition
slides.body: A token is the smallest unit of text a model processes — often a sub-word fragment, not a whole word. Tokenization is the step that splits a raw string into these units and maps each to an integer ID. The model never sees letters; it sees a list of numbers.

slides.title: Why not just use words?
slides.body: A whole-word vocabulary would need millions of entries and still choke on typos, names, and new slang. Characters are too fine — the model loses meaning and sequences get huge. Sub-words are the sweet spot: a fixed vocabulary that can still spell out anything it has never seen.

slides.title: Text → IDs in three moves
slides.body: First the text is normalized (lowercasing, Unicode cleanup). Then it's split into tokens by the tokenizer's learned rules. Finally each token is looked up in the vocabulary and replaced by its integer ID. That list of IDs is the actual input to the neural network.

slides.title: See it happen
slides.code:
from transformers import AutoTokenizer
tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("gpt2")
print(tok.tokenize("tokenizing is fun"))
# ['token', 'izing', 'Ġis', 'Ġfun']
print(tok.encode("tokenizing is fun"))
# [30001, 2890, 318, 1257]

slides.title: What a vocabulary is
slides.body: Every model ships with a fixed vocabulary — a frozen dictionary mapping each known token to an ID. GPT-2 has ~50k entries; modern models have 100k+. Anything you type must be expressed using only those pieces. The vocabulary is learned once, during tokenizer training, and never changes after.

slides.title: What tokenization is NOT
slides.body: It's not splitting on spaces — punctuation, emoji, and word-pieces all matter. It's not language-aware grammar; the tokenizer has no idea what a noun is. And one token is not one word: a single emoji can be several tokens, while a common word is exactly one. Token count ≠ word count.

slides.title: The 30-second model
bullets: A token = the model's smallest text unit
Modern models use sub-words
Every token maps to an integer ID
The vocabulary is fixed and finite
Token count is not word count

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 57.
slides.body: Next: why this invisible step decides your cost, context limit, and quality.

56-2.caption:
You're billed by the token. Your context window is measured in tokens. Your model's blind spots are token artifacts. Tokenization isn't a detail — it's the unit your whole LLM economy runs on. 💸

Most people treat tokenization as plumbing they'll never touch. But it silently sets your API bill, decides how much text fits in a prompt, explains why models miscount letters, and even changes how well other languages work. Understanding it turns a string of mysteries into predictable engineering.

This post is the WHY: the concrete, money-and-quality reasons tokenization matters.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• why you pay per token, not per word
• context windows are token budgets
• why GPT can't count the r's in strawberry
• the language tax on non-English text
• tokens as the real cost of RAG

Understand this and your prompts get cheaper and smarter. Save it. 👇

slides.title: You pay per token
slides.body: Every major LLM API prices input and output in tokens, not words or characters. A rough rule for English: 1 token ≈ 0.75 words, or ~4 characters. A 1,000-word prompt costs you ~1,300 tokens before the model writes a single reply — and the reply costs tokens too.

slides.title: Context windows are token budgets
slides.body: A model's 'context window' — 8k, 128k, 1M — is counted in tokens, not words. Your system prompt, chat history, retrieved documents, and the model's answer all draw from the same pool. Run out and the oldest content gets dropped. Knowing token math is knowing how much actually fits.

slides.title: The strawberry problem
slides.body: Ask a model how many r's are in 'strawberry' and it often gets it wrong. Why? It never sees letters — 'strawberry' might be one or two tokens, so counting characters means reasoning about something it can't directly observe. Many 'dumb' model errors are really tokenization artifacts.

slides.title: The language tax
