slides.body: Tokenizers are trained mostly on English text, so English packs efficiently. The same sentence in Hindi, Thai, or Burmese can take 2-3x more tokens — meaning slower responses, higher cost, and less room in the context window for speakers of those languages. Tokenization encodes a real inequity.

slides.title: Measure the language tax
slides.code:
import tiktoken
enc = tiktoken.get_encoding("cl100k_base")
en = "Artificial intelligence is powerful"
hi = "\u0915\u0943\u0924\u094d\u0930\u093f\u092e \u092c\u0941\u0926\u094d\u0927\u093f\u092e\u0924\u094d\u0924\u093e \u0936\u0915\u094d\u0924\u093f\u0936\u093e\u0932\u0940 \u0939\u0948"
print(len(enc.encode(en)))   # 5
print(len(enc.encode(hi)))   # 20+

slides.title: Tokens are the cost of RAG
slides.body: Retrieval-augmented generation stuffs documents into the prompt — and each chunk costs tokens on every call. Chunk size, overlap, and how many results you retrieve are all token-budget decisions. Tokenization is the hidden accountant behind every RAG system's latency and bill.

slides.title: Why it matters, in one breath
bullets: You're billed per token, not per word
Context windows are token budgets
Letter tasks fail — models see tokens
Non-English text costs 2-3x more
RAG cost is a token-budget problem

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 57.
slides.body: Next: open the hood — exactly how BPE builds a vocabulary from raw text.

56-3.caption:
How does a tokenizer decide that 'tokenizing' splits into 'token' + 'izing' but 'the' stays whole? It's not magic and it's not grammar — it's a greedy merge algorithm called BPE, learned from data. ⚙️

Byte-Pair Encoding starts from raw bytes and repeatedly merges the most frequent adjacent pair into a new token, building a vocabulary bottom-up. Common words become single tokens; rare words get spelled out from fragments. WordPiece and Unigram are variations on the same idea. Once you see the algorithm, every weird split makes sense.

This post opens the hood: how the vocabulary is trained and how text gets encoded against it.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• the byte-level starting point
• how BPE learns merges from frequency
• encoding text with the learned merges
• BPE vs WordPiece vs Unigram
• special tokens and why they exist

See the algorithm and the black box becomes a loop. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Start from bytes
slides.body: Modern tokenizers start at the byte level: every possible character is representable as 1-4 bytes, so there are no truly 'unknown' inputs — emoji, foreign scripts, and weird symbols all decompose into bytes. This is why byte-level BPE never emits an <UNK> token; worst case, it falls back to raw bytes.

slides.title: Learn merges by frequency
slides.body: BPE training scans a huge corpus and counts adjacent symbol pairs. It merges the most frequent pair into a new token, then recounts and repeats — thousands of times. Frequent sequences like 'th', 'ing', or 'the' get merged early and become single tokens; rare ones never merge.

slides.title: BPE training, by hand
slides.code:
from collections import Counter
vocab = list("l o w </w> l o w e r </w>".split())
pairs = Counter(zip(vocab, vocab[1:]))
top = pairs.most_common(1)[0][0]   # ('l','o')
print("merge:", top)
# merge 'l'+'o' -> 'lo', then rescan and repeat

slides.title: Encoding uses the merge rules
slides.body: At inference, the tokenizer splits your text into base characters, then applies the learned merges in the order they were created, greedily combining pairs. The result is the same tokenization every time — deterministic, fast, and reproducible. Training learns the rules; encoding just replays them.

slides.title: Encode against a real vocab
slides.code:
import tiktoken
enc = tiktoken.get_encoding("cl100k_base")
ids = enc.encode("lower lowest")
print(ids)                      # [11873, 28747]
for i in ids:
    print(i, repr(enc.decode([i])))
# 'lower' is one token; 'lowest' splits

slides.title: Special tokens
slides.body: Tokenizers reserve slots for control tokens the model relies on: [CLS]/[SEP] for BERT, <|endoftext|> for GPT, plus padding and mask tokens. These aren't in your text — the tokenizer injects them to mark boundaries, separate turns, or signal the end of generation. They occupy real IDs in the vocab.

slides.title: The algorithm in one breath
bullets: Start from bytes — no UNK
Merge the most frequent pair, repeat
Vocab is built bottom-up
Encoding replays merges greedily
Special tokens mark structure

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 57.
slides.body: Next: a runnable, paste-and-go tour of tokenizers in Python.

56-4.caption:
Enough theory — let's tokenize. In a few lines of Python you can count tokens, inspect splits, decode IDs back to text, train your own tokenizer, and price an API call before you make it. 💻

Tokenizers are some of the most accessible tools in NLP. tiktoken gives you exact OpenAI token counts; Hugging Face tokenizers let you inspect any model's splits and even train a fresh one. Once you can see the tokens, the model stops feeling like a black box and starts feeling like an API with a meter.

This is the code-heavy post: count, inspect, decode, train, and estimate cost.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• exact token counts with tiktoken
• inspecting how any model splits text
• decoding IDs back into strings
• training your own BPE tokenizer
• turning token counts into a dollar estimate

Copy it, run it, then meter your own prompts. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 0. Install + import
slides.code:
# pip install tiktoken transformers tokenizers
import tiktoken
from transformers import AutoTokenizer
from tokenizers import Tokenizer, models, trainers, pre_tokenizers

slides.title: 1. Exact token counts
slides.code:
enc = tiktoken.get_encoding("cl100k_base")  # GPT-4 family
text = "Tokenization decides your bill."
ids = enc.encode(text)
print(len(ids), "tokens")        # 6 tokens
print(ids)                        # [3923, 2065, ...]
print(enc.decode(ids) == text)    # True — round-trips

slides.title: 2. Inspect how a model splits
slides.code:
tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("bert-base-uncased")
print(tok.tokenize("tokenization isn't magic"))
# ['token', '##ization', 'isn', "'", 't', 'magic']
# '##' marks a continuation of the previous word

slides.title: 3. Decode IDs back to text
slides.code:
ids = tok.encode("hello world")
print(ids)                         # [101, 7592, 2088, 102]
print(tok.convert_ids_to_tokens(ids))
# ['[CLS]', 'hello', 'world', '[SEP]']
print(tok.decode(ids, skip_special_tokens=True))
# 'hello world'

slides.title: 4. Train your own BPE tokenizer
slides.code:
t = Tokenizer(models.BPE())
t.pre_tokenizer = pre_tokenizers.Whitespace()
trainer = trainers.BpeTrainer(vocab_size=500)
t.train(["my_corpus.txt"], trainer)
print(t.encode("hello there").tokens)
t.save("my-tokenizer.json")

slides.title: 5. Price a call before you send it
slides.code:
enc = tiktoken.get_encoding("cl100k_base")
prompt = open("prompt.txt").read()
n_in = len(enc.encode(prompt))
IN, OUT = 5/1e6, 15/1e6          # $ per token
cost = n_in*IN + 800*OUT          # assume ~800 out
print(f"{n_in} in tokens, est ${cost:.4f}")

slides.title: Why match the right encoding
slides.body: Each model family has its own tokenizer. cl100k_base counts for GPT-4 and GPT-3.5; o200k_base for newer models; BERT and Llama use entirely different vocabularies. Counting with the wrong tokenizer gives wrong numbers — always load the encoding that matches the model you'll actually call.

slides.title: Ship-it checklist
bullets: Use tiktoken for exact OpenAI counts
Match the encoding to your model
Count tokens before sending — not after
Decode to debug weird outputs
Cache tokenizers, don't reload each call

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 57.
slides.body: Next: the tokenization mistakes that quietly break prompts and bills.

56-5.caption:
Tokenization bugs don't throw errors. They just make your prompt cost double, your context silently truncate, or your model fail a task it should ace — and you never see why. 🧹

The failures cluster in the same few places: assuming a word is a token, forgetting special tokens in your count, splitting text in the middle of a token, trusting char limits as token limits, and ignoring the language tax. None of these crash. They quietly inflate bills and degrade quality until someone profiles the system.

This post is the pre-mortem: five tokenization mistakes and the fix for each.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• why 'words ≈ tokens' will burn you
• forgetting special tokens in the count
• truncating mid-token and corrupting text
• char limits are not token limits
• ignoring the non-English token tax

Learn the failure modes before they cost you money. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 1. Assuming words ≈ tokens
slides.body: Estimating cost or context by counting words is the most common error. Code, URLs, JSON, emoji, and non-English text all blow past the 0.75-words-per-token rule. A 1,000-word document of clean prose is ~1,300 tokens; the same word count of dense JSON can be double. Always count, never guess.

slides.title: Count, don't estimate
slides.code:
import tiktoken
enc = tiktoken.get_encoding("cl100k_base")
prose = "the quick brown fox jumps"      # 5 words
code = '{"id":42,"ok":true,"v":[1,2,3]}'  # 5 'words'?
print(len(enc.encode(prose)))  # 5
print(len(enc.encode(code)))   # 17 — same look, 3x cost

slides.title: 2. Forgetting special tokens
slides.body: When you count tokens to fit a context window, the model also adds [CLS], [SEP], chat-role markers, and separators you didn't write. Forget them and your 'exactly 512 tokens' input gets rejected or truncated. Always count with add_special_tokens=True, the way the model will actually see it.

slides.title: Include the hidden tokens
slides.code:
from transformers import AutoTokenizer
tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("bert-base-uncased")
bare = tok.encode("hi", add_special_tokens=False)
full = tok.encode("hi", add_special_tokens=True)
print(len(bare), len(full))   # 1  3  (+[CLS] +[SEP])

slides.title: 3. Truncating mid-token
slides.body: Cutting text by character count to 'fit' can slice a multi-byte character or word-piece in half, producing mojibake or a meaningless fragment. Worse, naive chunking can split a sentence so each chunk loses context. Truncate and chunk on token boundaries, not character offsets.

slides.title: 4. Char limits ≠ token limits
slides.body: A model's limit is in tokens, but your validation often checks characters. They don't track: one emoji can be 2-4 tokens, a Chinese character is often 1-2, and a long English word can be several. Validate length with the actual tokenizer, or you'll reject valid input and accept oversized input.

slides.title: Validate on tokens, not chars
slides.code:
MAX = 512
def fits(text, tok):
    n = len(tok.encode(text))      # real token count
    return n <= MAX, n
ok, n = fits(user_input, tok)
if not ok:
    raise ValueError(f"{n} tokens > {MAX}; chunk it")

slides.title: 5. Ignoring the language tax
slides.body: If your product serves non-English users, English-trained tokenizers quietly charge them more: 2-3x the tokens for the same meaning, eating context and budget. Budget per-language, test with real multilingual samples, and consider models with multilingual-trained tokenizers for global apps.

slides.title: The pre-flight checklist
bullets: Count tokens, never estimate from words
Include special tokens in the count
Truncate on token boundaries
Validate length in tokens, not chars
Budget extra for non-English text

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 57.
slides.body: Next day, next topic — deeper into the NLP and LLM stack.

57-1.caption:
How does a model with a 50,000-word vocabulary handle 'antidisestablishmentarianism', emojis, and a typo it has never seen? It doesn't store whole words. It stores fragments. 🧩

Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE) and WordPiece are subword tokenization algorithms. Instead of one ID per word (too many) or one ID per character (too slow), they learn a vocabulary of common chunks — 'token', 'ization', '##able' — and rebuild any word by stitching those chunks together. This is the layer underneath GPT, BERT, and Llama.

This post is the concept: what subword tokenization is, why it sits between words and characters, and how BPE and WordPiece relate.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• what BPE and WordPiece actually are
• the word-vs-character tradeoff they solve
• how a vocabulary of fragments works
• BPE vs WordPiece at a glance
• what these algorithms are NOT

Get the concept and the '##' marks stop looking like noise. Save it. 👇

slides.title: The one-line definition
slides.body: BPE and WordPiece are subword tokenization algorithms. They learn a fixed vocabulary of frequent character chunks from a corpus, then split any text into those chunks. Rare or unseen words get broken into smaller known pieces instead of becoming a single 'unknown' token.

slides.title: Why not just use words?
slides.body: A pure word vocabulary explodes: every plural, tense, typo, and name needs its own slot, and you still hit words you've never seen. Pure characters fix that but make sequences painfully long and strip away meaning. Subwords are the middle path — small vocabulary, no unknown words.

slides.title: Subwords: the sweet spot
slides.body: Subword tokenization keeps a vocabulary of ~30k-50k pieces. Common words stay whole ('the', 'token'), while rare ones split into meaningful parts ('tokenization' → 'token' + 'ization'). You get a manageable vocabulary, reasonable sequence length, and zero out-of-vocabulary failures.

slides.title: See the split yourself
slides.code:
from transformers import AutoTokenizer
bert = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("bert-base-uncased")
print(bert.tokenize("tokenization is unbelievable"))
# ['token', '##ization', 'is', 'un', '##bel', '##iev', '##able']

slides.title: What they are NOT
slides.body: These are not the model — they run before it, turning text into integer IDs. They are not based on meaning or grammar; the splits are purely statistical, so 'token' + 'ization' is luck, not linguistics. And they're not interchangeable: a model only understands the exact tokenizer it was trained with.

slides.title: The 30-second model
bullets: Subword = between words and chars
Learn a vocab of frequent chunks
Rare words split, never 'unknown'
BPE merges by frequency
WordPiece merges by likelihood

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 58.
slides.body: Next: why this tiny preprocessing step quietly decides your cost, context, and quality.

57-2.caption:
The tokenizer is the most ignored part of an LLM — and it silently controls your bill, your context window, and how well the model reads code, math, and other languages. 💸

Every API charge is per token, not per word. Every context limit is in tokens. A clumsy tokenizer can spend 3 tokens on one number or 10 on a non-English word, and you pay for all of them. BPE and WordPiece are why English is cheap, why some languages cost double, and why a model can even attempt a word it has never seen.

This post is the WHY: the concrete stakes of subword tokenization — cost, vocabulary size, fairness, and robustness.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• why tokens are the unit of cost and context
• no more out-of-vocabulary failures
• vocabulary size vs sequence length
• why some languages cost more
• how it makes models robust to typos

Understand the stakes and you'll write cheaper prompts. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Tokens are the unit of everything
slides.body: You don't pay per word or per request — you pay per token, and the context window is measured in tokens too. The tokenizer decides how many tokens a given string becomes, so it directly sets your cost, your latency, and how much text fits before the model forgets the start.

slides.title: No more 'unknown' tokens
slides.body: Old word-level systems hit a wall on any word missing from the vocabulary — names, slang, new terms all collapsed to a single [UNK]. Subword tokenization removes that failure mode entirely: worst case, a word is spelled out from smaller pieces or raw bytes, so the model always has something to work with.

slides.title: The language tax
slides.body: Most tokenizers are trained mostly on English, so English words map to few tokens. Languages with other scripts — Hindi, Thai, Arabic — often fragment into many more tokens for the same meaning. That means non-English users pay more and fit less context: a real fairness and cost issue.

slides.title: Robust to mess
slides.body: Because words decompose into pieces, a typo like 'teh' or a brand-new product name still tokenizes into something usable instead of breaking. Byte-level BPE goes further — it can encode literally any character, including emoji and rare scripts, by falling back to raw bytes.

slides.title: Count tokens before you pay
slides.code:
import tiktoken
enc = tiktoken.encoding_for_model("gpt-4o")
text = "The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog"
print(len(enc.encode(text)))   # 9 tokens
print(enc.encode("antidisestablishmentarianism"))  # multiple pieces

slides.title: Practical takeaways
bullets: Budget in tokens, not words
Non-English text costs more
Shorter prompts = cheaper + faster
Subwords kill [UNK] failures
Count tokens before shipping

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 58.
slides.body: Next: open the hood — exactly how BPE merges pairs to build its vocabulary.

57-3.caption:
BPE started as a 1994 data-compression trick. The idea is almost embarrassingly simple: find the most common adjacent pair, glue it into one symbol, repeat. Do that 30,000 times and you have a tokenizer. ⚙️

Both BPE and WordPiece are trained on a corpus before the model ever runs. They start from individual characters and greedily build up larger units. BPE picks the most frequent pair to merge; WordPiece picks the pair that most increases the corpus likelihood. The output is an ordered list of merge rules plus a vocabulary.

This post opens the hood: the training loop, how merges work, the difference in the merge criterion, and how encoding applies the rules.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• the BPE training loop, step by step
• a worked merge example
• how WordPiece changes the merge rule
• encoding: applying merges to new text
• byte-level BPE and the '##' marker

See the loop and the magic becomes mechanical. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Step 1: start from characters
slides.body: Split the corpus into words, then each word into its characters with an end marker. The initial vocabulary is just the set of all characters. Every word is now a sequence of these atomic symbols — nothing has been merged yet.

lines.text: corpus: low low low lower newest
start: l o w / l o w e r / n e w e s t
merge -> 'es'  : n e w es t
vocab grows: l o w e r n s t es est

slides.title: Step 2: merge the top pair
slides.body: Scan every adjacent symbol pair across the corpus and count frequencies. The single most frequent pair is merged into a new symbol and added to the vocabulary, recorded as a merge rule. The corpus is rewritten using the new symbol, then you count again.

slides.title: Where WordPiece differs
slides.body: WordPiece runs the same loop but changes the choice rule. Instead of picking the most frequent pair, it picks the pair that maximizes the likelihood of the training data — roughly, the pair whose merge score (count of pair) / (count of part1 x count of part2) is highest. This favors merges that are informative, not just common.

slides.title: Step 3: encoding new text
slides.body: Training produces an ordered merge list. To tokenize new text, BPE applies those merges in order until none apply. WordPiece instead does greedy longest-match: from the start of each word, take the longest piece in the vocabulary, mark continuations with '##', and repeat on the remainder.

slides.title: BPE encode, from scratch
Byte-level + the '##' marker
slides.body: GPT-style models use byte-level BPE: they merge bytes, not characters, so any Unicode input is representable with no [UNK]. WordPiece prefixes word-internal pieces with '##' — 'playing' → 'play' + '##ing' — so the tokenizer knows which pieces start a word and which continue one.

slides.title: The algorithm in one breath
bullets: Start from characters
Count adjacent pairs
Merge the best pair, repeat
BPE = frequency, WP = likelihood
Encode by applying merges in order

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 58.
slides.body: Next: a full runnable example — train your own tokenizer and inspect the merges.

57-4.caption:
Enough theory — let's train a real subword tokenizer, inspect its merges, and watch BPE and WordPiece split the same word differently. All in Python, no GPU. 💻

The Hugging Face 'tokenizers' library trains a working BPE or WordPiece model in a few lines, and 'tiktoken' gives you the exact GPT-4 tokenizer for counting. You'll train one on a tiny corpus, encode text into IDs, decode back, and compare the two algorithms head to head.

This is the code-heavy post. Copy each block in order; every snippet runs on its own.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• install and grab a corpus
• train a BPE tokenizer from scratch
• encode, inspect IDs, and decode
• train WordPiece and compare splits
• use tiktoken to count GPT-4 tokens

Run it, then swap in your own text. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 0. Install + a tiny corpus
slides.code:
# pip install tokenizers tiktoken
with open("corpus.txt", "w") as f:
    f.write("low lower lowest newer newest " * 200)
    f.write("tokenization is unbelievable and reusable")

slides.title: 1. Train a BPE tokenizer
slides.code:
from tokenizers import Tokenizer, models, trainers, pre_tokenizers

tok = Tokenizer(models.BPE(unk_token="[UNK]"))
tok.pre_tokenizer = pre_tokenizers.Whitespace()
trainer = trainers.BpeTrainer(vocab_size=60,
                              special_tokens=["[UNK]"])
tok.train(["corpus.txt"], trainer)
print("vocab size:", tok.get_vocab_size())

slides.title: 2. Encode + decode
slides.code:
out = tok.encode("lowest newer")
print(out.tokens)   # ['low', 'est', 'newer'] (approx)
print(out.ids)      # learned integer IDs

print(tok.decode(out.ids))   # round-trips back to text

slides.title: 3. Train WordPiece + compare
slides.code:
wp = Tokenizer(models.WordPiece(unk_token="[UNK]"))
wp.pre_tokenizer = pre_tokenizers.Whitespace()
wp_trainer = trainers.WordPieceTrainer(vocab_size=60,
                                       special_tokens=["[UNK]"])
wp.train(["corpus.txt"], wp_trainer)
print(wp.encode("unbelievable").tokens)
# note the '##' continuation marks

slides.title: 4. The real GPT-4 tokenizer
slides.code:
import tiktoken
enc = tiktoken.encoding_for_model("gpt-4o")
ids = enc.encode("tokenization is unbelievable")
print(len(ids), ids)
for i in ids:
    print(repr(enc.decode([i])))   # see each piece

slides.title: 5. Save + reload your tokenizer
slides.code:
tok.save("my-bpe.json")
from tokenizers import Tokenizer
reloaded = Tokenizer.from_file("my-bpe.json")
print(reloaded.encode("lowest").tokens)
# tokenizer is fully portable as one JSON file

slides.title: The line beginners botch
bullets: Mixing a model with the wrong tokenizer
Forgetting special tokens ([CLS]/[SEP])
Assuming 1 token = 1 word for cost
Ignoring the leading-space convention
Retraining vocab on too little data

slides.title: Ship-it checklist
bullets: Always load the model's own tokenizer
Count tokens with tiktoken before billing
Save tokenizer + model together
Test decode round-trips your text

slides.body: Next: the tokenization mistakes that silently corrupt models and inflate bills.

57-5.caption:
Most tokenization bugs are silent. The code runs, the output looks fine, and your model is quietly worse, your bill quietly higher, and your numbers quietly wrong. 🪤

Subword tokenization is easy to use and easy to misuse. Pairing the wrong tokenizer with a model, mishandling leading spaces, assuming a token equals a word, or trusting the model to do arithmetic on fragmented digits — these are the traps that bite real systems in production.

This post is the field guide to the failure modes, why each happens, and the fix.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• using a mismatched tokenizer
• the leading-space trap
• 'one token = one word' math errors
• why LLMs fumble digits and spelling
• the non-English token tax

Learn the traps now so you don't debug them at 2am. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Mistake 1: mismatched tokenizer
slides.body: Each model is trained on one specific tokenizer, mapping pieces to IDs. Feed it IDs from a different tokenizer and every embedding lookup is wrong — the model reads gibberish but still produces confident output. This is the most common and most damaging tokenization bug.

slides.title: Fix: load the matched pair
slides.code:
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModel
name = "bert-base-uncased"
tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(name)   # SAME name
model = AutoModel.from_pretrained(name)     # for both
# never mix tok from one model with weights from another

slides.title: Mistake 2: the leading space
slides.body: In byte-level BPE (GPT), a leading space is part of the token: ' dog' and 'dog' are different IDs. Concatenating strings or stripping spaces by hand can produce token sequences the model never saw in training, hurting quality in subtle ways.

slides.title: Mistake 3: token != word
slides.body: Estimating cost or context as 'one token per word' is wrong by a lot. English averages ~0.75 words per token; code, numbers, and other languages are far denser. Budgets built on the word assumption blow past context limits and under-estimate spend.

slides.title: How far off the assumption is
Mistake 4: digits + spelling
slides.body: Tokenizers split numbers oddly — '1234' might be '12' + '34' — so the model never sees clean digits, which is one reason LLMs fumble arithmetic. The same fragmentation breaks letter-counting and spelling tasks: the model literally can't see individual characters.

slides.title: Watch a number fragment
slides.code:
import tiktoken
enc = tiktoken.encoding_for_model("gpt-4o")
for i in enc.encode("The total was 18452 dollars"):
    print(repr(enc.decode([i])))
# '18', '452' -> the model never sees '18452' as one unit

slides.title: Mistake 5: the language tax
slides.body: Building a product for non-English users on an English-centric tokenizer means their text costs 2-4x more tokens for the same meaning — higher bills, less context, slower responses. Check token counts in your target languages before you price or set limits.

slides.title: The fix list
bullets: Always pair tokenizer with its model
Let the tokenizer own spacing
Budget in real tokens, not words
Don't trust raw LLM arithmetic
Test token counts per language

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 58.
slides.body: Next: Day 58 moves up the stack — embeddings and how tokens become meaning.

14-1.caption:
"How far apart are these two things?" sounds simple — until you realize there are several different answers, and picking the wrong one quietly breaks your model. 📏

A distance metric turns a pair of vectors into a single number: small means similar, large means different. L1, L2, and cosine are the three you'll meet everywhere — in k-NN, clustering, search, and every embedding system. They don't measure the same thing, and that's the whole point.

This post builds the picture before any code: what a metric is, the three you must know, and why "distance" is a choice, not a fact.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• what a distance metric actually is
• L1 (Manhattan): walking the grid
• L2 (Euclidean): the straight line
• cosine: angle, not length
• why the choice changes your results
• the one-line intuition for each

Meet the three before you trust any "similarity" score. Save it. 👇

slides.title: What a distance metric is
slides.body: A distance metric is a function that takes two vectors and returns one number: how far apart they are. Zero means identical; bigger means more different. Almost every similarity, clustering, and search algorithm sits on top of one. Choosing it is a modeling decision, not a detail.

slides.title: L1 — Manhattan distance
slides.body: Sum the absolute differences, coordinate by coordinate: |x1−y1| + |x2−y2| + … It's the distance a taxi drives on a grid — you can't cut through buildings. It cares about total displacement across all dimensions and is robust to a few large outliers.

slides.title: L2 — Euclidean distance
slides.body: The straight-line distance you learned in geometry: square the differences, sum them, take the square root. It's the length of the arrow connecting the two points. Squaring means big gaps dominate, so L2 is sensitive to large coordinate differences.

slides.title: Cosine — measure the angle
slides.body: Cosine similarity ignores how long the vectors are and asks only: do they point the same way? It's the cosine of the angle between them — 1 means same direction, 0 means perpendicular, −1 means opposite. Distance is just 1 minus that.

slides.title: All three in NumPy
slides.code:
import numpy as np
a = np.array([3.0, 1.0])
b = np.array([1.0, 3.0])

l1 = np.abs(a - b).sum()                 # 4.0
l2 = np.sqrt(((a - b) ** 2).sum())       # ~2.83
cos = (a @ b) / (np.linalg.norm(a) * np.linalg.norm(b))
print(l1, round(l2, 2), round(cos, 2))   # 4.0 2.83 0.6

slides.title: Why the choice matters
slides.body: On the same data, L2 might call two points close while cosine calls them far — and vice versa. A recommender using cosine treats a long document and a short one the same if their topics match; using L2 it would punish the length gap. The metric encodes what you mean by "similar."

slides.title: One line each
bullets: L1 = grid walk: sum of |differences|
L2 = straight line: root of squared sum
Cosine = angle: do they point the same way?
L1/L2 see magnitude; cosine ignores it
Pick the one that matches your data

slides.title: "Distance" isn't one thing
slides.body: The first trap is assuming there's a single correct distance. There isn't. L1, L2, and cosine are answering different questions about the same pair of vectors. The right metric depends on whether magnitude matters, how many dimensions you have, and what "similar" means for your task.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 15.
slides.body: Next: why this single choice quietly decides the quality of your search, clustering, and recommendations.

14-2.caption:
Pick the wrong distance metric and your search returns garbage, your clusters split on the wrong axis, and your "similar items" aren't. None of it throws an error. 🚨

The metric is the lens every similarity-based system looks through. k-NN, k-means, recommenders, vector databases, RAG retrieval — all of them rank by distance. Change the metric and you change what "close" means, which changes every result downstream.

This post is the "so what": where the choice between L1, L2, and cosine actually decides whether your system works.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• why every similarity system depends on it
• magnitude vs direction — what you're really choosing
• why cosine dominates text & embeddings
• how scale and units silently break L2
• the curse of dimensionality angle
• the cost of a silent wrong choice

This is the decision that quietly sets your quality ceiling. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Every ranker uses one
slides.body: k-NN classifies by nearest neighbors, k-means assigns points to the closest center, a vector DB returns the closest embeddings, a recommender ranks by similarity. "Closest" is defined entirely by your metric. Swap it and every one of these systems behaves differently — with no error to warn you.

slides.title: Magnitude vs direction
slides.body: The core question a metric answers: does size matter? L1 and L2 say yes — a vector twice as long is far from the original. Cosine says no — only the angle counts. Choosing between them is really choosing whether the length of your vectors carries meaning or just noise.

left.title: Use L2 when…
items: Features are on the same scale

slides.title: Why text loves cosine
slides.body: A long article and a short note about the same topic produce vectors pointing the same way but with different lengths. Cosine sees them as nearly identical; L2 would penalize the length gap and call them far apart. That's why embeddings, TF-IDF, and RAG retrieval default to cosine.

slides.title: Same topic, different length
slides.code:
import numpy as np
short = np.array([1.0, 1.0, 0.0])     # short note
long  = np.array([5.0, 5.0, 0.0])     # long article, same topic

cos = (short @ long) / (np.linalg.norm(short) * np.linalg.norm(long))
l2  = np.sqrt(((short - long) ** 2).sum())
print(round(cos, 2), round(l2, 2))    # 1.0  ~5.66
# cosine: identical direction. L2: 'far apart' just from length

slides.title: Unscaled features wreck L2
slides.body: L2 adds up squared differences across columns. If income is in dollars (~50000) and age is in years (~40), income dominates entirely and age stops mattering. Without standardizing features first, your "Euclidean distance" is really just a distance in dollars.

slides.title: High dimensions favor cosine
slides.body: As dimensions grow, L2 distances between all points become eerily similar — the curse of dimensionality erodes the contrast that makes "nearest" meaningful. Cosine, which compares direction, holds up better in the high-dimensional embedding spaces that modern ML lives in.

slides.title: Where this bites
bullets: Vector DB / RAG retrieval quality
k-means cluster shapes
k-NN class boundaries
Recommender 'similar items'
Dedup & near-duplicate detection

slides.title: The silent wrong choice
slides.body: A bad learning rate gives you a loud, broken loss curve. A bad metric gives you results that look plausible but are subtly wrong — slightly worse recommendations, clusters that don't quite make sense. Nothing errors, so it goes unnoticed for months. That's what makes it dangerous.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 15.
slides.body: Next: the actual math — the formulas, the geometry, and how the three metrics relate.

14-3.caption:
Strip away the names and L1, L2, and cosine are three short formulas built from the same two ingredients: differences and dot products. 🔧

L1 sums absolute differences. L2 is the root of summed squares. Cosine is a normalized dot product. Once you see the formulas side by side — and the geometry behind each — the differences stop being mysterious and start being obvious.

This post is the engine room: the exact math, the unit "balls" each metric draws, and the relationships that tie them together.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• the three formulas, precisely
• the general Minkowski (Lp) family
• the shape of each metric's unit ball
• cosine = dot product over magnitudes
• why normalizing turns L2 into cosine
• the metric properties they (don't) satisfy

This is the part that makes the formulas click. Save it. 👇

slides.title: L1 and L2, precisely
slides.body: L1(x,y) = Σ|xᵢ−yᵢ|, the sum of absolute differences. L2(x,y) = √(Σ(xᵢ−yᵢ)²), the square root of summed squares. Same inputs, different penalty: L1 grows linearly with each gap, L2 grows with the square, so L2 reacts far more to a single large difference.

slides.title: The Minkowski family
slides.body: L1 and L2 are special cases of one formula: Lp(x,y) = (Σ|xᵢ−yᵢ|^p)^(1/p). p=1 gives Manhattan, p=2 gives Euclidean, and p→∞ gives Chebyshev (the single largest coordinate gap). Tuning p smoothly trades robustness for sensitivity to big differences.

slides.title: Cosine, precisely
slides.body: cos(x,y) = (x · y) / (‖x‖ · ‖y‖). The dot product x·y measures aligned magnitude; dividing by the two lengths cancels magnitude entirely, leaving pure direction. Cosine DISTANCE is 1 − cos, so identical direction = 0 distance, perpendicular = 1.

slides.title: The formulas as code
Unit balls: all points at distance 1
Normalize, and L2 becomes cosine
slides.body: Scale every vector to unit length first. Then the squared L2 distance between two of them equals 2 − 2·cos(θ) — a direct function of the angle. So on normalized vectors, ranking by Euclidean distance gives the exact same order as ranking by cosine. They're the same comparison.

slides.title: Are they true 'metrics'?
slides.body: L1 and L2 are proper metrics: non-negative, symmetric, zero only for identical points, and they obey the triangle inequality. Cosine DISTANCE is symmetric and non-negative but breaks the triangle inequality, so it's a 'dissimilarity,' not a strict metric — fine for ranking, risky for some algorithms.

slides.title: Keep these straight
bullets: L1 = Σ|Δ| (linear penalty)
L2 = √ΣΔ² (squared penalty)
Lp dial: p=1, 2, ∞
Cosine = dot / (lengths)
Normalize → L2 ranking = cosine ranking

slides.title: Forgetting cosine breaks triangle
slides.body: Some algorithms (like certain metric trees or proofs of correctness) assume the triangle inequality holds. Raw cosine distance doesn't satisfy it. If you need a true metric, convert to angular distance (arccos of similarity) or work with L2 on normalized vectors instead.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 15.
slides.body: Next: a runnable notebook — compute all three, see them disagree, and watch k-NN flip.

14-4.caption:
You don't really feel the difference between L1, L2, and cosine until you compute all three on the same pair and watch them disagree. 💻

No theory this time — just code. We'll implement each metric from scratch, verify against scikit-learn, build a tiny k-NN, and flip its prediction just by changing the metric. Then we'll see why normalizing first makes Euclidean and cosine agree.

This post is code-heavy on purpose. Run it, change the vectors, break it. That's how the intuition locks in.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• all three metrics from scratch
• vectorized pairwise distances
• cross-checking with scikit-learn
• k-NN that flips when you swap the metric
• proving normalize → cosine = L2
• the runnable takeaways

Run it once and it's yours forever. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 1. From scratch
2. Vectorized pairwise distances
slides.code:
import numpy as np
X = np.array([[3., 1.], [1., 3.], [2., 2.]])

# L2 distance from row 0 to every row, no loop
diff = X - X[0]
l2_all = np.sqrt((diff ** 2).sum(axis=1))
print(np.round(l2_all, 3))     # [0. 2.828 1.414]

# cosine similarity matrix
norms = np.linalg.norm(X, axis=1, keepdims=True)
U = X / norms
print(np.round(U @ U.T, 3))    # pairwise cosine sims

slides.title: 3. Cross-check with scikit-learn
slides.code:
from sklearn.metrics.pairwise import (
    manhattan_distances, euclidean_distances, cosine_distances)
import numpy as np
X = np.array([[3., 1.], [1., 3.]])

print(manhattan_distances(X)[0, 1])   # 4.0   -> L1
print(euclidean_distances(X)[0, 1])   # 2.828 -> L2
print(cosine_distances(X)[0, 1])      # 0.4   -> cosine
# matches our from-scratch numbers exactly

slides.title: 4. k-NN flips with the metric
slides.code:
from sklearn.neighbors import KNeighborsClassifier
import numpy as np
X = np.array([[10., 10.], [1., 1.], [2., 1.]])
y = np.array([0, 1, 1])              # class of each point
q = np.array([[8., 8.]])             # query point

for m in ['euclidean', 'cosine']:
    knn = KNeighborsClassifier(n_neighbors=1, metric=m).fit(X, y)
    print(m, '->', knn.predict(q)[0])
# euclidean -> 0 (nearest by length)
# cosine    -> 1 (same direction as [1,1])

slides.title: 5. Normalize → cosine equals L2
slides.code:
import numpy as np
X = np.random.rand(5, 8)
U = X / np.linalg.norm(X, axis=1, keepdims=True)   # unit length

i, j = 0, 3
l2_sq = ((U[i] - U[j]) ** 2).sum()
cos   = U[i] @ U[j]
print(round(l2_sq, 4), round(2 - 2 * cos, 4))      # identical
# squared L2 on unit vectors == 2 - 2*cos(theta)

slides.title: Reading the results
slides.body: The from-scratch numbers match scikit-learn exactly, which is your sanity check. The k-NN demo is the punchline: the same query, same data, opposite prediction — purely from the metric. And the last snippet proves normalizing collapses L2 and cosine into the same ranking.

slides.title: Try this yourself
bullets: Change the query to flip k-NN back
Add a feature with huge scale, watch L2 break
Use scipy.spatial.distance.cdist for big arrays
Normalize before cosine to avoid divide-by-zero
Time the loop vs the vectorized version

slides.title: Looping over rows in Python
slides.body: Writing a double for-loop to compute pairwise distances is correct but agonizingly slow on real data. Use vectorized NumPy, sklearn's pairwise functions, or scipy's cdist — they push the work to optimized C and run orders of magnitude faster. Never hand-loop distances at scale.

slides.title: 6. The runnable takeaway
slides.code:
import numpy as np
from sklearn.preprocessing import normalize

# 1) scale features so no column dominates L2
# 2) for embeddings/text, L2-normalize then use cosine OR euclidean
X = np.random.rand(100, 16)
Xn = normalize(X)                 # unit-length rows
# now euclidean and cosine rank neighbors identically
print(np.allclose(np.linalg.norm(Xn, axis=1), 1.0))   # True

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 15.
slides.body: Next: the mistakes that silently corrupt distances — scaling, dimensions, and divide-by-zero.

14-5.caption:
Most distance bugs don't crash anything. They just quietly make your results a little wrong — and "a little wrong" at scale is expensive. ⚠️

The failures are almost always the same handful: forgetting to scale features, using cosine where magnitude actually mattered, trusting L2 in hundreds of dimensions, or dividing by a zero vector. Each one produces plausible-looking output, which is exactly why they survive code review.

This post is the field guide to the traps — what goes wrong, why, and the one-line fix for each.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• not standardizing before L2/L1
• using cosine when length mattered
• trusting L2 in high dimensions
• the zero-vector divide-by-zero
• mixing a metric with the wrong algorithm
• forgetting cosine breaks the triangle rule

Know the failure modes before you trust the score. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 1. Not scaling features first
slides.body: The #1 mistake. L1 and L2 sum across columns, so a feature with a large range (salary in dollars) drowns out small-range features (age, ratios). Your distance becomes a distance in one column. Always standardize or normalize before any Euclidean-style metric.

slides.title: The scaling fix
slides.code:
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
import numpy as np
X = np.array([[50000., 25.], [52000., 60.]])   # $, years

# WRONG: dollars dominate, age ignored
raw = np.sqrt(((X[0] - X[1]) ** 2).sum())
# RIGHT: equal footing
Xs = StandardScaler().fit_transform(X)
print(round(raw, 1))            # ~2000.3, all dollars
print(Xs)                       # now both features comparable

slides.title: 2. Cosine when length mattered
slides.body: Cosine throws away magnitude. If two users both like the same genres but one rated 500 movies and the other 3, cosine calls them identical — even though the activity level is hugely informative. When magnitude carries signal, cosine silently discards it. Use L2 (scaled) instead.

slides.title: 3. Trusting L2 in high-D
slides.body: In hundreds or thousands of dimensions, Euclidean distances between all pairs concentrate to nearly the same value — the curse of dimensionality. "Nearest neighbor" becomes meaningless because everything is roughly equidistant. Reduce dimensions first, or switch to cosine on embeddings.

slides.title: 4. The zero-vector trap
slides.body: Cosine divides by each vector's length. A zero vector (an all-zero embedding, an empty TF-IDF row) has length 0, so you get a divide-by-zero: NaN or a crash. These sneak in from empty documents or padding. Guard the norm or filter zero rows before computing cosine.

slides.title: Safe cosine
5. Metric / algorithm mismatch
slides.body: k-means is defined around squared Euclidean distance — its mean-based update only minimizes L2. Plugging cosine into vanilla k-means is mathematically inconsistent. If you want angular clustering, L2-normalize your vectors first (spherical k-means) or use a method built for cosine.

slides.title: 6. Cosine isn't a true metric
slides.body: Cosine distance violates the triangle inequality, so algorithms that rely on it (ball trees, some bounds, clustering proofs) can misbehave. For a proper metric on directions, use angular distance = arccos(similarity)/π, or run L2 on normalized vectors, which is a true metric and ranks identically.

slides.title: The pre-flight checklist
bullets: Scaled features before L1/L2?
Does magnitude matter? → not cosine
High dimensions? → reduce or use cosine
Any zero vectors? → guard the norm
Metric matches the algorithm?

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 15.
slides.body: Day 15 starts a new topic — but you'll carry these three metrics into everything that ranks by similarity.

18-1.caption:
A function isn't just a way to avoid retyping code — it's a named box you hand inputs to and get an output from, without caring what's inside. 🧰

A function packages a piece of behavior behind a name and a parameter list. *args and **kwargs extend that idea: they let one function accept any number of extra positional or keyword arguments instead of a fixed, hard-coded list.

This post is the map — what a function, *args, and **kwargs actually ARE before we touch why or how.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• what a function really is
• parameters vs arguments
• what *args collects
• what **kwargs collects
• why the * and ** matter
• the one mental model to keep

Get the shape in your head and the syntax stops looking like noise. Save it. 👇

slides.title: What a function is
slides.body: A function is a named, reusable block of behavior. You define it once with inputs (parameters) and a body, then call it as many times as you like with real values (arguments). It hides the 'how' behind a name so the rest of your code can just say 'do this.'

slides.title: Parameters vs arguments
slides.body: Parameters are the names in the def line — the slots the function declares. Arguments are the actual values you pass when you call it. 'def greet(name)' has a parameter name; 'greet("Ada")' passes the argument "Ada". Same idea, two sides of the call.

slides.title: A function, defined and called
slides.code:
def add(a, b):        # a, b are parameters
    return a + b

result = add(3, 4)    # 3, 4 are arguments
print(result)         # 7

slides.title: What *args collects
slides.body: Prefix a parameter with one star and it soaks up every EXTRA positional argument into a tuple. 'def f(*args)' lets you call f(1, 2, 3) and inside, args is (1, 2, 3). The name 'args' is just convention — the star is what does the work.

slides.title: *args in action
slides.code:
def total(*args):       # args is a tuple
    return sum(args)

print(total(1, 2))      # 3
print(total(1, 2, 3, 4))# 10
print(total())          # 0

slides.title: What **kwargs collects
slides.body: Prefix a parameter with two stars and it soaks up every EXTRA keyword argument into a dict. 'def f(**kwargs)' lets you call f(x=1, y=2) and inside, kwargs is {'x': 1, 'y': 2}. Positional goes to *args; named goes to **kwargs.

slides.title: **kwargs in action
slides.code:
def show(**kwargs):       # kwargs is a dict
    for key, val in kwargs.items():
        print(key, "=", val)

show(name="Ada", age=30)
# name = Ada
# age = 30

slides.title: The star is packing
slides.body: In a def line, * and ** PACK leftover arguments into a tuple and a dict. The names args and kwargs are pure convention — you could write *nums or **opts. What matters is the count of stars: one for positional, two for keyword.

slides.title: The mental model
bullets: Function = named box: inputs in, output out
Parameters = slots; arguments = values
*args = extra positional -> tuple
**kwargs = extra keyword -> dict
The star count is what matters, not the name

slides.title: Thinking 'args' and 'kwargs' are keywords
slides.body: Beginners assume the words args and kwargs are special syntax. They aren't — they're just conventional variable names. 'def f(*things)' and 'def f(**opts)' work identically. Python only cares about the one or two stars; the name after them is yours to choose.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 19.
slides.body: Next: why *args and **kwargs are the difference between a rigid function and one that scales.

18-2.caption:
A function with a fixed five-parameter signature breaks the moment you need a sixth. One that accepts *args and **kwargs bends instead of breaking. 🌊

*args and **kwargs matter because they let functions stay flexible: wrappers that forward anything, APIs that grow without breaking callers, and decorators that don't need to know what they're wrapping.

This post is the 'so what' — where this flexibility actually pays off.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• why rigid signatures break
• how wrappers forward arguments blindly
• why decorators depend on it
• keeping APIs backward-compatible
• when a fixed signature is still better
• where you'll meet it in real libraries

This is what separates a function you can extend from one you have to rewrite. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Rigid signatures break
slides.body: A function that hard-codes every parameter is brittle: the day you need one more input, every caller and the function itself must change. *args and **kwargs let a function accept what it doesn't yet know about, so adding inputs doesn't force a rewrite of everything that calls it.

slides.title: Wrappers can forward anything
slides.body: The killer use case is the pass-through. A logging, timing, or retry wrapper shouldn't need to know the wrapped function's exact parameters. Catching *args and **kwargs and forwarding them lets one wrapper sit in front of ANY function, unchanged.

slides.title: One wrapper, any function
Decorators depend on it
slides.body: Almost every decorator you'll ever use — from Flask routes to functools.lru_cache — relies on *args and **kwargs. They wrap your function without knowing its signature, then forward the call intact. Without these, generic decorators simply couldn't exist.

slides.title: APIs stay backward-compatible
slides.body: Library authors use **kwargs to add new options without breaking old calls. A function can accept **kwargs, pull out new keys it understands, and ignore the rest. Existing callers keep working; new callers get new features. That's how mature APIs evolve safely.

right.title: Use a fixed signature
items: The inputs are known and few

slides.title: Growing an API without breaking it
slides.code:
def connect(host, port, **opts):
    timeout = opts.get("timeout", 30)   # new option
    retries = opts.get("retries", 3)    # new option
    print(host, port, timeout, retries)

connect("db", 5432)                     # old call still works
connect("db", 5432, timeout=5)          # new call uses new opt

slides.title: Where you'll meet it
bullets: Decorators (@app.route, @cache)
print(*values), max(*items)
Subclass __init__ forwarding super()
pandas / requests keyword options
Test fixtures and mocks

slides.title: Reaching for it when you have a fixed set of inputs
slides.body: Flexibility has a cost: a signature of (*args, **kwargs) tells the reader and the IDE nothing. If your function takes a known, small set of inputs, name them explicitly. Save *args and **kwargs for genuine pass-through and variadic cases — not as a way to dodge writing parameter names.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 19.
slides.body: Next: under the hood — exactly how Python matches a call to parameters, packs, and unpacks.

18-3.caption:
*args and **kwargs feel like magic until you watch Python match a call to a signature, slot by slot — then the rules about ordering and unpacking stop being mysterious. 🔍

Under the hood there are two mirror operations: PACKING (the def side gathers extras into a tuple and dict) and UNPACKING (the call side spreads a sequence or dict back into arguments). Get the matching order right and you'll never see a 'multiple values for argument' error again.

This post is the engine room.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• the exact parameter ordering rule
• packing on the def side
• unpacking on the call side
• how positional vs keyword get routed
• keyword-only arguments after *
• the matching algorithm Python runs

See the machine and the syntax becomes obvious. Save it. 👇

slides.title: The fixed parameter order
slides.body: A def line must list parameters in one legal order: positional/default first, then *args, then keyword-only, then **kwargs. You can't put a regular parameter after *args or anything after **kwargs. This ordering is what lets Python route every argument unambiguously.

slides.title: Packing: the def side gathers
slides.body: On the definition side, one star PACKS leftover positional values into a tuple and two stars PACK leftover named values into a dict. Python fills the named parameters first, then everything positional that's left goes to *args and everything keyword that's left goes to **kwargs.

slides.title: Packing in one signature
slides.code:
def f(a, b, *args, **kwargs):
    print("a, b   =", a, b)
    print("args   =", args)     # tuple
    print("kwargs =", kwargs)   # dict

f(1, 2, 3, 4, x=5, y=6)
# a, b   = 1 2
# args   = (3, 4)
# kwargs = {'x': 5, 'y': 6}

slides.title: Unpacking: the call side spreads
slides.body: The same stars do the OPPOSITE job at a call site. One star spreads a list or tuple into separate positional arguments; two stars spread a dict into keyword arguments. 'f(*[1, 2])' is exactly 'f(1, 2)'. Same symbol, mirror operation — pack in def, unpack in call.

slides.title: Unpacking at the call site
Keyword-only arguments
slides.body: Anything written after *args (or a bare *) becomes keyword-only: callers MUST pass it by name, never by position. 'def f(a, *, mode)' forces f(1, mode="x"). This is how libraries make important options explicit and stop them from being set by accident in the wrong slot.

slides.title: Keyword-only after a bare *
The rules that explain it all
bullets: Order: pos, *args, kw-only, **kwargs
* packs in def, spreads in a call
Positional leftovers -> tuple
Keyword leftovers -> dict
After * everything is keyword-only

slides.title: Confusing packing with unpacking
slides.body: The same star means opposite things by location. In a def, *args COLLECTS; in a call, *seq SPREADS. People expect 'f(*args)' inside a wrapper to pass the tuple as one argument — it actually unpacks it back into separate arguments. Knowing which side you're on removes the confusion entirely.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 19.
slides.body: Next: hands-on — the *args / **kwargs patterns you'll actually type every week, in code.

18-4.caption:
Reading about *args and **kwargs is fine. Typing the patterns you'll actually reuse — wrappers, forwarding, merging defaults — is what makes them automatic. 💻

This post is deliberately code-heavy. We'll build a variadic function, a transparent wrapper that forwards everything, a function that merges defaults with **kwargs, unpack a dict into a call, mix fixed params with both stars, and forward arguments up an inheritance chain. Every snippet is short, runnable, and the kind of thing you'll genuinely retype next week.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• a variadic sum-style function
• a wrapper that forwards anything
• merging defaults with **kwargs
• unpacking a config dict into a call
• mixing fixed params with *args/**kwargs
• the forwarding pattern, fully assembled

Run every snippet once and these stop being syntax you look up. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 1. A variadic function
2. A transparent wrapper
3. Merge defaults with **kwargs
4. Unpack a config dict into a call
5. Mix fixed params with both stars
slides.code:
def report(title, *rows, sep=" | ", **meta):
    print(title.upper())
    for r in rows:
        print("  ", sep.join(r))
    print("meta:", meta)

report("sales",
       ["q1", "100"], ["q2", "120"],
       sep=" -> ", region="EU")
# SALES
#    q1 -> 100
#    q2 -> 120
# meta: {'region': 'EU'}

slides.title: 6. Forwarding to super() in __init__
Patterns to memorize
bullets: def f(*nums) — variadic input
def wrapper(*a, **kw): func(*a, **kw) — forward
config.update(kwargs) — merge defaults
func(**settings) — unpack a dict
super().__init__(**kwargs) — forward up

slides.title: functools.wraps matters
slides.body: When you wrap a function, the wrapper replaces its name, docstring, and signature with the wrapper's. @functools.wraps copies that metadata back so tools, help(), and tracebacks still see the real function. It's a one-line decorator that makes your *args/**kwargs wrappers behave like the originals.

slides.title: Forwarding without forwarding (forgetting the stars)
slides.body: Inside a wrapper, calling func(args, kwargs) passes the tuple and dict as two ordinary arguments — not the spread-out values. You must write func(*args, **kwargs) with the stars to UNPACK them back. Dropping the stars is the single most common wrapper bug.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 19.
slides.body: Next: the *args / **kwargs mistakes that bite everyone — and how to dodge them.

18-5.caption:
*args and **kwargs are powerful, but the same flexibility that makes them elegant will hide bugs if you misuse mutable defaults, drop the stars, or bury your signature behind two stars. 🩹

Most of these bugs aren't syntax errors — they're silent: shared state across calls, arguments passed as the wrong type, and signatures so vague no one can read them. Knowing the failure modes is what turns a clever wrapper into production code.

This post is the field guide to what goes wrong.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• the mutable default argument trap
• forgetting the stars when forwarding
• the 'multiple values for argument' error
• hiding a real signature behind **kwargs
• swallowing typos silently in **kwargs
• when to just name your parameters

Learn the failure modes before they ship. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 1. The mutable default trap
slides.body: A default like 'def f(items=[])' is evaluated ONCE at definition, so every call shares the same list. Append to it and the 'default' grows between calls. The fix is the None sentinel: default to None, then create a fresh list inside the function body.

slides.title: Shared default vs fresh default
slides.code:
def bad(x, items=[]):       # one shared list!
    items.append(x)
    return items

print(bad(1))   # [1]
print(bad(2))   # [1, 2]  <- leaked!

def good(x, items=None):
    items = items if items is not None else []
    items.append(x)
    return items

print(good(1))  # [1]
print(good(2))  # [2]  <- fresh

slides.title: 2. Forgetting the stars when forwarding
slides.body: Inside a wrapper, func(args, kwargs) passes the tuple and dict as two positional arguments — the wrapped function gets a tuple and a dict instead of the real arguments. You must write func(*args, **kwargs) to unpack them. This is the most common wrapper bug there is.

slides.title: No stars vs stars
3. 'multiple values for argument'
slides.body: Pass a value positionally AND by keyword for the same parameter and Python raises 'got multiple values for argument.' It happens easily when you unpack a dict that contains a key already filled by a positional argument. Decide each value goes one way, not both.

slides.title: The double-assignment error
4. Hiding the real signature
slides.body: Writing 'def process(*args, **kwargs)' when the function actually needs exactly a path and a mode tells callers and IDEs nothing. They lose autocomplete, type hints, and clear errors on bad calls. Use the stars for genuine pass-through — not to avoid naming inputs you already know.

slides.title: 5. **kwargs swallows typos silently
slides.body: If a function accepts **kwargs and you misspell an option — timeoutt instead of timeout — it lands in kwargs and is silently ignored. No error, no warning, just a default quietly used. Validate known keys or prefer explicit keyword-only parameters when the option set is fixed.

slides.title: Silent typo vs caught typo

root.question: Do you know the inputs up front?

slides.title: Stay out of trouble
bullets: Never use a mutable default; use None
Forward with func(*args, **kwargs) — keep the stars
One value, one channel (pos OR keyword)
Don't hide a known signature behind **kwargs
Validate kwargs keys or use keyword-only params

58-1.caption:
Before BERT and GPT, there was a quieter revolution: turning words into arrows. 🧭

Word2Vec and GloVe are word embedding methods. They learn a dense vector — a list of ~100-300 numbers — for every word, placed so that words used in similar contexts sit close together. 'King' lands near 'queen', 'Paris' near 'France', and the geometry encodes meaning the model never saw spelled out. No labels, no dictionaries — just patterns in raw text.

This post is the concept: what a word embedding is, what 'distributional meaning' means, and how Word2Vec and GloVe relate as two routes to the same idea.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• what a word embedding actually is
• the distributional hypothesis in one line
• why dense vectors beat one-hot IDs
• Word2Vec vs GloVe at a glance
• what these embeddings are NOT

Get the concept and 'king − man + woman' stops being a parlor trick. Save it. 👇

slides.title: The one-line definition
slides.body: A word embedding is a dense vector of real numbers — usually 100 to 300 of them — that represents a word's meaning. Word2Vec and GloVe learn these vectors from raw text so that words with similar usage end up near each other in the space.

slides.title: The distributional hypothesis
slides.body: "You shall know a word by the company it keeps." Words that appear in similar contexts tend to mean similar things. 'Coffee' and 'tea' show up around 'cup', 'morning', 'drink' — so they get similar vectors. Meaning is inferred purely from co-occurrence, no human definitions needed.

slides.title: Meaning becomes math
slides.body: Once words are vectors, similarity is just distance (or cosine angle). Closer = more alike. Famously, the offset between vectors captures relationships: king − man + woman lands near queen. The model never learned grammar — this structure emerges from the statistics of text.

slides.title: Load and probe a vector
slides.code:
import gensim.downloader as api
wv = api.load("glove-wiki-gigaword-100")
print(wv["king"].shape)          # (100,)
print(wv.most_similar("king")[:3])
# [('queen', 0.75), ('prince', 0.73), ('throne', 0.70)]

slides.title: What they are NOT
slides.body: These are static embeddings: each word gets exactly ONE vector, so 'bank' (river) and 'bank' (money) collapse into one point. They are not contextual like BERT, where the vector shifts per sentence. And they have no sense of word order — they only see who appears near whom.

slides.title: The 30-second model
bullets: Embedding = dense vector per word
Built from context co-occurrence
Similar usage → nearby vectors
Word2Vec predicts, GloVe counts
One word = one fixed vector

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 59.
slides.body: Next: why these vectors quietly powered a decade of NLP — and still matter.

58-2.caption:
Word embeddings were the first time a model 'understood' that 'big' and 'large' are related — without anyone telling it. That single shift made NLP usable. 🚀

Before embeddings, words were just IDs: opaque, unrelated, and useless to a fresh model. Word2Vec and GloVe gave every word a meaningful starting point, so a sentiment classifier or search engine could generalize from 'great' to 'fantastic' it never saw labeled. They turned scarce labeled data into something a model could leverage, and they're still the cheap, fast, interpretable baseline.

This post is the WHY: transfer learning, generalization, the cost story, and where pretrained vectors still win today.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• why one-hot IDs crippled old NLP
• embeddings as the first transfer learning
• how they unlock generalization
• why they're cheap, fast, and interpretable
• where they still beat giant LLMs

Understand the stakes and you'll reach for vectors first. Save it. 👇

slides.title: The problem they solved
slides.body: With one-hot IDs, a model that learned 'excellent → positive' knew nothing about 'superb'. Every word was an island. You needed enormous labeled data to cover every synonym. Embeddings broke that wall: related words share geometry, so learning about one helps with all its neighbors.

slides.title: The first transfer learning
slides.body: Train embeddings once on billions of words of raw text, then drop them into any downstream task. The expensive part — learning what words mean — is done unsupervised and reused everywhere. This 'pretrain then reuse' pattern is exactly what GPT and BERT later scaled up.

slides.title: Generalization for free
slides.body: Embeddings let a classifier transfer signal between similar words. If 'delicious' was labeled positive in training, 'tasty' — sitting right next to it — gets handled correctly even if it never appeared in your labels. You get coverage far beyond your training set.

slides.title: Cheap, fast, transparent
slides.body: An embedding lookup is a single table read — no forward pass, no GPU. The whole model is a few hundred MB and runs on a laptop. You can literally print a word's nearest neighbors to debug it. For many production filters and search rankers, that's plenty.

slides.title: Generalization in action
slides.code:
wv.similarity("delicious", "tasty")   # 0.78
wv.similarity("delicious", "laptop")   # 0.04
# a sentiment model learns 'delicious'=+
# and transfers to 'tasty' for free

slides.title: Practical takeaways
bullets: Embeddings = reusable word knowledge
Generalize beyond your labels
Tiny, fast, GPU-free
Great baseline before reaching for LLMs
Static: no context disambiguation

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 59.
slides.body: Next: open the hood — skip-gram, negative sampling, and GloVe's matrix math.

58-3.caption:
How do you teach a model the meaning of a word using nothing but a sliding window over text? You turn it into a guessing game — and let backprop do the rest. ⚙️

Word2Vec trains a tiny neural net to predict context from a word (skip-gram) or a word from its context (CBOW). The trick that made it fast is negative sampling: instead of scoring all 50k words, score the true neighbor against a handful of random fakes. GloVe takes a different road — it builds a global word-word co-occurrence matrix and factorizes it so vector dot products match log co-occurrence counts.

This post opens the hood: skip-gram, negative sampling, the GloVe objective, and why both land in a similar place.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• skip-gram vs CBOW, the two Word2Vec tasks
• how the sliding window makes training pairs
• negative sampling, the speed trick
• GloVe's co-occurrence factorization
• why two methods, similar vectors

See the loop and the magic becomes mechanical. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Step 1: slide a window
slides.body: Move a small window (say ±2 words) across the corpus. For each center word, every neighbor inside the window becomes a (center, context) training pair. 'The cat sat on the mat' yields (cat, the), (cat, sat), (sat, cat), (sat, on), and so on.

slides.title: Step 2: the speed problem
slides.body: The naive objective uses a softmax over the whole vocabulary — 50,000+ words — for every single training pair. That's wildly expensive. Computing and normalizing all those scores millions of times would make training crawl. Word2Vec needs a shortcut.

slides.title: Step 3: negative sampling
slides.body: Reframe it as binary classification. For each real (center, context) pair, draw k random 'negative' words that didn't appear. Train the model to say YES to the real neighbor and NO to the fakes. You now update ~k+1 vectors per step instead of 50,000 — a massive speedup.

slides.title: Negative-sampling objective
slides.code:
import numpy as np
sig = lambda x: 1/(1+np.exp(-x))
# v: center vec, u_pos: true context, u_negs: k fakes
loss = -np.log(sig(v @ u_pos))
loss -= sum(np.log(sig(-v @ u)) for u in u_negs)
# maximize score of real pair, minimize fakes

slides.title: GloVe's different route
slides.body: GloVe skips prediction entirely. It first counts how often word i appears near word j across the whole corpus, building a co-occurrence matrix X. Then it learns vectors whose dot product matches log(X_ij), weighting frequent pairs more. It fits global statistics directly instead of streaming windows.

slides.title: The mechanics in five lines
bullets: Window makes (center, context) pairs
Skip-gram predicts context from word
Negative sampling replaces full softmax
GloVe factorizes a co-occurrence matrix
Both yield meaningful vector geometry

slides.body: Next: train your own vectors and run the famous king − man + woman analogy.

58-4.caption:
Let's stop talking and run it. In under 30 lines you'll train embeddings on your own text, query nearest neighbors, and watch king − man + woman ≈ queen fall out of the math. 💻

We'll use gensim — the standard, batteries-included library for Word2Vec. You'll see the full loop: tokenize a corpus, train skip-gram with negative sampling, inspect similarity, do vector arithmetic, and load Stanford's pretrained GloVe vectors when you don't have a big corpus of your own.

This post is code-heavy and copy-paste runnable.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• install and prep a corpus
• train Word2Vec in one call
• query similarity and neighbors
• the analogy: king − man + woman
• load pretrained GloVe vectors

Run it once and the concepts lock in. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 0. Install + import
slides.code:
# pip install gensim
from gensim.models import Word2Vec
import gensim.downloader as api

slides.title: 1. Prep a tokenized corpus
slides.code:
# each sentence = a list of lowercased tokens
sentences = [
    ["the", "king", "ruled", "the", "kingdom"],
    ["the", "queen", "ruled", "the", "realm"],
    ["a", "man", "walked", "the", "dog"],
    ["a", "woman", "walked", "the", "cat"],
]
# real use: stream millions of sentences

slides.title: 2. Train Word2Vec
slides.code:
model = Word2Vec(
    sentences,
    vector_size=100,   # embedding dimension
    window=5,          # context window
    min_count=1,       # keep rare words (demo)
    sg=1,              # 1 = skip-gram, 0 = CBOW
    negative=5,        # negative samples
    epochs=50,
)
wv = model.wv         # the trained vectors

slides.title: 3. Similarity & neighbors
slides.code:
print(wv.similarity("king", "queen"))
print(wv.most_similar("king", topn=3))
# vectors are tiny here; train on a big
# corpus for meaningful neighbors

slides.title: Key knobs explained
slides.body: vector_size sets how rich each vector is (100-300 is typical). window is how many neighbors count as context. sg=1 picks skip-gram. negative is the number of fake words per real pair. min_count drops rare words in real corpora — keep it low only for tiny demos.

slides.title: 4. The famous analogy
slides.code:
# load a properly trained model first:
wv = api.load("word2vec-google-news-300")
res = wv.most_similar(
    positive=["king", "woman"],
    negative=["man"], topn=1)
print(res)   # [('queen', 0.71)]

slides.title: 5. Load pretrained GloVe
slides.code:
# no big corpus? use Stanford's vectors
glove = api.load("glove-wiki-gigaword-100")
print(glove.most_similar("paris", topn=3))
print(glove.doesnt_match(
    ["breakfast", "lunch", "dinner", "car"]))
# 'car' is the odd one out

slides.title: Production tips
bullets: Train on a large, domain corpus
Raise min_count to drop noise
300 dims is a solid default
Save with model.save() and reload
Or just use pretrained GloVe

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 59.
slides.body: Next: the traps — polysemy, OOV words, bias, and the cosine-similarity gotchas.

58-5.caption:
The fastest way to ship a broken NLP feature is to trust word vectors without knowing where they lie to you. ⚠️

Word2Vec and GloVe are powerful but quietly limited. They give one vector per word — so 'apple' the company and 'apple' the fruit are the same point. They can't handle words they never saw. And because they learn from human text, they absorb human bias: 'doctor − man + woman' has notoriously pointed toward 'nurse'. Treat them as a fast baseline, not a meaning oracle.

This post is the failure modes: the mistakes that bite teams in production and how to dodge them.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• polysemy: one vector for every meaning
• the out-of-vocabulary cliff
• bias baked in from the corpus
• why similar ≠ same (antonyms cluster)
• preprocessing & cosine pitfalls

Know the failure modes before you trust the output. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Polysemy: one word, one vector
slides.body: Static embeddings give 'bank' a single vector that averages river-bank and money-bank. Any word with multiple senses gets a blurry compromise that fits none well. If your task hinges on disambiguating meaning by context, you need contextual models like BERT, not Word2Vec.

slides.title: Out-of-vocabulary words
slides.body: If a word wasn't in the training corpus, classic Word2Vec and GloVe have no vector for it — your code throws a KeyError or you get nothing. New slang, product names, and typos all fall off this cliff. FastText fixes this by using subword character n-grams.

slides.title: Guard against missing words
Bias is baked in
slides.body: Embeddings learn from human text, so they inherit human stereotypes. The classic finding: 'man : computer_programmer :: woman : homemaker'. If you use raw vectors in hiring, search, or recommendations, you can amplify discrimination. Audit and debias before deploying anything sensitive.

slides.title: Similar is not same
slides.body: 'Good' and 'bad' sit close together — both are adjectives used in identical contexts ('the movie was ___'). Embeddings capture relatedness, not sentiment polarity. Don't assume nearest neighbors share meaning; antonyms are often near-neighbors, which wrecks naive similarity rules.

slides.title: Cosine & preprocessing traps
slides.body: Use cosine similarity, not raw Euclidean — vector magnitude often reflects word frequency, not meaning. And match the training preprocessing: if vectors were trained lowercased and untokenized your way, 'King' or 'don't' may miss. Mismatched tokenization silently degrades everything.

slides.title: Cosine, done right
Avoid the traps
bullets: Polysemy? use contextual models
Handle OOV or use FastText
Audit for bias before shipping
Near ≠ synonym (antonyms cluster)
Use cosine; match preprocessing
