slides.body: Next day: contextual embeddings — how ELMo and BERT fixed the one-vector problem.

59-1.caption:
What is a 'language model', stripped of the hype? It is a function that, given some text, assigns a probability to what comes next. That's it. Everything else — chat, code, translation — is built on that single trick. 📖

A language model learns the statistical structure of language from huge amounts of text. It doesn't store sentences; it learns a distribution over the next token given everything before it. Sample from that distribution repeatedly and you get fluent text.

This post is the concept: what a language model actually is, the n-gram idea it grew from, and why 'predict the next token' is more powerful than it sounds.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• the one-line definition of a language model
• 'next-token prediction' explained plainly
• n-grams: the original language model
• why probability over text is so useful
• what a language model is NOT

Get the core idea and every LLM headline makes more sense. Save it. 👇

slides.title: What a Language Model Actually Is
The one-line definition
slides.body: A language model is a function that assigns a probability to a sequence of words, usually by predicting the next token given the tokens before it. Train it on enough text and it learns the patterns of a language — grammar, facts, and style — as a probability distribution it can sample from.

slides.title: Next-token prediction
slides.body: Give the model 'The cat sat on the ___' and it outputs a probability for every possible next token: 'mat' high, 'roof' lower, 'banana' tiny. Pick one, append it, and ask again. Repeat and you generate text one token at a time. That single loop is the engine behind every chatbot.

slides.title: n-grams: the original LM
slides.body: Long before neural nets, a language model was just counting. An n-gram model estimates P(next word | previous n-1 words) from raw frequencies in a corpus. A bigram model looks back one word, a trigram two. Simple, fast, and the direct ancestor of today's models.

lines.text: corpus: 'I like tea. I like coffee.'

slides.title: A bigram model in 8 lines
slides.code:
from collections import Counter, defaultdict
text = "i like tea i like coffee i love tea".split()
model = defaultdict(Counter)
for a, b in zip(text, text[1:]):
    model[a][b] += 1            # count what follows each word
print(dict(model["like"]))      # Counter({'tea': 1, 'coffee': 1})
print(dict(model["i"]))         # Counter({'like': 2, 'love': 1})

slides.title: Why probability over text wins
slides.body: Once you can score how likely any continuation is, you can do almost anything: generate text by sampling, rank translations, fix spelling, autocomplete, and detect gibberish. Many tasks reduce to 'which continuation is most probable?' — which is why this one capability scales into so many products.

slides.title: What it is NOT
slides.body: A language model is not a database of facts and not a reasoning engine with beliefs. It has no notion of truth — only of likelihood. Fluent, confident text is what it optimizes for, which is exactly why it can sound certain while being wrong. It models language, not the world.

slides.title: The 30-second model
bullets: LM = probability over text
Core skill: predict the next token
Generate by sampling, token by token
n-grams were the first LMs
Models likelihood, not truth

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 60.
slides.body: Next: why this 'predict the next word' trick became the foundation of modern AI.

59-2.caption:
Why did 'guess the next word' — a task that sounds almost trivial — turn into the foundation of modern AI? Because to predict the next word well, a model is quietly forced to learn grammar, facts, reasoning, and style. 🚀

Next-token prediction is a deceptively rich objective. To finish 'The capital of France is ___' correctly, the model must encode geography. To finish a proof, it must encode logic. Scale the data and the model, and these abilities emerge from the same simple goal — no task-specific training required.

This post is the WHY: why such a simple objective is so powerful, why scale changed everything, and why one model can now do thousands of tasks.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• why next-token prediction forces real understanding
• self-supervision: training without labels
• the scaling laws that changed the field
• emergent abilities from a single objective
• one model, thousands of tasks

Understand why this matters and the LLM boom stops being magic. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Why 'Guess the Next Word' Runs AI
A trivial task that isn't
slides.body: Predicting the next token sounds shallow, but doing it well is not. To finish 'The capital of France is ___' you need a fact. To finish a half-written function you need code semantics. The objective is simple; the knowledge required to ace it is enormous. That gap is where the intelligence lives.

slides.title: Self-supervision: free labels
slides.body: The training signal is the text itself. Hide the next token, ask the model to predict it, compare to the real one. No human labeling needed — every sentence on the internet is a free training example. This is why models can train on trillions of tokens that no team could ever annotate by hand.

slides.title: Scale changed everything
slides.body: For decades, bigger language models were only a little better. Around 2018-2020 that flipped: scaling data, parameters, and compute together produced smooth, predictable gains in capability. Performance followed power-law 'scaling laws', so spending more reliably bought more ability.

slides.title: Emergent abilities
slides.body: Some skills barely exist at small scale, then appear sharply past a threshold: multi-step arithmetic, following instructions, in-context learning. They were never trained for directly — they emerge from doing next-token prediction well enough, on enough data. That surprised even the researchers building them.

slides.title: One model, many tasks via prompts
slides.code:
# No retraining - just change the prompt
prompt_translate = "English: cat\nFrench:"
prompt_classify = "Review: 'loved it!'\nSentiment:"
prompt_extract  = "Email: hi@acme.com here\nFind email:"
# Same model handles translation, classification, extraction
# because all three are 'predict the next token'

slides.title: Why it matters, in 5 lines
bullets: Next-token prediction demands real knowledge
Self-supervision = unlimited free data
Scaling laws make gains predictable
Abilities emerge past scale thresholds
One model replaces hundreds of narrow ones

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 60.
slides.body: Next: open the hood — exactly how a model turns context into a next-token probability.

59-3.caption:
How does a language model actually go from 'The cat sat on the' to a probability for every possible next token? Four stages: tokenize, embed, contextualize, predict. ⚙️

Under the hood there is no magic, just a pipeline. Text becomes integer tokens, tokens become vectors, a stack of transformer layers mixes those vectors so each one 'sees' the others, and a final layer turns the last vector into a probability distribution over the whole vocabulary. Sampling from it gives the next token.

This post opens the hood: the full forward pass, what attention does, how training adjusts weights, and how generation loops.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• the four-stage forward pass
• what attention actually computes
• logits → softmax → probabilities
• how training tunes billions of weights
• the autoregressive generation loop

See the pipeline and 'how do LLMs work' stops being a mystery. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Stage 1-2: tokens to vectors
slides.body: Text is split into tokens and each token ID is mapped to a learned vector (an embedding). Position information is added so the model knows word order. After this stage your sentence is a grid of numbers — one row per token — that the network can do math on.

slides.title: Stage 3: attention mixes context
slides.body: The transformer layers let every token look at every other token and pull in relevant information. 'It' can attend to 'cat' to figure out what 'it' refers to. Stacking many attention layers builds an increasingly context-aware representation of each position.

slides.title: Stage 4: logits to probabilities
slides.body: The final layer maps the last token's vector to one raw score (a logit) per vocabulary entry. Softmax turns those scores into probabilities that sum to 1. Now you have P(next token) over the whole vocabulary — the model's prediction for what comes next.

slides.title: Logits → softmax → probs
slides.code:
import torch, torch.nn.functional as F
logits = torch.tensor([2.0, 1.0, 0.1])   # raw scores per token
probs = F.softmax(logits, dim=-1)
print(probs)       # tensor([0.659, 0.242, 0.099])
print(probs.sum()) # tensor(1.0) - a valid distribution

slides.title: How training works
slides.body: During training the model predicts the next token at every position, the loss measures how wrong it was (cross-entropy), and backpropagation nudges billions of weights to make the correct token more likely next time. Repeat over trillions of tokens and the weights encode the structure of language.

slides.title: Generate one token with a real model
slides.code:
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer
tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("gpt2")
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("gpt2")
ids = tok("The cat sat on the", return_tensors="pt").input_ids
logits = model(ids).logits[0, -1]      # scores for next token
next_id = logits.argmax()              # greedy pick
print(tok.decode(next_id))             # ' floor' (most likely)

slides.title: The pipeline in 5 lines
bullets: Tokenize -> embed -> transformer -> predict
Attention lets tokens share context
Softmax turns logits into probabilities
Training = predict, measure loss, update
Generate by looping the forward pass

59-4.caption:
Enough theory — let's run a real language model, inspect the probabilities it assigns, and watch sampling settings change what it writes. All in Python, no GPU needed. 💻

The Hugging Face 'transformers' library loads a small GPT-2 in a few lines. You'll see the raw next-token distribution, pick tokens greedily and by sampling, watch temperature change creativity, and generate full text — then peek at the top candidates the model considered at each step.

This is the code-heavy post. Copy each block in order; every snippet runs on its own.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• install and load GPT-2
• inspect the next-token distribution
• greedy vs sampling vs temperature
• generate full text with .generate()
• see the top-k candidates per step

Run it, then swap in your own prompts. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Run a Language Model in 30 Lines
0. Install + load GPT-2
slides.code:
# pip install transformers torch
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer
import torch
tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("gpt2")
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("gpt2").eval()
print(model.config.vocab_size)   # 50257 possible tokens

slides.title: 1. Inspect the next-token distribution
slides.code:
import torch.nn.functional as F
ids = tok("The capital of France is", return_tensors="pt").input_ids
with torch.no_grad():
    logits = model(ids).logits[0, -1]    # scores for next token
probs = F.softmax(logits, dim=-1)
top = torch.topk(probs, 5)
for p, i in zip(top.values, top.indices):
    print(f"{tok.decode(i)!r}: {p:.3f}")   # ' Paris' ranks high

slides.title: 2. Greedy vs sampling
slides.code:
# Greedy: always take the most likely token (deterministic)
greedy_id = logits.argmax()
print("greedy:", repr(tok.decode(greedy_id)))

# Sampling: draw from the distribution (varied output)
sampled_id = torch.multinomial(probs, num_samples=1)
print("sampled:", repr(tok.decode(sampled_id)))

slides.title: 3. Temperature controls creativity
4. Generate full text
slides.code:
ids = tok("In the future, AI will", return_tensors="pt").input_ids
out = model.generate(ids, max_new_tokens=30,
                     do_sample=True, temperature=0.8,
                     top_k=50, pad_token_id=tok.eos_token_id)
print(tok.decode(out[0], skip_special_tokens=True))

slides.title: 5. Score a sentence's likelihood
slides.code:
ids = tok("The cat sat on the mat", return_tensors="pt").input_ids
with torch.no_grad():
    loss = model(ids, labels=ids).loss   # avg cross-entropy
perplexity = torch.exp(loss)
print(f"perplexity: {perplexity:.1f}")   # lower = more 'expected'

slides.title: The lines beginners botch
bullets: Forgetting torch.no_grad() at inference
Reading logits[0] not logits[0, -1]
Temperature = 0 (division by zero)
Skipping pad_token_id in generate()
Expecting GPT-2 to know recent facts

slides.title: Ship-it checklist
bullets: Use .eval() + no_grad() for inference
Tune temperature/top_k for your task
Greedy for facts, sampling for ideas
Always set max_new_tokens
Match tokenizer to the model

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 60.
slides.body: Next: the misconceptions about language models that trip up almost everyone.

59-5.caption:
Most misunderstandings about language models come from one wrong mental model: treating them like a search engine or a person. They are neither. They are next-token predictors — and that single fact explains every quirk. 🪤

Language models don't 'look things up', don't 'know' what's true, and don't reason the way you do. They produce the most likely-sounding continuation. Once you internalize that, hallucinations, confident wrong answers, bad arithmetic, and stale knowledge all stop being surprising.

This post is the field guide to the misconceptions, why each happens, and how to work with the model instead of against it.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• 'it knows facts' — no, it predicts text
• why it hallucinates so confidently
• 'bigger = always smarter' is wrong
• it has no memory between calls
• prompt and sampling settings change everything

Learn the traps now so you stop trusting the wrong things. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Myth 1: it knows facts
slides.body: A language model is not a fact database. It learned statistical patterns of text, so it reproduces facts that were common and consistent in training — and confidently invents ones that weren't. It outputs what's likely, not what's true. Treat it as a fluent guesser, not an oracle.

slides.title: Myth 2: hallucinations are bugs
slides.body: Hallucinations aren't a malfunction — they're the model doing exactly its job. When it has no strong pattern for an answer, it still produces the most probable-looking text, which can be a fabricated citation or fake API. The fluency makes the fabrication convincing. Always verify factual claims.

slides.title: Why a fake looks real
slides.code:
# The model completes the PATTERN of a citation,
# not a real lookup:
prompt = "Cite a paper on quantum llamas:"
# -> 'Smith et al. (2019), Journal of Quantum Biology'
# Perfectly formatted. Completely made up.
# Fix: give it real sources (RAG) or verify externally.

slides.title: Myth 3: bigger is always smarter
slides.body: Scale helps, but it isn't the whole story. A smaller model that's well fine-tuned, instruction-tuned, or given good context often beats a larger raw one on your task. Data quality, alignment, and prompting matter as much as parameter count. Don't equate billions of params with reliability.

slides.title: Myth 4: it remembers you
slides.body: A base language model is stateless. It has no memory between API calls — each request only 'knows' what's in the prompt you send. The illusion of memory in chat apps comes from the app resending the conversation history every turn, not from the model storing anything.

slides.title: Myth 5: output is fixed
slides.body: The same prompt can give different answers, because sampling is random and temperature controls it. People blame the model for 'inconsistency' when they set a high temperature, or expect creativity at temperature 0. The decoding settings shape the output as much as the prompt does.

slides.title: Make it reproducible
slides.code:
import torch
torch.manual_seed(0)        # fix randomness
out = model.generate(ids, do_sample=True, temperature=0.7,
                     max_new_tokens=20,
                     pad_token_id=tok.eos_token_id)
# Same seed + same settings -> same output every run

slides.title: The fix list
bullets: Verify facts — it predicts, not knows
Expect hallucinations; ground with sources
Pick models by task fit, not just size
Send context; the model has no memory
Control temperature for consistency

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 60.
slides.body: Next: Day 60 builds up — how prompting and decoding strategies steer these predictions.

60-1.caption:
Same transformer DNA, opposite jobs. GPT reads left-to-right to PREDICT the next word. BERT reads both directions at once to UNDERSTAND a sentence. ↔️

They were born within a year of each other from the same paper — 'Attention Is All You Need' — but split the transformer in half. GPT kept the decoder and became a generator. BERT kept the encoder and became an analyzer. That single architectural choice decides almost everything else: what they're good at, how they train, and where they fail.

This post is the concept: what each model actually is, the encoder/decoder split, and why 'generate vs understand' is the cleanest way to hold them in your head.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• what GPT and BERT each are in one line
• encoder vs decoder, the core split
• unidirectional vs bidirectional attention
• generate vs understand framing
• what they are NOT

Get the split and every difference downstream makes sense. Save it. 👇

slides.title: GPT vs BERT: The Core Split
Two models, one ancestor
slides.body: Both descend from the 2017 Transformer. GPT (Generative Pre-trained Transformer, OpenAI) is a decoder-only model that predicts the next token. BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers, Google) is an encoder-only model that builds rich representations of existing text.

slides.title: Why direction matters
slides.body: GPT can only look at tokens before the current one — it must, or it would cheat at predicting the next word. BERT has no such constraint: it sees the whole sentence at once, so each word's representation is informed by what comes after it too. Direction is the root cause of every other difference.

slides.title: Generate vs understand
slides.body: The cleanest mental model: GPT is a writer, BERT is a reader. Ask GPT to continue a prompt and it streams new text. Ask BERT to read a sentence and it hands you a vector you can classify, search, or tag. One produces language; the other comprehends it.

slides.title: Two libraries, two purposes
slides.code:
from transformers import pipeline

# GPT-style: generate text
gen = pipeline("text-generation", model="gpt2")
print(gen("The future of AI is")[0]["generated_text"])

# BERT-style: fill a masked blank
fill = pipeline("fill-mask", model="bert-base-uncased")
print(fill("The capital of France is [MASK].")[0]["token_str"])

slides.title: What they are NOT
slides.body: BERT is not a chatbot — it can't freely generate coherent paragraphs, because it was never trained to produce text left-to-right. GPT is not primarily a classifier — you can fine-tune it, but bidirectional understanding tasks are BERT's home turf. They are not interchangeable drop-ins.

slides.title: The 30-second model
bullets: Same transformer roots, split in half
GPT = decoder = generate
BERT = encoder = understand
GPT reads one way; BERT reads both
Direction drives every other difference

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 61.
slides.body: Next: why this split matters — which one to reach for, and what it costs you to pick wrong.

60-2.caption:
Pick the wrong one and you'll burn money making GPT do classification, or wonder why BERT can't write your email. The architecture isn't trivia — it's a hiring decision. 💼

GPT and BERT optimize for opposite outcomes, so the choice between them is really a choice about your task. Need to generate, summarize, chat, or reason step-by-step? That's GPT's lane. Need to classify, search, tag entities, or rank documents fast and cheap? BERT was purpose-built for exactly that, and it'll do it on a fraction of the compute.

This post is the WHY: where each model wins, the cost and latency story, and how the field actually splits work between them.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• why the choice is a task decision
• generation jobs → GPT
• understanding jobs → BERT
• the cost, size, and latency tradeoff
• why BERT didn't disappear in the LLM era

Understand the stakes and you'll reach for the right tool first. Save it. 👇

slides.title: The choice IS the task
slides.body: You don't pick a model and then find work for it — you start from the task and the architecture follows. Generation tasks want a decoder; understanding tasks want an encoder. Getting this backwards is the single most common and expensive mistake teams make with transformers.

slides.title: Generation needs a generator
slides.body: Anything that produces new text — answering open questions, writing, translating, chatting — needs to emit tokens one at a time. That's exactly what GPT's autoregressive decoder does. BERT physically cannot do this well; it has no mechanism to generate fluent left-to-right output.

slides.title: Understanding needs full context
slides.body: Classification, tagging, and search care about the meaning of fixed text, not about producing more. BERT's bidirectional view gives each token the richest possible representation, which is why it dominates the GLUE-style understanding benchmarks it was designed for.

slides.title: Latency and money
slides.body: A BERT classifier runs one forward pass and returns an answer — milliseconds, often on CPU. A large GPT model generates token by token, each step a full forward pass, and the big ones need GPUs and API budgets. For high-volume classification, BERT is often 10-100x cheaper.

slides.title: How the field splits the work
Right tool, right job
slides.code:
from transformers import pipeline

# fast, cheap understanding -> BERT
clf = pipeline("sentiment-analysis")   # DistilBERT under the hood
print(clf("This product is fantastic!"))
# [{'label': 'POSITIVE', 'score': 0.999}]

# open-ended generation -> GPT
gen = pipeline("text-generation", model="gpt2")
print(gen("Write a tagline for a coffee shop:", max_new_tokens=20))

slides.title: Why BERT survived the LLM boom
slides.body: Generative LLMs grabbed the spotlight, but BERT-family encoders quietly power most production NLP: search ranking, content moderation, spam filters, intent detection. They're small, fast, and accurate on understanding tasks — using a 175B model to label sentiment is over-engineering.

slides.title: Practical takeaways
bullets: Start from the task, not the model
Generate → GPT; understand → BERT
BERT is far cheaper at high volume
Encoders still dominate search & classification
Don't use a giant LLM where BERT fits

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 61.
slides.body: Next: open the hood — causal LM vs masked LM, and how each one actually trains.

60-3.caption:
The architectures differ, but the real divergence is the training objective. GPT plays 'guess the next word'. BERT plays 'fill in the blank'. Those two games shape everything. ⚙️

GPT is trained as a causal language model: hide everything after position t, predict the token at t. Do that over trillions of tokens and you get a fluent next-word predictor. BERT is trained with masked language modeling: randomly hide ~15% of tokens and predict them from both sides, plus (originally) a next-sentence task. One objective forces left-to-right generation; the other forces deep bidirectional understanding.

This post opens the hood: causal LM, masked LM, the attention masks, and why each objective produces the model it does.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• causal language modeling (GPT)
• masked language modeling (BERT)
• the attention mask that enforces each
• why MLM can't generate fluently
• pretrain → fine-tune for both

See the training loop and the behavior stops being mysterious. Save it. 👇

slides.title: GPT: next-token prediction
slides.body: Feed in a sequence, shift it by one, and ask the model to predict each next token from everything before it. The loss is cross-entropy over the vocabulary at every position. Train this on trillions of tokens and fluent generation emerges as a side effect of getting good at the guessing game.

slides.title: BERT: masked language modeling
slides.body: Randomly corrupt about 15% of the input tokens, then ask the model to recover the originals using context from BOTH directions. Because it predicts blanks in the middle rather than the next word, BERT must build a representation that fuses left and right context — the source of its understanding power.

lines.text: Original: the cat sat on the mat
Masked:   the cat [MASK] on the mat

slides.title: The 80/10/10 masking detail
slides.body: Of the 15% chosen tokens, BERT replaces 80% with [MASK], 10% with a random word, and leaves 10% unchanged. This trick stops the model from only learning to handle the literal [MASK] symbol, which never appears at fine-tuning time. It forces robust representations of every token.

slides.title: Causal vs masked, in code
slides.code:
import torch
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoModelForMaskedLM

# GPT: causal LM head, predicts the NEXT token
gpt = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("gpt2")

# BERT: masked LM head, predicts MASKED tokens
bert = AutoModelForMaskedLM.from_pretrained("bert-base-uncased")

# different heads, because different objectives
print(type(gpt).__name__, type(bert).__name__)

slides.title: Why MLM can't generate well
slides.body: Masked LM only ever predicts a few blanks inside complete sentences — it never learns to build text from scratch, token by token. So BERT has no good way to start from nothing and stream coherent output. The objective that gives it bidirectional power is exactly what removes its ability to generate.

slides.title: The mechanics in five lines
bullets: GPT trains by predicting the next token
BERT trains by filling masked blanks
Causal mask hides the future for GPT
BERT sees both sides, so it can't generate
Both pretrain on raw text, then fine-tune

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 61.
slides.body: Next: run both — generate text with GPT-2 and classify with BERT in real code.

60-4.caption:
Enough theory — let's run both in the same notebook. You'll generate text with GPT-2, fill a blank with BERT, fine-tune BERT for classification, and feel the difference in your hands. 💻

We'll use Hugging Face transformers, the standard library for both model families. You'll see the full loop: load a GPT model and stream tokens, load BERT and predict a masked word, then wire BERT to a classification head and fine-tune it on a tiny dataset. Same library, same API shape — but two completely different jobs.

This post is code-heavy and copy-paste runnable.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• install and load both models
• generate text with GPT-2
• fill-mask with BERT
• fine-tune BERT for classification
• read GPT logits for next-token probs

Run it once and the GPT/BERT split stops being abstract. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 0. Install + import
slides.code:
# pip install transformers torch datasets
import torch
from transformers import (
    AutoTokenizer,
    AutoModelForCausalLM,
    AutoModelForMaskedLM,
    AutoModelForSequenceClassification,
)

slides.title: 1. GPT-2: generate text
slides.code:
tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("gpt2")
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("gpt2")

ids = tok("The future of AI is", return_tensors="pt")
out = model.generate(**ids, max_new_tokens=20, do_sample=True, top_k=50)
print(tok.decode(out[0], skip_special_tokens=True))
# GPT streams new tokens left-to-right

slides.title: 2. BERT: fill in the blank
slides.code:
tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("bert-base-uncased")
model = AutoModelForMaskedLM.from_pretrained("bert-base-uncased")

text = "Paris is the [MASK] of France."
ids = tok(text, return_tensors="pt")
logits = model(**ids).logits
mask_pos = (ids.input_ids == tok.mask_token_id)[0].nonzero()[0]
top = logits[0, mask_pos].topk(3).indices[0]
print(tok.decode(top))   # capital  ...

slides.title: 3. Fine-tune BERT to classify
slides.code:
model = AutoModelForSequenceClassification.from_pretrained(
    "bert-base-uncased", num_labels=2)
tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("bert-base-uncased")

batch = tok(["I loved it", "Total waste of money"],
            padding=True, return_tensors="pt")
labels = torch.tensor([1, 0])
loss = model(**batch, labels=labels).loss
loss.backward()   # one training step; loop with an optimizer

slides.title: Note the head, not the body
slides.body: All three snippets load the same kind of transformer backbone — the difference is the head on top. CausalLM adds a next-token head, MaskedLM a fill-blank head, SequenceClassification a small classifier. Picking the right AutoModel class is how you choose the job.

slides.title: 4. Read GPT's next-token probs
slides.code:
tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("gpt2")
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("gpt2")

ids = tok("The capital of France is", return_tensors="pt")
logits = model(**ids).logits[0, -1]      # last position
probs = logits.softmax(-1)
top = probs.topk(5).indices
print([tok.decode(t) for t in top])      # ' Paris', ' the', ...

slides.title: Why the BERT loss looks easy
slides.body: BERT classification needs only a tiny labeled set and a few epochs because the backbone already understands language from pretraining. You're just teaching a small head to map its representations to your labels — that's the whole promise of transfer learning, made concrete.

slides.title: Production tips
bullets: Use AutoModelFor* to pick the job
DistilBERT for cheap, fast classification
Set do_sample/top_k/temperature for GPT
Batch + pad inputs for throughput
Use the Trainer API for real fine-tuning

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 61.
slides.body: Next: the traps — wrong-model choices, [CLS] mistakes, and the bidirectional gotcha.

60-5.caption:
Most GPT-vs-BERT bugs aren't subtle — they're someone using a hammer as a screwdriver. Knowing the failure modes saves you weeks. ⚠️

GPT and BERT break in predictable ways when you misuse them. People try to make BERT generate text and get garbage. They fine-tune a 175B GPT for sentiment and torch their budget. They grab the wrong token for a sentence embedding, ignore the context window, or forget that both inherit bias and hallucination risks from their training data. None of these are mysterious — they're just mismatches between the model's design and the job.

This post is the failure modes: the mistakes that bite teams in production and how to dodge them.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• using BERT to generate (it can't)
• using a huge GPT where BERT fits
• the [CLS] vs mean-pooling trap
• context-window and tokenization limits
• bias & hallucination, inherited

Know the failure modes before you trust the output. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Making BERT generate text
slides.body: BERT was trained to fill blanks, not to write left-to-right. Trying to use it as a generator produces incoherent output, because it never learned to build a sentence token by token. If you need generation, reach for a decoder (GPT family) — no amount of prompting fixes the architecture.

root.question: Do you need to GENERATE new text?

slides.title: A 175B model to label sentiment
slides.body: Using a giant generative LLM for a task BERT nails is slow and expensive. A fine-tuned DistilBERT classifies thousands of texts per second on a CPU; a large GPT API call costs money and latency per request. Match model size to task — bigger is not automatically better.

slides.title: The [CLS] embedding trap
slides.body: People grab BERT's [CLS] token as a sentence vector and feed it to cosine similarity — but raw [CLS] is a poor semantic embedding. For sentence similarity, use a model trained for it (Sentence-BERT) or mean-pool the token vectors. Raw [CLS] only works well after task-specific fine-tuning.

slides.title: Pool properly for embeddings
slides.code:
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer, util

# purpose-built sentence embeddings, not raw [CLS]
m = SentenceTransformer("all-MiniLM-L6-v2")
emb = m.encode(["a dog runs", "a puppy sprints"])
print(util.cos_sim(emb[0], emb[1]))   # ~0.7, sensible

slides.title: Ignoring the context window
slides.body: BERT-base caps at 512 tokens; longer text is silently truncated, so the tail of a document just vanishes. GPT models have larger but still finite windows. If you feed a 5,000-word doc and only the first 512 tokens are read, your results quietly degrade. Chunk or use long-context models.

slides.title: Watch truncation
slides.code:
from transformers import AutoTokenizer
tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("bert-base-uncased")

long_text = "word " * 1000
enc = tok(long_text, truncation=True, max_length=512)
print(len(enc["input_ids"]))   # 512 -> the rest was dropped
# chunk long docs instead of silently truncating

slides.title: Trusting output blindly
slides.body: Both inherit their training data's biases, and GPT additionally hallucinates — it generates fluent text with no guarantee of truth. BERT can encode stereotypes into classifications. Neither 'knows' facts; they model token statistics. Audit, ground, and verify before trusting either in production.

slides.title: Avoid the traps
bullets: Generation? never BERT — use GPT
Don't use a giant LLM where BERT fits
Use Sentence-BERT, not raw [CLS]
Mind the token limit; chunk long text
Audit bias; verify GPT's facts

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 61.
slides.body: Next day: T5 and the encoder-decoder models that try to get the best of both worlds.

61-1.caption:
A base LLM knows a little about everything and a lot about nothing in particular. Fine-tuning is how you make it yours. 🎯

Fine-tuning means taking a pretrained model and continuing training on a smaller, curated dataset so it adapts to your task, tone, or domain. You're not teaching it language from scratch — that already happened during pretraining. You're nudging billions of existing weights toward the behavior you actually want.

This post is the concept: what fine-tuning is, how it differs from pretraining and prompting, and when reaching for it is the right move versus overkill.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• what fine-tuning actually changes
• pretraining vs fine-tuning vs prompting
• the spectrum from full FT to LoRA
• what fine-tuning can and can't fix
• when NOT to fine-tune at all

Get the concept right and you stop fine-tuning things that just needed a better prompt. Save it. 👇

slides.title: The one-line definition
slides.body: Fine-tuning is continuing the training of a pretrained model on a smaller, task-specific dataset. The base model already learned language and world structure; fine-tuning shifts its existing weights toward your task, domain, or style without starting over.

slides.title: Prompting vs fine-tuning
slides.body: Prompting steers a frozen model with instructions at inference time. Fine-tuning changes the weights so the behavior is baked in. Prompts are fast and free to iterate; fine-tuning costs data and compute but yields shorter prompts, more consistency, and behavior prompting can't reliably reach.

slides.title: The methods, cheapest to heaviest
bullets: Prompt / few-shot — no training
RAG — add knowledge, no weights changed
PEFT (LoRA/QLoRA) — train ~1% of params
Full fine-tune — update every weight

slides.title: What fine-tuning is GOOD at
bullets: Locking in a format or output schema
Adopting a consistent tone or persona
Teaching a narrow, repetitive skill
Shrinking long, brittle prompts

slides.title: What it WON'T fix
slides.body: Fine-tuning teaches behavior, not facts. It is a poor way to inject fresh knowledge — that's what retrieval (RAG) is for. It also won't rescue a model that's simply too small for the task, and it can't make up for a messy, inconsistent dataset.

slides.title: Fine-tuning in 6 lines (conceptual)
slides.code:
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, Trainer

model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("base-llm")
# dataset = your curated input/output pairs
trainer = Trainer(model=model, train_dataset=dataset)
trainer.train()          # weights shift toward your task
model.save_pretrained("my-tuned-llm")

slides.title: When NOT to fine-tune
bullets: A better prompt solves it
You have <50 quality examples
The need is fresh, changing facts
You haven't measured the baseline yet

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 62.
slides.body: Next: why fine-tuning matters — the business and product case.

61-2.caption:
Everyone can call the same base model. So why does one product feel sharp and on-brand while another feels like a generic chatbot? 🧠

The difference is often fine-tuning. It's the lever that turns a general model into something that sounds like you, follows your rules every time, and does your specific job at a fraction of the prompt length — which means lower latency and lower cost per call.

This post is the why: the business case, the moat, and the economics that make fine-tuning worth the effort when prompting alone hits a wall.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• why prompting eventually plateaus
• consistency as a product feature
• the cost math: short prompts, small models
• data as a defensible moat
• the real trade-offs to weigh

Stop paying for 2,000-token system prompts on every call. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Prompting hits a ceiling
slides.body: You can stuff a system prompt with rules, examples, and tone guidance — until it gets long, slow, expensive, and still drifts. Fine-tuning bakes that behavior into the weights, so the model follows it by default instead of being reminded every single call.

slides.title: Consistency is the product
slides.body: Users forgive a model that's wrong occasionally; they don't forgive one that's unpredictable. Fine-tuning narrows variance — same format, same voice, same refusals — turning a clever demo into something dependable enough to ship.

slides.title: Smaller model, same job
slides.body: A fine-tuned small model can match a giant prompted model on a narrow task — at a fraction of the inference cost and latency. You buy the specialization once in training and collect the savings on every request after.

slides.title: Data is the moat
bullets: Your labeled examples are proprietary
Competitors can't copy your weights
The model improves as data grows
It compounds: a real flywheel

slides.title: Quantify the prompt savings
slides.code:
calls_per_day = 100_000
sys_prompt_tokens = 2_000      # prompt-only baseline
tuned_prompt_tokens = 150      # behavior now in weights

saved = (sys_prompt_tokens - tuned_prompt_tokens) * calls_per_day
print(saved, "input tokens saved / day")  # 185,000,000

slides.title: The trade-offs are real
slides.body: Fine-tuning isn't free leverage. You own a training pipeline, an eval suite, and a model that must be re-tuned when the base updates. If your task changes weekly or your data is thin, that overhead can outweigh the savings.

slides.title: The honest checklist
bullets: High call volume → savings compound
Stable, well-defined task
You can produce quality labels
Prompting genuinely fell short

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 62.
slides.body: Next: how fine-tuning works under the hood — the gradient-level mechanics.

61-3.caption:
Fine-tuning isn't magic — it's the same backprop that trained the model, just pointed at your data with a gentle hand. 🔧

Under the hood, you feed input/output pairs, compute a loss on the tokens the model should have produced, and nudge the weights to reduce that loss. The whole art is in doing this gently enough that the model keeps everything it learned during pretraining while picking up your task.

This post is the mechanics: the training loop, the loss, learning rates, catastrophic forgetting, and what's actually different about parameter-efficient methods like LoRA.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• the fine-tuning loop, step by step
• how the loss is computed on labels
• why the learning rate is tiny
• catastrophic forgetting, explained
• how LoRA freezes the base model

Understand the loop and hyperparameters stop being guesswork. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Same engine, new fuel
slides.body: Fine-tuning reuses the exact training machinery from pretraining: forward pass, loss, backward pass, weight update. The only changes are the data (your curated pairs) and the dials (a much smaller learning rate and fewer steps).

slides.title: Where the loss comes from
slides.body: Each example is a prompt plus the desired completion. The model predicts a probability for each next token; cross-entropy loss measures how surprised it was by the correct token. You typically mask the prompt tokens so loss is computed only on the response you want it to learn.

slides.title: Loss on completion tokens only
slides.code:
# labels = input_ids, but prompt positions set to -100
# so cross-entropy ignores them
labels = input_ids.clone()
labels[:prompt_len] = -100        # mask the prompt

out = model(input_ids=input_ids, labels=labels)
loss = out.loss                   # avg over response tokens
loss.backward()

slides.title: Why the learning rate is tiny
slides.body: Pretraining used a large rate to learn from scratch. Fine-tuning uses a rate 100-1000x smaller (often 1e-5 to 2e-4) because you want to nudge, not overwrite. Too high and the model forgets its general abilities; too low and it never adapts.

slides.title: Catastrophic forgetting
slides.body: Push too hard on a narrow dataset and the model overwrites general skills to fit your examples — it gets great at your task and worse at everything else. Small learning rates, few epochs, and mixing in diverse data all fight this.

slides.title: The PEFT idea: freeze most of it
slides.body: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning freezes the original weights and trains a small set of new parameters instead. The base model can't drift, you store only the tiny add-on, and you can swap adapters per task. LoRA is the most popular variant.

slides.title: Wrap a model with LoRA
slides.code:
from peft import LoraConfig, get_peft_model

cfg = LoraConfig(r=8, lora_alpha=16,
                 target_modules=["q_proj", "v_proj"],
                 lora_dropout=0.05)
model = get_peft_model(base_model, cfg)
model.print_trainable_parameters()  # ~0.2% of params

slides.title: The dials that matter
bullets: learning rate: start ~2e-4 (LoRA)
epochs: 1-3, watch for overfit
LoRA rank r: 8-16 is plenty
batch size: as big as VRAM allows

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 62.
slides.body: Next: a complete, runnable LoRA fine-tuning script.

61-4.caption:
Enough theory. Here's an end-to-end LoRA fine-tune you can actually run on a single GPU. 💻

This is the QLoRA recipe: load a base model in 4-bit, attach low-rank adapters, format your data into prompt/response pairs, train, and run inference. It's the workflow most people use in practice because it fits on consumer hardware and trains in hours, not days.

This post is the code: every stage from install to inference, with the lines that actually matter called out.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• install + 4-bit model load
• formatting your dataset correctly
• the LoRA + training config
• running the training loop
• saving, loading, and inference

Copy it, point it at your data, and you have a tuned model tonight. Save it. 👇

slides.title: What we'll build
bullets: Load a base model in 4-bit (QLoRA)
Format an instruction dataset
Attach LoRA adapters
Train, save, and run inference

slides.title: 1. Install + 4-bit load
slides.code:
# pip install transformers peft bitsandbytes datasets trl
import torch
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer, BitsAndBytesConfig

bnb = BitsAndBytesConfig(load_in_4bit=True,
                         bnb_4bit_quant_type="nf4",
                         bnb_4bit_compute_dtype=torch.bfloat16)
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("meta-llama/Llama-3.2-1B", quantization_config=bnb, device_map="auto")
tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("meta-llama/Llama-3.2-1B")
tok.pad_token = tok.eos_token

slides.title: 2. Format the data
slides.code:
from datasets import load_dataset

def to_text(ex):
    return {"text": f"### Instruction:\n{ex['instruction']}\n\n"
                    f"### Response:\n{ex['output']}{tok.eos_token}"}

ds = load_dataset("json", data_files="train.jsonl", split="train")
ds = ds.map(to_text)
# each row -> one clean instruction/response string

slides.title: 3. LoRA + training config
slides.code:
from peft import LoraConfig
from trl import SFTConfig, SFTTrainer

lora = LoraConfig(r=16, lora_alpha=32, lora_dropout=0.05,
                  target_modules=["q_proj", "v_proj"],
                  task_type="CAUSAL_LM")

args = SFTConfig(output_dir="out", num_train_epochs=2,
                 per_device_train_batch_size=4,
                 gradient_accumulation_steps=4,
                 learning_rate=2e-4, bf16=True, logging_steps=10)

slides.title: 4. Train
slides.code:
trainer = SFTTrainer(model=model, train_dataset=ds,
                     peft_config=lora, args=args,
                     dataset_text_field="text")

trainer.train()                 # the actual fine-tuning
trainer.save_model("adapter")   # saves only the LoRA weights (~MBs)

slides.title: 5. Load adapter + infer
slides.code:
from peft import PeftModel

base = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("meta-llama/Llama-3.2-1B", device_map="auto")
model = PeftModel.from_pretrained(base, "adapter")

prompt = "### Instruction:\nSummarize LoRA in one line.\n\n### Response:\n"
ids = tok(prompt, return_tensors="pt").to(model.device)
print(tok.decode(model.generate(**ids, max_new_tokens=60)[0]))

slides.title: Lines that actually matter
bullets: nf4 + bf16: fits a model in ~half the VRAM
eos_token after each response: model learns to stop
mask/format consistently across all rows
save_model stores adapters, not the 1B base

slides.title: Easy ways to break it
slides.body: Forgetting the EOS token means the model never learns when to stop generating. Mismatched prompt formatting between train and inference quietly tanks quality. And training for too many epochs on a small set overfits fast — watch the loss curve.

slides.title: Optional: merge for deployment
slides.code:
# fold the adapter into the base for a standalone model
merged = model.merge_and_unload()
merged.save_pretrained("my-llm-merged")
# now serve like any normal HF model, no PEFT needed

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 62.
slides.body: Next: the common mistakes that quietly wreck a fine-tune.

61-5.caption:
Most failed fine-tunes don't crash — they train cleanly, the loss goes down, and the model is somehow worse. ⚠️

The failures are quiet: a poisoned dataset, a format mismatch, an overfit model that memorized 200 examples, or fine-tuning a problem that never needed it. Each one looks fine in the logs and only shows up when real users hit it.

This post is the mistakes: the specific traps that waste GPU hours and ship regressions, and the habit that catches each one.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• fine-tuning when you should've prompted
• tiny, dirty, or imbalanced datasets
• overfitting and catastrophic forgetting
• train/inference format mismatch
• shipping with no real eval

Learn the failure modes once so you don't pay for them with GPU hours. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 1. Tuning what a prompt would fix
slides.body: The most expensive mistake happens before training starts: reaching for fine-tuning when a better prompt, a few examples, or RAG would have solved it. Always beat your prompted baseline first — if you can't measure it, you can't know fine-tuning helped.

slides.title: 2. Too little data, too dirty
slides.body: Fifty inconsistent examples teach the model your inconsistency. Quality and consistency beat raw count: clean formatting, correct labels, and one canonical style. A small, pristine set usually outperforms a large, noisy one.

slides.title: 3. Overfitting
slides.body: Train too many epochs on too few examples and the model memorizes them instead of generalizing. Training loss keeps dropping while real performance falls. Hold out a validation set and stop when validation loss turns up.

slides.title: Catch overfit with a held-out set
slides.code:
split = ds.train_test_split(test_size=0.1)
args = SFTConfig(eval_strategy="steps", eval_steps=50,
                 load_best_model_at_end=True,
                 metric_for_best_model="eval_loss")
# if eval_loss rises while train_loss falls -> overfitting

slides.title: 4. Catastrophic forgetting
slides.body: A learning rate that's too high or a dataset that's too narrow makes the model great at your task and worse at everything else. Keep the rate small, epochs few, and consider mixing in a slice of general data to preserve broad ability.

slides.title: 5. Train/inference format drift
slides.body: If you train on "### Instruction:\n..." but prompt differently at inference, the model sees an out-of-distribution input and quality collapses. The serving prompt template must match the training format character-for-character.

slides.title: 6. No eval, just vibes
slides.body: Shipping because the loss looked good is how regressions reach users. Build a fixed eval set with the cases you care about and score every candidate against it. A model that improves your metric is the only kind worth deploying.

slides.title: The pre-flight checklist
bullets: Beat the prompted baseline first
Clean, consistent, labeled data
Hold out a validation split
Match prompt template at serving
Score on a fixed eval set

slides.title: A minimal eval gate
Save this. Follow for Day 62.
slides.body: You can now fine-tune an LLM end to end — and know when not to.

62-1.caption:
You don't need to retrain 7 billion weights to teach a model a new trick. You need to train about 0.1% of them. 🪶

LoRA is the insight that the weight *change* from fine-tuning is low-rank — it can be captured by two skinny matrices instead of one giant one. So you freeze the original model entirely and train only a tiny pair of adapters on the side. QLoRA goes further: it also stores the frozen base in 4-bit, so the whole thing fits on one consumer GPU.

This post is the concept: what LoRA actually is, why "low-rank" matters, and how QLoRA stacks quantization on top.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• what LoRA changes vs full fine-tuning
• why a weight update can be low-rank
• the A and B matrices, plainly
• what the Q in QLoRA adds
• adapters as swappable plug-ins

Get the concept and the hyperparameters stop being magic. Save it. 👇

slides.title: The one-line definition
slides.body: LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) freezes a pretrained model and trains two small matrices that represent the *change* to a weight. QLoRA adds 4-bit quantization of the frozen base, so you fine-tune a large model on a single consumer GPU.

slides.title: The key insight: ΔW is low-rank
slides.body: Full fine-tuning learns a big update matrix ΔW the same shape as W. LoRA's bet is that this update doesn't need full rank — it lives in a small subspace. So you approximate ΔW ≈ B·A, where A and B are skinny. You train A and B; W never moves.

slides.title: Meet A and B
slides.body: A projects the input down to a tiny dimension r (the rank), and B projects it back up to full size. Their product B·A is the same shape as W but built from far fewer numbers. A starts random, B starts at zero — so at step 0 the adapter adds nothing and training begins exactly at the base model.

slides.title: The math, in 4 lines
slides.code:
# W: [d, d] frozen.  r << d.
# A: [r, d]  B: [d, r]  -> only 2*d*r params
h = W @ x + (B @ (A @ x)) * (alpha / r)
# train A, B; leave W alone
# adapter params: 2*d*r vs d*d for full FT

slides.title: What the Q in QLoRA adds
slides.body: QLoRA keeps the frozen base model in 4-bit (NF4) instead of 16-bit, cutting its memory roughly 4x. The LoRA adapters stay in higher precision and are what you train. Gradients flow through the de-quantized base, so accuracy stays close to full fine-tuning while memory drops dramatically.

slides.title: Adapters are plug-ins
bullets: One frozen base, many adapters
Each adapter is just a few MB
Swap tasks without reloading the base
Version and ship adapters like config

slides.title: What LoRA is NOT
slides.body: LoRA isn't a way to add new facts any more than full fine-tuning is — it changes behavior, not knowledge. It also isn't lossless: very large behavior shifts can need a higher rank or full FT. And QLoRA's 4-bit base is for training memory, not a free quality upgrade.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 63.
slides.body: Next: why LoRA & QLoRA matter — the economics that made fine-tuning accessible.

62-2.caption:
Before LoRA, fine-tuning a serious model meant a cluster and a budget. After QLoRA, it means a laptop GPU and an afternoon. ⚡

That shift isn't a minor optimization — it changed who gets to fine-tune at all. Storing a full model copy per task, holding optimizer states for billions of weights, paying to serve a separate giant model per use case: LoRA and QLoRA collapse every one of those costs.

This post is the why: the memory math, the storage and serving wins, and why "one base, many adapters" is an architecture, not just a trick.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• the memory wall full FT hits
• why optimizer state is the real cost
• MB adapters vs GB checkpoints
• multi-tenant serving on one base
• when QLoRA's trade-offs are worth it

Understand the economics and you'll know exactly when to reach for it. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Full fine-tuning hits a memory wall
slides.body: Training a model means holding the weights, their gradients, and the optimizer's momentum and variance states — for Adam that's roughly 4x the model size in memory, before activations. For a 7B model that's tens of GB just for the optimizer. LoRA trains a fraction of a percent of params, so that cost nearly vanishes.

slides.title: The optimizer state is the hidden cost
slides.body: People budget for the model weights and forget the optimizer. Adam keeps two extra full-size tensors per trainable parameter. Freeze the base with LoRA and those states only exist for the tiny adapter — turning the dominant memory cost into a rounding error.

slides.title: Storage: MB, not GB
slides.body: A full fine-tune produces a complete model copy — gigabytes per task. A LoRA adapter is just the A and B matrices: typically single-digit to low tens of megabytes. You can keep hundreds of task adapters in the space of one base model.

slides.title: One base, many tenants
slides.body: Because adapters are tiny and the base is shared, a single GPU holding one base model can serve many fine-tuned variants by swapping or batching adapters. That's the foundation of multi-tenant LLM serving — per-customer models without per-customer hardware.

slides.title: The democratization, quantified
slides.code:
model_params = 7_000_000_000
bytes_per_param_fp16 = 2
# Full FT (Adam): weights + grads + 2 optimizer states ~= 4x
full_ft_gb = model_params * bytes_per_param_fp16 * 4 / 1e9   # ~56 GB
# QLoRA: 4-bit base + tiny trainable adapter
qlora_gb  = model_params * 0.5 / 1e9 + 0.2                   # ~3.7 GB
print(full_ft_gb, qlora_gb)   # cluster vs single consumer GPU

slides.title: Why this changed the field
bullets: Indie devs can fine-tune real models
Experiments cost hours, not weeks
Each adapter is a cheap, ownable asset
Per-customer tuning becomes viable

slides.title: The trade-offs are real
slides.body: QLoRA's 4-bit base trains slower per step than 16-bit and can lose a little quality on the hardest tasks. LoRA may underfit very large behavior changes at low rank. These are usually worth it — but they're choices, not free wins. Match rank and precision to the job.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 63.
slides.body: Next: how LoRA & QLoRA actually work — rank, scaling, and 4-bit NF4 mechanics.

62-3.caption:
LoRA looks like magic until you see the matrix shapes. Then it's just clever bookkeeping about where the trainable parameters live. 🔬

Under the hood: pick the layers to adapt, attach a down-projection A and an up-projection B with a tiny shared dimension r, scale their output by alpha/r, and train only those. QLoRA adds a quantization layer underneath — NF4 weights that get de-quantized on the fly during the forward and backward pass.

This post is the mechanics: rank, alpha, target modules, the init trick, and exactly what NF4 and double quantization do.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• the forward pass, with shapes
• what rank r and alpha actually control
• why B starts at zero
• which modules to target
• NF4, double-quant, and paged optimizers

Know the dials and you'll tune in one pass, not ten. Save it. 👇

slides.title: The forward pass, exactly
slides.body: For a layer with frozen weight W and input x, LoRA computes h = W·x + (alpha/r)·B·(A·x). A is [r×d], B is [d×r], with r far smaller than d. Only A and B receive gradients; W is read-only. The scaling alpha/r keeps the adapter's effect stable as you change r.

slides.title: What rank r controls
slides.body: r is the bottleneck width of the adapter — how much the update can express. Small r (4-8) is cheap and works for tone or format tasks. Larger r (16-64) gives more capacity for bigger behavior shifts, at more memory and overfit risk. It's the main capacity dial.

slides.title: What alpha does
slides.body: alpha is a scaling factor; the adapter is multiplied by alpha/r. It decouples the learning-rate-like strength of the adapter from its rank, so you can change r without re-tuning everything. A common heuristic is alpha = 2·r, but it's worth treating as a tunable hyperparameter.

slides.title: Why B is initialized to zero
slides.code:
# A ~ normal(0, sigma), B = zeros
# at step 0:  B @ (A @ x) = 0
# so  h = W@x + 0  -> identical to the base model
# training starts from the pretrained model, not noise,
# then B moves away from zero as gradients flow.
assert (B == 0).all()   # before the first step

slides.title: Which modules to target
bullets: Attention q_proj, v_proj: the classic default
Add k_proj, o_proj for more capacity
MLP up/down/gate: bigger shifts, more params
More targets = more expressive, more memory

slides.title: NF4 and double quantization
slides.body: NF4 (4-bit NormalFloat) is a data type tuned for the bell-curve distribution of neural weights, so it loses less than plain int4. Double quantization then quantizes the quantization constants themselves, saving a bit more. The base is de-quantized to bf16 just-in-time for each matmul.

slides.title: The config that wires it all
slides.code:
from transformers import BitsAndBytesConfig
from peft import LoraConfig

bnb = BitsAndBytesConfig(
    load_in_4bit=True, bnb_4bit_quant_type="nf4",
    bnb_4bit_use_double_quant=True,        # quantize the constants too
    bnb_4bit_compute_dtype="bfloat16")     # de-quant for matmul

lora = LoraConfig(r=16, lora_alpha=32,     # alpha = 2*r heuristic
    target_modules=["q_proj", "v_proj"], lora_dropout=0.05)

slides.title: Paged optimizers stop OOM spikes
slides.body: Memory usage isn't flat — gradient spikes can momentarily blow past your VRAM. QLoRA uses paged optimizers that page optimizer state between GPU and CPU during these spikes, so a long run survives the occasional peak instead of crashing at hour three.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 63.
slides.body: Next: a full, runnable QLoRA fine-tuning script, line by line.

62-4.caption:
Here's a complete QLoRA fine-tune you can run on a single 16GB GPU tonight. Copy, point at your data, train. 💻

This is the standard recipe end to end: load a base model in 4-bit NF4, attach LoRA adapters to the attention layers, format an instruction dataset, train with TRL's SFTTrainer, then load the adapter and generate. Every line that actually matters is called out.

This post is the code: install, quantized load, adapters, training, inference, and merging for deployment.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• 4-bit NF4 + double-quant load
• prepare_model_for_kbit_training
• the LoRA config and trainable %
• train and save the adapter only
• load adapter + run inference

This is the exact workflow most teams ship with. Save it. 👇

slides.title: What we'll build
bullets: Load a 7B model in 4-bit NF4
Prep it for k-bit training
Attach LoRA adapters
Train, save adapter, infer

slides.title: 1. Install + 4-bit NF4 load
slides.code:
# pip install transformers peft bitsandbytes datasets trl accelerate
import torch
from transformers import (AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer,
                          BitsAndBytesConfig)

bnb = BitsAndBytesConfig(load_in_4bit=True,
    bnb_4bit_quant_type="nf4", bnb_4bit_use_double_quant=True,
    bnb_4bit_compute_dtype=torch.bfloat16)
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    "mistralai/Mistral-7B-v0.1", quantization_config=bnb, device_map="auto")
tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("mistralai/Mistral-7B-v0.1")
tok.pad_token = tok.eos_token

slides.title: 2. Prep for k-bit + attach LoRA
slides.code:
from peft import LoraConfig, get_peft_model, prepare_model_for_kbit_training

model = prepare_model_for_kbit_training(model)   # enables grad checkpointing,
                                                 # casts norms/embeds for stability
lora = LoraConfig(r=16, lora_alpha=32, lora_dropout=0.05,
    target_modules=["q_proj", "k_proj", "v_proj", "o_proj"],
    bias="none", task_type="CAUSAL_LM")
model = get_peft_model(model, lora)
model.print_trainable_parameters()   # trainable: ~0.3% of all params

slides.title: 3. Format the dataset
slides.code:
from datasets import load_dataset

def to_text(ex):
    return {"text": f"### Instruction:\n{ex['instruction']}\n\n"
                    f"### Response:\n{ex['output']}{tok.eos_token}"}

ds = load_dataset("json", data_files="train.jsonl", split="train")
ds = ds.map(to_text)
# one consistent template per row; EOS teaches the model to stop

slides.title: 4. Train + save the adapter
slides.code:
from trl import SFTConfig, SFTTrainer

args = SFTConfig(output_dir="out", num_train_epochs=2,
    per_device_train_batch_size=2, gradient_accumulation_steps=8,
    learning_rate=2e-4, bf16=True,
    optim="paged_adamw_8bit", logging_steps=10)   # paged optimizer

trainer = SFTTrainer(model=model, train_dataset=ds, args=args,
                     dataset_text_field="text")
trainer.train()
trainer.save_model("adapter")   # saves ONLY LoRA weights (~tens of MB)

slides.title: 5. Load adapter + infer
slides.code:
from peft import PeftModel

base = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    "mistralai/Mistral-7B-v0.1", quantization_config=bnb, device_map="auto")
model = PeftModel.from_pretrained(base, "adapter")

prompt = "### Instruction:\nExplain LoRA in one sentence.\n\n### Response:\n"
ids = tok(prompt, return_tensors="pt").to(model.device)
out = model.generate(**ids, max_new_tokens=80)
print(tok.decode(out[0], skip_special_tokens=True))

slides.title: Lines that actually matter
bullets: nf4 + double_quant: fits 7B in ~5GB
prepare_model_for_kbit_training: stability
paged_adamw_8bit: survives memory spikes
save_model stores adapters, not the 7B base

slides.title: Optional: merge for deployment
slides.code:
# To merge, reload the base in fp16 (NOT 4-bit) then fold in:
base = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    "mistralai/Mistral-7B-v0.1", torch_dtype=torch.float16, device_map="auto")
model = PeftModel.from_pretrained(base, "adapter")
merged = model.merge_and_unload()        # adapter folded into weights
merged.save_pretrained("mistral-tuned")  # standalone, no PEFT at serve time

slides.title: The 4-bit merge trap
slides.body: You cannot cleanly merge an adapter back into a 4-bit base — the quantization makes the fold lossy. To deploy a merged model, reload the base in fp16/bf16 first, then merge_and_unload. Mixing this up is the most common QLoRA deployment bug.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 63.
slides.body: Next: the common LoRA & QLoRA mistakes that quietly wreck results.

62-5.caption:
Most broken LoRA runs train without a single error. The loss drops, the script finishes — and the model is mediocre. ⚠️

The traps are quiet: rank set wrong for the task, alpha and rank fighting each other, adapters on the wrong layers, a 4-bit merge that silently corrupts weights, or shipping QLoRA where latency actually mattered. Each one looks fine in the logs.

This post is the mistakes: the specific LoRA and QLoRA traps that waste GPU hours, and the habit that catches each one.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• rank too low (or pointlessly high)
• forgetting prepare_model_for_kbit_training
• targeting too few modules
• the lossy 4-bit merge mistake
• 4-bit at inference when you wanted speed

Learn the failure modes once so you stop paying for them in GPU hours. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 1. Rank set wrong for the task
slides.body: Rank too low and a big behavior shift underfits — the adapter physically can't express the change. Rank pointlessly high wastes memory and overfits small datasets. Match r to ambition: 4-8 for tone/format, 16-32 for richer skills. Sweep it; don't guess once.

slides.title: 2. alpha and rank fighting
slides.body: Because the adapter scales by alpha/r, changing rank silently changes the effective strength unless you adjust alpha too. People bump r for capacity, leave alpha fixed, and quietly halve the adapter's influence. Track the alpha/r ratio, not the raw numbers.

slides.title: 3. Skipping kbit prep in QLoRA
slides.body: Forgetting prepare_model_for_kbit_training leaves layer norms and embeddings in a state that makes 4-bit training unstable — loss spikes, NaNs, or silent quality loss. It also enables gradient checkpointing. One line, and QLoRA runs are far more stable with it.

slides.title: The QLoRA stability checklist
slides.code:
model = prepare_model_for_kbit_training(model)   # don't skip
model.config.use_cache = False                   # required w/ grad ckpt
# during training: bf16 compute dtype, paged optimizer
# if you see NaNs: lower lr, check this line ran first
assert model.is_gradient_checkpointing

slides.title: 4. Targeting too few modules
slides.body: Adapting only q_proj and v_proj is a fine default, but for harder tasks it can bottleneck quality. Adding k_proj, o_proj, and the MLP projections gives the adapter more places to act. If quality plateaus and you have memory, broaden the targets before cranking rank.

slides.title: 5. The lossy 4-bit merge
slides.body: merge_and_unload on a 4-bit-loaded base corrupts weights — the fold can't be done cleanly through quantization. The fix: reload the base in fp16/bf16, attach the adapter, then merge. This is the single most common QLoRA deployment bug, and it produces a model that's quietly worse.

yes.leaf: Reload in fp16 FIRST, then merge

no.leaf: Keep adapter separate, load with PEFT

slides.title: 6. 4-bit at inference for speed
slides.body: QLoRA's 4-bit base saves training memory, but 4-bit inference is often slower per token because of de-quant overhead. If you fine-tuned with QLoRA but need fast serving, merge into fp16 and serve that — or use a proper inference quantization (GPTQ/AWQ) chosen for speed.

slides.title: A minimal sanity gate
slides.code:
# after training, before shipping:
baseline = score(base_model, eval_cases)     # prompted baseline
tuned    = score(merged_model, eval_cases)   # your LoRA result
assert tuned > baseline, f"no gain: {tuned:.2f} vs {baseline:.2f}"
# also eyeball 5 outputs: did it learn to STOP (EOS)?

slides.title: The pre-flight checklist
bullets: Sweep rank; track alpha/r ratio
Run prepare_model_for_kbit_training
Broaden target modules before maxing rank
Reload in fp16 before merging
Beat the prompted baseline on a fixed eval

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 63.
slides.body: You can now run LoRA & QLoRA end to end — and avoid the silent failures.

63-1.caption:
A 7B model in full precision needs ~28GB just to load. Quantize it to 4-bit and it fits on a laptop GPU. 🗜️

Quantization means storing a model's weights (and sometimes activations) in fewer bits than the 32 or 16 they were trained in. Instead of a high-precision float per weight, you keep an 8-bit or 4-bit integer plus a small scale factor that maps it back to the real range.

This post is the concept: what quantization actually is, what "4-bit" and "8-bit" mean, and why squeezing the numbers barely hurts a well-built model.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• what quantization changes (and doesn't)
• what FP32, FP16, INT8, INT4 really mean
• the scale + zero-point trick
• why models tolerate the squeeze
• where quantization fits vs other tricks

Get the concept right and "4-bit" stops being magic. Save it. 👇

slides.title: The one-line definition
slides.body: Quantization stores a model's numbers in fewer bits than they were trained in — say INT8 or INT4 instead of FP32/FP16. You trade a bit of numerical precision for a model that is 2-8x smaller and faster, with surprisingly little drop in quality.

slides.title: Why anyone cares
slides.body: Bits are memory, and memory is the bottleneck for big models. Fewer bits per weight means the model fits in less VRAM, loads faster, and moves less data per token — which on memory-bound LLM inference directly means more speed. Same weights, smaller container.

slides.title: The core trick: scale + zero-point
slides.body: A quantized integer isn't the real value — it's an index. You store one small float scale (and sometimes a zero-point) per group of weights. Real ≈ scale × (int − zero_point). A few floats describe the range; the bulk of the data shrinks to tiny integers.

slides.title: Why models survive it
slides.body: Neural nets are massively over-parameterized and trained to be robust to noise. Rounding each weight slightly is just one more small perturbation the network easily absorbs. The signal lives in the overall pattern of weights, not in the 7th decimal place of any single one.

slides.title: Quantize one tensor by hand
slides.code:
import torch

w = torch.randn(4, 4)            # FP32 weights
qmax = 127                       # INT8 signed range
scale = w.abs().max() / qmax     # one float per tensor
q = torch.round(w / scale).clamp(-127, 127).to(torch.int8)

w_hat = q.to(torch.float32) * scale   # dequantize
print((w - w_hat).abs().mean())       # tiny error

slides.title: What gets quantized
bullets: Weights — almost always, biggest win
Activations — harder, more error-prone
KV cache — big for long contexts
Keep sensitive layers in higher precision

slides.title: Where it sits in the pipeline
Save this. Follow for Day 64.
slides.body: Next: why quantization matters — the cost, hardware, and access story.

63-2.caption:
The reason you can run a capable model on a single consumer GPU at all? Quantization is doing the heavy lifting. 💸

Full-precision models are gated behind expensive, high-VRAM hardware. Quantization is the lever that drops a 70B model from data-center-only to something that runs on a workstation — and a 7B model from a server to a laptop. It's the difference between renting GPUs and owning your stack.

This post is the why: the memory math, the speed gains, the cost savings, and how 4-bit quantization quietly democratized local LLMs.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• the VRAM math, in real numbers
• why memory bandwidth caps token speed
• the cost-per-token argument
• how it put LLMs on laptops
• what you actually trade away

Memory is the budget. Quantization is how you spend less of it. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Memory is the real bottleneck
slides.body: For LLM inference, you're rarely compute-bound — you're memory-bound. Every token generated re-reads the entire weight set from VRAM. Halve the bits and you halve the bytes moved per token, which on most hardware nearly halves latency. Smaller weights aren't just storage; they're speed.

slides.title: The back-of-envelope formula
slides.body: Weight memory ≈ params × bytes-per-param. A 7B model: 7e9 × 4 = 28GB in FP32, but 7e9 × 0.5 = 3.5GB in INT4. That single factor of 8 is the difference between a rented A100 and a gaming laptop. Add some overhead for activations and KV cache.

slides.title: Compute the memory yourself
slides.code:
params = 7e9
for bits, name in [(32,'FP32'), (16,'FP16'), (8,'INT8'), (4,'INT4')]:
    gb = params * bits / 8 / 1e9
    print(f'{name:5} -> {gb:5.1f} GB')
# FP32 -> 28.0 GB   FP16 -> 14.0 GB
# INT8 ->  7.0 GB   INT4 ->  3.5 GB

slides.title: Bigger model on the same card
slides.body: Quantization isn't only about shrinking a fixed model. At a fixed VRAM budget it lets you run a smarter model: a 4-bit 13B often beats an FP16 7B that uses the same memory. You spend your bits on capability rather than precision.

slides.title: Where the savings show up
bullets: Lower VRAM → cheaper GPUs
Less data moved → faster tokens
Smaller footprint → edge + mobile
Local inference → privacy + no API bill

slides.title: It isn't free
slides.body: You trade some accuracy for all this, and the loss isn't uniform — INT8 is usually near-invisible, while aggressive INT4 can degrade reasoning or rare-token handling. On already-small models the hit is more noticeable. Always measure quality on your task, not just the size on disk.

slides.title: When it's clearly worth it
bullets: You're VRAM-constrained
You serve high request volume
You need on-device / offline
Latency or hosting cost is the pain

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 64.
slides.body: Next: how quantization works — scales, zero-points, and the math under the hood.

63-3.caption:
"4-bit" sounds lossy and scary. The mechanics are simpler than you think — it's mostly rounding with a good ruler. 🔧

Under the hood, quantization picks a range, divides it into a fixed number of integer buckets, and snaps each weight to the nearest one. The whole craft is in choosing the range well, grouping weights so one bad outlier doesn't wreck a whole tensor, and deciding what to do before vs after training.

This post is the mechanics: affine vs symmetric mapping, per-tensor vs per-group scales, PTQ vs QAT, and why outliers are the real enemy.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• the quantize / dequantize equations
• symmetric vs affine (zero-point) mapping
• per-tensor vs per-channel vs per-group
• the outlier problem and how it's handled
• PTQ vs quantization-aware training

Learn the knobs and 4-bit stops being a black box. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Quantize, then dequantize
slides.body: Quantize: q = round(x / scale) + zero_point, clamped to the integer range. Dequantize: x̂ = scale × (q − zero_point). The model computes on the integers; the scale and zero-point translate back to real values. The gap x − x̂ is the quantization error.

slides.title: Granularity: how many scales
slides.body: One scale per whole tensor is cheap but fragile — a single huge weight stretches the range and crushes precision for everyone else. Per-channel or per-group (e.g. one scale per 64 or 128 weights) gives each block its own ruler, so outliers stay contained. More scales, less error, slightly more overhead.

slides.title: Outliers are the enemy
slides.body: A handful of activation values in LLMs can be 10-100x larger than the rest. Quantize naively and they dominate the scale, wrecking precision for the common values. Methods like LLM.int8(), GPTQ, and AWQ exist specifically to isolate or protect these outliers.

slides.title: Per-group symmetric quantization
slides.code:
import torch

def quantize_per_group(w, group=128, bits=4):
    qmax = 2 ** (bits - 1) - 1            # 7 for INT4
    w = w.reshape(-1, group)              # rows of 128
    scale = w.abs().amax(dim=1, keepdim=True) / qmax
    q = torch.round(w / scale).clamp(-qmax, qmax)
    return q.to(torch.int8), scale        # store both

q, s = quantize_per_group(torch.randn(256, 128))

slides.title: How a real quantizer runs
bullets: Pick bits, granularity, sym/affine
Run calibration data to see ranges
Compute scales (+ zero-points)
Round weights, pack into ints
Save quantized weights + metadata

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 64.
slides.body: Next: a hands-on code example — load and run a 4-bit model end to end.

63-4.caption:
Reading about 4-bit is one thing. Let's actually load a model in 4-bit and watch the VRAM drop. 💻

The modern stack makes this almost embarrassingly easy: bitsandbytes for on-the-fly NF4 loading, or a pre-quantized GPTQ/AWQ checkpoint you just download. A few config lines and a 7B model that needed 14GB now needs under 4GB.

This post is code-heavy: loading in 8-bit and 4-bit with Transformers, the QLoRA-style NF4 config, running pre-quantized GPTQ weights, and measuring the memory you actually saved.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• 8-bit load with one flag
• 4-bit NF4 config (QLoRA-style)
• loading a pre-quantized GPTQ model
• measuring real VRAM usage
• generating text from the 4-bit model

Copy, run, watch the gigabytes disappear. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 0. Install the stack
slides.code:
# pip install transformers accelerate bitsandbytes
# (Linux/WSL + an NVIDIA GPU for bitsandbytes 4-bit)
import torch
from transformers import (AutoModelForCausalLM,
                          AutoTokenizer,
                          BitsAndBytesConfig)

slides.title: 1. Load in 8-bit (one flag)
slides.code:
model_id = "mistralai/Mistral-7B-v0.1"
tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_id)

model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    model_id,
    load_in_8bit=True,        # bitsandbytes INT8
    device_map="auto",
)
# ~14 GB (FP16) -> ~7 GB on the GPU

slides.title: 2. Load in 4-bit (NF4, QLoRA-style)
slides.code:
bnb = BitsAndBytesConfig(
    load_in_4bit=True,
    bnb_4bit_quant_type="nf4",          # normal-float 4
    bnb_4bit_compute_dtype=torch.bfloat16,
    bnb_4bit_use_double_quant=True,     # quantize the scales too
)
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    model_id, quantization_config=bnb, device_map="auto")
# now ~3.5-4 GB on the GPU

slides.title: What NF4 + double-quant do
slides.body: NF4 is a 4-bit datatype whose levels match the bell-curve distribution of neural-net weights, so it wastes fewer codes than plain INT4. Double quantization then quantizes the scale factors themselves, shaving another ~0.4 bits per weight. Together they're the core of QLoRA.

slides.title: 3. Generate from the 4-bit model
slides.code:
prompt = "Explain quantization in one sentence:"
ids = tok(prompt, return_tensors="pt").to(model.device)

out = model.generate(**ids, max_new_tokens=60,
                     do_sample=False)
print(tok.decode(out[0], skip_special_tokens=True))
# runs fine in 4-bit; quality stays close to FP16

slides.title: 4. Measure the VRAM you saved
slides.code:
gb = torch.cuda.max_memory_allocated() / 1e9
print(f"peak GPU memory: {gb:.2f} GB")

# FP16 baseline  ~14.0 GB
# 8-bit          ~7.2 GB
# 4-bit (NF4)    ~4.1 GB
# proof, not vibes

slides.title: 5. Or load a pre-quantized GPTQ model
slides.code:
# pip install auto-gptq optimum
# No bitsandbytes needed; weights are already 4-bit on disk
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    "TheBloke/Mistral-7B-v0.1-GPTQ",
    device_map="auto",          # GPTQ config auto-detected
)
# download is ~4 GB instead of ~14 GB

slides.title: What the loader does for you
Practical notes
bullets: bitsandbytes 4-bit wants Linux + NVIDIA
GPTQ/AWQ files: no quantize step at load
On CPU/Mac, use llama.cpp GGUF instead
Pin versions — this stack moves fast

slides.title: Common gotcha
