slides.body: You cannot full-fine-tune a bitsandbytes 4-bit model directly — the base weights are frozen integers. To train, you attach LoRA adapters on top (that's exactly what QLoRA does). Trying to call .train() and update the quantized weights will fail or silently do nothing useful.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 64.
slides.body: Next: the common mistakes that quietly wreck a quantized model.

63-5.caption:
Most "quantization ruined my model" stories aren't about quantization — they're about doing it wrong. 🧨

The technique is robust, but the failure modes are sneaky: judging quality by file size, using the wrong calibration data, quantizing the layers you should have left alone, or comparing apples to oranges across formats. Each one looks fine until your outputs quietly get worse.

This post is the traps: the mistakes that turn a near-lossless 4-bit model into a subtly broken one, and how to avoid every one of them.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• trusting size instead of evals
• bad or missing calibration data
• over-quantizing small / sensitive models
• ignoring activation outliers
• mixing up INT4 schemes and formats

Quantize confidently — measure, don't assume. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 1. Judging by size, not quality
slides.body: "It's 4GB now and it loads" tells you nothing about whether it still reasons. Quantization damage shows up in subtle ways — worse math, weaker long-context recall, more hallucination — that disk size and a single happy-path prompt will never reveal.

slides.title: Fix: evaluate before/after
bullets: Run the SAME eval set on FP16 and quantized
Track task accuracy, not just perplexity
Test edge cases: math, long context, code
Set a max acceptable drop, then decide

slides.title: 2. Bad calibration data
slides.body: PTQ methods like GPTQ and AWQ measure activation ranges on a small calibration set. If that data doesn't resemble your real traffic — wrong language, wrong domain, too few samples — the scales are tuned for the wrong distribution and accuracy suffers on what you actually run.

slides.title: Fix: calibrate on representative text
slides.code:
# Use samples that look like YOUR real inputs
calib = load_dataset("your-domain-corpus", split="train")
calib = [x["text"] for x in calib.select(range(256))]

quantizer.quantize(model, calibration_data=calib)
# generic web text != your medical/legal/code traffic

slides.title: 3. Over-quantizing the wrong things
slides.body: Not every model or layer survives 4-bit. Small models (1-3B) have less redundancy and degrade faster. Embeddings, the final LM head, and layernorms are sensitive. Blindly forcing INT4 everywhere is how you turn a coherent model into a confused one.

slides.title: Fix: be selective
bullets: Prefer INT8 for small (<3B) models
Keep head / embeddings higher precision
Use per-group, not per-tensor, at 4-bit
Mixed precision beats uniform INT4

slides.title: 4. Ignoring activation outliers
slides.body: Weights quantize cleanly; activations have extreme outliers that naive INT8 can't handle. Quantizing activations the same way you quantize weights is a classic blunder. This is exactly why LLM.int8() keeps outlier dimensions in FP16 and why weight-only schemes are popular.

slides.title: 5. Comparing apples to oranges
slides.body: "4-bit" is not one thing. GPTQ, AWQ, NF4, and GGUF Q4_K_M differ in grouping, datatype, and what stays in high precision. Comparing two "4-bit" models — or assuming a format that ran on your GPU will run elsewhere — leads to bad conclusions and broken deployments.

slides.title: Fix: pin the exact scheme
slides.code:
# Record the full recipe, not just "4-bit"
meta = {
    "method": "AWQ",
    "bits": 4,
    "group_size": 128,
    "sym": True,
    "keep_fp16": ["lm_head", "embed_tokens"],
}
# reproducibility = same method + group + exceptions

slides.title: The pre-flight checklist
bullets: Measured quality on a real eval set
Calibrated on representative data
Chose bits to match model size
Logged method + group size + exceptions

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 64.
slides.body: Next: a new topic in the NLP & LLMs track — keep building the stack.

64-1.caption:
A base language model can write fluently and still be useless — it'll happily continue a toxic prompt or ignore what you actually asked. 🎯

RLHF, DPO, and PPO are the family of techniques that close the gap between "predicts the next token well" and "behaves like a helpful assistant." They take human preferences — which of two answers is better — and turn them into a training signal the model can optimize against.

This post is the concept: what alignment-from-preferences actually means, and how PPO and DPO are two routes to the same destination.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• why next-token loss isn't enough
• what "preference" data really is
• RLHF as a 3-stage pipeline
• PPO vs DPO in one breath
• where the reward model fits

Get the map before the math. Save it. 👇

slides.title: The one-line definition
slides.body: RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback) tunes a language model to match human preferences instead of just imitating text. PPO is the RL algorithm that originally did the tuning. DPO (Direct Preference Optimization) reaches the same goal with a simpler loss and no RL loop.

slides.title: Why next-token loss isn't enough
slides.body: Pretraining teaches a model to imitate the internet — including the unhelpful, unsafe, and rambling parts. It learns *what text looks like*, not *what answer you wanted*. There's no signal in plain language modeling for "this response is better than that one." Preference tuning adds exactly that missing signal.

slides.title: What 'preference data' is
slides.body: The raw material is pairs: a prompt, plus two candidate answers, with a human label saying which one is better (chosen vs rejected). You don't need a perfect gold answer — just a comparison. Relative judgments are far easier and more reliable for humans to give than absolute scores.

slides.title: What a preference example looks like
slides.code:
example = {
  "prompt":   "How do I center a div?",
  "chosen":   "Use flexbox: display:flex; justify-content:center.",  # helpful
  "rejected": "Why would you want to do that?"                       # unhelpful
}
# no gold score needed — just 'chosen is better than rejected'
# thousands of these pairs ARE the training signal

slides.title: Stage 1: supervised fine-tuning
slides.body: Before any preferences, you teach the base model to follow instructions at all by fine-tuning on curated prompt/answer demonstrations. This SFT model is the starting point for everything that follows — both PPO and DPO begin from it. Without it, preference tuning has nothing reasonable to refine.

slides.title: Stage 2: the reward model
slides.body: Classic RLHF trains a separate reward model on the preference pairs. It takes a prompt and a response and outputs a single scalar score predicting how much a human would like it. This model becomes the stand-in judge that the RL stage optimizes against, so you don't need a human in the loop for every step.

slides.title: PPO vs DPO, side by side
The KL leash
slides.body: Both methods pull the model toward preferred answers while a KL-divergence penalty keeps it from drifting too far from the original SFT model. That leash prevents the model from gaming the reward with weird, degenerate text. Alignment is always a balance: chase reward, but stay close to a sane base.

slides.title: Why this matters in one line each
bullets: Turns 'sounds fluent' into 'is helpful'
Encodes human values as a loss
Powers ChatGPT, Claude, Llama-Chat
DPO made alignment cheap to run

slides.title: What RLHF is NOT
slides.body: It doesn't add new knowledge — it reshapes behavior and tone from what's already there. It also can't fix bad preference data: garbage comparisons produce a model that's confidently wrong in human-approved ways. And reward is a proxy for what you want, never the thing itself.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 65.
slides.body: Next: why preference tuning matters — the difference between a clever model and a usable one.

64-2.caption:
GPT-3 existed for two years before ChatGPT. Same family of models. The thing that changed everything wasn't scale — it was alignment. 💡

Preference tuning is what turned raw capability into a product people could actually talk to. It's the step that makes a model refuse the harmful request, follow the actual instruction, and stop rambling. And the shift from PPO to DPO is why that step went from a research-lab luxury to something any team can run.

This post is the why: what alignment buys you, the safety and helpfulness it encodes, and why DPO mattered economically.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• capability vs usability
• the three H's: helpful, honest, harmless
• why humans compare instead of grade
• reward hacking, and the KL leash
• why DPO lowered the barrier

Understand what's really being optimized. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Capability is not usability
slides.body: A pretrained model is a brilliant autocomplete with no manners. It can write code and poetry but won't reliably do what you ask, refuses nothing, and treats a harmful prompt like any other. Preference tuning is the layer that converts latent capability into reliable, controllable behavior.

slides.title: The three H's
slides.body: Alignment targets are usually framed as helpful, honest, and harmless. Helpful: actually answer the question. Honest: don't fabricate confidently. Harmless: refuse or redirect dangerous requests. Preference data is how you teach a model to trade these off the way humans want.

slides.title: Why humans compare, not grade
slides.body: Ask someone to score an answer 0-100 and you'll get noisy, inconsistent numbers. Ask which of two answers is better and you get a clean, reliable signal. Preference learning is built on this: relative comparisons are cheaper to collect and far more consistent across annotators than absolute ratings.

slides.title: The reward is only a proxy
slides.body: The reward model approximates human taste, but it's imperfect. Push hard enough and the policy finds inputs that score high yet are actually bad — overly long answers, sycophancy, hedging. This is reward hacking, and it's the central risk that makes the KL penalty non-negotiable.

slides.title: What the KL leash buys you
bullets: Keeps language fluent and on-distribution
Stops degenerate reward exploits
Trades a little reward for a lot of sanity
The beta knob sets how tight the leash is

slides.title: Why DPO changed the economics
slides.body: Classic PPO needs four models in memory, a sampling loop, and notoriously touchy hyperparameters. DPO collapses that into a single supervised-style loss over preference pairs — no reward model, no rollouts. Suddenly alignment fits on the hardware and skill budget of a normal team, not just a frontier lab.

slides.title: The capability gap, quantified
slides.code:
# illustrative: win-rate vs a strong reference, judged by humans
base_lm   = 0.18   # fluent but ignores intent
sft_model = 0.43   # follows instructions
dpo_model = 0.71   # aligned to preferences
# same weights underneath — the delta is alignment, not scale
print(dpo_model - base_lm)   # ~0.53 swing from preference tuning

slides.title: Alignment isn't a safety guarantee
slides.body: Preference tuning makes a model behave better on average; it doesn't make it provably safe. Jailbreaks, distribution shift, and biased preference data all leak through. Treat RLHF/DPO as raising the floor on behavior, not as a certificate — you still need evals, red-teaming, and guardrails.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 65.
slides.body: Next: how it actually works — the reward model, the PPO loop, and the DPO loss derived.

64-3.caption:
PPO and DPO look intimidating until you see they optimize the same objective — one with a noisy RL loop, the other with a clean closed-form loss. 🔬

Under the hood: train a reward model on preference pairs with a Bradley-Terry loss, then either (PPO) sample answers, score them, and nudge the policy with a clipped RL update, or (DPO) skip all that and push up the log-prob of chosen answers relative to rejected ones, with the reward folded directly into the loss.

This post is the mechanics: the reward loss, the four PPO models, the clip, the KL term, and the DPO derivation.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• the Bradley-Terry reward loss
• the four models PPO juggles
• PPO's clipped objective + KL
• how DPO removes the reward model
• the single DPO loss line

Know the objective and the knobs stop being magic. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Training the reward model
slides.body: Given a prompt with a chosen and rejected answer, the reward model r(x,y) must score the chosen one higher. The Bradley-Terry loss maximizes the log-probability that chosen beats rejected: -log σ(r_chosen − r_rejected). Minimize it across the dataset and r learns to mirror human preferences as a scalar.

slides.title: The reward model loss
The PPO loop
slides.body: Sample responses from the current policy, score them with the reward model, and compute how much better each was than the critic expected (the advantage). Then update the policy to make high-advantage actions more likely — but clip the update so no single step changes the policy too much.

slides.title: PPO's clipped objective
slides.code:
ratio = (logp_new - logp_old).exp()        # how much policy shifted
unclipped = ratio * advantage
clipped   = ratio.clamp(1 - eps, 1 + eps) * advantage
policy_loss = -torch.min(unclipped, clipped).mean()

# reward fed to PPO already includes a per-token KL penalty:
# reward_t = rm_score - beta * (logp_policy - logp_ref)

slides.title: Why the clip and KL exist
slides.body: The clip stops a single batch from yanking the policy into a region where the reward model is unreliable — RL is unstable without it. The KL penalty against the frozen reference keeps generations fluent and on-distribution. Together they're the guardrails that make reward-chasing safe.

slides.title: DPO's key insight
slides.body: DPO starts from the same KL-constrained objective PPO optimizes, then solves it analytically. The math shows the optimal policy's implicit reward is just the log-ratio of the tuned policy to the reference. Substitute that back in and the reward model disappears — you can optimize the policy directly on preference pairs.

right.title: DPO route
items: one gradient step on a loss

slides.title: The DPO loss, in full
slides.code:
# pi = policy, ref = frozen reference. logp = sum log-prob of the answer.
import torch.nn.functional as F

def dpo_loss(logp_pi_chosen, logp_pi_rej,
             logp_ref_chosen, logp_ref_rej, beta=0.1):
    chosen_logratio   = logp_pi_chosen - logp_ref_chosen
    rejected_logratio = logp_pi_rej   - logp_ref_rej
    # push chosen's log-ratio above rejected's, scaled by beta
    return -F.logsigmoid(beta * (chosen_logratio - rejected_logratio)).mean()

slides.title: What beta controls in DPO
slides.body: Beta is the KL strength, baked right into the loss. Small beta lets the policy move far from the reference for bigger behavior shifts; large beta keeps it close and conservative. It plays the same role as PPO's KL coefficient — the leash — but here it's a single, stable hyperparameter.

slides.title: The same objective, different paths
slides.body: It's tempting to think PPO and DPO are unrelated. They're not — DPO is a closed-form solution to PPO's exact objective under a Bradley-Terry preference model. PPO reaches it by trial-and-error sampling; DPO jumps straight to the answer. Same destination, very different cost.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 65.
slides.body: Next: a full, runnable DPO fine-tune — pairs in, aligned model out.

64-4.caption:
Here's a complete DPO fine-tune you can actually run: load an SFT model, feed it chosen/rejected pairs, train, and generate aligned answers — no reward model, no RL loop. 💻

This is the standard recipe with TRL's DPOTrainer: load a base + reference, format a preference dataset into prompt/chosen/rejected, set beta, train, and infer. I'll also show the bones of the PPO loop so you can see exactly why DPO is the simpler path most teams now take.

This post is the code: dataset, DPO config, training, inference, and a peek at PPO.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• format a preference dataset
• DPOTrainer config + beta
• train and save the policy
• generate from the aligned model
• the PPO loop, for contrast

This is the exact workflow teams ship alignment with. Save it. 👇

slides.title: What we'll build
bullets: Load an SFT model + reference
Format chosen/rejected pairs
Train with DPOTrainer (beta)
Generate from the aligned model

slides.title: 1. Install + load the SFT model
slides.code:
# pip install transformers trl peft datasets accelerate
import torch
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer

model_id = "HuggingFaceH4/mistral-7b-sft-beta"  # an already-SFT'd model
tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_id)
tok.pad_token = tok.eos_token
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    model_id, torch_dtype=torch.bfloat16, device_map="auto")
# DPO starts from an SFT model, NOT a raw base model

slides.title: 2. Format the preference dataset
3. Configure + run DPO
slides.code:
from trl import DPOConfig, DPOTrainer

args = DPOConfig(output_dir="dpo-out", num_train_epochs=1,
    per_device_train_batch_size=2, gradient_accumulation_steps=8,
    learning_rate=5e-6, bf16=True, logging_steps=10,
    beta=0.1)                       # KL strength: the leash

trainer = DPOTrainer(model=model,   # ref model auto-created if None
    args=args, train_dataset=ds, processing_class=tok)
trainer.train()
trainer.save_model("dpo-aligned")

slides.title: 4. Generate from the aligned model
slides.code:
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM

model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    "dpo-aligned", torch_dtype=torch.bfloat16, device_map="auto")

prompt = "Human: How do I pick a strong password?\n\nAssistant:"
ids = tok(prompt, return_tensors="pt").to(model.device)
out = model.generate(**ids, max_new_tokens=120, do_sample=False)
print(tok.decode(out[0], skip_special_tokens=True))

slides.title: Lines that actually matter
bullets: Start from an SFT model, not a base
Columns must be prompt/chosen/rejected
beta = the KL leash (start at 0.1)
Low LR (~5e-6): DPO is sensitive

slides.title: 5. For contrast: the PPO loop
slides.code:
from trl import PPOTrainer, PPOConfig

ppo = PPOTrainer(PPOConfig(batch_size=8), model, ref_model, tok)
for batch in dataloader:
    responses = ppo.generate(batch["input_ids"])      # 1. sample
    scores    = reward_model(batch["query"], responses)  # 2. score
    ppo.step(batch["input_ids"], responses, scores)   # 3. clipped update
# 3 explicit stages + a reward model — DPO's loss replaces all of it

slides.title: The reference-model trap
slides.body: DPO needs a frozen reference to compute log-ratios. If you forget it (or point it at the wrong checkpoint), the KL term is meaningless and the model drifts into gibberish that the loss still 'likes'. TRL creates a copy of your start model by default — make sure that copy is your real SFT model.

slides.title: A minimal sanity gate
slides.code:
pairs = [("Human: Write a haiku about the sea.\n\nAssistant:", None)]
for prompt, _ in pairs:
    ids = tok(prompt, return_tensors="pt").to(model.device)
    out = model.generate(**ids, max_new_tokens=60, do_sample=False)
    text = tok.decode(out[0], skip_special_tokens=True)
    assert text.strip() != prompt.strip(), "model produced nothing new"
    print(text)   # eyeball: helpful? on-topic? stops cleanly?

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 65.
slides.body: Next: the common RLHF, DPO & PPO mistakes that quietly wreck alignment.

64-5.caption:
Most broken alignment runs don't crash. The loss drops, the model sounds confident — and it's become a sycophant that agrees with everything and answers nothing. ⚠️

The traps are subtle: a noisy preference dataset, a beta that's too loose, the wrong reference model, reward hacking in PPO, or rewarding length and politeness instead of correctness. Each one passes the training logs and fails the user.

This post is the mistakes: the specific RLHF, DPO, and PPO traps that waste GPU hours and ship worse models — and the habit that catches each.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• garbage preference data in
• beta too low → reward hacking / drift
• starting from a base, not SFT
• rewarding length and sycophancy
• skipping the held-out eval

Learn the failure modes once so you stop paying for them. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 1. Noisy preference data
slides.body: Preference tuning can only be as good as its comparisons. Inconsistent annotators, ambiguous prompts, or labels that reward style over substance teach the model exactly the wrong thing — confidently. No loss function fixes contradictory labels; clean, agreed-upon pairs are the real lever.

slides.title: 2. Beta too low
slides.body: Beta (the KL strength) is the leash. Set it too loose and the policy drifts far from the reference, chasing reward into degenerate text — repetition, hedging, or in PPO, outright reward hacking. Too tight and it barely changes. Start near 0.1 and tune; watch the KL, not just the loss.

slides.title: 3. Starting from a base model
slides.body: DPO and PPO both assume a competent starting point. Run them on a raw pretrained model and there's no instruction-following behavior to refine — preference tuning just amplifies noise. Always do supervised fine-tuning first; the reference model must be a real SFT checkpoint, not the base.

slides.title: The reference-model check
slides.code:
# the reference MUST be your SFT model, frozen
assert ref_model.training is False           # frozen, no grads
for p in ref_model.parameters():
    assert p.requires_grad is False
# and it should be the SAME checkpoint you started the policy from,
# otherwise the KL term measures distance from the wrong place

slides.title: 4. Rewarding length & politeness
slides.body: Reward models latch onto easy correlates of 'good': longer answers, more hedging, more flattery. Optimize against that and you get verbose, sycophantic responses that score high and help less. Length-normalize, and include preference pairs where the shorter or more direct answer is the chosen one.

slides.title: 5. Trusting the loss curve
slides.body: A falling DPO loss or rising PPO reward only means the model is winning the game you set — not that it's better. The reward is a proxy. The only honest check is generations on held-out prompts, judged by humans or a strong model against the reference. Always keep an eval set the training never sees.

slides.title: Which method should you reach for?

root.question: Have clean preference pairs and limited infra?

no.question: Need online exploration / a live reward signal?

slides.title: The alignment sanity gate
slides.code:
# judge tuned vs reference on held-out prompts (LLM-as-judge or humans)
wins = sum(judge(prompt, tuned(prompt), ref(prompt)) == "tuned"
           for prompt in heldout)
win_rate = wins / len(heldout)
assert win_rate > 0.5, f"no real gain: {win_rate:.2f}"
# also red-team: did safety behavior survive the tuning?

slides.title: The pre-flight checklist
bullets: Audit preference data for noise & bias
Start from SFT; freeze the right reference
Tune beta; monitor KL, not just loss
Length-normalize; reward substance
Beat the reference on a held-out eval

slides.title: Don't mistake aligned for safe
slides.body: Even a clean run only raises the floor on behavior. Jailbreaks, edge cases, and biased pairs slip through, and over-optimization can erode the very safety you trained in. Alignment is one layer — pair it with red-teaming, evals, and runtime guardrails before you trust it in production.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 65.
slides.body: You can now run RLHF, DPO & PPO end to end — and avoid the silent failures.

65-1.caption:
An LLM has no memory. Every token you send, it re-reads from scratch — and the limit on how much it can read at once is the context window. 🪟

The context window is the maximum span of tokens a model can attend to in a single forward pass: your system prompt, the chat history, the documents you pasted, and the tokens it has generated so far. Go over the limit and the oldest tokens fall off the edge. The KV cache is the trick that makes generating those tokens fast — it stores the attention math so the model doesn't recompute the whole prompt for every new word.

Before we argue why this matters or open up the mechanics, get the two core objects clear in your head.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• what a context window actually is
• tokens, not words or characters
• what the KV cache stores and why
• why the window has a hard ceiling
• what is NOT in the window

Get the mental model first. Save it. 👇

slides.title: The one-line definition
slides.body: A context window is the maximum number of tokens a language model can read and attend to in one pass — prompt plus everything generated so far. It is the model's entire working memory for a single request. Nothing outside the window influences the next token.

slides.title: Measured in tokens, not words
slides.body: Tokens are sub-word chunks. 'unbelievable' might split into 'un', 'believ', 'able'. As a rough rule, 1 token is about 0.75 English words, or roughly 4 characters. A 128K window holds ~96,000 words — a short novel.

slides.title: Everything competes for the same budget
bullets: System prompt
Tool definitions & schemas
Retrieved documents (RAG)
Full conversation history
The model's own output so far

slides.title: What the KV cache is
slides.body: During attention, each token is turned into Key and Value vectors. The KV cache stores those vectors for every token already processed, so generating the next token reuses them instead of recomputing the whole sequence. It is a speed cache, not extra memory of meaning.

slides.title: Why there is a hard ceiling
slides.body: Self-attention compares every token to every other token, so cost grows with the square of the length. The window size is a deliberate cap chosen at training time. Some models extend it, but quality and speed degrade well before the advertised maximum.

slides.title: Context window vs training data
bullets: Training data: billions of tokens, baked into weights, frozen
Context window: tokens you supply live, per request
Weights = long-term memory; window = short-term scratchpad
The model cannot 'learn' from the window — it forgets after the call

slides.title: Counting tokens before you send
slides.code:
import tiktoken

enc = tiktoken.get_encoding("cl100k_base")
text = "Context windows are measured in tokens."
tokens = enc.encode(text)
print(len(tokens))      # 7
print(tokens[:5])       # [2014, 11276, 527, 17303, 304]

slides.title: What is NOT in the window
bullets: Anything from a previous, separate API call
Files the model 'saw' yesterday — gone
Knowledge it wasn't trained on and you didn't paste
Tokens that scrolled off the front edge

slides.title: The 30-second summary
bullets: Window = working memory, capped in tokens
Stateless: every call starts blank
KV cache = speed trick, not memory
Over the limit = oldest tokens dropped

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 66.
slides.body: Next up: why context windows quietly decide whether your whole AI feature works.

65-2.caption:
Most 'the AI got confused' bugs aren't bad prompts. They're context bugs — you blew past the window, or the important sentence drifted into the dead zone in the middle. 🎯

The context window is a budget, and budgets shape everything: how much history you can keep, how many documents you can stuff into a RAG prompt, how long a coding session stays coherent, and how much each API call costs. Bigger windows aren't free either — they cost more tokens, run slower, and suffer from 'lost in the middle,' where models reliably ignore content buried away from the edges.

This post is about the stakes, not the plumbing.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• why the window caps real features
• cost and latency scale with context
• the 'lost in the middle' problem
• why long chats degrade
• when bigger windows help — and when they don't

Understand the constraint before you fight it. Save it. 👇

slides.title: The window is a product constraint
slides.body: Every feature that feels like 'memory' — chat history, document Q&A, long coding sessions — is really just text packed into one window. When the feature breaks at scale, it's usually because real usage overflowed the budget you designed for a demo.

slides.title: Cost and latency scale with length
You pay for every token, every turn
slides.body: Most APIs bill on input tokens. A 50-turn chat re-sends the whole history each turn, so token cost grows roughly with the square of conversation length unless you trim. A long system prompt is a tax you pay on every single request.

slides.title: Lost in the middle
slides.body: Models attend most reliably to the start and end of the window. Facts placed in the middle of a long context are measurably more likely to be ignored — even when the answer is sitting right there. Bigger windows make this worse, not better.

slides.title: Why long chats go stupid
slides.body: As history fills the window, early instructions get pushed toward the lossy middle or off the edge entirely. The model isn't 'forgetting' — those tokens literally stopped being in context. This is why agents drift after many turns.

slides.title: Estimating cost from token count
slides.code:
def request_cost(input_tokens, output_tokens,
                 in_rate=3.0, out_rate=15.0):
    # rates are USD per 1M tokens
    return (input_tokens / 1e6) * in_rate \
         + (output_tokens / 1e6) * out_rate

# 40k-token prompt, 800-token answer
print(round(request_cost(40_000, 800), 4))  # 0.132

slides.title: Design rules that follow
bullets: Put the most important text first or last
Trim or summarize history aggressively
Retrieve relevant chunks, don't dump everything
Budget tokens like you budget money

slides.title: The bottom line
slides.body: Context isn't infinite and it isn't uniform. The window size and the position of your content silently decide whether a feature is reliable. Treat it as a first-class design constraint, not an afterthought.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 66.
slides.body: Next: open the hood — how attention and the KV cache actually move tokens through the model.

65-3.caption:
Here's the mechanism nobody explains: why generating the 1,000th token is fast, but only because of a cache that quietly eats your GPU memory. ⚙️

When a transformer reads your prompt, every token produces Query, Key, and Value vectors. Attention scores each Query against all the Keys, then mixes the Values. Generation is autoregressive — one token at a time — and the Keys and Values of past tokens never change. So we store them: that's the KV cache. New token, one new Query, reuse all the old Keys and Values. No recompute.

The catch: the cache grows linearly with sequence length and with every layer and head, so long contexts can use more memory than the model weights themselves.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• Q, K, V and what attention computes
• prefill vs decode phases
• why K and V can be cached
• how big the cache actually gets
• the tricks that shrink it

This is the real engine. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Attention in one breath
slides.body: Each token becomes three vectors: Query (what I'm looking for), Key (what I offer), Value (what I carry). Attention scores every Query against every Key, softmaxes the scores, and uses them to take a weighted sum of the Values. That mix is what flows to the next layer.

slides.title: Two phases: prefill then decode
slides.body: Prefill: the whole prompt is processed in one parallel pass, filling the KV cache. Decode: tokens are generated one at a time, each appending one new Key/Value pair to the cache. Prefill is compute-bound; decode is memory-bound — it spends most of its time reading the cache.

slides.title: Why K and V are cacheable
slides.body: The Key and Value of an earlier token depend only on that token and the ones before it — never on tokens that come later. Causal attention guarantees the past can't see the future, so old K/V vectors are frozen and safe to reuse forever within a request.

slides.title: How big does it get?
slides.body: Cache size = 2 (K and V) × layers × heads × head_dim × seq_len × bytes. For a 7B model with 32 layers at 16-bit, ~8K tokens costs over 2 GB of cache. Push to 128K context and the cache can dwarf the weights. This is the real ceiling on long context.

slides.title: The cache makes decode cheap
slides.code:
# Conceptual generation loop with a KV cache
cache = None
token = bos
for _ in range(max_new):
    # only the NEW token is fed in; cache holds the past
    logits, cache = model(token, past_kv=cache)
    token = logits[-1].argmax()
    if token == eos:
        break

slides.title: Estimating cache memory
slides.code:
def kv_cache_bytes(layers, heads, head_dim,
                   seq_len, dtype_bytes=2):
    # 2 = one for K, one for V
    return 2 * layers * heads * head_dim * seq_len * dtype_bytes

b = kv_cache_bytes(32, 32, 128, 8192)
print(round(b / 1e9, 2), "GB")   # 2.15 GB

slides.title: Tricks that shrink the cache
bullets: GQA/MQA: fewer K/V heads shared across queries
Quantize the cache to 8-bit or 4-bit
Sliding-window attention: keep only recent K/V
PagedAttention: stop wasting reserved memory

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 66.
slides.body: Next: a hands-on code walkthrough — see the cache speed up generation for real.

65-4.caption:
Talk is cheap. Let's measure it: the same model, the same prompt, with the KV cache on and off — and watch the speedup with your own eyes. 💻

This post is hands-on. We'll tokenize text and count tokens, generate with Hugging Face transformers using the cache, then turn the cache off and time the difference. We'll also inspect the cache's shape so the '2 × layers × heads × dim × seq' formula stops being abstract. Every snippet is real and runnable on a small open model.

If you've only ever called a hosted API, this is where context windows and the KV cache become concrete.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• count tokens and check the window
• generate with use_cache on
• benchmark cache on vs off
• inspect the actual cache tensors
• respect the model's max length

Copy, run, and feel the difference. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 1. Setup + count tokens
slides.code:
# pip install transformers torch
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM
import torch, time

name = "gpt2"
tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(name)
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(name)

prompt = "The context window is"
ids = tok(prompt, return_tensors="pt").input_ids
print("tokens:", ids.shape[1])          # 4
print("max ctx:", model.config.n_positions)  # 1024

slides.title: 2. Generate WITH the cache
slides.code:
torch.manual_seed(0)
out = model.generate(
    ids,
    max_new_tokens=60,
    use_cache=True,        # KV cache ON (default)
    do_sample=False,
)
print(tok.decode(out[0], skip_special_tokens=True))

slides.title: 3. Benchmark: cache on vs off
slides.code:
def timed(use_cache):
    t = time.perf_counter()
    model.generate(ids, max_new_tokens=120,
                    use_cache=use_cache, do_sample=False)
    return time.perf_counter() - t

print("cache on :", round(timed(True), 3), "s")
print("cache off:", round(timed(False), 3), "s")
# cache off is several times slower at this length

slides.title: 4. Inspect the cache tensors
slides.code:
out = model(ids, use_cache=True)
past = out.past_key_values        # tuple, one per layer
k, v = past[0]                    # layer 0: (K, V)
# shape: [batch, heads, seq_len, head_dim]
print("layers:", len(past))       # 12
print("key shape:", tuple(k.shape))  # (1, 12, 4, 64)

slides.title: 5. Feed the cache forward by hand
slides.code:
# Manual loop: pass past_key_values back in
past, cur = None, ids
for _ in range(20):
    o = model(cur, past_key_values=past, use_cache=True)
    past = o.past_key_values
    cur = o.logits[:, -1:].argmax(-1)   # only NEW token
print("cached seq len:", past[0][0].shape[2])  # grows each step

slides.title: 6. Guard the window limit
What the benchmark teaches
bullets: use_cache=True is the default for a reason
Speedup grows as the sequence gets longer
Cache shape = [batch, heads, seq, head_dim]
Cache length grows by 1 per generated token

slides.title: Easy ways to break it
bullets: Re-feeding the full sequence AND a cache
Forgetting cache is per-request, not persistent
Ignoring n_positions until generation crashes
Assuming token count equals word count

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 66.
slides.body: Last angle: the context and cache mistakes that bite teams in production.

65-5.caption:
The model didn't 'hallucinate randomly.' You overflowed the window, buried the key fact in the middle, or assumed it remembered the last call. These bugs look like model failures but they're yours. 🩹

Context and the KV cache fail in quiet, repeatable ways. People treat the window as infinite, confuse token count with word count, expect the model to remember across separate calls, and stuff long documents into the dead zone where recall collapses. On the systems side, the KV cache silently devours GPU memory and caps how many users you can serve at once.

Here are the traps — and the fixes.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• treating the window as infinite
• counting words instead of tokens
• expecting memory across calls
• burying facts in the middle
• cache memory blowing up serving

Learn these the cheap way. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 1. Assuming the window is infinite
slides.body: Demos use short inputs; production users paste novels. When total tokens exceed the window, the oldest content is silently dropped or the call errors. Always count tokens before sending and have a trim-or-summarize strategy for overflow.

slides.title: 2. Counting words, not tokens
slides.body: Budgeting by characters or words underestimates length. Code, JSON, emoji, and non-English text are token-dense — a 2,000-word doc can be 3,000+ tokens. Measure with the model's real tokenizer, not a rough divide-by-four guess for edge cases.

slides.title: Fix: measure, then budget
slides.code:
import tiktoken
enc = tiktoken.get_encoding("cl100k_base")

LIMIT = 8000
def fits(system, history, user, reserve=1000):
    used = sum(len(enc.encode(t))
               for t in [system, history, user])
    return used + reserve <= LIMIT  # reserve for the answer

slides.title: 3. Expecting memory across calls
slides.body: The API is stateless. If you don't resend the conversation, the model has no idea what happened last turn. 'It forgot' almost always means 'you didn't include it.' Persistent memory is something you build, not something the model has.

no.question: Was all needed text in the window?

yes.question: Was the key fact near the middle?

slides.title: 4. Burying the important part
slides.body: Putting the critical instruction or fact in the middle of a long prompt invites 'lost in the middle.' Lead with the task and the key constraints, or restate them at the very end. Don't trust the model to dig through the center.

slides.title: 5. Letting the cache blow up
slides.body: On self-hosted models the KV cache grows with every concurrent user and every token. Unbounded, it triggers out-of-memory crashes or kills throughput. Cap context length, use GQA/quantized caches, and serve with PagedAttention to pack memory efficiently.

slides.title: Fix: trim history to a budget
Mistake to fix, at a glance
The production checklist
bullets: Token-count every request before sending
Reserve tokens for the model's output
Resend or summarize history each turn
Put critical content first or last
Cap and compress the KV cache when self-hosting

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 66.
slides.body: You can now reason about context windows and the KV cache like an engineer, not a user.

66-1.caption:
An LLM doesn't 'decide' the next word. It produces a probability for every token in its vocabulary — and temperature, top-p, and top-k are the three dials that decide which of those candidates it's actually allowed to pick. 🎲

These are sampling parameters. After the model emits a score for each possible next token, a sampler turns those scores into a final choice. Temperature reshapes how sharp or flat the probabilities are. Top-k caps how many candidates stay in the running. Top-p keeps just enough candidates to cover a probability mass. Get them straight as three distinct knobs and most 'random vs robotic' confusion disappears.

This post is the clean mental model — what each dial is, before any math.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• what sampling even means for an LLM
• temperature in one sentence
• top-k in one sentence
• top-p (nucleus) in one sentence
• how the three differ at a glance

Get the three dials clear first. Save it. 👇

slides.title: The model outputs a distribution
slides.body: An LLM doesn't pick a word. For each step it produces a logit (a raw score) for every token in its vocabulary, then softmax turns those into probabilities. Sampling is the separate step that chooses one token from that distribution. These three dials all act on that choice.

slides.title: Temperature, in one line
slides.body: Temperature scales the logits before softmax. Low temperature sharpens the distribution toward the top token (more deterministic). High temperature flattens it, giving long-shot tokens a real chance. It changes the shape of the probabilities, not which tokens exist.

slides.title: Top-k, in one line
slides.body: Top-k keeps only the k most probable tokens and zeroes out the rest, then samples among the survivors. k=1 is greedy (always the top token). k=50 means 'consider the 50 best candidates, ignore the rest.' It's a fixed-count cutoff.

slides.title: Top-p (nucleus), in one line
slides.body: Top-p keeps the smallest set of top tokens whose probabilities add up to p, then samples among those. p=0.9 means 'keep candidates until we've covered 90% of the mass.' The count adapts to how confident the model is — that's the key difference from top-k.

left.title: Top-k
items: Can keep junk when model is unsure

right.title: Top-p
items: Keeps a fixed probability mass, p

slides.title: What each dial controls
bullets: Temperature → randomness / sharpness
Top-k → maximum number of candidates
Top-p → cumulative probability cutoff
All three only narrow or reshape choices
None of them add new knowledge

slides.title: All three in one API call
slides.code:
from openai import OpenAI
client = OpenAI()

resp = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="gpt-4o-mini",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Name a color."}],
    temperature=0.7,   # sharpness of distribution
    top_p=0.9,         # nucleus cutoff
    # note: top_k is exposed by many local APIs, not all hosted ones
)
print(resp.choices[0].message.content)

slides.title: What they are NOT
bullets: Not a 'creativity' setting that adds ideas
Not the same thing under different names
Not retraining — weights never change
Temperature ≠ top-p; they compose

slides.title: The 30-second summary
bullets: Model → probabilities; sampler → one token
Temperature reshapes the curve
Top-k = keep best k
Top-p = keep mass p, count adapts

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 67.
slides.body: Next up: why these three dials quietly decide whether your AI feature is reliable or chaotic.

66-2.caption:
Same model, same prompt, wildly different output — and the difference is usually one number you set and forgot. 🎯

Sampling parameters are not a cosmetic detail. They decide whether your extraction pipeline returns valid JSON every time or hallucinates a field, whether your support bot stays on-script or wanders, whether a brainstorm feels alive or reads like a template. The same temperature that makes poetry sparkle will quietly corrupt a SQL query. Picking these values is a product decision, not a default to inherit.

This post is about the stakes: when randomness helps, when it ruins you, and why reproducibility lives here too.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• determinism vs diversity as a real tradeoff
• why structured output needs low temperature
• why creative tasks need headroom
• the reproducibility problem in production
• the hidden cost of bad defaults

Tune these on purpose, not by accident. Save it. 👇

slides.title: The core tradeoff
slides.body: Every task sits somewhere on a line between 'I need the same correct answer every time' and 'I want variety and surprise.' Sampling parameters are how you place a task on that line. Default settings pick a spot for you — usually the wrong one for your specific job.

slides.title: Structured output breaks at high temp
slides.body: If you ask for strict JSON at temperature 1.0, the model occasionally samples a low-probability token — a stray comma, an invented key, a trailing comment — and your parser throws. Low temperature concentrates mass on the correct, well-formed continuation, which is exactly what a schema needs.

slides.title: Creative work needs headroom
slides.body: Set temperature near 0 for a brainstorm and you get the same safe, obvious answer every run. Diversity requires letting plausible-but-not-top tokens through. Here higher temperature plus a top-p around 0.9 keeps things fresh without collapsing into nonsense.

slides.title: The reproducibility trap
slides.body: Any temperature above 0 means the same prompt can give different answers, so your eval suite flakes and a bug you saw yesterday won't reproduce. Teams burn hours chasing 'intermittent' failures that are just sampling noise. For tests, pin temperature to 0 and set a seed.

slides.title: Cost and latency hide here too
slides.body: High randomness raises the odds of malformed output, which forces retries — and every retry is another billed call and another round-trip of latency. Tighter sampling on structured tasks isn't just safer; it's cheaper and faster because you stop paying for do-overs.

slides.title: Different settings per task
Rules that follow
bullets: Default to T=0 for anything parsed by code
Raise temperature only where variety is the goal
Pin T=0 + seed in your eval/test suite
Treat these as per-task config, not globals

slides.title: The bottom line
slides.body: These dials sit between a capable model and a reliable product. The model can be perfect and the feature still flaky if the sampling is wrong for the job. Choose the values deliberately for each task and most 'the AI is unpredictable' complaints disappear.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 67.
slides.body: Next: open the hood — the actual math of how each dial reshapes the probabilities.

66-3.caption:
Here's the part most explainers skip: the exact math. Temperature, top-p, and top-k aren't vibes — they're three precise operations on a vector of numbers. ⚙️

The model gives you logits, one per vocabulary token. Temperature divides every logit by T before softmax: divide by a small number and the gaps blow up, sharpening the distribution; divide by a large number and the gaps shrink, flattening it. Then top-k sorts and clips to the best k tokens. Top-p sorts, takes a cumulative sum, and keeps tokens until the running total reaches p. Whatever survives gets renormalized, and you sample from that.

Once you see the operations in order, the order they apply in — and why it matters — clicks.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• logits → softmax, the starting point
• the temperature formula and its limits
• top-k as a sort-and-clip
• top-p as a cumulative-mass cutoff
• the order operations actually run in

This is the real engine. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Start: logits to softmax
slides.body: The model's final layer outputs a logit z_i for each token i. Softmax converts them to probabilities: p_i = exp(z_i) / Σ exp(z_j). Bigger logit, bigger probability. Every sampling dial intervenes on this vector — before or after the softmax.

slides.title: Temperature: divide the logits
slides.body: Temperature applies p_i = softmax(z_i / T). At T<1 the logits spread apart, so softmax piles mass onto the top tokens — sharper, more deterministic. At T>1 the logits compress, so mass spreads to the tail — flatter, more random. T=1 leaves the model's native distribution untouched.

slides.title: The limits of T
slides.body: As T → 0 the distribution collapses onto the single highest logit: that's pure greedy decoding, fully deterministic. As T → ∞ it approaches uniform — every token equally likely, pure noise. In practice T usually lives between 0 and ~2; beyond that text degrades fast.

slides.title: Top-k: sort and clip
slides.body: Sort tokens by probability, keep the top k, set the rest to zero, then renormalize so the survivors sum to 1 and sample. k is a hard count: it ignores whether the model is confident or uncertain. The 51st-best token gets cut even if it was nearly as likely as the 50th.

slides.title: Top-p: cumulative-mass cutoff
slides.body: Sort tokens high to low, walk down accumulating probability, and stop once the running sum reaches p. Keep exactly that set (the 'nucleus'), renormalize, sample. When the model is confident, two or three tokens cover p; when it's unsure, the set grows automatically.

slides.title: All three from scratch
slides.code:
import numpy as np

def sample(logits, T=1.0, top_k=0, top_p=1.0):
    logits = logits / T                      # temperature
    order = np.argsort(logits)[::-1]
    if top_k:                                # top-k clip
        order = order[:top_k]
    probs = np.exp(logits[order] - logits[order].max())
    probs /= probs.sum()
    if top_p < 1.0:                          # nucleus cut
        cum = np.cumsum(probs)
        keep = np.searchsorted(cum, top_p) + 1
        order, probs = order[:keep], probs[:keep]
        probs /= probs.sum()
    return int(np.random.choice(order, p=probs))

slides.title: The order that matters
bullets: 1. Temperature scales the logits
2. Top-k clips to a fixed count
3. Top-p clips to a mass
4. Renormalize, then sample once

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 67.
slides.body: Next: a hands-on walkthrough — watch the same prompt change as we turn each dial.

66-4.caption:
Talk is cheap. Let's run it: one prompt, one model, and we'll turn temperature, top-k, and top-p one at a time so you can see exactly what each does. 💻

This post is hands-on. We'll generate greedily for a deterministic baseline, crank temperature to watch outputs diverge, then layer in top-k and top-p with Hugging Face transformers. We'll also reimplement the sampler in NumPy so the API arguments stop being magic. Every snippet is real and runs on a small open model on a laptop.

If you've only ever passed temperature=0.7 and hoped, this is where the dials become tangible.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• a deterministic greedy baseline
• sampling with temperature
• adding top-k and top-p
• seeding for reproducible runs
• inspecting the probabilities directly

Copy, run, and watch the dials move. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 1. Setup + greedy baseline
slides.code:
# pip install transformers torch
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM
import torch

name = "gpt2"
tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(name)
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(name)

ids = tok("The best way to learn AI is", return_tensors="pt").input_ids
# greedy: do_sample=False ignores temp/top_k/top_p
out = model.generate(ids, max_new_tokens=30, do_sample=False)
print(tok.decode(out[0], skip_special_tokens=True))

slides.title: 2. Turn ONE dial: temperature
slides.code:
torch.manual_seed(0)
for T in (0.3, 0.7, 1.3):
    out = model.generate(
        ids, max_new_tokens=30,
        do_sample=True,        # sampling must be ON
        temperature=T,
        top_k=0, top_p=1.0,    # disable the others
    )
    print(f"T={T}:", tok.decode(out[0][ids.shape[1]:],
                                 skip_special_tokens=True))

slides.title: 3. Add top-k and top-p
slides.code:
torch.manual_seed(0)
out = model.generate(
    ids, max_new_tokens=40,
    do_sample=True,
    temperature=0.9,
    top_k=50,      # keep 50 best candidates
    top_p=0.92,    # then keep 92% of the mass
)
print(tok.decode(out[0], skip_special_tokens=True))
# top_k and top_p compose: top_k runs first, then top_p

slides.title: 4. The sampler, by hand
What the code does each step
5. Reproducible sampling
slides.code:
# Same seed + same params  ->  identical output
def run(seed):
    torch.manual_seed(seed)
    out = model.generate(ids, max_new_tokens=20,
                         do_sample=True, temperature=0.8,
                         top_p=0.9)
    return tok.decode(out[0], skip_special_tokens=True)

print(run(42) == run(42))   # True
print(run(42) == run(7))    # False

slides.title: 6. Peek at the probabilities
slides.code:
with torch.no_grad():
    logits = model(ids).logits[0, -1]      # last-position logits
probs = torch.softmax(logits / 0.7, dim=-1)
topv, topi = torch.topk(probs, 5)
for p, i in zip(topv, topi):
    print(f"{tok.decode([i]):>12}  {p.item():.3f}")

slides.title: Gotchas you just saw
bullets: do_sample=True or temp/top_k/top_p are ignored
top_k=0 and top_p=1.0 disable those filters
top_k is applied before top_p
Set a seed for reproducible runs

slides.title: 7. Same idea via a hosted API
slides.code:
from openai import OpenAI
client = OpenAI()
resp = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="gpt-4o-mini",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Finish: The best way to learn AI is"}],
    temperature=0.9,
    top_p=0.92,
    seed=42,            # best-effort reproducibility
)
print(resp.choices[0].message.content)

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 67.
slides.body: Next: the mistakes that quietly wreck output — stacking dials and trusting bad defaults.

66-5.caption:
Most 'the model is broken' tickets are really 'someone set temperature to 1.0 on a JSON endpoint.' These dials fail quietly, and the failures look like model problems. 🚨

The traps repeat across every team: cranking temperature for 'creativity' and getting malformed output, fighting temperature and top-p against each other, copying a tutorial's settings into a totally different task, and forgetting that anything above zero kills reproducibility. None of these are exotic — they're the default way people misuse three simple numbers.

This post is the field guide to what goes wrong and how to set them right.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• high temperature on structured output
• stacking temperature AND top-p blindly
• confusing top-k with top-p
• copy-pasted defaults that don't fit
• ignoring reproducibility in tests

Learn the failure modes before they bite you. Save it. 👇

slides.title: High temp on structured output
slides.body: Asking for strict JSON, SQL, or a function call at temperature 1.0 invites a stray token that breaks the parser. The fix is simple: temperature=0 (or very low) whenever code reads the output. Save the creativity for prose, not schemas.

slides.title: Stacking temperature AND top-p
slides.body: People raise temperature for variety, then also widen top-p, then wonder why output went incoherent — the two compound. Pick one primary dial. A common stable recipe is moderate temperature with top-p around 0.9, not both pushed to the extremes at once.

slides.title: Confusing top-k with top-p
slides.body: Top-k keeps a fixed number of tokens; top-p keeps a fixed probability mass. With a confident model, top-k=50 still drags in 49 near-zero tokens, while top-p=0.9 keeps just the few that matter. Reaching for the wrong one is a classic mix-up.

slides.title: Copy-pasted defaults
slides.body: A blog's temperature=0.8 was tuned for chat, not your extraction job. Inheriting settings from an unrelated task is how the same number ends up powering both a poem generator and an invoice parser. Set values from the task's needs, not from a screenshot.

slides.title: Ignoring reproducibility
slides.body: Temperature above 0 means non-deterministic output, so an eval that 'passed yesterday' may fail today for no code reason. Teams misread sampling noise as regressions. For tests and benchmarks, pin temperature=0 and a fixed seed.

slides.title: Top-p = 0 thinking 'most strict'
slides.body: Some assume top_p=0 means 'most deterministic.' Depending on the implementation it can keep only the single top token (fine) or behave undefined at the edge. For true determinism, use temperature=0 / greedy decoding — don't rely on extreme top-p as a proxy.

slides.title: Safe defaults by task
The checklist
bullets: Parsed output? T=0, period
Don't push T and top_p to extremes together
top-k = count, top-p = mass — say which you mean
Pin T=0 + seed in tests
Tune per task, never copy blindly

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 67.
slides.body: Tomorrow: the next piece of the NLP & LLMs toolkit — building on how models choose words.

67-1.caption:
An LLM only knows what it learned during training. RAG is how you let it answer questions about your data — without retraining a single weight. 📚

Retrieval-Augmented Generation is a simple idea with a deep payoff: before the model answers, you fetch relevant text from an external source and paste it into the prompt. The model then generates its answer grounded in those fetched documents instead of relying purely on frozen, possibly-stale memory.

That's the whole trick. Retrieve, then generate. But getting the mental model exactly right is what separates a RAG system that cites real facts from one that confidently makes things up.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• what RAG actually stands for
• retrieval vs the model's parametric memory
• the two-step retrieve-then-generate loop
• what an embedding and a vector store are
• what RAG is NOT

Get the mental model first. Save it. 👇

slides.title: The one-line definition
slides.body: RAG — Retrieval-Augmented Generation — fetches relevant text from an external knowledge source and adds it to the prompt before the model answers. The model generates a response grounded in that retrieved text instead of relying only on what it memorized during training.

slides.title: Two kinds of knowledge
slides.body: Parametric knowledge lives in the model's frozen weights — vast, implicit, and stuck at the training cutoff. Non-parametric knowledge is the text you supply at query time. RAG bolts a non-parametric memory onto a parametric model, so it can use fresh, private, or specific facts.

slides.title: What an embedding is
slides.body: An embedding turns a chunk of text into a list of numbers — a vector — that captures its meaning. Texts about similar ideas land near each other in this space. Retrieval works by embedding the question and finding the stored chunks whose vectors sit closest to it.

slides.title: What a vector store is
slides.body: A vector store (vector database) holds the embeddings of your document chunks alongside the original text. Given a query vector, it returns the nearest neighbors fast — even across millions of chunks. It's the search engine that makes the 'retrieve' step practical.

slides.title: The lifecycle in two halves
slides.body: Indexing happens once, offline: split documents into chunks, embed each chunk, and store the vectors. Querying happens per request: embed the question, fetch the closest chunks, stuff them into the prompt, and let the model answer. Most RAG confusion comes from mixing these two halves up.

slides.title: RAG in eight lines
slides.code:
# the entire pattern, stripped down
query = "What's our refund window?"

chunks = vector_store.search(embed(query), k=4)
context = "\n\n".join(c.text for c in chunks)

prompt = f"Use ONLY this context:\n{context}\n\nQ: {query}"
answer = llm(prompt)
print(answer)

slides.title: What RAG is NOT
bullets: Not fine-tuning — no weights change
Not memory — nothing persists after the call
Not a search engine alone — it generates an answer
Not a guarantee of truth — bad retrieval = bad answer

slides.title: The 30-second summary
bullets: RAG = retrieve relevant text, then generate
Grounds answers in YOUR data, not just training
Built on embeddings + a vector store
Update knowledge by editing docs, not retraining

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 68.
slides.body: Next up: why RAG quietly became the default way to ship LLM features on private data.

67-2.caption:
Your customers don't care what the model learned in 2023. They care whether it knows your refund policy, today. That gap is exactly why RAG exists. 🎯

A raw LLM has three structural problems for real products: it can't see your private data, its knowledge is frozen at a training cutoff, and when it doesn't know something it tends to make up a confident answer. RAG attacks all three at once — without the cost, latency, and staleness of retraining a model every time a document changes.

This post is about the stakes: why retrieval became the default architecture for grounded, trustworthy, up-to-date LLM features.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• the knowledge-cutoff problem
• private data the model never saw
• hallucination and how grounding fights it
• why RAG beats retraining for fresh data
• citations, auditability, and trust

Understand the why before the how. Save it. 👇

slides.title: The knowledge cutoff problem
slides.body: Every base model is frozen at a training date. Ask about anything newer — a policy update, last quarter's numbers, yesterday's incident — and it can't know. RAG sidesteps this entirely: the answer comes from documents you retrieve right now, so 'fresh' is just a matter of updating the index.

slides.title: It never saw your private data
slides.body: The model was trained on public text, not your wiki, your tickets, or your contracts. No amount of clever prompting reveals data it never ingested. RAG is how that private knowledge reaches the model — fetched on demand and dropped into the prompt for this one request.

slides.title: Grounding fights hallucination
slides.body: When a model lacks a fact, it often invents a plausible one. RAG reduces this by putting the real answer in front of the model and instructing it to use only that text. It doesn't eliminate hallucination, but grounding shifts the model from recalling to reading.

slides.title: RAG vs retraining for fresh data
Citations build trust
slides.body: Because RAG knows which chunks it used, it can show them. Users get an answer with links back to the source paragraph, so they can verify rather than take it on faith. In regulated or high-stakes domains, that auditability is often the deciding feature.

slides.title: Grounding by instruction
slides.code:
system = (
  "Answer using ONLY the provided context. "
  "If the context doesn't contain the answer, "
  "say 'I don't know based on the documents.' "
  "Cite the source id after each claim."
)
# this single instruction is what turns retrieval
# into a trustworthy, refusable answer

slides.title: When RAG is the right call
bullets: Answers depend on private or changing data
Users need verifiable sources
Knowledge updates faster than you can retrain
You want to add/remove facts without a model run

slides.title: The bottom line
slides.body: RAG turns a general model into a specialist on your data without touching its weights. It makes answers current, grounded, and citable — the three things that separate a demo from something a business can actually trust in front of customers.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 68.
slides.body: Next: open the hood — exactly how a document becomes a chunk, a vector, and a grounded answer.

67-3.caption:
Here's the part most explanations skip: a RAG system has two completely separate pipelines, and confusing them is why so many builds quietly return garbage. ⚙️

One pipeline runs once, offline — it turns your documents into a searchable index. The other runs on every question — it finds the right chunks and feeds them to the model. Get the indexing wrong and no amount of prompt engineering at query time will save you.

This post walks both pipelines end to end: chunking, embedding, storing, retrieving, ranking, and assembling the final grounded prompt.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• indexing vs querying — the two pipelines
• why and how you chunk documents
• embeddings and similarity search
• top-k retrieval and what k controls
• re-ranking and assembling the prompt

This is the real machinery. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Two pipelines, one system
slides.body: Indexing is offline and runs once per document: load, chunk, embed, store. Querying is online and runs per question: embed the query, search, optionally re-rank, assemble the prompt, generate. They share the same embedding model — that's the only link between them.

slides.title: Why you chunk
slides.body: Whole documents are too big to embed meaningfully and too big to fit in a prompt. So you split them into chunks — often a few hundred tokens with a small overlap. Each chunk becomes one retrievable unit. Chunk too big and retrieval is imprecise; too small and you lose context.

slides.title: Embedding and similarity
slides.body: Each chunk is embedded into a vector that encodes its meaning. At query time the question is embedded by the same model. Similarity is usually cosine distance — the angle between vectors. Closest vectors mean closest meaning, so the nearest chunks are the most relevant ones.

slides.title: Top-k retrieval
slides.body: The vector store returns the k most similar chunks — often k between 3 and 8. k trades coverage against noise: too few and you miss the answer; too many and you bury it in irrelevant text and burn context budget. k is one of the most important knobs you'll tune.

slides.title: Re-ranking sharpens results
slides.body: Vector search is fast but approximate. A re-ranker — often a cross-encoder — re-scores the top candidates by reading the query and each chunk together, then keeps the best few. It's a second, more precise pass that fixes the cheap first pass's mistakes.

slides.title: Indexing: chunk, embed, store
slides.code:
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer
import chromadb

model = SentenceTransformer("all-MiniLM-L6-v2")
db = chromadb.Client().create_collection("docs")

def chunk(text, size=500, overlap=50):
    step = size - overlap
    return [text[i:i+size] for i in range(0, len(text), step)]

for i, c in enumerate(chunk(open("policy.txt").read())):
    db.add(ids=[str(i)], documents=[c],
           embeddings=[model.encode(c).tolist()])

slides.title: Querying: search, then generate
slides.code:
def answer(question, k=4):
    q_vec = model.encode(question).tolist()
    hits = db.query(query_embeddings=[q_vec], n_results=k)
    context = "\n\n".join(hits["documents"][0])
    prompt = (f"Context:\n{context}\n\n"
              f"Answer using only the context.\nQ: {question}")
    return llm(prompt)

print(answer("What is the refund window?"))

slides.title: The knobs that matter
bullets: Chunk size + overlap: precision vs context
Embedding model: quality of retrieval
k: coverage vs noise
Re-ranker: precision on the shortlist
Prompt template: grounding + refusal

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 68.
slides.body: Next: a full, runnable RAG pipeline in code — from raw text to a cited answer.

67-4.caption:
Enough theory. Let's build a working RAG pipeline you can actually run — load a document, index it, ask a question, and get a grounded, cited answer. 💻

This post is hands-on. We'll chunk a text file, embed the chunks with a small open model, store them in a vector database, retrieve the top matches for a question, assemble a grounded prompt, and call an LLM to answer. Then we'll add source citations so the answer is verifiable. Every snippet is real and runs on a laptop.

If you've only read about RAG, this is where it becomes a thing you've built with your own hands.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• install + load and chunk a document
• embed and store in a vector DB
• retrieve the top-k relevant chunks
• assemble the grounded prompt and answer
• return citations to the source

Copy, run, and see it work. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 1. Install + load + chunk
slides.code:
# pip install sentence-transformers chromadb openai
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer
import chromadb

text = open("handbook.txt").read()

def chunk(t, size=500, overlap=50):
    step = size - overlap
    return [t[i:i+size] for i in range(0, len(t), step)]

docs = chunk(text)
print("chunks:", len(docs))

slides.title: 2. Embed + store the chunks
slides.code:
embedder = SentenceTransformer("all-MiniLM-L6-v2")
collection = chromadb.Client().create_collection("handbook")

collection.add(
    ids=[f"chunk-{i}" for i in range(len(docs))],
    documents=docs,
    embeddings=[embedder.encode(d).tolist() for d in docs],
)
print("indexed:", collection.count())

slides.title: 3. Retrieve the top-k chunks
4. Assemble prompt + generate
slides.code:
from openai import OpenAI
client = OpenAI()

def answer(question, k=4):
    hits = retrieve(question, k)
    context = "\n\n".join(f"[{cid}] {txt}" for cid, txt in hits)
    msgs = [
      {"role": "system", "content":
       "Answer using ONLY the context. Cite [ids] you use. "
       "If unknown, say so."},
      {"role": "user", "content":
       f"Context:\n{context}\n\nQuestion: {question}"},
    ]
    out = client.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4o-mini", messages=msgs)
    return out.choices[0].message.content

slides.title: 5. Ask and read the cited answer
slides.code:
print(answer("How many vacation days do I get?"))
# -> "You get 20 paid vacation days per year [chunk-7]."

print(answer("What is the CEO's home address?"))
# -> "I don't know based on the provided documents."

slides.title: 6. Return structured citations
What the code teaches
bullets: Indexing and querying are separate functions
The same embedder must encode docs AND queries
Carry chunk ids through so you can cite
The system prompt enforces grounding + refusal

slides.title: Easy ways to break it
bullets: Using different embedders for index vs query
Forgetting overlap, splitting answers across chunks
k so large the context overflows the window
No refusal instruction — model guesses freely

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 68.
slides.body: Last angle: the RAG mistakes that quietly wreck retrieval quality in production.

67-5.caption:
Your RAG answer was wrong, but the model is fine. You chunked badly, retrieved the wrong text, or never told it to refuse. These look like model failures — they're retrieval failures. 🩹

RAG breaks in quiet, repeatable ways, and almost none of them are the LLM's fault. People chunk documents so the answer gets split in half, mismatch their embedding models, retrieve too much or too little, forget to handle 'the answer isn't in here,' and never measure whether retrieval is actually finding the right text.

Here are the traps that wreck RAG in production — and the fix for each.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• bad chunking that splits the answer
• mismatched embedding models
• wrong k — too much or too little
• no grounding or refusal instruction
• never evaluating retrieval quality

Learn these the cheap way. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 1. Chunking that splits the answer
slides.body: If a chunk boundary cuts through the sentence that holds the answer, neither chunk retrieves well and the model never sees the full fact. Fix it with sensible chunk sizes, an overlap between chunks, and splitting on natural boundaries like paragraphs or headings — not blind character counts.

slides.title: 2. Mismatched embedding models
slides.body: Documents must be embedded with the exact same model you use for queries. Mix two models — or re-index with a new one but forget to re-embed the query side — and the vectors live in different spaces, so 'nearest' becomes meaningless and retrieval returns noise.

slides.title: Fix: one embedder, used everywhere
slides.code:
EMBEDDER = SentenceTransformer("all-MiniLM-L6-v2")

def embed(text):            # single source of truth
    return EMBEDDER.encode(text).tolist()

# index time AND query time both call embed()
# never instantiate a second model by accident

slides.title: 3. The wrong k
slides.body: Too small a k and the relevant chunk never makes the cut. Too large and you flood the prompt with irrelevant text — raising cost, latency, and the chance the model fixates on the wrong passage. Start around k=4, then tune it against real questions.

slides.title: 4. No grounding or refusal
slides.body: If you don't instruct the model to answer only from the context and to say 'I don't know' when it's missing, it falls back on parametric memory and guesses — silently undoing the whole point of RAG. Always pin the grounding rule and an explicit refusal path in the system prompt.

slides.title: 5. Never evaluating retrieval
slides.body: Teams obsess over the prompt and ignore whether retrieval even surfaced the right text. Most RAG failures are retrieval failures. Build a small set of question–answer pairs and measure recall: did the gold chunk appear in the top-k? You can't fix what you don't measure.

slides.title: Fix: measure retrieval recall
Mistake to fix, at a glance

left.title: Mistake
items: k too big or too small

slides.title: The production checklist
bullets: Chunk on natural boundaries, with overlap
Embed docs and queries with the same model
Tune k; don't just dump everything
Instruct: answer only from context, else refuse
Measure recall@k before blaming the model

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 68.
slides.body: You can now design, build, and debug a RAG system like an engineer, not a user.

68-1.caption:
An LLM only knows what was baked into its weights at training time. RAG is how you let it answer about your documents, your tickets, your last release — without retraining anything. 📚

RAG stands for Retrieval-Augmented Generation. Instead of asking the model to recall an answer from memory, you first retrieve the most relevant chunks of your own data, paste them into the prompt as context, and then ask the model to answer using that context. The model becomes a reasoning engine on top of a search system you control.

This post is the clean mental model — what RAG is and why it's a pipeline, not a single trick.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• what RAG actually stands for
• the retrieve-then-generate idea in one line
• why it's a pipeline of stages, not one call
• what problem it solves that fine-tuning doesn't
• the vocabulary you'll see everywhere

Get the shape of the system first. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation
slides.body: RAG means: before the model answers, you fetch relevant text from your own data and put it in the prompt. The model then generates an answer grounded in that fetched text instead of relying only on what it memorized during training. Retrieve first, generate second.

slides.title: The problem it solves
slides.body: A base LLM has a fixed knowledge cutoff and no access to your private data. It can't know your internal docs, today's prices, or last night's incident report. RAG closes that gap by feeding fresh, specific context at query time — no retraining, no waiting.

slides.title: Why 'pipeline', not 'feature'
slides.body: RAG is not one API call. It's a chain: ingest documents, split them into chunks, embed each chunk into a vector, store those vectors, search them at query time, then assemble a prompt and call the model. Each stage has its own choices and its own failure modes.

slides.title: The core vocabulary
bullets: Chunk → a small slice of a document
Embedding → a vector that captures meaning
Vector store → a database you search by similarity
Retriever → the component that finds top matches
Context → retrieved text pasted into the prompt

slides.title: RAG in ~6 lines
slides.code:
def answer(question):
    chunks = retriever.search(question, k=4)   # retrieve
    context = "

".join(c.text for c in chunks)
    prompt = f"Use ONLY this context:
{context}

Q: {question}"
    return llm.generate(prompt)               # generate

slides.title: What RAG is NOT
bullets: Not the model 'learning' your data permanently
Not a replacement for clean source documents
Not just keyword search with extra steps
Not a guarantee of correctness — it grounds, not verifies

slides.title: The 30-second summary
bullets: LLM = reasoning; RAG = giving it the right facts
Retrieve relevant chunks, then generate
It's a multi-stage pipeline you control
Update knowledge by updating documents

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 69.
slides.body: Next up: why RAG matters — the real-world problems it fixes that a bigger model can't.

68-2.caption:
Teams reach for a bigger model when their AI gives wrong answers. Usually the model isn't the problem — it just never had the right facts in front of it. 🎯

RAG matters because it attacks the failure modes that scale and fine-tuning don't: stale knowledge, private data the model has never seen, confident hallucinations, and answers you can't trace back to a source. It turns 'the AI made something up' into 'the AI quoted document 12, which is wrong — fix the document.' That's a different, fixable class of problem.

This post is about the stakes: why retrieval beats memorization for most business questions.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• why a bigger model doesn't fix wrong facts
• how RAG cuts hallucinations
• keeping knowledge fresh without retraining
• citations and auditability you can ship
• the real cost and access-control wins

Know why before you build. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Bigger model ≠ right facts
slides.body: A larger LLM reasons better, but it still can't know facts it was never trained on — your contracts, this week's inventory, an internal policy. Scaling the model doesn't add your data; it just makes confident wrongness more fluent. Retrieval is what supplies the missing facts.

slides.title: Hallucinations drop when grounded
slides.body: Asked from memory, a model fills gaps with plausible fiction. Given the actual passage and told to answer only from it, it has far less room to invent. RAG doesn't eliminate hallucination, but it shifts the model from 'recall and guess' to 'read and report.'

slides.title: Fresh knowledge, no retraining
slides.body: When facts change, you re-index a document — seconds — instead of running a fine-tuning job and redeploying weights. A price update, a new policy, a fixed FAQ all flow in immediately. The model stays static while your knowledge base moves.

slides.title: Citations you can actually ship
slides.body: Because every answer is built from specific retrieved chunks, you can show the user exactly which document and passage backed it. That turns a black box into something auditable — reviewers verify, users trust, and wrong answers point straight at the bad source.

slides.title: Cost, control, and access
slides.body: Fine-tuning a frontier model is slow and expensive; RAG runs on a cheap base model plus a vector search. You also keep data governance: retrieval can filter by the user's permissions, so people only ever see chunks they're allowed to see.

slides.title: Grounding the prompt explicitly
slides.code:
SYSTEM = (
    "Answer ONLY using the provided context. "
    "If the answer is not in the context, say "
    "'I don't have that information.' Cite the source id."
)

prompt = f"{SYSTEM}\n\nContext:\n{context}\n\nQuestion: {q}"
answer = llm.generate(prompt)

slides.title: When RAG is the right call
bullets: Answers depend on private or changing data
You need citations or an audit trail
Knowledge updates faster than you can retrain
Access control must follow the data

slides.title: The bottom line
