slides.body: RAG matters because most 'the AI is wrong' problems are really 'the AI didn't have the facts' problems. Solve retrieval and you fix freshness, hallucination, traceability, and cost at once — without touching the model's weights.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 69.
slides.body: Next: open the hood — every stage of the pipeline, from raw document to grounded answer.

68-3.caption:
Here's the part most demos skip: RAG is a chain of stages, and a weak link anywhere quietly poisons the final answer. ⚙️

There are two halves. Offline (indexing) runs once: load documents, split them into chunks, embed each chunk into a vector, and store those vectors. Online (querying) runs per request: embed the question, search the store for the nearest chunks, rerank and trim them, build a prompt, and call the model. Most quality problems trace back to chunking and retrieval — long before the LLM is even involved.

This post walks the full pipeline end to end, stage by stage.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• the offline indexing half vs the online query half
• why chunking strategy makes or breaks recall
• how embeddings turn text into searchable vectors
• nearest-neighbor search and top-k
• reranking and assembling the final context

This is the real engine. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Stage 1 — Load & chunk
slides.body: Pull text out of PDFs, HTML, or docs, then split it into chunks of a few hundred tokens with some overlap. Chunks must be small enough to be precise but large enough to carry meaning. This single choice drives retrieval quality more than any other.

slides.title: Stage 2 — Embed
slides.body: Run each chunk through an embedding model to get a vector — a list of numbers where similar meanings sit close together in space. The same model later embeds the query, so question and answer land near each other even with different words.

slides.title: Stage 3 — Store & index
slides.body: Save the vectors (with their source text and metadata) in a vector database. It builds an index — often HNSW — so it can find the nearest vectors fast instead of scanning everything. This is your searchable knowledge base.

slides.title: Stage 4 — Retrieve top-k
slides.body: At query time, embed the question and ask the store for the k nearest chunks by cosine similarity. k is a dial: too small and you miss context, too large and you bury the model in noise. Typical starting point is k = 3 to 8.

slides.title: Stage 5 — Rerank (optional)
slides.body: Vector search is fast but rough. A reranker — a cross-encoder that scores each (query, chunk) pair directly — reorders the candidates so the truly relevant ones rise to the top. You retrieve 20, rerank, and keep the best 4 for the prompt.

slides.title: Indexing (offline) once
slides.code:
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer
embedder = SentenceTransformer("all-MiniLM-L6-v2")

chunks = split_documents(docs, size=400, overlap=50)
vectors = embedder.encode([c.text for c in chunks])
store.add(vectors, payloads=chunks)   # save to vector DB

slides.title: Querying (online) per request
slides.code:
def rag(question, k=4):
    qv = embedder.encode(question)
    hits = store.search(qv, top_k=20)        # retrieve
    hits = rerank(question, hits)[:k]        # rerank + trim
    context = "\n\n".join(h.text for h in hits)
    prompt = f"Context:\n{context}\n\nQ: {question}"
    return llm.generate(prompt)              # generate

slides.title: Where quality actually lives
bullets: Chunking decides what CAN be retrieved
Embedding model decides what's 'similar'
Top-k + rerank decide what reaches the LLM
The LLM only sees what survived all of that

68-4.caption:
Enough theory — here's a full RAG pipeline you can actually run, from raw text to a grounded answer with sources. 💻

We'll keep dependencies light: an embedding model, a tiny in-memory vector store (just NumPy and cosine similarity so the mechanics are visible), a retriever, and an LLM call. You'll see the offline indexing step and the online query step as separate, readable functions — then wire them into one ask() call. Swap the toy store for a real vector DB later; the shape stays identical.

This post is code-heavy on purpose. Read it top to bottom and you've built RAG.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• chunking documents with overlap
• embedding chunks and the query
• cosine similarity search from scratch
• assembling the grounded prompt
• the full ask() call wired together

Copy it, run it, then scale it. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 0. Install + imports
slides.code:
# pip install sentence-transformers numpy openai
import numpy as np
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer
from openai import OpenAI

embedder = SentenceTransformer("all-MiniLM-L6-v2")
client = OpenAI()

slides.title: 1. Chunk the documents
2. Embed + build the index
slides.code:
# embed every chunk once (offline step)
matrix = embedder.encode(chunks, normalize_embeddings=True)
matrix = np.array(matrix)            # shape: (n_chunks, dim)

# 'index' is just the matrix here; a real DB hides this
print(matrix.shape)

slides.title: 3. Retrieve by cosine similarity
slides.code:
def retrieve(query, k=4):
    qv = embedder.encode(query, normalize_embeddings=True)
    # vectors are unit-length, so dot product = cosine sim
    scores = matrix @ qv
    top = np.argsort(scores)[::-1][:k]
    return [chunks[i] for i in top]

slides.title: 4. Build the grounded prompt
5. Wire it all together
slides.code:
def ask(question, k=4):
    ctx = retrieve(question, k)
    prompt = build_prompt(question, ctx)
    resp = client.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4o-mini",
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
        temperature=0,          # grounded → deterministic
    )
    return resp.choices[0].message.content

print(ask("What is our refund window?"))

slides.title: Swapping in a real vector DB
bullets: Replace `matrix` with Qdrant / pgvector / Pinecone
store.add(vectors, payloads) at index time
store.search(qv, top_k) at query time
retrieve() signature stays the same
Add metadata filters for access control

slides.title: Easy bugs in this code
bullets: Forgetting normalize → dot product ≠ cosine
Embedding query with a different model than chunks
k too large → context window overflow
No 'I don't know' rule → confident guessing

slides.title: From toy to production
bullets: Persist embeddings; don't re-embed every boot
Add a reranker before the top-k cut
Return source ids alongside the answer
Cache identical queries

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 69.
slides.body: Next: the mistakes that make a 'working' RAG pipeline quietly return garbage.

68-5.caption:
A RAG demo that works on three documents can fall apart the moment it meets real data — and the failures are sneaky, because the model still answers confidently. ⚠️

Most broken pipelines aren't broken at the LLM. They're broken at chunking, at retrieval, or at the prompt. The model dutifully summarizes whatever junk you handed it, so a retrieval miss looks exactly like a smart answer that happens to be wrong. These are the mistakes I see most, and the cheap fixes for each.

This post is the debugging checklist for when RAG 'works' but the answers are bad.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• chunks too big or too small
• mismatched embedding models
• retrieving k chunks but never checking relevance
• stuffing too much context (lost in the middle)
• no 'I don't know' escape hatch

Fix the pipeline, not the prompt. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Mistake 1 — Bad chunk size
slides.body: Chunks too large dilute the signal: the right sentence drowns in paragraphs of unrelated text, so similarity scores blur. Chunks too small lose context: a number with no subject. Aim for coherent passages, a few hundred tokens, with small overlap so ideas aren't cut mid-thought.

slides.title: Mistake 2 — Mismatched embeddings
slides.body: The query and the chunks must be embedded by the same model. Index with one model and query with another and the vectors live in different spaces — similarity becomes meaningless and retrieval returns noise. Pin the embedding model and version in config.

slides.title: Mistake 3 — Trusting top-k blindly
slides.body: Vector search always returns k chunks, even when nothing relevant exists. If the answer isn't in your data, you still get the k 'least irrelevant' chunks — and the LLM answers from them. Add a similarity threshold and drop hits that score below it.

diagram.axisLabel: how well the model uses context by position

slides.title: Mistake 4 — Stuffing the context
slides.body: More chunks isn't more accuracy. Models attend best to the start and end of a long context and skim the middle, so a critical chunk buried at position 9 of 15 gets ignored. Retrieve broadly, rerank, then pass only the few best chunks.

slides.title: Mistake 5 — No escape hatch
slides.body: Without an explicit 'say I don't know if it's not in the context' instruction, the model fills gaps with fluent guesses. That's how RAG produces confident, cited-looking nonsense. The grounding rule belongs in the system prompt, every time.

slides.title: Threshold + escape hatch
slides.code:
def retrieve(query, k=4, min_score=0.35):
    qv = embedder.encode(query, normalize_embeddings=True)
    scores = matrix @ qv
    hits = [(s, chunks[i]) for i, s in enumerate(scores)
            if s >= min_score]
    hits.sort(reverse=True)
    return [c for _, c in hits[:k]]   # may be empty → say 'I don't know'

slides.title: Make 'I don't know' enforceable
slides.code:
ctx = retrieve(question)
if not ctx:
    return "I don't have that information in my sources."

SYSTEM = (
    "Answer ONLY from the context. If the context does "
    "not contain the answer, reply 'I don't know.'"
)

right.title: Usual cause
items: Chunk too big / lost in middle

slides.title: The debugging order
bullets: Inspect retrieved chunks BEFORE blaming the LLM
Log similarity scores; add a threshold
Fix chunking first — it's the usual culprit
Keep context tight; rerank, then trim
Always ship an 'I don't know' path

slides.body: You've got the whole RAG pipeline — concept, stakes, mechanics, code, and traps. Next category starts tomorrow.

69-1.caption:
Before a document can be retrieved, it has to be cut into pieces. How you cut it quietly decides whether your RAG system finds the right answer or a useless fragment. ✂️

Chunking is the step that splits long documents into smaller passages that you embed and store. It sounds trivial — just slice the text — but the size and boundaries of those slices control what your retriever can ever return. A chunk is the smallest unit of knowledge your system can fetch.

This post builds the mental model: what a chunk is, why you can't just embed whole documents, and the vocabulary — size, overlap, boundaries — you'll use for every strategy that follows.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• what a chunk actually is
• why not just embed the whole document
• chunk size, overlap, and boundaries
• the unit-of-retrieval idea
• the families of chunking strategies

Get the foundation first. Save it. 👇

slides.title: The one-line definition
slides.body: Chunking is splitting your documents into smaller passages — chunks — that get embedded and stored separately. At query time the retriever returns whole chunks, never raw documents. The chunk is the atomic unit of knowledge your RAG system can ever fetch and show the model.

slides.title: Why not embed the whole doc?
slides.body: An embedding squeezes any length of text into one fixed-size vector. A 40-page PDF becomes a single blurry average of everything it says, so it matches almost any query weakly and none precisely. Split it into focused chunks and each vector captures one clear idea the retriever can find.

slides.title: Chunk size
slides.body: Size is how much text goes in each chunk, usually measured in tokens or characters. Small chunks are precise but lose context; large chunks carry context but dilute meaning and crowd the prompt. Most production systems land somewhere between 200 and 800 tokens per chunk.

slides.title: Overlap
slides.body: Overlap repeats a slice of text between neighboring chunks — say the last 50 tokens of one chunk start the next. It stops a sentence or idea that straddles a boundary from being torn in half and lost by both chunks. The cost is some duplication in your index.

slides.title: Boundaries
slides.body: A boundary is where one chunk ends and the next begins. Naive splitting cuts at a fixed character count, mid-word. Good chunking prefers natural boundaries — sentence ends, paragraphs, headings — so each chunk reads as a coherent, self-contained passage.

slides.title: The unit of retrieval
slides.body: Whatever you can retrieve is exactly one chunk. If the answer to a question is spread across two chunks, top-k retrieval has to grab both or the model gets half the story. This is why chunk boundaries effectively define the questions your system can answer well.

slides.title: The simplest possible chunker
slides.code:
def chunk(text, size=500, overlap=50):
    chunks = []
    start = 0
    while start < len(text):
        end = start + size
        chunks.append(text[start:end])
        start = end - overlap   # step back to overlap
    return chunks

parts = chunk(open("doc.txt").read())
print(len(parts), "chunks")

slides.title: The 30-second summary
bullets: A chunk = the smallest thing you can retrieve
Whole-doc embeddings are too blurry to match
Tune size, overlap, and boundaries together
Boundaries decide which questions you can answer

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 70.
slides.body: Next: why this one preprocessing step quietly makes or breaks retrieval quality.

69-2.caption:
You can pick the best embedding model and the fastest vector database and still get bad answers — because the real bottleneck was how you cut the text. 🎯

Chunking is the least glamorous step in RAG and the one with the most leverage. Retrieval can only return chunks you created, so a bad split silently caps the quality of everything downstream. No re-ranker, prompt tweak, or bigger model fully recovers from a chunk that split an answer in half.

This post is about stakes: why chunking is upstream of everything, how it trades off precision against context, and how it hits your latency and token bill.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• why chunking caps retrieval quality
• the precision vs context trade-off
• how chunk size affects your token bill
• split answers and lost context
• why no downstream fix saves a bad split

Understand the why before the how. Save it. 👇

slides.title: It caps everything downstream
slides.body: Your retriever can only return chunks you made during indexing. If the right answer never landed cleanly inside a single chunk, no query, re-ranker, or bigger LLM can retrieve it. Chunking sits at the top of the pipeline, so its mistakes propagate to every answer.

slides.title: The precision-context trade-off
slides.body: Smaller chunks match queries more sharply because each vector means one thing — but they may not carry enough surrounding context to actually answer. Larger chunks carry context but match vaguely and dilute the signal. Chunking is choosing where to sit on that curve for your data.

slides.title: It hits your token bill
slides.body: Every retrieved chunk is pasted into the prompt and billed as input tokens on every query. Oversized chunks multiply cost and latency for context the model never needed. Right-sized chunks keep prompts lean, fast, and cheap — chunking is a cost lever, not just a quality one.

slides.title: The split-answer failure
slides.body: A user asks about a refund window. The policy says '30 days' in one sentence and 'from delivery date' in the next — but your fixed-size split put them in different chunks. The retriever returns one, the model answers with half the rule, and it sounds confident while being wrong.

slides.title: Measuring the token cost
slides.code:
import tiktoken
enc = tiktoken.get_encoding("cl100k_base")

k = 4                      # chunks per query
size = 600                 # tokens per chunk
queries_per_day = 50_000

tokens = k * size * queries_per_day
print(f"{tokens:,} input tokens/day just for context")
# halving chunk size halves this line of your bill

slides.title: No downstream fix saves it
slides.body: Teams reach for re-rankers, hybrid search, and bigger context windows to rescue weak retrieval. Those help — but they reshuffle and pad the chunks you already made. If the answer was shredded across boundaries at indexing time, you're polishing broken pieces. Fix chunking first.

slides.title: Why this matters
bullets: Chunking caps the ceiling of retrieval quality
Size trades precision against context
Chunk size is a direct cost and latency lever
Downstream tricks can't unscramble a bad split

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 70.
slides.body: Next: open the hood on the actual splitting algorithms — fixed, recursive, semantic, and hierarchical.

69-3.caption:
There isn't one chunker — there's a ladder of them, each smarter about where text naturally breaks. Knowing the ladder lets you pick the cheapest one that actually works for your data. ⚙️

Under the hood, chunking strategies climb from dumb to context-aware: fixed-size cuts blindly by count, recursive splitting respects structure, semantic chunking watches for meaning shifts, and hierarchical chunking keeps a small piece for matching and a big piece for answering.

This post walks each strategy end to end — how it decides boundaries, what it costs, and when it's the right tool.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• fixed-size and the role of overlap
• recursive splitting on separators
• token-aware vs character-aware sizing
• semantic chunking by embedding distance
• parent-child / hierarchical retrieval

This is the real machinery. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Fixed-size splitting
slides.body: The baseline: walk the text and cut every N tokens or characters, adding a fixed overlap between neighbors. It's fast, predictable, and ignores meaning entirely — so it happily slices mid-sentence. Use it as a starting point, then climb the ladder if retrieval is fuzzy.

slides.title: Recursive splitting
slides.body: Try to split on the biggest natural separator first — double newlines for paragraphs. If a piece is still too big, recurse with the next separator: single newline, then sentence, then word. This keeps chunks under your size limit while breaking at the most natural boundary available.

slides.title: Recursive splitter (LangChain)
Count tokens, not characters
slides.body: Embedding and LLM limits are in tokens, not characters. Sizing by characters means your real token counts drift unpredictably across languages and code. Use the model's tokenizer to measure size so every chunk reliably fits the embedder's window and your prompt budget.

slides.title: Semantic chunking
slides.body: Split the text into sentences, embed each one, and walk through measuring the distance between consecutive sentences. Where the meaning jumps — a large distance — you place a boundary. Chunks then group sentences that are actually about the same thing, regardless of length.

slides.title: Hierarchical / parent-child
slides.body: Embed small child chunks for sharp matching, but store a pointer to a larger parent chunk. You retrieve on the precise child, then hand the model the surrounding parent for full context. This gets you precision and context at once, at the cost of a more complex store.

slides.title: Picking a strategy
bullets: Start fixed-size, measure, then climb
Recursive is the safe default for prose
Semantic shines on mixed-topic docs
Hierarchical when precision AND context both matter

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 70.
slides.body: Next: a complete, runnable chunking pipeline you can drop into your own RAG build.

69-4.caption:
Enough theory — here's chunking you can actually run. We'll go from a raw document to token-sized, overlapping, structure-aware chunks ready to embed. 💻

This post is a hands-on build. We start with the naive fixed-size splitter to feel the problem, switch to a token-aware recursive splitter that respects paragraphs and sentences, then add a lightweight semantic split so boundaries follow meaning. Every snippet is real and runs as-is.

By the end you'll have a function that turns any document into clean chunks with metadata, ready to drop straight into your vector store.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• naive fixed-size baseline
• token-aware recursive splitting
• attaching metadata to each chunk
• a minimal semantic chunker
• wiring chunks into a vector store

Copy, run, adapt. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 0. Install + sample doc
slides.code:
# pip install langchain-text-splitters tiktoken \
#   sentence-transformers numpy

document = open("handbook.md", encoding="utf-8").read()
print(len(document), "characters")

slides.title: 1. Naive fixed-size baseline
slides.code:
def fixed_chunks(text, size=500, overlap=50):
    out, start = [], 0
    while start < len(text):
        out.append(text[start:start + size])
        start += size - overlap
    return out

base = fixed_chunks(document)
print(base[0][-60:])   # note: often cuts mid-word

slides.title: 2. Token-aware recursive split
slides.code:
from langchain_text_splitters import (
    RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter,
)

splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter.from_tiktoken_encoder(
    encoding_name="cl100k_base",
    chunk_size=256,        # tokens, not chars
    chunk_overlap=32,
    separators=["\n\n", "\n", ". ", " ", ""],
)
chunks = splitter.split_text(document)
print(len(chunks), "chunks; first:\n", chunks[0][:120])

slides.title: 3. Attach metadata
4. A minimal semantic chunker
slides.code:
import re, numpy as np
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer

model = SentenceTransformer("all-MiniLM-L6-v2")
sents = re.split(r"(?<=[.!?])\s+", document)
vecs = model.encode(sents, normalize_embeddings=True)

chunks, cur = [], [sents[0]]
for i in range(1, len(sents)):
    sim = float(vecs[i] @ vecs[i - 1])
    if sim < 0.6:                 # meaning shifted
        chunks.append(" ".join(cur)); cur = []
    cur.append(sents[i])
chunks.append(" ".join(cur))
print(len(chunks), "semantic chunks")

slides.title: 5. Embed + store the chunks
slides.code:
import chromadb
client = chromadb.Client()
col = client.create_collection("handbook")

col.add(
    ids=[r["id"] for r in records],
    documents=[r["text"] for r in records],
    metadatas=[{"source": r["source"],
                "chunk_index": r["chunk_index"]}
               for r in records],
)
print(col.count(), "chunks indexed")

slides.title: 6. Sanity-check a retrieval
slides.code:
res = col.query(
    query_texts=["how many vacation days do I get?"],
    n_results=3,
)
for doc, meta in zip(res["documents"][0],
                     res["metadatas"][0]):
    print(meta["chunk_index"], ":", doc[:80])

slides.title: Make it production-ready
bullets: Log avg + max chunk token counts
Verify chunks fit your embedder's window
Store source + index for citations
Re-chunk and re-eval when docs change shape

slides.body: Next: the chunking mistakes that quietly wreck retrieval — and how to avoid each one.

69-5.caption:
Most broken RAG demos don't have a model problem or a database problem. They have a chunking problem nobody looked at. ⚠️

Chunking fails quietly. There's no error, no crash — just answers that are subtly incomplete or off-topic, and a team blaming the LLM. The mistakes are predictable: chunks too big or too small, splitting mid-idea, ignoring document structure, forgetting metadata, and never measuring the result.

This post is the field guide to those failure modes — what each one looks like, why it happens, and the concrete fix.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• one chunk size for every document type
• no overlap, so answers get severed
• ignoring structure like tables and headings
• chunks too large, drowning the answer
• shipping with zero retrieval evaluation

Dodge these before you scale. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 1. One size for everything
slides.body: A 500-token split that works for blog prose mangles code, chat logs, and tables. Different content has different natural units. Match the strategy to the document type instead of forcing one global chunk size across your entire corpus.

slides.title: 2. No overlap
slides.body: With zero overlap, an idea that spans a boundary gets cleanly severed — half in one chunk, half in the next, and neither is retrievable as a whole. A modest overlap of 10-20% of chunk size keeps cross-boundary context intact. Skipping it is the most common silent killer.

slides.title: 3. Ignoring structure
slides.body: Splitting raw text by count shreds tables into meaningless number soup, separates a heading from its section, and tears code blocks apart. Parse the structure first — keep tables, list items, and code blocks whole — then chunk within those units.

slides.title: 4. Chunks too large
slides.body: Huge chunks feel safe — surely the answer is in there. But a 2000-token chunk gives a blurry embedding that matches weakly, and even when retrieved it buries the one relevant sentence in noise the model has to wade through. Bigger is not safer.

slides.title: 5. Dropping metadata
slides.body: Chunks with no source, title, or position can't be cited and can't be filtered. You lose the ability to say where an answer came from or to scope retrieval to one document. Always carry source id, section, and chunk index alongside the text.

slides.title: Fix: structure-aware + metadata
6. Never evaluating
slides.body: The deepest mistake is shipping a chunk size you guessed and never checking it. Build a small set of real questions with known answers, then measure whether the right chunk lands in your top-k. Chunking is empirical — tune it against retrieval metrics, not vibes.

slides.title: The checklist
bullets: Vary strategy by document type
Always add 10-20% overlap
Respect tables, headings, code blocks
Keep chunks tight; carry metadata
Measure recall@k before scaling

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 70.
slides.body: Next up: embeddings and vector search — turning these clean chunks into fast, accurate retrieval.

70-1.caption:
Your whole RAG system is only as smart as the embedding model under it. Pick the wrong one and great chunks still never get found. 🧭

An embedding model is the function that turns text into a vector — a fixed list of numbers where similar meanings sit close together. 'Comparing embedding models' means comparing how well different models place text in that space: do they put a question near its true answer, and far from the distractors? Everything downstream — search quality, recall, cost, latency — flows from that one choice.

This post sets the foundation: what an embedding model actually is, and what 'better' even means before we start ranking them.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• what an embedding model really outputs
• why dimension and the vector space matter
• what makes one model 'better' than another
• dense vs sparse vs late-interaction families
• the axes every comparison really turns on

Get the mental model first. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Text in, vector out
slides.body: An embedding model maps a piece of text to a fixed-length vector — say 384, 768, or 1536 numbers. The numbers aren't meaningful alone; what matters is geometry. Texts with similar meaning land close together, so 'search' becomes 'find the nearest vectors.'

slides.title: Why this is the keystone
slides.body: In RAG, retrieval decides what the LLM ever sees. If the embedding model places a question far from its real answer, no amount of reranking or prompting recovers it — the right chunk simply never makes the candidate list. The model defines what 'similar' means.

slides.title: Dimension is a tradeoff
slides.body: More dimensions can capture finer distinctions but cost more memory, slower search, and a bigger index. A 384-dim model is light and fast; a 1536-dim model is richer but heavier. Bigger is not automatically better — only if the extra room is used well.

slides.title: What 'better' can mean
bullets: Retrieval quality — finds the right chunk
Domain fit — legal, code, medical, multilingual
Cost — API price or self-host compute
Latency — ms per embed at your volume
Dimension — index size and search speed

slides.title: An embedding in one call
slides.code:
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer
model = SentenceTransformer("all-MiniLM-L6-v2")

vec = model.encode("How do refunds work?")
print(vec.shape)        # (384,)
print(vec[:4])          # [-0.021, 0.044, 0.013, ...]

slides.title: Symmetric vs asymmetric
slides.body: Some models are trained for symmetric similarity (sentence vs sentence); others for asymmetric search (a short query vs a long passage). Using a sentence-similarity model for query→document retrieval is a classic mismatch — the task it was tuned for isn't the task you have.

slides.title: The 30-second summary
bullets: Embedding model = the definition of 'similar'
Output is a fixed vector; geometry is everything
Dimension trades quality for speed and size
Match the model to your task and domain

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 71.
slides.body: Next: why this single choice quietly decides whether your whole RAG system works.

70-2.caption:
Teams blame the LLM when answers are wrong. Half the time the embedding model never surfaced the right passage in the first place. 🎯

The embedding model matters because it sets the ceiling on retrieval. The LLM can only reason over chunks that made it into the prompt, and those chunks come from nearest-neighbor search in embedding space. A weak model on your domain means low recall — and low recall is invisible: the system answers confidently, just from the wrong context.

This post is about the stakes: why comparing and choosing carefully pays off in quality, cost, and trust.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• why retrieval recall caps answer quality
• how domain mismatch silently tanks results
• the real cost of dimension at scale
• latency and throughput at production volume
• why 'just use the biggest model' is a trap

Know why before you pick. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Retrieval is the ceiling
slides.body: Your LLM never sees a chunk that retrieval didn't return. If the embedding model ranks the correct passage at position 50 and you only fetch the top 5, that fact is gone. The best generator in the world can't answer from context it never received.

slides.title: Domain mismatch is silent
slides.body: A model that tops a general benchmark may flop on legal clauses, medical notes, or code. It still returns five chunks — they're just the wrong five. Nothing errors out. You only notice when someone audits the answers, which is exactly the worst time to find out.

slides.title: Dimension costs real money
slides.body: Every vector is stored and searched. A 1536-dim model uses 4x the memory and roughly 4x the search compute of a 384-dim one. Across millions of chunks that's the difference between one cheap node and an expensive cluster — for accuracy you may not even gain.

slides.title: Latency adds up per request
slides.body: You embed the query on every single request. A heavy model or a slow API adds tens to hundreds of milliseconds to each call, before search even starts. At scale, embedding throughput becomes a real bottleneck — and a real line on the bill.

slides.title: Migration cost is brutal
slides.body: Switching embedding models later means re-embedding your entire corpus and rebuilding the index — query and document vectors must come from the same model. Choosing well up front saves a full, expensive reprocessing pass once you're at millions of documents.

slides.title: Measuring recall, not vibes
What's actually at stake
bullets: Recall caps every answer downstream
Domain fit beats benchmark rank
Dimension drives storage + search cost
Query latency hits every request
Re-embedding later is painful

slides.title: The bottom line
slides.body: Comparing embedding models matters because it's the cheapest, highest-leverage lever in RAG. Get it right and recall, cost, and latency all improve at once. Get it wrong and you'll spend months tuning prompts to paper over a retrieval problem you can't see.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 71.
slides.body: Next: how embedding models are actually trained and compared, under the hood.

70-3.caption:
Two embedding models can both output 768 numbers and still rank your documents completely differently. The reason is how they were trained. ⚙️

Embedding models are trained with contrastive learning: pull matching (query, passage) pairs together in vector space and push mismatched pairs apart. The architecture, the training data, the pooling method, and whether the model expects a special prefix all change where text lands. Comparisons live or die on standardized benchmarks like MTEB — but those are a starting signal, not a verdict on your data.

This post opens the hood: how these models learn, how similarity is measured, and how the leaderboards actually work.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• contrastive training in one picture
• pooling: how tokens become one vector
• cosine similarity as the scoring rule
• instruction prefixes and why they matter
• what MTEB measures (and what it doesn't)

This is the real engine. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Contrastive learning
slides.body: The model trains on pairs: a query and its true passage are positives; random or hard-negative passages are negatives. Loss pulls positives together and pushes negatives apart. Over millions of pairs, semantically related text converges in space — that's what 'understands similarity' really means.

slides.title: Pooling: tokens → one vector
slides.body: A transformer outputs one vector per token. To get a single embedding you pool them — mean-pooling averages all token vectors, while CLS-pooling takes the special leading token. The choice is baked into the model; using the wrong pooling at inference quietly wrecks results.

slides.title: Cosine similarity scores it
slides.body: Once both query and passage are vectors, similarity is the cosine of the angle between them — 1.0 is identical direction, 0 is unrelated. Most models normalize vectors to length 1, which makes cosine and dot-product equivalent and search faster.

slides.title: Cosine similarity from scratch
slides.code:
import numpy as np

def cosine(a, b):
    a, b = np.array(a), np.array(b)
    return a @ b / (np.linalg.norm(a) * np.linalg.norm(b))

q  = model.encode("reset my password")
d1 = model.encode("how to recover your account login")
d2 = model.encode("our refund policy is 30 days")
print(cosine(q, d1), cosine(q, d2))   # 0.71  vs  0.18

slides.title: Instruction prefixes
slides.body: Many modern models (E5, BGE, GTE) expect a prefix like 'query:' or 'passage:' so they know the role of the text. Skip the prefix and you silently lose accuracy — the model was trained to read it. Always check the model card before encoding.

slides.title: Prefixes done right
slides.code:
# BGE / E5-style asymmetric encoding
q_vec = model.encode("query: how do refunds work",
                     normalize_embeddings=True)

docs = ["passage: " + d for d in documents]
d_vecs = model.encode(docs, normalize_embeddings=True)

slides.title: What MTEB measures
slides.body: MTEB is a public benchmark running models across many tasks — retrieval, clustering, classification, reranking — and dozens of datasets. It's the best cross-model signal available, but it averages over generic data. A high score means 'generally strong,' not 'best for your legal corpus.'

slides.title: Where behavior comes from
bullets: Training pairs decide what's 'similar'
Pooling decides how tokens combine
Cosine on normalized vectors is the score
Prefixes are part of the contract
Benchmarks rank generally, not for you

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 71.
slides.body: Next: a real, runnable bake-off — compare three models on your own data.

70-4.caption:
Stop arguing about which embedding model is best — run a bake-off on your own data in 40 lines. 💻

We'll load three models (a fast local MiniLM, a stronger BGE, and an OpenAI API model), embed a small labeled eval set, run nearest-neighbor search, and score recall@k and latency for each. Same data, same queries, head to head. The winner is whichever model finds your gold passages — not whichever tops a leaderboard.

This post is code-heavy on purpose. Copy it, point it at your corpus, and let the numbers decide.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• loading local + API embedding models
• a tiny labeled eval set to score against
• embedding documents and queries
• recall@k and latency per model
• printing a clean comparison table

Measure, don't guess. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 0. Install + imports
slides.code:
# pip install sentence-transformers numpy openai
import time
import numpy as np
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer
from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI()

slides.title: 1. A labeled eval set
slides.code:
# small but REAL: pulled from your own domain
docs = [
    "Refunds are issued within 30 days of purchase.",
    "Reset your password from the account settings page.",
    "Our API rate limit is 60 requests per minute.",
]
# each query maps to the index of its correct doc
queries = [
    ("how do I get my money back", 0),
    ("I forgot my login",         1),
    ("how many calls can I make",  2),
]

slides.title: 2. Wrap each model uniformly
3. Search + recall@k
4. Time + score every model
slides.code:
q_text = [q for q, _ in queries]
gold   = [g for _, g in queries]

def bench(name, embed_fn):
    t0 = time.perf_counter()
    dv = embed_fn(docs)
    qv = embed_fn(q_text)
    ms = (time.perf_counter() - t0) * 1000
    r  = recall_at_k(np.array(dv), np.array(qv), gold, k=2)
    return name, round(r, 2), round(ms, 1), dv.shape[1]

slides.title: 5. Run the bake-off
slides.code:
rows = [
    bench("MiniLM-L6",  lambda t: embed_local(mini, t)),
    bench("BGE-small",  lambda t: embed_local(
        bge, t, prefix="query: ")),
    bench("OpenAI-3-sm", embed_openai),
]
print(f"{'model':12} {'recall':>7} {'ms':>7} {'dim':>5}")
for name, r, ms, dim in rows:
    print(f"{name:12} {r:>7} {ms:>7} {dim:>5}")

lines.text: # tie on recall -> pick the cheap, fast one

slides.title: Make the test honest
bullets: Use 50-200 real query→doc pairs, not 3
Score recall@k AND nDCG if order matters
Apply each model's required prefix
Measure latency at your real batch size
Re-run on a held-out set before committing

slides.title: Reading the result
slides.body: When models tie on recall, latency, dimension, and cost break the tie — a 384-dim local model that matches a 1536-dim API model is the obvious win. When recall differs, that gap is your real signal; everything else is secondary to finding the right chunk.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 71.
slides.body: Next: the mistakes that quietly sabotage embedding comparisons — and how to avoid them.

70-5.caption:
Most 'embedding model X is better' claims are wrong — not because the model is bad, but because the comparison was. 🚫

Embedding mistakes are sneaky because nothing crashes. You mix models, skip a required prefix, normalize inconsistently, or trust a leaderboard, and the system keeps returning five chunks — just the wrong ones. The errors hide in recall you never measured.

This post is the field guide to the traps: the ones that silently wreck retrieval and make your comparison meaningless.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• mixing two models for query and docs
• trusting MTEB instead of your own data
• skipping the required instruction prefix
• inconsistent normalization and metrics
• ignoring cost, latency, and re-embedding

Avoid these and your results become trustworthy. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 1. Mixing models
slides.body: Embedding query and documents with different models — or a different version of the same model — puts them in incompatible spaces. Cosine scores become noise. Query and corpus must always be encoded by the exact same model with the same settings.

slides.title: The mixing trap
slides.code:
# WRONG: query and docs in different spaces
q  = openai_embed("reset password")        # 1536-dim
dv = minilm.encode(documents)              # 384-dim
# dimensions don't even match; scores meaningless

# RIGHT: one model for both sides
q  = minilm.encode("reset password", normalize_embeddings=True)
dv = minilm.encode(documents,       normalize_embeddings=True)

slides.title: 2. Trusting the leaderboard
slides.body: A top MTEB score means 'good on average across generic data.' Your legal contracts, support tickets, or codebase are not that data. Picking by leaderboard rank alone routinely loses to a lower-ranked model that happens to fit your domain.

slides.title: 3. Skipping the prefix
slides.body: E5, BGE, and GTE models are trained to see 'query:' / 'passage:' tags. Forget them and similarity scores drop measurably — but silently. People then conclude 'this model is weak' when they simply used it wrong. Read the model card first.

slides.title: 4. Inconsistent normalization
slides.body: Comparing cosine on normalized vectors from one model against raw dot-products from another isn't a fair test. Normalize everything the same way and use the same metric (recall@k, nDCG) across all models, or the ranking tells you nothing.

slides.title: 5. A toy eval set
slides.body: Three hand-picked queries prove nothing. With a tiny set, every model scores 100% and the test is useless. You need dozens to hundreds of real query→document pairs from production to separate models with any confidence.

slides.title: 6. Ignoring the operational cost
slides.body: Recall isn't the only number. A model that wins by 1% but costs 4x the storage, 8x the latency, and an API bill per query may lose in production. And remember: switching later means re-embedding everything. Factor that in up front.

slides.title: A fair comparison checklist
slides.code:
assert query_model is doc_model            # 1. same model
assert all(d.startswith(PREFIX) for d in docs)  # 3. prefix
vecs = model.encode(texts, normalize_embeddings=True)  # 4. norm
assert len(eval_set) >= 50                  # 5. real eval set
score = recall_at_k(vecs, qv, gold, k=5)    # 4. same metric
# also log: latency_ms, dim, $ / 1k embeds   # 6. cost

slides.title: Do this instead
bullets: One model encodes both query and docs
Test on YOUR data, not just MTEB
Apply each model's required prefix
Normalize + use one metric for all
Score recall@k on 50+ real pairs
Weigh cost, latency, and re-embedding

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 71.
slides.body: Next day: chunking strategies — how splitting your docs decides what can be retrieved at all.

71-1.caption:
Keyword search asks "do these exact words match?" Vector search asks "do these things mean the same?" 🧭

Vector search is the quiet engine behind RAG, semantic search, and recommendation systems. It finds what you mean, not just what you typed. The trick is simple to state: text, images, and audio get turned into a list of numbers — an embedding — that captures meaning, so "similar meaning" becomes "close together in space." Once meaning is a position, finding the most relevant result is just finding the nearest point, and that is something computers do incredibly fast.

This post builds the mental model you need before any of the math makes sense.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• what an embedding actually is, in plain terms
• why "meaning" gets turned into "distance"
• how this differs from keyword/lexical search
• what a vector database really stores
• where vector search slots into a RAG stack

Get the intuition first, then the details land easily. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Vector Search: Finding by Meaning, Not Words
What is vector search?
slides.body: Vector search finds items whose meaning is closest to your query. Text, images, or audio are converted into embeddings — fixed-length lists of numbers — and search becomes: which stored vectors sit nearest this query vector?

slides.title: An embedding in one sentence
slides.body: An embedding is a point in high-dimensional space (often 384–3072 numbers) where similar meanings land close together. "Dog" sits near "puppy" and far from "invoice" — no keyword overlap required.

slides.title: Keyword vs vector search
bullets: Keyword: matches literal tokens, fast, brittle to wording
Keyword: "car" misses "automobile"
Vector: matches meaning, robust to synonyms/paraphrase
Vector: "car" finds "automobile", "sedan", "vehicle"

slides.title: Why a number list?
slides.body: Computers can't compare "meaning" directly, but they can compare numbers fast. Once meaning is encoded as coordinates, similarity is just a distance calculation — and distance scales to millions of items.

slides.title: What a vector DB stores
bullets: The embedding vector itself
The original text/chunk (or a pointer to it)
Metadata: source, date, tags, author
An index for fast nearest-neighbor lookup

slides.title: An embedding is just floats
slides.code:
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer

model = SentenceTransformer("all-MiniLM-L6-v2")
vec = model.encode("a cute dog")
print(len(vec))   # 384
print(vec[:4])    # [0.021, -0.118, 0.073, ...]

slides.title: Mental model to keep
bullets: Same model embeds both query AND documents
Closer vector = more semantically related
"Relevant" is approximate, never exact
Vector search ranks; it does not understand

slides.title: The one-line takeaway
slides.body: Vector search = encode everything into the same meaning-space, then return the nearest neighbors to your query. It is the retrieval engine behind modern semantic search and RAG.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 72.
slides.body: Next up: why semantic retrieval changes what your AI can actually answer.

71-2.caption:
Your LLM is brilliant and frozen in time. Vector search is how you hand it the facts it never memorized. ⚡

Without retrieval, a model answers from stale training weights and confidently guesses on anything private, recent, or specific to your business. Vector search lets you ground every answer in your own documents at query time — turning "plausible-sounding" into "actually correct, and cited." Nothing about the model changes; you simply change what it reads before it answers. That one shift is what makes large language models safe to deploy on real knowledge.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• why keyword search quietly fails real users
• how semantic retrieval cuts hallucination
• why it beats fine-tuning for fresh facts
• the honest cost and latency tradeoffs
• where retrieval genuinely doesn't help

This is the "so what" behind every RAG demo you have seen. Save it. 👇

slides.title: The core problem
slides.body: LLMs only know what was in their training data, up to a cutoff. Ask about your internal wiki, last week's prices, or a customer's history and they confabulate. Vector search injects the right facts at query time.

slides.title: It reduces hallucination
slides.body: When the model is handed real, relevant passages, it paraphrases facts instead of inventing them. You also get citations — every claim can point back to a retrieved chunk a user can verify.

slides.title: vs Fine-tuning
bullets: Fine-tune: bakes behavior/style into weights
Fine-tune: slow, costly, hard to update
Vectors: swap a document, knowledge updates instantly
Use both: tune the style, retrieve the facts

slides.title: Real-world payoffs
bullets: Support bots that cite policy docs
Internal search that actually finds things
Recommendations by similarity
De-duplication and clustering at scale

slides.title: Grounding a prompt
slides.code:
hits = index.search(query_vec, top_k=3)
context = "\n".join(h.text for h in hits)
prompt = (
    f"Answer using ONLY this context:\n{context}\n\n"
    f"Question: {user_question}"
)
answer = llm(prompt)

slides.title: The tradeoffs (be honest)
bullets: Extra latency: embed + search before the LLM
Storage + infra cost for the index
Retrieval quality caps answer quality
Garbage chunks in = garbage answers out

slides.title: When NOT to use it
slides.body: If the answer is pure reasoning, math, or already in the model's general knowledge, retrieval adds noise and latency for nothing. Vector search shines on private, large, or fast-changing knowledge — not on "what is 17 times 4?"

slides.title: The bottom line
slides.body: Vector search turns a static model into a system that can answer about your data, today, with receipts. That shift — from memory to retrieval — is what makes LLMs deployable in the enterprise.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 72.
slides.body: Next: the actual mechanics — embeddings, distance metrics, and ANN indexes.

71-3.caption:
"Find similar" sounds magical. Underneath, it's really three concrete steps: embed, measure distance, then search smartly. 🔍

Vector search is geometry plus one clever shortcut. You turn data into vectors, define what "close" means with a distance metric, then use an approximate index so you never have to compare your query against every single item in the database. Skip that shortcut and search collapses under its own weight at scale; use it well and you get near-exact results in milliseconds across millions of vectors.

This is the engine room — the part most tutorials wave their hands at.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• the embed → index → query loop
• cosine vs dot product vs Euclidean distance
• why brute-force search doesn't scale
• how ANN indexes like HNSW cheat correctly
• the recall-vs-speed dial you control

Understand this and you can actually tune it. Save it. 👇

slides.title: The three-step loop
bullets: 1. Embed: data → vectors with a model
2. Index: store vectors for fast lookup
3. Query: embed the question, find nearest

slides.title: Distance metrics
bullets: Cosine: angle between vectors (direction)
Dot product: magnitude + direction
Euclidean (L2): straight-line distance
Cosine is most common for text embeddings

slides.title: Cosine similarity by hand
Brute force doesn't scale
slides.body: Exact search compares the query to every vector: 10M docs = 10M distance calcs per query. Accurate but far too slow for real-time. So we trade a sliver of accuracy for huge speed: approximate nearest neighbor (ANN).

slides.title: HNSW in plain words
slides.body: HNSW builds a layered graph of vectors. You enter at a sparse top layer, greedily hop toward the query, then drop into denser layers to refine. You touch a few hundred nodes instead of millions — near-exact results, blazing fast.

slides.title: ANN with FAISS
slides.code:
import faiss, numpy as np

d = 384
index = faiss.IndexHNSWFlat(d, 32)  # 32 = graph degree
index.add(doc_vectors)               # shape (N, d)
D, I = index.search(query_vec, k=5)  # top-5 ids + dists
print(I)

slides.title: Recall vs latency
slides.body: Recall = fraction of the true top-k you actually returned. Knobs like ef_search and graph degree trade recall for speed. Most apps tune for ~95%+ recall at single-digit milliseconds.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 72.
slides.body: Next: a full, runnable vector-search example end to end.

71-4.caption:
Enough theory — here's vector search you can actually run, in about 30 lines. 💻

We load an embedding model, embed a few documents, build an index, embed a query, and pull back the nearest matches with their scores. The win is watching a query with zero keyword overlap still surface the right document, purely on meaning. The shape stays identical whether you run FAISS locally in a notebook or a managed database like Pinecone or pgvector in production — only the scale and the operations change.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• install and load an embedding model
• embed a tiny document set correctly
• build a FAISS index in three lines
• run a real semantic query
• read and threshold the scores
• the pgvector / SQL equivalent

Copy it, run it, then point it at your own data. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 0. Install
slides.code: pip install sentence-transformers faiss-cpu numpy

slides.title: 1. Embed your documents
slides.code:
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer
import numpy as np

model = SentenceTransformer("all-MiniLM-L6-v2")
docs = [
    "Cats are independent pets.",
    "Dogs love going for walks.",
    "Python is a programming language.",
]
emb = model.encode(docs, normalize_embeddings=True)
emb = np.asarray(emb, dtype="float32")

slides.title: 2. Build the index
slides.code:
import faiss

d = emb.shape[1]            # 384 dims
index = faiss.IndexFlatIP(d)  # IP on normalized = cosine
index.add(emb)
print(index.ntotal)        # 3 vectors stored

slides.title: 3. Query semantically
slides.code:
q = model.encode(["which animal enjoys walks?"],
                 normalize_embeddings=True)
q = np.asarray(q, dtype="float32")

scores, ids = index.search(q, k=2)
for s, i in zip(scores[0], ids[0]):
    print(f"{s:.3f}  {docs[i]}")

slides.title: Reading the scores
bullets: Higher inner-product = more similar (normalized)
Top result should clearly beat the rest
Flat/low scores = weak or no good match
Set a threshold to drop irrelevant hits

slides.title: 4. The pgvector equivalent
slides.code:
-- once: CREATE EXTENSION vector;
-- column: embedding vector(384)

SELECT content,
       1 - (embedding <=> :query_vec) AS score
FROM documents
ORDER BY embedding <=> :query_vec   -- <=> = cosine dist
LIMIT 5;

slides.title: Make it production-ready
bullets: Persist the index (faiss.write_index)
Store doc text + metadata alongside ids
Batch-encode for throughput
Add metadata filters (date, tenant, tags)

slides.title: What you just built
slides.body: A self-contained semantic search: embed → index → query → ranked results. Swap the doc list for your real chunks and FAISS for a managed DB, and the same four steps power production RAG.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 72.
slides.body: Next: the mistakes that silently wreck retrieval quality.

71-5.caption:
Most "the AI gives bad answers" bugs aren't the LLM at all — they're broken retrieval upstream. ⚠️

Vector search fails quietly. The index always returns something, the scores look plausible, and nobody notices the wrong chunks came back until users start complaining. There is no stack trace, no exception, just slowly eroding answer quality that gets blamed on the model. These are the specific traps that cause it, and every one of them is preventable once you know to look.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• mixing embedding models (the number-one killer)
• chunking documents too big or too small
• ignoring metadata filters (an accuracy AND security bug)
• trusting raw similarity scores blindly
• forgetting to re-embed after a model swap
• when you actually need hybrid search + reranking

Fix these before you scale, not after. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 1. Mismatched embedding models
slides.body: Queries and documents MUST be embedded by the same model. Index with one model and query with another and the spaces don't align — results become near-random. This is the most common silent failure.

slides.title: Right vs wrong
slides.code:
# WRONG: different models, misaligned space
doc_vec = model_a.encode(doc)
q_vec   = model_b.encode(query)   # broken!

# RIGHT: one model for both
doc_vec = model.encode(doc)
q_vec   = model.encode(query)

slides.title: 2. Bad chunk sizes
bullets: Too big: many topics per vector, blurry meaning
Too small: fragments lose context
Sweet spot: ~200–500 tokens, with overlap
Chunk on semantic boundaries, not byte counts

slides.title: 3. Ignoring metadata filters
slides.body: Pure similarity will happily return last year's deleted policy or another tenant's data. Filter by date, tenant, status, or permissions FIRST, then rank by similarity. Relevance without filtering is a security and accuracy bug.

slides.title: 4. Trusting raw scores
slides.body: A top result always exists, even when nothing relevant was stored. Without a similarity threshold you feed garbage to the LLM. Always check: is the best score actually high, and does it clearly beat the runner-up?

slides.title: Guard with a threshold
slides.code:
scores, ids = index.search(q, k=5)
MIN = 0.35
hits = [(s, i) for s, i in zip(scores[0], ids[0])
        if s >= MIN]
if not hits:
    return "No relevant context found."  # don't guess

slides.title: 5. Forgetting to re-embed
slides.body: Upgrade your embedding model and the old vectors are now in a different space. You must re-embed the entire corpus, not just new docs. Mixed-vintage indexes degrade silently — version your embeddings.

slides.title: 6. Skipping hybrid + rerank
bullets: Pure vectors miss exact IDs, codes, names
Hybrid = vector + keyword (BM25) fusion
Rerank top-50 with a cross-encoder for precision
Cheap retrieve wide, then rerank narrow

slides.title: The checklist
slides.body: Same model both sides. Smart chunks with overlap. Filter then rank. Threshold the scores. Re-embed on upgrades. Add hybrid + rerank when precision matters. Nail these and retrieval stops being your bottleneck.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 72.
slides.body: Tomorrow: building on vector search toward full RAG pipelines.

72-1.caption:
Vector search finds what you mean. Keyword search finds the exact word you typed. Hybrid search refuses to choose. 🔀

Hybrid search runs two retrievers side by side: BM25, a classic keyword scorer that rewards rare exact terms, and dense vector search, which matches on meaning. Each is strong exactly where the other is weak — BM25 nails product codes, names, and acronyms; vectors nail paraphrases and synonyms. Hybrid fuses their results so a query can win on either signal.

This post sets the foundation: what BM25 and vector search each actually do, and why combining them beats either alone.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• what BM25 really scores
• what dense vectors really match
• where each one fails on its own
• what 'hybrid' actually fuses
• the mental model to carry forward

Get the two halves first. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Two retrievers, one result list
slides.body: Hybrid search means querying a sparse keyword retriever (BM25) and a dense vector retriever at the same time, then merging their ranked lists into one. A document can rank high because it shares exact words, because it shares meaning, or both.

slides.title: BM25: exact terms, weighted
slides.body: BM25 scores how well a document matches the query's literal words. It rewards rare terms (via IDF), counts term frequency with diminishing returns, and penalizes long documents. No embeddings, no GPU — just tuned word statistics that have anchored search for decades.

slides.title: Vectors: meaning, not letters
slides.body: Dense retrieval embeds query and documents into a shared vector space, then finds nearest neighbors by cosine similarity. 'Money back' lands near 'refund' even with zero shared words. It captures intent and paraphrase — but can drift on exact tokens it never saw in training.

slides.title: Each is strong where the other is weak
Why not just pick one?
slides.body: Real queries mix both kinds of signal. "How do I fix error E-2041 on checkout" needs the exact code (BM25) and the intent (vector). Choose one retriever and you systematically lose half of these queries. Hybrid lets each query win on whichever signal is strongest.

slides.title: Both scores, side by side
slides.code:
from rank_bm25 import BM25Okapi
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer, util

docs = ["Reset SKU-449X from the device menu.",
        "How to get your money back within 30 days."]

bm25 = BM25Okapi([d.split() for d in docs])
print(bm25.get_scores("reset SKU-449X".split()))  # [1.9, 0.0]

m = SentenceTransformer("all-MiniLM-L6-v2")
q = m.encode("refund", convert_to_tensor=True)
d = m.encode(docs, convert_to_tensor=True)
print(util.cos_sim(q, d))                          # [[0.12, 0.61]]

slides.title: Sparse vs dense vectors
slides.body: BM25 produces a sparse vector: one dimension per vocabulary word, mostly zeros. Embeddings are dense: a few hundred numbers, all nonzero. Sparse encodes 'which exact words'; dense encodes 'what it means.' Hybrid keeps both representations instead of collapsing to one.

slides.title: The 30-second model
bullets: Hybrid = keyword retriever + vector retriever, fused
BM25 = exact terms, rarity-weighted
Vectors = meaning, nearest neighbors
Each covers the other's blind spot
Fusion decides the final order

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 73.
slides.body: Next: why hybrid quietly outperforms pure vector search on real production queries.

72-2.caption:
Everyone upgraded to vector search and quietly broke search for product codes, error numbers, and people's names. 😬

Pure vector search is seductive — it handles paraphrase beautifully in demos. Then a user types an exact SKU, an account ID, or a rare acronym, and the embedding model, which never really saw that token, returns confident garbage. Pure keyword search has the opposite failure: it can't match a synonym to save its life. Hybrid matters because production traffic contains both kinds of query, mixed, all day.

This post is about the stakes: why fusing BM25 and vectors lifts recall, robustness, and trust in ways neither retriever reaches alone.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• the exact-match failure of pure vectors
• the synonym failure of pure keywords
• why recall is the metric that pays
• robustness to weird, out-of-domain queries
• when hybrid is worth the extra moving parts

Know why before you build it. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Vectors fumble exact tokens
slides.body: Embedding models smear text into meaning, which is exactly wrong for an identifier. "Order #88231" or "part GTX-1660" carries no semantics — the digits ARE the signal. A dense model often ranks a vaguely-related passage above the document that literally contains the ID. BM25 never makes that mistake.

slides.title: Keywords fumble meaning
slides.body: Pure BM25 needs shared words. Ask "how do I cancel my plan" against a doc titled "terminating your subscription" and BM25 scores it near zero — no overlapping terms. The user is describing the exact right page and keyword search throws it away. Vectors catch this instantly.

slides.title: Recall is the metric that pays
slides.body: In RAG, the LLM can only answer from chunks retrieval surfaced. A query that fails on vectors but succeeds on BM25 (or vice versa) is a query hybrid saves. Across thousands of real queries, those rescued cases are the difference between 'usually right' and 'reliable.'

slides.title: Robust to queries you didn't plan for
slides.body: Users paste log lines, half-remembered phrases, internal codenames, typos. A single retriever has one failure mode; hybrid has two retrievers with different blind spots, so a weird query that breaks one often still lands on the other. Robustness comes from the redundancy.

slides.title: What you gain vs what it costs
When it's worth it
slides.body: Hybrid earns its keep when your corpus mixes natural language with identifiers — docs with part numbers, code, error codes, names, legal citations. If your content is pure prose and queries are pure prose, plain vectors may be enough. Most real corpora aren't that clean.

slides.title: The query a single retriever loses
slides.code:
# Pure vector misses the exact ID; pure BM25 misses the intent.
query = "why is invoice INV-7741 still unpaid"

# vector top-1: "understanding your billing cycle"   (wrong doc)
# bm25  top-1: "Invoice INV-7741 — status: overdue"   (right doc!)
#
# hybrid fuses both, so INV-7741 surfaces AND billing
# context comes along for the LLM to reason over.

slides.title: What's actually at stake
bullets: Pure vectors break on IDs & codes
Pure keywords break on synonyms
Hybrid recall beats either alone
Two blind spots cancel out
Worth it when content mixes prose + tokens

slides.title: The bottom line
slides.body: Hybrid search matters because production queries aren't tidy. They're exact and fuzzy, technical and casual, in the same stream. Running both retrievers and fusing them turns two partial solutions into one that covers the query space — at the price of a little extra plumbing.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 73.
slides.body: Next: how BM25, dense search, and the fusion step actually work under the hood.

72-3.caption:
Two retrievers give you two scores on totally different scales. The real magic of hybrid search is how you fairly combine them. ⚙️

BM25 returns scores like 14.2; cosine similarity returns 0.71. You can't just add them — the bigger scale would dominate. So hybrid systems either normalize both scores and take a weighted sum, or ignore scores entirely and fuse by rank with Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF). Under the hood, BM25 leans on IDF and term frequency, dense search leans on approximate nearest-neighbor indexes, and fusion reconciles the two.

This post opens the hood: the BM25 formula, the ANN lookup, and the two dominant fusion strategies.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• the BM25 scoring formula, demystified
• how dense ANN retrieval finds neighbors
• why raw scores can't just be added
• weighted score fusion vs RRF
• the full hybrid pipeline end to end

This is the real engine. Save it. 👇

slides.title: BM25, in plain terms
slides.body: For each query term, BM25 multiplies three things: IDF (rare words count more), a saturating term-frequency (the 5th occurrence adds little), and a length penalty (long docs don't win just by being long). Sum across query terms for the document's score.

slides.title: The BM25 formula
slides.code:
# score(D, Q) = sum over query terms t of:
#
#                       f(t,D) * (k1 + 1)
#   IDF(t) * -----------------------------------------
#            f(t,D) + k1 * (1 - b + b * |D| / avgdl)
#
# f(t,D)  = times t appears in doc D
# |D|     = length of D,  avgdl = average doc length
# k1 ~1.2 controls TF saturation;  b ~0.75 controls
#          length normalization

slides.title: Dense side: ANN search
slides.body: Documents are pre-embedded into vectors and stored in an index (HNSW, IVF). At query time the query is embedded once, then the index returns approximate nearest neighbors by cosine similarity in sublinear time — you don't compare against every document, just a smart subset.

slides.title: The scale problem
slides.body: BM25 outputs unbounded positive scores (0 to ~30+); cosine sits in [-1, 1] or [0, 1]. Add them raw and BM25 swamps the vector signal purely because its numbers are bigger. You must either normalize both to a common range, or drop scores and fuse by rank instead.

slides.title: Reciprocal Rank Fusion
Why RRF is the default
slides.body: RRF needs no score normalization and barely any tuning — k=60 works almost everywhere. It rewards documents that rank well in either list, so a doc that's #1 on BM25 and absent from vectors still surfaces. That robustness is why most engines ship RRF as the default hybrid mode.

slides.title: Where the behavior comes from
bullets: BM25 = IDF × saturating TF × length penalty
Dense side = ANN over precomputed vectors
Raw scores live on different scales
Normalize-and-weight OR rank-fuse (RRF)
Optional reranker reorders the fused top-k

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 73.
slides.body: Next: a complete, runnable hybrid search you can paste and point at your own docs.

72-4.caption:
Hybrid search sounds heavy. It's about 50 lines: BM25, embeddings, and a fusion function. Let's build the whole thing. 💻

We'll index a small document set two ways — a BM25 keyword index and a dense vector index — run a query through both, and fuse the rankings with Reciprocal Rank Fusion. No vector database required; just rank_bm25, sentence-transformers, and numpy so you can see every moving part. Then you swap in your own corpus and a real ANN index.

This post is code-heavy on purpose. Copy it, run it, and watch a query win on the signal that fits it.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• building the BM25 index
• building the dense vector index
• retrieving top-k from each side
• fusing the two lists with RRF
• one search() call that ties it together

Build it, don't just read it. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 0. Install + imports
slides.code:
# pip install rank-bm25 sentence-transformers numpy
import numpy as np
from rank_bm25 import BM25Okapi
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer

model = SentenceTransformer("all-MiniLM-L6-v2")

slides.title: 1. The corpus
slides.code:
docs = [
    "Reset device SKU-449X from the settings menu.",
    "Refunds are issued within 30 days of purchase.",
    "Cancel your subscription under Billing > Plan.",
    "Invoice INV-7741 is currently marked overdue.",
    "Recover a forgotten password via the login page.",
]

slides.title: 2. Build both indexes
slides.code:
# Sparse: BM25 over tokenized docs
tokenized = [d.lower().split() for d in docs]
bm25 = BM25Okapi(tokenized)

# Dense: precompute normalized embeddings
doc_vecs = model.encode(docs, normalize_embeddings=True)

slides.title: 3. Retrieve top-k from each side
4. Fuse with RRF
5. One hybrid search() call
slides.code:
def search(query, k=3):
    sparse = bm25_topk(query, k=10)
    dense  = vector_topk(query, k=10)
    fused  = rrf([sparse, dense])
    return [docs[i] for i in fused[:k]]

for d in search("why is INV-7741 unpaid"):
    print(d)
# -> 'Invoice INV-7741 is currently marked overdue.'  (BM25 win)
# -> 'Refunds are issued within 30 days of purchase.'  (vector ctx)

slides.title: Taking it to production
bullets: Swap numpy dot for an ANN index (FAISS/HNSW)
Use Elasticsearch/OpenSearch for real BM25
Or use one engine with native hybrid (Weaviate, Qdrant)
Add a cross-encoder reranker on the fused top-k
Tune k in RRF (start at 60)

slides.title: Native hybrid in one engine
slides.code:
# Qdrant example: server fuses sparse + dense for you
from qdrant_client import QdrantClient, models

client.query_points(
    collection_name="docs",
    prefetch=[
        models.Prefetch(query=sparse_vec, using="bm25", limit=20),
        models.Prefetch(query=dense_vec,  using="text", limit=20),
    ],
    query=models.FusionQuery(fusion=models.Fusion.RRF),
    limit=3,
)

slides.title: What you just built
bullets: Two independent indexes, one query
Each returns its own ranked top-k
RRF merges by rank, no score scaling
search() returns the fused best docs

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 73.
slides.body: Next: the mistakes that quietly make hybrid search worse than either retriever alone.

72-5.caption:
Most broken hybrid search isn't broken retrievers — it's a broken fusion step bolted on without measuring anything. 🧨

Hybrid has more moving parts than single-retriever search, so it has more ways to silently degrade. People add BM25 and cosine scores raw, fetch a tiny top-k before fusing, weight the two sources by gut feeling, or skip evaluation entirely and assume 'two is better than one.' Sometimes hybrid is genuinely worse than the better single retriever — and you'll never know without a labeled eval set.

This post is the failure-mode map: the traps that turn a promising hybrid setup into a regression.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• adding scores on different scales
• fusing too few candidates
• guessing the fusion weight
• mismatched tokenization & analyzers
• shipping without measuring recall

Avoid the landmines. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Adding raw scores
slides.body: BM25 (0–30+) and cosine (0–1) live on different scales. Sum them directly and BM25 dominates by magnitude alone — your 'hybrid' is really just BM25 with noise. Fix: min-max normalize each score before weighting, or use RRF, which ignores raw scores entirely.

slides.title: Fusing too few candidates
slides.body: If each retriever only returns top-3 before fusion, a document that's #1 on vectors but #6 on BM25 never gets a chance to combine. Fetch a generous pool (top-20 to top-100) from EACH retriever, fuse, then trim to the final k. Fusion can't rescue what it never saw.

slides.title: Fetch wide, then trim
slides.code:
# WRONG: fuse only 3 from each — good docs get truncated early
fused = rrf([bm25_topk(q, 3), vector_topk(q, 3)])[:3]

# RIGHT: fetch a wide pool, fuse, THEN cut to final k
fused = rrf([bm25_topk(q, 50), vector_topk(q, 50)])[:3]

slides.title: Guessing the weight α
slides.body: Picking α=0.5 (or any number) by feel is a coin flip. The right balance depends on your corpus and query mix. Either sweep α on a labeled eval set and pick the best, or sidestep the problem with RRF, which needs no per-source weight at all.

slides.title: Mismatched tokenization
slides.body: BM25 quality hinges on the analyzer — lowercasing, stemming, stopword and punctuation handling. If 'SKU-449X' is split or stripped differently at index time vs query time, exact-match retrieval silently fails. Use the SAME analyzer for indexing and querying.

slides.title: Decision: do you even need hybrid?

yes.leaf: Vectors may suffice; test hybrid anyway

slides.title: Shipping without measuring
slides.body: 'Two retrievers must beat one' is an assumption, not a result. On some corpora a tuned single retriever wins, and a sloppy fusion drags it down. Build a small labeled eval set and compare BM25-only, vector-only, and hybrid on recall@k before you commit.

slides.title: Prove hybrid actually helps
slides.code:
def recall_at_k(retrieve, eval_set, k=10):
    hits = sum(gold in retrieve(q, k) for q, gold in eval_set)
    return hits / len(eval_set)

print("bm25  ", recall_at_k(bm25_topk,   eval_set))
print("vector", recall_at_k(vector_topk, eval_set))
print("hybrid", recall_at_k(
    lambda q, k: rrf([bm25_topk(q,50), vector_topk(q,50)])[:k],
    eval_set))
# only ship hybrid if it actually beats both

slides.title: Forgetting the reranker
slides.body: Fusion gives a good candidate set, not a perfectly ordered one. RRF doesn't know which fused doc truly best answers the query. A cross-encoder reranker over the fused top-20 often adds more precision than any fusion tuning — and it's the cheapest big win left.

slides.title: The checklist
bullets: Normalize scores or use RRF — never add raw
Fetch wide from each side before fusing
Tune α on data, or skip it with RRF
Same analyzer at index and query time
Measure recall before shipping hybrid
Add a reranker on the fused top-k

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 73.
slides.body: Next: rerankers — squeezing precision out of your retrieved candidates.

73-1.caption:
Your vector search returns the top 50. But the truly best answer is sitting at rank 17. A reranker is what drags it back to the top. 🎯

Bi-encoders (the embedding models behind vector search) score query and document separately, then compare. Fast, but they never let the two texts actually "read" each other. A cross-encoder does the opposite: it feeds the query AND document into one model together and outputs a single relevance score. Slower, but far more accurate. The standard pattern is two stages — retrieve cheaply with vectors, then rerank the survivors with a cross-encoder.

This post builds the mental model before any code.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• what reranking actually is
• bi-encoder vs cross-encoder, plainly
• why "reading together" beats "compare separately"
• where the reranker sits in a RAG stack
• what a relevance score really means

Get the intuition first. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Reranking: Fixing the Order of Your Search Results
What is reranking?
slides.body: Reranking is a second pass over your search results. A first-stage retriever pulls a rough top-N (say 50) fast. A reranker then re-scores just those N candidates with a stronger, slower model and reorders them by true relevance.

slides.title: What is a cross-encoder?
slides.body: A cross-encoder takes the query and a document TOGETHER as one input and outputs a single relevance score. Because both texts attend to each other inside the model, it captures fine-grained matching that separate embeddings miss.

slides.title: Why read them together?
slides.body: When the model sees query and document in the same forward pass, attention can link "refund" in the question to "return policy" in the passage directly. A bi-encoder squashes each side into a fixed vector first, losing that token-level interaction.

slides.title: What the score means
bullets: A single number per (query, doc) pair
Higher = more relevant to THIS query
Not a probability unless normalized
Only meaningful for ranking, not absolute truth

slides.title: A cross-encoder in 4 lines
slides.code:
from sentence_transformers import CrossEncoder

model = CrossEncoder("cross-encoder/ms-marco-MiniLM-L-6-v2")
score = model.predict([("how to reset password",
                        "Go to settings to change your password.")])
print(score)   # e.g. [8.92] — higher means more relevant

slides.title: The two-stage idea
slides.body: Stage 1 (retrieve) trades accuracy for speed so you can scan everything. Stage 2 (rerank) trades speed for accuracy on the few survivors. Together you get both: broad reach AND precise ordering.

slides.title: Mental model to keep
bullets: Retriever finds candidates; reranker orders them
Cross-encoder never sees the full corpus
It only re-scores what stage 1 hands it
Better recall upstream = better reranking downstream

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 74.
slides.body: Next: why this second pass is worth the extra latency.

73-2.caption:
Plain vector search feels good in a demo and then quietly serves the wrong passage to your LLM in production. Reranking is the cheapest fix you're not using. ⚡

Embedding retrieval is optimized for recall — getting the right answer somewhere in the top-50. It is not optimized for putting it at rank 1. But your LLM usually only reads the top 3-5 chunks. If the best passage sits at rank 12, the model never sees it and hallucinates instead. A cross-encoder reorders those candidates so the genuinely best context lands where the model actually looks.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• why high recall still gives bad answers
• how reranking lifts precision@k
• the impact on RAG answer quality
• the honest latency and cost tradeoff
• when reranking is NOT worth it

This is the "so what" behind every retrieval upgrade. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Recall is not enough
slides.body: Vector search is tuned to get the answer somewhere in the top-50. But your LLM reads only the top 3-5 chunks. If the best passage is ranked 12th, it never reaches the model — and you get a confident wrong answer.

slides.title: Recall vs precision@k
bullets: Recall@50: is the answer in the pool at all?
Precision@5: are the TOP 5 actually good?
Retrievers maximize recall, not top ordering
Rerankers maximize precision at small k

slides.title: It directly cuts hallucination
slides.body: An LLM grounds its answer in whatever chunks you feed it. Hand it the right passage at rank 1 and it paraphrases the truth. Bury that passage and the model fills the gap by inventing. Reranking changes what the model reads.

slides.title: Lift the best chunk to the top
slides.code:
candidates = vector_search(query, top_k=50)   # high recall
pairs = [(query, c.text) for c in candidates]
scores = reranker.predict(pairs)
ranked = sorted(zip(candidates, scores),
                key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)
context = [c.text for c, s in ranked[:5]]      # precise top-5

slides.title: The honest tradeoff
bullets: Adds 50-300ms per query (model forward pass)
Cost scales with N candidates reranked
Quality is capped by stage-1 recall
Rerank 20-100 candidates, not 1000s

slides.title: When NOT to rerank
slides.body: If your retriever already returns one obvious answer, or latency is brutal and recall is fine, reranking adds cost for little gain. It pays off when results are many, similar, and ordering actually matters — exactly the RAG case.

slides.title: Real-world payoffs
bullets: Support bots cite the correct policy, not a cousin
Search results that feel "smart", not keyword-y
