Fewer chunks needed = cheaper LLM calls
Higher answer accuracy with no model retraining

slides.title: The bottom line
slides.body: Reranking is the highest-leverage, lowest-effort upgrade to a RAG pipeline: no retraining, no new data, just a second scoring pass that puts the right context where the model can use it.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 74.
slides.body: Next: the actual mechanics — tokens, attention, and the score head.

73-3.caption:
"It re-scores the results" hides a lot. Here's what a cross-encoder actually does to a query and a document, token by token. 🔍

A cross-encoder is a transformer (often BERT-sized) with a tiny regression head bolted on top. You concatenate the query and document into one sequence with a separator, run a single forward pass, and read a relevance score off the [CLS] token. The magic is full cross-attention: every query token can attend to every document token, which is exactly what a bi-encoder gives up for speed.

This is the engine room most tutorials skip.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• how query + doc become one input
• why cross-attention is the whole point
• the [CLS] token and the score head
• how these models are trained (MS MARCO)
• the cost math: why it's O(N) forward passes

Understand this and you can tune it. Save it. 👇

slides.title: The forward pass, step by step
bullets: 1. Concatenate: [CLS] query [SEP] document [SEP]
2. Tokenize the joined sequence
3. Run one transformer forward pass
4. Read score from [CLS] via a linear head

slides.title: Cross-attention is the point
slides.body: Inside the model, every query token can attend to every document token and vice versa. The word "refund" can directly align to "money back" in the passage. A bi-encoder collapses each side to a vector first, so this interaction never happens.

slides.title: The [CLS] token + head
slides.body: BERT prepends a special [CLS] token whose final hidden state summarizes the whole pair. A single linear layer maps that vector to one number — the relevance logit. Training nudges this score up for relevant pairs, down for irrelevant ones.

slides.title: What predict() does under the hood
slides.code:
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForSequenceClassification
import torch

tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("cross-encoder/ms-marco-MiniLM-L-6-v2")
mdl = AutoModelForSequenceClassification.from_pretrained("cross-encoder/ms-marco-MiniLM-L-6-v2")

feat = tok(["how to reset password"], ["Open settings to change it."],
           padding=True, truncation=True, return_tensors="pt")
with torch.no_grad():
    logit = mdl(**feat).logits   # shape (1, 1) -> the relevance score
print(logit)

slides.title: How they are trained
bullets: Data: MS MARCO — query + relevant/irrelevant passages
Positives: real relevant passages
Negatives: hard negatives from a retriever
Loss: rank positives above negatives

slides.title: The cost math: O(N) passes
slides.code:
# Bi-encoder: 1 query embed + cached doc vectors
#   -> 1 forward pass total at query time
#
# Cross-encoder: must score EACH pair fresh
#   -> N forward passes for N candidates
N = 50
latency_ms = N * per_pair_ms   # why you rerank top-N, not the corpus

slides.title: Why you can't precompute
slides.body: The score depends on the query and document seen jointly. Change the query and every pair must be re-scored. That is why cross-encoders run at query time on a small candidate set — never offline over the whole index.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 74.
slides.body: Next: a full, runnable two-stage retrieve-then-rerank pipeline.

73-4.caption:
Enough theory — here's a real two-stage retrieve-then-rerank pipeline you can run in a notebook today. 💻

We embed a few documents, do fast vector retrieval to get a candidate pool, then reorder that pool with a cross-encoder. The payoff is watching a document that retrieval ranked 3rd jump to rank 1 once the cross-encoder actually reads it against the query. The shape is identical whether you use FAISS locally or a managed retriever plus a hosted reranker in production.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• install the two models you need
• fast bi-encoder retrieval for candidates
• score every candidate with a cross-encoder
• sort and take the precise top-k
• batch for speed and read the scores
• the production shape with a hosted reranker

Copy it, run it, point it at your data. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 0. Install
slides.code: pip install sentence-transformers faiss-cpu numpy

slides.title: 1. Stage one: fast retrieval
slides.code:
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer
import numpy as np, faiss

bi = SentenceTransformer("all-MiniLM-L6-v2")
docs = [
    "Reset your password from the account settings page.",
    "Our refund policy allows returns within 30 days.",
    "To change your password, open settings and click security.",
    "Shipping is free on orders over fifty dollars.",
]
emb = np.asarray(bi.encode(docs, normalize_embeddings=True), "float32")
index = faiss.IndexFlatIP(emb.shape[1])
index.add(emb)

slides.title: 2. Get candidate pool
slides.code:
query = "how do I change my login password?"
q = np.asarray(bi.encode([query], normalize_embeddings=True), "float32")

top_n = 4
D, I = index.search(q, top_n)        # fast, high recall
candidates = [docs[i] for i in I[0]]
for rank, (i, d) in enumerate(zip(I[0], D[0]), 1):
    print(rank, round(float(d), 3), docs[i])

slides.title: 3. Stage two: rerank
slides.code:
from sentence_transformers import CrossEncoder

ce = CrossEncoder("cross-encoder/ms-marco-MiniLM-L-6-v2")
pairs = [(query, c) for c in candidates]   # query paired with each
scores = ce.predict(pairs)                 # one float per pair

reranked = sorted(zip(candidates, scores),
                  key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)
for doc, s in reranked:
    print(round(float(s), 2), doc)

slides.title: 4. Take the precise top-k
slides.code:
k = 2
best = [doc for doc, s in reranked[:k]]
context = "\n".join(best)

prompt = (
    f"Answer using ONLY this context:\n{context}\n\n"
    f"Question: {query}"
)
# feed prompt to your LLM -> grounded answer

slides.title: 5. Batch for speed
slides.code:
# predict() already batches; control it explicitly:
scores = ce.predict(pairs, batch_size=32)

# GPU helps a lot for reranking:
ce = CrossEncoder("cross-encoder/ms-marco-MiniLM-L-6-v2",
                  device="cuda")

slides.title: Reading the scores
bullets: Cross-encoder scores are logits, not 0-1
Higher = more relevant; compare WITHIN a query
Don't compare scores across different queries
Optionally apply a cutoff to drop weak chunks

slides.title: 6. Production shape (hosted)
slides.code:
import cohere
co = cohere.Client(api_key="...")

resp = co.rerank(
    model="rerank-english-v3.0",
    query=query,
    documents=candidates,
    top_n=2,
)
for r in resp.results:
    print(r.relevance_score, candidates[r.index])

slides.title: The takeaway
slides.body: Retrieve wide and cheap, rerank narrow and accurate. The same five-line second stage works whether your reranker is a local cross-encoder or a hosted API — only scale and ops change.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 74.
slides.body: Next: the mistakes that quietly wreck a reranking setup.

73-5.caption:
Reranking is easy to add and easy to get subtly wrong. Most broken setups fail in the same five ways. 🚧

A cross-encoder can only reorder what your retriever hands it — so if stage one misses the answer, no reranker can save you. People also rerank too many candidates, blow up latency, compare scores across queries as if they mean the same thing, and forget that document truncation silently throws away the relevant sentence.

These aren't exotic edge cases. They're the defaults you trip over the first week.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• reranking a pool that's missing the answer
• re-scoring thousands of candidates
• treating logit scores as probabilities
• truncation cutting off the relevant part
• ignoring the latency budget entirely

Learn the failure modes before you ship. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 1. Bad stage-1 recall
slides.body: A reranker only reorders the candidates it receives. If the right document isn't in your retriever's top-N, no amount of reranking conjures it. Fix recall first — widen top-N or improve embeddings — then rerank.

slides.title: 2. Reranking too many
slides.body: Cross-encoders cost one forward pass PER candidate. Rerank the whole 1000-doc pool and latency explodes. Retrieve wide for recall, but rerank only the top 20-100. The candidate count is your main latency dial.

slides.title: 3. Misreading the scores
slides.body: Cross-encoder outputs are logits, not calibrated probabilities. A score of 8 isn't "80% relevant." They're only meaningful for ranking within one query — never compare scores across different queries as if they share a scale.

slides.title: Normalize only if you must
slides.code:
import numpy as np

def sigmoid(x):
    return 1 / (1 + np.exp(-x))

# Maps a logit to 0-1 for a rough cutoff, NOT a true probability
scores = ce.predict(pairs)
conf = sigmoid(scores)
keep = [c for c, p in zip(candidates, conf) if p > 0.5]

slides.title: 4. Silent truncation
slides.body: Cross-encoders have a max sequence length (often 512 tokens). A long document gets truncated, and the one relevant sentence may be the part that's cut. Chunk documents before reranking so each candidate fits the window.

slides.title: Guard the input length
slides.code:
ce = CrossEncoder("cross-encoder/ms-marco-MiniLM-L-6-v2",
                  max_length=512)   # be explicit

# Rerank CHUNKS, not whole documents, so the
# relevant passage isn't truncated away:
pairs = [(query, chunk) for chunk in candidate_chunks]
scores = ce.predict(pairs)

slides.title: 5. Ignoring the latency budget
slides.body: Reranking adds a real, measured cost on the critical path before the LLM even starts. Measure it. If 100 candidates is too slow, cut to 50 or use a smaller/distilled reranker. Don't discover this in production.

slides.title: The checklist
bullets: Verify recall BEFORE adding a reranker
Rerank 20-100 candidates, not thousands
Treat scores as ranks, not probabilities
Chunk so candidates fit max_length
Measure added latency on the real path

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 74.
slides.body: Next up: a new topic in the 100 Days of AI series.

74-1.caption:
Your RAG demo looked great. Then it shipped, and nobody could tell if it was actually right. 📏

Evaluating RAG quality means measuring two separate things, not one. First: did retrieval surface the right context? Second: did the generator faithfully use that context to answer? A system can retrieve perfectly and then hallucinate, or retrieve garbage and still bluff a plausible answer. 'It feels good' is not a metric.

This post sets the foundation: what RAG evaluation actually measures, and why you have to split retrieval quality from generation quality before any number means anything.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• why 'looks right' is not an evaluation
• the two halves: retrieval vs generation
• the four core quality questions
• why one score hides the real failure
• the mental model to carry forward

Measure before you trust. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Evaluation, not vibes
slides.body: RAG evaluation is the practice of measuring whether a retrieval-augmented system answers correctly, grounded in real evidence. It replaces 'this demo felt smart' with numbers you can track across versions, models, and prompt changes. Without it, you cannot tell an improvement from a regression.

slides.title: Two systems wearing one coat
slides.body: Every RAG answer is the product of two stages: a retriever that fetches context, and a generator that writes the answer from it. They fail independently. Evaluating the pipeline as one black box tells you something broke — but never which half. You must measure each separately.

slides.title: Where quality is won or lost
The four core questions
bullets: Did we retrieve the right context? (recall)
Was the retrieved context mostly relevant? (precision)
Is the answer grounded in that context? (faithfulness)
Does the answer actually address the question? (relevance)

slides.title: One score hides the failure
slides.body: Suppose your end-to-end accuracy is 70%. Is retrieval missing documents, or is the model ignoring good context? A single number can't say. Split it and you find the lever: bad retrieval means fix chunking or the index; bad generation means fix the prompt or model.

slides.title: The shape of one eval row
slides.code:
# A single evaluation example is more than question + answer.
row = {
    "question": "What is the refund window?",
    "ground_truth": "30 days from purchase.",
    "retrieved_contexts": [
        "Refunds are accepted within 30 days of purchase.",
        "Shipping takes 3-5 business days.",  # noise
    ],
    "answer": "You can request a refund within 30 days.",
}
# You can now score retrieval AND generation from this one row.

slides.title: Two flavors of metric
slides.body: Reference-based metrics compare the answer to a known ground truth you wrote in advance — precise, but expensive to label. Reference-free metrics use an LLM judge to score grounding and relevance with no gold answer — cheap and scalable, but noisier. Real suites use both.

slides.title: The 30-second model
bullets: RAG eval = retrieval quality + generation quality
Measure the two halves separately
Recall + precision judge retrieval
Faithfulness + relevance judge the answer
One blended score hides which half broke

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 75.
slides.body: Next: why skipping RAG evaluation quietly ships hallucinations to production.

74-2.caption:
A RAG system that hallucinates with citations is more dangerous than one that just says 'I don't know.' 😬

Unmeasured RAG fails silently. The answer looks fluent, cites a source, and is confidently wrong — and nobody notices until a customer or auditor does. Evaluation matters because it's the only thing that catches grounding failures before users do, and the only way to know if your last 'improvement' actually helped or quietly broke retrieval for half your queries.

This post is about the stakes: why RAG evaluation is the difference between a system you can trust in production and a fluent guessing machine.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• why fluent + wrong is the worst failure
• how unmeasured changes cause silent regressions
• why trust and compliance depend on grounding
• the cost of evaluating too late
• what good evaluation actually buys you

Know the stakes before you ship. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Fluent and wrong is the trap
slides.body: LLMs are optimized to sound right, not to be right. A RAG answer can quote a real-looking source, use confident language, and still invent the fact. Users trust fluency. Without faithfulness checks, your most dangerous outputs are the ones that read the best.

slides.title: Silent regressions are the norm
slides.body: You swap the embedding model, tweak chunk size, or change the prompt. End-to-end it 'seems fine' on the three queries you tried. Meanwhile recall dropped 12% on a whole class of questions. Without an eval set, that regression ships and you find out from complaints.

slides.title: The cost of catching it late

diagram.axisLabel: relative cost to fix a grounding bug

slides.title: Trust is the real product
slides.body: Users abandon an assistant after a few confident wrong answers — far faster than they reward correct ones. In legal, medical, or finance contexts, an ungrounded answer isn't just embarrassing; it's a liability. Evaluation is how you defend the one thing RAG is supposed to give you: trustworthy, sourced answers.

slides.title: You can't improve what you don't measure
slides.body: Every RAG knob — k, chunk size, reranker, prompt, model — trades off against others. Bigger k raises recall but adds noise that hurts faithfulness. Without metrics you're tuning blind, optimizing one demo while breaking ten queries you never looked at.

slides.title: Compliance and auditability
slides.body: Regulated domains increasingly demand you show why a system gave an answer. Citation accuracy and faithfulness scores are evidence. An evaluation pipeline produces the paper trail that turns 'the AI said so' into 'here is the source and the grounding score.'

slides.title: One ungrounded answer, quantified
slides.code:
# A faithfulness judge turns 'feels off' into a number.
claim   = "The refund window is 90 days."
context = "Refunds are accepted within 30 days of purchase."

# LLM judge prompt (simplified):
#   "Is the CLAIM fully supported by the CONTEXT? yes/no + why"
# -> verdict: NO. context says 30 days, claim says 90.
# faithfulness = supported_claims / total_claims = 0 / 1 = 0.0
print("faithfulness:", 0.0)  # this is the answer you must catch

slides.title: What evaluation buys you
bullets: Hallucinations caught before users see them
Confidence that a change actually helped
A defensible audit trail for answers
Data to tune k, chunking, and prompts
Trust that survives contact with real traffic

slides.title: The bottom line
slides.body: RAG evaluation matters because the failure mode is invisible by default: a wrong answer that looks perfect. Measurement is the only thing that surfaces grounding failures early, proves changes are real, and lets you ship a system people can actually rely on.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 75.
slides.body: Next: how retrieval and generation metrics are actually computed, step by step.

74-3.caption:
The secret of RAG evaluation: every metric is just counting overlaps between four things — the question, the retrieved chunks, the answer, and the truth. ⚙️

Retrieval metrics ask whether the chunks you fetched contain the answer (recall) and whether they're mostly on-topic (precision), often ranked by hit rate, MRR, or nDCG. Generation metrics ask whether the answer is supported by those chunks (faithfulness) and whether it addresses the question (answer relevance). When you have no gold labels, an LLM judge decomposes the answer into claims and verifies each against the context.

This post opens the hood: the exact computations behind the headline RAG metrics.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• context recall and precision, computed
• ranking metrics: hit rate, MRR, nDCG
• faithfulness via claim decomposition
• answer relevance via the LLM judge
• how it all assembles into one scorecard

This is the real engine. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Context recall
slides.body: Recall asks: of the facts needed to answer, how many appear in the retrieved chunks? Take the ground-truth answer, break it into required statements, and check what fraction is present in the context. Recall = supported statements / total required statements. Low recall means retrieval missed the evidence.

slides.title: Context precision
slides.body: Precision asks the opposite: of the chunks you retrieved, how many were actually relevant? It penalizes stuffing the context window with noise. High recall but low precision means you're feeding the model a haystack with one needle — which dilutes attention and invites distraction.

slides.title: Ranking metrics
slides.body: Order matters: the model attends most to early chunks. Hit Rate@k = fraction of queries whose answer appears in the top k. MRR rewards putting the right chunk first (1/rank of first hit). nDCG weights relevance by position with a log discount, so a relevant chunk at rank 1 beats the same chunk at rank 8.

slides.title: Faithfulness by claim
slides.body: To score grounding, an LLM judge splits the answer into atomic claims, then checks each claim against the retrieved context. Faithfulness = claims supported by context / total claims. A claim with no support is a hallucination, even if it happens to be true in the world.

slides.title: Answer relevance
slides.body: An answer can be perfectly grounded yet dodge the question. To measure relevance, a judge generates questions that the answer would address, then compares them to the original question by embedding similarity. High similarity means the answer is on-target, not evasive or padded.

slides.title: Recall and precision from sets
Faithfulness with an LLM judge
How it assembles
bullets: Recall + precision → retrieval score
Hit rate / MRR / nDCG → ranking quality
Faithfulness → grounding, by claim
Answer relevance → on-topic check
Aggregate per query, then average the suite

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 75.
slides.body: Next: a full, runnable evaluation harness you can drop onto your own RAG pipeline.

74-4.caption:
Here's a complete RAG evaluation harness — define a dataset, run your pipeline, and get retrieval and generation scores you can track over time. 🧑‍💻

We'll build it twice: first a transparent from-scratch version so you see exactly what each metric counts, then the same idea using Ragas, the standard library for LLM-judged RAG metrics. The pattern is always the same: assemble (question, contexts, answer, ground_truth) rows, score retrieval and generation, and aggregate into a scorecard you re-run on every change.

This post is code-heavy. Copy it, point it at your pipeline, and you have a regression test for quality.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• define an evaluation dataset
• score retrieval from scratch
• score faithfulness with an LLM judge
• run the same metrics via Ragas
• wire it into CI as a quality gate

Steal this harness. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 1. Define the eval dataset
slides.code:
# Each row pairs a question with the gold answer.
evalset = [
    {"question": "What is the refund window?",
     "ground_truth": "30 days from purchase."},
    {"question": "How long does shipping take?",
     "ground_truth": "3 to 5 business days."},
]
# Curate 50-200 of these from real queries + edge cases.
# This set is your regression suite; version it in git.

slides.title: 2. Run the pipeline, capture context
3. Score retrieval from scratch
4. Faithfulness via LLM judge
slides.code:
import json

def faithfulness(answer, chunks, llm):
    ctx = "\n".join(chunks)
    prompt = (f"CONTEXT:\n{ctx}\nANSWER:\n{answer}\n"
              "Is every statement in ANSWER supported by "
              "CONTEXT? Reply JSON: {\"score\": 0-1}")
    return json.loads(llm(prompt))["score"]

for row in evalset:
    row["faithfulness"] = faithfulness(
        row["answer"], row["retrieved_contexts"], judge_llm)

slides.title: 5. The same metrics via Ragas
slides.code:
# pip install ragas datasets
from datasets import Dataset
from ragas import evaluate
from ragas.metrics import (faithfulness, answer_relevancy,
                           context_precision, context_recall)

ds = Dataset.from_list(evalset)  # needs the 4 standard keys
report = evaluate(ds, metrics=[
    faithfulness, answer_relevancy,
    context_precision, context_recall])
print(report)  # {'faithfulness': 0.91, 'context_recall': 0.84, ...}

slides.title: 6. Turn it into a CI gate
Make it real
bullets: Seed the eval set from real production queries
Pin the judge model + temperature 0 for stability
Track scores per version in git
Fail CI on regressions, not just log them
Add hard edge cases as you find bugs

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 75.
slides.body: Next: the evaluation mistakes that make your green dashboard lie to you.

74-5.caption:
Your RAG eval dashboard is all green. That's exactly when you should be suspicious. 🚩

Most broken RAG evaluations fail in the same predictable ways: tiny test sets that prove nothing, leaking the answer into the metric, trusting a single LLM judge with no calibration, and reporting one blended score that hides which half of the pipeline is bleeding. A green metric you don't understand is worse than no metric — it manufactures false confidence.

This post is the field guide to the traps, and how to build an evaluation you can actually trust.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• why a 10-row eval set lies
• measuring fluency instead of faithfulness
• trusting the LLM judge blindly
• the data leakage that inflates scores
• collapsing everything into one number

Avoid the green-dashboard trap. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 1. The eval set is too small
slides.body: Ten questions can't represent thousands of real queries. A change that looks like +20% is statistical noise on a tiny set. Aim for 50-200 curated examples spanning common queries, edge cases, and known past failures — and grow it every time a bug escapes.

slides.title: 2. Scoring fluency, not grounding
slides.body: Metrics like BLEU or raw embedding similarity reward answers that sound like the reference, not answers that are actually supported by the retrieved context. A confident hallucination scores well. Always include a faithfulness check that ties each claim back to the evidence.

slides.title: 3. Trusting the judge blindly
slides.body: LLM judges are noisy: they drift with temperature, favor longer answers, and can be inconsistent run to run. Treat the judge as a model that itself needs validation — calibrate it against a few hundred human labels and measure its agreement before you trust its verdicts.

slides.title: 4. Leaking the answer
slides.body: If your ground-truth answer text is itself in the retrieved context, or the eval question was used to tune the retriever, scores inflate and won't survive production. Keep a held-out test set the system never trains or tunes on, just like any ML evaluation.

slides.title: 5. One number, no diagnosis
slides.body: Reporting a single 'RAG score' tells you something moved but not what. Retrieval and generation must stay separate columns. A drop in faithfulness with steady recall points at the prompt or model; a drop in recall points at chunking or the index.

slides.title: 6. Ignoring 'I don't know'
slides.body: A good RAG system should refuse when the context lacks the answer. If your eval only rewards giving an answer, you train the system to bluff. Score abstention as correct when retrieval genuinely failed — otherwise you're optimizing for confident wrongness.

slides.title: Catch leakage and variance
slides.code:
# 1) Leakage check: gold answer must NOT be verbatim in context.
def leak(row):
    return row["ground_truth"].lower() in \
           " ".join(row["retrieved_contexts"]).lower()
assert not any(leak(r) for r in evalset), "answer leaked!"

# 2) Judge stability: same input must score consistently.
scores = [faithfulness(a, c, judge) for _ in range(5)]
assert max(scores) - min(scores) < 0.1, "judge too noisy"

slides.title: Build an eval you trust
bullets: 100+ held-out rows, not 10
Faithfulness, not surface similarity
Calibrate the judge against humans
No answer leakage into context
Report retrieval and generation apart
Reward correct 'I don't know'

slides.body: Next day, next building block in the 100 Days of AI series.

75-1.caption:
A chatbot answers. An agent acts. That one word is the whole difference. 🤖

An LLM by itself is a text predictor — you ask, it replies, and nothing happens in the world. An AI agent wraps that same model in a loop: it can decide to use tools, call APIs, read files, run code, and then look at the results and decide what to do next. The model becomes the brain; tools become the hands; the loop is what turns thinking into doing.

This post builds the mental model before any code.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• what an AI agent actually is
• the four parts: model, tools, memory, loop
• agent vs a plain LLM call
• what "agency" and "autonomy" really mean
• the goal-driven loop in plain language

Get the intuition first. Save it. 👇

slides.title: What Is an AI Agent? Beyond the Chatbot
What is an AI agent?
slides.body: An AI agent is an LLM placed inside a loop where it can take actions — call tools, run code, fetch data — observe the results, and decide the next step toward a goal. It doesn't just answer; it acts, checks, and adjusts until the task is done.

right.title: AI agent
items: Pursues a goal over many steps

slides.title: The four core parts
bullets: Model — the reasoning brain (the LLM)
Tools — functions it can call to act
Memory — what it remembers across steps
Loop — think, act, observe, repeat

slides.title: What "agency" means here
slides.body: Agency is the model deciding WHICH action to take next, instead of you scripting every step. You give it a goal and a set of tools; it chooses how to combine them. The autonomy is bounded — it can only do what its tools allow — but within that, it drives.

slides.title: The loop in plain words
bullets: 1. Read the goal and current state
2. Decide: answer now or use a tool?
3. If tool: call it, get a result
4. Feed the result back, repeat
5. Stop when the goal is met

slides.title: An agent is a loop
Tools are the hands
slides.body: On its own, an LLM can only emit text. A tool turns text into action: a function the model can call to search the web, query a database, send an email, or run code. The model picks the tool and the arguments; your code executes it and hands back the result.

slides.title: Mental model to keep
bullets: Brain (LLM) + hands (tools) + a loop
Agent = pursue a goal, not answer once
It decides the steps; you grant the powers
More autonomy = more capability AND more risk

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 76.
slides.body: Next: why agents matter — and what they unlock that a chatbot can't.

75-2.caption:
A chatbot can tell you how to book a flight. An agent can actually book it. That gap is why everyone is suddenly building agents. ✈️

The leap from answering to acting changes what software can do. Instead of writing rigid code for every step of a workflow, you hand a goal to an agent and let it figure out the path — adapting when a step fails, retrying, and combining tools you never explicitly chained together. It moves AI from a clever autocomplete to something that can carry out multi-step work on its own.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• why "acting" unlocks real work
• what agents do that LLMs alone can't
• where they genuinely shine today
• the honest limits and real risks
• when an agent is overkill

This is the "so what" behind the hype. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Answering vs doing
slides.body: A plain LLM gives you instructions; you still do the work. An agent closes that gap — it executes the steps itself, checks the outcome, and recovers from errors. The value isn't smarter text, it's offloading whole multi-step tasks instead of single questions.

slides.title: What agents add
bullets: Access to live data, not just training cut-off
Real actions: write, send, query, deploy
Multi-step plans, not one-shot replies
Self-correction when a step fails

slides.title: Why now?
slides.body: Two things matured at once: LLMs got good enough at reasoning and structured output to choose tools reliably, and tool/function-calling APIs standardized. Together they made the loop dependable enough to trust with real tasks — not perfect, but useful.

slides.title: Where agents shine today
bullets: Research: search, read, synthesize a report
Coding: read repo, edit files, run tests
Ops: triage tickets, query logs, take action
Data: pull, join, and summarize on demand

slides.title: Why the loop beats one shot
slides.code:
# One-shot: model guesses, no feedback
answer = model("Sum of revenue in sales.csv?")  # may hallucinate

# Agent: it actually reads the file and computes
# step 1 -> call read_csv('sales.csv')
# step 2 -> call run_python('df.revenue.sum()')
# step 3 -> observe 48211.50 -> report grounded answer
run_agent("Sum of revenue in sales.csv?")  # verified

slides.title: The honest limits
bullets: Errors compound across many steps
Can take wrong actions with real consequences
Slower and pricier than one LLM call
Hard to fully predict or test

slides.title: When an agent is overkill
slides.body: If the task is a single, well-defined transformation — classify this, summarize that — a plain LLM call is cheaper, faster, and easier to trust. Reach for an agent only when the task genuinely needs multiple steps, live data, or real-world actions.

slides.title: The bottom line
bullets: Agents turn AI from advice into action
Best for multi-step, tool-using, live tasks
Power comes with new failure modes
Use the simplest thing that solves it

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 76.
slides.body: Next: the actual mechanics — the reason-act loop, step by step.

75-3.caption:
"It uses tools and figures things out" hides the real machinery. Here's the loop that turns an LLM into an agent, step by step. 🔍

Under the hood, an agent is a controlled loop around a single idea: let the model reason about what to do, let it emit a structured tool call, execute that call in YOUR code, then feed the result back as new context. Repeat until the model says it's finished. The popular ReAct pattern just makes this explicit — interleaving a thought, an action, and an observation on every turn.

This is the engine room most demos skip.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• the reason–act–observe loop
• how tool calling actually works
• the ReAct pattern, plainly
• how memory and context fit in
• how the loop knows when to stop

Understand this and you can build one. Save it. 👇

slides.title: The core loop, step by step
bullets: 1. Send goal + tools + history to the model
2. Model returns a thought + an action
3. Your code executes the chosen tool
4. Append the result as an observation
5. Repeat until the model emits a final answer

slides.title: How tool calling works
slides.body: You describe each tool to the model as a name, a purpose, and a JSON schema for its arguments. The model doesn't run the tool — it outputs structured JSON saying which tool and which arguments. Your code parses that, runs the real function, and returns the result for the next turn.

slides.title: The ReAct pattern
slides.body: ReAct = Reasoning + Acting. On each turn the model writes a short thought (its plan), then an action (a tool call), then reads an observation (the result). Making the reasoning explicit before acting measurably improves which tool it picks and how it recovers from mistakes.

slides.title: A ReAct turn, concretely
Where memory fits
bullets: Short-term: the running message/observation list
The context window IS the working memory
Long-term: a vector store or DB it can query
Summarize old steps when context fills up

slides.title: The control loop in code
slides.code:
messages = [system_prompt, user_goal]
for _ in range(MAX_STEPS):            # always bound the loop
    out = model(messages, tools=tools)
    if out.tool_call:
        result = TOOLS[out.tool_call.name](**out.tool_call.args)
        messages.append(out)          # the model's action
        messages.append(tool_msg(result))  # the observation
    else:
        return out.text               # final answer

slides.title: How it decides to stop
slides.body: The loop ends when the model returns a normal text response instead of a tool call — its signal that it has enough to answer. You also enforce a hard step limit so a confused agent can't loop forever. Stopping is part safety, part the model judging it's done.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 76.
slides.body: Next: build a real, runnable tool-using agent from scratch.

75-4.caption:
Enough theory — here's a real, runnable AI agent you can paste into a file today. No framework, just the loop. 💻

We define two tools, describe them to the model with JSON schemas, and write the reason–act–observe loop by hand so you can see exactly where the model's decision becomes a real function call. Watch it choose a tool, read the result, and only then answer. The shape here is identical to what LangChain or the OpenAI Agents SDK do for you — they just hide this loop.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• define real tools as plain functions
• describe tools to the model as schemas
• send the request and read the tool call
• execute it and feed the result back
• loop until a final answer
• the same thing with a framework

Copy it, run it, give it your own tools. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Build an AI Agent From Scratch
0. Install
slides.code: pip install openai

slides.title: 1. Define real tools
2. Describe tools to the model
slides.code:
schemas = [
  {"type": "function", "function": {
    "name": "get_weather",
    "description": "Current weather for a city",
    "parameters": {"type": "object",
      "properties": {"city": {"type": "string"}},
      "required": ["city"]}}},
  {"type": "function", "function": {
    "name": "calculator",
    "description": "Evaluate a math expression",
    "parameters": {"type": "object",
      "properties": {"expression": {"type": "string"}},
      "required": ["expression"]}}},
]

slides.title: 3. The agent loop
slides.code:
from openai import OpenAI
client = OpenAI()

messages = [{"role": "user",
  "content": "Weather in Tokyo, and what's 22*9/5+32?"}]

for _ in range(5):                       # bound the loop
    resp = client.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4o-mini", messages=messages, tools=schemas)
    msg = resp.choices[0].message
    messages.append(msg)
    if not msg.tool_calls:
        print(msg.content); break        # final answer

slides.title: 4. Execute the tool calls
Reading the trace
bullets: Turn 1: model asks for get_weather + calculator
You run both, append two tool messages
Turn 2: model has results, writes final answer
No tool_calls = the loop's exit signal

slides.title: 5. Same agent, with a framework
From here to production
bullets: Sandbox tools that touch code, files, or money
Add retries and a per-tool timeout
Log every thought, call, and observation
Keep MAX_STEPS — never an unbounded loop

slides.title: The takeaway
slides.body: An agent is just this loop: describe tools, let the model choose, execute in your code, feed results back, repeat. Frameworks add memory, tracing, and retries — but the engine is the dozen lines you just wrote.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 76.
slides.body: Next: the mistakes that quietly wreck an agent in production.

75-5.caption:
Agents are easy to demo and brutal to ship. Most broken agents fail in the same handful of ways. 🚧

The failures aren't crashes — they're an agent that loops forever, burns your budget, calls the wrong tool with confidence, or takes a real, irreversible action because nobody put a guardrail in front of it. Give a model tools without limits, observability, or a human checkpoint and a small reasoning slip becomes an expensive incident.

These aren't exotic edge cases. They're the defaults you trip over the first week.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• unbounded loops that never stop
• vague tools the model misuses
• errors that compound across steps
• giving an agent dangerous powers
• flying blind with no tracing

Learn the failure modes before you ship. Save it. 👇

slides.title: AI Agents: 5 Mistakes That Quietly Hurt
1. The unbounded loop
slides.body: Without a step limit, a confused agent can call tools forever — racking up cost and latency while never converging. The fix is non-negotiable: cap the number of steps and stop with a clear message when you hit it. Every loop needs a brake.

slides.title: Always bound the loop
slides.code:
for step in range(MAX_STEPS):     # hard ceiling
    out = model(messages, tools=tools)
    if not out.tool_calls:
        return out.content
    run_tools(out)
return "Stopped: step limit reached. Partial result attached."

slides.title: 2. Vague tool descriptions
slides.body: The model picks tools from their descriptions alone. A name like "process" with no schema invites wrong calls and malformed arguments. Write each tool's purpose, parameters, and limits as if briefing a new hire who only reads that one paragraph.

slides.title: 3. Compounding errors
slides.body: Each step builds on the last, so an early wrong turn poisons everything after it. A 95%-reliable step run ten times is only ~60% reliable end to end. Keep agents short, validate tool outputs, and let the agent re-check instead of blindly trusting its own prior steps.

slides.title: 4. Dangerous powers, no guardrail
slides.body: An agent will eventually call any tool you give it — including the one that deletes data, spends money, or emails customers. Never expose an irreversible action without a confirmation step, scoped permissions, or a human in the loop for the risky calls.

slides.title: Gate the risky actions
5. Flying blind
slides.body: When an agent misbehaves you must see WHY. Without logging every thought, tool call, argument, and observation, debugging is impossible — you only see a wrong final answer. Trace the full loop from day one; it's the single best investment in agent reliability.

slides.title: The checklist
bullets: Cap MAX_STEPS — never an open loop
Write tools like a precise API spec
Validate outputs; keep chains short
Gate irreversible actions behind approval
Log every thought, call, and observation

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 76.
slides.body: Next up: a new topic in the 100 Days of AI series.

76-1.caption:
An LLM that only thinks is a brilliant guesser. An LLM that only acts is a blindfolded robot. ReAct lets it do both — out loud. 🧠

ReAct stands for Reasoning + Acting. Instead of forcing the model to answer in one shot, you let it interleave short thoughts with concrete actions — call a tool, read the result, think again — until it has enough to answer. The reasoning steers the next action; the action grounds the next thought.

This post sets the foundation: what ReAct actually is, and why interleaving thought and action beats pure reasoning or pure tool-calling alone.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• what 'ReAct' literally stands for
• the Thought-Action-Observation loop
• why reasoning alone hallucinates facts
• why acting alone wanders aimlessly
• the mental model to carry forward

This is the backbone of agents. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Reasoning + Acting
slides.body: ReAct is a prompting pattern where an LLM alternates between reasoning in natural language and taking actions in the world — usually tool calls. It thinks about what to do, does it, observes the result, then thinks again. The name is literally Reason + Act, fused into one loop.

slides.title: Three moves, repeated
slides.body: Every ReAct step is one of three things: a Thought (private reasoning about what to do next), an Action (a concrete tool call with arguments), and an Observation (the tool's result fed back in). The model cycles through these until a Thought concludes it can answer.

slides.title: Reasoning alone hallucinates
slides.body: Ask a model a factual question it doesn't know and it will often invent a confident, plausible answer. Pure chain-of-thought reasons beautifully but has no way to check itself against the real world. The logic can be flawless and the facts still wrong.

slides.title: Acting alone wanders
slides.body: Give a model tools but no space to reason and it calls them blindly — wrong tool, wrong arguments, no recovery when a result surprises it. Without an explicit Thought before each Action, there's no plan, just reflexive tool-poking that rarely converges.

slides.title: Pure reasoning vs pure acting vs ReAct
Why interleaving wins
slides.body: The magic is the feedback. Each Observation updates the model's reasoning before it commits to the next move. A surprising tool result triggers a new Thought instead of a blind retry. Reasoning makes actions deliberate; actions make reasoning honest.

slides.title: What a ReAct trace looks like
slides.code:
Question: How old is the director of 'Oppenheimer'?

Thought: I need the director, then their age.
Action: search("Oppenheimer 2023 director")
Observation: Christopher Nolan directed it.
Thought: Now I need Nolan's birth year.
Action: search("Christopher Nolan birth year")
Observation: 1970.
Thought: 2026 - 1970 = 56.
Answer: He is 56 years old.

slides.title: The 30-second model
bullets: ReAct = Reasoning + Acting, interleaved
Loop: Thought -> Action -> Observation
Thought plans, Action grounds, repeat
Reasoning fixes blind tool use
Acting fixes ungrounded reasoning

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 77.
slides.body: Next: why ReAct matters — how it turns a chatbot into an agent that can actually do things.

76-2.caption:
A chatbot answers from memory. An agent goes and finds out. The thing that turns one into the other is ReAct. 🚀

ReAct matters because it is the simplest pattern that gives an LLM agency without giving up control. The model can reach into live tools — search, databases, calculators, APIs — so its answers are grounded in current reality instead of frozen training data. And because every step is a visible Thought and Action, you get a trace you can read, debug, and trust.

This post is about the stakes: why ReAct became the default architecture for LLM agents and what it unlocks.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• how ReAct grounds answers in live data
• why the visible trace is a debugging superpower
• how it enables genuine multi-step tasks
• why it cuts hallucination without fine-tuning
• where ReAct fits in the agent stack

Know why it won. Save it. 👇

slides.title: It grounds answers in reality
slides.body: A frozen model can't know today's price, your database's contents, or the result of a calculation it hasn't done. ReAct lets it fetch those facts mid-answer via tools. The answer stops being a guess from training data and becomes a conclusion drawn from live evidence.

slides.title: The trace is a superpower
slides.body: Because ReAct externalizes every Thought and Action as text, you can read exactly why the agent did what it did. When it fails, you see the wrong thought or the bad tool call — not an opaque black box. That observability is what makes agents debuggable in production.

slides.title: It enables real multi-step tasks
slides.body: Hard tasks need several dependent steps: look this up, use it to compute that, then decide. ReAct's loop naturally chains them — each Observation feeds the next Thought. A single forward pass can't do this; the loop is what makes genuine task completion possible.

slides.title: Less hallucination, no retraining
slides.body: The original ReAct paper showed that letting the model verify facts via a search tool sharply reduced hallucination versus chain-of-thought alone — with zero fine-tuning. You change behavior through prompting and tools, not expensive retraining. That's a huge practical win.

slides.title: Where ReAct sits in the stack
It is the agent baseline
slides.body: ReAct is the pattern almost every agent framework starts from — LangChain agents, LlamaIndex, and countless custom loops are ReAct under the hood. Understanding it means understanding the foundation the fancier patterns (planning, reflection, multi-agent) are built on top of.

slides.title: Grounded vs ungrounded, side by side
slides.code:
# Ungrounded (pure LLM): guesses from training data
ask("What's our Q3 refund total?")
# -> "Approximately $42,000"   # invented, no data

# ReAct (grounded): the Thought triggers a real query
# Thought: I must query the finance DB, not guess.
# Action: sql("SELECT SUM(amount) FROM refunds WHERE q=3")
# Observation: 51820.00
# Answer: "Q3 refunds totaled $51,820."  # from real data

slides.title: What ReAct buys you
bullets: Answers grounded in live, current data
A readable trace for every decision
Genuine multi-step task completion
Lower hallucination, no fine-tuning
A foundation the whole agent stack builds on

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 77.
slides.body: Next: how the loop actually works, step by step, including the prompt that drives it.

76-3.caption:
ReAct looks like magic in a demo. Under the hood it's a boringly simple loop and one carefully written prompt. ⚙️

The whole pattern is a while-loop: send the conversation to the model, read what it emits, and branch. If it emitted an Action, run the tool and append the Observation. If it emitted a final Answer, stop. The 'intelligence' lives in a system prompt that lists the tools, shows the Thought/Action/Observation format, and tells the model to reason before acting.

This post opens the hood: the exact control flow, the prompt that drives it, and how the model is made to stop.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• the control loop in plain terms
• the system prompt that defines the format
• how actions get parsed and executed
• how observations are fed back in
• stop conditions and step limits

This is the real engine. Save it. 👇

slides.title: It's a while-loop
slides.body: Strip away the hype and ReAct is a loop: feed the running transcript to the LLM, read its next emission, and act on it. If the model produced an Action, execute the tool and append the result. If it produced an Answer, return it. Otherwise loop again.

slides.title: The prompt does the heavy lifting
slides.body: A system prompt teaches the format. It lists the available tools with descriptions, shows the exact Thought/Action/Observation/Answer structure, and instructs the model to always reason before acting. The model's job becomes filling in that template one step at a time.

slides.title: Parsing the action
slides.body: The model emits text like Action: search("..."). The controller parses out the tool name and arguments — via regex on the old text format, or cleanly via the structured tool-calling API on modern models. It then dispatches to the matching Python function.

slides.title: Feeding observations back
slides.body: The tool's return value is wrapped as an Observation and appended to the transcript. Crucially, the model never sees the tool run — it only sees the Observation text. That single line is the model's entire window into what the action actually did.

slides.title: Stop conditions
slides.body: The loop ends one of two ways: the model emits a final Answer, or it hits a max-steps cap. The cap is essential — without it a confused agent loops forever, burning tokens. Real systems set a step budget and a timeout, then fail gracefully.

slides.title: The system prompt template
slides.code:
SYSTEM = '''You solve tasks using tools.
Always follow this exact format:

Thought: reason about what to do next
Action: tool_name("argument")
Observation: <filled in by the system>
... (repeat Thought/Action/Observation as needed)
Thought: I now know the answer
Answer: the final answer

Available tools:
- search(query): web search, returns a snippet
- calc(expr): evaluate a math expression
'''

slides.title: The loop itself
The mechanics in five lines
bullets: The loop: prompt -> LLM -> parse -> tool -> repeat
A system prompt defines the Thought/Action format
Use 'stop' tokens so the LLM yields after an Action
Wrap each tool result as an Observation
Always cap max steps to prevent infinite loops

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 77.
slides.body: Next: a full, runnable ReAct agent you can paste and point at your own tools.

76-4.caption:
Here's a complete ReAct agent in under 50 lines — no framework, just the loop, two tools, and a prompt. Paste it and watch it reason. 🧑‍💻

We'll build it twice. First the raw text-format version so you see every moving part: the prompt, the parser, the loop, the stop logic. Then the modern, production-shaped version using the model's native tool-calling API, which removes the brittle regex parsing entirely.

This post is code-heavy. Copy it, swap in your own tools, and you have a working agent you actually understand.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• define the tools the agent can use
• write the ReAct system prompt
• parse actions and run the loop
• the same agent with native tool-calling
• guardrails: step caps and error handling

Steal this agent. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Build a ReAct Agent in Code
1. Define the tools
2. Write the system prompt
slides.code:
SYSTEM = '''Answer the question using these tools.
Format each step EXACTLY as:

Thought: your reasoning
Action: tool_name|argument

Then STOP and wait for an Observation.
When done, write:
Thought: I can answer now
Answer: final answer

Tools:
- search|<query>  : look up a fact
- calc|<expr>     : evaluate arithmetic
'''

slides.title: 3. Parse the action line
slides.code:
import re

def parse_action(text: str):
    # find the last 'Action: tool|arg' line
    m = re.search(r"Action:\s*(\w+)\s*\|\s*(.+)", text)
    if not m:
        return None, None
    name, arg = m.group(1).strip(), m.group(2).strip()
    return name, arg

slides.title: 4. The ReAct loop
What the agent does at runtime
5. Modern version: native tool-calling
slides.code:
# No regex. The API returns structured tool calls.
resp = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-5",
    tools=[{"name": "search", "description": "look up a fact",
            "input_schema": {"type": "object",
              "properties": {"query": {"type": "string"}}}}],
    messages=msgs,
)
if resp.stop_reason == "tool_use":
    call = next(b for b in resp.content if b.type == "tool_use")
    result = TOOLS[call.name](**call.input)   # clean dispatch

slides.title: 6. Feed the result back
slides.code:
# Append the model's turn, then the tool result, then loop.
msgs.append({"role": "assistant", "content": resp.content})
msgs.append({"role": "user", "content": [{
    "type": "tool_result",
    "tool_use_id": call.id,
    "content": str(result),
}]})
# Re-call the model; repeat until stop_reason != 'tool_use'.

slides.title: Make it production-ready
bullets: Always cap max_steps to stop runaways
Catch tool errors and feed them back as Observations
Prefer native tool-calling over regex parsing
Log the full transcript for every run
Never eval untrusted input — sandbox tools

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 77.
slides.body: Next: the ReAct mistakes that turn a clever agent into a token-burning loop.

76-5.caption:
Your ReAct agent worked in the demo. In production it looped twelve times, called the wrong tool, and burned $4 to answer 'I don't know.' 🚩

Most ReAct failures are predictable: no step cap so the agent loops forever, brittle parsing that breaks the moment the model rephrases, vague tool descriptions the model can't use correctly, and a context window that bloats with observations until reasoning degrades. A clever loop with bad guardrails is just an expensive way to fail.

This post is the field guide to the traps, and how to build a ReAct agent that survives real traffic.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• the infinite-loop trap and step caps
• why fragile parsing breaks in production
• vague tool descriptions that confuse the model
• context bloat from unfiltered observations
• swallowing tool errors instead of reflecting

Avoid the token-burning loop. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 1. No step cap
slides.body: Without a hard limit on iterations, a confused agent loops forever — re-running the same failing action, never converging, quietly draining your token budget. Always set max_steps and a wall-clock timeout, and return a graceful failure when you hit them.

slides.title: 2. Fragile action parsing
slides.body: Regex that matches Action: tool("x") breaks the instant the model writes Action: tool('x') or adds a stray newline. Text-format ReAct is brittle by nature. In production, use the model's native tool-calling API so the action arrives as structured data, not text to scrape.

slides.title: 3. Vague tool descriptions
slides.body: The model picks tools from their descriptions alone. 'lookup: gets data' is useless — the agent can't tell when to use it or what to pass. Write tool descriptions like API docs: what it does, when to use it, exact argument format, and what it returns.

slides.title: 4. Context bloat
slides.body: Every Observation gets appended, so a long task balloons the prompt. The agent slows, costs more, and reasoning degrades as early facts get buried under raw tool output. Summarize or truncate observations, and keep only what later steps actually need.

slides.title: 5. Swallowing tool errors
slides.body: When a tool throws, don't crash and don't hide it. Feed the error back as an Observation so the model can reason about it — retry with fixed arguments, try a different tool, or report the failure. A caught error the agent can see is a chance to self-correct.

slides.title: 6. No 'give up' path
slides.body: An agent that can't admit defeat will fabricate an answer or loop until the cap. Give it an explicit way out — a 'finish' action it can call to report that it couldn't complete the task — so failure is honest and observable, not a hallucinated success.

slides.title: Guardrails in code
Build a ReAct agent you trust
bullets: Cap steps and add a timeout
Use native tool-calling, not regex
Write tool docs like real API docs
Summarize observations to fight bloat
Feed tool errors back, never swallow them
Give the agent an explicit way to give up

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 77.
slides.body: Next day, next building block in the 100 Days of AI series.

77-1.caption:
An LLM can't check today's weather, query your database, or send an email. Tool calling is how it asks YOUR code to do it. 🔌

A language model only ever produces text. Tool calling (also called function calling) is the contract that lets that text trigger real actions: you describe the functions the model is allowed to use, and instead of guessing an answer, the model outputs a structured request — 'call get_weather with city=Tokyo.' Your code runs the real function and hands the result back. The model never touches your systems; it only asks.

This post builds the mental model before any code.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• what tool calling actually is
• why it's a request, not an execution
• the three pieces: schema, call, result
• tool calling vs plain text generation
• where the model ends and your code begins

Get the intuition first. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Tool Calling: How an LLM Reaches the Real World
What is tool calling?
slides.body: Tool calling lets a model request that one of your functions run. You describe each tool as a name and an argument schema; the model replies with structured JSON saying which tool to call and with what arguments. Your code executes it and returns the result. The model decides; your code acts.

slides.title: It's a request, not an action
slides.body: This is the part everyone gets wrong. The model never runs your function. It only emits a structured intent — 'I'd like to call get_weather("Tokyo").' Whether, when, and how that runs is entirely up to your code. That boundary is the whole safety and control story.

slides.title: The three moving parts
bullets: Schema — you describe each tool to the model
Call — the model emits a structured request
Result — your code runs it, returns the output

slides.title: Why structured output matters
slides.body: A tool call isn't free-form text — it's JSON that matches the schema you provided. That structure is what makes it executable. Your code can parse it, validate the types, and call the real function with confidence instead of trying to scrape arguments out of a sentence.

slides.title: The shape of a tool call
Tool vs function vs API
bullets: Function: the actual code that runs
Tool: a function exposed to the model + its schema
API: often what the tool calls under the hood
"Tool calling" = "function calling" — same thing

slides.title: Mental model to keep
bullets: The model proposes; your code disposes
A tool = a function + a schema describing it
Calls are JSON, not prose — so they're runnable
Tools give the model reach beyond its training data

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 78.
slides.body: Next: why tool calling is the upgrade that turned LLMs into real software.

77-2.caption:
Before tool calling, every LLM answer was a confident guess from frozen memory. After it, the model could check, fetch, and act. That's the whole leap. 🚀

Tool calling is the feature that turned chatbots into software. It solves three problems at once: it gets the model fresh, real data instead of stale training knowledge; it lets the model take actions in the world; and it forces structured, parseable output you can actually wire into a system. Without it, agents, RAG pipelines, and AI copilots simply don't work.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• the three problems tool calling solves
• how it kills a whole class of hallucinations
• why it's the foundation of every agent
• reliable structured output as a side effect
• when you DON'T need a tool call

This is the "so what" behind the feature. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Three problems, one feature
slides.body: Tool calling solves three things at once. Stale knowledge: it fetches live data. No agency: it takes real actions. Messy output: it forces structured JSON. Each of these is a hard wall for a plain LLM, and tool calling knocks down all three with one mechanism.

slides.title: It kills a class of hallucinations
slides.body: A model asked 'what's our refund policy?' will invent a plausible answer. Give it a search_docs tool and it can look up the real one instead. Tool calling doesn't make the model smarter — it lets the model replace a guess with a fact pulled from a trusted source.

right.title: With tools
items: As fresh as the data is

slides.title: It's the foundation of agents
slides.body: An agent is an LLM in a loop that takes actions. Tool calling IS the action mechanism. No tool calling, no agent — just a chatbot. Every agent framework, RAG system, and AI copilot you've heard of is built on top of this one primitive.

slides.title: Structured output for free
Where it genuinely shines
bullets: Live data: weather, prices, inventory, news
Actions: book, email, refund, deploy
Lookups: search docs, query a database
Extraction: pull typed fields from messy text

slides.title: When you DON'T need it
slides.body: If the task is a pure text transformation the model already does well — summarize this, translate that, classify sentiment — a tool call adds latency and complexity for nothing. Reach for tools when you need fresh data, a real action, or guaranteed structure. Otherwise, just ask.

slides.title: The bottom line
bullets: Tool calling = fresh data + actions + structure
It replaces confident guesses with real facts
It's the primitive under agents and RAG
Use it when prose alone can't do the job

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 78.
slides.body: Next: the actual round-trip — how a tool call works under the hood, step by step.

77-3.caption:
"The model calls a function" hides a precise, multi-step round-trip. Here's exactly what happens under the hood. 🔍

A tool call is a conversation in four moves: you send the prompt plus tool schemas, the model decides to call a tool and emits structured JSON, your runtime executes the real function, and you send the result back so the model can finish. Critically, this often takes two model calls — one to decide, one to answer — and the result rides back on a special 'tool' message tied to the call by ID.

This is the machinery most tutorials skip.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• the full request-to-answer round-trip
• how schemas tell the model what's callable
• why it takes two model calls, not one
• tool_call_id and how results get matched
• parallel calls and multi-step chains

Understand this and you can wire it yourself. Save it. 👇

slides.title: How Tool Calling Works Under the Hood
The round-trip, step by step
bullets: 1. Send prompt + tool schemas to the model
2. Model returns a tool_call (name + args JSON)
3. Your code parses and runs the real function
4. Send the result back as a 'tool' message
5. Model reads it and writes the final answer

slides.title: How schemas drive the choice
slides.body: Each tool is described as a name, a one-line purpose, and a JSON Schema for its arguments. The model sees only this — no source code. It pattern-matches your prompt against these descriptions to decide which tool fits and what arguments to fill in. The schema is the model's entire view of the tool.

slides.title: What a tool schema looks like
Why it takes two model calls
slides.body: Call one: the model sees the question and tools, decides it needs data, and returns a tool_call instead of an answer. You run the function. Call two: you send the result back and the model, now holding real data, writes the final answer. One human question, two trips to the model.

slides.title: tool_call_id ties it together
slides.body: Each call the model emits carries a unique id. When you send the result back, you echo that same id on the 'tool' message. This lets the model match each result to the exact call it made — essential when the model fires several tool calls at once and the answers come back out of order.

slides.title: The message thread that builds up
slides.code:
messages = [
  {"role": "user", "content": "Price of AAPL?"},
  # model's turn 1 -> a tool call:
  {"role": "assistant", "tool_calls": [
      {"id": "call_01", "function":
        {"name": "get_stock_price",
         "arguments": '{"ticker": "AAPL"}'}}]},
  # you run it, then append the result with the SAME id:
  {"role": "tool", "tool_call_id": "call_01",
   "content": "227.52"},
]
# Next model call reads this and answers in plain text.

slides.title: Parallel and chained calls
bullets: Parallel: model can emit several calls in one turn
You run them all, append one tool msg per id
Chained: result of one call informs the next
Looping these is exactly what an agent does

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 78.
slides.body: Next: build the whole round-trip in real, runnable code you can paste today.

77-4.caption:
Enough theory — here's the full tool-calling round-trip in real, runnable Python you can paste into a file today. 💻

We define a real function, describe it to the model with a JSON schema, send the question, read the tool call the model emits, execute the function ourselves, feed the result back, and get the grounded final answer. You'll see exactly where the model's decision becomes a real call — and how the result returns on a 'tool' message tied by id. This is the same dance every framework runs for you.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• define a real function as a tool
• describe it with a JSON schema
• send the request and read the tool_call
• execute it and append the result by id
• second call -> the grounded answer
• the same thing with the Anthropic SDK

Copy it, run it, swap in your own tools. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Tool Calling in Code, End to End
0. Install
slides.code: pip install openai

slides.title: 1. Define the real function
2. Describe it to the model
3. Send it and read the call
slides.code:
from openai import OpenAI
client = OpenAI()

messages = [{"role": "user",
             "content": "What's the weather in Tokyo, in F?"}]

resp = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="gpt-4o-mini", messages=messages, tools=tools)

msg = resp.choices[0].message
messages.append(msg)              # keep the model's tool call
call = msg.tool_calls[0]
print(call.function.name)         # -> get_weather

slides.title: 4. Execute it, append by id
5. Second call -> the answer
slides.code:
final = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="gpt-4o-mini", messages=messages, tools=tools)

print(final.choices[0].message.content)
# -> "It's 72 degrees and clear in Tokyo right now."
# The model now answers from REAL data, not a guess.

slides.title: Same thing, Anthropic SDK
slides.code:
import anthropic
client = anthropic.Anthropic()

resp = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-5", max_tokens=512,
    tools=[{"name": "get_weather",
            "description": "Current weather for a city",
            "input_schema": tools[0]["function"]["parameters"]}],
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Weather in Tokyo?"}])
# resp.stop_reason == "tool_use"; same loop: run, return, ask again.

slides.title: Reading the trace
bullets: Call 1: model returns tool_calls, no text
You parse args, run get_weather, get a result
Append role:'tool' with the matching id
Call 2: model has data -> writes the answer

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 78.
slides.body: Next: the mistakes that quietly break tool calling in production.

77-5.caption:
Tool calling looks trivial in a demo and breaks in subtle, expensive ways in production. Most failures come from the same handful of causes. 🚧

The model doesn't crash — it calls the wrong tool because your description was vague, hallucinates an argument you never validated, runs a dangerous action you never gated, or you forget to send the result back and the loop hangs. Treat tool definitions as part of your prompt and tool execution as untrusted input, and most of these vanish.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• vague tool descriptions the model misreads
• trusting arguments you never validated
• forgetting to return the result
• exposing dangerous, ungated actions
• ignoring when the model picks no tool

Learn the failure modes before you ship. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Tool Calling: 5 Mistakes That Bite in Production
1. Vague tool descriptions
slides.body: The model chooses tools from their names, descriptions, and schemas alone — never the source. A tool called 'process' with an untyped 'data' field invites wrong calls and malformed args. Write each description like you're briefing someone who reads only that one paragraph: what it does, when to use it, every parameter typed.

slides.title: 2. Trusting the arguments
slides.body: The model generates the arguments — which means it can hallucinate an order_id that doesn't exist, a negative quantity, or a path outside your sandbox. Tool arguments are untrusted input. Validate types, ranges, and existence before you act on them, exactly as you would for a user-submitted form.

slides.title: Validate before you act
3. Forgetting to return the result
slides.body: Every tool_call the model makes MUST get a matching result message sent back — keyed by its tool_call_id. Skip it and the conversation is malformed: the API errors, or the model hangs waiting for an answer it never receives. One call out, one result in, every time.

slides.title: 4. Ungated dangerous actions
slides.body: The model will eventually call any tool you expose — including delete_user, charge_card, or send_email. A single hallucinated argument on a destructive tool is a real incident. Never wire an irreversible action straight through. Put scoped permissions, a confirmation, or a human approval in front of it.

slides.title: Gate the risky calls
5. Ignoring 'no tool' replies
slides.body: Sometimes the model answers in plain text instead of calling a tool — either it already knows, or your description didn't make the tool's purpose clear. Code that assumes a tool_call always exists will crash on msg.tool_calls[0]. Always branch: if there's a tool call, run it; otherwise, take the text as the answer.

slides.title: Always branch on the reply
The checklist
bullets: Write tool specs like a precise API doc
Treat every argument as untrusted input
Return a result for every tool_call_id
Gate irreversible actions behind approval
Handle the no-tool-call branch every time

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 78.
slides.body: Next up: a new topic in the 100 Days of AI series.

78-1.caption:
One agent doing everything is a generalist juggling ten jobs badly. A team of focused agents is a company. 🧩

A multi-agent system is several LLM-driven agents that each own a narrow role, talk to each other, and coordinate to finish a task no single agent handles well alone. Instead of one giant prompt trying to research, write, critique, and format all at once, you split the work — a researcher, a writer, a reviewer — and let them pass results between roles.

This post sets the foundation: what a multi-agent system actually is, and how it differs from a single agent with a lot of tools.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• what 'multi-agent system' literally means
• agent, role, and message — the core parts
• single agent vs a team of agents
• why specialization beats one mega-prompt
• the mental model to carry forward

This is the org chart for AI. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Many agents, one goal
slides.body: A multi-agent system is several LLM agents that each hold a narrow role and coordinate — by passing messages — to solve a task no single agent does well alone. Think researcher, writer, and critic working together, not one model trying to be all three at once.

slides.title: The three core parts
slides.body: Every system has agents (an LLM plus a role and tools), roles (the narrow job each agent owns), and messages (the typed handoffs between them). An orchestrator decides who runs next. Strip those four ideas down and you can read any framework.

slides.title: An agent is role + tools + memory
slides.body: Each agent is a configured LLM: a system prompt that defines its role, a set of tools it may call, and its own slice of context. The same base model becomes a researcher or a reviewer purely by changing the prompt and the tools it is handed.

slides.title: Specialization beats one mega-prompt
slides.body: A prompt that asks a model to research, write, critique, and format at once spreads its attention thin and blurs the instructions. Splitting those jobs gives each agent a short, sharp prompt it can actually follow — the same reason teams beat one overworked person.

slides.title: Not just one agent with tools
slides.body: A single agent calling many tools is still one reasoning loop with one context. A multi-agent system has separate reasoning loops, each with its own prompt and memory, that exchange messages. The difference is independent minds versus one mind with many hands.

slides.title: Two roles, defined as prompts
The 30-second model
bullets: Multi-agent = many focused agents, one goal
Agent = LLM + role + tools + memory
They coordinate by passing messages
An orchestrator decides who runs next
Specialization beats one overloaded prompt

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 79.
slides.body: Next: why multi-agent systems matter — what splitting the work actually unlocks.

78-2.caption:
You can keep stuffing one prompt until it does six jobs poorly — or split the work and watch each piece get sharp. That split is why multi-agent systems exist. 🚀

Multi-agent design matters because it gives you the things a single bloated agent can't: focused context windows, parallel work, separation of concerns, and verification by a second agent that didn't write the answer. A specialist with a short prompt follows instructions a generalist drowns in, and a critic that didn't produce the draft catches errors the author is blind to.

This post is about the stakes: why teams reach for multiple agents and what it genuinely buys them.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• how role separation sharpens each prompt
• why a separate critic catches more errors
• parallelism: many agents working at once
• context isolation that fights prompt bloat
• where multi-agent fits — and where it doesn't

Know why it wins. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Separation sharpens every prompt
slides.body: One agent told to research, write, and fact-check holds three conflicting instruction sets at once and follows each weakly. Give each job its own agent and every prompt becomes short and single-purpose — which is exactly the kind of instruction an LLM follows reliably.

slides.title: A separate critic sees more
slides.body: An agent reviewing its own output is biased toward the choices it just made. A fresh critic agent, with no stake in the draft and its own checklist, catches contradictions and gaps the author is blind to. Separation of author and reviewer is the whole point.

slides.title: Agents can work in parallel
slides.body: Independent subtasks don't have to run one after another. Five researcher agents can each chase a different sub-question simultaneously, then hand their findings to a synthesizer. For research-heavy or fan-out tasks, parallelism turns minutes into seconds.

slides.title: Context isolation fights bloat
slides.body: Each agent only sees what its role needs. The writer never wades through raw search dumps; the researcher never holds the formatting rules. Smaller, relevant contexts mean cheaper calls and sharper reasoning, instead of one window stuffed with everything.

slides.title: When NOT to use it
slides.body: Multi-agent is not free. More agents mean more LLM calls, more latency, more cost, and more ways to fail. If one well-prompted agent with a few tools solves your task, use that. Reach for multiple agents only when the work genuinely splits into distinct roles.

slides.title: Where the win shows up
slides.code:
# Single agent: one call, one overloaded prompt
answer = agent("Research X, write a report, then fact-check it.")
# -> mediocre at all three, no independent check

# Multi-agent: roles + an independent reviewer
facts  = researcher.run("Find facts about X")
draft  = writer.run(facts)
review = critic.run(draft)          # fresh eyes, own checklist
final  = writer.run(draft, fixes=review)

slides.title: What multi-agent buys you
bullets: Short, single-purpose prompts per role
Independent review that catches real errors
Parallel work on independent subtasks
Isolated context — cheaper, sharper calls
Clean scaling: add a role instead of bloating one

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 79.
slides.body: Next: how it actually works — orchestration, message passing, and shared memory.

78-3.caption:
A multi-agent system looks like a swarm of intelligences. Under the hood it's an orchestrator, a message format, and a rule for who talks next. ⚙️

The machinery has three parts. An orchestration topology decides the flow — a supervisor handing tasks down, or peers passing work sideways. A message protocol defines what agents send each other so handoffs are structured, not free-text guesses. And shared or scoped memory decides what each agent can see. Get those three right and the 'swarm' is just disciplined plumbing.

This post opens the hood: the common topologies, how messages route, and how state is shared without chaos.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• supervisor vs network topologies
• the message protocol agents use
• how the orchestrator routes turns
• shared vs scoped memory
• termination — knowing when to stop

This is the real engine. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Pick a topology first
slides.body: The topology is the wiring. In a supervisor pattern, one orchestrator agent delegates to workers and collects results. In a network pattern, peer agents pass work directly to whoever is best suited next. The shape you pick decides how control and information flow.

slides.title: Messages are structured
slides.body: Agents don't shout free-text at each other. Each handoff is a structured message: who sent it, the role, the content, and often a task or result field. Structure is what lets the orchestrator route correctly and lets you log every exchange for debugging.

slides.title: The orchestrator routes turns
slides.body: Something has to decide who speaks next. A supervisor agent reads the state and picks the next worker; a simpler system follows a fixed sequence; a router uses rules or an LLM to choose. This routing logic is the control plane of the whole system.

slides.title: Shared vs scoped memory
slides.body: Agents need to share state, but not all of it. A shared scratchpad holds the running task and results everyone can see; scoped memory keeps each agent's private working notes out of others' contexts. Deciding what is shared versus private is a core design choice.

slides.title: Termination matters
slides.body: A multi-agent loop must know when to stop — a final answer reached, a max-turns cap, or a supervisor declaring done. Without an explicit stop rule, agents can ping-pong tasks forever, each deferring to the other, burning tokens with no output.

slides.title: A minimal supervisor loop
The mechanics in five lines
bullets: Choose a topology: supervisor or network
Pass structured messages, not free text
An orchestrator decides whose turn it is
Split memory into shared vs scoped
Always set a termination rule + turn cap
