Learn the traps before they page you. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Wrapping the column in a function
slides.body: WHERE LOWER(email) = 'ava@x.io' can't use an index on email — the engine must compute LOWER on every row first, forcing a full scan. The fix: index the expression, or store the value already normalized. Same trap with date math on the column.

slides.title: The function trap + fix
The leading-column rule
slides.body: A composite index on (a, b) helps queries filtering on a, or a and b — but not ones filtering on b alone. The index is sorted by a first, so without a you can't seek into it. Order your composite index by how you actually query.

slides.title: Indexing everything
slides.body: An index on every column feels safe but taxes every write and bloats storage. Unused indexes are pure overhead — the planner never picks them, yet each one slows inserts. Index for your real query patterns, then drop indexes that never get used.

slides.title: Find unused indexes
slides.code:
-- Postgres: indexes the planner has never chosen
SELECT relname AS table, indexrelname AS index, idx_scan
FROM pg_stat_user_indexes
WHERE idx_scan = 0
ORDER BY relname;

-- idx_scan = 0  ->  candidate to DROP

slides.title: Low-selectivity columns
slides.body: Indexing a column with few distinct values — like status with 'active'/'inactive' — rarely helps. If half the rows match, the planner correctly chooses a full scan; the index just costs writes. Indexes pay off when a value narrows to a small fraction of rows.

slides.title: Trusting EXPLAIN alone
slides.body: Plain EXPLAIN shows estimates, not reality. A plan that looks fine can hide a planner mis-estimate that only EXPLAIN ANALYZE reveals by running it. Always check actual rows and timings before declaring a query fixed.

slides.title: Verify with real numbers
slides.code:
-- Compare estimated vs actual rows for mis-estimates
EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS)
SELECT * FROM orders WHERE customer_id = 42;

-- Big gap between rows=est and actual rows? Run:
ANALYZE orders;   -- refresh planner statistics

slides.title: The traps in one line each
bullets: No functions on indexed columns
Composite needs the leading column
Drop indexes the planner never uses
Skip low-selectivity columns
Verify with EXPLAIN ANALYZE, not EXPLAIN

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 84.
slides.body: Next: Day 84 opens a new topic — transactions, locking, and concurrency control.

84-1.caption:
Relational databases make you decide your schema before you've written a line of app code. MongoDB lets the data look like the objects in your program. 🍃

MongoDB is a document database. Instead of rows in rigid tables, it stores flexible JSON-like documents grouped into collections. A document can nest arrays and sub-objects, so one record holds an entire user-with-their-orders without a single join. It's the most popular NoSQL database, and 'NoSQL' just means it dropped the relational table model on purpose.

This post lays the foundation: the words you need before anything about Mongo makes sense.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• what 'document database' actually means
• document, collection, database — the 3 nouns
• BSON vs JSON and the _id field
• how Mongo maps to SQL terms you know
• where it fits (and where it doesn't)

Start here. Save it. 👇

slides.title: A document database
slides.body: MongoDB stores data as documents — flexible, JSON-like records that can nest arrays and sub-objects. Documents live in collections, the rough equivalent of tables, but with no fixed schema. It's the leading NoSQL database: 'NoSQL' just means it dropped the relational table model on purpose, not that SQL is banned.

slides.title: The model in 3 nouns
slides.body: A database holds collections. A collection is a named group of documents — like a table, but rows can differ in shape. A document is one record: a set of field-value pairs, where a value can itself be a number, string, array, or another document. That nesting is the whole point.

slides.title: One document = one user
BSON, not plain JSON
slides.body: On disk Mongo stores BSON — Binary JSON. It's a binary encoding of JSON that adds types JSON lacks: dates, 64-bit integers, binary blobs, and ObjectId. You write and read what looks like JSON, but BSON makes it typed, compact, and fast to traverse.

slides.title: Every document has an _id
slides.body: Each document carries a unique _id field, its primary key. If you don't supply one, Mongo generates an ObjectId — a 12-byte value that embeds a timestamp, so _ids are roughly time-ordered and globally unique without a central counter.

slides.title: Mongo in 3 lines
slides.code:
// insert one document, then read it back
db.users.insertOne({ name: "Ada", roles: ["admin"] })
db.users.findOne({ name: "Ada" })
db.users.countDocuments()

slides.title: The 30-second model
bullets: MongoDB = document database that stores JSON-like records
Database -> Collection -> Document -> Field
Documents nest arrays + sub-objects (no joins needed)
BSON = typed, binary JSON on disk
Every document has a unique _id

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 85.
slides.body: Next: why MongoDB matters — what flexible documents actually buy you.

84-2.caption:
Adding a field to a relational table can mean a migration, a maintenance window, and a tense Slack thread. In MongoDB you just start writing the new field. 🚀

MongoDB matters because it lets your data shape match your code's objects and evolve as fast as your product does. No upfront schema. No joins to assemble one object. And when one machine isn't enough, it shards across many — horizontal scale was designed in, not bolted on.

This post is about the stakes: what document databases save you from, and the trade-offs you accept in return.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• why a flexible schema speeds early development
• reading a whole object in one query (no joins)
• horizontal scaling via sharding
• the cost: no joins, eventual consistency, duplication
• when a document model is the wrong fit

Know why it wins — and when it loses. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Schema that moves at your speed
slides.body: In a relational DB, changing the shape of your data means ALTER TABLE and a migration. In Mongo you just write documents with the new field. Old documents keep their old shape; your code handles the difference. For fast-moving products that don't yet know their final data model, this removes a real source of friction.

slides.title: One read, one whole object
slides.body: If a user and their addresses and roles all live in one document, fetching that user is a single lookup — no joins across five tables. The data is stored the way your app uses it. For read-heavy access patterns where you always need the whole object, this is dramatically simpler and often faster.

slides.title: Scale out, not just up
slides.body: When one server can't hold the load, Mongo shards: it splits a collection across many machines by a shard key, and routes each query to the right shard. You add commodity servers instead of buying one giant box. Horizontal scale was a first-class design goal, not an afterthought.

slides.title: The trade-offs you accept
slides.body: Flexibility costs something. There are no real cross-collection joins, so related data is either duplicated (denormalized) or stitched in app code. Duplication means updates must touch many places. And scaled clusters lean on eventual consistency. You trade rigid guarantees for speed and flexibility.

slides.title: Add a field — no migration
Why it wins
bullets: Flexible schema removes migration friction early
Whole objects in one read, no joins
Sharding gives real horizontal scale
Cost: no joins, duplication, eventual consistency
Reach for SQL when data is deeply relational

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 85.
slides.body: Next: how it works — the storage engine, indexes, and replica sets.

84-3.caption:
MongoDB feels like a magic JSON bucket until you see the machinery: a storage engine, B-tree indexes, replica sets for durability, and a router that finds your shard. ⚙️

When you run a query, Mongo uses indexes to avoid scanning every document, just like a relational DB. Writes go to a primary node and replicate to secondaries, so a failed server doesn't lose your data. The WiredTiger storage engine handles compression and document-level locking, and a write-ahead journal makes crashes recoverable.

This post opens the hood: how Mongo answers a query and survives a node dying.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• WiredTiger: compression + document-level locking
• how indexes turn a collection scan into a seek
• replica sets, primaries, and automatic failover
• the oplog and how secondaries stay in sync
• explain() and read/write concerns

This is the real engine. Save it. 👇

slides.title: WiredTiger storage engine
slides.body: Under the hood, the default WiredTiger engine stores BSON on disk with compression and uses document-level concurrency control: two writers touching different documents don't block each other. A write-ahead journal records changes before they hit the data files, so a crash can be replayed and nothing committed is lost.

slides.title: Indexes turn scan into seek
slides.body: Without an index, a query scans every document in the collection — O(n). An index is a sorted B-tree on a field; Mongo walks it to jump straight to matching documents. Every collection auto-indexes _id. You add indexes for the fields you actually filter and sort on.

slides.title: Replica sets keep you alive
slides.body: A replica set is a group of nodes holding the same data: one primary takes writes, secondaries copy them. If the primary dies, the set holds an election and a secondary is promoted automatically, usually within seconds. Your app reconnects and keeps going — no manual failover, no data loss for committed writes.

slides.title: The oplog syncs secondaries
slides.body: The primary records every change to a special capped collection called the oplog (operations log). Secondaries continuously tail the oplog and replay those operations to stay in sync. The oplog is also how change streams and many backup tools watch the database in real time.

slides.title: Build an index, then prove it's used
Read & write concerns
slides.body: Write concern controls how many nodes must confirm a write before it's acknowledged — w:1 is fast, w:'majority' is safe. Read concern controls how fresh and durable the data you read must be. Together they let you dial the consistency/latency trade-off per operation.

slides.title: The engine in 5 lines
bullets: WiredTiger: compression + document-level locking
Indexes turn O(n) scans into B-tree seeks
Replica set = primary + secondaries + auto-failover
Oplog lets secondaries replay the primary's writes
Read/write concerns dial consistency vs speed

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 85.
slides.body: Next: a hands-on code tour — CRUD, queries, and the aggregation pipeline.

84-4.caption:
Theory is nice, but you learn a database by running queries against it. Here's MongoDB end to end: insert, find, update, delete, and the aggregation pipeline. 💻

This is the code-heavy post. We'll connect, do all four CRUD operations, run real filters with operators like $gt and $in, then build a small aggregation pipeline to group and count. Every snippet is shell/driver syntax you can paste and run against a live database.

Keep this one as your cheat sheet.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• insertOne / insertMany
• find with query operators ($gt, $in, projection)
• updateOne with $set and $inc
• deleteOne and the danger of empty filters
• the aggregation pipeline ($match, $group, $sort)
• the same flow from a Python driver

Paste, run, learn. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 1. Insert documents
2. Find with operators
3. Update documents
4. Delete (carefully)
5. The aggregation pipeline
6. Index + upsert combo
7. Same flow in Python
slides.code:
# pip install pymongo
from pymongo import MongoClient

db = MongoClient("mongodb://localhost:27017").shop
db.orders.insert_one({"user": "ada", "total": 42, "status": "paid"})

for o in db.orders.find({"total": {"$gt": 10}}):
    print(o["user"], o["total"])

slides.title: Cheat-sheet recap
bullets: insertOne / insertMany to write
find(filter, projection).sort() to read
$set, $inc, $in, $gt — the everyday operators
aggregate([...]) for group + transform
Always scope deletes — never deleteMany({})

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 85.
slides.body: Next: the common mistakes that wreck MongoDB performance and data.

84-5.caption:
MongoDB doesn't fail loudly. It quietly scans every document, balloons your storage, or lets a buggy script wipe a collection — and you find out in production. 🛑

Most MongoDB pain isn't the database's fault. It's missing indexes, unbounded arrays, treating a flexible schema as 'no rules', and trusting fire-and-forget writes. The good news: every one of these has a known fix once you can name it.

This post is the failure-mode map: the mistakes that bite, why they happen, and how to avoid them.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• queries with no index (the silent COLLSCAN)
• unbounded arrays growing without limit
• 'schemaless' meaning no validation at all
• default write concern losing data
• empty-filter deletes and updates
• modeling everything as deep nesting

Learn these before prod does. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Queries with no index
slides.body: The most common cause of a slow Mongo app: filtering on a field that isn't indexed. Mongo silently does a COLLSCAN — reading every document — which is fine on 100 docs and a disaster on 10 million. It works in dev and dies in prod. Always index the fields you filter and sort on.

slides.title: Catch the COLLSCAN
Unbounded arrays
slides.body: Embedding is great — until you push to an array forever. A document that appends every event, comment, or login grows without limit, slows every read of that doc, and can hit the 16 MB BSON document size cap. If a list grows unboundedly, store its items in their own collection instead.

slides.title: 'Schemaless' means no rules
slides.body: Flexible schema is not zero schema. Without discipline you get the same field stored as a string in some docs, a number in others, missing in the rest — and your queries silently miss data. Use MongoDB's schema validation and an app-layer model (Mongoose, Pydantic) to enforce shape.

slides.title: Enforce a schema in Mongo
Fire-and-forget writes
slides.body: If you use a weak write concern, a write can be acknowledged before it's safely replicated — then a node fails and the data is gone. For anything that matters, use w:'majority' so a quorum confirms the write before your app moves on. Speed isn't worth silently losing data.

slides.title: The empty-filter footgun
slides.body: deleteMany({}) and updateMany({}, ...) match every document in the collection. One copy-paste with a blank filter and you've wiped or overwritten everything. Always scope destructive operations with a specific filter, and test the filter with a find() first.

slides.title: The safe-driver checklist
bullets: explain() your hot queries — kill COLLSCANs
Cap or externalize growing arrays (16 MB limit)
Validate shape — flexible is not lawless
Use w:'majority' for data you can't lose
Never run an unscoped delete or update

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 85.
slides.body: Day 84 complete. Next day: a new database, same depth.

85-1.caption:
Most people think Redis is "a cache." That's like calling a Swiss Army knife "a blade." 🧰

Redis is an in-memory data store that keeps your data in RAM instead of on disk, which is why a read can take microseconds instead of milliseconds. But the real trick is that it isn't just key-value strings — it speaks lists, hashes, sets, sorted sets, streams, and more, each with commands tuned to that shape.

This post builds the mental model before any code.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• what Redis actually is
• why "in-memory" changes everything
• the core data structures, not just strings
• single-threaded but blazing fast
• cache vs database vs message broker

Get the map before you reach for it. Save it. 👇

slides.title: What is Redis?
slides.body: Redis is an in-memory data structure store. It keeps data in RAM, so reads and writes are measured in microseconds. You talk to it with simple commands over a network, and it gives you not just strings but real data structures — lists, sets, hashes, sorted sets, and streams.

slides.title: Why in-memory matters
slides.body: A disk read costs milliseconds; a RAM read costs microseconds — roughly a thousandfold gap. By holding the working set in memory, Redis answers in well under a millisecond. That speed is what lets it sit in front of a slow database and absorb the bulk of your read traffic.

slides.title: It's not just strings
slides.body: The 'data structure' in the name is the point. A string holds a counter or a blob. A hash models an object's fields. A list is a queue. A set tracks membership. A sorted set powers leaderboards. Picking the right structure is half of using Redis well.

slides.title: Single-threaded, still fast
slides.body: Redis runs your commands on a single thread, one at a time. That sounds slow but removes lock contention entirely — every command is effectively atomic. Because the work is in-memory and the thread never blocks on disk, one core handles 100k+ operations per second.

slides.title: Your first commands
slides.code:
# Set a key, read it back
SET user:42:name "Ava"
GET user:42:name        # -> "Ava"

# Atomic counter
INCR page:home:views    # -> 1
INCR page:home:views    # -> 2

# Expire a key after 60 seconds
SET session:abc "..." EX 60

slides.title: The mental model in one line each
bullets: Redis = data structures in RAM
Microsecond reads, not millisecond
Pick the structure, not just a string
Single thread = atomic commands
It's a cache and far more

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 86.
slides.body: Next: why Redis matters — the real-world problems it solves that a database alone can't.

85-2.caption:
Your database isn't slow because it's bad. It's slow because it's doing careful, durable, disk-backed work — on every single request. 🐢

Redis matters because most apps read the same data far more often than it changes. A cache in front of the database turns thousands of repeated reads into one. But "beyond" is where it earns its keep: rate limiting, sessions, queues, and locks are awkward in a relational database and natural in Redis.

This post is the "so what" behind the speed.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• why reads dominate and repeat
• how caching cuts database load
• latency users actually feel
• jobs your SQL database hates
• what it costs you to skip it

Understand the stakes before the traffic spike. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Reads repeat, data doesn't
slides.body: In most apps the same rows are read thousands of times between changes — a product page, a user profile, a config flag. Hitting the database for each identical read is wasted work. Caching that result once and serving it from memory turns N expensive reads into one.

slides.title: Latency users feel
slides.body: A database query under load might take 50-200 ms. A Redis read is under 1 ms. Multiply that across the dozens of lookups behind one page, and the cache is the difference between a snappy app and a sluggish one — without changing your database at all.

slides.title: It absorbs traffic spikes
slides.body: When traffic surges, the database is the fragile part — connections are finite and queries queue. A warm cache shields it, serving the flood from memory so the database only sees the trickle of cache misses. Redis is the shock absorber in front of your data.

slides.title: The shield in front of the DB
Jobs SQL databases hate
slides.body: Some work is painful in a relational database and trivial in Redis: counting events per second, deduplicating with sets, expiring sessions automatically, ranking with sorted sets, passing messages between services. These aren't caching — they're the 'beyond' that makes Redis a core tool.

slides.title: Rate limiting in 3 commands
slides.code:
# Allow 100 requests per user per minute
# INCR returns the new count atomically
INCR rate:user:42
EXPIRE rate:user:42 60   # only sets TTL on first hit

# If the returned count > 100, reject the request.
# No table, no row locks, microseconds per check.

slides.title: Caching is not optional at scale
slides.body: Teams often treat caching as a 'later' optimization. But the read patterns that make caching valuable exist from day one; they just hurt more as you grow. Skipping it doesn't avoid complexity — it relocates it into an oversized, overworked database.

slides.title: The stakes in one line each
bullets: Reads repeat far more than writes
Cache turns N reads into one
Sub-ms beats 50-200 ms
Redis shields the DB on spikes
It does jobs SQL is bad at

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 86.
slides.body: Next: how Redis actually works — memory, persistence, eviction, and the single-threaded loop.

85-3.caption:
Redis feels like magic until you ask the obvious question: if it's all in RAM, what happens when the power goes out? ⚙️

The answer is the heart of how Redis works. It serves everything from memory on a single-threaded event loop, but it also writes to disk in the background — snapshots and an append-only log — so it can recover. And because RAM is finite, it has eviction policies that decide what to drop when it fills up.

This is the architecture most tutorials skip.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• the single-threaded event loop
• how persistence works (RDB + AOF)
• key expiration and TTLs
• eviction when memory fills
• replication and basic HA

See the mechanics and Redis stops being magic. Save it. 👇

slides.title: How Redis Works Under the Hood
The single-threaded loop
slides.body: Redis processes commands on one thread using an event loop. It accepts connections, reads a command, runs it to completion, and writes the reply — then moves to the next. Because each command finishes before the next starts, every operation is atomic with no locks needed.

slides.title: Persistence: RDB + AOF
slides.body: Redis offers two ways to survive a restart. RDB takes point-in-time snapshots of the whole dataset on a schedule — compact, fast to load. AOF logs every write command so it can replay them. Many setups run both: AOF for durability, RDB for fast restarts.

slides.title: Keys can expire
slides.body: Every key can carry a TTL — a time to live. Redis removes expired keys two ways: lazily, when you next touch the key, and actively, by sampling random keys in the background. TTLs are what make Redis a natural fit for sessions, caches, and temporary locks.

slides.title: TTLs in practice
slides.code:
SET session:abc "data" EX 3600   # expires in 1 hour
TTL session:abc                  # -> 3600 (seconds left)

SET lock:job EX 30 NX            # set only if not exists
PERSIST session:abc              # remove the TTL, keep forever

slides.title: Eviction when memory fills
slides.body: RAM is finite. With maxmemory set, Redis evicts keys when full using a policy you choose. allkeys-lru drops the least recently used key; volatile-ttl drops the key expiring soonest; noeviction refuses new writes. The right policy depends on whether Redis is a pure cache or a store.

slides.title: Replication and HA
slides.body: A replica keeps a live copy of a primary by streaming its writes. Replicas serve reads and stand by to take over. Redis Sentinel watches the primary and promotes a replica if it dies; Redis Cluster shards data across many primaries for scale. That's how Redis stays available.

slides.title: Configure memory + persistence
slides.code:
# redis.conf essentials
maxmemory 2gb
maxmemory-policy allkeys-lru   # evict LRU keys when full

appendonly yes                 # enable AOF durability
appendfsync everysec           # fsync once per second
save 900 1                     # RDB snapshot rules

slides.title: The mechanics in a nutshell
bullets: One thread, atomic commands
RDB snapshots + AOF log = durability
TTLs expire keys lazily + actively
maxmemory + policy control eviction
Replicas + Sentinel/Cluster = HA

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 86.
slides.body: Next: a hands-on cache — wire Redis into real code with the cache-aside pattern.

85-4.caption:
Let's stop talking and wire Redis into real code. We'll build the cache-aside pattern — the one you'll use 90% of the time — and watch a slow database call turn into a microsecond memory hit. 💻

You'll see how to check the cache, fall back to the database on a miss, write the result back with a TTL, and invalidate it on update. Every snippet is real Python with redis-py that you can paste and run against a local Redis.

This post is code-heavy on purpose, miss and hit shown side by side.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• connect to Redis from Python
• the cache-aside read pattern
• set values with a TTL
• invalidate the cache on writes
• use a hash for structured objects

Copy, run, and watch it speed up. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Caching with Redis, Step by Step
The pattern: cache-aside
slides.body: Cache-aside is the default caching strategy. On a read, check Redis first. If the value is there (a hit), return it. If not (a miss), read the database, store the result in Redis with a TTL, and return it. The app owns the cache logic — simple and predictable.

slides.title: 1. Connect from Python
slides.code:
# pip install redis
import redis

r = redis.Redis(
    host="localhost", port=6379, db=0,
    decode_responses=True,   # return str, not bytes
)

r.ping()   # -> True if connected

slides.title: 2. The cache-aside read
slides.code:
import json

def get_user(user_id):
    key = f"user:{user_id}"
    cached = r.get(key)
    if cached is not None:
        return json.loads(cached)        # cache HIT

    user = db_fetch_user(user_id)        # cache MISS
    r.set(key, json.dumps(user), ex=300) # store, 5 min TTL
    return user

slides.title: 3. Invalidate on write
slides.code:
def update_user(user_id, changes):
    db_update_user(user_id, changes)     # write source of truth
    r.delete(f"user:{user_id}")          # drop the stale cache
    # Next read is a miss -> reloads fresh data, re-caches it

slides.title: Why delete, not update
slides.body: On a write, deleting the cached key is safer than rewriting it. Delete-then-reload guarantees the next read fetches the true current state from the database. Trying to write the new value into the cache risks racing with concurrent updates and caching a stale or partial value.

slides.title: 4. Structured data with a hash
Always set a TTL
slides.body: A cache entry with no expiry lives forever — and goes stale forever if an invalidation is ever missed. Set a TTL on cached values so even a missed delete self-heals within minutes. Permanent keys belong to data Redis owns, not to copies of your database.

slides.title: The recipe
bullets: Check Redis, fall back to DB
Store with a TTL on every miss
Delete the key on writes
Use hashes for field-level access
Never cache without an expiry

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 86.
slides.body: Next: the caching mistakes that cause stampedes, stale data, and outages — and the fixes.

85-5.caption:
A cache that works in testing can still take down production — not despite being a cache, but because of how caches fail under load. 🚩

The classic disasters are quiet: a key expires and a thousand requests stampede the database at once, an invalidation gets missed and users see stale data for hours, or memory fills and Redis silently evicts the keys you needed most. Each one survives code review and works perfectly until the day real traffic finds it.

This post is the field guide to the traps that bite under load.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• cache stampede (thundering herd)
• the stale data problem
• treating Redis as durable
• no maxmemory policy set
• big keys and unbounded growth

Learn the traps before they page you. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Redis Mistakes That Bite in Production
The cache stampede
slides.body: A popular key expires. In the same instant, a thousand requests miss the cache and all hit the database to rebuild it — a thundering herd that can crash the very database the cache was protecting. The expiry that should help becomes the trigger for an outage.

slides.title: Stampede fix: a rebuild lock
Stale data forever
slides.body: If a write updates the database but the cache invalidation fails or is forgotten, users see old data until the key happens to expire. Without a TTL, that's forever. The fix is twofold: invalidate on every write path, and always keep a TTL as a safety net.

slides.title: Treating Redis as durable
slides.body: Redis can persist, but it isn't your system of record. A crash between writes, an eviction, or a misconfigured flush can lose recent data. Never store the only copy of something you can't lose — orders, payments, user accounts — in Redis as if it were a database.

slides.title: No maxmemory policy
slides.body: Leave maxmemory unset and Redis grows until the OS kills it. Set noeviction by accident and writes start failing under load. You must consciously choose a policy that matches the role: allkeys-lru for a pure cache, volatile-* when only some keys are disposable.

slides.title: Set the policy on purpose
slides.code:
# For a pure cache: cap memory and evict LRU keys
CONFIG SET maxmemory 2gb
CONFIG SET maxmemory-policy allkeys-lru

# Inspect what's happening
INFO memory          # used_memory, evicted_keys
CONFIG GET maxmemory-policy

slides.title: Big keys and slow commands
slides.body: A single huge key — a list of millions of items, a giant hash — makes commands on it slow, and since Redis is single-threaded, one slow command blocks everyone. Avoid KEYS * in production (it scans everything); use SCAN. Keep individual keys bounded.

slides.title: Scan, don't block
slides.code:
# BAD: KEYS blocks the single thread on a big DB
# KEYS user:*

# GOOD: SCAN iterates in small cursor-based batches
SCAN 0 MATCH user:* COUNT 100
# returns a cursor + a batch; repeat until cursor is 0

slides.title: The traps in one line each
bullets: Guard hot rebuilds against stampedes
Invalidate on write AND keep a TTL
Redis is a cache, not your truth
Always set a maxmemory policy
Avoid big keys and KEYS *; use SCAN

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 86.
slides.body: Next: Day 86 opens a new topic in the NoSQL track — choosing the right database for the job.

86-1.caption:
Your database can find rows where name = 'Ada'. It cannot find rows that mean roughly the same thing as 'a pioneering programmer'. That gap is what vector search fills. 📍

Pinecone is a managed vector database: a system built to store embeddings — long lists of numbers that capture the meaning of text, images, or audio — and find the nearest ones to a query embedding in milliseconds, across billions of vectors. It's the storage-and-search layer most teams reach for when they build semantic search or RAG.

This post lays the foundation: the words you need before any of the 'how' makes sense.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• what an embedding and a vector actually are
• what 'vector database' means vs a normal DB
• similarity search and nearest-neighbor
• index, namespace, metadata — the core nouns
• where Pinecone fits (and where it doesn't)

Start here. Save it. 👇

slides.title: A managed vector database
slides.body: Pinecone stores vectors — embeddings produced by an AI model — and finds the ones most similar to a query vector, fast, at scale. 'Managed' means you don't run servers, tune the index, or handle sharding; you call an API. It's the search layer behind semantic search, recommendations, and RAG.

slides.title: What an embedding is
slides.body: An embedding is a list of numbers — say 1536 of them — that a model assigns to a piece of text, an image, or audio. Things with similar meaning get vectors that point in similar directions. The numbers aren't human-readable; their geometry is what carries the meaning.

slides.title: Similarity search
slides.body: Instead of exact matches, vector search ranks items by how close their vectors are to the query vector. 'Close' is measured by cosine similarity or dot product. The result is items that mean something similar — even if they share no keywords at all.

slides.title: Nearest-neighbor at scale
slides.body: Finding the closest vectors is the k-nearest-neighbor problem. Comparing the query to every stored vector (exact kNN) is too slow at scale, so Pinecone uses approximate nearest neighbor (ANN): it trades a tiny bit of accuracy for huge speed, returning top matches in milliseconds across billions of vectors.

slides.title: The core nouns
slides.body: An index is the container that holds your vectors and defines their dimension and distance metric. A namespace partitions an index so you can isolate tenants or datasets. Metadata is the JSON you attach to each vector (source, date, tags) so you can filter results by it.

slides.title: A vector record
The 30-second model
bullets: Pinecone = managed vector database
Embedding = numbers that encode meaning
Search by similarity, not exact match
ANN finds nearest neighbors fast at scale
Index -> Namespace -> Vector + Metadata

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 87.
slides.body: Next: why vector search matters — what semantic search and RAG actually unlock.

86-2.caption:
Keyword search fails the moment a user phrases a question differently than your docs do. Vector search doesn't care about the words — it cares about the meaning. 🚀

Pinecone matters because it makes semantic search and Retrieval-Augmented Generation practical at production scale. It's the memory layer that lets an LLM answer from your private data, the recall engine behind 'find me documents like this one', and the part of a RAG stack that has to be fast, fresh, and filterable.

This post is about the stakes: what vector search unlocks, why managed beats DIY, and the trade-offs you accept.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• why keyword search breaks on meaning
• how RAG uses a vector DB as the LLM's memory
• why 'managed' saves you real operational pain
• metadata filtering = semantic + structured search
• the trade-offs: cost, freshness, approximate results

Know why it wins — and when it doesn't. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Search by meaning, not words
slides.body: Keyword search matches tokens, so 'how do I reset my password' misses a doc titled 'recovering account access'. Vector search embeds both and compares meaning, so it finds the right doc despite zero shared words. This is the difference between frustrated users and a search that just works.

slides.title: It's the memory behind RAG
slides.body: LLMs don't know your private data and have a fixed context window. RAG fixes both: embed your documents into Pinecone, retrieve the few most relevant chunks for each question, and feed only those to the model. The vector DB is what lets a general model answer from your specific knowledge.

slides.title: Managed removes the hard parts
slides.body: Running vector search yourself means tuning ANN index parameters, sharding across machines, replicating for uptime, and rebuilding indexes as data grows. Pinecone hides all of that behind an API. You upsert and query; it handles scale, availability, and the index internals you'd otherwise babysit.

slides.title: Filter while you search
slides.body: Pure similarity isn't enough in production — you often need 'similar AND from this user AND newer than last month'. Pinecone applies metadata filters during the search, combining semantic relevance with structured constraints in one query. That keeps results both meaningful and scoped to what's allowed.

slides.title: Filtered semantic query
The trade-offs you accept
slides.body: Vector search isn't free. Results are approximate, so a relevant item can occasionally be missed. Storing embeddings costs money that grows with your corpus. And the index only knows what you've upserted — stale data means stale answers. You trade exactness and zero-cost for speed, scale, and semantic recall.

right.title: Wrong tool
items: small data a list scan handles

slides.title: Why it wins
bullets: Finds meaning keyword search misses
Gives LLMs grounded, private memory (RAG)
Managed: no index tuning or sharding
Metadata filters = semantic + structured
Cost: approximate, priced, only as fresh as upserts

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 87.
slides.body: Next: how it works — embeddings, ANN indexes, and the query path inside.

86-3.caption:
Pinecone feels like magic until you see the machinery: an embedding model out front, an approximate-nearest-neighbor index in the middle, and a distance metric deciding what 'similar' means. ⚙️

When you query, Pinecone doesn't compare your vector to every stored vector — that's too slow. It walks a graph or partitions space to reach the likely neighbors fast, scores them by cosine or dot product, applies your metadata filters, and returns the top-k. Understanding this is how you debug bad results and tune for speed vs accuracy.

This post opens the hood.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• embeddings in, vectors out — dimension must match
• distance metrics: cosine, dot, euclidean
• ANN vs exact kNN — the speed/accuracy trade
• how HNSW graphs find neighbors fast
• the query path: filter, score, return top-k

This is the real engine. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Embeddings in, vectors out
slides.body: Pinecone doesn't make embeddings — you do, with a model like OpenAI's text-embedding-3 or an open-source one. Every vector you store and every query must use the same model and the same dimension as the index. Mismatched dimensions are rejected; mismatched models silently return garbage results.

slides.title: Distance defines 'similar'
slides.body: The index is created with one metric. Cosine measures the angle between vectors (direction, ignoring length) and is the common default for text. Dot product factors in magnitude too. Euclidean measures straight-line distance. The metric must match how your embedding model was trained, or scores mean nothing.

slides.title: HNSW: the graph trick
slides.body: A common ANN structure is HNSW — a layered graph where each vector links to nearby vectors. Search starts at a sparse top layer, greedily hops toward the query, then drops to denser layers to refine. It reaches the neighborhood in a few hops instead of scanning everything, which is why queries stay fast as data grows.

slides.title: The query path
slides.body: On query, Pinecone narrows candidates with your metadata filter, traverses the ANN index to find near vectors, scores each by the index metric, then returns the top-k with ids, scores, and metadata. Filtering and scoring happen inside the engine so you get back exactly the ranked results you asked for.

slides.title: Create index, then query
slides.code:
from pinecone import Pinecone, ServerlessSpec

pc = Pinecone(api_key="...")
pc.create_index(
    name="docs",
    dimension=1536,        # must match your model
    metric="cosine",       # the distance metric
    spec=ServerlessSpec(cloud="aws", region="us-east-1")
)
index = pc.Index("docs")
index.query(vector=query_emb, top_k=5, include_metadata=True)

slides.title: Recall vs latency
slides.body: ANN lets you dial a trade-off: search more of the graph for higher recall (fewer missed neighbors) at the cost of latency, or less for speed. Pinecone manages most of this for you, but the concept matters — 'approximate' means a relevant result can occasionally be missed, and that's the price of millisecond search.

slides.title: The engine in 5 lines
bullets: You bring embeddings; dimension must match the index
Metric (cosine/dot/euclidean) defines 'similar'
ANN beats exact kNN: sub-linear, ~99% recall
HNSW graphs hop to neighbors in a few steps
Query = filter -> traverse -> score -> top-k

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 87.
slides.body: Next: a hands-on code tour — embed, upsert, query, and filter end to end.

86-4.caption:
You learn a vector database by pushing real data into it and querying it back. Here's Pinecone end to end: create an index, embed text, upsert, query with filters, and update. 💻

This is the code-heavy post. We'll connect, build embeddings with an off-the-shelf model, batch-upsert vectors with metadata, run a top-k semantic query, add metadata filtering, then upsert into namespaces. Every snippet is real Python you can paste against a live Pinecone project.

Keep this one as your cheat sheet.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• init the client + create a serverless index
• embed text into vectors
• batched upsert with ids + metadata
• top-k semantic query, reading scores
• metadata-filtered query
• namespaces for multi-tenant data

Paste, run, learn. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 1. Init + create index
slides.code:
# pip install pinecone openai
from pinecone import Pinecone, ServerlessSpec

pc = Pinecone(api_key="PINECONE_API_KEY")
if "docs" not in pc.list_indexes().names():
    pc.create_index(
        name="docs", dimension=1536, metric="cosine",
        spec=ServerlessSpec(cloud="aws", region="us-east-1")
    )
index = pc.Index("docs")

slides.title: 2. Embed text
slides.code:
from openai import OpenAI
client = OpenAI()

def embed(texts):
    r = client.embeddings.create(
        model="text-embedding-3-small",  # 1536 dims
        input=texts
    )
    return [d.embedding for d in r.data]

vecs = embed(["Reset your password from Settings.",
              "Cancel a subscription anytime."])

slides.title: 3. Upsert with metadata
slides.code:
# id, vector values, and filterable metadata
index.upsert(vectors=[
    {"id": "a1", "values": vecs[0],
     "metadata": {"topic": "account", "page": 3}},
    {"id": "a2", "values": vecs[1],
     "metadata": {"topic": "billing", "page": 8}},
])
# tip: upsert in batches of ~100 for throughput

slides.title: 4. Semantic query + scores
slides.code:
q = embed(["how do I change my login?"])[0]
res = index.query(vector=q, top_k=3,
                  include_metadata=True)

for m in res["matches"]:
    print(m["id"], round(m["score"], 3), m["metadata"])
# higher score = more similar (cosine)

slides.title: 5. Query with a filter
6. Namespaces for tenants
slides.code:
# isolate each customer's data in a namespace
index.upsert(vectors=[...], namespace="acme")
index.upsert(vectors=[...], namespace="globex")

# queries stay within one namespace
index.query(vector=q, top_k=5, namespace="acme")

slides.title: 7. Update + delete
slides.code:
# update just the metadata of an existing vector
index.update(id="a1", set_metadata={"topic": "security"})

# delete by id, or wipe a whole namespace
index.delete(ids=["a2"])
index.delete(delete_all=True, namespace="globex")

slides.title: Cheat-sheet recap
bullets: create_index(dimension, metric) once
embed() text with a fixed model
upsert ids + values + metadata, in batches
query(vector, top_k, filter, namespace)
update metadata; delete by id or namespace

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 87.
slides.body: Next: the common mistakes that wreck Pinecone results and budgets.

86-5.caption:
Pinecone rarely throws an error. It just returns subtly wrong results, silently mixes models, or quietly runs up a bill — and you find out when users complain. 🛑

Most Pinecone pain isn't the database's fault. It's mismatched embedding models, the wrong distance metric, bad chunking, forgotten metadata, and treating 'approximate' as 'broken'. Every one of these has a known fix once you can name it.

This post is the failure-mode map: the mistakes that bite, why they happen, and how to avoid them.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• mixing embedding models between upsert and query
• picking the wrong distance metric
• chunks too big or too small
• no metadata, so you can't filter
• expecting exact results from ANN
• ignoring cost and stale data

Learn these before prod does. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Mixing embedding models
slides.body: Vectors are only comparable if they come from the same model. Upsert with one model and query with another — or with a different version — and the geometry no longer lines up. No error fires; you just get nonsense rankings. Pin one model and version for both indexing and querying.

slides.title: Pin the model in one place
slides.code:
# single source of truth for embeddings
EMBED_MODEL = "text-embedding-3-small"  # 1536 dims

def embed(texts):
    r = client.embeddings.create(model=EMBED_MODEL, input=texts)
    return [d.embedding for d in r.data]
# use embed() for BOTH upsert and query

slides.title: The wrong distance metric
slides.body: The index metric must match how your embedding model was trained to be compared. Most modern text models expect cosine (or normalized dot product). Create the index with euclidean by mistake and your similarity scores rank poorly. Check your model's docs, then set the metric once at index creation — it can't be changed later.

slides.title: Chunking too big or small
slides.body: Embed a whole 20-page doc as one vector and the meaning is diluted; retrieval gets vague. Embed single sentences and you lose context, returning fragments. Aim for coherent passages — roughly a few hundred tokens — with a little overlap, so each chunk is specific yet self-contained.

slides.title: Storing no metadata
slides.body: If you upsert only ids and vectors, you can't filter by tenant, date, or source later — and you can't trace a result back to its original document. Always attach metadata: the source, a stable reference, and any fields you'll want to filter on. Adding it after the fact means re-upserting everything.

slides.title: Upsert with traceable metadata
Expecting exact results
slides.body: ANN is approximate by design. Demanding that the absolute top match always appears, or treating an occasional miss as a bug, fights the tool. If you truly need exact recall for a small set, do an exact scan instead. For scale, accept ~99% recall and tune top_k generously so the right answer is in the returned set.

slides.title: Ignoring cost and freshness
slides.body: Every vector stored costs money, and the index only knows what you've upserted. Forgetting to delete dead data inflates the bill; forgetting to re-embed changed documents serves stale answers. Build an update path: upsert on change, delete on removal, and prune what you no longer search.

slides.title: The safe-driver checklist
bullets: Same model + version for upsert and query
Set metric to match the model (usually cosine)
Chunk into coherent passages with overlap
Always store filterable, traceable metadata
Treat ANN as approximate; keep data fresh

slides.body: Day 86 complete. Next day: a new tool, same depth.

87-1.caption:
Most vector databases hand you embeddings and walk away. Weaviate hands you a schema, an inference pipeline, and a hybrid search engine in one box. 🧩

Weaviate is an open-source vector database that stores objects together with their vector embeddings and lets you query by meaning, not just keywords. You define classes (collections) with typed properties, then either bring your own vectors or let a built-in vectorizer module generate them at import time.

The big idea: it treats "the object" and "its meaning" as a single unit. You don't bolt a vector index onto a separate document store — the object, its metadata, and its embedding live and move together.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• what Weaviate actually is
• how it differs from a plain ANN library
• collections, objects, and vectors
• modules and the vectorizer concept
• when Weaviate is the right pick

Get the mental model before the code. Save it. 👇

slides.title: The one-sentence definition
slides.body: Weaviate is an open-source vector database: it stores data objects and their vector embeddings together, then lets you search by semantic similarity, by keyword, or both at once. It is a full database — schema, persistence, filtering, replication — not just an index.

slides.title: Not just an ANN library
bullets: FAISS/HNSWlib: a raw index, vectors only, no persistence
Weaviate: objects + vectors + metadata + a query API
It owns storage, schema, filters, and CRUD
It can generate the vectors for you via modules

slides.title: Collections (classes)
slides.body: Data lives in collections (historically called classes). A collection defines a typed shape: named properties like title (text), price (number), createdAt (date). Every object you insert belongs to one collection and follows its schema, much like a table in a relational DB.

slides.title: Objects + their vectors
slides.body: Each object stores its properties AND one or more vector embeddings. The vector is what enables similarity search; the properties enable filtering and keyword search. They are stored as one unit, so a single query can do both.

slides.title: Modules do the embedding
slides.body: A vectorizer module (text2vec-openai, text2vec-cohere, text2vec-transformers, and more) turns your text into vectors automatically at import and query time. You can also disable it and supply your own vectors with the none vectorizer.

slides.title: What you get for free
bullets: GraphQL + REST + gRPC query APIs
Vector + keyword (BM25) + hybrid search
Metadata filtering combined with vector search
Built-in modules for RAG, reranking, generation

slides.title: Connect to Weaviate
slides.code:
# pip install -U weaviate-client
import weaviate

# Local instance via Docker
client = weaviate.connect_to_local()

print(client.is_ready())  # True
client.close()

slides.title: When to reach for it
bullets: Semantic search over docs/products/support tickets
RAG backends that need filtering + similarity
You want vectorization handled in-database
You need a real DB, not an in-memory index

slides.title: Where it sits in your stack
Save this. Follow for Day 88.
slides.body: Next: why semantic search beats keyword matching — and why that matters for Weaviate.

87-2.caption:
Keyword search asks "does this string appear?" Semantic search asks "does this mean the same thing?" That gap is why teams adopt Weaviate. 🚀

The value of a vector database isn't speed for its own sake — it's that it lets users find things by intent. A search for "laptop overheating" surfaces a ticket titled "MacBook runs hot under load" with zero shared keywords. That's impossible with BM25 alone.

But meaning-only search has blind spots too: exact codes, names, and rare tokens. Weaviate's answer is hybrid search and tight metadata filtering, so you get recall AND precision in one query.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• why keyword search quietly fails
• what semantic search unlocks
• why pure vector search isn't enough either
• how hybrid fusion fixes both
• the cost/latency tradeoffs to budget for

Know why before you commit infrastructure. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Keyword search misses meaning
slides.body: BM25 and SQL LIKE match tokens, not intent. "car won't start" never matches "engine fails to crank" despite identical meaning. As vocabulary diverges between users and documents, recall collapses — and you never see what you missed.

slides.title: Two ways to search
bullets: Keyword: exact tokens, great for codes & names
Semantic: meaning, great for paraphrase & synonyms
Keyword fails on synonyms; semantic fails on rare IDs
Real apps need both — that's the whole pitch

slides.title: Semantic search unlocks intent
slides.body: Embeddings place similar meanings near each other in vector space. A query vector lands close to relevant objects even with no word overlap. This is what makes multilingual, typo-tolerant, and conceptual search feel like magic to users.

slides.title: But pure vectors aren't enough
slides.body: Vector-only search struggles with exact part numbers, names, and rare strings — embeddings smear them into nearby concepts. It also can't easily enforce "price < 50". You still need keyword matching and structured filters.

slides.title: Hybrid fusion is the answer
slides.body: Weaviate runs vector and BM25 searches in parallel and fuses the rankings (relativeScore or rankedFusion). An alpha parameter dials between pure keyword (0) and pure vector (1). You get conceptual recall plus exact-match precision in a single call.

slides.title: One query, both worlds
slides.code:
from weaviate.classes.query import HybridFusion

articles = client.collections.get("Article")
res = articles.query.hybrid(
    query="laptop overheating",
    alpha=0.5,  # 0=keyword, 1=vector
    fusion_type=HybridFusion.RELATIVE_SCORE,
    limit=5,
)
for o in res.objects:
    print(o.properties["title"])

slides.title: Business payoffs
bullets: Higher recall on support & docs search
Fewer "no results" dead ends
Cross-lingual matching without translation
RAG retrieval that actually finds the right chunk

slides.title: Costs to budget for
bullets: Embedding API calls cost money + latency
HNSW index lives in RAM — size it
Re-embedding on model change is expensive
Filters can slow ANN if not configured

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 88.
slides.body: Next: open the hood — HNSW, the inverted index, and how a query actually runs.

87-3.caption:
When you fire a query at Weaviate, four things happen in milliseconds: vectorize, traverse a graph, scan an inverted index, and fuse. Here's the machinery. ⚙️

Weaviate isn't magic — it's an HNSW graph for approximate nearest-neighbor search sitting next to a BM25 inverted index, both pointing at the same object store. Understanding the moving parts tells you exactly which knobs change recall, latency, and memory.

This post traces a single request end to end and explains the data structures it touches.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• the lifecycle of one query
• how HNSW finds neighbors fast
• what the inverted index does
• how filtering interacts with ANN
• the parameters that control the tradeoffs

Understand the engine and tuning stops being guesswork. Save it. 👇

slides.title: The query lifecycle
slides.body: A query is vectorized (by a module or supplied directly), the vector traverses the HNSW graph to find nearest objects, any filters prune candidates, optional BM25 runs in parallel, scores are fused, then objects are fetched and returned.

slides.title: HNSW in plain terms
slides.body: Hierarchical Navigable Small World is a layered graph. Search starts at a sparse top layer for big jumps, then descends to denser layers for fine-grained hops, greedily moving toward the query vector. It finds approximate nearest neighbors in roughly log time instead of scanning everything.

slides.title: The inverted index
slides.body: For keyword search and filtering, Weaviate keeps an inverted index mapping tokens and property values to the objects containing them. BM25 scores documents by term frequency; filters use the same structure to quickly find objects matching where-conditions.

slides.title: Two indexes, one store
bullets: HNSW vector index: similarity, approximate
Inverted index: keywords + exact filters
Both reference the same object IDs
A query can use either or fuse both

slides.title: Filtering meets ANN
slides.body: Weaviate supports pre-filtering: it builds an allow-list from the inverted index, then restricts the HNSW walk to those objects. This keeps results correct without a separate post-filter pass that could return too few hits.

slides.title: Tune the HNSW index
slides.code:
import weaviate.classes.config as wc

client.collections.create(
    name="Article",
    vector_index_config=wc.Configure.VectorIndex.hnsw(
        ef_construction=128,   # build-time quality
        max_connections=32,    # graph degree (M)
        ef=64,                 # query-time recall/speed
        distance_metric=wc.VectorDistances.COSINE,
    ),
)

slides.title: Knobs that matter
bullets: ef (query): higher = better recall, slower
ef_construction / M: build quality vs memory
distance metric: cosine, dot, l2 — match your model
PQ / BQ compression: cut RAM at small recall cost

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 88.
slides.body: Next: a full hands-on — schema, import, and search end to end in code.

87-4.caption:
Theory is cheap. Here's a complete Weaviate workflow you can paste and run: define a collection, batch-import objects, and search three different ways. 💻

We use the v4 Python client (the modern, gRPC-backed one). The flow is always the same: connect, create a collection with a vectorizer, import data in a batch, then query. We'll do near-text semantic search, hybrid search, and a filtered search so you see how the pieces compose.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• connect + create a typed collection
• batch-import with auto-vectorization
• semantic (near_text) search
• hybrid search with a filter
• read distances and clean up

Copy it, run it, then bend it to your data. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Weaviate in Code, End to End
1. Connect + create collection
slides.code:
import weaviate
import weaviate.classes.config as wc

client = weaviate.connect_to_local()

client.collections.create(
    name="Article",
    vectorizer_config=wc.Configure.Vectorizer.text2vec_openai(),
    properties=[
        wc.Property(name="title", data_type=wc.DataType.TEXT),
        wc.Property(name="body", data_type=wc.DataType.TEXT),
        wc.Property(name="topic", data_type=wc.DataType.TEXT),
    ],
)

slides.title: 2. Batch import (auto-vectorized)
slides.code:
articles = client.collections.get("Article")

docs = [
    {"title": "Why my laptop runs hot",
     "body": "Thermal throttling under load...",
     "topic": "hardware"},
    {"title": "Intro to vector search",
     "body": "Embeddings place meaning in space...",
     "topic": "ml"},
]

with articles.batch.dynamic() as batch:
    for d in docs:
        batch.add_object(properties=d)
print("failed:", articles.batch.failed_objects)

slides.title: 3. Semantic search (near_text)
slides.code:
from weaviate.classes.query import MetadataQuery

res = articles.query.near_text(
    query="computer overheating",
    limit=3,
    return_metadata=MetadataQuery(distance=True),
)
for o in res.objects:
    print(o.properties["title"],
          round(o.metadata.distance, 3))

slides.title: 4. Hybrid search + filter
slides.code:
from weaviate.classes.query import Filter

res = articles.query.hybrid(
    query="vector embeddings",
    alpha=0.5,
    filters=Filter.by_property("topic").equal("ml"),
    limit=3,
)
for o in res.objects:
    print(o.properties["title"])

slides.title: Why batch import matters
slides.body: batch.dynamic() streams objects in tuned batches and parallel requests, so vectorization and indexing happen efficiently. Inserting one-by-one is dramatically slower and hammers your embedding API. Always check failed_objects after a batch.

slides.title: 5. Inspect + clean up
slides.code:
# fetch by count
print(len(articles.query.fetch_objects(limit=100).objects))

# delete the whole collection
client.collections.delete("Article")

client.close()  # always close the client

slides.title: Practical notes
bullets: Set OPENAI_APIKEY in the env or headers for the module
near_text needs a vectorizer; near_vector does not
distance meaning depends on the metric you chose
Use context manager or always client.close()

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 88.
slides.body: Next: the mistakes that silently wreck recall, RAM, and your bill.

87-5.caption:
Weaviate fails quietly. No error — just bad results, a ballooning RAM bill, or a query that returns three rows when you expected thirty. 🧯

Most Weaviate pain isn't bugs; it's mismatches: a distance metric that doesn't fit your embedding model, a vectorizer swapped without re-embedding, filters that quietly shrink your candidate set. These don't crash. They degrade.

This post collects the traps that bite real deployments and the one-line fixes for each.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• mixing embedding models across imports
• the wrong distance metric
• ignoring memory and RAM limits
• one-by-one inserts instead of batching
• filter and consistency surprises

Learn these now, not in production. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 1. Mixing embedding models
slides.body: Vectors from text-embedding-3-small and ada-002 live in incompatible spaces. If you import some objects with one model and query with another, similarity is meaningless. Pin ONE model per collection; re-embed everything if you change it.

slides.title: 2. Wrong distance metric
slides.body: OpenAI and most modern text models are tuned for cosine similarity. Leaving the default or picking l2 when your model expects cosine quietly tanks recall. Always set distance_metric to match your model's recommendation.

slides.title: Fix: pin model + metric
slides.code:
import weaviate.classes.config as wc

client.collections.create(
    name="Article",
    vectorizer_config=wc.Configure.Vectorizer
        .text2vec_openai(model="text-embedding-3-small"),
    vector_index_config=wc.Configure.VectorIndex.hnsw(
        distance_metric=wc.VectorDistances.COSINE,
    ),
)

slides.title: 3. Forgetting RAM cost
slides.body: The HNSW index is held in memory. Millions of 1536-dim float32 vectors plus graph links can need tens of GB. People deploy on a tiny node and hit OOM. Estimate RAM up front, or enable PQ/BQ compression.

slides.title: Fix: enable compression
4. Inserting one at a time
slides.body: Looping insert() per object is slow and pummels your embedding API with serial calls. On big imports this turns minutes into hours. Use batch.dynamic() and always inspect batch.failed_objects afterwards.

slides.title: 5. Filter & consistency traps
slides.body: Aggressive filters can shrink the candidate set so much that ANN returns fewer hits than your limit. And on multi-node clusters, default eventual consistency means a just-written object may not appear instantly. Set consistency_level when you need read-after-write.

slides.title: Quick prevention checklist
bullets: One embedding model per collection, documented
Distance metric matches the model
Estimate RAM; compress if large
Batch imports + check failed_objects
Test filters at realistic data scale

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 88.
slides.body: You can now run Weaviate without the silent failures. Next day, new topic.

88-1.caption:
Most vector databases ask you to choose: open-source you self-host, or a managed cloud you can't see inside. Qdrant refuses the trade — the same Rust engine runs on your laptop and in their cloud. 🦀

Qdrant is an open-source vector database written in Rust. It stores embeddings — the long lists of numbers a model uses to encode meaning — and finds the nearest ones to a query in milliseconds, with rich payload filtering baked into the search itself.

This post lays the vocabulary before any of the 'how' makes sense.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• what a vector database actually does
• Qdrant's core nouns: collection, point, payload
• how Qdrant differs from Pinecone and pgvector
• why 'open-source + Rust' is the headline
• where Qdrant fits in a real stack

Start here. Save it. 👇

slides.title: An open-source vector database
slides.body: Qdrant stores embeddings — vectors a model produces for text, images, or audio — and returns the ones most similar to a query vector, fast. It's written in Rust, runs as a single binary or Docker container, and exposes a REST and gRPC API. The same engine powers local dev and Qdrant Cloud.

slides.title: What a vector is here
slides.body: A vector is a fixed-length list of floats — say 768 or 1536 of them — that an embedding model assigns to a piece of content. Similar meaning produces vectors pointing in similar directions. Qdrant never creates these; you embed your data with a model, then hand Qdrant the numbers.

slides.title: Collection, point, payload
slides.body: A collection is the container that fixes a vector size and distance metric. A point is one stored item: an id, its vector, and an optional payload. The payload is arbitrary JSON — tags, dates, user ids — that you can filter on during search. These three nouns are 90% of Qdrant's mental model.

slides.title: A single point
Similarity, not exact match
slides.body: Qdrant ranks points by how close their vector is to the query vector. 'Close' is defined per collection: cosine, dot product, or euclidean. The result is items that mean something similar — even if they share zero keywords with the query. That semantic recall is the whole point.

slides.title: Why 'Rust + open-source' matters
slides.body: Rust gives Qdrant predictable memory use and speed without a garbage-collector pause mid-query. Open-source means you can read the engine, run it air-gapped, and avoid vendor lock-in. You can prototype on a laptop with the exact code that runs in production — no surprise behavior gaps between tiers.

slides.title: The 30-second model
bullets: Qdrant = open-source vector DB in Rust
Collection fixes vector size + metric
Point = id + vector + payload
Search by similarity, filter by payload
Same engine: laptop to cloud

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 89.
slides.body: Next: why Qdrant matters — what payload filtering and self-hosting actually unlock.

88-2.caption:
You can have the most relevant document in your index and still serve the wrong answer — because it belonged to another tenant, or it was deleted last week. Pure similarity isn't enough. 🎯

Qdrant matters because it treats filtering as a first-class part of search, not an afterthought. Combine 'semantically similar' with 'AND this user AND newer than March' in one query, run it on your own hardware for data you can't ship to a vendor, and pay nothing in license fees.

This post is about the stakes: what Qdrant unlocks and the trade-offs you accept.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• why filtering belongs inside the search
• how self-hosting solves data-residency pain
• Rust speed: no GC pauses under load
• RAG, recommendations, dedup, multimodal
• the trade-offs: ops burden, approximate results

Know why it wins — and when it doesn't. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Filtering is first-class
slides.body: Production search is rarely 'just find similar'. You need 'similar AND owned by this tenant AND not archived'. Qdrant applies payload filters inside the ANN search using a filterable index, so you don't over-fetch then discard. That keeps results both meaningful and correctly scoped without blowing up latency.

slides.title: Your data stays on your hardware
slides.body: Healthcare, finance, and EU data often can't leave your infrastructure. Because Qdrant is open-source and self-hostable, you run it inside your own VPC or even air-gapped. No embeddings ever leave the building. Managed-only databases can't offer that, which rules them out for whole industries.

slides.title: Rust speed without GC pauses
slides.body: Garbage-collected engines can stall mid-query when the collector runs, spiking tail latency. Rust has no GC, so Qdrant's p99 stays tight under load. Combined with SIMD-accelerated distance math, it serves high query volumes from a small footprint — fewer machines for the same throughput.

slides.title: It's the memory behind RAG
slides.body: An LLM doesn't know your private data and has a fixed context window. Embed your documents into Qdrant, retrieve the few most relevant chunks per question, and feed only those to the model. The vector DB is what lets a general model answer from your specific, current knowledge.

slides.title: Filtered semantic search
slides.code:
from qdrant_client import models

client.query_points(
    collection_name="docs",
    query=query_vector,
    query_filter=models.Filter(must=[
        models.FieldCondition(
            key="tenant",
            match=models.MatchValue(value="acme"))
    ]),
    limit=5,
)

slides.title: The trade-offs you accept
slides.body: Self-hosting means you own uptime, backups, and upgrades — real operational work the managed tier removes. ANN results are approximate, so a relevant point can occasionally be missed. And storage grows with your corpus. You trade some convenience and exactness for control, speed, and zero license cost.

slides.title: Why it wins
bullets: Filtering built into the search path
Self-host: data never leaves your VPC
Rust: tight p99, no GC stalls
Powers RAG, recsys, dedup, hybrid
Cost: ops burden, approximate, priced by storage

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 89.
slides.body: Next: how it works — HNSW, segments, quantization, and the query path inside.

88-3.caption:
Qdrant feels instant until you picture the machinery: an HNSW graph you climb down like a skip-list, segments merging in the background, and a filter index that reshapes the search as you walk. ⚙️

When you query, Qdrant never scores every point — that's too slow. It enters an HNSW graph at the top layer, greedily hops toward your query vector, applies payload filters during traversal, scores candidates by the collection's metric, and returns top-k. Knowing this is how you debug bad recall and tune speed vs accuracy.

This post opens the hood.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• HNSW: the navigable small-world graph
• segments, and how writes become searchable
• filterable HNSW — filtering during traversal
• quantization: shrinking vectors to fit RAM
• the query path: search, filter, score, return

This is the real engine. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Embeddings in, you supply them
slides.body: Qdrant doesn't embed your data — you do, with a model like sentence-transformers or OpenAI's text-embedding-3. Every stored vector and every query must use the same model and match the collection's configured size. Mismatched sizes are rejected; mismatched models silently return nonsense.

slides.title: HNSW: the core index
slides.body: Qdrant indexes vectors in a Hierarchical Navigable Small World graph. Think of layered shortcuts: the top layer has few nodes with long links, lower layers add detail. A search enters the top, greedily hops to the nearest node, then drops a layer and repeats — reaching neighbors in logarithmic time instead of scanning everything.

slides.title: Segments and indexing
slides.body: Qdrant stores points in segments — independent shards of data. New writes land in a mutable segment first, so they're searchable immediately, then get optimized and HNSW-indexed in the background. Querying fans out across all segments and merges the results. This is why a fresh upsert is found instantly even before full indexing.

slides.title: Filterable HNSW
slides.body: Naive filtering either pre-filters (build a candidate set first) or post-filters (search then discard) — both break at scale. Qdrant builds a payload index and applies the filter during graph traversal, skipping points that don't match as it walks. You get filtered results without sacrificing the speed of the ANN search.

slides.title: Quantization fits RAM
slides.body: Float32 vectors are large; a million 1536-dim vectors is ~6GB. Qdrant can quantize them — scalar (int8) or binary — shrinking memory 4x to 32x so the index fits in RAM. It searches on the compressed form, then optionally rescores top candidates with full vectors to recover accuracy.

slides.title: Create a tuned collection
slides.code:
from qdrant_client import QdrantClient, models

client = QdrantClient("localhost", port=6333)
client.create_collection(
    collection_name="docs",
    vectors_config=models.VectorParams(
        size=768,
        distance=models.Distance.COSINE,
    ),
    quantization_config=models.ScalarQuantization(
        scalar=models.ScalarQuantizationConfig(
            type=models.ScalarType.INT8,
            always_ram=True,
        )
    ),
)

slides.title: The engine in five lines
bullets: You embed; Qdrant indexes the vectors
HNSW = layered graph, log-time search
Segments make writes instantly searchable
Filterable HNSW filters during traversal
Quantization shrinks vectors to fit RAM

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 89.
slides.body: Next: a full code walkthrough — connect, create, upsert, search, filter.

88-4.caption:
Reading about HNSW is one thing. Watching ten lines go from an empty collection to a filtered semantic search is what makes it click. 💻

This post is a runnable end-to-end walkthrough with the official qdrant-client. We spin Qdrant up in Docker, create a collection, upsert points with payloads, run a plain similarity search, then add a payload filter — the exact loop you'll write in any RAG or search service.

Everything here is copy-pasteable. Read it, then type it.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• run Qdrant locally in one Docker command
• connect and create a collection in Python
• upsert points with vectors + payloads
• search by similarity, then add a filter
• batch upserts and the gotchas that bite

Type it out, don't just read. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 0. Run Qdrant in Docker
slides.code:
# starts Qdrant on REST 6333 and gRPC 6334
docker run -p 6333:6333 -p 6334:6334 \
  -v $(pwd)/qdrant_storage:/qdrant/storage \
  qdrant/qdrant

# then: pip install qdrant-client

slides.title: 1. Connect + create collection
slides.code:
from qdrant_client import QdrantClient, models

client = QdrantClient(url="http://localhost:6333")

client.recreate_collection(
    collection_name="articles",
    vectors_config=models.VectorParams(
        size=4,                       # tiny size for the demo
        distance=models.Distance.COSINE,
    ),
)

slides.title: 2. Upsert points with payloads
slides.code:
client.upsert(
    collection_name="articles",
    points=[
        models.PointStruct(
            id=1,
            vector=[0.10, 0.20, 0.30, 0.40],
            payload={"topic": "ai", "year": 2025},
        ),
        models.PointStruct(
            id=2,
            vector=[0.90, 0.10, 0.05, 0.02],
            payload={"topic": "sports", "year": 2024},
        ),
    ],
)

slides.title: 3. Plain similarity search
4. Add a payload filter
slides.code:
from qdrant_client import models

hits = client.query_points(
    collection_name="articles",
    query=[0.11, 0.19, 0.31, 0.39],
    query_filter=models.Filter(must=[
        models.FieldCondition(
            key="topic",
            match=models.MatchValue(value="ai")),
        models.FieldCondition(
            key="year",
            range=models.Range(gte=2025)),
    ]),
    limit=5,
).points

slides.title: 5. Batch upserts at scale
slides.code:
ids = list(range(len(vectors)))
client.upsert(
    collection_name="articles",
    points=models.Batch(
        ids=ids,
        vectors=vectors,        # list of equal-length lists
        payloads=payloads,      # list of dicts, same order
    ),
    wait=True,                  # block until applied
)

slides.title: Gotchas that bite
bullets: query vector size must equal collection size
ids must be uint or UUID, not arbitrary strings
index payload fields you filter on, or it's slow
wait=True before you query freshly upserted data
use gRPC (port 6334) for high-throughput loads

slides.title: What you just built
bullets: A live local Qdrant in Docker
A collection with the right size + metric
Points carrying vectors and payloads
Similarity search returning scored hits
The same search, scoped by a filter

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 89.
slides.body: Next: the mistakes that wreck recall, latency, and your storage bill.

88-5.caption:
Qdrant rarely fails loudly. It fails quietly — wrong metric, unindexed filter field, vectors that won't fit RAM — and you blame the model or the data instead of the config. 🧨

This post is the field guide to the mistakes that quietly wreck recall, spike latency, and inflate your storage bill. Each one is easy to make and easy to fix once you've seen it named.

Learn them before they cost you a debugging weekend.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• distance metric that doesn't match your model
• filtering on fields you never indexed
• ignoring quantization until RAM runs out
• querying before writes are applied
• one giant collection for every tenant

Dodge these five. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Wrong distance metric
slides.body: You configure a collection with Euclidean but your embedding model was trained for cosine similarity. Results look plausible but ranking is subtly wrong, and you never notice until recall is bad in production. Match the metric to your model — most text embedders want cosine or dot product, not euclidean.

slides.title: Filtering unindexed fields
slides.body: Filtering on a payload field that has no index forces Qdrant to scan, which collapses latency at scale. People filter on 'tenant' or 'category' for months and wonder why queries crawl. Create a payload index on every field you filter, and pick the index type that matches the field.

slides.title: Fix: index your filter fields
slides.code:
client.create_payload_index(
    collection_name="articles",
    field_name="tenant",
    field_schema=models.PayloadSchemaType.KEYWORD,
)
client.create_payload_index(
    collection_name="articles",
    field_name="year",
    field_schema=models.PayloadSchemaType.INTEGER,
)

slides.title: Ignoring quantization
slides.body: You load millions of float32 vectors and the index spills out of RAM onto disk, or the node OOMs. People treat memory as infinite until it isn't. For large collections, enable scalar or binary quantization and optionally rescore — you cut memory 4x to 32x with little recall loss.

slides.title: Querying before writes land
slides.body: You upsert points then immediately query and get stale or missing results, because the write hadn't been applied yet. In tests this looks like a flaky bug. Pass wait=True on upserts when correctness depends on the data being visible, or accept eventual consistency knowingly.

root.question: Must the next query see this write?

slides.title: One collection for all tenants
slides.body: Dumping every customer into a single collection and relying only on a filter mixes their data and makes per-tenant deletes and scaling painful. For real isolation, partition with separate collections or use multitenancy via a tenant payload index that Qdrant optimizes for — and always enforce the filter server-side.

slides.title: Fix: tenant-aware payload index
slides.code:
# optimize the index for tenant isolation
client.create_payload_index(
    collection_name="articles",
    field_name="tenant",
    field_schema=models.KeywordIndexParams(
        type="keyword",
        is_tenant=True,   # group points by tenant
    ),
)

slides.title: The pre-flight checklist
bullets: Metric matches your embedding model
Payload index on every filtered field
Quantization on for large collections
wait=True when reads depend on writes
Plan tenant isolation before launch

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 89.
slides.body: Next vector-database deep dive — another engine, same rigor.

89-1.caption:
You don't need a cloud account, a cluster, or a credit card to ship retrieval-augmented generation. You need one pip install and a folder on disk. 🧪

Chroma is an open-source, embeddings-first vector database built to be the simplest possible store for local RAG. It runs in-process inside your Python app, persists to a directory you choose, and treats "add some text, get back the most relevant chunks" as the whole API.

The big idea: Chroma optimizes for developer ergonomics on a laptop. It will even embed your text for you with a default model, so you can have a working retriever in five lines before deciding anything about infrastructure.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• what Chroma actually is
• what "local" and "in-process" really mean
• collections, documents, and embeddings
• why it's the default for prototyping RAG
• when to outgrow it

Get the mental model before the code. Save it. 👇

slides.title: The one-sentence definition
slides.body: Chroma is an open-source, embeddings-first vector database designed for local RAG. It stores text, embeddings, and metadata together, persists to a local directory, and runs in-process so there's no server to operate during development.

slides.title: "Local" and "in-process"
