slides.body: In-process means Chroma runs inside your Python program, not as a separate service you connect to over the network. PersistentClient writes everything to a folder on disk, so your data survives restarts. No Docker, no ports, no cluster — just a path.

slides.title: Embeddings-first by design
bullets: Most DBs: you bring vectors, it stores them
Chroma: hand it text, it embeds for you
Default model runs locally (all-MiniLM-L6-v2)
Swap in OpenAI/Cohere via embedding functions

slides.title: Collections
slides.body: A collection is Chroma's table-equivalent: a named group of documents that share one embedding function and one distance space. You create a collection, add documents to it, and query it. Most small projects use a single collection per corpus.

slides.title: Documents, embeddings, metadata, IDs
slides.body: Each record holds four things: the document text, its embedding vector, a metadata dict for filtering, and a unique string id. You supply text and ids; Chroma fills in the embedding. Metadata powers where-filters at query time.

slides.title: Why it's the prototyping default
bullets: Zero infra: pip install and go
Embeds text out of the box
Persists to disk with one line
Same API in-process or client/server

slides.title: The five-line retriever
slides.code:
# pip install chromadb
import chromadb

client = chromadb.PersistentClient(path="./chroma_db")
col = client.get_or_create_collection("docs")
col.add(documents=["the sky is blue", "cats purr"],
        ids=["d1", "d2"])
print(col.query(query_texts=["feline sounds"], n_results=1))

slides.title: When to outgrow it
slides.body: Chroma shines from prototype to mid-size single-node workloads. For very large corpora, heavy concurrent write traffic, or multi-node high availability, a server-grade store (Weaviate, Qdrant, pgvector) fits better. Start on Chroma; migrate when scale demands it.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 90.
slides.body: Next: why local-first retrieval changes how you prototype RAG — and your bill.

89-2.caption:
The fastest way to kill a RAG project is to spend week one provisioning infrastructure instead of testing whether retrieval even works. Chroma flips that. 🚀

The value of a local-first vector store isn't that it's the most scalable thing on earth — it isn't. It's that it removes every barrier between an idea and a working retriever. No accounts, no network, no cost per query, no data leaving your machine.

That changes how you work: you iterate on chunking, embeddings, and prompts in tight loops, and you only reach for heavier infrastructure once you've proven the approach is worth it.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• why infra friction kills RAG prototypes
• the privacy win of data never leaving your box
• cost: free local embeddings vs per-call APIs
• fast iteration loops on chunking + retrieval
• the honest limits of local-first

Know why before you commit a stack. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Infra friction kills prototypes
slides.body: Many RAG ideas die before the first real test because setting up a managed vector DB — accounts, clusters, keys, networking — eats the energy you needed for the actual experiment. Chroma collapses that to one pip install, so you find out fast whether retrieval works.

slides.title: Your data never leaves the box
slides.body: With the local default embedding model and PersistentClient, documents and embeddings stay on your machine. For sensitive corpora — internal docs, contracts, health data — that means no third-party API ever sees the text. Privacy by architecture, not by policy.

slides.title: Tight iteration loops
slides.body: Because there's no network round-trip and no provisioning, you can re-chunk, re-embed, and re-query in seconds. That speed is what lets you actually tune the variables that decide RAG quality: chunk size, overlap, embedding model, and k.

slides.title: Local-first vs managed-first
bullets: Local: instant start, zero cost, private
Local: limited by one machine's RAM/CPU
Managed: scales, HA, but setup + cost upfront
Smart path: prototype local, scale managed

slides.title: From idea to retrieval in seconds
slides.code:
import chromadb
client = chromadb.Client()  # ephemeral, in-memory
col = client.create_collection("notes")
col.add(documents=["refunds take 5 days",
                   "shipping is free over $50"],
        ids=["a", "b"])
print(col.query(query_texts=["how long for money back"],
                n_results=1)["documents"])

slides.title: What this unlocks
bullets: Test retrieval quality before buying infra
Keep regulated data fully on-prem
Demo RAG on a laptop with no internet
Cheap experiments → confident scaling decisions

slides.title: The honest limits
slides.body: Local-first is not a production answer for every case. One process means one machine's memory and CPU bound your corpus and throughput. Concurrent multi-writer workloads and high-availability needs push you toward a server. Know this before you over-commit.

events.label: Scale
events.detail: migrate to a server-grade store if needed

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 90.
slides.body: Next: open the hood — how Chroma embeds, indexes, and answers a query.

89-3.caption:
When you call collection.add(text), a quiet pipeline kicks off: tokenize, embed, store, index. When you query, it embeds again and walks an HNSW graph. Here's the machinery. ⚙️

Chroma feels like magic because it hides the embedding step, but under the hood it's a familiar stack: a default sentence-transformer model, an HNSW vector index, a metadata store for filtering, and a persistence layer (SQLite plus index files) writing to your directory.

Understanding the pieces tells you exactly what changes recall, speed, and what "distance" in your results actually means.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• the add() and query() lifecycles
• the default embedding function
• HNSW: approximate nearest neighbors
• metadata filtering with where clauses
• how persistence actually works on disk

Understand the engine and tuning stops being guesswork. Save it. 👇

slides.title: The add() lifecycle
slides.body: When you add documents without vectors, Chroma runs the collection's embedding function on each one, then stores the text, the resulting vector, the metadata, and the id. The vector is inserted into the HNSW index; the rest goes to the metadata/document store.

slides.title: The query() lifecycle
slides.body: A query_text is embedded with the SAME function used at add time, then compared against the HNSW index to find nearest vectors. Any where filter prunes by metadata, and Chroma returns the matching documents, distances, metadatas, and ids together.

slides.title: The default embedding function
slides.body: Out of the box Chroma uses all-MiniLM-L6-v2 via sentence-transformers — a small, fast model producing 384-dim vectors, downloaded once and run locally. It's good enough to prototype with and costs nothing per call. You can swap it for any other embedding function.

slides.title: HNSW in plain terms
slides.body: Hierarchical Navigable Small World is a layered graph for approximate nearest-neighbor search. It hops across sparse top layers for big jumps, then descends to dense layers to refine, finding close vectors in roughly log time instead of scanning every record.

slides.title: Metadata filtering
slides.body: Each document carries a metadata dict. At query time a where clause (e.g. {"source": "faq"}) restricts results to matching records, and where_document filters by substring in the text. Chroma combines this with vector similarity in a single query call.

slides.title: Distance, filters, and k
slides.code:
res = col.query(
    query_texts=["return policy"],
    n_results=3,
    where={"source": "faq"},        # metadata filter
    where_document={"$contains": "refund"},
)
for doc, dist in zip(res["documents"][0],
                     res["distances"][0]):
    print(round(dist, 3), doc)   # lower = closer

slides.title: How persistence works
slides.body: PersistentClient writes to the path you give it: a SQLite database for documents, metadata, and ids, plus binary HNSW index files per collection. On startup Chroma loads them back, so your corpus and index survive restarts with no rebuild.

slides.title: Knobs and gotchas
bullets: Distance space: default cosine; set in metadata
Same embedding fn for add + query — always
HNSW params (M, ef) configurable per collection
First run downloads the model — expect a pause

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 90.
slides.body: Next: a full hands-on RAG pipeline — chunk, embed, store, retrieve, answer.

89-4.caption:
Theory is cheap. Here's a complete local RAG pipeline in Chroma you can paste and run: chunk a document, store it, retrieve the relevant pieces, and feed them to an LLM. 💻

We build it end to end with the modern Chroma API. The flow never changes: create a persistent collection, add chunked text (Chroma embeds it), query for the top-k relevant chunks, then stuff those into a prompt as context. We'll also show a custom embedding function and metadata filtering so you see how the pieces compose.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• create a persistent collection
• chunk + add documents with metadata
• retrieve top-k relevant chunks
• plug retrieval into an LLM prompt
• swap in a custom embedding function

Copy it, run it, then point it at your own docs. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Local RAG in Code, End to End
1. Persistent collection
slides.code:
# pip install chromadb
import chromadb

client = chromadb.PersistentClient(path="./rag_db")
col = client.get_or_create_collection(
    name="handbook",
    metadata={"hnsw:space": "cosine"},
)
print(col.count())  # 0 on first run

slides.title: 2. Chunk + add with metadata
slides.code:
def chunk(text, size=400, overlap=50):
    out, i = [], 0
    while i < len(text):
        out.append(text[i:i + size])
        i += size - overlap
    return out

chunks = chunk(open("handbook.txt").read())
col.add(
    documents=chunks,
    ids=[f"c{i}" for i in range(len(chunks))],
    metadatas=[{"source": "handbook"} for _ in chunks],
)

slides.title: 3. Retrieve top-k chunks
4. Retrieval → LLM prompt
slides.code:
from openai import OpenAI
llm = OpenAI()

def answer(question):
    ctx = "\n\n".join(retrieve(question))
    prompt = (f"Use ONLY this context:\n{ctx}\n\n"
              f"Question: {question}")
    r = llm.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4o-mini",
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}])
    return r.choices[0].message.content

print(answer("how many vacation days do I get?"))

lines.text: > how many vacation days do I get?

slides.title: 5. Custom embedding function
slides.code:
from chromadb.utils import embedding_functions as ef

oai = ef.OpenAIEmbeddingFunction(
    model_name="text-embedding-3-small")
col2 = client.get_or_create_collection(
    name="handbook_oai",
    embedding_function=oai,
)
# now add()/query() embed via OpenAI instead of MiniLM

slides.title: Why chunk + overlap
slides.body: LLMs and embeddings work best on focused passages, so you split long docs into chunks. Overlap carries a few tokens across the boundary so a sentence split in two still retrieves intact. Tune size and overlap — they directly drive retrieval quality.

slides.title: Practical notes
bullets: get_or_create_collection avoids re-create errors
Pin one embedding function per collection
Set OPENAI_API_KEY for the API embedder + LLM
Store source/page in metadata for citations

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 90.
slides.body: Next: the mistakes that silently wreck retrieval, persistence, and answers.

89-5.caption:
Chroma fails quietly. No stack trace — just irrelevant chunks, an LLM hallucinating, or a database that's mysteriously empty after you restart. 🧯

Most Chroma pain isn't bugs; it's mismatches and missing steps: querying with a different embedding model than you added with, using an in-memory client and losing everything, chunks too big to retrieve precisely, ignoring metadata you'll wish you had. These don't crash. They degrade your answers.

This post collects the traps that bite real local-RAG builds and the one-line fix for each.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• ephemeral client → data vanishes on restart
• mismatched embedding functions
• chunks too big or too small
• forgetting metadata for filters + citations
• trusting retrieval without evaluating it

Learn these now, not in your demo. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 1. Using the in-memory client
slides.body: chromadb.Client() (or EphemeralClient) keeps everything in RAM and discards it when the process ends. People prototype with it, restart, and find an empty database — then blame Chroma. For anything you want to keep, use PersistentClient with a path.

slides.title: Fix: persist to disk
slides.code:
import chromadb

# NOT chromadb.Client()  -> vanishes on exit
client = chromadb.PersistentClient(path="./chroma_db")
col = client.get_or_create_collection("docs")
# data now survives restarts in ./chroma_db

slides.title: 2. Mismatched embedding functions
slides.body: If you add() with MiniLM but later query() a collection configured for OpenAI embeddings — or change the function between runs — vectors live in different spaces and similarity is meaningless. Pin ONE embedding function per collection and never switch without re-embedding.

slides.title: 3. Chunks too big or too small
slides.body: Huge chunks dilute relevance — the right sentence drowns in a page of context and the LLM gets noise. Tiny chunks lose the surrounding meaning and retrieve fragments. Both wreck answers. Aim for focused passages (~200-500 tokens) with small overlap.

slides.title: 4. No metadata, no citations
slides.body: If you only store raw text, you can't filter by source, date, or section — and you can't tell the user where an answer came from. Add metadata (source, page, url) at add() time. Retrofitting it later means re-importing everything.

slides.title: Fix: store metadata up front
5. Trusting retrieval blindly
slides.body: A pipeline that runs is not a pipeline that's correct. If retrieval surfaces the wrong chunks, the LLM confidently answers from garbage. Inspect the retrieved documents and distances, and build a small eval set of question→expected-source pairs before you trust it.

slides.title: Quick prevention checklist
bullets: PersistentClient with a real path
One embedding function per collection, documented
Tune chunk size + overlap; ~200-500 tokens
Store source/page metadata from day one
Eval retrieval, not just the final answer

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 90.
slides.body: You can now run local RAG on Chroma without the silent failures. Next day, new topic.

90-1.caption:
Before there were managed vector databases with dashboards and APIs, there was a library that just did the math — fast. FAISS is the engine half the industry quietly runs underneath. 🧭

FAISS (Facebook AI Similarity Search) is a C++ library with Python bindings for storing and searching dense vectors at scale. It isn't a server, it has no network API, and it doesn't store your text — it stores float arrays and answers one question brutally well: which of these millions of vectors are closest to this one?

This post lays the vocabulary before any of the 'how' makes sense.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• what FAISS actually is — and isn't
• the core noun: an index
• flat vs approximate search
• why it's a library, not a database
• where FAISS sits in a real stack

Start here. Save it. 👇

slides.title: A similarity-search library
slides.body: FAISS (Facebook AI Similarity Search) is a C++ library with Python bindings for indexing and searching dense vectors. You give it a matrix of float32 embeddings; it returns, for any query vector, the k nearest neighbors by distance. It runs in your process — no server, no network, no query language.

slides.title: The one noun: an index
slides.body: Everything in FAISS is an index object. You create one, `add()` your vectors to it, then `search()` it with query vectors. The index type you pick decides the speed/accuracy/memory trade-off. Learn the index zoo and you've learned 90% of FAISS.

slides.title: What a vector is here
slides.body: A vector is a fixed-length array of float32 — say 384 or 768 of them — that an embedding model assigns to text, an image, or audio. FAISS never creates these. You embed your data with a model, then hand FAISS the raw numbers as a NumPy array of shape (n, d).

slides.title: Flat vs approximate
slides.body: A flat index compares your query to every stored vector — exact, but linear in dataset size. An approximate index (ANN) trades a sliver of accuracy for huge speedups by only checking a promising subset. FAISS's whole reason to exist is doing the approximate case well at billion-scale.

slides.title: The whole API in five lines
slides.code:
import faiss, numpy as np

d = 64                              # vector dimension
index = faiss.IndexFlatL2(d)        # exact L2 index
index.add(np.random.rand(1000, d).astype('float32'))
D, I = index.search(np.random.rand(5, d).astype('float32'), k=4)
# D = distances, I = neighbor ids, both shape (5, 4)

slides.title: It's a library, not a database
slides.body: FAISS has no persistence layer you didn't write, no auth, no replication, no metadata filtering, no REST endpoint. It's a math engine you embed in your own service. That's a feature: zero operational surface, raw speed, full control — and the reason vector DBs like Milvus and Weaviate use FAISS under the hood.

slides.title: The 30-second model
bullets: FAISS = C++/Python similarity search library
Everything is an index object
add() vectors, search() for k nearest
Flat = exact; ANN = fast & approximate
No server, no metadata, no ops — by design

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 91.
slides.body: Next: why FAISS matters — the speed, scale, and control it unlocks.

90-2.caption:
A brute-force search over a million 768-dim vectors is a few hundred million multiply-adds per query. Do that for every user request and your service melts. FAISS exists so you never have to. 🚀

FAISS matters because it makes nearest-neighbor search practical at a scale where the naive loop falls over. It's the difference between 'works in a notebook' and 'serves a billion vectors from one box'. It's also the reference implementation — the place new ANN ideas land first and the engine many higher-level databases quietly run.

This post is about the stakes: what FAISS unlocks and what you trade for it.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• why brute force breaks at scale
• speed: SIMD, batching, and the GPU path
• billion-scale on commodity hardware
• why it's the industry's reference engine
• the trade-offs: recall, no filters, manual ops

Know why it wins — and when it doesn't. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Brute force doesn't scale
slides.body: Exact search compares the query to every vector: O(n·d) per query. At a million 768-dim vectors that's ~768M operations — fine once, ruinous at thousands of queries per second. Approximate indexes cut the candidate set by orders of magnitude, turning an impossible workload into a millisecond response.

slides.title: Built for raw speed
slides.body: FAISS is C++ with hand-tuned SIMD distance kernels, multithreaded batch search, and BLAS-backed matrix math. Searching many queries at once amortizes memory traffic. You operate close to the hardware's limit — something a pure-Python or naive loop can never approach, even with NumPy.

slides.title: The GPU path
slides.body: FAISS ships GPU indexes that move both vectors and search onto CUDA. For large batches, a single GPU can outrun dozens of CPU cores by 5–20x. The same index abstractions work on GPU, so you prototype on CPU and flip to GPU for the heavy offline jobs like clustering a billion vectors.

slides.title: The industry's reference engine
slides.body: FAISS came out of Meta's research and became the de-facto baseline for ANN. New index ideas — IVF, PQ, HNSW variants — show up here, and databases like Milvus historically wrapped it. When a paper reports 'recall@10', it's often measured against a FAISS flat index as ground truth.

slides.title: GPU search in a few lines
slides.code:
import faiss, numpy as np

res = faiss.StandardGpuResources()          # one GPU's scratch memory
cpu_index = faiss.IndexFlatL2(128)
gpu_index = faiss.index_cpu_to_gpu(res, 0, cpu_index)

gpu_index.add(np.random.rand(1_000_000, 128).astype('float32'))
D, I = gpu_index.search(np.random.rand(256, 128).astype('float32'), k=10)

slides.title: The trade-offs you accept
slides.body: ANN results are approximate — a true neighbor can be missed, measured as recall below 100%. FAISS stores no metadata, so filtering by tenant or date is your job outside the index. And there's no server: persistence, sharding, updates, and uptime are all code you write. You trade convenience for speed and control.

right.title: Wrong tool
items: tiny data a brute scan handles

slides.title: Why it wins
bullets: Beats brute force by orders of magnitude
SIMD + BLAS + multithread on CPU
GPU indexes for billion-scale jobs
The reference ANN implementation
Cost: approximate recall, no filters, manual ops

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 91.
slides.body: Next: how it works — IVF, PQ, HNSW, and the index zoo explained.

90-3.caption:
FAISS feels like magic until you see the trick: don't search everything, search a smart subset — and if memory is tight, don't store full vectors, store compressed sketches of them. ⚙️

When you query a tuned FAISS index it never scores every vector. It clusters the space into cells (IVF), visits only the cells near your query, and often compares against compressed codes (PQ) instead of raw floats. Two knobs — how many cells you probe and how hard you compress — control the speed/accuracy/memory triangle. Understanding them is how you debug bad recall.

This post opens the hood.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• IVF: partition the space into cells
• nprobe: the speed vs recall dial
• Product Quantization: compress the vectors
• HNSW: the graph alternative
• reading an index 'factory string'

This is the real engine. Save it. 👇

slides.title: You embed; FAISS indexes
slides.body: FAISS doesn't make vectors — you do, with a model like sentence-transformers. Every vector and every query must be the same dimension d and the same model. Mismatched dimensions raise an error; mismatched models silently return garbage neighbors that look plausible but mean nothing.

slides.title: IVF: partition the space
slides.body: An inverted-file index runs k-means once to split the vector space into `nlist` cells, each with a centroid. Every vector is filed under its nearest centroid. At query time FAISS finds the closest centroids and only searches vectors in those cells — skipping the vast majority. This is the core trick behind fast ANN.

slides.title: nprobe: the recall dial
slides.body: `nprobe` is how many cells FAISS searches per query. nprobe=1 is fastest but may miss neighbors near a cell boundary. Raising it widens the search, improving recall toward exact at the cost of speed. It's the single most important runtime knob — tune it against a recall target, not by guessing.

slides.title: PQ: compress the vectors
slides.body: Product Quantization splits each vector into m subvectors and replaces each with the id of its nearest entry in a tiny learned codebook. A 768-dim float32 vector (3KB) shrinks to a handful of bytes. FAISS computes distances directly on these codes, so a billion vectors fit in RAM that flat storage could never hold.

slides.title: HNSW: the graph alternative
slides.body: Instead of cells, HNSW builds a layered navigable graph: top layers have long shortcut links, lower layers add detail. A search enters the top, greedily hops toward the query, then descends a layer and repeats — log-time. FAISS includes HNSW for high-recall, low-latency search when you can spend the RAM.

slides.title: Train + tune an IVF+PQ index
slides.code:
import faiss, numpy as np

d, nlist, m = 128, 256, 16
quantizer = faiss.IndexFlatL2(d)
index = faiss.IndexIVFPQ(quantizer, d, nlist, m, 8)  # 8 bits/code

xb = np.random.rand(100_000, d).astype('float32')
index.train(xb)        # IVF + PQ must be trained first
index.add(xb)
index.nprobe = 16      # search 16 of 256 cells per query
D, I = index.search(xb[:5], k=10)

slides.title: Or describe it with a factory string
slides.code:
import faiss

# "IVF256,PQ16x8" = 256 cells, PQ with 16 codes of 8 bits
index = faiss.index_factory(128, "IVF256,PQ16x8", faiss.METRIC_L2)

# other recipes:
#   "Flat"           -> exact, no training
#   "HNSW32"         -> graph index, 32 neighbors/node
#   "OPQ16,IVF1024,PQ16" -> rotation + IVF + PQ

slides.title: The engine in five lines
bullets: You embed; FAISS indexes the vectors
IVF splits space into cells via k-means
nprobe = how many cells to search (recall dial)
PQ compresses vectors into codebook bytes
HNSW = graph index: fast, accurate, RAM-heavy

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 91.
slides.body: Next: a full code walkthrough — build, train, search, save, and map ids to text.

90-4.caption:
Reading about IVF and PQ is one thing. Watching real embeddings go from a NumPy array to a saved, queryable index — and back to the actual sentences — is what makes it click. 💻

This post is a runnable end-to-end walkthrough. We embed a few sentences, build a flat index, upgrade to a trained IVF index, search it, persist it to disk, and reload it. Crucially we also handle the thing FAISS won't: mapping result ids back to your original text.

Everything here is copy-pasteable. Read it, then type it.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• embed sentences into float32 vectors
• build and search a flat index
• train an IVF index and tune nprobe
• map neighbor ids back to your text
• save the index to disk and reload it

Type it out, don't just read. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 0. Install + embed your text
slides.code:
# pip install faiss-cpu sentence-transformers numpy
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer
import numpy as np

model = SentenceTransformer("all-MiniLM-L6-v2")   # d = 384
docs = ["cats purr", "dogs bark", "the GPU trains fast", "kittens nap"]
xb = model.encode(docs).astype('float32')          # shape (4, 384)

slides.title: 1. Build + search a flat index
slides.code:
import faiss

d = xb.shape[1]                 # 384
index = faiss.IndexFlatL2(d)    # exact, no training needed
index.add(xb)
print(index.ntotal)             # 4 vectors stored

q = model.encode(["a small cat sleeping"]).astype('float32')
D, I = index.search(q, k=2)
print(I)                        # e.g. [[3 0]] -> 'kittens nap', 'cats purr'

slides.title: 2. Map ids back to text
slides.code:
# FAISS returns positions, not your data. You keep the mapping.
results = [(docs[idx], float(dist))
           for idx, dist in zip(I[0], D[0])]

for text, dist in results:
    print(f"{dist:6.3f}  {text}")
# 0.812  kittens nap
# 0.944  cats purr

slides.title: 3. Upgrade to a trained IVF index
slides.code:
nlist = 2                       # tiny for the demo; use ~sqrt(n)
quantizer = faiss.IndexFlatL2(d)
ivf = faiss.IndexIVFPQ(quantizer, d, nlist, 8, 8)

ivf.train(xb)                   # MUST train before add
ivf.add(xb)
ivf.nprobe = 2                  # search both cells -> high recall
D, I = ivf.search(q, k=2)

slides.title: 4. Save to disk and reload
slides.code:
faiss.write_index(ivf, "docs.index")

# later, in another process:
index = faiss.read_index("docs.index")
index.nprobe = 2                # runtime knobs reset on load
D, I = index.search(q, k=2)
# remember to also persist `docs` (the id -> text map)!

slides.title: Gotchas that bite
bullets: Vectors must be float32 and C-contiguous
IVF/PQ indexes must be train()ed before add()
search() returns ids, not your data — keep the map
nprobe resets to 1 after read_index — set it again
Flat index ignores training; don't call train() on it

slides.title: Cosine similarity? Normalize first
slides.code:
import faiss, numpy as np

# FAISS has no cosine metric. Use inner product on L2-normalized vectors.
faiss.normalize_L2(xb)                 # in-place, requires float32
index = faiss.IndexFlatIP(d)           # IP on unit vectors == cosine
index.add(xb)

faiss.normalize_L2(q)
D, I = index.search(q, k=2)            # D now in [-1, 1], higher = closer

slides.title: The loop you'll always write
bullets: Embed docs once, keep the text list
Pick index: Flat to start, IVF/PQ at scale
train() if needed, then add()
Embed query the same way, search()
Map ids -> text; persist index + map together

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 91.
slides.body: Next: the common mistakes that wreck FAISS recall and results.

90-5.caption:
Most 'FAISS is returning garbage' bugs aren't FAISS bugs. They're a forgotten train(), an L2 index pretending to be cosine, or a results list silently misaligned from your text. 🧨

FAISS is unforgiving in a specific way: it almost never errors on a logical mistake. Wrong metric, untuned nprobe, lost id mapping — it returns confident, wrong neighbors. This post is the field guide to the failures that actually happen, so you recognize them before they reach production.

Learn the failure modes before you trust the output.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• forgetting to train an IVF/PQ index
• using L2 when you meant cosine
• nprobe=1 quietly killing your recall
• losing the id → text mapping
• float64, contiguity, and dimension drift

Debug it before it ships. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 1. Forgetting to train
slides.body: IVF and PQ indexes learn centroids and codebooks from data. Skip train() and add() either errors or silently produces a useless index. Flat indexes need no training, which lulls people into skipping it everywhere. Rule: if the type contains IVF, PQ, or OPQ, you must train on a representative sample first.

slides.title: 2. L2 when you meant cosine
slides.body: FAISS has no cosine metric. People reach for IndexFlatL2, feed un-normalized embeddings, and wonder why ranking looks off. For cosine, L2-normalize every vector and use IndexFlatIP (inner product). Mixing metrics between add and search, or normalizing queries but not the database, quietly corrupts results.

slides.title: The cosine fix
slides.code:
import faiss

# WRONG: cosine intent, L2 metric, no normalization
# index = faiss.IndexFlatL2(d); index.add(xb)

# RIGHT: normalize both sides, use inner product
faiss.normalize_L2(xb)
index = faiss.IndexFlatIP(d)
index.add(xb)
faiss.normalize_L2(q)          # normalize the query too!
D, I = index.search(q, k=5)

slides.title: 3. nprobe=1 kills recall
slides.body: A fresh IVF index defaults to nprobe=1 — it searches a single cell. Vectors just across a cell boundary are invisible, so recall can crater to 60% or worse while latency looks great. Always tune nprobe against a recall target on a held-out query set; don't ship the default.

slides.title: 4. Losing the id mapping
slides.body: search() returns integer positions and distances — never your text. If you rebuild or reorder the index without persisting the parallel id→document list, every result points to the wrong content. Use IndexIDMap to attach your own ids, or save the mapping alongside the index, always together.

slides.title: Attach your own ids
slides.code:
import faiss, numpy as np

base = faiss.IndexFlatL2(d)
index = faiss.IndexIDMap(base)            # let FAISS store custom ids

ids = np.array([101, 102, 103, 104], dtype='int64')
index.add_with_ids(xb, ids)
D, I = index.search(q, k=2)
print(I)                                  # returns YOUR ids, e.g. [[104 101]]

slides.title: 5. dtype, contiguity, dimension
slides.body: FAISS expects float32, C-contiguous arrays. Pass float64 and you get an error or a silent copy; pass a non-contiguous slice and behavior is undefined. And the query dimension must equal the index dimension exactly — a different embedding model for queries vs documents returns confident nonsense.

slides.title: Defensive input hygiene
slides.code:
import numpy as np

def prep(x, d):
    x = np.ascontiguousarray(x, dtype='float32')   # dtype + layout
    assert x.ndim == 2 and x.shape[1] == d, x.shape  # dimension check
    return x

xb = prep(xb, d)
q  = prep(q, d)                # same model, same d, both sides

slides.title: The pre-flight checklist
bullets: train() any IVF/PQ index before add()
Cosine? normalize_L2 + IndexFlatIP, both sides
Tune nprobe to a recall target, not the default
Persist the id→text map with the index
float32, contiguous, matching dimension everywhere

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 91.
slides.body: Next day, next topic in the Vector Databases track — same depth.

91-1.caption:
You don't need a separate vector database to ship AI search. The boring Postgres you already run can store embeddings and find nearest neighbors. 🐘

pgvector is a Postgres extension that adds a `vector` column type plus distance operators. Suddenly your relational database can hold a 1536-dimension embedding next to its row, and answer "which rows are most similar to this query vector?" in SQL.

This post is the map: what pgvector actually IS, before why it matters or how the index math works.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• what pgvector adds to Postgres
• what a `vector` column really stores
• the three distance operators it gives you
• how a similarity query looks in SQL
• where it sits in a RAG stack
• what it does and doesn't replace

Get the shape first and the rest stops looking like magic. Save it. 👇

slides.title: What pgvector is
slides.body: pgvector is an open-source Postgres extension. Once installed, Postgres gains a new `vector` column type and a set of distance operators. You can store an embedding in a normal table column, alongside the text and metadata it came from, and run nearest-neighbor search with plain SQL.

slides.title: An embedding is just numbers
slides.body: An embedding is a fixed-length array of floats that captures the meaning of a piece of text, image, or audio. Similar items land near each other in that high-dimensional space. pgvector's job is to store those arrays and measure how close any two of them are.

slides.title: Turn it on
Three distance operators
slides.body: pgvector ships operators that map to similarity metrics: `<->` for L2 (Euclidean) distance, `<=>` for cosine distance, and `<#>` for negative inner product. You pick the one that matches how your embedding model was trained. Cosine is the common default for text embeddings.

slides.title: Nearest-neighbor in SQL
slides.code:
SELECT id, body
FROM docs
ORDER BY embedding <=> '[0.1, 0.2, ...]'
LIMIT 5;

slides.title: It rides on real Postgres
slides.body: Because it's just an extension, your vectors live in the same database as your users, orders, and permissions. Transactions, joins, foreign keys, backups, and replication all apply to your embeddings for free. There is no second system to keep in sync.

slides.title: The mental model
bullets: Embeddings are arrays of floats
pgvector stores them in a column
Operators measure distance
ORDER BY distance LIMIT k = search
It's Postgres, so SQL still applies

slides.title: Thinking it replaces your embedder
slides.body: pgvector does not generate embeddings. You still call an external model (OpenAI, Cohere, a local sentence-transformer) to turn text into a vector, then hand that array to Postgres. pgvector only stores and compares — it never learns meaning on its own.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 92.
slides.body: Next: why teams keep their vectors in Postgres instead of bolting on a dedicated vector DB.

91-2.caption:
Most apps reach for a shiny new vector database the moment they add AI search. Then they spend months syncing two systems that disagree. 🔁

pgvector matters because it kills that split. Your embeddings live in the same Postgres as your business data, so a similarity search can JOIN against permissions, filter by tenant, and ride the same transaction — without copying rows between systems or reconciling stale state.

This post is the 'so what': where pgvector earns its keep over a dedicated vector store.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• the two-system sync problem it solves
• why one source of truth beats two
• filtering + joins on metadata
• transactions, backups, and ACL reuse
• the operational cost you avoid
• when a dedicated vector DB still wins

Fewer moving parts is a feature, not a compromise. Save it. 👇

slides.title: One database, not two
slides.body: Bolt a dedicated vector DB onto a Postgres app and you now run two stores that must agree. Every insert, update, and delete has to be mirrored. pgvector removes the second system: the vector lives in the same row as the text it describes, so there is nothing to sync.

left.title: Separate vector DB
items: Write to Postgres AND vector DB

slides.title: Filter and join, then rank
slides.body: Real queries aren't 'find similar docs' — they're 'find similar docs this user is allowed to see, in this workspace, created this year.' With pgvector that's a single WHERE clause plus an ORDER BY distance. Standalone vector stores force coarse metadata filters that can't express a real JOIN.

slides.title: Search WITH a real filter
slides.code:
SELECT d.id, d.body
FROM docs d
JOIN memberships m ON m.workspace = d.workspace
WHERE m.user_id = $1            -- access control
  AND d.created_at > now() - interval '1 year'
ORDER BY d.embedding <=> $2     -- semantic rank
LIMIT 10;

slides.title: Transactions cover your vectors
slides.body: When you write a document and its embedding in one transaction, either both land or neither does. No window where the row exists but its vector doesn't. Foreign keys, cascading deletes, and row-level security you already trust now guard your embeddings too.

slides.title: Reuse the ops you already have
slides.body: Your team already knows how to back up, replicate, monitor, and secure Postgres. pgvector inherits all of it. No new failure domain, no new on-call runbook, no new auth model — just a column type in a system you've run for years.

slides.title: The payoff
bullets: Single source of truth
Transactional consistency for free
Joins and filters before ranking
Reuse Postgres ops + security
Fewer services to operate

right.title: Reach for a dedicated DB
items: Vector search is the whole product

slides.title: Adding a vector DB by default
slides.body: Teams reach for a specialized vector store reflexively, then drown in dual-write logic and consistency bugs for a workload Postgres would have handled fine. Start with pgvector. Only graduate to a dedicated system when you have measured a scale or latency wall you can't tune past.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 92.
slides.body: Next: under the hood — exact search vs approximate indexes, and how HNSW actually finds neighbors fast.

91-3.caption:
A `LIMIT 5` similarity query looks innocent — but on a million rows, the wrong setup scans every single vector. The index is where pgvector lives or dies. 🔍

Under the hood, pgvector does two very different things. Without an index it computes the distance to every row (exact, but slow). With an HNSW or IVFFlat index it walks an approximate structure that touches a tiny fraction of vectors — trading a sliver of recall for huge speed.

This post is the engine room.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• exact vs approximate nearest neighbor
• how IVFFlat clusters then probes
• how HNSW navigates a graph
• the recall vs speed knobs
• why the operator must match the index
• when no index is the right call

See the machine and your latency numbers stop being mysterious. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Exact search is a full scan
slides.body: With no index, `ORDER BY embedding <=> $1 LIMIT 5` computes the distance from your query to every row, then sorts. The result is perfectly accurate but the cost grows linearly with table size. Fine for thousands of rows, painful for millions.

slides.title: Approximate search trades recall for speed
slides.body: ANN indexes give up the guarantee of finding the true top-k in exchange for touching far fewer vectors. You might miss one of the real five neighbors occasionally. For most semantic search that's invisible, and the latency drops by orders of magnitude.

slides.title: IVFFlat: cluster, then probe
slides.body: IVFFlat splits vectors into `lists` clusters at build time. A query finds the nearest cluster centroids and only searches inside the closest `probes` of them. More probes means higher recall but slower queries. It must be built AFTER data is loaded so the clusters are representative.

slides.title: Build an IVFFlat index
HNSW: navigate a graph
slides.body: HNSW builds a multi-layer graph where each vector links to its near neighbors. Search starts at a sparse top layer and greedily hops toward the query, descending layers to refine. It needs no pre-loaded data, gives strong recall, and is the usual default — at the cost of more memory and slower builds.

slides.title: Build an HNSW index
The knobs that matter
bullets: IVFFlat: lists (build) + probes (query)
HNSW: m + ef_construction (build)
HNSW: ef_search raises recall/query
Higher recall = slower queries
Operator class must match metric

slides.title: Index op-class vs query operator mismatch
slides.body: An index built with `vector_l2_ops` cannot serve a query using `<=>` (cosine). Postgres silently falls back to a full scan and your index does nothing. Match them: `vector_cosine_ops` with `<=>`, `vector_l2_ops` with `<->`, `vector_ip_ops` with `<#>`.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 92.
slides.body: Next: a full hands-on walkthrough — schema, embeddings, index, and a filtered similarity query end to end.

91-4.caption:
Theory is fine, but you ship code. Here's a complete pgvector flow: create the schema, store an embedding from Python, index it, and run a filtered semantic search. 💻

This is the end-to-end build. We use Postgres with the vector extension, generate embeddings with an external model, insert them with psycopg, build an HNSW index, then query for nearest neighbors with a real metadata filter applied first.

This post is code-heavy on purpose — copy, adapt, run.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• the SQL schema and extension setup
• embedding text in Python
• inserting vectors with psycopg
• building the right index
• a filtered top-k similarity query
• reading distance as a score

Keep this as your starting template. Save it. 👇

slides.title: What we'll build
bullets: Enable extension + create table
Embed text with a model
Insert text + vector together
Build an HNSW index
Run a filtered nearest-neighbor query

slides.title: 1. Schema + extension
2. Embed text in Python
slides.code:
from openai import OpenAI
client = OpenAI()

def embed(text: str) -> list[float]:
    resp = client.embeddings.create(
        model="text-embedding-3-small",
        input=text,
    )
    return resp.data[0].embedding   # 1536 floats

slides.title: 3. Insert text + vector
slides.code:
import psycopg
from pgvector.psycopg import register_vector

conn = psycopg.connect("postgresql://...")
register_vector(conn)

vec = embed("Postgres can do vector search.")
conn.execute(
    "INSERT INTO docs (tenant_id, body, embedding) "
    "VALUES (%s, %s, %s)",
    (1, "Postgres can do vector search.", vec),
)
conn.commit()

slides.title: 4. Build the index
5. Filtered similarity search
slides.code:
q = embed("can my database store embeddings?")

rows = conn.execute(
    """
    SELECT id, body,
           embedding <=> %s AS distance
    FROM docs
    WHERE tenant_id = %s        -- filter first
    ORDER BY embedding <=> %s   -- then rank
    LIMIT 5
    """,
    (q, 1, q),
).fetchall()

slides.title: Reading the distance
slides.body: With cosine distance (`<=>`), 0 means identical direction and 2 means opposite. To present a friendly 0-1 similarity score, use `1 - distance` for cosine. Smaller distance always means more similar, so `ORDER BY distance` naturally puts the best matches first.

slides.title: Production touches
bullets: register_vector so lists round-trip cleanly
Batch embed to cut API calls
Filter with WHERE before ORDER BY
Tune ef_search for recall vs latency
Store the model name to allow re-embedding

slides.title: Building the index before any data
slides.body: Create the index on an empty or tiny table and IVFFlat clusters are meaningless, and even HNSW skips planning benefits. Load a representative batch first, then build, then ANALYZE so the planner knows row counts. Re-embed and rebuild if you ever switch embedding models.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 92.
slides.body: Next: the mistakes that quietly wreck pgvector in production — and how to avoid each one.

91-5.caption:
pgvector queries that look correct can silently scan every row, return stale neighbors, or blow up your memory. The failures are quiet, not loud. 🚧

Most pgvector pain isn't bugs in the extension — it's predictable misuse: a forgotten index, a metric mismatch, mixing embedding models, or filtering in a way that defeats the index. Each one degrades results or latency without throwing an error.

This post is the field guide to the traps.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• the silent full-scan from a missing/mismatched index
• mixing incompatible embedding models
• un-normalized vectors with inner product
• filters that bypass the ANN index
• ignoring recall when tuning for speed
• forgetting to ANALYZE after big loads

Know the failure modes before they reach prod. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 1. The silent full scan
slides.body: If no index matches your query's operator, Postgres just sequentially scans every vector. No error, correct results, terrible latency. Always confirm the index is used before trusting your numbers.

slides.title: Check the plan
slides.code:
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT id FROM docs
ORDER BY embedding <=> '[...]'
LIMIT 5;

-- Want: 'Index Scan using docs_embedding_hnsw'
-- Bad:  'Seq Scan on docs'

slides.title: 2. Metric / op-class mismatch
slides.body: An index built with `vector_l2_ops` won't serve a `<=>` cosine query — Postgres falls back to a seq scan. The operator in your ORDER BY must match the operator class on the index, every time. This is the most common cause of a 'why is my index ignored?' ticket.

slides.title: 3. Mixing embedding models
slides.body: Vectors from `text-embedding-3-small` and `text-embedding-3-large` live in different spaces and aren't comparable, even with the same dimension count. Distances between them are noise. Store the model name per row and re-embed everything when you switch models.

slides.title: Track the model
slides.code:
ALTER TABLE docs
  ADD COLUMN embed_model text NOT NULL
  DEFAULT 'text-embedding-3-small';

-- query only one space at a time
SELECT id FROM docs
WHERE embed_model = 'text-embedding-3-small'
ORDER BY embedding <=> $1 LIMIT 5;

slides.title: 4. Inner product on un-normalized vectors
slides.body: The `<#>` inner-product operator only behaves like cosine similarity if your vectors are L2-normalized. Feed it raw magnitudes and longer vectors win regardless of direction. Either normalize before storing, or just use `<=>` cosine distance and stop worrying about it.

slides.title: 5. Filters that defeat the index
slides.body: A highly selective WHERE clause on a non-indexed column can force Postgres to filter first and then scan exactly, ignoring the ANN index. Use partial indexes, or push the filter into the indexed path, when you need both tight filters and fast vector search.

slides.title: 6. Forgetting ANALYZE
slides.body: After a big bulk load or an index build, the planner's statistics are stale and it may misjudge costs — sometimes skipping the index entirely. Run `ANALYZE docs;` after large changes so the planner sees real row counts and picks the index path.

slides.title: The pre-flight checklist
bullets: EXPLAIN shows an index scan, not seq scan
Op-class matches query operator
One embedding model per query space
Normalize if using inner product
ANALYZE after big loads
Tune ef_search/probes for target recall

92-1.caption:
Two companies, two flagship model families, and one decision you'll make on almost every AI project: OpenAI or Claude. 🤝

The OpenAI API and the Anthropic (Claude) API are HTTP services that turn a prompt into a model completion. You send JSON over HTTPS with a key, you get tokens back. The hard part isn't the request — it's understanding that these are two different APIs with different shapes, different message formats, and different defaults, even though they solve the same problem.

This post sets the vocabulary before any code makes sense.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• what each API actually is
• the request/response mental model
• tokens, context windows, and pricing units
• how the two API shapes differ
• where these APIs sit in your stack

Learn the nouns first. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Two HTTP APIs, one job
slides.body: Both the OpenAI API and Anthropic's Claude API are REST endpoints you call over HTTPS. You send a prompt plus settings as JSON, authenticate with an API key, and receive generated text (and metadata) back. No model runs on your machine — you rent inference from their servers, billed per token.

slides.title: The core noun: a completion
slides.body: You give the model a list of messages; it produces a completion — the next chunk of text continuing the conversation. Everything else (tools, JSON mode, streaming, vision) is built on this single primitive. Learn the message-in, message-out loop and you've learned 80% of both APIs.

slides.title: Tokens are the unit
slides.body: Models don't see characters or words — they see tokens, sub-word chunks roughly 4 characters each. You're billed per input token and per output token, at different rates. "Context window" is the max tokens (input + output) a single request can hold. Count tokens, not words, when you estimate cost.

slides.title: Same goal, different shapes
bullets: OpenAI: /v1/chat/completions, choices[] array
Claude: /v1/messages, content[] blocks
OpenAI: system as a message role
Claude: system as a top-level field
Both: messages list of role + content

slides.title: Why an API, not a download
slides.body: Frontier models are hundreds of gigabytes and need clusters of GPUs to serve fast. The API model means you never host weights, never manage hardware, and always get the latest version. You trade control and data locality for zero ops and instant scale.

slides.title: Model names are versioned
slides.body: You don't call "GPT" or "Claude" — you call a specific snapshot like gpt-4o or claude-sonnet-4. Each has its own price, speed, context size, and capabilities. Pin a model string in code so a silent upgrade can't change your output overnight.

slides.title: The smallest OpenAI call
slides.code:
from openai import OpenAI
client = OpenAI()  # reads OPENAI_API_KEY

resp = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="gpt-4o",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Say hi"}],
)
print(resp.choices[0].message.content)

slides.title: The smallest Claude call
slides.code:
from anthropic import Anthropic
client = Anthropic()  # reads ANTHROPIC_API_KEY

msg = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-20250514",
    max_tokens=256,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Say hi"}],
)
print(msg.content[0].text)

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 93.
slides.body: Next: why API choice quietly shapes your whole product.

92-2.caption:
Picking an LLM API feels like a checkout decision. It's actually an architecture decision that follows you for the life of the product. 🧱

The model you call shapes your latency budget, your monthly bill, your data-governance story, and even how you write prompts. Switching later isn't a one-line change — message formats, tool schemas, and safety behavior all differ. That's why the OpenAI-vs-Claude question deserves real thought, not a coin flip.

This post is about consequences, not syntax.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• why lock-in is subtler than it looks
• cost that scales with usage, not seats
• latency and the user-experience tax
• data, privacy, and training opt-outs
• why capability gaps move every few months

Decide deliberately. Save it. 👇

slides.title: It's an architecture decision
slides.body: Your prompt format, tool definitions, streaming code, and error handling all couple to one provider's API shape. Treat "which API" as a foundational choice, not a setting. The cost of getting it wrong shows up months later as a migration project.

slides.title: Cost scales with tokens
slides.body: Unlike SaaS seats, LLM cost grows with every request and every token in and out. A feature that's cheap in a demo can be ruinous at scale if prompts are bloated. Input and output are priced separately, and output is usually 3-5x more expensive, so verbose responses hurt twice.

slides.title: Latency is a UX feature
slides.body: Time-to-first-token decides whether a chat feels alive or frozen. Larger models think longer; streaming hides some of it but not all. Your model choice and your context size directly set the speed your users feel on every interaction.

slides.title: Data and governance differ
bullets: Both offer no-training-on-your-data for API traffic
Enterprise tiers add data residency + retention controls
Read the policy, don't assume — it changes
PII handling may need a BAA / DPA
Logging your own prompts is on you

slides.title: Capability gaps move
slides.body: The "better" model flips every few months as each lab ships. Coding, long-context reasoning, vision, and tool use each have a current leader, and it rotates. Build so you can re-benchmark and switch, rather than betting the product on today's leaderboard.

slides.title: Pin model + cost in config
slides.code:
# config.py — make the model a knob, not a hardcode
MODEL = "claude-sonnet-4-20250514"
PRICE_IN = 3.00 / 1_000_000   # $ per input token
PRICE_OUT = 15.00 / 1_000_000 # $ per output token

def cost(usage):
    return (usage.input_tokens * PRICE_IN +
            usage.output_tokens * PRICE_OUT)

slides.title: The expensive assumption
slides.body: Teams pick a provider from a tweet, hardcode it everywhere, then discover the bill or a capability gap in production. The fix is structural: isolate the provider call behind one function from day one, so the decision stays reversible.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 93.
slides.body: Next: how a request actually becomes tokens on the wire.

92-3.caption:
Type a sentence, get an answer. Between those two moments is a precise machine: tokenizer, HTTP request, sampler, and a token-by-token stream. ⚙️

Understanding the mechanics turns the API from magic into something you can debug. Why is it slow? Why did it cut off? Why did the cost spike? All of it is explainable once you see how a request flows from your JSON to a stream of tokens and back.

This post traces one call end to end.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• how text becomes tokens
• the anatomy of a chat request
• how the model picks each next token
• streaming vs waiting for the full reply
• stop reasons and why replies end

See the machine. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Step 1: tokenize
slides.body: Your text is split into tokens by a tokenizer specific to the model family. "Carousel" might be one token; "ANTHROPIC_API_KEY" several. This is why cost depends on content, not character count, and why the same string costs differently across providers.

slides.title: Step 2: the request body
slides.body: You POST JSON containing the model name, a messages array, and sampling params like temperature and max_tokens. The server validates your key, assembles the full prompt (including system instructions), and runs inference. Bad JSON or a missing max_tokens is a common 400.

slides.title: Step 3: next-token sampling
slides.body: The model outputs a probability over the whole vocabulary for the next token. The sampler picks one using temperature (randomness) and top_p (nucleus cutoff). It appends that token, then repeats — the reply is generated one token at a time, not all at once.

slides.title: Sampling knobs
slides.code:
client.chat.completions.create(
    model="gpt-4o",
    messages=msgs,
    temperature=0.2,   # lower = more deterministic
    top_p=1.0,         # nucleus sampling cutoff
    max_tokens=500,    # cap on OUTPUT tokens
    stop=["\n\n"],     # halt on these strings
)

slides.title: Step 4: streaming
slides.body: Set stream=True and the server sends tokens as they're generated via server-sent events. Your UI can render words live instead of waiting for the whole reply. The total cost is identical — streaming only changes when you see the text, not how much you pay.

slides.title: Streaming a Claude reply
slides.code:
with client.messages.stream(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-20250514",
    max_tokens=512,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Explain DNS"}],
) as stream:
    for text in stream.text_stream:
        print(text, end="", flush=True)

slides.title: Step 5: why it stops
slides.body: Generation ends for a reason the API reports: the model emitted an end token (finish_reason "stop" / stop_reason "end_turn"), or it hit your max_tokens limit ("length" / "max_tokens"). A truncated reply almost always means your max_tokens was too low — check the stop reason first.

slides.title: Read the usage object
bullets: input_tokens / prompt_tokens = what you sent
output_tokens / completion_tokens = what it wrote
Cached input tokens are cheaper
Log usage on every call
Cost = usage x per-token price

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 93.
slides.body: Next: a full, runnable client for both providers.

92-4.caption:
Enough theory. Here's a single, runnable script that calls both OpenAI and Claude, streams output, uses tools, and handles errors like production code. 🧑‍💻

The goal isn't a toy snippet — it's a pattern you can paste into a real backend: one thin wrapper per provider, structured output, function calling, and retries with backoff. Copy it, swap the keys, ship it.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• environment + client setup for both
• a unified chat wrapper
• forcing structured JSON output
• tool / function calling on each API
• retries, timeouts, and error handling

Code you can actually run. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 0. Install + keys
slides.code:
# pip install openai anthropic
# set keys in your shell, never in code:
#   export OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-...
#   export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-...
import os
assert os.getenv("OPENAI_API_KEY")
assert os.getenv("ANTHROPIC_API_KEY")

slides.title: 1. Create both clients
slides.code:
from openai import OpenAI
from anthropic import Anthropic

oai = OpenAI()        # picks up OPENAI_API_KEY
ant = Anthropic()     # picks up ANTHROPIC_API_KEY

OAI_MODEL = "gpt-4o"
ANT_MODEL = "claude-sonnet-4-20250514"

slides.title: 2. One wrapper, two backends
slides.code:
def chat(provider, system, user, max_tokens=512):
    if provider == "openai":
        r = oai.chat.completions.create(
            model=OAI_MODEL,
            messages=[{"role": "system", "content": system},
                      {"role": "user", "content": user}],
            max_tokens=max_tokens)
        return r.choices[0].message.content
    r = ant.messages.create(
        model=ANT_MODEL, max_tokens=max_tokens,
        system=system,                       # top-level!
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user}])
    return r.content[0].text

slides.title: 3. Force structured JSON
slides.code:
# OpenAI: native JSON mode
r = oai.chat.completions.create(
    model=OAI_MODEL,
    response_format={"type": "json_object"},
    messages=[{"role": "user",
               "content": "Return {city, country} for Paris"}])
import json
data = json.loads(r.choices[0].message.content)
print(data["country"])  # France

slides.title: 4. Tool / function calling
slides.code:
tools = [{"type": "function", "function": {
    "name": "get_weather",
    "description": "Current weather for a city",
    "parameters": {"type": "object",
        "properties": {"city": {"type": "string"}},
        "required": ["city"]}}}]

r = oai.chat.completions.create(
    model=OAI_MODEL, tools=tools,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Weather in Oslo?"}])
call = r.choices[0].message.tool_calls[0]
print(call.function.name, call.function.arguments)

slides.title: 4b. Claude tool use
slides.code:
tools = [{"name": "get_weather",
    "description": "Current weather for a city",
    "input_schema": {"type": "object",
        "properties": {"city": {"type": "string"}},
        "required": ["city"]}}]

r = ant.messages.create(model=ANT_MODEL, max_tokens=512,
    tools=tools,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Weather in Oslo?"}])
for block in r.content:
    if block.type == "tool_use":
        print(block.name, block.input)

slides.title: 5. Retries with backoff
Production checklist
bullets: Keys from env, never committed
Set explicit timeouts on the client
Log model, usage, and latency per call
Validate JSON output before trusting it
Always send a fresh idempotent request on retry

slides.body: Next: the mistakes that quietly break LLM apps.

92-5.caption:
The OpenAI and Claude APIs are easy to call and easy to misuse. Most production incidents trace back to the same handful of avoidable mistakes. 🧯

None of these are exotic. They're the boring failures — a leaked key, an uncapped output, a brittle parse — that take down real apps. Knowing them up front is cheaper than learning them from a postmortem.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• leaking API keys in client code
• ignoring max_tokens and stop reasons
• mixing up the two message formats
• trusting model output as valid JSON
• no rate-limit or retry strategy

Learn these the cheap way. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 1. Key in the browser
slides.body: Calling the API directly from frontend JS ships your secret key to every visitor — anyone can read it in network tools and run up your bill. API keys belong only on a server you control. The browser should call your backend, which holds the key.

slides.title: Fix: proxy through a server
2. Ignoring stop reasons
slides.body: If a reply is cut mid-sentence, the cause is almost always a low max_tokens, not a model bug. People truncate output, then parse the broken half and ship it. Always set max_tokens generously and check the finish/stop reason before trusting the text.

no.leaf: Tool call or refusal — handle it

slides.title: 3. Mixing the two formats
slides.body: OpenAI takes system as a message in the array; Claude takes system as a top-level field and requires max_tokens. Copy-pasting one provider's request shape into the other yields confusing 400 errors. Keep a thin per-provider adapter so the shapes never bleed together.

slides.title: 4. Trusting the JSON
slides.body: Even with JSON mode the model can return invalid or schema-violating JSON. Blindly json.loads-ing into your code path crashes on the first odd response. Parse defensively: validate against a schema, and retry or fall back when it doesn't fit.

slides.title: Fix: validate, don't assume
5. No retry strategy
slides.body: Both APIs return 429 (rate limit) and occasional 5xx under load. Code with no retries turns a transient blip into a user-facing failure. Use exponential backoff with jitter, respect the Retry-After header, and set a hard timeout so a slow call can't hang your request forever.

slides.title: The pre-ship checklist
bullets: Keys server-side only, rotated
max_tokens set + stop reason checked
One adapter per provider
JSON validated against a schema
Backoff + timeout on every call
Usage + errors logged

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 93.
slides.body: Day 92 done — you can ship against both APIs safely now.

93-1.caption:
Most people think "Hugging Face" is just a Python library. It isn't. The library is how you talk to it — the Hub is the thing. 🤗

The Hugging Face Hub is a Git-based platform that hosts models, datasets, and live demos, with versioning, a web API, and a Python client wrapped around the whole thing. Think GitHub, but built specifically for machine learning artifacts that are gigabytes of weights, not kilobytes of code.

This post is the map: what the Hub actually IS, before we get into why it matters or how it works under the hood.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• what the Hub is, separate from transformers
• the three repo types: models, datasets, spaces
• why it's Git + Git LFS underneath
• what a model card and config really hold
• how the Python client pulls a repo
• where the Hub sits in your ML workflow

Get the shape first and the rest stops looking like magic. Save it. 👇

slides.title: What the Hub actually is
slides.body: The Hugging Face Hub is a hosting platform for machine learning artifacts. It stores models, datasets, and demo apps in versioned Git repositories, exposes them through a web UI and REST API, and gives you a Python client to download or upload them. It is the registry; the libraries are just clients.

slides.title: Three kinds of repository
slides.body: Every artifact on the Hub lives in one of three repo types. Models hold weights, config, and a model card. Datasets hold data files plus a dataset card. Spaces hold runnable demo apps (Gradio, Streamlit, Docker). Same Git plumbing underneath, three different purposes.

slides.title: It's Git, with LFS for the big files
slides.body: Each repo is a real Git repository. Code and metadata are tracked normally, but multi-gigabyte weight files are stored with Git LFS, so the repo holds pointers and the binaries live in object storage. That gives you commits, branches, and full version history for models that are far too big for plain Git.

slides.title: Pull a repo with the client
slides.code:
# pip install huggingface_hub
from huggingface_hub import snapshot_download

# downloads every file in the repo, cached locally
path = snapshot_download(repo_id="bert-base-uncased")
print(path)  # ~/.cache/huggingface/hub/...

slides.title: The model card is the README
slides.body: Every model repo ships a README.md called the model card, with YAML metadata at the top (license, tags, datasets, metrics) and prose below describing intended use, training data, and limitations. The metadata powers search and filtering; the prose is how you decide whether to trust the model.

slides.title: The mental model
bullets: The Hub stores artifacts; libraries fetch them
Three repo types: model, dataset, space
Git + LFS gives versioning for huge files
A repo_id is just org/name
The model card is metadata + docs

slides.title: Confusing the Hub with transformers
slides.body: Beginners assume `transformers` IS Hugging Face. It isn't. transformers is a library that knows how to build and run models; the Hub is where those model files live. You can use the Hub with no Python at all, and you can run transformers against files you stored anywhere. Keep the registry and the client separate in your head.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 94.
slides.body: Next: why the Hub matters — how it turned model sharing into a reproducible, one-line operation.

93-2.caption:
Before the Hub, sharing a model meant a Google Drive link, a zip file, and a prayer that the other person could reconstruct your environment. 😮‍💨

The Hub matters because it made machine learning artifacts shareable the way code became shareable with GitHub. A model is now an address — `org/name` — that anyone can load in one line, with the exact version pinned, the license attached, and a card explaining how it was trained.

This post is the "so what": what the Hub actually changed about how teams build with AI.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• the reproducibility problem it solved
• why a repo_id beats a download link
• version pinning and the danger of "latest"
• model cards as accountability, not decoration
• the network effect of a shared registry
• where it still falls short

Reproducible by default is a feature, not a nicety. Save it. 👇

slides.title: It killed the zip-file workflow
slides.body: Sharing a model used to mean uploading a folder of weights somewhere and writing fragile instructions to rebuild it. The Hub replaced that with a stable address and a one-line load. The receiver gets the exact files, the config, and the tokenizer together — no guessing about which checkpoint or which preprocessing you meant.

slides.title: A repo_id is a contract
slides.body: `bert-base-uncased` resolves to the same files for everyone, everywhere. That turns a model into a citable, reproducible dependency you can put in a requirements file or a paper. The address is stable; the bytes behind it are versioned. Collaboration stops depending on private file shares.

slides.title: Pin a version, don't trust 'latest'
slides.code:
from transformers import AutoModel

# reproducible: pin to a specific commit
model = AutoModel.from_pretrained(
    "bert-base-uncased",
    revision="86b5e0934494bd15c9632b12f734a8a67f723594",
)
# vs. fragile: whatever 'main' is today
# model = AutoModel.from_pretrained("bert-base-uncased")

slides.title: Cards make models accountable
slides.body: Because every serious repo carries a model card, license, intended use, training data, and known limitations travel WITH the weights. That turns 'is this model safe to use commercially?' from an email thread into a field you can read and filter on. Provenance becomes part of the artifact, not tribal knowledge.

slides.title: The network effect
slides.body: A shared registry compounds. Each uploaded model, dataset, and Space makes the next project faster, because fine-tunes, quantizations, and demos all reference each other. Search, tags, and a common format mean you can find a starting point in minutes instead of training from scratch.

slides.title: The payoff
bullets: One-line, reproducible loads
Stable, citable model addresses
Version pinning by commit hash
License + provenance attached
A compounding shared ecosystem

slides.title: Treating the card as the truth
slides.body: A model card is author-written marketing as much as documentation. Claimed benchmarks may be cherry-picked, training data may be vaguely described, and 'no known limitations' usually means 'not tested.' Read cards as a starting point, then evaluate on your own data before you ship. Provenance attached is not the same as provenance verified.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 94.
slides.body: Next: under the hood — Git, LFS, the cache, and exactly how a download resolves.

93-3.caption:
When you call `from_pretrained("bert-base-uncased")`, a surprising amount happens before a single weight loads into memory. 🔍

Under the hood the Hub is Git repositories fronted by an HTTP API and a content-addressed cache. The big weight files are handled by Git LFS, every file is fetched by its hash, and downloads are deduplicated so the same blob is never stored twice. Understanding this turns 'why is my cache 40GB?' into something you can actually reason about.

This post is the engine room.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• how a repo is Git + Git LFS
• what a revision (branch / tag / commit) resolves to
• how the local cache is laid out
• why downloads are hash-addressed and deduped
• how the resolve API serves a file
• how auth tokens gate private repos

See the machine and your storage and latency stop being mysterious. Save it. 👇

slides.title: A repo is Git, big files are LFS
slides.body: Small files — config.json, tokenizer.json, the README — live in normal Git. Large binaries like model.safetensors are tracked by Git LFS: Git stores a tiny pointer file, while the real bytes sit in object storage keyed by their SHA-256 hash. That keeps the Git history light even when weights are gigabytes.

slides.title: A revision is just a Git ref
slides.body: The `revision` argument resolves like any Git ref. It can be a branch (`main`), a tag (`v1.0`), or a full commit SHA. Passing a commit SHA pins you to exact bytes forever; passing `main` means 'whatever is latest,' which can change under you. The Hub maps the ref to a commit, then to the file blobs.

slides.title: The cache is content-addressed
slides.body: Downloaded files land in ~/.cache/huggingface/hub. Inside each repo folder, blobs are stored by hash, and the snapshot for a given commit is a tree of symlinks pointing at those blobs. So two revisions that share a file share one copy on disk — the cache deduplicates automatically.

slides.title: Inspect the cache layout
slides.code:
huggingface-cli scan-cache

# REPO ID            SIZE ON DISK   NB FILES   REVISIONS
# bert-base-uncased  440.5M         9          2 (main, abc123)
#
# blobs/   <- files keyed by hash
# snapshots/<commit>/  <- symlinks into blobs

slides.title: Downloads are by hash, not by name
slides.body: Each file's identity is its content hash. The client asks the resolve endpoint for a path at a revision, gets back the hash and a download URL, and stores the blob under that hash. If the hash already exists locally, nothing downloads — that's how re-running a script is instant the second time.

slides.title: Resolve URL under the hood
slides.code:
# what the client hits to fetch one file:
GET https://huggingface.co/bert-base-uncased/resolve/main/config.json

# private repo? send a token:
curl -H "Authorization: Bearer hf_xxx" \
  https://huggingface.co/org/private-model/resolve/main/config.json

slides.title: Tokens gate access
slides.body: Public repos download anonymously. Private and gated repos require a user access token, sent as a bearer header. The token also identifies you for rate limits and write permissions. Scope it to read-only when you only need to pull, and never bake a write token into client-side code.

root.question: Is the file's hash already cached?

no.leaf: Download from CDN, store by hash

slides.title: What to remember
bullets: Repo = Git + LFS for big files
revision resolves a Git ref to a commit
Cache stores blobs by content hash
Shared files are deduped on disk
Tokens gate private/gated repos

slides.title: Assuming 'main' is stable
slides.body: Loading by branch name pulls whatever the maintainer last pushed, which can silently change weights or tokenizer between your training and deploy runs. For anything reproducible, pin `revision` to a commit SHA. The Hub will happily serve a different 'main' tomorrow and your results will quietly drift.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 94.
slides.body: Next: a full hands-on walkthrough — download, run, create a repo, and push your own model.

93-4.caption:
Theory is fine, but you ship code. Here's a complete Hub round-trip: download a model, run it, then create your own repo and push files to it. 💻

This is the end-to-end build. We log in with a token, pull a model two ways (the high-level pipeline and the low-level client), inspect what landed in the cache, then create a brand-new repo and upload a file — the same flow you'd use to publish a fine-tune.

This post is code-heavy on purpose — copy, adapt, run.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• authenticating with an access token
• downloading + running with pipeline
• pulling specific files with the client
• creating a repo from Python
• uploading files and folders
• pushing a transformers model directly

Keep this as your starting template. Save it. 👇

slides.title: What we'll do
bullets: Install + authenticate with a token
Download and run a model
Pull individual files via the client
Create a new repo from Python
Upload files, then push a full model

slides.title: 1. Install + log in
slides.code:
# pip install huggingface_hub transformers

# interactive: paste a token from hf.co/settings/tokens
huggingface-cli login

# or non-interactive in code:
from huggingface_hub import login
login(token="hf_xxx")  # use an env var in real code

slides.title: 2. Download + run a model
slides.code:
from transformers import pipeline

# pulls the repo from the Hub, caches it, then runs
clf = pipeline(
    "sentiment-analysis",
    model="distilbert-base-uncased-finetuned-sst-2-english",
)
print(clf("The Hub makes sharing models trivial."))
# [{'label': 'POSITIVE', 'score': 0.9998}]

slides.title: 3. Pull specific files
slides.code:
from huggingface_hub import hf_hub_download

# grab just one file instead of the whole repo
cfg = hf_hub_download(
    repo_id="bert-base-uncased",
    filename="config.json",
    revision="main",
)
print(cfg)  # local path to the cached file

slides.title: 4. Create a repo from Python
slides.code:
from huggingface_hub import HfApi

api = HfApi()
repo_id = api.create_repo(
    repo_id="my-username/my-first-model",
    repo_type="model",
    private=True,
).repo_id
print(repo_id)  # my-username/my-first-model

slides.title: 5. Upload files to the repo
slides.code:
# upload one file
api.upload_file(
    path_or_fileobj="./README.md",
    path_in_repo="README.md",
    repo_id=repo_id,
)

# or a whole folder (uses LFS for big files automatically)
api.upload_folder(folder_path="./model_out", repo_id=repo_id)

slides.title: 6. Push a model directly
slides.code:
from transformers import AutoModel, AutoTokenizer

model = AutoModel.from_pretrained("bert-base-uncased")
tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("bert-base-uncased")

# creates the repo if needed, commits weights + tokenizer
model.push_to_hub("my-username/my-bert")
tok.push_to_hub("my-username/my-bert")

slides.title: What push_to_hub does
slides.body: `push_to_hub` is a convenience wrapper: it creates the repo if missing, serializes the model and config, tracks big weight files with LFS, and makes a commit — all over the same HTTP API. Under the hood it's the same upload_file calls you saw in step 5, bundled for you.

slides.title: Production touches
bullets: Read token from an env var, never hardcode
Pin revision when you download for repro
Use upload_folder for large model dirs
Write a real model card before going public
Use create_commit to batch many files atomically

slides.title: Committing secrets or a write token
slides.body: It's easy to upload a folder that still contains an .env, a checkpoint with embedded keys, or to hardcode a write-scoped token in a notebook you then push. Add a .gitignore-style allowlist for uploads, scope tokens to the minimum, and rotate any token that touched client code. The Hub keeps full history — a leaked secret stays in old commits.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 94.
slides.body: Next: the mistakes that quietly bite people on the Hub — and how to dodge each one.

93-5.caption:
The Hub feels frictionless, which is exactly why people get burned by it. The failures are quiet: a leaked token, a moved model, a license you never read. 🚧

Most Hub pain isn't bugs — it's predictable misuse. Loading by `main` and getting different weights next month. Trusting a card's benchmark. Blowing your disk with an un-pruned cache. Running a Space's arbitrary code without thinking. Each one degrades reproducibility, security, or trust without throwing an error.

This post is the field guide to the traps.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• unpinned revisions and silent drift
• tokens leaked into code and history
• ignoring license + gated terms
• the cache that eats your disk
• trusting cards and remote code blindly
• assuming a repo is permanent

Know the failure modes before they reach prod. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 1. Unpinned revisions drift
slides.body: Loading a model by branch name pulls whatever the maintainer last pushed. Your training run and your deploy run can quietly get different weights, breaking reproducibility with no error. Always pin `revision` to a commit SHA for anything you need to reproduce or audit later.

slides.title: Pin it
slides.code:
# fragile — 'main' can change
model = AutoModel.from_pretrained("org/model")

# reproducible — exact bytes, forever
model = AutoModel.from_pretrained(
    "org/model",
    revision="e1f2a3b...",  # full commit SHA
)

slides.title: 2. Leaking tokens
slides.body: A write-scoped token pasted into a notebook, committed to Git, or printed in logs is a full account credential. Anyone who finds it can push malicious weights to your repos.
