98-1.caption:
Your model is a Python function. The internet can't call a Python function — it can call an HTTP endpoint. FastAPI is the layer that turns one into the other. 🌐

Serving a model means wrapping its predict step in a web API: a process that listens for requests, runs inference, and returns predictions as JSON. FastAPI has become the default for this in Python because it's async, fast, and validates every request against a typed schema before your model ever sees it.

This post sets the vocabulary before any deployment makes sense.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• what "serving a model" actually means
• why an API, not a script or notebook
• request → inference → response, end to end
• where FastAPI, Pydantic, and Uvicorn fit
• online vs batch serving

Learn the map first. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Serving = your model behind an API
slides.body: Serving a model means putting its predict step behind a network interface so other systems can use it. Instead of importing your model in a script, an app sends an HTTP request with input features and gets a prediction back as JSON. The model becomes a service any language can call.

slides.title: A notebook is not a service
slides.body: A trained model in a .pkl file or a notebook cell can only be used by you, on your machine, right now. To power a product it has to be reachable, always-on, and callable by other code. Serving is the step that turns a saved artifact into something the rest of your system can actually depend on.

slides.title: The three pieces
bullets: FastAPI — defines routes + validation
Pydantic — types + validates the request body
Uvicorn — the ASGI server that runs it

slides.title: Why FastAPI specifically
slides.body: FastAPI is built on async (ASGI), so it handles many concurrent requests without blocking. It uses Python type hints to validate input automatically and generates interactive API docs for free. For model serving, that means less boilerplate and a typed contract between caller and model — fewer bad inputs reach inference.

slides.title: The smallest serving API
Where the model lives
slides.body: The trained model is loaded once when the server starts, not on every request. It sits in memory as a global object; each request reuses it. Loading per request would re-read the file and re-initialize the model every time — slow and wasteful. Load once, predict many is the core serving pattern.

slides.title: The mental model
bullets: A model in prod is a service, not a file
Validate input before it hits the model
Load the model once, at startup
JSON in, JSON out — language agnostic
The API is a contract, keep it stable

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 99.
slides.body: Next: why FastAPI specifically — and what serving wrong actually costs you.

98-2.caption:
You can serve a model with Flask in ten lines. So why does almost every new ML serving stack reach for FastAPI instead? Because the things that break in production aren't the ten lines — they're everything around them. 🛡️

FastAPI matters for serving because it solves the unglamorous problems that sink real endpoints: malformed input, blocking I/O under load, undocumented contracts, and silent type errors. It bakes validation, concurrency, and docs into the framework instead of leaving them to you.

This post is about why the tool choice actually pays off.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• why typed validation stops a whole class of bugs
• async vs sync under concurrent load
• auto-generated docs as a living contract
• the cost of garbage input reaching your model
• where FastAPI fits next to Flask and TorchServe

Understand the payoff. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Garbage input is the #1 failure
slides.body: Most serving incidents aren't the model being wrong — they're a caller sending a string where a float was expected, a missing field, or a null. Without validation, that garbage reaches the model and produces a crash or a silently nonsensical prediction. FastAPI rejects it at the door with a clear 422 error.

slides.title: Validation you get for free
slides.code:
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field

class LoanRequest(BaseModel):
    income: float = Field(gt=0)
    age: int = Field(ge=18, le=120)
    credit_score: int = Field(ge=300, le=850)

# A bad request -> automatic 422, model never runs:
# {"income": "lots", "age": 9}  ->  rejected

slides.title: Async handles real concurrency
slides.body: Inference often waits — on a GPU, a feature store, another API. A synchronous server blocks one whole worker per waiting request. FastAPI's async model lets one worker juggle many in-flight requests during those waits, so the same hardware serves far more traffic before it falls over.

slides.title: Docs are a living contract
slides.body: FastAPI generates interactive OpenAPI docs from your type hints automatically. Callers see exactly what fields to send, what types, and what comes back — without you writing a wiki page that goes stale. The schema is the documentation, so they can never drift apart.

diagram.axisLabel: share of serving bugs FastAPI defuses

slides.title: Type hints catch errors early
slides.body: Because requests and responses are typed Pydantic models, mistakes surface at parse time with a precise message, not three layers deep inside your inference code. You debug 'field income must be > 0' instead of 'TypeError: unsupported operand' from somewhere in NumPy.

right.title: Flask / TorchServe
items: More setup for the same guards

slides.title: Why the choice pays off
bullets: Bad input dies at the door, not in the model
Same hardware serves more traffic
Docs can't drift from the API
Errors are precise and early
Works for any model framework

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 99.
slides.body: Next: the mechanics — how a FastAPI serving app actually works inside.

98-3.caption:
A FastAPI serving app is four moving parts: a server that listens, a schema that validates, a model loaded once, and a handler that runs inference. Understand how they connect and the whole thing stops being magic. ⚙️

This post traces a request from the moment it hits the socket to the JSON that comes back — and shows where lifespan startup, dependency injection, and async fit. Each piece has one job, and seeing the wiring is what lets you debug and scale it later.

This post is about the mechanics, not the syntax.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• the ASGI server → app → handler chain
• loading the model once on startup (lifespan)
• how Pydantic parses and validates the body
• sync vs async path inside the handler
• returning typed, predictable responses

See the machine. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Step 1: the server listens
slides.body: Uvicorn is the ASGI server. It owns the socket and the event loop: it accepts TCP connections, parses raw HTTP into request objects, and hands them to your FastAPI app. FastAPI itself isn't a server — it's the application Uvicorn runs. That separation is why you launch with 'uvicorn main:app'.

slides.title: Step 2: load the model once
slides.body: On startup, before any request, you load the model into memory using a lifespan handler. It runs once, stores the model in app state, and the same object serves every request. Loading inside the request handler instead would re-read the file each call — adding hundreds of milliseconds to every prediction.

slides.title: Load on startup with lifespan
slides.code:
from contextlib import asynccontextmanager
from fastapi import FastAPI
import joblib

ml = {}

@asynccontextmanager
async def lifespan(app: FastAPI):
    ml["model"] = joblib.load("model.pkl")  # once
    yield
    ml.clear()  # cleanup on shutdown

app = FastAPI(lifespan=lifespan)

slides.title: Step 3: route + validate
slides.body: FastAPI matches the URL and method to a handler, then uses the handler's type hints to parse and validate the request body via Pydantic. If validation fails, FastAPI returns a 422 with field-level errors and your code never runs. Only well-formed input reaches inference.

slides.title: The handler runs inference
Step 4: sync vs async handlers
slides.body: Define a handler with 'def' and FastAPI runs it in a threadpool so CPU-bound inference doesn't block the event loop. Define it with 'async def' only if the body awaits I/O. Getting this wrong — async around blocking CPU work — silently serializes your whole server.

root.question: Does the handler await I/O (DB, HTTP, queue)?

slides.title: Step 5: typed response out
slides.body: The handler returns a dict (or a Pydantic response model) that FastAPI serializes to JSON. Declaring a response_model gives you a documented, validated output shape and strips anything extra. The caller gets a predictable contract: the same fields, same types, every time.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 99.
slides.body: Next: a complete, runnable FastAPI serving app you can paste and launch.

98-4.caption:
Enough theory. Here's a complete FastAPI serving app: load a real scikit-learn model on startup, validate input with Pydantic, return a typed response, add a health check, and test it with curl. 🧑‍💻

The goal isn't a toy — it's the shape of a real endpoint you can grow. Each piece is small alone, but together they turn a saved .pkl into a validated, documented, deployable service. Train the model, run uvicorn, and you have a working API.

Copy it, swap the model, and you've got a serving skeleton.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• train + save a model to load later
• define the request and response schemas
• load once on startup, predict per request
• a /health endpoint for readiness checks
• test the live API with curl

Code you can actually run. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Build It: A FastAPI Model API
0. Setup
slides.code:
# pip install fastapi uvicorn scikit-learn joblib numpy
from sklearn.datasets import load_iris
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
import joblib

slides.title: 1. Train + save the model
slides.code:
# train.py  (run once)
X, y = load_iris(return_X_y=True)
clf = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=200).fit(X, y)
joblib.dump(clf, "iris.pkl")
print("saved iris.pkl")
# now the model is an artifact the API can load

slides.title: 2. Schemas: typed in and out
slides.code:
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field

class IrisInput(BaseModel):
    features: list[float] = Field(min_length=4, max_length=4)

class Prediction(BaseModel):
    class_id: int
    label: str
    confidence: float

slides.title: 3. App: load once, predict per request
slides.code:
from contextlib import asynccontextmanager
from fastapi import FastAPI
import joblib, numpy as np

ml, LABELS = {}, ["setosa", "versicolor", "virginica"]

@asynccontextmanager
async def lifespan(app: FastAPI):
    ml["model"] = joblib.load("iris.pkl")
    yield
    ml.clear()

app = FastAPI(lifespan=lifespan)

@app.post("/predict", response_model=Prediction)
def predict(item: IrisInput):
    X = np.array(item.features).reshape(1, -1)
    proba = ml["model"].predict_proba(X)[0]
    cid = int(proba.argmax())
    return Prediction(class_id=cid, label=LABELS[cid],
                      confidence=float(proba[cid]))

slides.title: 4. A health check for readiness
5. Run it + call it
slides.code:
# run the server
uvicorn main:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000

# in another terminal, send a request
curl -X POST http://localhost:8000/predict \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"features": [5.1, 3.5, 1.4, 0.2]}'

slides.title: Make it production-grade
bullets: Pin the model version + log it at startup
Add /health AND a deeper /ready check
Set request timeouts and a max body size
Containerize: Dockerfile + uvicorn workers
Log every prediction for monitoring later

slides.body: Next: the FastAPI serving mistakes that quietly wreck latency and reliability.

98-5.caption:
Most FastAPI serving disasters aren't bugs in the model. They're the model loaded on every request, blocking inference jammed into an async handler, and zero validation — small slips that quietly destroy latency and uptime. 🧯

None of these are exotic. They're the everyday serving mistakes that turn a fast model into a slow, fragile endpoint that falls over under real traffic. Knowing them up front is far cheaper than discovering them during a load spike in production.

Each one comes with the fix, so treat this as a checklist before you ship an endpoint.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• loading the model on every request
• blocking CPU work inside async def
• skipping input validation entirely
• returning raw model objects, not JSON
• one worker, no batching, no limits

Learn these the cheap way. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 1. Loading the model per request
slides.body: Calling joblib.load() inside the handler re-reads the file and rebuilds the model on every single request — adding hundreds of milliseconds and crushing throughput. The fix is to load once at startup via a lifespan handler and keep the model in memory, so every request reuses the same object.

slides.title: Fix: load once, not per call
2. Blocking work in async def
slides.body: Putting CPU-heavy inference inside 'async def' without awaiting anything blocks the event loop — so the server handles one request at a time, no matter how many workers you think you have. The fix: use plain 'def' for CPU-bound handlers so FastAPI runs them in a threadpool, or offload to a process pool.

slides.title: Fix: don't block the loop
3. No input validation
slides.body: Accepting a raw dict or untyped body means malformed input flows straight into the model — a string where a float belongs, a missing field, the wrong number of features. You get a cryptic crash or a silently wrong prediction. The fix is a Pydantic model with field constraints so bad input is rejected with a clear 422.

slides.title: 4. Returning non-serializable objects
slides.body: Returning a NumPy array, a tensor, or a raw model output often throws a serialization error or leaks an unstable shape to callers. The fix is to convert to native Python types (float, int, list) and ideally declare a response_model so the output contract is typed, validated, and documented.

slides.title: Fix: return clean JSON
5. No workers, limits, or batching
slides.body: Running a single Uvicorn process with no timeouts, no body-size cap, and no batching means one slow or huge request can stall everything, and CPU cores sit idle. The fix is multiple workers (or Gunicorn), request timeouts, a max body size, and micro-batching when your model supports it.

slides.title: Fix: scale + guard the server
slides.code:
# Multiple workers to use all CPU cores
uvicorn main:app --workers 4 --timeout-keep-alive 5

# Or Gunicorn managing Uvicorn workers:
gunicorn main:app -k uvicorn.workers.UvicornWorker \
  --workers 4 --timeout 30
# add a reverse proxy (nginx) for body-size limits

slides.title: The pre-ship checklist
bullets: Model loaded once at startup, not per request
Plain def for CPU inference, async only for I/O
Pydantic validates every request body
Responses are native types + a response_model
Multiple workers, timeouts, and body limits set
/health and /ready endpoints for probes

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 99.
slides.body: Day 98 done — you can serve a model as a fast, reliable API now.

99-1.caption:
Your model passed every offline eval, shipped clean, and then quietly started lying to users three weeks later. Nobody noticed for nine days. 🔭

Monitoring an LLM in production means continuously watching what the live system actually does — its outputs, latency, cost, and quality — so you catch regressions, drift, and abuse before your users (or your bill) do. A traditional service has clear errors; an LLM fails by being confidently wrong while returning HTTP 200.

This post is the map: what LLM monitoring actually is, and why it's nothing like watching a normal web service.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• what "monitoring an LLM" actually means
• why a 200 OK tells you almost nothing
• the four pillars: quality, latency, cost, safety
• observability vs. monitoring
• where monitoring sits in the LLM stack

Get the shape first, then we go deep. Save it. 👇

slides.title: What LLM monitoring means
slides.body: Monitoring an LLM in production is continuously measuring what the live system does — every prompt, response, latency, token cost, and quality signal — and alerting when behavior degrades. It's not a one-time eval before launch; it's an always-on feedback loop on a system whose outputs are non-deterministic and whose failures are often invisible.

slides.title: A 200 OK tells you nothing
slides.body: A normal API fails loudly: 500s, timeouts, stack traces. An LLM returns a confident, well-formatted, completely wrong answer with HTTP 200. The infrastructure is healthy; the output is garbage. That gap is the whole reason LLM monitoring is its own discipline — you must watch the content, not just the status code.

slides.title: Observability vs. monitoring
slides.body: Monitoring answers 'is something wrong?' via predefined metrics and alerts. Observability answers 'why is it wrong?' by capturing rich traces — the full prompt, retrieved context, tool calls, and response — so you can reconstruct any single request after the fact. For LLMs you need both: metrics to catch it, traces to debug it.

slides.title: The smallest useful trace
slides.code:
import time, json

def call_llm(prompt):
    t0 = time.time()
    resp = client.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4o-mini",
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    )
    log = {
        "prompt": prompt,
        "output": resp.choices[0].message.content,
        "latency_ms": int((time.time() - t0) * 1000),
        "prompt_tokens": resp.usage.prompt_tokens,
        "output_tokens": resp.usage.completion_tokens,
    }
    print(json.dumps(log))  # ship to your log sink
    return log

slides.title: The mental model
bullets: Watch outputs, not just status codes
Four pillars: quality, latency, cost, safety
Metrics catch it; traces explain it
Non-deterministic output needs sampling
Monitoring is always-on, not a launch gate

slides.title: Thinking offline evals are enough
slides.body: Teams run a benchmark suite before launch, see 94% accuracy, and assume they're done. But production traffic looks nothing like your test set: real users phrase things weirdly, the world changes, and your retrieved documents go stale. Offline evals are a gate, not a guarantee. Without live monitoring, the first sign of a regression is an angry customer.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 100.
slides.body: Next: why this matters — the silent failures, runaway costs, and drift that only show up in prod.

99-2.caption:
An unmonitored LLM feature is a blank check signed by your users and your finance team. 💸

Monitoring matters because LLMs fail in ways traditional software doesn't: they hallucinate while looking confident, they drift as the world changes underneath a frozen model, a prompt tweak silently regresses quality, and a retry loop can 10x your token bill overnight. None of it shows up as an error — it shows up as churn, a refund, or an invoice.

This post is the "so what": the concrete failure modes that only surface in production, and what they cost.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• silent quality failures vs. loud errors
• model + data drift over time
• why cost is a first-class metric for LLMs
• prompt and provider regressions
• safety, abuse, and the compliance angle
• the real ROI of catching it early

The cost of not monitoring is paid in trust. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Failures are silent, not loud
slides.body: A crashed service pages you in minutes. A hallucinating LLM returns a fluent, wrong answer that your monitoring dashboard sees as a perfectly healthy 200. The damage — a wrong medical summary, a fabricated citation, a hallucinated refund policy — lands on the user, not on your error log. Silent failure is the defining risk of shipping LLMs.

slides.title: The model is frozen; the world isn't
slides.body: Your model's weights are fixed at training time, but user behavior, slang, products, and facts keep moving. This is drift: the live input distribution slowly diverges from what the model saw, and quality erodes without any code change. For RAG systems, your indexed documents go stale too. Drift is gradual, which is exactly why you only catch it by measuring over time.

slides.title: Cost is a first-class metric
slides.body: Unlike a fixed-price API call, an LLM's cost scales with tokens — and a single bad prompt template, a runaway agent loop, or an unbounded context window can multiply spend overnight. Without per-request cost tracking, you discover the problem on the monthly invoice. Token cost belongs on the same dashboard as latency and errors, not in a billing surprise.

slides.title: A prompt tweak is a deploy
slides.body: Editing a system prompt, swapping a model version, or changing a temperature is a production change with no compiler to catch regressions. A wording tweak that helps one use case can quietly break another. Provider-side model updates can shift behavior under you. Monitoring is how you detect that 'harmless' change degraded quality before users do.

slides.title: Safety and compliance aren't optional
slides.body: In production your model meets adversarial users: prompt injections, jailbreaks, and attempts to extract training data or other users' PII. It can also emit toxic content or leak sensitive data it was given. Monitoring for these isn't just good hygiene — under regimes like GDPR and the EU AI Act, demonstrating oversight of an AI system is a legal expectation.

slides.title: Catch a runaway cost early
What you're actually protecting
bullets: User trust — silent wrong answers churn users
The budget — tokens scale, surprises don't
Quality over time — drift is invisible day to day
Your last deploy — prompt tweaks regress
Compliance — oversight is increasingly required

slides.title: Treating LLM ops like web ops
slides.body: Reusing your existing uptime-and-error-rate dashboards feels efficient, but it monitors the wrong layer. The server can be 100% healthy while every answer is subtly wrong, twice as expensive as last week, and drifting. Web ops watches the pipe; LLM ops has to watch what flows through it. You need both — but the second one is the one teams skip.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 100.
slides.body: Next: how it works — tracing, sampling, LLM-as-judge, and drift detection under the hood.

99-3.caption:
You can't run a human reviewer on every one of 50,000 daily LLM responses. So how do you actually know your quality at scale? ⚙️

Under the hood, LLM monitoring is four moving parts: trace every request, sample a slice for deep scoring, judge quality automatically (often with another LLM), and detect drift by comparing distributions over time. Add online metrics for latency and cost, and you have a system that watches both the pipe and what flows through it.

This post is the engine room.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• distributed tracing for LLM requests
• sampling so you don't score everything
• LLM-as-a-judge for automated quality
• reference-free metrics that need no labels
• drift detection on inputs and outputs
• closing the loop with user feedback

See the mechanism and it stops being guesswork. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Trace every request end to end
slides.body: A trace captures one request's full journey: the user input, the retrieved RAG context, each tool call, the final prompt sent to the model, the raw response, and timing for each step. Spans nest under a trace ID so you can replay exactly what happened. This is the raw material everything else — scoring, debugging, drift — is built on.

slides.title: Sample, don't score everything
slides.body: Running an LLM judge on 100% of traffic is slow and expensive — you'd pay for inference twice. Instead, score a random sample (say 1-5%) for trend tracking, plus targeted samples: low-confidence outputs, thumbs-down responses, and high-cost requests. You get statistical signal on the whole, with a microscope on the cases most likely to be broken.

slides.title: LLM-as-a-judge
slides.body: Since you rarely have ground-truth labels in production, you use a strong model to grade outputs against a rubric: is this relevant, grounded in the context, correct in format? The judge returns a score and a reason. It's not perfect — judges have biases — but calibrated against human ratings, it scales quality measurement to volumes no human team could touch.

slides.title: An LLM-as-a-judge call
slides.code:
RUBRIC = '''Rate the ANSWER for faithfulness to the CONTEXT.
Return JSON: {"score": 1-5, "reason": "..."}.
5 = fully supported, 1 = contradicted or fabricated.'''

def judge(context, answer):
    msg = f"CONTEXT:\n{context}\n\nANSWER:\n{answer}"
    r = client.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4o",
        messages=[{"role": "system", "content": RUBRIC},
                  {"role": "user", "content": msg}],
        response_format={"type": "json_object"},
        temperature=0,
    )
    return json.loads(r.choices[0].message.content)

slides.title: Reference-free metrics
slides.body: Some quality signals need no labels at all. Measure groundedness by checking whether claims appear in retrieved context, format validity by parsing the output against a schema, and refusal or empty-response rates with simple rules. These cheap, deterministic checks run on every request and catch whole categories of failure before you spend a judge call.

slides.title: Drift detection in practice
slides.body: Pick features — input length, embedding clusters, judge scores, refusal rate — and compute their distribution in a baseline window. Compare recent traffic against it with a statistical test (PSI or KS). When divergence crosses a threshold, you alert: the inputs or outputs have shifted, even though no one changed the code. That's your early warning for silent degradation.

slides.title: Population Stability Index
slides.code:
import numpy as np

def psi(baseline, live, bins=10):
    edges = np.histogram_bin_edges(baseline, bins=bins)
    b = np.histogram(baseline, edges)[0] / len(baseline)
    l = np.histogram(live, edges)[0] / len(live)
    b, l = np.clip(b, 1e-6, None), np.clip(l, 1e-6, None)
    return float(np.sum((l - b) * np.log(l / b)))

# PSI > 0.2 -> significant drift, investigate
score = psi(baseline_lengths, live_lengths)

slides.title: Close the loop with feedback
slides.body: The strongest quality signal is the user themselves: thumbs up/down, edits to your output, copy events, or whether they retried. Pipe that feedback back into your traces so a thumbs-down both flags a request for judge scoring and accumulates into a labeled dataset. Over time that dataset becomes your eval suite and your fine-tuning data.

slides.title: Trusting the judge blindly
slides.body: An LLM judge is a model, so it inherits model failure modes: it favors longer answers, can be swayed by confident tone, and may rate its own family of models higher. If you never check it against human labels, you're monitoring with an unvalidated instrument. Calibrate the judge on a few hundred human-rated examples, track its agreement rate, and re-check when you change judge models.

99-4.caption:
Knowing the theory of LLM monitoring is one thing. Wiring up tracing, automated scoring, drift jobs, and alerts in real code is another. 💻

This post is the working build: a decorator that traces every LLM call with latency and token cost, a sampling-based judge pipeline, a nightly drift job using PSI, a SQL query that powers the dashboard, and an alert rule that pages you on a quality drop. Copy, adapt, ship.

Each block is small on purpose so you can drop it straight into an existing service. This post is code-heavy.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• a tracing decorator for every LLM call
• logging tokens, cost, and latency cleanly
• a sampled LLM-as-judge scorer
• a nightly drift detection job
• the dashboard SQL behind the metrics
• an alert that fires on quality drops

Steal the patterns and skip the trial-and-error. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 1. A tracing decorator
2. Log cost with the trace
3. Sampled judge scorer
slides.code:
import random

def maybe_score(trace, rate=0.03):
    # always score thumbs-down + high-cost; else sample
    forced = trace.get("feedback") == "down" or trace["cost_usd"] > 0.02
    if not (forced or random.random() < rate):
        return None
    verdict = judge(trace["context"], trace["output"])
    save_score(trace["trace_id"], verdict["score"], verdict["reason"])
    if verdict["score"] <= 2:
        alert(f"Low quality {trace['trace_id']}: {verdict['reason']}")
    return verdict

slides.title: 4. Nightly drift job
slides.code:
# runs daily; compares last 24h vs. baseline week
def drift_job(db):
    base = db.query("SELECT judge_score FROM scores "
                    "WHERE day BETWEEN :a AND :b", a=-14, b=-7)
    live = db.query("SELECT judge_score FROM scores "
                    "WHERE day >= -1")
    p = psi(np.array(base), np.array(live))
    record_metric("quality_psi", p)
    if p > 0.2:
        alert(f"Quality drift detected: PSI={p:.3f}")

slides.title: 5. The dashboard query
slides.code:
-- hourly health: volume, latency, cost, quality
SELECT date_trunc('hour', ts)        AS hour,
       count(*)                       AS requests,
       percentile_cont(0.95)
         WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY latency_ms) AS p95_ms,
       round(sum(cost_usd), 2)        AS spend_usd,
       round(avg(judge_score), 2)     AS avg_quality
FROM llm_traces
WHERE ts > now() - interval '24 hours'
GROUP BY 1
ORDER BY 1;

slides.title: 6. An alert rule
slides.code:
# Prometheus-style alert on a quality drop
# rules.yml
groups:
- name: llm
  rules:
  - alert: LLMQualityDrop
    expr: avg_over_time(llm_judge_score[1h]) < 3.5
    for: 15m
    labels: { severity: page }
    annotations:
      summary: "LLM avg quality {{ $value }} below 3.5"

slides.title: Production tips
bullets: Emit traces async — never block the response
Sample for cost; force-score the suspicious
Store raw prompt + context for replay
Alert on quality and cost, not just errors
Version your prompts so you can attribute regressions

slides.title: Logging PII into your traces
slides.body: The fastest way to turn a monitoring system into a liability is to dump raw user prompts — full of names, emails, and account numbers — into logs that the whole company can query. Redact or hash PII before it hits the sink, set retention limits, and gate trace access. Otherwise your debugging tool becomes the breach you have to report.

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 100.
slides.body: Next: the common mistakes that make LLM monitoring noisy, blind, or worse than nothing.

99-5.caption:
Most broken LLM monitoring isn't broken because the tooling failed. It's broken because the team measured the wrong things, drowned in noise, or logged data they shouldn't have. 🧨

Monitoring looks easy — add a dashboard, watch latency, done. In practice teams reuse web-ops metrics that miss silent failures, alert on everything until everyone ignores alerts, trust an uncalibrated judge, log PII into queryable logs, and never close the loop. Each one quietly costs trust, money, or a 2 a.m. page that means nothing.

This post is the failure manual.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• monitoring infra health, not output quality
• alert fatigue from bad thresholds
• trusting the judge without calibration
• ignoring cost until the invoice
• logging PII and creating a breach
• collecting metrics nobody acts on

Learn the failure modes before they cost you. Save it. 👇

slides.title: 1. Watching infra, not output
slides.body: The most common mistake is reusing uptime, CPU, and error-rate dashboards and calling it LLM monitoring. Those confirm the server is alive; they say nothing about whether answers are correct, grounded, or safe. A model can hallucinate every response while every infra metric stays green. Monitor the content — quality, groundedness, safety — not just the pipe carrying it.

slides.title: 2. Alert fatigue
slides.body: If everything pages, nothing does. Teams set aggressive thresholds, get woken by noise, and within a week the channel is muted and a real incident slips through. Alert only on actionable, sustained signals — a quality drop held for 15 minutes, not one bad response. Page for what needs a human now; route everything else to a dashboard.

slides.title: 3. Trusting the judge blindly
slides.body: An LLM judge is itself a model with biases: it prefers longer answers, rewards confident tone, and may favor its own model family. If you never calibrate it against human labels, your quality numbers are fiction. Hand-rate a few hundred examples, track judge-human agreement, and re-validate whenever you change the judge model or its prompt.

slides.title: Calibrate the judge
slides.code:
# compare judge scores to human labels
from sklearn.metrics import cohen_kappa_score

human = [5, 4, 2, 1, 3, 5, 2]   # rated sample
model = [5, 5, 2, 1, 2, 4, 3]   # judge scores

kappa = cohen_kappa_score(human, model, weights="quadratic")
print(f"judge-human agreement: {kappa:.2f}")
# < 0.4 -> judge is unreliable, fix the rubric

slides.title: 4. Ignoring cost until the invoice
slides.body: Cost is treated as a finance problem, not an engineering metric — until a runaway agent loop or an unbounded context window 10x's the bill in a weekend. Track cost per request and per feature in real time, set per-request budget guards, and alert on spend the way you alert on errors. The invoice should confirm what your dashboard already told you.

slides.title: 5. Logging PII into your logs
slides.body: Dumping raw prompts and responses — names, emails, health details — into logs the whole org can query turns your monitoring stack into a compliance incident waiting to happen. Redact or hash PII at the edge before it reaches storage, set short retention, and restrict who can read traces. A debugging convenience must never become the breach you report.

slides.title: Redact before you log
6. Metrics nobody acts on
slides.body: A dashboard with forty charts and no owner is theater. If a quality drop has no alert, no runbook, and no one responsible, monitoring is just decoration. Define a small set of metrics tied to actions: this number crosses this line, this person does this thing. Monitoring only pays off when it closes the loop into a fix.

slides.title: The checklist
bullets: Monitor output quality, not just infra
Alert on sustained, actionable signals
Calibrate the judge against humans
Track cost as an engineering metric
Redact PII before it hits storage
Tie every metric to an action

slides.title: Save this. Follow for Day 100.
slides.body: Next day, Day 100 — we close the series by turning all of this into your AI portfolio.

100-1.caption:
Nobody hires you for the courses you finished. They hire you for the things you can show them you built. 🎯

An AI portfolio is the evidence layer of your career — a curated set of projects, write-ups, and artifacts that prove you can take an idea from data to deployed, working system. It's not a list of skills; it's proof those skills produced something real.

This post sets the definition before we get into why it matters or how to build one.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• what an AI portfolio actually is (and isn't)
• why it beats a resume of buzzwords
• the three pillars: projects, writing, presence
• what a single strong project contains
• the difference between a demo and a portfolio piece

The last skill of the series is showing your work. Save it. 👇

slides.title: A portfolio = proof, not promises
slides.body: An AI portfolio is a curated collection of work that proves you can build, evaluate, and ship machine learning systems. It's the difference between saying 'I know transformers' and showing a fine-tuned model with an evaluation report and a live demo. The portfolio is the evidence; the resume is just the index.

slides.title: Skills are claims, work is evidence
slides.body: Anyone can list 'PyTorch, LLMs, RAG' on a profile. A portfolio turns those claims into checkable artifacts: a repo someone can read, a demo they can click, a write-up they can follow. Reviewers trust what they can inspect far more than what you assert. The portfolio is you doing the verifying for them.

slides.title: What one strong project contains
bullets: A clear problem and who it's for
Real data, not just toy datasets
Honest evaluation with metrics
A README that tells the story
A way to actually run or try it

slides.title: Quality over quantity
slides.body: Three deep, finished projects beat fifteen abandoned tutorials. A reviewer spends minutes, not hours, on your profile. They form an opinion from your best one or two pieces. A pile of half-done forks actually hurts you — it signals you start things and don't finish them.

slides.title: What a reviewer sees first
slides.code:
$ git clone https://github.com/you/churn-predictor
$ cd churn-predictor
$ cat README.md
# Customer Churn Predictor
# Problem, data, approach, results, how to run
$ make demo      # one command, it just works
# -> serving on http://localhost:8000

slides.title: What it is NOT
bullets: Not a list of certificates
Not 12 cloned tutorial repos
Not a 200-line resume of tools
Not a private repo nobody can see
Not 'I'll build it after I'm hired'

slides.title: The mental model
bullets: A portfolio is evidence, not a claim
One great project beats ten started ones
If a stranger can't run it, it doesn't count
The story matters as much as the code
You are the curator of your own proof

slides.title: Save this. Follow for what comes after Day 100.
slides.body: Next: why a portfolio out-converts a resume of buzzwords — every time.

100-2.caption:
Two candidates, same resume. One links a deployed project with an honest eval report. The other lists 'experienced with LLMs.' Guess who gets the call. 🚀

A portfolio matters because hiring for AI is fundamentally a trust problem. The field moves fast, titles are inconsistent, and everyone claims the same skills. Evidence cuts through all of it. A working artifact answers the only question that matters: can this person actually build the thing?

This post is about why showing beats telling — in concrete, career terms.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• why AI hiring is a trust-and-evidence game
• how a portfolio shortcuts the screening funnel
• what it signals beyond raw skill
• the compounding effect of public work
• why it matters even more in a noisy, AI-saturated market

The market is loud. Proof is quiet and louder. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Hiring is a trust problem
slides.body: A hiring manager can't verify your skills in a 30-minute screen. They're forced to bet on signals. A resume is a weak signal — everyone writes the same words. A portfolio is a strong signal: it lets them check your claims before they ever talk to you, which de-risks the bet of hiring you.

slides.title: Everyone lists the same skills
slides.body: Search any AI job board and you'll see the identical stack on every profile: Python, PyTorch, LLMs, RAG, fine-tuning. Those words no longer differentiate anyone. What differentiates you is a link that says 'here is me doing exactly that, with results.' The portfolio is how you escape the sea of sameness.

slides.title: Where a portfolio shortcuts the funnel
It signals more than skill
slides.body: A finished, documented project proves things a test never can: that you can scope a problem, finish what you start, communicate clearly, and care about quality. These 'soft' signals are exactly what separate a junior who needs supervision from one who can be trusted with ambiguity.

slides.title: Public work compounds
slides.body: A resume sits in an inbox and dies. A public project keeps working: it gets starred, shared, found in search, linked from your posts. One genuinely useful repo can generate inbound interest for years. The effort is front-loaded; the returns keep arriving long after you hit publish.

diagram.axisLabel: reach and durability of the artifact

slides.title: What 'verifiable' looks like to them
slides.code:
# A reviewer can run your claim in 30 seconds:
curl -X POST http://your-demo.app/predict \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"text": "this product is amazing"}'
# -> {"sentiment": "positive", "confidence": 0.97}
# The claim 'I built a sentiment API' is now proven.

slides.title: It matters MORE in an AI-saturated market
slides.body: When anyone can generate a slick-looking project with an LLM in an afternoon, the bar shifts. What stands out now is depth: honest evaluation, hard tradeoffs, things that actually work at the edges. A portfolio that shows real engineering judgment is rarer — and more valuable — than ever.

slides.title: Why it pays off
bullets: Turns a hiring bet into a sure thing
Lifts you out of the sea of sameness
Signals judgment, not just skill
Public work compounds for years
Depth beats AI-generated polish

slides.title: Save this. Follow for what comes after Day 100.
slides.body: Next: how to actually build one — the anatomy of a portfolio that lands jobs.

100-3.caption:
A portfolio isn't something you 'have.' It's something you assemble deliberately, piece by piece, with a structure behind it. Here's the actual blueprint. 🏗️

Building a portfolio works like building a system: choose projects that show range, take each one end to end, document the decisions, and put it where people look. The mechanics are learnable. Most people fail not from lack of skill but from no structure — random tutorials instead of a designed body of work.

This post is the how: the anatomy, the workflow, and the layout.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• how to choose projects that show range
• the end-to-end anatomy of one piece
• the README structure reviewers actually read
• where to host code, demos, and writing
• the build → document → publish loop

Stop collecting. Start assembling. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Choose projects for range
slides.body: Pick 3-4 projects that together show breadth, not the same thing four times. A classic ML pipeline, an LLM/RAG app, a deployed API, and a from-scratch implementation cover most of what employers test for. Each one should answer a different question about what you can do.

slides.title: The anatomy of one piece
bullets: 1. Frame a real problem + who it helps
2. Get real data, clean it, document it
3. Build a baseline, then improve it
4. Evaluate honestly with the right metric
5. Deploy a demo + write the story up

slides.title: The README is the product
slides.body: Reviewers read the README, not your code, first. Lead with the problem and the result, then approach, then how to run it. A great README in a modest project beats brilliant code with no explanation. Treat the README as the front door — most visitors never go further.

slides.title: A README structure that works
slides.code:
# Project Title
One sentence: what it does + who it's for.

## Results
Key metric vs baseline. A chart or number.

## Approach
Data -> features -> model -> eval, briefly.

## Run it
```bash
make setup && make demo
```

## What I'd do next
Honest limitations + next steps.

slides.title: Where to put everything
bullets: Code: GitHub, public, clean history
Demo: Hugging Face Spaces / Streamlit / a small VM
Writing: a blog or repo write-ups
Index: a one-page personal site linking it all

slides.title: Make it run in one command
slides.code:
# Makefile — the difference between 'clickable' and ignored
setup:
	pip install -r requirements.txt

demo:
	uvicorn app:api --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000

test:
	pytest -q
# A stranger clones, types `make demo`, and it works.

slides.title: Build discipline
bullets: Finish before you start the next one
Baseline first — always have a number to beat
Write the README as you build, not after
Every project gets a live, clickable proof
Link everything from one place

slides.title: Save this. Follow for what comes after Day 100.
slides.body: Next: a real, runnable portfolio piece — a model card and a model behind an API.

100-4.caption:
Talk is cheap, so here's actual code. A portfolio piece isn't a notebook — it's a model with an honest model card, a typed API, and a test that proves it works. 💻

This post walks the artifacts that turn a trained model into a portfolio-grade piece: a structured model card so reviewers know what it does and where it fails, a clean serving endpoint, and a smoke test that runs in CI. These are the things that say 'I do this professionally,' not 'I followed a tutorial.'

This post is code-heavy. Copy it, adapt it, ship it.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• a model card template reviewers trust
• a typed prediction API in a few lines
• a test that proves the demo actually runs
• a CI workflow that keeps it green
• the project layout that ties it together

Ship the proof, not the promise. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Three artifacts make it portfolio-grade
bullets: A model card — what it does + limits
A serving API — typed, runnable
A test + CI — proof it stays working

slides.title: 1. The model card (the trust doc)
slides.code:
# Model Card: Churn Predictor v1
## Intended use
Flag customers likely to cancel within 30 days.
## Data
12k rows, 2023-2024, anonymized billing + usage.
## Metrics
PR-AUC 0.71 (baseline 0.52). Tuned on recall.
## Limitations
Underperforms on accounts < 30 days old.
Not validated outside the SaaS segment.
## Ethical notes
No protected attributes used as features.

slides.title: 2. Load the model once at startup
slides.code:
import joblib
from fastapi import FastAPI
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field

api = FastAPI(title="Churn Predictor")
model = joblib.load("model.joblib")  # loaded ONCE

class Customer(BaseModel):
    tenure_months: int = Field(ge=0)
    monthly_spend: float = Field(ge=0)
    support_tickets: int = Field(ge=0)

slides.title: 3. A typed prediction endpoint
4. A test that proves it runs
slides.code:
from fastapi.testclient import TestClient
from app import api

client = TestClient(api)

def test_predict_returns_probability():
    r = client.post("/predict", json={
        "tenure_months": 3,
        "monthly_spend": 49.0,
        "support_tickets": 5,
    })
    assert r.status_code == 200
    body = r.json()
    assert 0.0 <= body["churn_probability"] <= 1.0

slides.title: What CI runs on every push
5. CI keeps the proof honest
slides.code:
name: ci
on: [push, pull_request]
jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: { python-version: "3.11" }
      - run: pip install -r requirements.txt
      - run: pytest -q

slides.title: Why these specific artifacts
bullets: Model card = you understand limits, not just accuracy
Typed API = you can ship, not just train
Test + CI = it stays working without you
One-command run = anyone can verify it
Together they say 'professional', not 'tutorial'

slides.title: Save this. Follow for what comes after Day 100.
slides.body: Next: the mistakes that quietly sink portfolios — and how to avoid every one.

100-5.caption:
Most AI portfolios fail in silence. No error, no rejection email — just a recruiter who clicked, saw a 60-cell notebook with no README, and moved on. ⚠️

The common mistakes aren't about skill. They're about presentation, finishing, and honesty. A strong builder with a sloppy portfolio loses to an average builder with a clear one. The good news: every one of these failures is cheap to fix once you know to look for it.

This post is the anti-pattern list — what kills portfolios and what to do instead.

INSIDE THIS POST:
• the tutorial-clone trap that signals zero originality
• unfinished projects that broadcast 'I don't ship'
• the missing-README death (nobody reads code first)
• fake metrics + leakage that destroy trust
• private repos, dead demos, and no through-line

Fix these and you're ahead of most. Save it. 👇

slides.title: Mistake 1: the tutorial clone
slides.body: A repo named 'titanic-survival' or a copy of a famous course project tells a reviewer nothing — except that you can follow steps everyone else followed. It shows zero independent thinking. Build on a dataset or problem you actually care about, even a small original one. Originality beats polish on a clone.

slides.title: Mistake 2: half-finished everything
slides.body: Ten repos, each abandoned at 40%, is worse than one finished project. It directly signals the trait employers fear most: you don't ship. Hide or delete the dead ones. A portfolio of fewer, complete pieces tells a stronger story than a graveyard of starts.

diagram.axisLabel: how often this kills a portfolio piece

slides.title: Mistake 3: no README, no story
slides.body: Reviewers do not read your code first — they read the README, or they leave. A repo with no README is a locked door. Even excellent code behind it goes unseen. The single highest-leverage fix in most portfolios is writing a clear README for each project.

slides.title: Mistake 4: metrics that lie
slides.body: Reporting 99% accuracy on imbalanced data, or test scores inflated by data leakage, is the fastest way to lose a knowledgeable reviewer. They will spot it, and it poisons everything else on your profile. Report the right metric, show the baseline, and name your limitations honestly.

slides.title: The leakage that fakes great scores
slides.code:
# WRONG: scale on all data, then split -> leakage
X = scaler.fit_transform(X)          # sees test stats
X_tr, X_te, y_tr, y_te = split(X, y) # too late

# RIGHT: split first, fit only on train
X_tr, X_te, y_tr, y_te = split(X, y)
scaler.fit(X_tr)                     # train only
X_tr = scaler.transform(X_tr)
X_te = scaler.transform(X_te)        # apply, don't refit

slides.title: Mistake 5: nobody can see or run it
slides.body: A private repo, a dead demo link, or a project that only runs with your exact local setup might as well not exist. If a reviewer can't open it and can't run it, it counts for nothing. Public, clickable, and reproducible are non-negotiable for every piece.

slides.title: Mistake 6: no through-line
slides.body: A portfolio of unrelated one-offs reads as drift. A loose theme — a domain, a problem type, a kind of system you keep getting better at — turns scattered projects into a narrative of growth. Reviewers remember a story far longer than a list.

slides.title: The avoid-list, distilled
bullets: Originality over polished clones
Finish before starting the next
README first — it's the front door
Honest metrics + named limitations
Public, runnable, and tied together

slides.title: That's Day 100. Thank you for building with me.
slides.body: 100 days done. Now go ship the portfolio that proves it. The next move is yours.
